Where the parties and the arbitral tribunal cannot agree on the determination of the fees or the arbitration agreement does not contain any provision concerning the determination of the fees, or where no reference has been made by the parties in this respect to the established international rules or institutional arbitration rules, the fees of the arbitral tribunal shall be determined in accordance with the schedule of fees determined annually by the Ministry of Justice following consultations with the relevant professional organizations with public body status. Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the fees of the presiding arbitrator shall be calculated as ten percent more than the fee to be paid to each arbitrator. No additional fees shall be payable for the correction or interpretation of the award, or in connection with the issuance of an additional award. B) The arbitral tribunal shall state the costs of the arbitration in its arbitral award. The costs [of the arbitration or proceedings] shall include: 1. the fees of the arbitrators; 2. the arbitrators' travel and other expenses; 3. the fees paid to the arbitral tribunal appointed experts and other persons whose assistance was sought, as well as the costs of any site inspection; 4. the witnesses' travel and other expenses, to the extent approved by the arbitral tribunal; 5. the legal representation costs of the successful party, if any, as determined by the arbitral tribunal and calculated in accordance with the minimum fee schedule for attorneys; 6. court fees and charges payable in respect of applications to the courts made pursuant to this Law; 7. notification expenses with respect to the arbitral proceedings. C) The arbitral tribunal may request the claimant to deposit an advance for the arbitration costs. Where the advance is not paid within the period determined in the arbitral tribunal's decision, the arbitral tribunal may suspend the proceedings. The arbitral proceedings shall be continued should the advance be paid within thirty days from the notification to the parties of the suspension of the proceedings; otherwise, the arbitration shall terminate. Following the issuance of the arbitral award, the arbitral tribunal shall provide to the parties a document showing the places of expenditure and the amount of the advance paid, and return any remaining balance to the paying party. D) Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the costs of the proceedings shall be borne by the unsuccessful party. Where both parties' claims are partially upheld in the arbitral award, the costs of the proceedings shall be apportioned among the parties having regard to their measure of rightfulness. The award of the arbitral tribunal terminating the arbitral proceedings or recording the settlement between the parties shall also set out the costs of the arbitral proceedings.

Chapter 7. Final Provisions Article 17 Repealed and inapplicable provisions Unless otherwise stated, the provisions of the Code of Civil Procedure shall not apply with respect to the matters regulated by this Law. Article 5 of the Law Concerning the Principles to be adhered to in Respect of Arbitration Proceedings Arising from Concession Contracts Concerning Public Services, Law No. 4501 of 21 January 2000 is repealed. Additional Article 1 (Added 28/2/2018-7101/54). Competent court in respect of subject matter of dispute Duties and authorities granted to the civil court of first instance (asliye hukuk mahkemesi) in this Law shall be exercised by the civil court of first instance (asliye hukuk mahkemesi) or the commercial court of first instance (asliye ticaret mahkemesi), according to the subject matter of the dispute. Temporary Article 1 Where the parties and the arbitral tribunal cannot agree on the fees , or the arbitration agreement does not contain any provision regarding the determination of the fees or the parties have made no reference in respect of the fees to either internationally established rules or institutional arbitration rules, the fees of the arbitrators shall be determined by civil court of first instance (asliye hukuk mahkemesi), having regard to the nature of the dispute and the [likely] period of the arbitral proceedings, until such time the Ministry of Justice determines the relevant fee schedule. The principles and procedures regarding the preparation of the fee schedule shall be specified in the regulation to be issued by the Ministry of Justice within six months following the publication of this Law. Article 18 Entry in force This Law enters into force on the date of its publication. Article 19 Implementation The provisions of this Law shall be implemented by the Council of Ministers.

title: State Immunity and the Recognition and Enforcement of ICSID Awards: Taking Stock

organizations: International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes

sections.title: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: 1 INTRODUCTION Is a sovereign state entitled to immunity from the jurisdiction of a court before which the recognition and enforcement of an award obtained under the Convention on the Settlement of Investment Disputes between States and Nationals of Other States (‘ICSID Convention’)1 Convention on the Settlement of Investment Disputes between States and Nationals of Other States, 14 Oct. 1966, 575 U.N.T.S. 159. are sought? Courts from various jurisdictions have grappled with this question in recent times. This article is primarily a response to the decision of the English Court of Appeal in Infrastructure Services Luxembourg v. Kingdom of Spain (‘Infrastructure Services (EWCA)’),2 [2024] EWCA Civ 1257. Permission to appeal to the UK Supreme Court was recently granted. which dealt squarely with this question, and the issues that decision raises in English law. Secondarily, it attempts to canvass the plurality of approaches taken to the same question by courts from several common law jurisdictions. While each decision must be considered in the context of its originating jurisdiction’s state immunity legislation, this article aims to highlight certain generally applicable principles. Infrastructure Services (EWCA) was a conjoined appeal from two decisions of the English High Court: one in the same case (‘Infrastructure Services (EWHC)’)3 Infrastructure Services Luxembourg Sarl v. Spain [2023] EWHC 1226 (Comm). and the other in the separate, but analogous, decision in Border Timbers Ltd v. Zimbabwe (‘Border Timbers’).4 Border Timbers Ltd v. Zimbabwe [2024] EWHC 58 (Comm); [2024] 1 WLUK 181. In both cases, the High Court decided that the respondent states were not entitled to resist the registration5 ‘Registration’, in the English context, has the effect of granting the ICSID award the ‘same force and effect for the purposes of execution as if it had been a judgment of the High Court’ and allows proceedings to be taken on the pecuniary obligations under the award: Arbitration (International Investment Disputes) Act 1966, s. 2(1). It is thus equivalent to ‘recognition and enforcement’ under the ICSID Convention. of ICSID awards in England and Wales on the basis of state immunity, albeit for different reasons. The Court of Appeal reached the same conclusion on appeal via a slightly different route. In essence, and departing from the reasoning of the courts in Infrastructure Services (EWHC) and Border Timbers, the Court of Appeal held that when an ICSID award creditor seeks to register an ICSID award in the United Kingdom (‘UK’), the English State Immunity Act 1978 (‘SIA’) is in principle engaged, so as to confer immunity on the respondent state by virtue of section 1 of the SIA. That provision states that a state is immune from the jurisdiction of courts in the UK unless one of the exceptions to immunity in the following provisions of the SIA applies. The Court of Appeal held that in such registration proceedings, (at least) one such exception is made out, namely the ‘submission to the jurisdiction’ exception contained in section 2 of the SIA. Specifically, the Court held that Article 54 of the ICSID Convention amounts to an express and sufficiently clear submission to the jurisdiction within the meaning of section 2 of the SIA.6 Infrastructure Services (EWCA), paras 59–103. Article 54(1) of the ICSID Convention is the key sub-provision within Article 54 that imposes an obligation on ICSID Contracting states to recognize and enforce ICSID awards. That provides as follows:Each Contracting State shall recognize an award rendered pursuant to this Convention as binding and enforce the pecuniary obligations imposed by that award within its territories as if it were a final judgment of a court in that State. Article 54(2) requires an award creditor seeking the recognition and enforcement of an ICSID award to furnish a certified copy of the award to a designated court or other authority of the Contracting state. Article 54(3), meanwhile, provides that the execution of ICSID awards shall be governed by the laws concerning the execution of judgments in force in the Contracting state in which such execution is sought. Finally, Article 55 complements Article 54 by providing that ‘[n]othing in Article 54 shall be construed as derogating from the law in force in any Contracting State’ relating to a (foreign) state’s immunity from execution (as opposed to its jurisdictional immunity). For the sake of clarity and consistency, this article adopts the tripartite distinction between ‘recognition’, ‘enforcement’ and ‘execution’ elucidated by the High Court of Australia (‘HCA’) in its recent decision in Kingdom of Spain v. Infrastructure Services Luxembourg S.a.r.l. (‘Infrastructure Services (HCA)’),7 [2023] HCA 11. such that ‘recognition’ refers to the process by which the courts of an ICSID Contracting state recognizes an ICSID award as binding8 Ibid., para. 43.; ‘enforcement’ is the process of granting an award the status of a judgment of a domestic court9 Ibid., para. 61. It is outside the scope of this article to comment on whether the obligation to ‘enforce’ an ICSID award entails treating it as equivalent to a domestic court judgment for all purposes, or merely for the purpose of providing a legal basis for execution. See Micula & ors v. Romania (European Commission intervening) [2020] UKSC 5; [2020] 1 WLR 1033.; and ‘execution’ refers to the taking of measures of constraint against the property of the award debtor in satisfaction of the award.10 Ibid., para. 45. In light of its conclusion on the ‘submission to the jurisdiction’ exception to immunity contained in section 2 of the SIA, the Court of Appeal in Infrastructure Services (EWCA) considered it unnecessary to decide whether there was also an exception to the respondent state’s immunity by virtue of the arbitration exception contained in section 9 of the SIA.11 Infrastructure Services (EWCA), paras 104–105. That section provides that when a state has agreed in writing to submit a dispute to arbitration, that state is not immune ‘as respects proceedings in the courts of the United Kingdom’ which relate to the arbitration.12 SIA, s. 9. This article makes two alternative arguments in response to the decision in Infrastructure Services (EWCA). First, English proceedings for the recognition and enforcement of ICSID awards should be ‘automatic’, that is, with the question of state immunity not even arising at all.13 See George A. Bermann, Understanding ICSID Article 54, 35(2) ICSID Rev. 311 (2020), doi: 10.1093/icsidreview/siaa020. It is only at the ‘execution’ phase – where measures of constraint are taken against the property of the award debtor – that the question of state immunity properly arises. This is dealt with in Part 2 of this article. Second, if the first proposition is incorrect, and state immunity is indeed a proper subject for judicial determination in proceedings to recognize and enforce ICSID awards, then it is improper to rely solely on the language of Article 54 of the ICSID Convention for the purposes of establishing that a respondent state has waived its immunity. Thus, in the English context specifically, if the general immunity conferred on the respondent state by section 1 of the SIA is engaged in recognition and enforcement proceedings, it should generally be the arbitration exception contained in section 9 of the SIA, rather than the ‘submission to the jurisdiction’ exception contained in section 2 of the SIA, that supplies the relevant exception to immunity. This is dealt with in Part 3. While the main focus of this article is on ICSID awards, the arguments advanced in this Part are just as applicable when dealing with New York Convention14 Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards, 10 Jun. 1958, 330 U.N.T.S. 3. awards, and indeed many of the cases relied on concerned awards of the latter type. Part 4 concludes. It is worth stating at the outset that many of the cases referred to in this article involved the same or similarly-named entities, and as such, these cases tend to have very similar names. It is hoped that, by distinguishing them by the court from which they originated, less confusion will arise.

sections.title: ‘AUTOMATIC’ RECOGNITION AND ENFORCEMENT
sections.text: 2 ‘AUTOMATIC’ RECOGNITION AND ENFORCEMENT The first argument made in this article is that questions of state immunity do not, and should not, arise at all in proceedings for the recognition and enforcement of ICSID awards. 2.1 Niceties of the English civil procedure rules The starting point for the following analysis is the English High Court’s decision in Border Timbers. In that decision, Dias J analysed the relevant parts of the English civil procedure rules (‘CPR’) governing applications to register ICSID awards in England and Wales and concluded that it was arguable that ICSID award registration proceedings did not engage the doctrine of state immunity at all.15 Border Timbers, para. 101. Unfortunately, that line of reasoning was rejected on appeal. However, none of the reasons given by the Court of Appeal in Infrastructure Services (EWCA) for its conclusion are persuasive. In this author’s view, Dias J was right to characterize ICSID award registration proceedings as essentially ‘ministerial’ in character, meaning that the doctrine of state immunity is not engaged at that stage.16 Ibid., para. 106(b). In rejecting Dias J’s view, the Court of Appeal reasoned that the registration of an ICSID award is an adjudicative act and not merely a ministerial or administrative act, because it requires a judge ‘to be satisfied to the requisite standard as to the proof of authenticity and the “other evidential requirements” of the [Arbitration (International Investment Disputes) Act 1966 (“1966 Act”)] (and CPR Part 62.21)’, a task that even if straightforward was no less adjudicative in nature.17 Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 37. Examples of these evidential requirements include the name of the award creditor and its address for service within the jurisdiction and the name of the award debtor and its address or place of business, if known. See CPR, R. 62.21(2)–(4), R. 74.4(2)(a)–(d). The 1966 Act is the statute by which the ICSID Convention was implemented in English law18 Border Timbers, para. 15.; it establishes the domestic regime for enforcing ICSID awards in the UK and meets the UK’s obligations under the ICSID Convention. The significance of the Court’s holding was that registration proceedings involved an exercise of jurisdiction over the respondent state, which in turn justified the application of section 1 of the SIA since that section specifically provides that a state is immune from the ‘jurisdiction’ of the UK courts (unless an exception to immunity is established). The Court of Appeal buttressed its position by relying on Stanley Burton J’s analogous decision in AIC Limited v. Federal Government of Nigeria (‘AIC’).19 [2003] EWHC 1357 (QB). There, Stanley Burton J held that the registration of a foreign judgment under the Administration of Justice Act 1920 (‘1920 Act’) always involves the exercise by the court of its jurisdiction and was subject to the immunity conferred by section 1 of the SIA, even if such an act does not require the court to exercise its judgment (i.e., discretion).20 AIC, paras 18–19. It is regrettable that the Court of Appeal did not engage with the observations made by Dias J in Border Timbers as to why the analogy with the 1920 Act is imperfect. As Dias J recognized, registering foreign judgments under the 1920 Act involves the court exercising a discretion, whereas registering an ICSID Award under the 1966 Act does not. Where a party seeks to register a foreign judgment under the 1920 Act, the court ‘may, if in all the circumstances of the case they think it just and convenient that the judgment should be enforced … order the judgment to be registered accordingly’.21 1920 Act, s. 9(1) (emphasis added). Indeed, under the 1920 Act, the registering court must also determine if any of the situations precluding registration as listed under the Act applies, including whether ‘the original court acted without jurisdiction’,22 Ibid., s. 9(2)(a). ‘the judgment was obtained by fraud’,23 Ibid., s. 9(2)(d). or whether the registering court could not have entertained the original cause of action ‘for reasons of public policy or for some other similar reason’.24 Ibid., s. 9(2)(f). In contrast, under the 1966 Act, the ICSID award creditor is ‘entitled’ to have its award registered,25 1966 Act, s. 1(2) (emphasis added). subject only to the award creditor exhibiting an authenticated copy of the award (as well as certain other procedural requirements).26 CPR, R. 62.21. Unlike the 1920 Act, the 1966 Act (reflecting Article 54 of the ICSID Convention) does not expressly establish any grounds on which the respondent state may resist enforcement. It is moreover significant that the example that Stanley Burton J used in AIC to support his position that an act need not involve the exercise of judgment by a court for it to be an ‘exercise of jurisdiction’ is not readily transposable to the context of ICSID award registration proceedings. The judge had cited the issuing of a claim form against a state as an instance of an ‘administrative act’ that was ‘nonetheless an exercise of jurisdiction’.27 AIC, para. 19. This is because section 12(1) of the SIA requires a claim form to be served by transmission through the UK Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the state in question, which in turn requires the permission of the court (for service out of the jurisdiction), after the court has been satisfied that the state is not immune.28 See CPR, Part 6; ibid. However, there is no similar requirement for an ICSID award creditor to serve an arbitration claim form on the respondent state.29 Unión Fenosa Gas, S A. v. Arab Republic of Egypt [2020] EWHC 1723 (Comm); [2020] 1 WLR 4732. As Dias J in Border Timbers correctly recognized, in reliance on the UK Supreme Court’s 2021 decision in General Dynamics UK Ltd v. Libya (‘General Dynamics (UKSC)’),30 [2021] UKSC 22; [2022] AC 318. it is only the court’s order granting registration of an ICSID award that must be served on the respondent state31 As required under CPR, R. 74.6. and it is only then that the state is ‘formally impleaded and the jurisdiction of the English court formally invoked against the state’ and when the doctrine of state immunity is therefore engaged.32 Border Timbers, para. 106(c). As the UK Supreme Court in General Dynamics (UKSC) clarified, the main rationale for this is that if the order granting permission were not served on the respondent state,33 A position unsuccessfully contended for by the award creditor in that case. then the state might well be ‘unaware of the enforcement proceedings and may not have the opportunity to assert immunity from enforcement’ before measures of constraint were taken against its property in the jurisdiction.34 General Dynamics (UKSC), para. 44. Considerations of comity underpinned this view35 Ibid., para. 58.; the majority was anxious to avoid a situation whereby the initiation of the process to subject the respondent state to the jurisdiction of the English courts breached international law.36 Ibid., para. 60. See also Christopher Harris QC & Cameron Miles, General Dynamics United Kingdom Ltd. v. State of Libya [2021] UKSC 22, [2021] 3 WLR 231, 116(1) Am. J. Int’l L. 157, 161 (2022), doi: 10.1017/ajil.2021.67. Furthermore, unlike enforcement orders made under the English Arbitration Act 1996 (‘1996 Act’) for awards obtained under the New York Convention, the CPR expressly excludes, insofar as ICSID award registration orders are concerned, the requirement of notifying the respondent state of its right to set aside or appeal against the registration order and the time period for doing so, as well as the fact that no measures of enforcement will be taken until the expiry of that time period.37 CPR, R. 62.21(2)(d), R. 74.6(3)(c)–(e). See Mustill & Boyd: Commercial and Investor State Arbitration (David Foxton, Siddharth Dhar KC, Ricky Diwan KC, Salim Moollan KC, Paul Tan, Peter Webster & Samuel Wordsworth KC eds, 3d ed. 2024), para. 20.32. See also Border Timbers, para. 106(f). This strongly supports the view that the process of recognizing and enforcing ICSID awards is meant to be ‘automatic’ (as distinct from the execution phase), for why else would there be no requirement to notify the respondent state of its right to set aside or appeal the registration order? Moreover, the absence of any obligation to notify the respondent state of the fact that enforcement measures38 Which, in this context, likely refers to ‘execution’ measures. will not be taken until the expiry of the time period normally reserved for the award debtor to apply to set aside or appeal the registration order strongly suggests that execution proceedings to satisfy the respondent state’s pecuniary obligations under an ICSID award were always contemplated to be separate and additional to the first phase (recognition and enforcement), in line with the distinction drawn in Articles 54 and 55 of the ICSID Convention. The position defended here accords with the classic statement by Lord Atkin in Compania Naviera Vascongado v. Steamship Cristina (The Cristina), namely, that a state’s courts ‘will not implead a foreign sovereign, that is, they will not by their process make him against his will a party to legal proceedings whether the proceedings involve process against his person or seek to recover from him specific property or damages’.39 [1938] AC 485, at 490. Far from impleading the respondent state against its will, ICSID award registration proceedings do no more than give effect to the UK’s international obligations under Article 54 of the ICSID Convention, which the other ICSID Member States have consented to in signing the Convention. Further, the recovery of ‘specific property or damages’ from the respondent state occurs only, if at all, during the subsequent execution phase. For the sake of completeness, it should be stated that the relevant part of the English CPR concerning the registration of ICSID awards – CPR Part 62.21 – applies certain specified provisions of Part 74 of the CPR ‘with such modifications as may be necessary in relation to an award as they apply in relation to a judgment to which Part I of the Foreign Judgments (Reciprocal Enforcement) Act 1933 [(“1933 Act”)] applies’.40 CPR, R. 62.21(2). The 1933 Act is concerned, inter alia, with the enforcement of foreign judgments in the UK originating from countries that accord reciprocal treatment to UK judgments. One of those rules states that an application under the 1933 Act must confirm that registration cannot be set aside under section 4 of that Act.41 Ibid., R. 62.21(2)(c), R. 74.4(4)(c). Reading this provision ‘with such modifications as may be necessary’ in relation to an ICSID award,42 Ibid., R. 62.21(2). this suggests on its face that the court must determine on an application to register an ICSID award under the 1966 Act that none of the provisions of section 4 of the 1933 Act could cause registration to be set aside. The 1933 Act contains a familiar list of grounds for setting aside a registered foreign judgment, including that the original court lacked jurisdiction, that the judgment was obtained by fraud, and that enforcement of the judgment would be contrary to the public policy of the registering court.43 1933 Act, s. 4(1)(a). Does that mean that when an English court is contemplating an application for the registration of an ICSID award under the 1966 Act, the 1933 Act is engaged in such a way that the court must exercise its discretion in a manner akin to under the 1920 Act (as considered in AIC), with the result that section 1 of the SIA applies? The point does not appear to have been considered in detail by Dias J in Border Timbers,44 Save for a brief reference to the 1933 Act, para. 97. but in this author’s view the answer is that it does not. It cannot be the case that the drafters of the CPR intended to provide for a discretionary review of the award on matters such as the tribunal’s jurisdiction via the backdoor, particularly as the drafters expressly specified that ‘such modifications as may be necessary’ must be made when applying Part 74 of the CPR to registration proceedings under the 1966 Act. In the closed system established by the ICSID Convention, matters such as the tribunal’s jurisdiction are intended to be argued and settled within the system itself (i.e., by the tribunal itself and potentially by an ad hoc annulment committee), not by the enforcing courts. This interpretation is bolstered by the fact that Rule 74.7 of the CPR – which provides for applications to set aside the registration of foreign judgments on grounds akin to those stated above – is not listed in Rule 62.21 as one of the Rules from CPR Part 74 that are to be applied mutatis mutandis in the context of registering ICSID awards.45 CPR, R. 62.21(2). 2.2 An ‘act of sovereignty’ In holding that ICSID award registration proceedings did engage section 1 of the SIA, the Court of Appeal in Infrastructure Services (EWCA) further reasoned that the act of an English court entering judgment against a foreign state on the basis of a decision that the requirements of a UK statute had been met was a ‘clear case of the English court exercising its adjudicative jurisdiction over the foreign State’,46 Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 38. and constituted an ‘act of sovereignty’ (as that phrase was used in General Dynamics (UKSC)).47 General Dynamics (UKSC), para. 43 (Lord Lloyd-Jones). Yet it is doubtful whether any sort of ‘adjudication’ is being carried out by the English court if all it is doing is verifying that the administrative requirements of the 1966 Act are met and giving effect to the award creditor’s entitlement under the 1966 Act to have the award registered. It is even more doubtful if anything can be gleaned from how the phrase ‘act of sovereignty’ was used in General Dynamics (UKSC): all that was said there is that ‘[t]he exercise of jurisdiction by the courts of one state over another state is an act of sovereignty’, the service of process on a state being one such example giving rise to ‘particular sensibilities’.48 Ibid. As argued above, the process of registering an ICSID award can hardly be said to meet that threshold. Whether something is an ‘exercise of jurisdiction’ (giving rise to ‘particular sensibilities’) was the very matter that the Court of Appeal had to determine, yet its decision does little more than merely assert that this is so. In another recent decision of the UK Supreme Court – The Royal Embassy of Saudi Arabia (Cultural Bureau) v. Costantine – the Court stated that exercising jurisdiction over a foreign state violates its sovereignty and involves a breach of international law when it entails requiring a foreign state ‘entitled to immunity to appear before a court and to enquire into its conduct of sovereign affairs’, unless an exception to immunity applies.49 [2025] UKSC 9, para. 38. Even putting aside the fact that this formulation assumes that the foreign state in question is prima facie entitled to state immunity, it is again difficult to see how the registration of an ICSID award against a respondent state would meet this standard. In certifying that the administrative prerequisites to registration have been met, with the outcome that the award is entered into the register and kept for that purpose at the Admiralty and Commercial Registry,50 Civil Rules & Practice Directions, Practice Direction 62 – Arbitration, para. 16.1. the registering court is not enquiring into the respondent state’s ‘conduct of sovereign affairs’, nor requiring the state to appear before it. 2.3 The SIA as a ‘complete code’ Section 1 of the SIA provides that a state is immune from the jurisdiction of courts in the UK unless one of the exceptions to immunity in the following provisions of the SIA applies. Another reason the Court of Appeal in Infrastructure Services (EWCA) cited in support of its view that section 1 of the SIA had to apply to ICSID registration proceedings was that, according to prior case law of the UK Supreme Court, the SIA is a ‘complete code’.51 Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 52, citing Benkharbouche v. Embassy of the Republic of Sudan (Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs and others intervening) [2017] UKSC 62; [2019] AC 777, para. 39 (Lord Sumption). Such reasoning illustrates the risk of placing too much emphasis on a turn of phrase and extrapolating from it a more general principle. Again, context is key. In the part of the judgment in Benkharbouche v. Embassy of the Republic of Sudan (Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs and others intervening) (‘Benkharbouche’) in which Lord Sumption used that phrase, His Lordship was dismissing the suggestion that the UK had adopted an absolute doctrine of state immunity. Rather, as Lord Sumption clarified, the restrictive doctrine of state immunity applies in the UK, by which a state is entitled to immunity only in respect of acts done in the exercise of sovereign authority, despite the way that the provisions in the SIA are ordered.52 Benkharbouche, paras 37–39. Part 1 of the SIA is thus a ‘complete code’, as Lord Sumption recognized, in the sense that all of the exceptions to state immunity recognized in English law are to be found in that Act. But whether any particular case involving a state falls under the ‘jurisdiction of the courts of the United Kingdom’ within the meaning of section 1(1) of the SIA was not a matter which Lord Sumption addressed in Benkharbouche. Registration of an ICSID award pursuant to the 1966 Act may well involve a state, in the very broad sense of the respondent state having participated in an ICSID arbitration and been made the subject of an adverse award against it, but whether the registration process itself entails the state being subject to an adjudicative act of an English court so as to trigger the application of the SIA is another matter altogether. Indeed, the recent decision in Hulley & ors v. Russian Federation (‘Hulley’) neatly identifies the dangers of overreliance on the idea of the SIA as a ‘complete code’.53 [2025] EWCA Civ 108. Permission to appeal to the UK Supreme Court was recently refused. The Court of Appeal held in that case that an issue estoppel based on a foreign judgment is available against a foreign state when an English court is determining whether state immunity applies. The Court acknowledged that the SIA is ‘comprehensive in that it sets out the only circumstances in which a state may lose immunity from the adjudicative and enforcement jurisdiction’ of English courts, but noted that the Act is silent ‘about the legal principles by which it is to be determined whether one of the exceptions to immunity applies’.54 Hulley, para. 22. In addition to issue estoppel, which the Court held was an English substantive law right that the claimants could rely on,55 Ibid., paras 55–67. the Court cited the standard of proof which must be satisfied if an exception to state immunity is to be found – the balance of probabilities – as another ‘creature of the common law’56 Ibid., para. 22. that lies outside the four corners of the SIA but which nevertheless is engaged when applying the SIA. Hulley thus demonstrates the conceptual permissibility of describing the SIA as a ‘complete’ or ‘comprehensive’ code while also recognizing that it is not the sole source of the principles applicable whenever state immunity is (or may be) in question. To say – as the Court of Appeal did in Infrastructure Services (EWCA) – that the SIA has to apply because it is a complete code involves an improper leap of logic that begs the question of whether that ‘code’ is even engaged in the first place. 2.4 Drafting history of the SIA One final aspect of the Court of Appeal’s reasoning in Infrastructure Services (EWCA) involved examining the drafting history of section 9 of the SIA. As referred to earlier, this contains the ‘arbitration exception’ to state immunity. In its current form, it provides that: ‘Where a State has agreed in writing to submit a dispute which has arisen, or may arise, to arbitration, the State is not immune as respects proceedings in the courts of the United Kingdom which relate to the arbitration’. The arbitration exception to immunity contained in section 9 of the Bill that later became the SIA originally only excepted immunity where the arbitration was in or according to the law of the UK.57 Debate on the State Immunity Bill in Standing Committee D of the House of Lords, 18 May 1978, at 3856. I am especially grateful to Dr Renae Satterley of The Honourable Society of the Middle Temple for her invaluable help in tracking down a copy of this document, which proved particularly elusive and difficult to find. However, the then-Solicitor-General moved an amendment to remove that qualification to give effect to the UK’s treaty obligations under Article 54 of the ICSID Convention and to ‘ensure that a State has no immunity in respect of enforcement proceedings for any foreign arbitral award’.58 Ibid. The Court of Appeal in Infrastructure Services (EWCA) reasoned that this amendment revealed a ‘legislative intent … that the SIA would apply but that registration of ICSID awards would be capable of passing through one of the exceptions’.59 Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 58. This reasoning is suspect. Removing a statutory conflict with an international treaty obligation does not necessarily indicate an intention for ICSID award registration proceedings to pass through one of the exceptions to the SIA, for it would be equally compatible with the ICSID regime to say that the SIA simply does not apply at all at the registration stage. 2.5 Other considerations Looking outside the confines of the Court of Appeal’s reasoning in Infrastructure Services (EWCA), which was focused primarily on English case law and the SIA, there are several generally applicable reasons supporting the conclusion that proceedings for the recognition and enforcement of ICSID awards should not engage questions of state immunity at all, in whichever jurisdiction recognition and enforcement are sought. First, Mr Aron Broches, the former General Counsel of the World Bank and ‘principal architect of the [ICSID] Convention’,60 Infrastructure Services (HCA), para. 49. appears to have supported the view that recognition and enforcement proceedings were intended to be ‘automatic’. In an article written in 1987, some twenty years after the ICSID Convention came into force (an article on which the HCA in Infrastructure Services (HCA) placed considerable reliance), Mr Broches commented on the reasoning of the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York (‘SDNY’) in Liberian Eastern Timber Corporation (LETCO) v. Government of the Republic of Liberia (‘LETCO’).61 650 F. Supp. 73 (S. D. NY. 1986). In that case, the SDNY held that it had subject-matter jurisdiction under sections 1604 and 1605 of the United States Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act62 Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act, 28 U.S.C. §§1602–1611. (‘FSIA’) to enter judgment based upon an ICSID award. Mr Broches noted that, in doing so, the court ‘paraphrased section 1604 of the FSIA, stating that “a foreign State is immune from the jurisdiction of the courts of the United States […] except for specifically enumerated exceptions” and that one such exception is an explicit or implicit waiver of immunity as provided in FSIA section 1605(a)(i)’.63 Aron Broches, Awards Rendered Pursuant to the ICSID Convention: Binding Force, Finality, Recognition, Enforcement, Execution, 2(2) ICSID Rev. 287, 325 (1987), doi: 10.1093/icsidreview/2.2.287. Mr Broches observed, however, that the SDNY omitted to mention that section 1604 is expressed to be ‘[s]ubject to existing international agreements to which the United States is a party’, including the ICSID Convention.64 Ibid., at 325. Accordingly, Mr Broches suggested, while the SDNY was not wrong to reach its conclusion via the section 1605 waiver exception, it would have been preferable for its primary reasoning to be ‘based on the Convention, which … denies immunity of jurisdiction’.65 Ibid., at 326. Importantly, he stated that the urge to ‘reach a conclusion which is mandated by an international agreement through the application of … national laws [i.e., through the waiver exception in section 1605]’, based on a desire to ‘avoid issues such as the relation between domestic and treaty law and the self-executing character vel non of the treaty provision in question’, could not apply where section 1604 was explicitly made subject to the ICSID Convention.66 Ibid., at 325. As this reveals, the ICSID regime’s chief architect considered that state immunity ought not even to have been in question in the first place when dealing with the recognition and enforcement of ICSID awards. Second, the suggestion that ICSID award recognition and enforcement proceedings should not engage questions of state immunity is not new. From at least as early as 1983, it has been observed in the literature (albeit largely in the context of the United States) that the ICSID Convention ‘depoliticizes commercial dispute resolution by obviating the need to consider, at least at the initial stage, whether a foreign state is entitled to immunity’67 G. B. Sullivan, Implicit Waiver of Sovereign Immunity by Consent to Arbitration: Territorial Scope and Procedural Limits, 18 Tex. Int’l L.J. 329, 334 (1983), doi: 10.1093/law/9780198744412.003.0012.; that the LETCO court should not have considered the FSIA at all since it ‘conflicts with Article 54’s automatic recognition language because it begins with the proposition that all states are entitled to immunity and then carves out specific exceptions to the rule’68 Susan Choi, Judicial Enforcement of Arbitration Awards under the ICSID and New York Conventions, 28 N.Y.U. J. Int’l L. & Pol. 175, 185 (1995), doi: 10.1093/arbitration/5.1.2. Compare, Roger P. Alford, Federal Courts, International Tribunals, and the Continuum of Deference, 43(3) Va. J. Int’l L. 675, 690 (2003), doi: 10.1093/acprof:oso/9780198716945.003.0003.; and that the LETCO court ‘assumed without explanation that the FSIA was at all applicable when deciding that Liberia had waived its sovereign immunity when it entered into the concession agreement’,69 Dorothy Black Franzoni, International Law – Enforcement of International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes Arbitral Awards in the United States – Signatories to the Convention on the Settlement of Investment Disputes Between States and Nationals of Other States are not Entitled to Sovereign Immunity With Respect to Enforcement of ICSID Arbitral Awards, Liberian Eastern Timber Corp. v. Government of Republic of Liberia, 650 F. Supp. 73 (S.D.N.Y. 1986), 18(1) Ga. J. Int’l & Compar. L. 101, 112 (1988), doi: 10.1017/cbo9780511581137.016 (emphasis added). even though the ‘mandatory enforcement language in Article 54 of the Convention implicitly denies sovereign immunity at the enforcement stage’.70 Ibid., at 107. Finally, by failing to adopt the ‘automatic’ approach to the recognition and enforcement of ICSID awards that the ICSID Convention arguably mandates, judicial attention in England has instead been diverted to trying to squeeze the square peg of the language of Article 54(1) of the ICSID Convention into the round hole of section 2 of the SIA. The resulting debates about whether the language of Article 54(1) is sufficient to amount to a submission to the jurisdiction of the court before which recognition and enforcement are sought (and/or a waiver of state immunity) are arguably antithetical to the spirit and purpose of the ICSID Convention, as Mr Broches has alluded to.

sections.title: SECTION 9, NOT SECTION 2; SPECIFICITY IS PARAMOUNT
sections.text: 3 SECTION 9, NOT SECTION 2; SPECIFICITY IS PARAMOUNT If the first proposition advanced in Part 2 of this article is incorrect, and the registration of ICSID awards in the UK should not be ‘automatic’, then the alternative argument this article advances is that it is improper to rely solely on the language of Article 54 of the ICSID Convention for the purposes of establishing that a respondent state has waived its immunity from the jurisdiction of the courts before which recognition and enforcement are sought. Moreover, in ICSID recognition and enforcement proceedings, it should normally be section 9 of the SIA (the arbitration exception), not section 2 of the SIA (the ‘submission to jurisdiction’ provision), to which the courts should look to determine whether the state has waived its claim to state immunity. 3.1 The HCA’s decision in Infrastructure Services The Court of Appeal in Infrastructure Services (EWCA) cited the HCA’s earlier decision in Infrastructure Services (HCA) at length in reaching the conclusion that Article 54 of the ICSID Convention constituted a waiver of adjudicative immunity, such that Spain and Zimbabwe were deemed to have submitted to the jurisdiction of the English courts for the purposes of section 2 of the SIA. Central to the Court of Appeal’s reasoning was the idea that the words of Article 54 constitute an express and sufficiently clear submission to the court’s jurisdiction.71 Infrastructure Services (EWCA), paras 91–98. It is therefore helpful to first outline the HCA’s decision, given the significant role it played in the English Court of Appeal’s own reasoning. In that case, the HCA had to decide – on materially the same facts as before the English Court of Appeal later – whether Spain’s being a party to the ICSID Convention meant that it had waived its immunity from the jurisdiction of the Australian courts on the basis that Article 54 of the ICSID Convention amounted to an ‘express’ agreement to submit to the jurisdiction within the meaning of section 10(2) of the Australian Foreign States Immunities Act (a provision analogous to section 2 of the SIA in the UK).72 Foreign States Immunities Act 1985, s. 10(2) (‘A foreign State may submit to the jurisdiction at any time, whether by agreement or otherwise, but a foreign State shall not be taken to have so submitted by reason only that it is a party to an agreement the proper law of which is the law of Australia’.). On the question of what it means for a waiver of immunity in an international agreement to be ‘express’, the HCA first considered a number of international law authorities, including a passage from the decision of the International Court of Justice in the Case Concerning Armed Activities on the Territory of the Congo stating that ‘waivers or renunciations of claims or rights must either be express or unequivocally implied from the conduct of the State alleged to have waived or renounced its right’.73 Infrastructure Services (HCA), para. 19, citing Case Concerning Armed Activities on the Territory of the Congo (Democratic Republic of the Congo v. Uganda), Judgment, I.C.J. Rep. 2005 (Dec. 19), at 168, para. 293. The HCA then went on to state that ‘express meaning can include implications, which constitute the unexpressed content of a statement or term and which are identified by inference’.74 Infrastructure Services (HCA), para. 23 (emphasis added). By contrast, there are ‘implied term[s], the meaning of which is derived primarily by inference from the conduct of the parties to the agreement and the circumstances in light of the express terms’.75 Ibid., para. 24 (emphasis added). To adopt the language used by commentators like Miles, the HCA’s reasoning suggests a recognition of the idea that there are, on the one hand, ‘express terms’ and their corresponding ‘explicatures’ – meaning such information as is ‘directly implied’ by the express terms themselves – and on the other hand, ‘implied terms’ that find their existence where the explicatures end.76 Cameron Miles, Implied Terms in Treaties, 119(1) Am. J. Int’l L. 57, 63–64 (2025), doi: 10.1017/ajil.2024.68. In light of that background, the HCA counselled against reading the ‘international authorities that insist upon express waiver of immunity in a treaty … as denying the ordinary and natural role of implications in elucidating the meaning of the express words of the treaty’.77 Infrastructure Services (HCA), para. 24. Since the implications are understood here as elucidating the meaning of the express words, it seems that the HCA had in mind implications in the form of explicatures as alluded to above. In other words, the HCA considered that an ‘express’ waiver could arise not only from the express words of the international agreement itself (i.e., the so-called text on the page), but also from the unexpressed meaning one infers from them. The HCA went on to state, however, that the requirement that a waiver be ‘express’ required ‘only that the expression of waiver be derived from the express words of the international agreement, whether as an express term or as a term implied for reasons including necessity’.78 Ibid., para. 25 (emphasis added). This last phrase – providing for terms implied for reasons including necessity – was said to derive from Lord Goff’s statement in R v. Bow Street Metropolitan Stipendiary Magistrate, ex p. Pinochet Ugarte (No. 3) 79 [2000] 1 AC 147. (‘Pinochet (No 3)’) that consent must be ‘express’ (which was in turn based on his acceptance that ‘[a] term can only be [recognized as] implied [in] a treaty for necessity, not to give the treaty maximum effect’).80 Infrastructure Services (HCA), para. 25, citing Pinochet (No 3), at 216. Yet it appears that, whereas Lord Goff thought that necessity was the ‘only’ circumstance in which a term could be implied in a treaty, the HCA took a broader view when it stated that terms could be implied for reasons ‘including’ necessity. Further, given the use of the word ‘or’, the HCA – in apparent tension with its earlier statement of principle – appears to suggest that the waiver can be ‘derived’ from either an explicature or an implicature (i.e., an implied term). The HCA went on to say that when international authorities insisted on the waiver being ‘express’, that was ‘an insistence that any inference of a waiver of immunity … be drawn with great care’, without denying that ‘implications are almost invariably contained in any (expressed) words of a treaty’.81 Infrastructure Services (HCA), para. 26. By putting the word ‘expressed’ in parentheses, the HCA appears again to have been alluding to the explicature/implicature distinction as used above, as well as the possibility that an ‘express’ waiver could be drawn from an implied term in the treaty. For that reason, the HCA held, even where an international agreement did not expressly use the word ‘waiver’, it was still possible to infer that it involved a waiver of immunity provided that the implication was ‘clear from the words used and the context’.82 Ibid. The HCA then considered Article 54 of the ICSID Convention itself. The Court rejected Spain’s contention that the express words of Article 54 were insufficiently clear to amount to a waiver of adjudicative immunity from recognition or enforcement proceedings.83 Ibid., para. 67. It would ‘distort’ the language of Article 54(1), the Court held, ‘to require separate conduct’ amounting to a waiver before an award could be recognized and enforced against a foreign state in Australia.84 Ibid., para. 69. While the Court’s preferred interpretation would mean that Article 55 of the ICSID Convention was ‘possible surplusage’, the travaux préparatoires to the ICSID Convention supported the view that Article 55 ‘has no independent work to do’ apart from reinforcing Articles 53 and 54.85 Ibid., para. 70. Spain was right to argue that Article 54s purpose was to ‘ensure that Contracting States were able to obtain effective remedies against private investors’, but to say this meant that Article 54 was not concerned with awards sought to be enforced against a state in a foreign court, as Spain had argued,86 Ibid., para. 67. missed the forest for the trees: Article 54 was intended to establish a parity of rights and obligations between states and investors alike.87 Ibid., paras 71–72. Rather surprisingly, there is little in the HCA’s judgment that applies the Court’s own analysis of what constitutes an ‘express’ term, as set out in the earlier part of its judgment, directly to the text of Article 54.88 Save for what is at most a very brief reference to it, paras 74–75. The HCA nonetheless concluded that ‘the express terms of Art. 54(1) involve a waiver of immunity from jurisdiction in relation to recognition and enforcement’.89 Infrastructure Services (HCA), para. 75 (emphasis added). It is not immediately obvious what the HCA meant by its observation that express words can ‘involve’ a waiver. Did it mean that the expression of waiver supposedly found in Article 54(1) was to be derived from the implications included within the express words of Article 54 (i.e., explicatures), a concept which the HCA earlier alluded to?90 Infrastructure Services (HCA), supra n. 77. Or did it mean that the expression of waiver could also be found by a ‘term implied for reasons including necessity’,91 Ibid. which, by definition, lies outside the realm of express terms and their explicatures? The distinction may seem theoretical and inconsequential at first blush, but we are dealing here with a putative waiver of a state’s fundamental entitlement at international law (sovereign immunity). As has been recognized, the HCA’s opaque reasoning risks ‘collapsing or rendering illusory the distinction between express and implied terms’ and creating inconsistent decisions around the world92 Miles, supra n. 76, at 98. (which has in fact already occurred, as considered below). On the assumption that what the HCA meant was that the waiver of immunity said to be contained in Article 54 was derived from a term implied for necessity, it is difficult to see what would ground such necessity. As explained in the first part of this article, it is equally valid to contend that the obligation to recognize and enforce ICSID awards was not meant to engage questions of state immunity at all. If that is right, then it no longer becomes necessary to imply a waiver of immunity into the text of Article 54. Additionally, Lord Goff’s speech in Pinochet (No 3) suggests that ‘necessity’ in the context of implied treaty terms is a very high bar to meet. After citing from academic commentaries and the work of the International Law Commission (‘ILC’) in its Draft articles on state immunity,93 Report of the International Law Commission on the Jurisdictional Immunities of States and their Property, YILC (1991) Vol. II, Part 2. Lord Goff observed that ‘implied consent to the exercise of [one state’s jurisdiction over another] is to be regarded only as an added explanation or justification for an otherwise valid and recognized exception’, the only example of which he found was ‘actual submission to the jurisdiction of the courts of another state’.94 Pinochet (No 3), at 216 (emphasis added). In other words, insofar as there could be an ‘implied’ waiver of state immunity, that provided at best an additional explanation or justification for some other expression of waiver, not an independent source of the waiver itself. Lord Goff also cited the United States Supreme Court’s dicta in Argentine Republic v. Amerada Hess Shipping Corporation that it could not see ‘how a foreign state can waive its immunity under para. 1605(a)(1) [FSIA] by signing an international agreement that contains no mention of a waiver of immunity to suit in United States courts’.95 109 US 683, 693 (1989). As Lord Goff noted, this emphasized the need for an express waiver; the Supreme Court’s reasoning could not be ‘explained away as due to the provisions of the United States Act’ since paragraph 1605 of the FSIA ‘contemplates the possibility of waiver by implication; but in the context of a treaty the Supreme Court was only prepared to contemplate express waiver’.96 Pinochet (No 3), at 216. This is all the more true in the case of the ICSID Convention, which declares in its Preamble that ‘no Contracting State shall by the mere fact of its ratification, acceptance or approval of this Convention and without its consent be deemed to be under any obligation to submit any particular dispute to conciliation or arbitration’.97 ICSID Convention, supra n. 5. Merely signing up to the ICSID regime does not oblige a Contracting state to use it; any waiver of state immunity must be found by something more, viz., a Contracting state specifically agreeing to submit a particular dispute to arbitration under the ICSID Convention.98 Mees Brenninkmeijer & Fabien Gélinas, The Problem of Execution Immunities and the ICSID Convention, 22(3) J. World Inv. & Trade 429, 450 (2021), doi: 10.1163/22119000-12340214. See also Christoph H. Schreuer, State Immunity: Some Recent Developments 90–91 (Cambridge Univ. Press 1988). Ironically, the English Court of Appeal in Infrastructure Services (EWCA) seems to have had this principle in mind when it acknowledged that the ICSID Convention is not itself an agreement to arbitrate but merely a ‘framework for the resolution of such disputes as the parties may agree in writing to submit to ICSID’.99 Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 17. This probably also explains why in NextEra Energy Global Holdings B.V. v. Kingdom of Spain (‘NextEra’), the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit preferred to leave open the question of whether merely signing up to the ICSID Convention or the New York Convention amounts to an implicit waiver of a state’s jurisdictional immunity for the purposes of recognition and enforcement proceedings.100 NextEra Energy Global Holdings B.V. v. Kingdom of Spain, No. 23–7031, 2024 WL 3837484 (D.C. Cir. 16 Aug. 2024), at 19–20. Instead, the Court found that the arbitration exception to immunity under the FSIA was engaged.101 Ibid., at 20–28. The Court reached this conclusion even though it acknowledged the existence of some lower United States court decisions holding that merely signing up to one of those two ‘framework’ Conventions did amount to a waiver of state immunity.102 Blue Ridge Invs., LLC v. Republic of Argentina, 735 F.3d 72, 84 (2d Cir. 2013) (ICSID Convention); Seetransport Wiking Trader v. Navimpex Centrala, 989 F.2d 572, 578–79 (2d Cir. 1993) (New York Convention). As one of the amicus briefs submitted to the Court put it:Becoming a party to either the New York Convention or the ICSID Convention, without more, does not provide the necessary ‘strong evidence’ that a foreign state intended to waive its sovereign immunity in United States courts. … These conventions establish frameworks for arbitrating disputes and enforcing arbitral awards that may result. But the conventions by themselves do not commit a foreign state to engage in arbitration and therefore they could not implicitly waive sovereign immunity for any enforcement action. See ICSID Convention [Preamble] … Rather, as explained above, a foreign state can be subject to arbitration only when it consents to an arbitration agreement. … That is why the district court in each of these cases recognized that ‘a specific arbitration agreement remains a jurisdictional prerequisite’ when a party attempts to invoke the waiver exception to enforce an arbitral award. … [I]n cases involving attempts to enforce arbitral awards against foreign states under the New York Convention or ICSID Convention, courts should rely on the FSIA’s specific arbitration exception rather than the more general waiver exception. 103 Brief for the United States as Amicus Curiae, filed 2 Feb. 2024, at 20–22 (emphasis added). Conceptual coherence aside, part of the reason for the Court’s maintaining this position is likely tactical. As the same amicus brief noted, if a waiver is to be found solely from a state being a party to a ‘framework’ agreement such as the New York Convention or the ICSID Convention, that could also have implications for the treatment of the United States in foreign courts: if foreign courts perceive the United States as taking a more liberal construction of implied waivers, they may well follow suit.104 Ibid., at 24. 3.2 The approach in Canada For the above reasons, it is submitted that the approach taken by the Superior Court of Québec in CC Devas (Mauritius) Ltd v. Republic of India (‘CC Devas’), focusing on multiple indicia of waiver rather than the text of ‘framework’ conventions such as the ICSID or New York Conventions alone, is the better one.105 [2022] QCCS 4785. In that case, the applicant and Antrix, an Indian state-owned company, had entered into an agreement for the provision of a commercial communication system to Indian customers. A number of Mauritian shareholders had made investments in the applicant which were covered by the India-Mauritius bilateral investment treaty (‘India-Mauritius BIT’).106 Agreement Between the Republic of India and The Republic of Mauritius For The Promotion and Reciprocal Protection of Investments (India-Mauritius), 4 Sep. 1998 (entered into force 20 Jun. 2000, terminated 22 Mar. 2017). Article 8(2) thereof provided for ICSID arbitration and ad hoc arbitration under the 1976 UNCITRAL Rules with certain modifications, including that the ‘arbitral award … shall be binding on the parties to the dispute’.107 Ibid., Art. 8(2). Antrix repudiated the agreement in 2011. This led to an UNCITRAL arbitration, which in turn resulted in a number of awards made in favour of the applicant. The applicant sought the recognition and enforcement of those awards in Québec. The Québec Superior Court found that the commercial activity exception in the Canadian state immunity legislation was satisfied, which sufficed for the Court to find that India could not rely on state immunity to oppose the claim.108 CC Devas, paras 139–141. Thus, the Court’s discussion about the waiver exception was, strictly speaking, obiter. The relevant provision of the Canadian SIA provides that a foreign state submits to the jurisdiction of the court where, inter alia, it ‘explicitly submits to the jurisdiction of the court by written agreement or otherwise either before or after the proceedings commence’.109 Canada State Immunity Act 1985, s. 4(2). Notably, unlike the English SIA, the Canadian SIA does not contain an arbitration exception. Much emphasis was placed by the Québec Superior Court on the phrase ‘shall be binding on the parties to the dispute’ within Article 8(2) of the India-Mauritius BIT. The Court considered that India had ‘explicitly submitted itself to the jurisdiction … for enforcement of the Treaty Awards by expressing its consent otherwise than by written agreement before the present proceedings commenced within the purview of Subsection 4(2)(a) of the [Canadian] SIA’.110 CC Devas, para. 152 (emphasis in original). The Court reasoned that the words ‘or otherwise’, indicated that the legislature did not intend to limit a state’s explicit consent to a written agreement.111 Ibid., paras 153–155. Instead, the Court found that the test was whether the waiver was clear and unambiguous.112 Ibid., para. 155. It went on to conclude that India’s agreement to proceed with the arbitration pursuant to the provisions of the BIT and its participation in the arbitration process constituted a clear and unequivocal waiver of its jurisdictional immunity in enforcement proceedings resulting therefrom.113 Ibid., para. 160. This conclusion was supported by previous Canadian decisions in which the courts had held that by entering into bilateral investment treaties and participating in arbitration thereunder, states had consented to have arbitral awards issued and enforced against them.114 Ibid., paras 161–162. The fact that the Superior Court’s analysis was predicated on an understanding that India’s submission to the jurisdiction was ‘otherwise’ than ‘by written agreement’ is notable if curious.115 Ibid., para. 152. See also ibid., para. 158 (by ‘unwritten agreement or consent’). It indicates that, for the Court, neither the text of the arbitration agreement within the BIT nor a framework treaty like the New York Convention amounted to a ‘written agreement’ for the purposes of establishing the waiver.116 Compare, Infrastructure Services (EWCA). The Court went on to state that, ‘having ratified the New York Convention, [India] confirmed again its clear and unequivocal waiver of immunity in the present proceedings’.117 CC Devas., para. 164 (emphasis added). This formulation suggests that ratification of a framework treaty like the New York Convention provided at most confirmatory force to the Court’s conclusion. Further, apart from entering into the India-Mauritius BIT and the New York Convention, the Superior Court concluded that India’s choice of the Netherlands – another signatory of the New York Convention – as the seat of the arbitration was another reason evidencing the finding of a clear and unequivocal submission ‘that [was] tantamount to an express waiver’.118 Ibid., para. 175. Finally, the Superior Court held that the fact that Rule 32(2) of the UNCITRAL Rules 1976 states that awards will be ‘final and binding’ on the parties ‘further demonstrate[d]’ that India had clearly and unequivocally agreed that UNCITRAL arbitration awards could be rendered and enforced against it.119 Ibid., paras 176–178. Thus, the Superior Court found that the requirement that a waiver of state immunity be clear and unambiguous was met through at least four means: (1) India’s agreement to arbitrate (and its participation in the arbitration) under the India-Mauritius BIT, (2) India’s ratification of the New York Convention, (3) India’s choice of a New York Convention signatory state as the seat of arbitration, and (4) the text of the rules applicable to the arbitration itself. It appears that the mere fact that India had signed up to the New York Convention provided at most confirmatory force – not a self-standing justification – for the court’s finding that it had waived its immunity. Admittedly, the Court’s reasoning is at times opaque; the use of language such as ‘confirmed again’ and ‘further demonstrate’, alongside the repeated use of summative language like ‘therefore’ in its reasoning, all in close proximity to one another,120 See ibid., paras 149–178. makes it difficult at times to clearly delineate which factor is, on its own, necessary and/or sufficient to establish a sufficiently clear and unequivocal submission to the jurisdiction. The decision of the Québec Superior Court was upheld on appeal by the Québec Court of Appeal in Republic of India v. CCDM Holdings (‘CCDM Holdings (QCCA)’).121 (2024) QCCA 1620. According to the English translation of the judgment, which was originally handed down in French, it appears as if the Québec Court of Appeal took a largely similar approach to that taken by the Québec Superior Court. There are however two notable areas of distinction. The first is that the Québec Court of Appeal only referred to three, not four, indicia in finding that India had waived its jurisdictional immunity under the Canadian SIA: India’s ratification of the New York Convention, India’s entering into the investment treaty which includes an arbitration clause, and India’s participation in the underlying arbitration proceedings without reserving its right to claim immunity.122 CCDM Holdings (QCCA), para. 80. Second, whereas the Québec Superior Court appears to have found an express waiver of India’s immunity,123 CC Devas, para. 175. the Québec Court of Appeal found that the three indicia mentioned above amounted to a waiver ‘by necessary implication’ of India’s immunity,124 CCDM Holdings (QCCA), para. 81 (emphasis in original). which the Court held ‘constitute[d] an explicit waiver’ under the Canadian state immunity legislation.125 Ibid. (emphasis added). 3.3 The importance of specificity What the above discussion seeks to highlight is the importance of specificity. It is insufficient merely to say that, because a state has signed up to the ICSID regime (or the New York Convention), that alone provides all that is necessary to constitute a waiver of its immunity. This accords with the position taken in Article 7(1) of the Convention on the Jurisdictional Immunities of States and Their Property (‘UNCJISP’),126 UN General Assembly, Convention on the Jurisdictional Immunities of States and Their Property (30 Nov. 2004) U.N. Doc. A/59/508, not yet in force. which provides that ‘[a] State cannot invoke immunity from jurisdiction in a proceeding before a court of another State with regard to a matter or case if it has expressly consented to the exercise of jurisdiction by the court with regard to the matter or case … by international agreement’. As the authors of the leading commentary on state immunity have put it, to be effective this requires an express waiver to identify the ‘matter or case’ to which the consent relates as well as the ‘proceeding and court in which the proceeding is instituted and to whose exercise of jurisdiction the waiver relates’.127 Hazel Fox & Philippa Webb, The Law of State Immunity 383 (Oxford Univ. Press 2015). Article 7 of the UNCJISP is itself a waiver by a state which ratifies the convention, but it is on its own ‘insufficiently precise to conform to its own requirement that the consent of the State be express and relate “to the exercise of the jurisdiction by the court with regard to the matter or case”’.128 Ibid., at 384. Returning to the Court of Appeal’s decision in Infrastructure Services (EWCA), the above explains why the Court was, in this author’s view, wrong to criticize Dias J in Border Timbers for saying that Article 54 of the ICSID Convention was insufficiently particularized as to the relevant parties and the relevant dispute.129 Border Timbers, para. 68. The Court of Appeal grounded its view of Article 54 on the basis that section 17(2) of the SIA provides that the reference to an ‘agreement’ in section 2 of the SIA includes references to a treaty or convention, and that such treaties ‘will often be made between multiple states and will provide for events many years into the future’.130 Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 98. Accordingly, the Court stated, ‘[p]rovisions for submission to the jurisdiction of contracting parties in such … conventions will necessarily not identify the parties to the dispute or any other details’.131 Ibid. The trouble with this reasoning is twofold. First, to say that provisions for submission to the jurisdiction in such instruments will necessarily not be particularized as to the parties and the dispute allows the tail to wag the dog. Whether or not the instrument does in fact provide for ‘submission to the jurisdiction’ was the very matter the Court had to ascertain; the Court’s reasoning, however, assumes the conclusion it is meant to justify. Second, while section 17(2) of the SIA does indeed say that an ‘agreement’ can take the form of a treaty or convention, it says nothing about the degree of specificity required in the language of such instruments. What this means as a matter of methodology is that section 9 of the SIA (the arbitration exception) should normally be the primary means of finding a waiver of state immunity insofar as ICSID or New York Convention enforcement proceedings are concerned. The state’s specific conduct in consenting to a particular arbitration, coupled with the language of the particular arbitration agreement in question, as well as the choice of any particular arbitral rules (if any), should shape the court’s inquiry into the question of waiver.132 It has been suggested that, in the ICSID context, the existence of an ICSID award alone should suffice to provide conclusive evidence of the existence of a valid arbitration agreement for the purposes of establishing a waiver of immunity via the s. 9 SIA exception. See Foxton et al., supra n. 37, para. 20.85. Compare, Border Timbers, para. 88; Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 105. The Court of Appeal in Svenska Petroleum Exploration AB v. Lithuania (No. 2) (‘Svenska’) got it right by focusing its analysis on the section 9 SIA exception, which it held was satisfied on the facts of the case (which concerned the attempted enforcement of an International Chamber of Commerce (‘ICC’) award).133 [2006] EWCA Civ 1529; [2007] QB 886. The underlying joint venture agreement had provided for disputes to be settled either by the Lithuanian courts or by arbitration under the auspices of the ICC.134 Ibid., para. 6. Another clause headed ‘Governing law and sovereign immunity’ provided that ‘Government and EPG hereby irrevocably waives [sic] all rights to sovereign immunity’. The Court in Svenska considered in obiter that the latter clause was ‘too imprecise’ to amount to a submission to the jurisdiction of the English courts within the meaning of section 2(1) of the SIA, although the Court noted that in any case the clause had to be read in the context of the Lithuanian government’s agreement to submit to ICC arbitration.135 Ibid., para. 128. See also Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 97. Further, the Court held that the requirements of section 2(1) of the SIA could not have been satisfied, since ‘[a] choice of jurisdiction clause if limited to the courts of a named State [viz., Lithuania], whilst it may be construed as a waiver of immunity before the courts of the named State, cannot readily be construed as a general waiver of immunity in relation to courts of other jurisdictions’.136 Fox & Webb, supra n. 127, at 389. Finally, it is worth noting the very recent decision of the English High Court in CC Devas (Mauritius) Ltd v. India (‘CC Devas (EWHC)’), which (correctly, in this author’s opinion) held that India’s ratification of the New York Convention could not on its own amount to a submission to the jurisdiction by prior written agreement within the meaning of section 2(2) of the SIA.137 [2025] EWHC 964 (Comm). In this respect, cf. the outcome in Infrastructure Services (EWCA). This was because the ratification of Article III of the New York Convention alone did not meet the threshold of a waiver in English law, which the Court gleaned inter alia from the decisions in Pinochet (No 3), Infrastructure Services (HCA) and Infrastructure Services (EWCA) – such a ratification was not ‘express, and expressed in a clear and recognisable manner, as by an unequivocal agreement’.138 CC Devas (EWHC), para. 82. The reason for this was said to be that, in many commercial and financial transactions involving states and state-owned enterprises, waiver of state immunity is a matter of contract.139 Ibid., para. 83. Against that backdrop, it ‘may be thought that the market is capable of restricting state immunity where it may arise’; given the ‘delicate and currently controversial’ balance between investors and states in investment disputes, there is ‘nothing … to impel a conclusion that ratification of the NYC is in and of itself a waiver of immunity, where express waivers are a commercial commonplace’.140 Ibid., para. 85. While the outcome of this decision is to be welcomed, it is questionable whether it is methodologically apposite to have regard to the so-called ‘market practice’ in determining whether the purported waiver was express, clear and unmistakable, especially when other courts like the one in Infrastructure Services (HCA) have approached the question from a purely linguistic perspective. 3.4 Interaction with the approach taken in finding waivers of execution immunity Not having sole regard to the language of framework instruments like the ICSID Convention or New York Convention for the purposes of finding a waiver of a state’s jurisdictional immunity also has the merit of aligning the approach with that taken in determining whether a state has waived its immunities from execution, since the latter typically involves a more microscopic analysis of a wider range of sources to establish a waiver. The recent decision of the English Court of Appeal in General Dynamics UK v. Libya 141 [2025] EWCA Civ 134; [2025] 2 WLUK 328. (‘General Dynamics (EWCA)’) – not to be confused with the earlier decision of the UK Supreme Court in the same case, General Dynamics (UKSC), as discussed above142 [2021] UKSC 22, supra n. 30. – is an excellent illustration of this point. General Dynamics was successful in an ICC arbitration against Libya. Clause 32 of the underlying contract, providing for ICC arbitration, provided that the decision of the arbitral panel ‘shall be final, binding and wholly enforceable’.143 General Dynamics (EWCA), para. 1. It was common ground that this sufficed to waive Libya’s immunity from the court’s jurisdiction within the meaning of section 9 of the SIA.144 Ibid., para. 2. The key question was whether Libya had also consented thereby, within the meaning of section 13(3) of the SIA, to the execution against its property of any ICC arbitration award made against it.145 Ibid. The Court of Appeal answered that question in the affirmative. What is notable in the Court of Appeal’s decision is the slight divergence in the views of the three judges who heard the appeal (although all three ultimately reached the same conclusion):(1) Phillips LJ thought that the phrase ‘final, binding and wholly enforceable’ on its own was insufficient to constitute a waiver of Libya’s immunity from execution.146 Ibid., para. 43. The lower court had erred in concluding that the words ‘final and binding’ alone achieved a waiver of adjudicative immunity, such that the phrase ‘wholly enforceable’ must have been intended to go further and refer to a waiver of execution immunity.147 Ibid., para. 42. This was because the phrase ‘final and binding’ merely signalled that an award would conclusively determine the issues between the parties (rather than be interim or provisional). Further, while ‘[m]any states’ regarded submission to arbitration as submission to the adjudicative jurisdiction in respect of that arbitration, that did not ‘render otiose a contractual submission to the adjudicative jurisdiction’ (through the remaining words ‘wholly enforceable’), especially in relation to states without the equivalent of section 9 of the SIA.148 Ibid. In light of that finding, Phillips LJ instead relied on the fact that clause 32 of the contract had provided for the arbitration to be conducted under the ICC Rules of Arbitration 1998. Article 28(6) thereof provides that parties who submit a dispute to arbitration under those Rules ‘undertake to carry out any award without delay and shall be deemed to have waived their right to any form of recourse insofar as such waiver can be validly made’. After citing two decisions of the French and US courts interpreting that provision (or its predecessor) as constituting a waiver of execution immunity,149 Creighton Ltd v. Qatar, Cour de Cassation 6 Jul. 2000; Walker International Holdings Ltd v. The Republic of Congo, 395 F.3d 229 (5th cir. 2004). Phillips LJ concluded that the undertaking in Article 28(6) of the ICC Rules to carry out the award without delay provided the ‘context’ against which Libya’s agreement in clause 32 of the contract that awards should be ‘wholly enforceable’ had to be interpreted.150 General Dynamics (EWCA), paras 43–45. Libya had therefore waived its immunity from execution. (2) Zacaroli LJ, on the other hand, thought that the phrase ‘wholly enforceable’ alone sufficed to establish a waiver of execution immunity. In his view, the wording of the ICC Rules only ‘reinforce[d] that conclusion’.151 Ibid., para. 48. This implies that Zacaroli LJ thought that the phrase ‘final and binding’ alone did achieve a waiver of adjudicative immunity, contra Phillips LJ. His Lordship otherwise agreed with Phillips LJ’s reasoning. (3) As for Lewison LJ, he considered that the mere fact of Libya’s submission of the dispute to arbitration sufficed to waive its adjudicative immunity in the UK courts by virtue of section 9 of the SIA. The words in clause 32 of the parties’ contract were only there ‘[f]or good measure’; that is, they merely had a confirmatory effect in relation to the question of waiver of adjudicative immunity.152 Ibid., para. 59. Somewhat confusingly, Lewison LJ also stated that since the award was a New York Convention award, recognition of the award was already achieved by virtue of Article III of the New York Convention, without the need for the phrase ‘final, binding and wholly enforceable’ in clause 32.153 Ibid., para. 64. As explained in this article, the latter proposition cannot be right, and His Lordship’s former position is to be preferred. He concluded that the ‘straightforward’ reading of the operative part of clause 32 amounted to a waiver both of Libya’s adjudicative and execution immunities.154 Ibid., para. 62. Accordingly, it appears as if all three judges in General Dynamics (EWCA) considered that the mere fact of submitting the dispute to arbitration sufficed to waive the state’s adjudicative immunity by virtue of section 9 of the SIA. All three judges also appear to have agreed that the wording of the specific arbitration agreement confirmed the waiver of adjudicative immunity (albeit with some disagreement about which words in particular achieved this effect). One judge – Lewison LJ – thought that the words of a framework Convention like the New York Convention could also constitute a waiver of adjudicative immunity. All three judges approached the issue of waiver of execution immunity through the lens of the specific wording of clause 32 of the parties’ contract, although Phillips LJ was alone in thinking that recourse to the parties’ choice of arbitral rules was necessary to find a waiver. Contrast General Dynamics (EWCA) with the recent decision of the Full Court of the Federal Court of Australia (‘FCAFC’) in Republic of India v. CCDM Holdings, LLC (‘CCDM Holdings (FCAFC)’),155 [2025] FCAFC 2. involving the same material facts as in CC Devas (considered above).156 [2022] QCCS 4785, supra n. 105. CCDM Holdings (FCAFC) involved an application of the HCA’s decision in Infrastructure Services (HCA) to the New York Convention context.
