The state’s specific conduct in consenting to a particular arbitration, coupled with the language of the particular arbitration agreement in question, as well as the choice of any particular arbitral rules (if any), should shape the court’s inquiry into the question of waiver.132 It has been suggested that, in the ICSID context, the existence of an ICSID award alone should suffice to provide conclusive evidence of the existence of a valid arbitration agreement for the purposes of establishing a waiver of immunity via the s. 9 SIA exception. See Foxton et al., supra n. 37, para. 20.85. Compare, Border Timbers, para. 88; Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 105. The Court of Appeal in Svenska Petroleum Exploration AB v. Lithuania (No. 2) (‘Svenska’) got it right by focusing its analysis on the section 9 SIA exception, which it held was satisfied on the facts of the case (which concerned the attempted enforcement of an International Chamber of Commerce (‘ICC’) award).133 [2006] EWCA Civ 1529; [2007] QB 886. The underlying joint venture agreement had provided for disputes to be settled either by the Lithuanian courts or by arbitration under the auspices of the ICC.134 Ibid., para. 6. Another clause headed ‘Governing law and sovereign immunity’ provided that ‘Government and EPG hereby irrevocably waives [sic] all rights to sovereign immunity’. The Court in Svenska considered in obiter that the latter clause was ‘too imprecise’ to amount to a submission to the jurisdiction of the English courts within the meaning of section 2(1) of the SIA, although the Court noted that in any case the clause had to be read in the context of the Lithuanian government’s agreement to submit to ICC arbitration.135 Ibid., para. 128. See also Infrastructure Services (EWCA), para. 97. Further, the Court held that the requirements of section 2(1) of the SIA could not have been satisfied, since ‘[a] choice of jurisdiction clause if limited to the courts of a named State [viz., Lithuania], whilst it may be construed as a waiver of immunity before the courts of the named State, cannot readily be construed as a general waiver of immunity in relation to courts of other jurisdictions’.136 Fox & Webb, supra n. 127, at 389. Finally, it is worth noting the very recent decision of the English High Court in CC Devas (Mauritius) Ltd v. India (‘CC Devas (EWHC)’), which (correctly, in this author’s opinion) held that India’s ratification of the New York Convention could not on its own amount to a submission to the jurisdiction by prior written agreement within the meaning of section 2(2) of the SIA.137 [2025] EWHC 964 (Comm). In this respect, cf. the outcome in Infrastructure Services (EWCA). This was because the ratification of Article III of the New York Convention alone did not meet the threshold of a waiver in English law, which the Court gleaned inter alia from the decisions in Pinochet (No 3), Infrastructure Services (HCA) and Infrastructure Services (EWCA) – such a ratification was not ‘express, and expressed in a clear and recognisable manner, as by an unequivocal agreement’.138 CC Devas (EWHC), para. 82. The reason for this was said to be that, in many commercial and financial transactions involving states and state-owned enterprises, waiver of state immunity is a matter of contract.139 Ibid., para. 83. Against that backdrop, it ‘may be thought that the market is capable of restricting state immunity where it may arise’; given the ‘delicate and currently controversial’ balance between investors and states in investment disputes, there is ‘nothing … to impel a conclusion that ratification of the NYC is in and of itself a waiver of immunity, where express waivers are a commercial commonplace’.140 Ibid., para. 85. While the outcome of this decision is to be welcomed, it is questionable whether it is methodologically apposite to have regard to the so-called ‘market practice’ in determining whether the purported waiver was express, clear and unmistakable, especially when other courts like the one in Infrastructure Services (HCA) have approached the question from a purely linguistic perspective. 3.4 Interaction with the approach taken in finding waivers of execution immunity Not having sole regard to the language of framework instruments like the ICSID Convention or New York Convention for the purposes of finding a waiver of a state’s jurisdictional immunity also has the merit of aligning the approach with that taken in determining whether a state has waived its immunities from execution, since the latter typically involves a more microscopic analysis of a wider range of sources to establish a waiver. The recent decision of the English Court of Appeal in General Dynamics UK v. Libya 141 [2025] EWCA Civ 134; [2025] 2 WLUK 328. (‘General Dynamics (EWCA)’) – not to be confused with the earlier decision of the UK Supreme Court in the same case, General Dynamics (UKSC), as discussed above142 [2021] UKSC 22, supra n. 30. – is an excellent illustration of this point. General Dynamics was successful in an ICC arbitration against Libya. Clause 32 of the underlying contract, providing for ICC arbitration, provided that the decision of the arbitral panel ‘shall be final, binding and wholly enforceable’.143 General Dynamics (EWCA), para. 1. It was common ground that this sufficed to waive Libya’s immunity from the court’s jurisdiction within the meaning of section 9 of the SIA.144 Ibid., para. 2. The key question was whether Libya had also consented thereby, within the meaning of section 13(3) of the SIA, to the execution against its property of any ICC arbitration award made against it.145 Ibid. The Court of Appeal answered that question in the affirmative. What is notable in the Court of Appeal’s decision is the slight divergence in the views of the three judges who heard the appeal (although all three ultimately reached the same conclusion):(1) Phillips LJ thought that the phrase ‘final, binding and wholly enforceable’ on its own was insufficient to constitute a waiver of Libya’s immunity from execution.146 Ibid., para. 43. The lower court had erred in concluding that the words ‘final and binding’ alone achieved a waiver of adjudicative immunity, such that the phrase ‘wholly enforceable’ must have been intended to go further and refer to a waiver of execution immunity.147 Ibid., para. 42. This was because the phrase ‘final and binding’ merely signalled that an award would conclusively determine the issues between the parties (rather than be interim or provisional). Further, while ‘[m]any states’ regarded submission to arbitration as submission to the adjudicative jurisdiction in respect of that arbitration, that did not ‘render otiose a contractual submission to the adjudicative jurisdiction’ (through the remaining words ‘wholly enforceable’), especially in relation to states without the equivalent of section 9 of the SIA.148 Ibid. In light of that finding, Phillips LJ instead relied on the fact that clause 32 of the contract had provided for the arbitration to be conducted under the ICC Rules of Arbitration 1998. Article 28(6) thereof provides that parties who submit a dispute to arbitration under those Rules ‘undertake to carry out any award without delay and shall be deemed to have waived their right to any form of recourse insofar as such waiver can be validly made’. After citing two decisions of the French and US courts interpreting that provision (or its predecessor) as constituting a waiver of execution immunity,149 Creighton Ltd v. Qatar, Cour de Cassation 6 Jul. 2000; Walker International Holdings Ltd v. The Republic of Congo, 395 F.3d 229 (5th cir. 2004). Phillips LJ concluded that the undertaking in Article 28(6) of the ICC Rules to carry out the award without delay provided the ‘context’ against which Libya’s agreement in clause 32 of the contract that awards should be ‘wholly enforceable’ had to be interpreted.150 General Dynamics (EWCA), paras 43–45. Libya had therefore waived its immunity from execution. (2) Zacaroli LJ, on the other hand, thought that the phrase ‘wholly enforceable’ alone sufficed to establish a waiver of execution immunity. In his view, the wording of the ICC Rules only ‘reinforce[d] that conclusion’.151 Ibid., para. 48. This implies that Zacaroli LJ thought that the phrase ‘final and binding’ alone did achieve a waiver of adjudicative immunity, contra Phillips LJ. His Lordship otherwise agreed with Phillips LJ’s reasoning. (3) As for Lewison LJ, he considered that the mere fact of Libya’s submission of the dispute to arbitration sufficed to waive its adjudicative immunity in the UK courts by virtue of section 9 of the SIA. The words in clause 32 of the parties’ contract were only there ‘[f]or good measure’; that is, they merely had a confirmatory effect in relation to the question of waiver of adjudicative immunity.152 Ibid., para. 59. Somewhat confusingly, Lewison LJ also stated that since the award was a New York Convention award, recognition of the award was already achieved by virtue of Article III of the New York Convention, without the need for the phrase ‘final, binding and wholly enforceable’ in clause 32.153 Ibid., para. 64. As explained in this article, the latter proposition cannot be right, and His Lordship’s former position is to be preferred. He concluded that the ‘straightforward’ reading of the operative part of clause 32 amounted to a waiver both of Libya’s adjudicative and execution immunities.154 Ibid., para. 62. Accordingly, it appears as if all three judges in General Dynamics (EWCA) considered that the mere fact of submitting the dispute to arbitration sufficed to waive the state’s adjudicative immunity by virtue of section 9 of the SIA. All three judges also appear to have agreed that the wording of the specific arbitration agreement confirmed the waiver of adjudicative immunity (albeit with some disagreement about which words in particular achieved this effect). One judge – Lewison LJ – thought that the words of a framework Convention like the New York Convention could also constitute a waiver of adjudicative immunity. All three judges approached the issue of waiver of execution immunity through the lens of the specific wording of clause 32 of the parties’ contract, although Phillips LJ was alone in thinking that recourse to the parties’ choice of arbitral rules was necessary to find a waiver. Contrast General Dynamics (EWCA) with the recent decision of the Full Court of the Federal Court of Australia (‘FCAFC’) in Republic of India v. CCDM Holdings, LLC (‘CCDM Holdings (FCAFC)’),155 [2025] FCAFC 2. involving the same material facts as in CC Devas (considered above).156 [2022] QCCS 4785, supra n. 105. CCDM Holdings (FCAFC) involved an application of the HCA’s decision in Infrastructure Services (HCA) to the New York Convention context. At first instance in the Federal Court of Australia (‘FCA’), Jackman J had applied the HCA’s decision in Infrastructure Services (HCA) in finding that India’s ratification of the New York Convention amounted to a waiver of its immunity and a submission to the jurisdiction of the Australian courts within the meaning of section 10(2) of Australia’s Foreign States Immunities Act 1985.157 CCDM Holdings, LLC v. Republic of India (No. 3) [2023] FCA 1266. The FCAFC overturned that decision. It held that India had not done so, because any possible waiver by India under the New York Convention was limited by its commercial reservation pursuant to Article 1(3) thereof.158 CCDM Holdings (FCAFC), para. 3. Upon ratifying the New York Convention, India had made a reservation – as it was permitted to do under Article 1(3) – that it would ‘apply the Convention only to differences arising out of legal relationships, whether contractual or not, which are considered as commercial under the Law of India’.159 Ibid., para. 24 (emphasis added). Such a reservation operated reciprocally, such that Australia too had ‘no obligation to India to enforce awards that do not arise from differences arising from legal relationships which, in India, would not be considered as commercial, and India has no right to insist on Australia enforcing such awards’.160 Ibid., para. 68 (emphasis added). The FCAFC distinguished Infrastructure Services (HCA) on the basis that Spain’s obligation to recognize and enforce ICSID awards under Article 54(1) of the ICSID Convention (as at issue in Infrastructure Services (HCA)) was not qualified in the way India’s obligation under the New York Convention was.161 Ibid., para. 74. Since neither party had adduced evidence about whether the award at hand was within the scope of India’s reservation, the Court applied the presumption that Indian law was the same as Australian law in concluding that the differences between the parties that were the subject of the arbitration were not ‘commercial’ in nature.162 Ibid., paras 76–81. Accordingly, the Court held that India was immune from the jurisdiction of the court (and therefore immune from execution of the award). Before comparing this decision with the English Court of Appeal’s decision in General Dynamics (EWCA), three points are worth mentioning. First, it is questionable if the effect of reciprocity is what the FCAFC held it to be. The FCAFC relied on the ILC’s Guide to Practice on Reservations to Treaties (the ‘Guide’ or ‘ILC Guide’).163 Text of the Draft Guidelines Constituting the Guide to Practice on Reservations to Treaties, With Commentaries, as Provisionally Adopted by the International Law Commission, YILC (2011) Vol. II. It cited the following passages from that Guide:(26) It follows that the author of the reservation is not only released from compliance with the treaty obligations which are the subject of the reservation but also loses the right to require the State … with regard to which the reservation is established to fulfil the treaty obligations that are the subject of the reservation. In addition, the State … with regard to which the reservation is established is released from compliance with the obligation which is the subject of the reservation with respect to the reserving State or organization. (27) This principle of reciprocal application is based on common sense. … A reservation operates reciprocally between the reserving State and any other party to the treaty, so that both are exempted from the reserved provisions in their mutual relations.164 CCDM Holdings (FCAFC), para. 66, citing ILC Guide, Guideline 4.2.4, Commentary paras 26–27. On its face, this language does seem to support the FCAFC’s contention that the effect of reciprocity is such that the Australian courts are not obliged to enforce New York Convention awards that do not arise from differences arising from legal relationships not considered as commercial in India. But it is important to have regard to what else the ILC says in its Guide. The passages cited above come at the end of a protracted discussion on how a reservation may either exclude or modify the legal effect of the relevant provision for its author in its relations with other treaty parties.165 ILC Guide, supra n. 164, Guideline 4.2.4(1). The ILC correctly recognizes the difficulty of ascertaining whether a reservation is a ‘modifying’ or ‘excluding’ reservation and that sometimes the same reservation may display both effects.166 Ibid., Guideline 4.2.4, para. 3. The FCAFC recognized too that it was debatable whether India’s reservation under Article 1(3) of the New York Convention excludes or modifies the legal effect of certain provisions of the treaty.167 CCDM Holdings (FCAFC), para. 65. In relation to a reservation’s exclusionary effects, the ILC observed that whereas the reservation’s author is released from the obligation stemming from the underlying treaty provision in question, the other states with regard to which the reservation is established ‘have waived their right to demand performance of the obligation stemming from the treaty provision in question in the context of their treaty relationship with the author of the reservation’.168 ILC Guide, supra n. 164, Guideline 4.2.4, Commentary para. 14. Meanwhile, as the ILC recognizes, the ‘concrete effect of a modifying reservation … is … more difficult to grasp’.169 Ibid., Guideline 4.2.4, para. 17. And for good reason – the relevant guideline provides:To the extent that an established reservation modifies the legal effect of certain provisions of a treaty, the author of that reservation has rights and obligations under those provisions, as modified by the reservation, in its relations with the other parties with regard to which the reservation is established. Those other parties shall have rights and obligations under those provisions, as modified by the reservation, in their relations with the author of the reservation.170 Ibid., Guideline 4.2.4(3). The repetition of the phrase ‘as modified by the reservation’ was meant to highlight that reciprocity, in relation to a reservation’s modifying effects, can sometimes have asymmetric consequences. Some reservations ‘modify only the rights and obligations of the author of the reservation vis-à-vis the other parties without affecting the content of the rights and obligations of the latter’171 Ibid., Guideline 4.2.4, paras 20–21. One of the examples cited is the Finnish reservation to Art. 18 of the Convention on Road Signs and Signals of 1968, whereby Finland purported to use certain symbols in place of specific signs to indicate the beginning and end of a built-up area. Such a reservation was intended only to replace Finland’s original obligation with a modified one, without altering the content of the rights and obligations of the other parties.; meanwhile, other reservations also affect the rights and obligations of those other parties.172 Ibid., Guideline 4.2.4, paras 22–23. One of the examples cited is the Israeli reservation to the first Geneva Convention, whereby Israel modified its obligation under the relevant treaty provision to allow it to use the Red Shield of David as the emblem and distinctive sign of its own medical personnel, while imposing an obligation on other parties, not originally provided for, to respect its new emblem. Thus, insofar as Article 1(3) New York Convention has an exclusionary effect, it is arguable that the extent of the effect of reciprocity is merely to disable Australia (or indeed any other state party to the New York Convention) from demanding that the Indian courts apply the Convention to differences that are not considered as commercial under Indian law. Meanwhile, insofar as Article 1(3) modifies the parties’ treaty relations, it is arguable that it is precisely the sort of reservation that is only meant to modify India’s rights and obligations vis-à-vis the other contracting states, without affecting the content of the rights and obligations of the latter (save perhaps for respecting that reservation by waiving their rights to demand that the Indian courts apply the Convention to differences not considered commercial under Indian law, as where a foreign state appears before an Indian court purporting to seek the recognition and enforcement of a New York Convention award arising from a ‘non-commercial’ dispute). More fundamentally, as a matter of logic, it would seem that by applying the reservation reciprocally in the manner that the FCAFC did, it placed Australia in breach of its own unqualified obligations under the New York Convention. It is, however, outside the scope of this article to elaborate on this point.173 Compare, CC Devas (EWHC), para. 91(2) (where the claimants in that case raised an argument on this point that was, unfortunately, not dealt with by the court). Second, it is questionable why the FCAFC emphasized that India lacked the right – on its analysis – to insist on Australia enforcing the Convention in respect of differences arising from non-commercial relationships (as a matter of Indian law).174 See CCDM Holdings (FCAFC), paras 68–69. Even if the Court’s analysis were right, it was CCDM Holdings, the award creditor, that was so ‘insisting’, not India as the award debtor. Third, the FCAFC’s decision appears out of step with the decision in Infrastructure Services (HCA), and the FCAFC’s argument for distinguishing that decision (that Spain had not made a reservation to Article 54(1) of the ICSID Convention) is unpersuasive. The HCA’s decision is predicated on the logic that Article 54 must involve a waiver of state immunity because otherwise the enforcing court would be unable to meet its obligation under Article 54 to recognize and enforce the ICSID award. The focus is on the court from which recognition and enforcement are sought. If that is correct, then it should not matter that India had made a commercial reservation when it signed up to the New York Convention. The focus should have been on Australia’s obligations under the New York Convention. It is, finally, instructive to return to contrasting the FCAFC’s judgment in CCDM Holdings (FCAFC) with the English Court of Appeal’s judgment in General Dynamics (EWCA). The underlying arbitration in CCDM Holdings (FCAFC) had been conducted under the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules 1976. As has been observed,175 Vlada Lemaic, Court of Appeal Affirms Broad Approach to Construing Waivers of Sovereign Immunity from Execution (25 Feb. 2025), https://www.cliffordchance.com/insights/resources/blogs/arbitration-insights/2025/02/court-of-appeal-affirms-broad-approach.html (accessed 3 Apr. 2025). Article 32(2) of the 1976 Rules176 Article 32(2) of the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules 1976 states: ‘The award shall be made in writing and shall be final and binding on the parties. The parties undertake to carry out the award without delay’. is phrased very similarly to Article 28(6) of the ICC Rules 1998, which Phillips LJ had relied on in General Dynamics (EWCA) in determining that Libya had waived its immunity from execution (this was also relied on, to a more limited extent, by Zacaroli LJ). As others have observed, the fact that two courts faced with very similarly worded provisions of arbitration rules arrived at very different outcomes creates an unsatisfactory ‘fragmented patchwork of national laws and court decisions on sovereign immunity and its scope’.177 Lemaic, supra n. 175. In the future, an enforcing court deciding the question of waiver would be confronted with two competing approaches under the current jurisprudence. On the one hand, it may look at whether the award debtor state had made a reservation when ratifying the New York Convention178 The ICSID Convention does not permit reservations but allows Contracting States to notify the Centre of the class(es) of disputes which it would or would not consider submitting to the jurisdiction of ICSID. or, more generally, at the relevant framework Convention in question179 That is, either the ICSID Convention or New York Convention, applying the approach taken in Infrastructure Services (HCA) and Infrastructure Services (EWCA).; and on the other hand, it may look at more specific potential indicators of waiver, such as the terms of the parties’ specific arbitration agreement or the set of arbitral rules chosen by the parties to govern the arbitration. In line with the overarching argument made in this article, it is submitted that the specific should triumph the general. Courts should more generally look at states’ specific indicia of consent to arbitration rather than merely their agreement to framework treaties.

4 CONCLUSION In concluding this article, it is difficult to improve on the remarks made by Fraser J in the English High Court decision in Infrastructure Services (EWHC): I have produced this judgment in order to explain the analysis that I consider both underpins the domestic enforcement regime for ICSID awards under the 1966 Act, and to address Spain’s carefully advanced and argued multiple grounds of opposition to the Order. This should not be taken as encouragement by any state in a similar position to Spain that there is a lengthy and costly legal argument, based on wide-ranging arguments under international law, to be had on all or any attempts to obtain recognition of an ICSID award by an investor under the 1966 Act. There is not. The entire purpose of the ICSID Convention and the 1966 Act would be undermined if lengthy and complex arguments of the type advanced by Spain in this case were routinely advanced. … In cases such as this one in the future, if the ICSID Committee have considered and dismissed objections under the Convention procedure and the award is a valid and authentic one, I wish to make it clear that there are no grounds for repetition or rehearing of those in the Commercial Court. Unless a case is truly exceptional, it is difficult to foresee how a hearing of the length required in this case, and a judgment of this length, would occur again. To do so would be contrary to the ICSID Convention and the 1966 Act, and is exactly what international arbitration is designed to avoid. 180 Infrastructure Services (EWHC), paras 162–163 (emphasis added). This author agrees. The primary argument made in this article, founded upon Dias J’s masterful analysis in Border Timbers, seeks to avoid the multifarious possible ways in which simple attempts at obtaining the registration of an ICSID award by an investor under the 1966 Act could be derailed. The registration of ICSID awards in the UK should be ‘automatic’, with no scope for states to raise objections on the basis of state immunity (for the simple reason that under section 1 of the SIA, state immunity does not arise). Meanwhile, the secondary argument advanced – that waivers of immunity should be found from state conduct and instruments specific to the arbitration in question and not from general framework Conventions, and that section 9 of the SIA should normally be the primary means of finding a waiver of jurisdictional immunity insofar as ICSID or New York Convention enforcement proceedings are concerned – seeks to bring more conceptual coherence to this area of law. Even though, in theory, deciding that the text of a framework Convention alone suffices to establish an exception to immunity via section 2 of the SIA would save considerable time and may help avoid protracted proceedings,181 See CC Devas (EWHC), para. 10. it is important not to allow these supposed practical benefits to overshadow the conceptual incoherence that would result from doing so and the need for robust judicial reasoning.

title: Template Justice on Trial: A Critical Analysis of DJP v. DJO’s New Standards for Arbitrators

sections.title: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: 1 INTRODUCTION In international commercial arbitration, a delicate balance exists between safeguarding procedural integrity and preventing tactical disruptions. William W. Park aptly captured this tension in his article ‘Arbitrator Bias’, observing that an ‘evil gremlin’ seeking to undermine arbitration might pursue one of two routes: either tolerate the appointment of biased arbitrators unable to judge independently, or establish ‘unrealistic ethical standards that render the arbitrator’s position precarious and susceptible to destabilisation by litigants engaged in dilatory tactics or seeking to annul unfavourable awards’. 1 William W. Park, Arbitrator Bias, 12 Transn’l Dispute Mgmt. J. (2015). This tension has taken on a new dimensions with the Singapore Court of Appeal’s (‘SGCA’) landmark decision in DJP v. DJO. 2 [2025] SGCA(I), at 2. In this case, the SGCA set aside an arbitral award after finding that ‘out of the 451 paragraphs in the Award … at least 212 paragraphs were copied and pasted from the Parallel Awards’, with former Chief Justice of India Dipak Misra serving as the Presiding Arbitrator across all three related proceedings.3 Ibid., para. 2. It concluded that Justice Misra’s use of content from the Parallel awards amounted to pre-judging a case, and that his accumulated knowledge and willingness to use that knowledge in preparing the Award constituted impermissible pre-judging. Through this ruling, the SGCA established new standards for arbitrator conduct that significantly impact repeat appointments, award drafting practices, and information asymmetries among co-arbitrators. Now, to properly assess the decision’s implications, we must first distinguish between three distinct scenarios of repeat appointments: first, appointment by the same party in a completely unrelated cases; second, appointment by the same party in cases involving similar legal issues but different facts; and third, appointment by the same party in cases with substantially overlapping facts, subject-matter, and evidence.4 Olexander Droug, The Issue of Appointment of the Same Arbitrator in Multiple Arbitrations (Sayenko Kharenko 15 Feb. 2023), sk.ua/the-issue-of-appointment-of-the-same-arbitrator-in-multiple-arbitrations/ (accessed 12 Apr. 2025). This article focuses specifically on the third scenario, which was central to DJP v. DJO and presents the most complex questions about arbitral appointments and conduct. In examining this third category of repeat appointments through the lens of DJP v. DJO, the SGCA’s decision raises two critical questions which form the central thesis of this article: the threshold question of permissible ‘copying’ in arbitral awards and the potentially paradoxical concept of information equality among tribunal members. While these principles aim to protect procedural fairness, they introduce significant challenges to established arbitration practices and may create tensions with the system’s foundational attributes of expertise, efficiency, and finality. By setting standards that may prove difficult to implement in practice, SGCA’s approach raises important questions about the proper balance between procedural safeguards and the practical realities of international arbitration. To that end, this article proceeds by first examining the jurisprudential evolution of ‘issue conflict’ in international arbitration, tracing key developments from Halliburton through landmark decisions that established the legal context for understanding DJP v. DJO. Then, it provides a detailed examination of DJP v. DJO itself, focusing on the Court’s findings regarding prejudgment, apparent bias, and the ‘equality of information’ principle. Finally, this article critically evaluates these standards, examining the fallacy of information equality and the extent of permissible ‘copying’ in awards, arguing that the Court’s approach may inadvertently undermine arbitration’s foundational values while failing to provide clear guidance for arbitrators navigating related proceedings.

sections.title: THE BOUNDARIES OF PREJUDGMENT: A JURISPRUDENTIAL REVIEW OF ISSUE CONFLICT
sections.text: 2 THE BOUNDARIES OF PREJUDGMENT: A JURISPRUDENTIAL REVIEW OF ISSUE CONFLICT As evident from above, DJP v. DJO brings to the forefront the complex challenge of ‘issue conflict’ in international arbitration, a concept that refers to circumstances where an arbitrator’s impartiality may be compromised due to their relationship with the subject matter of the dispute rather than with the parties themselves.5 Gavan Griffith, & Daniel Kalderimis, ‘Pure’ Issue Conflicts in Investment Treaty Arbitration, in Practising Virtue: Inside International Arbitration (David D. Caron et al. eds, Oxford 2015; online ed., Oxford Academic 21 Jan. 2016), https://doi.org/10.1093/acprof:oso/9780198739807.003.0038 (accessed 14 Apr. 2025); R Zamour, Issue Conflicts and the Reasonable Expectation of an Open Mind: The Challenge Decision in Devas v. India and Its Impact, in Challenges and Recusals of Judges and Arbitrators in International Courts and Tribunals 227–246 (C Giorgetti ed., Brill Nijhoff Leiden 2015). This can arise when an arbitrator has previously considered similar legal or factual issues, potentially forming views that may affect their ability to approach a new case with an open mind.6 Anthony Sinclair & Matthew Gearing, Partiality and Issue Conflicts, 5(4) Transnat’l Dispute Mgmt. (Jul. 2008). Malcolm Langford, Daniel Behn & Runar Hilleren Lie, The Revolving Door in International Investment Arbitration, 20(2) J. Int’l Econ. L. 301–332 (Jun. 2017), doi: 10.1093/jiel/jgx018p (accessed 9 Apr. 2025); Luke Eric Peterson, Arbitrator Decries ‘Revolving Door’ Roles of Lawyers in Investment Treaty Arbitration (Investment Arbitration Reporter 25 Feb. 2010). Before analysing how the SGCA tackled this thorny issue, it is essential to understand how courts and tribunals alike have previously navigated this terrain, as these precedents form the backdrop against which the significance of DJP v. DJO can be properly assessed. The foundation for evaluating issue conflict lies in the objective test for apparent bias, articulated most clearly in Halliburton Company v. Chubb Bermuda Insurance Ltd,7 Halliburton Company v. Chubb Bermuda Insurance Ltd (formerly known as Ace Bermuda Insurance Ltd) UKSC 48 (2020). where the UK Supreme Court confirmed that the assessment should consider whether ‘a fair-minded and informed observer, having considered the facts, would conclude that there was a real possibility that the tribunal was biased’. 8 Ibid., para. 52. This objective standard focuses not on actual bias but on reasonable perceptions that might undermine confidence in the arbitral process.9 Samuel Yee Ching Leung & Alex Chun Hei Chan, The Duties of Impartiality, Disclosure, and Confidentiality: Lessons from a London-Seated Arbitration, 37(3) Arb. Int’l 667–684 (Sep. 2021), doi: 10.1093/arbint/aiab013 (accessed 14 Apr. 2025). Also see Charles Kimmins, Luke Pearce, Nigel Rawding & Olivia Valner, The Test for Apparent Bias and Arbitrators’ Duties of Disclosure Following Halliburton v. Chubb: Welcome Clarification, but Questions Remain, 38(3) J. Int’l Arbitration 359–376 (2021), doi: 10.54648/JOIA2021018. https://kluwerlawonline.com/journalarticle/Journal+of+International+Arbitration/38.3/JOIA2021018 (accessed 10 Apr. 2025) Early decisions generally set a high threshold for successful challenges.10 Ina C. Popova & Jessica L. Polebaum, Emerging Expectations for Arbitrators: ‘Issue Conflict’ in Investor-State Arbitration and Beyond, 41 Fordham Int’l L.J. 937 (2018). In Suez v. Argentina,11 Suez, Sociedad General de Aguas de Barcelona SA & others v. Argentine Republic, ICSID Case No ARB/03/17, Decision on the Proposal for the Disqualification of a Member of the Arbitral Tribunal (22 Oct. 2007). the tribunal rejected the notion that prior exposure to similar legal questions automatically indicated bias, emphasizing that ‘the fact that a judge or an arbitrator has made a determination of law or a finding of fact in one case does not mean that they cannot decide the law and facts impartially in another case’.12 Ibid., para. 36. This principle was reinforced in Tidewater v. Venezuela,13 Tidewater Investment SRL and Tidewater Caribe, C.A. v. Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela, ICSID Case No. ARB/10/5, Decision on Claimants’ Proposal to Disqualify Professor Brigitte Stern, Arbitrator (23 Dec. 2010). where the tribunal held that multiple appointments alone could not ‘without more’ indicate a lack of independence. According to this decision, prejudgment concerns would arise only where an arbitrator had ‘in effect prejudged the liability of one of the parties in the context of the specific factual matrix’.14 Ibid., para. 67. A key development in this jurisprudence, however, emerged with Caratube v. Kazakhstan,15 Caratube International Oil Company LLP and Devincci Salah Hourani v. Republic of Kazakhstan (II), ICSID Case No. ARB/13/13, Decision on the Proposal for Disqualification of Bruno Boesch (20 Mar. 2014). Also see EnCana Corporation v. Republic of Ecuador, LCIA Case No. UN3481, Partial Award on Jurisdiction, dated 27 Feb. 2004, para. 43; Participationes Inversiones Porturias Sàrl v. Gabonese Republic, ICSID Case No. ARB/08/17, Decision on proposal to disqualify an arbitration, dated 12 Nov. 2009. which introduced a crucial distinction between legal interpretations and factual determinations.16 See Jan Paulsson, The Idea of Arbitration (Oxford: Oxford University Press 2013). It seems to assimilate issue conflict and questions of law. Here, an arbitrator was disqualified based on his exposure to overlapping facts in a related case. The tribunal differentiated between legal interpretations involving ‘generally available knowledge’ and factual determinations based on ‘documents and witnesses specific to each dispute’, suggesting that factual overlap posed greater concerns for impartiality than legal opinions alone.17 See International Council for Commercial Arbitration, Report of the ASIL-ICCA Joint Task Force on Issue Conflicts in Investor-state Arbitration, the ICCA Reports No. 3, 17 Mar. 2016. This notes that differences between special knowledge of facts relevant to the merits and those relevant to the interpretation of a legal provision have been key in dismissing a challenge to an arbitrator. In this case, the unchallenged arbitrators concluded that Boesch could not reasonably maintain a ‘Chinese wall’ in his own mind regarding information acquired in the parallel proceeding.18 Ibid., para. 75. Therefore, this distinction will prove particularly relevant to our analysis of DJP v. DJO, where extensive copying from previous awards reflected both legal interpretations and case-specific factual determinations. CC/Devas v. India pushed these boundaries further, where Judge Peter Tomka upheld a challenge against Professor Francisco Orrego Vicuña based on his prior decisions interpreting an ‘essential security interests’ clause and his subsequent academic defines of those interpretations despite their annulment.19 CC/Devas (Mauritius) Ltd., Devas Employees Mauritius Private Limited, and Telcom Devas Mauritius Limited v. Republic of India, UNCITRAL, Decision on the Respondent’s Challenge to the Hon. Mark Lalonde as Presiding Arbitrator and Prof. Francisco Orrego Vicuña as Co-Arbitrator (30 Sep. 2013). This decision suggested that an arbitrator’s repeated engagement with the same legal issues, coupled with a steadfast defence of an overturned position, could create an appearance of prejudgment even without factual overlap.20 However, as SW Schill has noted, sanctioning arbitrators and aspiring arbitrators for engaging in academic discourse could have the unwanted effect of reducing substantially their contributions to scholarship: Stephan W. Schill, Editorial: The New Journal of World Investment and Trade; Arbitrator Independence and Academic Freedom; In This Issue 15 J. World Inv. & Trade 1, 7–8 (2014). Judge Tomka concluded that ‘a reasonable observer would not believe that [the arbitrator] could approach the question with an open mind’.21 Ibid., para. 64. The tension between these lines of jurisprudence was also particularly evident in Blue Bank v. Venezuela,22 Blue Bank International & Trust (Barbados) Ltd. v. Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela, ICSID Case No. ARB/12/20, Decision on the Parties’ Proposals to Disqualify a Majority of the Tribunal, 12 Nov. 2013. where an arbitrator was disqualified due to his concurrent roles as arbitrator and advocate involving similar issues and the same respondent. The deciding authority concluded that ‘a third party would find an evident or obvious appearance of lack of impartiality’ given the similarity of issues likely to be discussed in both cases.23 Ibid., para. 69. After reviewing this line of jurisprudence on issue conflict, we gained insight into how tribunals and courts have traditionally evaluated arbitrator challenges based on prior exposure to similar issues. What makes DJP v. DJO particularly significant against this backdrop is its direct confrontation with one of the most challenging manifestations of issue conflict: an arbitrator’s concurrent involvement in multiple related proceedings with substantively overlapping subject matter and evidence. By articulating an ‘equality of information’ principle and setting a vague threshold for permissible ‘copying’ in arbitral awards, the SGCA has potentially redefined the boundaries of acceptable arbitrator conduct. However, rather than resolving the tension identified by W.W. Park between procedural integrity and tactical disruption, this may have inadvertently tilted toward establishing ‘unrealistic ethical standards that render the arbitrator’s position precarious and susceptible to destabilisation by litigants engaged in dilatory tactics or seeking to annul unfavourable awards’.24 Park, supra n. 1. The full implications of this approach and whether it properly balances the competing interests at stake will be examined in the following sections.

sections.title: CASE SUMMARY: ANATOMY OF DJP v. DJO
sections.text: 3 CASE SUMMARY: ANATOMY OF DJP v. DJO 3.1 Background and parties The case involved an appeal against a decision of the High Court of Singapore to set aside an arbitral award on the grounds of breach of natural justice. The appellants were three companies – DJP, DJQ, and DJR – which had formed a consortium to tender for a contract relating to the western Dedicated Freight Corridors in India. The respondent was DJO, a special purpose vehicle established to manage a network of Dedicated Freight Corridors in India. The dispute arose from a contract known as the ‘CPT-13 Contract’ awarded to the appellants in August 2015. 3.2 The underlying dispute On 19 January 2017, approximately one and a half years after the contract commenced, the Indian Ministry of Labour and Employment issued a notification that immediately increased the daily rates of minimum wages payable to workmen in India. The CPT-13 Contract contained mechanisms allowing for adjustments in the event of changes in legislation (Clause 13.7) or changes in labour costs (Clause 13.8). However, it was not until 6 March 2020– about three years after the issuance of the Notification – that the appellants lodged a claim for additional payment, arguing that the Notification constituted a ‘change in legislation’ as defined in Clause 13.7. The respondent rejected this claim. 3.3 The arbitration and parallel proceedings The appellants commenced arbitration in December 2021 (the ‘Arbitration’). Under the CPT-13 Contract’s dispute resolution clause, the Arbitration was seated in Singapore and conducted in accordance with the Rules of Arbitration of the ICC. The arbitral tribunal (the ‘Tribunal’) consisted of three eminent retired Indian judges: The Honourable Justice Krishn Kumar Lahoti and The Honourable Justice Gita Mittal as co-arbitrators, with The Honourable Justice Dipak Misra, a former Chief Justice of India, as the presiding arbitrator (the ‘President’). Crucially, the respondent was simultaneously defending two other sets of arbitration proceedings (the ‘Parallel Arbitrations’) involving similar claims. These were: (1) The ‘CP-301 Arbitration’, commenced in May 2021 by DJR (also an appellant in the present case) and an Indian company against the respondent. This resulted in the ‘CP-301 Award’ issued in July 2023. (2) The ‘CP-302 Arbitration’, commenced in October 2021 by DJR and another Indian company against the respondent. This resulted in the ‘CP-302 Award’ issued in August 2023. The President of the Tribunal in the present Arbitration also served as the presiding arbitrator in both Parallel Arbitrations, though the co-arbitrators were different. 3.4 The award and its preparation In the Award dated 24 November 2023, the Tribunal ruled substantially in the appellants’ favour, finding that the Notification was a ‘change of legislation’ under Clause 13.7, that the respondent was liable to make additional payments, and that the claim was not precluded on any ground advanced by the respondent. Significantly, the Award was not drafted entirely afresh but used the Parallel Awards as templates. Out of the 451 paragraphs in the Award, at least 212 paragraphs were copied and pasted from the Parallel Awards. The Tribunal had also referenced authorities not cited by the parties, applied an incorrect version of Clause 13.8, and applied the wrong lex arbitri to issues of interest and costs. 3.5 The setting aside application The respondent applied to set aside the Award on three grounds: (1) That the Tribunal had acted in breach of the agreed arbitral procedure by failing to independently assess the issues and give proper reasons (Article 34(2)(a)(iv) of the UNCITRAL Model Law). (2) That the Tribunal had conducted the Arbitration contrary to Singapore public policy (Article 34(2)(b)(ii) of the Model Law) (3) That the Tribunal had acted in breach of the rules of natural justice (section 24(b) of the International Arbitration Act 1994) 3.6 The high court decision The High Court Judge set aside the Award on the third ground, finding that it had been rendered in breach of the rules of natural justice. The Judge identified four specific problems: (1) The Tribunal had failed to restrict itself to submissions made in the Arbitration, instead reproducing verbatim submissions from the CP-301 Arbitration. (2) The Award referenced authorities not cited by the parties. (3) The Tribunal cited an incorrect version of Clause 13.8, applying the CP-301 version instead of the CPT-13 version, thereby using wrong coefficients and data points. (4) The Tribunal applied the wrong lex arbitri (Indian Arbitration Act instead of Singapore IAA) to issues of interest and costs. The Judge concluded that a fair-minded observer would reasonably apprehend that the President had prejudged the Arbitration, allowing his accumulated knowledge from the Parallel Arbitrations to influence his decision. 3.7 The appeal to the court of appeal The appellants appealed, arguing that the Judge had been unduly preoccupied with the similarity between the Award and the Parallel Awards, which they claimed had no material impact on the outcome. They contended that using the Parallel Awards as templates was merely a ‘short cut’ and did not compromise the integrity of the Arbitration. While acknowledging errors in the Award, they maintained these were inconsequential. The respondent countered that the Tribunal’s decision-making process was fundamentally flawed because it had extensively referenced material from outside the Arbitration and resolved common issues in exactly the same manner across all Three Arbitrations. 3.8 The court of appeal’s analysis The Court of Appeal identified three main complaints in the respondent’s case: (1) A fair-minded observer would reasonably apprehend that the Tribunal had approached the Arbitration with a closed mind, influenced by anchoring and confirmation biases from the Parallel Arbitrations. (2) The President had drawn on material from the Parallel Arbitrations that the parties in the present Arbitration had not been given the opportunity to address. (3) The expectation of equality among arbitrators was compromised by the unequal access to information between the President and his co-arbitrators. The Court rejected the appellants’ approach of evaluating each complaint in isolation, stating that the respondent’s case concerned the entire decision-making process, not discrete breaches. 3.9 The court of appeal’s findings The Court of Appeal undertook a comprehensive analysis of the alleged breaches of natural justice and ultimately upheld the High Court’s decision to set aside the Award. The Court found that the Tribunal’s decision-making process was fundamentally flawed on multiple grounds. Regarding prejudgment and apparent bias, the Court determined that ‘a fair-minded and informed observer would, after considering all the relevant facts and circumstances, reasonably apprehend or harbour the suspicion that by reason of what the President had done, he was materially influenced by the earlier decisions that he had been party to in the Parallel Arbitrations’. This conclusion was supported by the extensive copying from the Parallel Awards (at least 212 out of 451 paragraphs), where ‘the Parallel Awards were used as templates, with adjustments made to account for what were thought to be the specificities of the Arbitration’. The Court found that despite material differences between the arbitrations, ‘the Parallel Awards were used as templates in drafting the Award to a very substantial degree’, resulting in ‘the identical resolution of common issues despite different arguments being raised’ and ‘the awkward insertion of new analyses between copied paragraphs’. Particularly, the Court was concerned about the manifestation of cognitive biases, finding that ‘the impression conveyed to the fair-minded observer would reasonably be that the decision-maker might have been wrongly anchored to the earlier decision in a way that compromised his ability and openness to consider matters afresh’. The Court noted that such decision-making would ‘also appear to be plagued by a confirmation bias, referring to the difficulty of persuading a decision-maker that has come to an initial view to then change its view’. Accordingly, the multiple errors in the Award, including citation of the wrong contract clause, application of the wrong procedural law, and reference to computations not in evidence, ‘evidence[d] the problem with the underlying decision-making process’ and suggested to ‘the fair-minded and informed observer, it would appear that the Tribunal might not have adequately applied itself to the facts and submissions actually made in the Arbitration’. The Court also found that the Tribunal had impermissibly relied on ‘material falling outside the scope of the Arbitration, which [the President] had acquired from his involvement in the Parallel Arbitrations and which he drew upon in preparing the Award’. The Court determined this constituted extraneous considerations that ‘had not been raised to the parties’ attention’ and ‘formed such a pervasive part of the Award that it simply could not be overlooked’. This violated the fair hearing rule as ‘it was plain that it was neither contemplated nor agreed to by the parties that the Award could be prepared by such a process’. Additionally, the Court articulated a principle of equality among arbitrators, finding that ‘the expectation of equality as between the Arbitrators was compromised’. The Court explained that: where an arbitrator is not equally placed with his co-arbitrators, it may be said that the other arbitrators have not had an equal, fair and independent opportunity to discharge their adjudicative function and this may be found to have undermined the core expectation of the parties when they choose to resolve their disputes by arbitration. The Court emphasized that ‘each arbitrator should be equally placed to have the same access to the details of the dispute at hand and of the material on the basis of which, they are to resolve it’, and that the co-arbitrators’ lack of access to this influential material ‘further compromised’ the integrity of the arbitral process. While the appellants argued for only setting aside parts of the Award that were directly tainted by the breach, the Court rejected this approach, finding that the breach of natural justice permeated the entire Award. The Court also clarified that remission was not available as a remedy since the Award had already been set aside at first instance.

sections.title: BALANCE UNDONE: DJP v. DJO’S TROUBLING IMPLICATIONS FOR INTERNATIONAL ARBITRATION
sections.text: 4 BALANCE UNDONE: DJP v. DJO’S TROUBLING IMPLICATIONS FOR INTERNATIONAL ARBITRATION The SGCA’s decision reflects a nuanced approach to judicial review of arbitral awards, demonstrating admirable restraint in certain respects while introducing potentially problematic innovations in others. First, the Singapore International Commercial Court’s earlier decision reaffirms the principle of minimal curial intervention that remains foundational to international arbitration. When confronted with DJO’s argument that the Tribunal had failed to properly consider the significant delay between the Notification and the appellants’ claim,25 DJO v. DJP, SGHC(I) 24 (2024), paras 75, 76. the Court appropriately declined to evaluate this contention on the grounds that doing so would require assessing the substantive merits of the case – a task beyond the scope of a seat court’s authority.26 BLC v. BLB, SGCA 40 (2014), para. 53; AJU v. AJT, SGCA 41 (2011), para. 65. Moreover, by choosing to base its analysis on the principles of natural justice rather than scrutinizing the Tribunal’s application of the incorrect lex arbitri or questioning its factual findings,27 DJO v. DJP, supra n. 25, para. 74. the Court again reinforced the limited role that seat courts should play when reviewing arbitral awards, thereby enhancing the finality and predictability of the arbitral process. This judicial restraint extends also to the Court’s treatment of repeat appointments. The decision clarifies that Justice Misra’s appointment as president across all three arbitrations did not, by itself, justify setting aside the award.28 Ibid., para. 71. Neither the ICC nor the Court considered this repeat appointment sufficient grounds for questioning the Award’s validity.29 Ibid., para. 48. This position aligns with prevailing jurisprudence, preserves party autonomy in arbitrator appointment, and acknowledges that experienced arbitrators frequently serve in multiple related disputes. The Court recognized that such arrangements could, in theory, be implemented successfully, though it did note they would be ‘fraught with difficulties’ given the accumulated knowledge an arbitrator would inevitably acquire.30 Ibid., para. 50. However, despite these positive elements, the decision introduces certain problematic principles that could significantly affect how arbitral appointments operate henceforth. 4.1 The fallacy of information equality Perhaps the most problematic principle emerging from this decision is the SGCA’s ‘equality of information’ requirement. This represents a troubling expansion of natural justice requirements in arbitration. While seemingly reasonable at first glance, this principle creates profound practical challenges that strike at the core of how arbitration functions. By holding that ‘where an arbitrator is not equally placed with his co-arbitrators, it may be said that the other arbitrators have not had an equal, fair and independent opportunity to discharge their adjudicative function’, 31 Ibid., paras 56–63. the Court has established a new ground for challenging awards. The decision places arbitrators in an untenable position regarding information management and disclosure. The Court states that it ‘does not think anything turns on’ whether co-arbitrators knew about the copying.32 Ibid., para. 62. This creates an unattainable standard – arbitrators cannot reasonably disclose information they don’t realize is relevant, yet awards can apparently be set aside based on information asymmetries nobody detected. This raises an important question: How can arbitrators possibly guard against unknown knowledge imbalances? This creates a paradoxical situation where an arbitrator could be found to have violated natural justice despite acting in complete good faith and without any awareness of possessing exclusive information. Such a standard effectively imposes an obligation that no arbitrator, no matter how diligent, could reliably satisfy. This principle also directly conflicts with the party-appointment system that is fundamental to arbitration. Parties deliberately select arbitrators with specific expertise precisely because these individuals bring distinct perspectives to a tribunal. An energy specialist understands technical aspects that legal experts might not; a construction lawyer possesses deeper knowledge of standard contracts than generalists. This knowledge diversity has always been considered a strength of arbitration, not a procedural defect requiring remedy. The Court’s approach, however, threatens to transform arbitration from a process where arbitrators exercise independent judgment into one where perfect information symmetry becomes a procedural requirement, regardless of its impact on the actual outcome. As held in QYFM v. Minister for Immigration, Citizenship, Migrant Services and Multicultural Affairs,33 QYFM v. Minister for Immigration, Citizenship, Migrant Services and Multicultural Affairs HCA 15 (2023). each member of a decision-making body has ‘an individual duty to adjudicate on the decision by reference to his or her own true view’. The equality of information principle undermines this foundational concept. This decision also recalls broader concerns about selecting arbitrators with deep subject-matter expertise. Such arbitrators have inevitably developed certain views over their careers that they must consciously set aside to remain impartial. The Court’s approach may inadvertently encourage parties to select arbitrators who lack specialized knowledge to avoid challenges based on information asymmetry – a counterintuitive outcome that diminishes the quality of arbitral decision-making. This creates a troubling paradox: arbitrators are selected for their expertise but then potentially penalized for possessing it. If widely adopted, this approach could lead to the perverse outcome where parties deliberately avoid the most qualified arbitrators to minimize the risk of subsequent challenges, ultimately reducing the quality of arbitral decision-making across the field. For instance, a presiding arbitrator appointed across related cases now faces an impossible choice: either decline all but one appointment (reducing efficiency and risking inconsistent outcomes) or accept multiple appointments while pretending to approach each case entirely afresh, artificially compartmentalizing knowledge from related proceedings. Neither option serves the interests of efficient dispute resolution. Indeed, this decision, particularly, creates tension with dispute resolution boards in construction projects, which are specifically designed to handle multiple disputes between the same parties over a project’s duration.34 Cyril Chern, Chern on Dispute Boards: Practice and Procedure (3d ed. 2015); Gwyn Owen & Brian Totterdill, Dispute Boards: Procedures and Practice (2007); Nael G. Bunni, The FIDIC Forms of Contract (3d ed. 2005) and Cyril Chern, The Law of Construction Disputes (2010). These boards are expected to maintain knowledge continuity across disputes, including overlapping issues. Unlike the SGCA’s approach in DJP v. DJO, this knowledge continuity is considered beneficial rather than problematic in that context. In the dispute board setting, members routinely draw on their accumulated project knowledge to ensure consistent and efficient resolution of multiple related disputes – the very practice the SGCA now suggests might violate natural justice in arbitration. This divergence creates uncertainty about whether similar standards should apply across different dispute resolution mechanisms, potentially disrupting established practices in major infrastructure projects worldwide. This requirement by the SGCA also ignores practical realities of how adjudicators process information. Experienced decision-makers inevitably draw on their accumulated knowledge when facing similar questions and this is simply how the human mind works.35 W. Mark C. Weidemaier, Toward a Theory of Precedent in Arbitration, 51 Wm. & Mary L. Rev. 1895 (2010), https://scholarship.law.wm.edu/wmlr/vol51/iss5/5 (accessed 18 Apr. 2025). The Court effectively demands that arbitrators engage in artificial mental segregation. This requirement for artificial compartmentalization places arbitrators in the impossible position of having to pretend they can approach each related case with a completely fresh mind, a standard that may be literally impossible to meet, regardless of an arbitrator’s good faith efforts. Now, this creates a dangerous precedent where parties dissatisfied with an outcome can weaponize this principle as a shield, claiming information inequality in virtually any repeat appointment scenario by simply arguing that mental segregation of knowledge is inherently implausible for human arbitrators. Therefore, by attempting to impose perfect information symmetry in a process where knowledge asymmetry is not only inevitable but often beneficial, the decision risks making the perfect the enemy of the good in international arbitration. If widely adopted, this approach would transform natural justice from a shield protecting procedural fairness into a sword that can be strategically wielded against unfavourable awards. The ultimate risk is substantial damage to arbitration’s core advantages of expertise, efficiency, and finality. 4.2 The threshold question: extent of permissible ‘copying’ in awards Another crucial problem with the SGCA’s decision is the dangerous uncertainty it creates about acceptable drafting practices in arbitral awards. While the Court acknowledged that ‘not every instance of an arbitrator making an award where portions had been copied from other sources without attribution would render the award liable to be set aside’, it provides minimal guidance on acceptable thresholds for future tribunals. The Court noted that assessment depends on ‘the nature, extent and effect of the copying in question’, but this leaves crucial questions unanswered: Would proper citation and attribution satisfy natural justice requirements? Does the problem concern the quantity of material copied or its nature? The decision creates a precarious landscape for arbitrators to navigate without clear boundaries. The Court also fails to distinguish between different types of copied content, treating reproduction of legal analysis identically to copying factual findings. There is a material difference between consistent legal interpretation of standard provisions across related cases versus copying case-specific factual determinations. By treating all copying as equally problematic, the Court missed an opportunity to provide nuanced guidance appropriate to different situations arbitrators routinely encounter. Most troublingly, this decision may inadvertently encourage arbitrators to artificially differentiate their reasoning across related cases, even when identical conclusions are warranted. This resembles the problem identified in Yap Ah Lai v. Public Prosecutor,36 Yap Ah Lai v. Public Prosecutor, 3 SLR 180 (2014). where a district judge was criticized for identical reasoning in different cases despite reaching the correct outcome both times. The district judge had copied crucial passages of reasoning from his own earlier decision, sentencing two offenders identically despite material differences between their cases. Importantly, when the appellate court examined the substance of the decision after setting it aside for procedural defects, it actually reached a similar conclusion – though with a reduced sentence – showing that the copying itself wasn’t the fundamental problem; rather, it was the appearance that the judge hadn’t independently considered each case’s unique aspects. Similarly, in Lim Chee Huat v. Public Prosecutor,37 Lim Chee Huat v. Public Prosecutor 5 SLR 433 (2019). a district judge had reproduced one party’s written submissions in his grounds of decision (even including a typographical error from those submissions). The appellate court found independent judgment was lacking but, notably, declined to order a retrial. After reviewing the evidence afresh, the court ultimately reached the same outcome as the original decision, demonstrating that the procedural flaw in copying didn’t necessarily invalidate the substantive correctness of the decision. These cases highlight a critical distinction between procedure and substance that DJP v. DJO fails to adequately address. In both Yap Ah Lai and Lim Chee Huat, the courts recognized that copying might indicate procedural flaws while still acknowledging that the underlying decisions could be substantively correct. In those cases, the appellate courts could remedy the situation by considering the evidence afresh while preserving correct outcomes – an option unavailable in arbitration where courts cannot determine underlying disputes. Thus, DJP v. DJO creates a problematic asymmetry: it imports judicial standards of drafting into arbitration without the corresponding remedial mechanisms available in court proceedings. Arbitrators must now meet judicial standards of independence in their award drafting, but parties face the severe consequence of complete award invalidation rather than the more proportionate remedies available in judicial proceedings. This creates a disproportionate risk for parties choosing arbitration, where a drafting approach that might be remedied through appeal in court proceedings instead results in the nuclear option of award annulment, even when the underlying decision might be substantively correct.

sections.title: CONCLUSION: A PATH FORWARD
sections.text: 5 CONCLUSION: A PATH FORWARD International commercial arbitration stands at a crossroads following DJP v. DJO, with the SGCA’s decision potentially redefining the boundaries of acceptable arbitrator conduct. While the Court’s intention to safeguard procedural fairness is laudable, its approach creates significant tensions with arbitration’s foundational values of expertise, efficiency, and finality. The distinction articulated in Caratube v. Kazakhstan, 38 Caratube International Oil Company LLP and Devincci Salah Hourani v. Republic of Kazakhstan (II), supra n. 15. between ‘generally available knowledge’ and case-specific factual determinations offered a nuanced framework that could have informed a more balanced approach to the complex issues of information asymmetry and award drafting. However, by treating all copied content equally and imposing an unrealistic ‘equality of information’ standard, the Court has created an impossible dilemma for arbitrators navigating related proceedings. This standard demands artificial mental compartmentalization that contradicts fundamental cognitive processes and threatens to transform natural justice from a shield protecting procedural fairness into a sword wielded against unfavourable awards. 5.1 Toward a balanced framework To that end, rather than abandoning the lessons of DJP v. DJO entirely, the international arbitration community should develop a more nuanced framework that preserves procedural integrity while maintaining arbitration’s core advantages. This framework should be built on three foundational principles: First, a differentiated approach to information asymmetries must be followed. As discussed above, not all knowledge imbalances threaten the concept of natural justice equally. Building on this, both arbitrators and institutions should distinguish between three categories of arbitrator knowledge: (1) general legal expertise and interpretative approaches that arbitrators bring to all cases (the very reason parties select experienced arbitrators); (2) legally relevant precedents and reasoning from related proceedings involving similar legal issues but different factual matrices, which Tidewater v. Venezuela recognized as permissible absent evidence of actual prejudgment; and (3) case-specific factual knowledge and confidential information from parallel proceedings involving overlapping evidence or parties. Only this third category should trigger mandatory disclosure and potential disqualification procedures since this approach would preserve the benefits of appointing an expert arbitrator while addressing genuine concerns about prejudgment based on undisclosed case-specific information. Second, a structured framework governing the permissible use of prior reasoning and materials must address the threshold question that DJP v. DJO left unanswered. The critical lesson from judicial decisions like Yap Ah Lai v. Public Prosecutor and Lim Chee Huat v. Public Prosecutor is not that drawing on prior reasoning is inherently problematic, but that decision-makers must demonstrate independent consideration of each case’s unique circumstances. Arbitrators facing similar legal issues across related proceedings should therefore adopt a structured approach: they may legitimately draw on their prior legal analysis and reasoning, but must explicitly acknowledge material factual differences, address party-specific arguments not previously considered, and provide reasoned explanations for why similar legal conclusions remain appropriate despite contextual variations. Crucially, any incorporation of reasoning from parallel proceedings should be properly attributed and disclosed to all parties and co-arbitrators, allowing them to address concerns about the applicability of such reasoning to the specific case at hand. This transparency requirement would address the fundamental procedural flaw identified in DJP v. DJO, not the fact that similar reasoning was used, but that parties were deprived of the opportunity to engage with the source and basis of the tribunal’s reasoning. Such an approach would permit the efficiency benefits of consistent legal interpretation while ensuring that each case receives the independent consideration that the concept of natural justice demands. Therefore, this framework recognizes that arbitration’s strength lies not in pretending that expert arbitrators approach each case with blank minds, but in ensuring that their accumulated expertise serves, rather than supplants, fair and independent adjudication.

sections.title: DISCLOSURE STATEMENT
sections.text: 6 DISCLOSURE STATEMENT The author declares that no funding was received for the research, authorship, or publication of this manuscript. The author has no personal or institutional involvement in any of the cases or developments discussed in this submission. The views expressed in this article are solely those of the author and do not represent the views of their employers or affiliated institutions.

plain_text:
1 INTRODUCTION In international commercial arbitration, a delicate balance exists between safeguarding procedural integrity and preventing tactical disruptions. William W. Park aptly captured this tension in his article ‘Arbitrator Bias’, observing that an ‘evil gremlin’ seeking to undermine arbitration might pursue one of two routes: either tolerate the appointment of biased arbitrators unable to judge independently, or establish ‘unrealistic ethical standards that render the arbitrator’s position precarious and susceptible to destabilisation by litigants engaged in dilatory tactics or seeking to annul unfavourable awards’. 1 William W. Park, Arbitrator Bias, 12 Transn’l Dispute Mgmt. J. (2015). This tension has taken on a new dimensions with the Singapore Court of Appeal’s (‘SGCA’) landmark decision in DJP v. DJO. 2 [2025] SGCA(I), at 2. In this case, the SGCA set aside an arbitral award after finding that ‘out of the 451 paragraphs in the Award … at least 212 paragraphs were copied and pasted from the Parallel Awards’, with former Chief Justice of India Dipak Misra serving as the Presiding Arbitrator across all three related proceedings.3 Ibid., para. 2. It concluded that Justice Misra’s use of content from the Parallel awards amounted to pre-judging a case, and that his accumulated knowledge and willingness to use that knowledge in preparing the Award constituted impermissible pre-judging. Through this ruling, the SGCA established new standards for arbitrator conduct that significantly impact repeat appointments, award drafting practices, and information asymmetries among co-arbitrators. Now, to properly assess the decision’s implications, we must first distinguish between three distinct scenarios of repeat appointments: first, appointment by the same party in a completely unrelated cases; second, appointment by the same party in cases involving similar legal issues but different facts; and third, appointment by the same party in cases with substantially overlapping facts, subject-matter, and evidence.4 Olexander Droug, The Issue of Appointment of the Same Arbitrator in Multiple Arbitrations (Sayenko Kharenko 15 Feb. 2023), sk.ua/the-issue-of-appointment-of-the-same-arbitrator-in-multiple-arbitrations/ (accessed 12 Apr. 2025). This article focuses specifically on the third scenario, which was central to DJP v. DJO and presents the most complex questions about arbitral appointments and conduct. In examining this third category of repeat appointments through the lens of DJP v. DJO, the SGCA’s decision raises two critical questions which form the central thesis of this article: the threshold question of permissible ‘copying’ in arbitral awards and the potentially paradoxical concept of information equality among tribunal members. While these principles aim to protect procedural fairness, they introduce significant challenges to established arbitration practices and may create tensions with the system’s foundational attributes of expertise, efficiency, and finality. By setting standards that may prove difficult to implement in practice, SGCA’s approach raises important questions about the proper balance between procedural safeguards and the practical realities of international arbitration. To that end, this article proceeds by first examining the jurisprudential evolution of ‘issue conflict’ in international arbitration, tracing key developments from Halliburton through landmark decisions that established the legal context for understanding DJP v. DJO. Then, it provides a detailed examination of DJP v. DJO itself, focusing on the Court’s findings regarding prejudgment, apparent bias, and the ‘equality of information’ principle. Finally, this article critically evaluates these standards, examining the fallacy of information equality and the extent of permissible ‘copying’ in awards, arguing that the Court’s approach may inadvertently undermine arbitration’s foundational values while failing to provide clear guidance for arbitrators navigating related proceedings.

2 THE BOUNDARIES OF PREJUDGMENT: A JURISPRUDENTIAL REVIEW OF ISSUE CONFLICT As evident from above, DJP v. DJO brings to the forefront the complex challenge of ‘issue conflict’ in international arbitration, a concept that refers to circumstances where an arbitrator’s impartiality may be compromised due to their relationship with the subject matter of the dispute rather than with the parties themselves.5 Gavan Griffith, & Daniel Kalderimis, ‘Pure’ Issue Conflicts in Investment Treaty Arbitration, in Practising Virtue: Inside International Arbitration (David D. Caron et al. eds, Oxford 2015; online ed., Oxford Academic 21 Jan. 2016), https://doi.org/10.1093/acprof:oso/9780198739807.003.0038 (accessed 14 Apr. 2025); R Zamour, Issue Conflicts and the Reasonable Expectation of an Open Mind: The Challenge Decision in Devas v. India and Its Impact, in Challenges and Recusals of Judges and Arbitrators in International Courts and Tribunals 227–246 (C Giorgetti ed., Brill Nijhoff Leiden 2015). This can arise when an arbitrator has previously considered similar legal or factual issues, potentially forming views that may affect their ability to approach a new case with an open mind.6 Anthony Sinclair & Matthew Gearing, Partiality and Issue Conflicts, 5(4) Transnat’l Dispute Mgmt. (Jul. 2008). Malcolm Langford, Daniel Behn & Runar Hilleren Lie, The Revolving Door in International Investment Arbitration, 20(2) J. Int’l Econ. L. 301–332 (Jun. 2017), doi: 10.1093/jiel/jgx018p (accessed 9 Apr. 2025); Luke Eric Peterson, Arbitrator Decries ‘Revolving Door’ Roles of Lawyers in Investment Treaty Arbitration (Investment Arbitration Reporter 25 Feb. 2010). Before analysing how the SGCA tackled this thorny issue, it is essential to understand how courts and tribunals alike have previously navigated this terrain, as these precedents form the backdrop against which the significance of DJP v. DJO can be properly assessed. The foundation for evaluating issue conflict lies in the objective test for apparent bias, articulated most clearly in Halliburton Company v. Chubb Bermuda Insurance Ltd,7 Halliburton Company v. Chubb Bermuda Insurance Ltd (formerly known as Ace Bermuda Insurance Ltd) UKSC 48 (2020). where the UK Supreme Court confirmed that the assessment should consider whether ‘a fair-minded and informed observer, having considered the facts, would conclude that there was a real possibility that the tribunal was biased’. 8 Ibid., para. 52. This objective standard focuses not on actual bias but on reasonable perceptions that might undermine confidence in the arbitral process.9 Samuel Yee Ching Leung & Alex Chun Hei Chan, The Duties of Impartiality, Disclosure, and Confidentiality: Lessons from a London-Seated Arbitration, 37(3) Arb. Int’l 667–684 (Sep. 2021), doi: 10.1093/arbint/aiab013 (accessed 14 Apr. 2025). Also see Charles Kimmins, Luke Pearce, Nigel Rawding & Olivia Valner, The Test for Apparent Bias and Arbitrators’ Duties of Disclosure Following Halliburton v. Chubb: Welcome Clarification, but Questions Remain, 38(3) J. Int’l Arbitration 359–376 (2021), doi: 10.54648/JOIA2021018. https://kluwerlawonline.com/journalarticle/Journal+of+International+Arbitration/38.3/JOIA2021018 (accessed 10 Apr. 2025) Early decisions generally set a high threshold for successful challenges.10 Ina C. Popova & Jessica L. Polebaum, Emerging Expectations for Arbitrators: ‘Issue Conflict’ in Investor-State Arbitration and Beyond, 41 Fordham Int’l L.J. 937 (2018). In Suez v. Argentina,11 Suez, Sociedad General de Aguas de Barcelona SA & others v. Argentine Republic, ICSID Case No ARB/03/17, Decision on the Proposal for the Disqualification of a Member of the Arbitral Tribunal (22 Oct. 2007). the tribunal rejected the notion that prior exposure to similar legal questions automatically indicated bias, emphasizing that ‘the fact that a judge or an arbitrator has made a determination of law or a finding of fact in one case does not mean that they cannot decide the law and facts impartially in another case’.12 Ibid., para. 36. This principle was reinforced in Tidewater v. Venezuela,13 Tidewater Investment SRL and Tidewater Caribe, C.A. v. Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela, ICSID Case No. ARB/10/5, Decision on Claimants’ Proposal to Disqualify Professor Brigitte Stern, Arbitrator (23 Dec. 2010). where the tribunal held that multiple appointments alone could not ‘without more’ indicate a lack of independence. According to this decision, prejudgment concerns would arise only where an arbitrator had ‘in effect prejudged the liability of one of the parties in the context of the specific factual matrix’.14 Ibid., para. 67. A key development in this jurisprudence, however, emerged with Caratube v. Kazakhstan,15 Caratube International Oil Company LLP and Devincci Salah Hourani v. Republic of Kazakhstan (II), ICSID Case No. ARB/13/13, Decision on the Proposal for Disqualification of Bruno Boesch (20 Mar. 2014). Also see EnCana Corporation v. Republic of Ecuador, LCIA Case No. UN3481, Partial Award on Jurisdiction, dated 27 Feb. 2004, para. 43; Participationes Inversiones Porturias Sàrl v. Gabonese Republic, ICSID Case No. ARB/08/17, Decision on proposal to disqualify an arbitration, dated 12 Nov. 2009. which introduced a crucial distinction between legal interpretations and factual determinations.16 See Jan Paulsson, The Idea of Arbitration (Oxford: Oxford University Press 2013). It seems to assimilate issue conflict and questions of law. Here, an arbitrator was disqualified based on his exposure to overlapping facts in a related case.
