The tribunal differentiated between legal interpretations involving ‘generally available knowledge’ and factual determinations based on ‘documents and witnesses specific to each dispute’, suggesting that factual overlap posed greater concerns for impartiality than legal opinions alone.17 See International Council for Commercial Arbitration, Report of the ASIL-ICCA Joint Task Force on Issue Conflicts in Investor-state Arbitration, the ICCA Reports No. 3, 17 Mar. 2016. This notes that differences between special knowledge of facts relevant to the merits and those relevant to the interpretation of a legal provision have been key in dismissing a challenge to an arbitrator. In this case, the unchallenged arbitrators concluded that Boesch could not reasonably maintain a ‘Chinese wall’ in his own mind regarding information acquired in the parallel proceeding.18 Ibid., para. 75. Therefore, this distinction will prove particularly relevant to our analysis of DJP v. DJO, where extensive copying from previous awards reflected both legal interpretations and case-specific factual determinations. CC/Devas v. India pushed these boundaries further, where Judge Peter Tomka upheld a challenge against Professor Francisco Orrego Vicuña based on his prior decisions interpreting an ‘essential security interests’ clause and his subsequent academic defines of those interpretations despite their annulment.19 CC/Devas (Mauritius) Ltd., Devas Employees Mauritius Private Limited, and Telcom Devas Mauritius Limited v. Republic of India, UNCITRAL, Decision on the Respondent’s Challenge to the Hon. Mark Lalonde as Presiding Arbitrator and Prof. Francisco Orrego Vicuña as Co-Arbitrator (30 Sep. 2013). This decision suggested that an arbitrator’s repeated engagement with the same legal issues, coupled with a steadfast defence of an overturned position, could create an appearance of prejudgment even without factual overlap.20 However, as SW Schill has noted, sanctioning arbitrators and aspiring arbitrators for engaging in academic discourse could have the unwanted effect of reducing substantially their contributions to scholarship: Stephan W. Schill, Editorial: The New Journal of World Investment and Trade; Arbitrator Independence and Academic Freedom; In This Issue 15 J. World Inv. & Trade 1, 7–8 (2014). Judge Tomka concluded that ‘a reasonable observer would not believe that [the arbitrator] could approach the question with an open mind’.21 Ibid., para. 64. The tension between these lines of jurisprudence was also particularly evident in Blue Bank v. Venezuela,22 Blue Bank International & Trust (Barbados) Ltd. v. Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela, ICSID Case No. ARB/12/20, Decision on the Parties’ Proposals to Disqualify a Majority of the Tribunal, 12 Nov. 2013. where an arbitrator was disqualified due to his concurrent roles as arbitrator and advocate involving similar issues and the same respondent. The deciding authority concluded that ‘a third party would find an evident or obvious appearance of lack of impartiality’ given the similarity of issues likely to be discussed in both cases.23 Ibid., para. 69. After reviewing this line of jurisprudence on issue conflict, we gained insight into how tribunals and courts have traditionally evaluated arbitrator challenges based on prior exposure to similar issues. What makes DJP v. DJO particularly significant against this backdrop is its direct confrontation with one of the most challenging manifestations of issue conflict: an arbitrator’s concurrent involvement in multiple related proceedings with substantively overlapping subject matter and evidence. By articulating an ‘equality of information’ principle and setting a vague threshold for permissible ‘copying’ in arbitral awards, the SGCA has potentially redefined the boundaries of acceptable arbitrator conduct. However, rather than resolving the tension identified by W.W. Park between procedural integrity and tactical disruption, this may have inadvertently tilted toward establishing ‘unrealistic ethical standards that render the arbitrator’s position precarious and susceptible to destabilisation by litigants engaged in dilatory tactics or seeking to annul unfavourable awards’.24 Park, supra n. 1. The full implications of this approach and whether it properly balances the competing interests at stake will be examined in the following sections.

3 CASE SUMMARY: ANATOMY OF DJP v. DJO 3.1 Background and parties The case involved an appeal against a decision of the High Court of Singapore to set aside an arbitral award on the grounds of breach of natural justice. The appellants were three companies – DJP, DJQ, and DJR – which had formed a consortium to tender for a contract relating to the western Dedicated Freight Corridors in India. The respondent was DJO, a special purpose vehicle established to manage a network of Dedicated Freight Corridors in India. The dispute arose from a contract known as the ‘CPT-13 Contract’ awarded to the appellants in August 2015. 3.2 The underlying dispute On 19 January 2017, approximately one and a half years after the contract commenced, the Indian Ministry of Labour and Employment issued a notification that immediately increased the daily rates of minimum wages payable to workmen in India. The CPT-13 Contract contained mechanisms allowing for adjustments in the event of changes in legislation (Clause 13.7) or changes in labour costs (Clause 13.8). However, it was not until 6 March 2020– about three years after the issuance of the Notification – that the appellants lodged a claim for additional payment, arguing that the Notification constituted a ‘change in legislation’ as defined in Clause 13.7. The respondent rejected this claim. 3.3 The arbitration and parallel proceedings The appellants commenced arbitration in December 2021 (the ‘Arbitration’). Under the CPT-13 Contract’s dispute resolution clause, the Arbitration was seated in Singapore and conducted in accordance with the Rules of Arbitration of the ICC. The arbitral tribunal (the ‘Tribunal’) consisted of three eminent retired Indian judges: The Honourable Justice Krishn Kumar Lahoti and The Honourable Justice Gita Mittal as co-arbitrators, with The Honourable Justice Dipak Misra, a former Chief Justice of India, as the presiding arbitrator (the ‘President’). Crucially, the respondent was simultaneously defending two other sets of arbitration proceedings (the ‘Parallel Arbitrations’) involving similar claims. These were: (1) The ‘CP-301 Arbitration’, commenced in May 2021 by DJR (also an appellant in the present case) and an Indian company against the respondent. This resulted in the ‘CP-301 Award’ issued in July 2023. (2) The ‘CP-302 Arbitration’, commenced in October 2021 by DJR and another Indian company against the respondent. This resulted in the ‘CP-302 Award’ issued in August 2023. The President of the Tribunal in the present Arbitration also served as the presiding arbitrator in both Parallel Arbitrations, though the co-arbitrators were different. 3.4 The award and its preparation In the Award dated 24 November 2023, the Tribunal ruled substantially in the appellants’ favour, finding that the Notification was a ‘change of legislation’ under Clause 13.7, that the respondent was liable to make additional payments, and that the claim was not precluded on any ground advanced by the respondent. Significantly, the Award was not drafted entirely afresh but used the Parallel Awards as templates. Out of the 451 paragraphs in the Award, at least 212 paragraphs were copied and pasted from the Parallel Awards. The Tribunal had also referenced authorities not cited by the parties, applied an incorrect version of Clause 13.8, and applied the wrong lex arbitri to issues of interest and costs. 3.5 The setting aside application The respondent applied to set aside the Award on three grounds: (1) That the Tribunal had acted in breach of the agreed arbitral procedure by failing to independently assess the issues and give proper reasons (Article 34(2)(a)(iv) of the UNCITRAL Model Law). (2) That the Tribunal had conducted the Arbitration contrary to Singapore public policy (Article 34(2)(b)(ii) of the Model Law) (3) That the Tribunal had acted in breach of the rules of natural justice (section 24(b) of the International Arbitration Act 1994) 3.6 The high court decision The High Court Judge set aside the Award on the third ground, finding that it had been rendered in breach of the rules of natural justice. The Judge identified four specific problems: (1) The Tribunal had failed to restrict itself to submissions made in the Arbitration, instead reproducing verbatim submissions from the CP-301 Arbitration. (2) The Award referenced authorities not cited by the parties. (3) The Tribunal cited an incorrect version of Clause 13.8, applying the CP-301 version instead of the CPT-13 version, thereby using wrong coefficients and data points. (4) The Tribunal applied the wrong lex arbitri (Indian Arbitration Act instead of Singapore IAA) to issues of interest and costs. The Judge concluded that a fair-minded observer would reasonably apprehend that the President had prejudged the Arbitration, allowing his accumulated knowledge from the Parallel Arbitrations to influence his decision. 3.7 The appeal to the court of appeal The appellants appealed, arguing that the Judge had been unduly preoccupied with the similarity between the Award and the Parallel Awards, which they claimed had no material impact on the outcome. They contended that using the Parallel Awards as templates was merely a ‘short cut’ and did not compromise the integrity of the Arbitration. While acknowledging errors in the Award, they maintained these were inconsequential. The respondent countered that the Tribunal’s decision-making process was fundamentally flawed because it had extensively referenced material from outside the Arbitration and resolved common issues in exactly the same manner across all Three Arbitrations. 3.8 The court of appeal’s analysis The Court of Appeal identified three main complaints in the respondent’s case: (1) A fair-minded observer would reasonably apprehend that the Tribunal had approached the Arbitration with a closed mind, influenced by anchoring and confirmation biases from the Parallel Arbitrations. (2) The President had drawn on material from the Parallel Arbitrations that the parties in the present Arbitration had not been given the opportunity to address. (3) The expectation of equality among arbitrators was compromised by the unequal access to information between the President and his co-arbitrators. The Court rejected the appellants’ approach of evaluating each complaint in isolation, stating that the respondent’s case concerned the entire decision-making process, not discrete breaches. 3.9 The court of appeal’s findings The Court of Appeal undertook a comprehensive analysis of the alleged breaches of natural justice and ultimately upheld the High Court’s decision to set aside the Award. The Court found that the Tribunal’s decision-making process was fundamentally flawed on multiple grounds. Regarding prejudgment and apparent bias, the Court determined that ‘a fair-minded and informed observer would, after considering all the relevant facts and circumstances, reasonably apprehend or harbour the suspicion that by reason of what the President had done, he was materially influenced by the earlier decisions that he had been party to in the Parallel Arbitrations’. This conclusion was supported by the extensive copying from the Parallel Awards (at least 212 out of 451 paragraphs), where ‘the Parallel Awards were used as templates, with adjustments made to account for what were thought to be the specificities of the Arbitration’. The Court found that despite material differences between the arbitrations, ‘the Parallel Awards were used as templates in drafting the Award to a very substantial degree’, resulting in ‘the identical resolution of common issues despite different arguments being raised’ and ‘the awkward insertion of new analyses between copied paragraphs’. Particularly, the Court was concerned about the manifestation of cognitive biases, finding that ‘the impression conveyed to the fair-minded observer would reasonably be that the decision-maker might have been wrongly anchored to the earlier decision in a way that compromised his ability and openness to consider matters afresh’. The Court noted that such decision-making would ‘also appear to be plagued by a confirmation bias, referring to the difficulty of persuading a decision-maker that has come to an initial view to then change its view’. Accordingly, the multiple errors in the Award, including citation of the wrong contract clause, application of the wrong procedural law, and reference to computations not in evidence, ‘evidence[d] the problem with the underlying decision-making process’ and suggested to ‘the fair-minded and informed observer, it would appear that the Tribunal might not have adequately applied itself to the facts and submissions actually made in the Arbitration’. The Court also found that the Tribunal had impermissibly relied on ‘material falling outside the scope of the Arbitration, which [the President] had acquired from his involvement in the Parallel Arbitrations and which he drew upon in preparing the Award’. The Court determined this constituted extraneous considerations that ‘had not been raised to the parties’ attention’ and ‘formed such a pervasive part of the Award that it simply could not be overlooked’. This violated the fair hearing rule as ‘it was plain that it was neither contemplated nor agreed to by the parties that the Award could be prepared by such a process’. Additionally, the Court articulated a principle of equality among arbitrators, finding that ‘the expectation of equality as between the Arbitrators was compromised’. The Court explained that: where an arbitrator is not equally placed with his co-arbitrators, it may be said that the other arbitrators have not had an equal, fair and independent opportunity to discharge their adjudicative function and this may be found to have undermined the core expectation of the parties when they choose to resolve their disputes by arbitration. The Court emphasized that ‘each arbitrator should be equally placed to have the same access to the details of the dispute at hand and of the material on the basis of which, they are to resolve it’, and that the co-arbitrators’ lack of access to this influential material ‘further compromised’ the integrity of the arbitral process. While the appellants argued for only setting aside parts of the Award that were directly tainted by the breach, the Court rejected this approach, finding that the breach of natural justice permeated the entire Award. The Court also clarified that remission was not available as a remedy since the Award had already been set aside at first instance.

4 BALANCE UNDONE: DJP v. DJO’S TROUBLING IMPLICATIONS FOR INTERNATIONAL ARBITRATION The SGCA’s decision reflects a nuanced approach to judicial review of arbitral awards, demonstrating admirable restraint in certain respects while introducing potentially problematic innovations in others. First, the Singapore International Commercial Court’s earlier decision reaffirms the principle of minimal curial intervention that remains foundational to international arbitration. When confronted with DJO’s argument that the Tribunal had failed to properly consider the significant delay between the Notification and the appellants’ claim,25 DJO v. DJP, SGHC(I) 24 (2024), paras 75, 76. the Court appropriately declined to evaluate this contention on the grounds that doing so would require assessing the substantive merits of the case – a task beyond the scope of a seat court’s authority.26 BLC v. BLB, SGCA 40 (2014), para. 53; AJU v. AJT, SGCA 41 (2011), para. 65. Moreover, by choosing to base its analysis on the principles of natural justice rather than scrutinizing the Tribunal’s application of the incorrect lex arbitri or questioning its factual findings,27 DJO v. DJP, supra n. 25, para. 74. the Court again reinforced the limited role that seat courts should play when reviewing arbitral awards, thereby enhancing the finality and predictability of the arbitral process. This judicial restraint extends also to the Court’s treatment of repeat appointments. The decision clarifies that Justice Misra’s appointment as president across all three arbitrations did not, by itself, justify setting aside the award.28 Ibid., para. 71. Neither the ICC nor the Court considered this repeat appointment sufficient grounds for questioning the Award’s validity.29 Ibid., para. 48. This position aligns with prevailing jurisprudence, preserves party autonomy in arbitrator appointment, and acknowledges that experienced arbitrators frequently serve in multiple related disputes. The Court recognized that such arrangements could, in theory, be implemented successfully, though it did note they would be ‘fraught with difficulties’ given the accumulated knowledge an arbitrator would inevitably acquire.30 Ibid., para. 50. However, despite these positive elements, the decision introduces certain problematic principles that could significantly affect how arbitral appointments operate henceforth. 4.1 The fallacy of information equality Perhaps the most problematic principle emerging from this decision is the SGCA’s ‘equality of information’ requirement. This represents a troubling expansion of natural justice requirements in arbitration. While seemingly reasonable at first glance, this principle creates profound practical challenges that strike at the core of how arbitration functions. By holding that ‘where an arbitrator is not equally placed with his co-arbitrators, it may be said that the other arbitrators have not had an equal, fair and independent opportunity to discharge their adjudicative function’, 31 Ibid., paras 56–63. the Court has established a new ground for challenging awards. The decision places arbitrators in an untenable position regarding information management and disclosure. The Court states that it ‘does not think anything turns on’ whether co-arbitrators knew about the copying.32 Ibid., para. 62. This creates an unattainable standard – arbitrators cannot reasonably disclose information they don’t realize is relevant, yet awards can apparently be set aside based on information asymmetries nobody detected. This raises an important question: How can arbitrators possibly guard against unknown knowledge imbalances? This creates a paradoxical situation where an arbitrator could be found to have violated natural justice despite acting in complete good faith and without any awareness of possessing exclusive information. Such a standard effectively imposes an obligation that no arbitrator, no matter how diligent, could reliably satisfy. This principle also directly conflicts with the party-appointment system that is fundamental to arbitration. Parties deliberately select arbitrators with specific expertise precisely because these individuals bring distinct perspectives to a tribunal. An energy specialist understands technical aspects that legal experts might not; a construction lawyer possesses deeper knowledge of standard contracts than generalists. This knowledge diversity has always been considered a strength of arbitration, not a procedural defect requiring remedy. The Court’s approach, however, threatens to transform arbitration from a process where arbitrators exercise independent judgment into one where perfect information symmetry becomes a procedural requirement, regardless of its impact on the actual outcome. As held in QYFM v. Minister for Immigration, Citizenship, Migrant Services and Multicultural Affairs,33 QYFM v. Minister for Immigration, Citizenship, Migrant Services and Multicultural Affairs HCA 15 (2023). each member of a decision-making body has ‘an individual duty to adjudicate on the decision by reference to his or her own true view’. The equality of information principle undermines this foundational concept. This decision also recalls broader concerns about selecting arbitrators with deep subject-matter expertise. Such arbitrators have inevitably developed certain views over their careers that they must consciously set aside to remain impartial. The Court’s approach may inadvertently encourage parties to select arbitrators who lack specialized knowledge to avoid challenges based on information asymmetry – a counterintuitive outcome that diminishes the quality of arbitral decision-making. This creates a troubling paradox: arbitrators are selected for their expertise but then potentially penalized for possessing it. If widely adopted, this approach could lead to the perverse outcome where parties deliberately avoid the most qualified arbitrators to minimize the risk of subsequent challenges, ultimately reducing the quality of arbitral decision-making across the field. For instance, a presiding arbitrator appointed across related cases now faces an impossible choice: either decline all but one appointment (reducing efficiency and risking inconsistent outcomes) or accept multiple appointments while pretending to approach each case entirely afresh, artificially compartmentalizing knowledge from related proceedings. Neither option serves the interests of efficient dispute resolution. Indeed, this decision, particularly, creates tension with dispute resolution boards in construction projects, which are specifically designed to handle multiple disputes between the same parties over a project’s duration.34 Cyril Chern, Chern on Dispute Boards: Practice and Procedure (3d ed. 2015); Gwyn Owen & Brian Totterdill, Dispute Boards: Procedures and Practice (2007); Nael G. Bunni, The FIDIC Forms of Contract (3d ed. 2005) and Cyril Chern, The Law of Construction Disputes (2010). These boards are expected to maintain knowledge continuity across disputes, including overlapping issues. Unlike the SGCA’s approach in DJP v. DJO, this knowledge continuity is considered beneficial rather than problematic in that context. In the dispute board setting, members routinely draw on their accumulated project knowledge to ensure consistent and efficient resolution of multiple related disputes – the very practice the SGCA now suggests might violate natural justice in arbitration. This divergence creates uncertainty about whether similar standards should apply across different dispute resolution mechanisms, potentially disrupting established practices in major infrastructure projects worldwide. This requirement by the SGCA also ignores practical realities of how adjudicators process information. Experienced decision-makers inevitably draw on their accumulated knowledge when facing similar questions and this is simply how the human mind works.35 W. Mark C. Weidemaier, Toward a Theory of Precedent in Arbitration, 51 Wm. & Mary L. Rev. 1895 (2010), https://scholarship.law.wm.edu/wmlr/vol51/iss5/5 (accessed 18 Apr. 2025). The Court effectively demands that arbitrators engage in artificial mental segregation. This requirement for artificial compartmentalization places arbitrators in the impossible position of having to pretend they can approach each related case with a completely fresh mind, a standard that may be literally impossible to meet, regardless of an arbitrator’s good faith efforts. Now, this creates a dangerous precedent where parties dissatisfied with an outcome can weaponize this principle as a shield, claiming information inequality in virtually any repeat appointment scenario by simply arguing that mental segregation of knowledge is inherently implausible for human arbitrators. Therefore, by attempting to impose perfect information symmetry in a process where knowledge asymmetry is not only inevitable but often beneficial, the decision risks making the perfect the enemy of the good in international arbitration. If widely adopted, this approach would transform natural justice from a shield protecting procedural fairness into a sword that can be strategically wielded against unfavourable awards. The ultimate risk is substantial damage to arbitration’s core advantages of expertise, efficiency, and finality. 4.2 The threshold question: extent of permissible ‘copying’ in awards Another crucial problem with the SGCA’s decision is the dangerous uncertainty it creates about acceptable drafting practices in arbitral awards. While the Court acknowledged that ‘not every instance of an arbitrator making an award where portions had been copied from other sources without attribution would render the award liable to be set aside’, it provides minimal guidance on acceptable thresholds for future tribunals. The Court noted that assessment depends on ‘the nature, extent and effect of the copying in question’, but this leaves crucial questions unanswered: Would proper citation and attribution satisfy natural justice requirements? Does the problem concern the quantity of material copied or its nature? The decision creates a precarious landscape for arbitrators to navigate without clear boundaries. The Court also fails to distinguish between different types of copied content, treating reproduction of legal analysis identically to copying factual findings. There is a material difference between consistent legal interpretation of standard provisions across related cases versus copying case-specific factual determinations. By treating all copying as equally problematic, the Court missed an opportunity to provide nuanced guidance appropriate to different situations arbitrators routinely encounter. Most troublingly, this decision may inadvertently encourage arbitrators to artificially differentiate their reasoning across related cases, even when identical conclusions are warranted. This resembles the problem identified in Yap Ah Lai v. Public Prosecutor,36 Yap Ah Lai v. Public Prosecutor, 3 SLR 180 (2014). where a district judge was criticized for identical reasoning in different cases despite reaching the correct outcome both times. The district judge had copied crucial passages of reasoning from his own earlier decision, sentencing two offenders identically despite material differences between their cases. Importantly, when the appellate court examined the substance of the decision after setting it aside for procedural defects, it actually reached a similar conclusion – though with a reduced sentence – showing that the copying itself wasn’t the fundamental problem; rather, it was the appearance that the judge hadn’t independently considered each case’s unique aspects. Similarly, in Lim Chee Huat v. Public Prosecutor,37 Lim Chee Huat v. Public Prosecutor 5 SLR 433 (2019). a district judge had reproduced one party’s written submissions in his grounds of decision (even including a typographical error from those submissions). The appellate court found independent judgment was lacking but, notably, declined to order a retrial. After reviewing the evidence afresh, the court ultimately reached the same outcome as the original decision, demonstrating that the procedural flaw in copying didn’t necessarily invalidate the substantive correctness of the decision. These cases highlight a critical distinction between procedure and substance that DJP v. DJO fails to adequately address. In both Yap Ah Lai and Lim Chee Huat, the courts recognized that copying might indicate procedural flaws while still acknowledging that the underlying decisions could be substantively correct. In those cases, the appellate courts could remedy the situation by considering the evidence afresh while preserving correct outcomes – an option unavailable in arbitration where courts cannot determine underlying disputes. Thus, DJP v. DJO creates a problematic asymmetry: it imports judicial standards of drafting into arbitration without the corresponding remedial mechanisms available in court proceedings. Arbitrators must now meet judicial standards of independence in their award drafting, but parties face the severe consequence of complete award invalidation rather than the more proportionate remedies available in judicial proceedings. This creates a disproportionate risk for parties choosing arbitration, where a drafting approach that might be remedied through appeal in court proceedings instead results in the nuclear option of award annulment, even when the underlying decision might be substantively correct.

5 CONCLUSION: A PATH FORWARD International commercial arbitration stands at a crossroads following DJP v. DJO, with the SGCA’s decision potentially redefining the boundaries of acceptable arbitrator conduct. While the Court’s intention to safeguard procedural fairness is laudable, its approach creates significant tensions with arbitration’s foundational values of expertise, efficiency, and finality. The distinction articulated in Caratube v. Kazakhstan, 38 Caratube International Oil Company LLP and Devincci Salah Hourani v. Republic of Kazakhstan (II), supra n. 15. between ‘generally available knowledge’ and case-specific factual determinations offered a nuanced framework that could have informed a more balanced approach to the complex issues of information asymmetry and award drafting. However, by treating all copied content equally and imposing an unrealistic ‘equality of information’ standard, the Court has created an impossible dilemma for arbitrators navigating related proceedings. This standard demands artificial mental compartmentalization that contradicts fundamental cognitive processes and threatens to transform natural justice from a shield protecting procedural fairness into a sword wielded against unfavourable awards. 5.1 Toward a balanced framework To that end, rather than abandoning the lessons of DJP v. DJO entirely, the international arbitration community should develop a more nuanced framework that preserves procedural integrity while maintaining arbitration’s core advantages. This framework should be built on three foundational principles: First, a differentiated approach to information asymmetries must be followed. As discussed above, not all knowledge imbalances threaten the concept of natural justice equally. Building on this, both arbitrators and institutions should distinguish between three categories of arbitrator knowledge: (1) general legal expertise and interpretative approaches that arbitrators bring to all cases (the very reason parties select experienced arbitrators); (2) legally relevant precedents and reasoning from related proceedings involving similar legal issues but different factual matrices, which Tidewater v. Venezuela recognized as permissible absent evidence of actual prejudgment; and (3) case-specific factual knowledge and confidential information from parallel proceedings involving overlapping evidence or parties. Only this third category should trigger mandatory disclosure and potential disqualification procedures since this approach would preserve the benefits of appointing an expert arbitrator while addressing genuine concerns about prejudgment based on undisclosed case-specific information. Second, a structured framework governing the permissible use of prior reasoning and materials must address the threshold question that DJP v. DJO left unanswered. The critical lesson from judicial decisions like Yap Ah Lai v. Public Prosecutor and Lim Chee Huat v. Public Prosecutor is not that drawing on prior reasoning is inherently problematic, but that decision-makers must demonstrate independent consideration of each case’s unique circumstances. Arbitrators facing similar legal issues across related proceedings should therefore adopt a structured approach: they may legitimately draw on their prior legal analysis and reasoning, but must explicitly acknowledge material factual differences, address party-specific arguments not previously considered, and provide reasoned explanations for why similar legal conclusions remain appropriate despite contextual variations. Crucially, any incorporation of reasoning from parallel proceedings should be properly attributed and disclosed to all parties and co-arbitrators, allowing them to address concerns about the applicability of such reasoning to the specific case at hand. This transparency requirement would address the fundamental procedural flaw identified in DJP v. DJO, not the fact that similar reasoning was used, but that parties were deprived of the opportunity to engage with the source and basis of the tribunal’s reasoning. Such an approach would permit the efficiency benefits of consistent legal interpretation while ensuring that each case receives the independent consideration that the concept of natural justice demands. Therefore, this framework recognizes that arbitration’s strength lies not in pretending that expert arbitrators approach each case with blank minds, but in ensuring that their accumulated expertise serves, rather than supplants, fair and independent adjudication.

6 DISCLOSURE STATEMENT The author declares that no funding was received for the research, authorship, or publication of this manuscript. The author has no personal or institutional involvement in any of the cases or developments discussed in this submission. The views expressed in this article are solely those of the author and do not represent the views of their employers or affiliated institutions.
juris_case_name: DJP and others v. DJO [2024] SGHC(I) 24

title: AI Guidelines in International Arbitration: Revolutionary or Cautionary? A Comparative Analysis

sections.title: THE RISE OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN THE LEGAL INDUSTRY
sections.text: 1 THE RISE OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN THE LEGAL INDUSTRY Generative artificial intelligence (AI) models are rapidly evolving, and being increasingly adopted within traditional industry globally. The legal industry is no exception. Today, the legal industry benefits from of over 100 legal AI models that are tailored to meet the needs of lawyers in law firms and corporate legal departments and provide services such as contract drafting and amendment, legal research, and legal analysis.1 See Eric Chin, Legal Generative AI: The New Equation for Lawyers of the Future [2025] SAL Prac 2, at [4]. For example, the American Arbitration Association – International Centre for Dispute Resolution has partnered with Optima Juris to develop an AI-powered transcription service. Naturally, the advent of legal AI has led to an increase in the use of AI in the realm of international arbitration. For example, AI has been used for arbitrator selection and predictive analysis of arbitrators’ decisions and inclinations, which are assessed through a review of past case experience and judgments or published awards,2 See Aditya Singh Chauhan, Future of AI in Arb itration: The Fine Line Between Fiction and Reality (Kluwer Arbitration Blog 26 Sep. 2020), https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2020/09/26/future-of-ai-in-arbitration-the-fine-line-between-fiction-and-reality/ (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). and may extend to reviewing publications of potential tribunal members and even social media posts. In the future, advances in technology may mean that we could even see the use of AI in tribunal deliberations.3 See Maxi Scherer, Artificial Intelligence and Legal Decision-Making: The Wide Open?, 36(5) J. Int’l Arb. 539–574 (2019) (© Maxi Scherer ed., Kluwer Law International; Kluwer Law International 2019), doi: 10.54648/joia2019028. See also How Arbitrators are Harnessing Artificial Intelligence (American Arbitration Association 20 Feb. 2024), https://www.adr.org/blog/how-arbitrators-are-harnessing-artificial-intelligence (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). However, such advancements in technology do not come without risk. Specifically, the unregulated use of AI can pose serious dangers not only to a client’s right to adequate legal representation, but also to their due process rights. As a consequence, we have witnessed the recent development and publication of guidelines for the use of AI in arbitration by a number of leading arbitral institutions and bodies globally. Within this article, we consider some of the risks attaching to the use of AI in international arbitration, whilst also comparing the terms of the leading published guidelines in the field.

sections.title: RISKS OF THE USE OF AI IN INTERNATIONAL ARBITRATION
sections.text: 2 RISKS OF THE USE OF AI IN INTERNATIONAL ARBITRATION The use of AI as an adjudication tool (either by arbitrators or the judiciary) poses the risk of undermining parties’ rights of due process in the proceedings, as well as posing public policy and potentially ethical concerns, which may be highly relevant to the issue of enforcement of arbitral awards in foreign jurisdictions. Indiscriminate use of generative AI in the course of the determination of disputes might undermine a client’s right to a fair hearing, should they indicate a lack of due attention paid by the tribunal in deliberating on issues, and may in turn result in a tribunal not fully appreciating the nuances of a party’s case, thereby resulting in a miscarriage of justice. As arbitration practitioners must know, a sufficient breach of a party’s due process rights may constitute grounds for setting aside under the UNCITRAL Model Law, on which many nations’ arbitration statutes are founded. As a result, any award rendered may be liable to be set aside by the relevant court (which may or may not be the eventual enforcement court or the setting aside court). Increasing AI usage by parties to arbitration proceedings also poses risk. One of the classic issues that has arisen is the risk of hallucination by legal AI models, particularly by Large Language Models (LLM). A study by Stanford RegLab and the Institute for Human-Centred AI found that the risk of hallucination in LLMs ranges from 69% to 88%, in response to specific legal queries, and that LLMs have a tendency to reinforce ‘incorrect legal assumptions’.4 See Matthew Dahl, Varun Magesh, Mirac Suzgun & Daniel E. Ho, Hallucinating Law: Legal Mistakes With Large Language Models are Pervasive (Legal Aggregate, Stanford Law School 11 Jan. 2024), https://law.stanford.edu/2024/01/11/hallucinating-law-legal-mistakes-with-large-language-models-are-pervasive (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). This development poses an unfortunate consequence for the use of AI in legal work, whereby high-profile cases involving legal counsels’ misplaced reliance on generative AI to their detriment (and in certain cases, public humiliation) has made headlines around the world.5 See Benjamin Weiser, Here’s What Happens When Your Lawyer Uses ChatGPT (The New York Times, 27 May 2023), https://www.nytimes.com/2023/05/27/nyregion/avianca-airline-lawsuit-chatgpt.html (accessed 8 Jul. 2025); Sara Merken, AI ‘Hallucinations’ in Court Papers Spell Trouble for Lawyers (Reuters 19 Feb. 2025) https://www.reuters.com/technology/artificial-intelligence/ai-hallucinations-court-papers-spell-trouble-lawyers-2025-02-18 (accessed 8 Jul. 2025); and Sara Merken, Lawyers in Walmart Lawsuit Admit AI ‘Hallucinated’ Case Citations (Reuters 11 Feb. 2025), https://www.reuters.com/legal/legalindustry/lawyers-walmart-lawsuit-admit-ai-hallucinated-case-citations-2025-02-10 (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). By way of example, a junior barrister was found to have cited multiple fake cases in a hearing before the English Courts to which the Court commented6 See Judge Fury After ‘Fake’ Cases Cited by Rookie Barrister in High Court (AJO Solicitors), https://ajosolicitors.co.uk/judge-fury-after-fake-cases-cited-by-rookie-barrister-in-high-court (accessed 8 Jul. 2025).: On the balance of probabilities, I consider that it would have been negligent for this barrister, if she used AI and did not check it, to put that text into her pleading. However, I am not in a position to determine whether she did use AI. I find as a fact that Ms Forey intentionally put these cases into her statement of facts and grounds, not caring whether they existed or not, because she had got them from a source which I do not know but certainly was not photocopying cases, putting them in a box and tabulating them, and certainly not from any law report. I do not accept that it is possible to photocopy a non-existent case and tabulate it. Although the court did not positively identify that the barrister in question relied on AI, the use of non-existent cases is a hallmark of the hallucinatory consequences of using general off-the-shelf AIs in legal practice. The barrister in question was eventually ordered to pay GBP 2,000 in costs.7 See ibid. Instances such as these represent not only the inherent limitations of AI, but also a threat to a client’s right to adequate legal representation. Against this backdrop, the emergence of guidelines surrounding AI usage in arbitration and disputes is welcomed as a means to manage these aforementioned risks and to prevent similar incidents from arising. The reality is that AI is only going to continue to play a role in arbitration, and to grow in significance going forward. A good example of this may be seen in the Ordinance on the Shenzhen Court of International Arbitration (SCIA) (the SCIA Ordinance) which recognizes, at Article 7, that: The SCIA shall pursue smart arbitration by fully utilizing the internet, big data, artificial intelligence , and other information technologies to provide efficient and convenient dispute resolution services to the parties 8 See Art. 7, Ordinance on the Shenzhen Court of International Arbitration. (emphasis added).In this regard, caution, and the adoption of appropriate guidelines on the use of AI, is and will continue to be critical.

sections.title: WHAT ARE THE EXISTING GUIDELINES ON THE USE OF AI IN ARBITRATION?
sections.text: 3 WHAT ARE THE EXISTING GUIDELINES ON THE USE OF AI IN ARBITRATION? At present, the institutions and bodies which have led the way in the development and publication of guidelines pertaining to the use of AI in arbitration are the Silicon Valley Arbitration & Mediation Centre (SVAMC); the Chartered Institute of Arbitrators (CIArb); the Stockholm Chamber of Commerce (SCC); the Vienna International Arbitral Centre (VIAC); the American Arbitration Association – International Centre for Dispute Resolution (AAA-ICDR); the Associations of Arbitrators (Southern Africa) NPC (AOASA); and the China International Economic and Trade Arbitration Commission (CIETAC). Given the growing importance of AI in not only the legal field generally, but also specifically in international arbitration, the guidelines published by these institutions already represent a significant step towards providing clear guard rails on AI usage in international arbitration.9 We also note that there are likely to be an increasing number of AI-related disputes going forward where the central issue involves an issue of AI liability (see Dan Perera & Aaron Tan Kai Ran, Legal Liability for AI-Driven Decisions – When AI Gets It Wrong, Who Can You Turn To? (Holman Fenwick Willan LLP, 15 Apr. 2025), https://www.hfw.com/insights/legal-liability-for-ai-driven-decisions-when-ai-gets-it-wrong-who-can-you-turn-to/#:~:text=Given%20the%20rapidly%20increasing%20integration%20of%20artificial%20intelligence,focus%20on%20the%20energy%2C%20resources%20and%20commodities%20sectors (accessed 8 Jul. 2025)). In such cases, specialized rules might be necessary such as the JAMS Artificial Intelligence Disputes Clause, Rules and Protective Order, which governs AI arbitrations. However, this is beyond the current scope of this piece. As the SVAMC Consultation Draft observed:10 See SVMAC Consultation Draft dated 31 Aug. 2023, issued for public consultation prior to the publication of the final guidelines (the SVAMC Consultation Draft). Any person involved in the arbitration proceedings that uses an AI tool must be aware of the risks of such use (breach of confidentiality, cyberattacks, bias, inaccuracies/hallucinations, violation of personal data protection, etc. ) and shall be accountable in case of occurrence of these risks. The guidelines for the use of AI in arbitration published by these organizations in chronological order are as follows: (1) AAA-ICDR AAA-ICDR Principles Supporting the Use of AI in Alternative Dispute Resolution, published on 30 January 2024 (AAA-ICDR Guidelines) (2) SVAMC 2024 SVAMC Guidelines on the Use of AI in Arbitration, published on 30 April 2024 (SVAMC Guidelines); (3) SCC SCC Arbitration Institute Guide to the use of AI in cases administered under the SCC rules, published on 16 October 2024 (SCC Guidelines); (4) CIArb 2025 CIArb Guidelines on the Use of AI in Arbitration, published on 19 March 2025 (CIArb Guidelines); (5) VIAC’s Note on the Use of AI in Arbitration Proceedings, published on 10 April 2025 (VIAC Guidelines); and (6) AOASA Guidelines on the use of AI in Arbitrations and Adjudications on 20 May 2025 (AOASA Guidelines). (7) CIETAC Provisional guidelines on the use of AI in arbitration on 18 July 2025 (CIETAC Guidelines) Together, these are collectively referred to as the Guidelines. The SVAMC Guidelines, CIArb Guidelines, and AOASA Guidelines are intended to be comprehensive and instructive suggestions, capable for general adoption by parties or tribunals in a manner similar to the IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence in International Arbitration, whereas the SCC Guidelines, AAA-ICDR Guidelines, CIETAC Guidelines and VIAC Guidelines are plainly intended to be more general in nature and, as noted by VIAC, ‘intended to facilitate discussions related to the use of AI in the context of VIAC arbitration proceedings’.11 See at 2 of the VIAC Guidelines at the section titled ‘Applicability’. In our view, the guidelines surrounding AI will only continue to grow over the years as governments and international organizations continue to publish AI guidelines for various industries and sectors. By way of example, the European Union (EU) AI Act12 See EU AI Act: First Regulation on Artificial Intelligence (European Parliament 8 Jun. 2023), https://www.europarl.europa.eu/topics/en/article/20230601STO93804/eu-ai-act-first-regulation-on-artificial-intelligence (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). is a comprehensive legislation governing the use of AI in Europe while the International Chamber of Commerce has released its AI governance guidelines for businesses.13 See Overarching Narrative on Artificial Intelligence (International Chamber of Commerce), https://iccwbo.org/global-insights/digital-economy/icc-overarching-narrative-on-artificial-intelligence (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). The prevalence and importance of AI guidelines further extends to areas as diverse as mediation (IBA Guidelines on the use of Gen AI in Mediation), general litigation (Supreme Court of Singapore Guide on the Use of Generative AI Tools by Court Users), and even guidelines created to govern AI disputes themselves (JAMS AI Dispute Rules). Unfortunately, these alternative guidelines are beyond the scope of the present article.

sections.title: EVALUATING THE EFFECTIVENESS OF THE VARIOUS GUIDELINES
sections.text: 4 EVALUATING THE EFFECTIVENESS OF THE VARIOUS GUIDELINES In our view, the effectiveness of any AI guidelines should be assessed based on the following criteria: (1) whether the guidelines provide sufficient clarity and scope of application; (2) whether the parties and the arbitrators are sufficiently protected and empowered by the guidelines; and (3) whether the integrity, confidentiality of the arbitration process, and party autonomy is adequately safeguarded. In summary, with reference to what we consider to be the most critical elements surrounding the use of AI in arbitration, we set out below (and in tabular form at Annex 1 hereto, with reference to the specific provisions) a comparative analysis of the Guidelines: (1) Definition of AI: Having a carefully considered definition of AI adds clarity to the scope of application of the Guidelines, although this is not present in all of the Guidelines; (2) Due Process and Independence: The CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines provide the greatest degree of due process protection for parties, thereby ensuring the integrity of the arbitration; (3) Disclosure of Use of AI: In relation to party usage, the CIArb Guidelines provide the tribunal with the broadest discretion to order AI-related disclosure. However, the SVAMC Guidelines provide helpful clarity on how such disclosure should be effected. In respect of tribunal usage, the various guidelines provide that arbitrators themselves would need to disclose AI usage where appropriate; (4) Confidentiality: Although the CIArb Guidelines protect parties’ confidentiality, best practices would need to be imported from the SVAMC Guidelines as well as prior CIArb Guidelines; (5) Arbitrator Powers: The CIArb Guidelines provide the clearest guidance and empower the tribunal to exercise their powers to regulate the usage of AI by the parties in the arbitration to the fullest extent; and (6) Parties’ Usage: the SVAMC Guidelines place the onus on parties to respect the integrity of arbitration proceedings in using AI. We examine each of these critical elements in greater detail below: Comparative an aly sis of critical elements of Guidelines 1. Definition of AI: Having a carefully considered definition of AI adds clarity to the scope of application of the Guidelines, although this is not present in all of the Guidelines. In considering the effectiveness of any AI guideline, a starting point is to consider: what constitutes AI? To which question, there does not appear to be any universal consensus.14 Daniel Seng, Jerrold Soh & Lim How Khang, An Introduction to the Relevant Technologies, in Law and Technology in Sin gapore (Singapore Academy of Law Publishing, 2nd ed., Simon Chesterman, Goh Yihan, and Andrew Phang, 2025) at 26 and 27. By way of example, the CIArb Guidelines define AI as a: machine-based system that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments. Different AI systems vary in their levels of autonomy and adaptiveness after deployment.In comparison, the SVAMC Guidelines define AI as: computer systems that perform tasks commonly associated with human cognition, such as understanding natural language, recognising complex semantic patterns, and generating human-like outputs.Another useful comparator is the SCC Guidelines, which adopts the EU’s definition of AI, which is a15 See EU Regulation 2024/1689 (EU AI Act).: machine-based system that is designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy and that may exhibit adaptiveness after deployment, and that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers, from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments. Another comparator is the AOASA Guidelines, which adopts a comparatively unique definition of AI, which: shall mean non-human power capable of performing tasks traditionally requiring human cognitive functions and human intelligence, such as sequencing and collating facts, summarising such facts, reasoning, learning, problem-solving, perception, and decision-making.As is apparent, the definitions of AI are already comparatively different. For example, the CIArb Guidelines and the SCC Guidelines adopt an external-facing approach, which emphasizes the ability of the AI to generate outputs and have an influence on the physical and virtual environment. In contrast, the SVAMC Guidelines place a greater emphasis on internal processes and human cognition, as opposed to any external output. In even sharper contrast to the aforementioned three sets of Guidelines, the VIAC Guidelines deliberately eschew any prior definition. Instead, the VIAC Guidelines make it clear that the ‘VIAC does not subscribe to a single definition’, on the basis that ‘a fixed definition would likely not cover the broadening range of tools that will be used in the near future’. This represents the VIAC’s pragmatic approach in recognizing the evolving technology that is AI, though the lack of a definition might result in an over-inclusive approach. This approach is similar to the AAA-ICDR Guidelines and the CIETAC Guidelines, which do not put forth a definition of AI. While the VIAC Guidelines recognize the speed of development of emerging technology, and consequently expressly a loose description of AI to account for this, in our view this comes with both benefits and drawbacks. It is arguably more important to have a clearly identifiable definition of AI, for the purposes of the application of any rules or guidelines. The CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines, in particular, propose extensive sets of rules for parties to consider adopting, which permit the use of AI to be regulated during arbitral proceedings through their adoption. In circumstances where such guidelines are adopted by the parties, or their application is ordered by a tribunal, parties and their counsel would benefit from the clarity of knowing whether their tools may attract an enhanced level of oversight. Adopting the VIAC Guidelines approach opens the door to a greater degree of uncertainty, insofar that parties will not be aware of which tools they would need to subject to a higher standard of caution. In contrast, the definition proffered by the CIArb Guidelines is in our view likely to see the widest adoption in the realm of international arbitration. In our view, the definition builds on the guidelines that have come before whilst recognizing that various AIs provide different levels of capabilities. In turn, this captures the widest possible scope of AIs of all capabilities, thereby preventing issues over whether the guidelines should apply to less advanced AIs: 2. Due Process and Independence: The CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines provide the greatest degree of due process protection for parties, thereby ensuring the integrity of the arbitration.At its core, due process comprises the parties’ legitimate expectations to receive protection from arbitrary, discriminatory, or unfair treatment. In relation to AI specifically, the CIArb Guidelines recognize that the use of AI tools in arbitration: may give rise to due process issues including inequality of arms and significant discrepancies in parties’ respective ability to understand the case advanced against them. The use of an AI Tool as an aid in factual case analysis might impinge upon the right to present a party’s case and the implicit obligation of arbitrators to consider and address all of the parties’ arguments and exhibits, rather than a limited selection determined by an AI Tool. Use of an AI Tool as an aid to identify the relevant legal framework may also carry such risk.The issue of due process is further compounded by the fact that generative AI tends to face the ‘black box’ problem (a particularly thorny issue when generative AI is being used by the decision maker). By way of context, the ‘black box’ problem arises as a result of AI models that use opaque deep learning methodologies to generate output, which is problematic insofar that the reasoning undertaken by the AI model might not be accessible or comprehensible.16 See Lou Blouin, AI’s Mysterious ‘Black Box’ Problem, Explained (University of Michigan-Dearborn 6 Mar. 2023), https://umdearborn.edu/news/ais-mysterious-black-box-problem-explained (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). This is a point expressly recognized by the AOASA Guidelines which note that the ‘black box’ problem is in essence a situation in which AI Tools’ decision-making processes are opaque, hindering oversight and accountability.Naturally, this leads to questions of whether parties on the receiving end of an AI model’s decision have suffered from arbitrary, discriminatory, or unfair treatment, thereby violating their due process rights – particularly in cases where AI has been used to assist tribunals with the deliberation of issues. To elaborate, the importance of the role of the arbitrator and the reasoning behind the awards granted is critical. As put by philosopher Jurgen Habermas, a decision is only legitimate when it results from a legitimate and deliberative process which must be undertaken in a manner in which the problems have been clearly formulated and the way in which solutions have been assessed and evaluated are made public. The importance of reasoning is highlighted by the fact that: (1) reasoning is evidence of comprehension; (2) reasoning is evidence of care provided to the parties’ fundamental rights of due process; and (3) reasoning is a tool necessary for the relevant court to scrutinize the award. The use of AI within the scope of the various guidelines assists in ensuring that the concept of reasoning is not lost in the process of relying on AI. Although AI might assist the arbitrators with assessing facts or research, it is clear that the use of AI is not a substitute for the arbitrators’ critical thinking, reasoning, and ensuring due process. Fortunately, this is a reality recognized by several of the guidelines, such as the AAA-ICDR Guidelines, which take time to expressly highlight the importance of ADR professionals continuing to exercise independent judgment and the need to continue relying on their expertise, experience, and judgment.17 See Colin Rule, AAA ICDR Principles Supporting the Use of AI in Alternative Dispute Resolution (Arbitrate.com 30 Jan. 2024), https://arbitrate.com/aaa-icdr-principles-supporting-the-use-of-ai-in-alternative-dispute-resolution (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). To that end, the CIArb Guidelines have provided clear guidance on mitigating these potential due process issues surrounding AI usage. In particular, Guideline 9 of the CIArb Guidelines encourages arbitrators to be fully transparent in their use of any AI tool to which a similar position is adopted by the AOASA Guidelines at 10.3, which states that ‘AI usage should be transparent to all Parties involved’ and advises that they should inform parties of the use of any such tool. Crucially, Guideline 8.2 of the CIArb Guidelines highlights that: Arbitrators should not relinquish their decision-making powers to AI but may use AI to support more accurate and efficient processing of submitted information, always ensuring independent judgment … the Tribunal should avoid delegating any tasks to AI Tools, such as legal analysis, research and interpretation of facts and law, or application of the law to the facts, if such use could influence procedural or substantive decisions (emphasis added).In this regard, the CIArb Guidelines make it clear that arbitrators are required to continue applying their minds to the issues pleaded before them, and are not permitted to relinquish key decisions to their AI tools. This is an eminently sensible and clear position that protects parties’ rights to due process. The SVAMC Guidelines adopt a similar position as the CIArb Guidelines. Guideline 7 makes it clear that AI usage by arbitrators must respect due process. For reference, Guideline 7 states: An arbitrator shall not rely on AI-generated information outside the record without making appropriate disclosures to the parties beforehand and, as far as practical, allowing the parties to comment on it.Similarly to the CIArb Guidelines, the commentary on the SVAMC Guidelines makes it clear that the arbitrator has a clear duty to disclose reliance on any AI tools that might influence their understanding of the case, and goes a step further to highlight that parties must have the right to comment on decisions and ‘to be heard’. The SVAMC Guidelines also include clear guidance that arbitrators must not ‘delegate any part of their personal mandate to any AI tool’.18 See Guideline 6 of the SVAMC Guidelines. This serves as a helpful reminder that arbitral appointments are indeed personal mandates, which take careful account of the knowledge, experience, and judgment of the appointed arbitrators. Their execution of such mandates is not capable of being delegated and outsourced at will – either to any other individual, or to the collective assumed knowledge of an AI model. Next, both the CIArb Guidelines and the SVAMC Guidelines highlight that arbitrators should take the time to verify the outputs generated by any AI tools. In this regard, this is a sensible and necessary due process protection to safeguard against hallucination and, by extension, an egregious breach of party rights. The consequence of failing to prevent hallucination or overreliance on AI to generate decisions is significant, and may result in applications to set aside awards. A good example of this was highlighted recently in the application of John Lapaglia v. Valve Corporation (Valve), before the United States District Court in the Southern District of California, in which the applicant filed an application to vacate an arbitration award on the basis that the arbitrator had ‘outsourced his adjudicative role to Artificial Intelligence’.19 See en-john-lapaglia-v-valve-corporation-petition-to-vacate-arbitration-award-memorandum-of-points-and-authorities-in-support-thereof-tuesday-8th-april-2025. In that case, following the issuance of the final post-hearing brief on 23 Dec. 2024, the award was issued on 7 Jan. 2025 on the same day that the arbitrator was intended to travel to the Galapagos. In the application, the claimant argued that the arbitrator had used AI to adjudicate and draft the award on the basis that there were telltale signs of AI generation, the fact that the arbitrator was aiming to get the award out before his trip, and the awkward phrasing, incoherence, and generalization of the award that was indicative of AI generation. We foresee an increased number of such challenges going forward, as the use of AI in arbitral proceedings increases exponentially. For completeness, both the VIAC Guidelines (Guideline 2.1) and SCC Guidelines (page 3) likewise make it clear that arbitrators should not delegate key decision-making functions to AI tools, and must retain their independent analysis. A similar position is adopted by the CIETAC Guidelines which notes that: Before employing an AI tool, a tribunal must assess the necessity and proportionality of its use, weigh efficiency gains against the risks, evaluate the data security and accuracy of the specific AI tool, and understand the legal and regulatory landscape concerning AI in the relevant jurisdiction 20 See at 2 of the CIETACT Guidelines at sub-section ‘Precautions for Arbitral Tribunals’.Additionally, the CIETAC Guidelines make it clear that the tribunal must ‘independently analyse facts and laws and are required to independently provide reasoning and decisions’.21 See ibid. Overall, these guidelines adopt a clear stance that tribunals must not secede their independent decision making powers to AI. However, none of these set of guidelines goes as far as the CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines, to directly consider the impact of hallucination and necessary safeguards against it. The lack of clear guidance on this critical element poses the risk of another Valve situation arising. To summarize, the CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines are comprehensive in ensuring that the issue of due process rights in the age of AI is recognized and protected. Although the VIAC Guidelines and SCC Guidelines likewise recognize the importance of protecting due process rights, closer attention might need to be paid to issues of hallucination: 3. Disclosure of use of AI: In relation to party usage, the CIArb Guidelines provide the tribunal with the broadest discretion to order AI-related disclosure. However, the SVAMC Guidelines provide helpful clarity on how such disclosure should be effected. In respect of tribunal usage, the various guidelines provide that arbitrators themselves would need to disclose AI usage where appropriate.As a starting point, disclosure in the present case refers to the disclosure of parties’ use of various AI tools. In particular, parties might be required to disclose their use of the tools if there is an impact on the evidence, or outcome of the arbitration.22 See Guideline 7.1 of the CIArb Guidelines. In this regard, the CIArb Guidelines recognizes that the disclosure of an AI might be helpful insofar that such disclosure ensures the transparency of the arbitration. The rationale for such disclosure as noted by the CIArb Guidelines is to provide an opportunity for other arbitration participants to understand the manner and context in which AI has been used or is to be used.The CIArb Guidelines also empower the arbitrator to impose certain AI-related disclosure obligations on the parties, including on any party-appointed experts or factual witnesses, and to make directions as to the type of AI covered by the obligation to disclose.23 See Guideline 7.3 of the CIArb Guidelines. The CIArb Guidelines finally make it clear that in the event of failure to adhere to disclosure orders, the arbitrator is empowered to take steps to inquire. However, beyond a general ability to take steps, the CIArb Guidelines are, at present, only guidelines for parties and do not possess a clear set of consequences for failure to adhere. Nonetheless, the fact of the matter is that the CIArb Guidelines as a general rule seek to empower the tribunal to act where possible to ensure compliance. Although only time will tell whether any pattern of clear consequences for a breach of the CIArb Guidelines would arise, the discretion provided to tribunals would be a good starting point for ensuring proper compliance, seeing as parties are more likely than not to apply for the tribunal to exercise these powers should a breach by the opposing party become evident. In any case, the scope of the tribunal’s powers under the CIArb Guidelines is elaborated on in subsection 5 below. In comparison, the other guidelines are either silent or brief on the issue of disclosure of AI tools. As a starting point, the AAA-ICDR Guidelines are silent on the issue of disclosure. They only go as far as to highlight that ADR professionals should continue to exercise independent judgment. The SCC Guidelines are also noticeably silent on the need to disclose the use of AI tools, and the VIAC Guidelines make only brief mention to it. Guideline 5.1 of the VIAC Guidelines does recognize that: [the parties and/or the arbitrator] may wish to discuss in the case management conference, the potential use of AI in the proceedings, the requirement of disclosure as well as the potential impact of AI on the arbitration timeline and costs.The VIAC Guidelines also recognize the importance of disclosure beyond the mere usage of AI by the parties. The VIAC Guidelines also note that arbitrators should have the power to request disclosure of evidence provided by AI, or with the support of AI.24 See Guideline 6.1 of the VIAC Guidelines. Next, the CIETAC Guidelines likewise recognize the importance of disclosure and adopt a generalist approach towards it25 See at 2 of the CIETAC Guidelines at sub-section ‘Basic Principles’. With respect to party autonomy, the IA Guidelines provide that the parties are empowered to agree on the rules governing AI use. This includes deciding whether to permit, restrict, or entirely prohibit AI, as well as establishing protocols for disclosureIn comparison, the AOASA Guidelines adopt the position that disclosure is an issue to be covered by agreement or by the tribunal. To illustrate, paragraph 12.2 of the AOASA Guidelines state26 See para. 12.2 of the AOASA Guidelines.: The use of AI and AI Tools may be covered in the Agreement or clarified by the Tribunal with the Parties. The Tribunal, the Parties, their representatives or witnesses should consider whether it is appropriate to disclose any other use of AI and AI Tools, and the nature and extent of such useHowever, the SVAMC Guidelines address the issue of disclosure of AI tools in quite some detail. Guideline 3 of the SVAMC Guidelines state that: Disclosure that AI tools were used in connection with an arbitration is not necessary as a general matter. Decisions regarding disclosure of the use of AI tools shall be made on a case-by-case basis taking account of the relevant circumstances, including due process and any applicable privilege. Where appropriate, the following details may help reproduce and evaluate the output of an AI tool: 1. the name, version, and relevant settings of the tool used; 2. a short description of how the tool was used; and 3. the complete prompt (including any template, additional context, and conversation thread) and associated output (emphasis added).Similarly to the CIArb Guidelines, the SVAMC Guidelines recognize that disclosure of AI usage is ultimately to be considered and effected on a case-by-case basis, and does not constitute a mandatory requirement. This decision is justified in the commentary to the SVAMC guidelines, on the basis that in an ‘evolving landscape, defining a set of generally applicable criteria for disclosure of AI does not seem feasible and could create more controversy than it resolves’.27 See Guideline 3 of the SVAMC Guidelines. Another similarity to the CIArb Guidelines is the acknowledgment that disclosure might be appropriate in certain circumstances – in particular, where disclosure is required by professional rules or more broadly to avoid misleading other participants in the arbitration. In fact, the SVAMC Guidelines make it clear that any disputes concerning AI disclosure should be ‘resolved using the existing mechanisms for resolving disclosure disputes in the context of an arbitration, which, in most cases, is done by the tribunal under the procedural rules applicable to the arbitration’.28 See at 17 of the SVAMC Guidelines. Importantly, the commentary also highlights that where the specific outputs of an AI tool are deemed to warrant disclosure, such disclosure should, in principle, cover the information necessary to reproduce and evaluate such output. In the case of generative AI tools, the input parameters typically include the full conversation history and additional text submitted to the AI model along with the query. The same considerations would apply, mutatis mutandis, to the use of AI tools that are not ‘generative’ but rather evaluative, such as recommender or classification systems. The SVAMC Guidelines in fact adopt a pseudo-compromise, as compared with the SVAMC Consultation Draft, in which two options were provided. Option A required that factors such as the reliance on AI outputs ‘instead of primary sources and the material impact of AI on proceedings might necessitate disclosure’.29 See also Rabia Batool & Scarlet Tunney, California International Arbitration Week 2024: AI in ADR: Building Consensus Around The Path Forward (Kluwer Arbitration Blog, 12 May 2024), https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2024/05/12/california-international-arbitration-week-2024-ai-in-adr-building-consensus-around-the-path-forward (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). Option B adopted a stricter approach which required proactive disclosure when ‘AI-generated documents are materially relied upon or when AI has a significant effect on the outcome of the case’. However, there was widespread disagreement on the use of either option. This was because commentary highlighted that both options were unclear in providing for when disclosure would be needed, and would be difficult to apply in practice.30 See SVAMC Consultation Draft dated 31 Aug. 2023. In particular, a number of law firms which had already acted to integrate AI and other legal technology into their daily work practices disagreed with the obligation to disclose the use of AI.31 See Felix Kasseroler, From Draft to Published Version: A Commentary on the Changes Implemented in the SVAMC AI Guidelines (Kluwer Arbitration Blog 21 Jun. 2024), https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2024/06/21/from-draft-to-published-version-a-commentary-on-the-changes-implemented-in-the-svamc-ai-guidelines (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). Following such feedback, the SVAMC reworked the guidelines into the final version, extracts of which are reproduced above, and which emphasize that the need for disclosure is to be addressed on a case-by-case basis. Fortunately, where such disclosure is deemed necessary, the SVAMC Guidelines provide clear requirements for adequate disclosure. In particular, the SVAMC Guidelines make it clear that parties would need to provide information such as the name, version and relevant settings of the tool used; a short description of the specific use; and the complete prompt. In light of the above, we have prepared an illustrative table highlighting the extent of disclosure required, the timing of disclosure, and the form of disclosure. We feel may this table may assist readers in assessing the various disclosure obligations owed under each guideline. Guideline Appropriateness of Disclosure Timing of Disclosure Form of Disclosure AAA-ICDR Silent Silent Silent SVAMC Disclosure is not necessary as a general matter. Instead, disclosure is to be made on a case-by-case basis (see Guideline 3). Arbitrators relying on AI tools would need to make appropriate disclosures and allow parties to comment on it (see Guideline 7). Case-by-case basis (see Guideline 3) Disclosure should include: (1) the name, version, and relevant settings of the tool used; (2) a short description of how the tool was used; and (3) the complete prompt (including any template, additional context, and conversation thread) and associated output (see Guideline 3). SCC Silent Silent Silent CIArb Appropriateness to disclose is required to the extent that its use may have an impact on the evidence or the outcome of the arbitration, or otherwise involve a delegation of an express duty toward the arbitrators or any other party (see Article 7). Silent Silent. The sample Procedural Order on the Use of AI in Arbitration provides timelines for the disclosure of ‘High Risk AI Use’. VIAC Appropriateness has to be brought up by the parties at the case management proceedings (see paragraph 5.1). The arbitrator may request for the parties to disclose the use of AI as well (paragraph 6.1). Case Management Conference (see paragraph 5.1) Silent AOASA Appropriateness of disclosure is determined by way of party agreement or by the Tribunal (see paragraph 12.2). Silent. Timing of disclosure is likely to be determined by way of party agreement or Tribunal direction. Form of disclosure may be issued by way of formal written agreements (see paragraph 12.4). CIETAC Silent. Subject to the party’s agreement (see sub-section ‘Basic Principles’) Silent. Subject to the party’s agreement (see sub-section ‘Basic Principles’) Silent. Subject to the party’s agreement (see sub-section ‘Basic Principles’) 4. Confidentiality: Although the CIArb Guidelines protect parties’ confidentiality, best practices would need to be imported from the SVAMC Guidelines as well as prior CIArb Guidelines. Confidentiality is of central importance in international arbitration, and this element of the arbitral process helps make arbitration so attractive to parties.32 See Avinash Poorooye & Ronán Feehily, Confidentiality and Transparency in International Commercial Arbitration: Finding the Right Balance (Harvard Negotiation Law Review, vol. 22:275 Spring 2017), https://journals.law.harvard.edu/hnlr/wp-content/uploads/sites/91/HNR203_crop-1.pdf (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). However, the use of AI in arbitration poses substantial risks to confidentiality which is a point directly recognized by the CIETAC Guidelines.33 See at 2 of the CIETAC Guidelines at sub-section ‘Benefits and Risks of Using AI’. The reality of AI usage for legal proceedings is the inescapable fact that AIs need to be trained on large data sets which invariably include confidential documents. An example would be in a substantially large arbitration, such as an investor state arbitration, parties might be inundated with a large number of confidential documents. The reliance on an AI to sort those documents inevitably results in the AI being exposed and trained on a large number of sensitive materials. Fortunately, this is a reality that has been recognized by the various guidelines. As a starting point, the CIArb Guidelines at Guideline 2.2 highlights that: Inputting confidential data into a third-party AI Tool will inevitably raise concerns about how such data is stored, protected from outside access, used for machine learning purposes and potentially presented to third parties as output from their use of the same AI platform … it is important to consider whether the AI Tool has been vetted, has sufficient privacy and security standards, and whether it is an open or closed model.In addressing this issue, the CIArb Guidelines suggest that parties should consider the use of confidentiality agreements (see Guideline 2.2), and should redact or anonymize inputs (see Guideline 7.7 in Appendix A of the CIArb Guidelines). However, these suggestions do not go very far in suggesting a comprehensive set of confidentiality protections, designed to mitigate the risk of AI usage. A similar issue of a lack of specificity is highlighted in the SCC Guidelines, AAA-ICDR Guidelines, and VIAC Guidelines, which only recognize that parties and, by extension, the arbitral tribunal, should be ‘encouraged to inform themselves as to how any data input is employed and deployed when using AI’, and that parties ‘shall respect the confidentiality of the arbitral process as a whole and ensure that AI tools are compliant with any such confidentiality obligations … thereby take all reasonable steps to prevent unauthorised access to sensitive case-related data’, respectively. This leaves much to subjective interpretation. In this regard, the SVAMC Guidelines are instructive. Guideline 2 of the SVAMC Guidelines states: All participants in international arbitration are responsible for ensuring their use of AI tools is consistent with their obligations to safeguard confidential information (including privileged, private, secret, or otherwise protected data). They should not submit confidential information to any AI tool without appropriate vetting and authorisation. Special attention should be paid to policies on recording, storage, and use of prompt or output histories and of any other confidential data submitted to the AI tool . Only AI tools that adequately safeguard confidentiality should be used with confidential information. Participants should assess the data use and retention policies offered by available AI tools and opt for secure solutions. Where appropriate, participants should redact or anonymise materials submitted to an AI tool (emphasis added)The SVAMC Guidelines add additional clarity and guidance as to what tools and functions should be maintained in order to preserve confidentiality. In fact, the SVAMC Guidelines go further by highlighting that publicly available tools represent a risk of disclosing confidential information and that, as a matter of best practice, parties should assess the confidentiality policies, features, and limitations of the tool, engaging technical experts as appropriate.34 See SVMAC Guidelines at Commentary to Guideline 2 on, at 17. It is clear that the SVAMC Guidelines adopt a clearer position as to the technical protections that parties should consider. Next, the AOASA Guidelines likewise recognize the impact of AI on parties’ confidentiality. To that end, paragraph 12.3 states:35 See para. 12.3 of the AOASA Guidelines.
