Any party using AI Tools should ensure confidentiality and security is maintained throughout the Proceeding and for as long as is legally necessary thereafter. This may be achieved by: 12.3.1. Vetting providers of AI Tools thoroughly, including their privacy policies, information storage, and security measures; 12.3.2. Considering data sovereignty, including storage and processing locations; 12.3.3. Avoiding open and free AI Tools, preferring paid subscription services with enhanced privacy; 12.3.4. Anonymising information before inputting case materials; 12.3.5. Using secure AI Tools with appropriate access controls to prevent unauthorised access to confidential information; 12.3.6. Regularly reviewing updates to the AI Tools and their terms of use such as those relating to privacy;The AOASA Guidelines provide one of the most comprehensive overviews of key confidentiality protections for the use of AI in arbitration. In fact, the AOASA Guidelines directly adopt the technical considerations of the AI model and their impact on confidentiality. In a similar vein to the SVAMC Guidelines, the AOASA Guidelines are alive to the issue of relying on open and free AI tools and directly address this concern by recommending reliance on paid services with enhanced security. In this regard, the AOASA Guidelines and their emphasis on the technical specifications relating to confidentiality demonstrate that the drafters are alive to the concerns. Quite similarly to the SVAMC Guidelines, the AOASA Guidelines also place the onus of assessing the quality of tools on the parties themselves. Overall, the AOASA Guidelines on confidentiality are helpful insofar that they highlight key technical specifications that should be taken into account to ensure that AI does not breach confidentiality. Additionally, the 2021 CIArb Framework Guideline on the Use of Technology in International Arbitration (CIArb Technology Framework) is also instructive. The CIArb Technology Framework provides clear guidance on how to protect confidentiality with modern technology, much of which can be adapted to supplement current guidance as a matter of best practices. For example: (1) the use of passwords, encryptions, and multi-factor authentication when transmitting soft copies of confidential documents and data; and (2) the use of secure file sharing services to transfer and store confidential information.These practices are easily integrable in the use of AI tools, and are aligned with the SVAMC Guidelines. They would go the furthest in ensuring that the parties’ confidentiality and security are upheld and protected. The importance of security and confidentiality will continue to be a key focus for arbitration institutions in the digital age and it is clear that other best practices from around the world should also be considered when using AI. A good example is the SCIA Online Arbitration Rules (SCIA Online Rules) at Article 13, which recognizes that: [a]n arbitral tribunal may, in consideration of the results of examination of evidence, review and judge the authenticity of the generation, collection, storage and transmission of the electronic data, with particular focus on: (a) whether the computer systems and other hardware and software environment relied on for the generation, collection, storage, and transmission of the electronic data are secure and reliable ; (b) whether the electronic data have a clear generator and time of generation, and whether the electronic data present clear, objective, and accurate information ; (c) whether the electronic data have a clear storage and safekeeping medium and have been kept safe by appropriate means and methods ; (d) whether the entities, tools, and methods that extract and preserve the electronic data are reliable and whether the extraction process can be reproduced ; (e) whether the electronic data are incomplete or have been altered due to addition, deletion, or modification of information ; and (f) whether the electronic data may be verified through a specified form (emphasis added) In this regard, the SCIA Online Rules go an extra step, by suggesting a clear framework that is needed to ensure the security of data and confidentiality of the parties. The SCIA Online Rules are also forward-looking, insofar as they recognize that a tribunal may request or use its powers to engage third parties to verify the authenticity of the electronic data, or gather other related evidence for cross-check.36 See Art. 13(3) of the SCIA Online Rules. These would be important additional steps that tribunals relying on other guidelines, such as the CIArb Guidelines, may consider when dealing with AI: 5. Arbitrator Powers: The CIArb Guidelines provide the clearest guidance and empower the tribunal to exercise their powers to regulate the usage of AI by the parties in the arbitration to the fullest extent.The scope of a tribunal’s right to issue directions and procedural rulings on the use of AI in arbitration is essential. This is consistent with the maxim that the arbitrator is the master of its own procedure, and Article 19(2) of the Model Law, which allows the tribunal to ‘conduct the arbitration in such manner as it considers appropriate’.37 See Republic of India v. Vedanta Resources plc [2021] 2 SLR 354. In this regard, arbitrators would benefit from clear guidance as to when they may rule on the use of AI in arbitral proceedings. On this matter, the CIArb Guidelines provide arbitrators a great deal of discretion in giving directions and making procedural rulings. This is expressly recognized in Guideline 4.1 of the CIArb Guidelines, which states that: [t]he use of AI by parties falls within the general power of arbitrators to conduct the proceedings including giving directions and making procedural rulings, subject to any express prohibition agreed by the parties or flowing from mandatory applicable laws, regulations, policies, or institutional rules, to the extent that it may have an impact on the procedure (emphasis added).The CIArb Guidelines go further by highlighting that the tribunal itself is empowered to appoint AI experts to understand tools (see Guideline 4.2); order disclosure of use of the tools (see above as well as Guideline 4.4); and, most importantly, order consequences to follow (be it the drawing of adverse inferences or costs orders) (see Guideline 4.7). As a matter of procedure, the CIArb Guidelines also place it beyond doubt that, in the event of a disagreement between the parties, the arbitrator is empowered to make a ruling on the use of AI by the parties.38 See Guideline 6 of the CIArb Guidelines. Arbitrators are encouraged to take into account ‘any benefits of using the relevant AI Tool (including any cost and/or time saving) and any risks associated with such AI Tool including whether it may have an impact on the evidence, fairness including due process and equality of arms, and confidentiality’.39 See Guideline 6.3 of the CIArb Guidelines. In determining the input of AI tools, the arbitrators are also provided with guidance to: assess the extent to which source data for the machine-generated content is on the record. Where the link between evidential source material and the model output is not clear, and where that link is a relevant factor in assessing whether the content should be admitted, arbitrators may seek submissions from the parties to explain how inputs from the record are linked to machine-generated outputs. 40 See Guideline 6.6 of the CIArb Guidelines.Finally, tribunals are also encouraged to consider the training sets used for the AI models that are being relied on by the parties.41 See Guideline 6.5 of the CIArb Guidelines. In this regard, the CIArb Guidelines represent a comprehensive set of guides and best practices that arbitrators should consider when faced with issues of AI usage. The rules are clear, extensive, and empower the tribunal to make critical decisions on their usage. In comparison, other arbitral institutional guidelines have paid considerably less attention to this critical matter. The SVAMC Guidelines do not go as far as the CIArb Guidelines in prescribing an extensive list of powers that arbitrators have to manage the use of AI. Instead, the SVAMC Guidelines take a broad approach, cautioning arbitrators on the use of AI tools, and reiterating the prudent approach that should be taken when considering the use of AI as part of the deliberation process. In this regard, the SVAMC Guidelines do not go as far as empowering the tribunal with the power to rule on the usage of AI. However, the commentary to Guideline 5 of the SVAMC Guidelines does make clear that, in the event that parties breach the guidelines, the tribunal would be empowered to consider taking measures available under the governing law, drawing adverse inferences, or making adverse cost orders. Likewise, the AOASA Guidelines do not delve into as much detail as the CIArb Guidelines. Instead, the AOASA Guidelines are more general in comparison. Specifically, paragraph 12.1 of the AOASA Guidelines places the onus on the tribunal to assess whether the use of AI in the proceedings should be allowed. By way of example, the AOASA Guidelines state that ‘[b]efore using AI Tools … Tribunals should establish whether the use of AI is permissible. Tribunals should review the Agreement, consider applicable law, consult Parties if the Agreement is silent, document consensus if agreed, and ensure AI use is permissible’.42 See para. 12.1 of the AOASA Guidelines. In this regard, the AOASA Guidelines adopt a framework approach which instructs tribunals to review the various written agreements first before consulting the parties. This pseudo framework approach ensures that the tribunal has a principled basis for assessing the use of AI in arbitration. Interestingly, the tribunal under the AOASA Guidelines plays a comparatively facilitative role in the adoption of AI and ultimately places the question of permissibility in the parties’ hands and their ability to agree on its use. Further, the SCC Guidelines do not highlight specific rules allowing the arbitrator to rule on the parties’ use of AI. Similar to the SVAMC Guidelines, the SCC Guidelines only go as far as to highlight that the use of AI must uphold confidentiality, quality, integrity, and non-delegation of decision-making powers. A similar position is adopted by the AAA-ICDR Guidelines and the CIETAC Guidelines. In particular, the CIETAC Guidelines only recognize that the tribunal would need to ‘assess the necessity and proportionality of its use, weigh efficiency gains against the risks, evaluate the data security and accuracy of the specific AI tool, and understand the legal and regulatory landscape concerning AI in the relevant jurisdiction’.43 See ‘Precautions for Arbitral Tribunals’, supra n. 20, at 2. In this regard, the SCC Guidelines, AAA-ICDR Guidelines, and the CIETAC Guidelines are comparatively general and do not delve into any specifics. The VIAC Guidelines go further than the SVAMC Guidelines and the SCC Guidelines, to recognize that the use of AI should be discussed at relevant case management conferences, and agreement amongst the parties as to its usage should be sought and adopted.44 See Guidelines 5.1 and 5.2 of the VIAC Guidelines. In this regard, the VIAC Guidelines are closer to the CIArb Guidelines, as they explicitly recognize the role that arbitrators have to play in determining the use of AI. In particular, the VIAC Guidelines make note of the possibility of needing arbitrators and parties to come to an agreement in the procedural orders for the use of AI in the proceedings,45 See Guidelines 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 of the VIAC Guidelines. which practice we expect will eventually become commonplace, until such time as arbitral institutions adopt express rules to address the use of AI. In our view, the CIArb Guidelines, which provide the most extensive guidance for arbitrators, should be a point of reference for any arbitrator who is asked to consider the usage of any AI. Most importantly, the CIArb Guidelines put beyond doubt the notion that arbitrators have the power to make procedural rulings on the matter and make directions as needed. 6. Parties’ Usage: the SVAMC Guidelines place the onus on parties to respect the integrity of arbitration proceedings in using AI.As a starting point, there is no uniform code of conduct or global standard practice for party conduct in international arbitration.46 See Raid Abu-Manneh & Lisa Dubot, The Conduct of the Parties in International Arbitration: The Current State of Play (International Bar Association 21 Oct. 2022), https://www.ibanet.org/conduct-of-parties-in-international-arbitration (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). Instead, legal representatives’ practices would likely be shaped or governed by a variety of different sources, including the regulatory authorities in the jurisdiction in which they are licensed; any relevant national laws; and any institutional guidelines or rules.47 See Guideline 6.5 of the CIArb Guidelines, supra n. 41. Fortunately, both the CIArb Guidelines and the SVAMC Guidelines provide clear guidance for parties who are considering the use of AI – although the SVAMC Guidelines go further and place it beyond doubt that parties bear the burden of ensuring responsible AI usage. The CIArb Guidelines make it clear that, as a starting point, parties have the right to agree on whether and how AI may be used by parties in the arbitration.48 See Guideline 5.1 of the CIArb Guidelines. This aligns with fundamental tenets of international arbitration as a voluntary, consensual process, recognizing party autonomy and, where relevant, the contractual relationship formed not only with their counterparties in the proceedings, but also the administering institution. Going further, the CIArb Guidelines allow for parties to discuss the use of AI in the proceedings.49 See Guideline 5.4 of the CIArb Guidelines. However, beyond these points, the CIArb Guidelines do not provide further elaboration. Understandably, more attention is placed on the arbitrator’s right to decide whether, and when, AI usage should be permitted. In this regard, the SCC Guidelines likewise do not place a great emphasis on the parties’ usage of AI tools. Instead, the majority of the SCC Guidelines focus heavily on the duty of the tribunal in using AI. In comparison, the VIAC Guidelines do make note of the fact that parties have a role in responsible AI usage. In comparison to the above, a more general approach is adopted by the AAA-ICDR Guidelines, which suggest only a general set of principles for the tribunal and the parties. Quite similarly, the AOASA Guidelines place a greater emphasis on ensuring that both the tribunal and the parties have a duty to consider the core principles of accountability, confidentiality, transparency, and fair decision-making in the use of AI tools. In particular, the AOASA Guidelines recognize that both the tribunal and the parties’ usage of AI tools should be subject to a degree of oversight. As a final note, the decision to use AI under the AOASA Guidelines rests primarily in the parties’ agreement with the tribunal having a comparatively more limited role in allowing the usage of AI. A similar approach to the AOASA Guidelines is also taken by the CIETAC Guidelines. In particular, the CIETAC Guidelines recognize that both parties and tribunals have a role to play in managing AI usage in arbitration. In this regard, the CIETAC Guidelines provide a checklist of recommended actions to mitigate the risk of AI50 See at 2 of the CIETAC Guidelines at sub-section ‘Recommended Risk Mitigation Measures’.: Parties to include stipulations regarding AI usage in their arbitration agreements. Tribunals to invite the parties to express their views on the use of AI in the procedural order or pre-hearing conference.Interestingly, the CIETAC Guidelines eschew a more comprehensive set of principles on party usage. The CIETAC has instead adopted a position that the CIETAC Guidelines are a starting point and should not prescribed a set of detailed and restrictive rules.51 See ibid. Regardless, what is clear is that the majority of the Guidelines place greater emphasis on the tribunal’s usage, as compared to the parties’ usage – most likely, a recognition of the potential for setting aside proceedings, in the event that the tribunal does not act consistently with critical principles of due process and natural justice. However, the SVAMC Guidelines go further by highlighting that parties should have a duty of competence in the use of AI (Guideline 4), and must respect the integrity of the proceedings and the evidence (Guideline 5), to ensure the ethical and responsible use of AI. We elaborate below. First, Guideline 4 of the SVAMC Guidelines states: Party representatives shall observe any applicable ethical rules or professional standards of competent or diligent representation when using AI tools in the context of an arbitration. Parties shall review the output of any AI tool used to prepare submissions to verify it is accurate from a factual and legal standpoint. Parties and party representatives on record shall be deemed responsible for any uncorrected errors or inaccuracies in any output produced by an AI tool they use in an arbitration.Guideline 4 recognizes that party representatives and parties themselves should avoid delegating key tasks without the proper review. In this regard, the commentary to the SVAMC Guidelines makes it clear that, when using AI tools, party representatives still owe a professional and ethical duty to review any work product created by AI.52 See SVAMC Guidelines, at 18. The SVAMC Guidelines make it clear that, ultimately, Guideline 4 does not impose an independent standard of review of party representatives’ conduct. Rather, it contains renvoi to any applicable rules of professional conduct or responsibility, to determine the level of diligence and reasonableness required when using AI tools. Therefore, the party on record will ultimately be deemed responsible for any non-compliance with the SVAMC’s Guideline 4. The position taken by the SVAMC is therefore one of cautious guidance, without seeking to impose a strict set of rules that parties would be required to abide by. Another important piece of guidance is contained in Guideline 5 of the SVAMC Guidelines, which requires that the parties must respect the integrity of the proceedings and the evidence. The SVAMC Guidelines take a firm stance that the parties continue to have a duty not to mislead the tribunal. Specifically, the commentary to the SVAMC Guidelines takes the view that ‘[p]arties, party representatives and experts should simply not use AI tools to fabricate evidence, distort evidence, or compromise the integrity of the proceedings under any circumstances’. In such circumstances, the SVAMC Guidelines take the position that, in the event of a breach, the tribunal may consider, in addition to any other measures available under the governing law and the applicable arbitration rules or the lex arbitri, deriving adverse inferences, and taking the infringing party representatives’ conduct into account, in its allocation of the costs of the arbitration. In our view, the SVAMC Guidelines and their suggestions on the best practices for party usage should be applied and considered by various tribunals where appropriate.

sections.title: THE FUTURE OF AI GUIDELINES IN INTERNATIONAL ARBITRATION
sections.text: 5 THE FUTURE OF AI GUIDELINES IN INTERNATIONAL ARBITRATION Within the space of just under two years, since the time of publication of the SVAMC Consultation Draft, we have seen a significant evolution in the development of AI guidelines for use in international arbitration. This is perhaps unsurprising, given the rapid increase in general usage of AI, and its undoubted increased adoption in the sphere of dispute resolution and international arbitration. We expect to see further innovations and the publication of updated guidelines in this space going forward, and also the adoption of rules around the ethical and responsible use of AI appearing in arbitral institutional rules, and perhaps even national legislation. The CIArb Guidelines represent the culmination of AI guidance in arbitration up to the present date. Both they and the SVAMC Guidelines are extremely helpful tools for both parties and tribunals. The SCC Guidelines and the SVAMC Guidelines were both published in 2024, while the CIArb Guidelines, VIAC Guidelines, and AOASA Guidelines were published in March, April, and May 2025 respectively. It is clear that the CIArb Guidelines have adopted and improved on many of the provisions provided in the predecessor guidelines in several ways. For example, the rules on disclosure of AI usage clearly show a degree of development from the SVAMC Guidelines, through the introduction of clear consequences of failing to disclose. However, the CIArb Guidelines continue to have several gaps that may be filled through reference to other guidelines – for example, on the issue of whether parties bear a burden for ensuring the ethical and responsible use of AI tools, where the SVAMC Guidelines are comprehensive. In any case, the continued development of relevant AI guidelines is a practical requirement, as AI tools themselves develop. Parties will likely only see their technology usage increase as greater and more efficient tools are used. The use of AI tools continues to pose several challenges, which are likely to be exacerbated in the event that guidelines are not adhered to. For example, arbitrators who rely on AI to generate parts of their awards might well find them being set aside on the basis of a breach of natural justice. In particular, situations where the use of AI results in awards which contain incomplete reasoning, omissions of key facts, and hallucinated arguments or sources would likely constitute a breach of natural justice. A close (though imperfect) analogy is in the recent case of DJP & DJO, in which the Singapore Court of Appeal held that an arbitration award prepared by the former Chief Justice of India should be set aside, on the basis that the award had been prepared by his copying and pasting significant chunks from other awards, plainly without consideration of the facts at hand.53 See DJO v. DJO [2025] SGCA(I) 2. In such cases, where an arbitrator has failed to adequately use their mind in preparing awards, the Singapore courts (and other courts around the world) would ‘not hesitate to set aside awards that are proven to fall below the requisite standards of natural justice’.54 See Aaron Tan Kai Ran & Ryan Baptista, The Need to Secure Trust and Accountability in Arbitration: DJO v. DJP or the ‘Copy and Paste’ Case (Kluwer Arbitration Blog 6 Dec. 2024), https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2024/12/06/the-need-to-secure-trust-and-accountability-in-arbitration-djo-v-djp-or-the-copy-and-paste-case (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). This is clearly consistent with the need to ‘enforce minimum standards of conduct’ in arbitration proceedings.55 See Guideline 6.5 of the CIArb Guidelines, supra n. 47. As Chief Justice Sundaresh Menon of the Supreme Court of Singapore noted in his extrajudicial speech at the Singapore International Arbitration Centre Annual India Conference 2024: trust can no longer be taken for granted as an inherent feature of arbitration; […] there is a pressing need for all stakeholders in the arbitration community to collectively and proactively act in the endeavour to secure trust in arbitration. These words are relevant in the age of AI, especially where there are numerous generative AI models that are theoretically capable of generating high-quality submissions, and even arbitral awards. However, the various Guidelines make it clear that, although such innovations are helpful, they must not undermine the critical importance of the integrity of the arbitration process. Thus, the presence of AI guidelines is a welcome and helpful innovation for arbitrations in the age of AI. After all, AI represents both unlimited transformational potential with similarly unlimited risks. Recognizing that both concerns co-exist and how to balance them is key. As the G7 leaders recently highlighted56 See G7 Leaders’ Statement on the Hiroshima AI Process (30 Oct. 2023), https://www.mofa.go.jp/files/100573466.pdf (accessed 8 Jul. 2025).: We, the Leaders of the Group of Seven (G7), stress the innovative opportunities and transformative potential of advanced Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems, in particular, foundation models and generative AI. We also recognize the need to manage risks and to protect individuals, society, and our shared principles including the rule of law and democratic values, keeping humankind at the center.

sections.title: ANNEX 1: COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS OF AI GUIDELINES ON KEY ISSUES57 The AAA-ICDR Guidelines are notably general in nature as compared to the other guidelines. For instance, the AAA-ICDR Guidelines are entirely silent on the issue of disclosure and do not provide specific guidance on the appropriateness, timing, or form of disclosure required when AI is employed. Compared to other guidelines, the AAA-ICDR Guidelines offer a much more generic set of principles for both tribunals and parties, leaving considerable room for subjective interpretation and application by both the parties and the tribunal. Due to the high-level approach adopted by the AAA-ICDR Guidelines, the rules have been omitted from Annex 1 which seeks to distil the key applicable rules in each situation.
sections.text: 6 ANNEX 1: COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS OF AI GUIDELINES ON KEY ISSUES57 The AAA-ICDR Guidelines are notably general in nature as compared to the other guidelines. For instance, the AAA-ICDR Guidelines are entirely silent on the issue of disclosure and do not provide specific guidance on the appropriateness, timing, or form of disclosure required when AI is employed. Compared to other guidelines, the AAA-ICDR Guidelines offer a much more generic set of principles for both tribunals and parties, leaving considerable room for subjective interpretation and application by both the parties and the tribunal. Due to the high-level approach adopted by the AAA-ICDR Guidelines, the rules have been omitted from Annex 1 which seeks to distil the key applicable rules in each situation. Category CIArb Guidelines SVAMC Guidelines SCC Guidelines VIAC Guidelines AOASA Guidelines CIETAC Guidelines Definition of AI Preamble The Preamble states: ‘machine-based system that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments. Different AI systems vary in their levels of autonomy and adaptiveness after deployment’. Preliminary Provisions The Preliminary Provisions state: ‘the term “AI” refers to computer systems that perform tasks commonly associated with human cognition, such as understanding natural language, recognising complex semantic patterns, and generating human-like outputs’. Adopting EU AI Act definition Page 1 at the section titled ‘AI and its uses’ states: ‘machine-based system that is designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy and that may exhibit adaptiveness after deployment, and that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers, from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments’ and ‘AI systems are capable of adapting their behaviour to a certain degree by analysing the effects of previous actions and working autonomously’. No definition adopted Page 2 at the section titled ‘Applicability’ states: ‘While there are numerous definitions of AI (including Generative AI) published by various organisations and bodies across various industries, VIAC does not subscribe to a single definition. This is due to the speed at which AI-derived tools are proliferating, not least in the legal field. As such, a fixed definition would likely not cover the broadening range of tools that will be used in the near future’. Paragraph 3 Paragraph 3 at the section titled ‘Definitions’ states: ‘shall mean non-human power capable of performing tasks traditionally requiring human cognitive functions and human intelligence, such as sequencing and collating facts, summarising such facts, reasoning, learning, problem-solving, perception, and decision-making’. Silent Due Process and Independence Guideline 8 and 9 Guideline 8.2 provides that arbitrators should ‘avoid delegating any tasks to AI Tools, such as legal analysis, research and interpretation of facts and law, or application of the law to the facts, if such use could influence procedural or substantive decisions’. Guideline 8.3 further elaborates that ‘[a]rbitrators should also independently verify the accuracy and correctness of information obtained through AI, ensuring their judgment is free from confirmation bias and other distortions. They should conduct their own research, using AI-generated information as a supportive tool, while maintaining a critical perspective to prevent undue influence on their decisions, including through appropriate supervision’. Please also see Guidelines 8.1 and 8.4. Guideline 9.1 requires that arbitrators consult with the parties on the use of any AI tools by the arbitrators. Guideline 7 Guideline 7 requires that the arbitrator respect due process. It states: ‘An arbitrator shall not rely on AI-generated information outside the record without making appropriate disclosures to the parties beforehand and, as far as practical, allowing the parties to comment on it. Where an AI tool cannot cite sources that can be independently verified, an arbitrator shall not assume that such sources exist or are characterised accurately by the AI tool’. Guideline at page 3 The Guideline states: ‘AI tools may be used to support arbitral decision-making but cannot replace it. Arbitral Tribunals cannot delegate the decision or the reasoning leading to it to anyone or anything’ and ‘The integrity of arbitration is essential to its continued success. Transparency and accountability are key components of integrity. Arbitral Tribunals are therefore encouraged to disclose any use of AI in researching and interpreting facts and the law or applying the law to facts. Such disclosure may assist in ensuring the parties’ rights to be heard, and that the Arbitral Tribunal does not exceed its mandate’. Guideline 2 Guideline 2.1 states: ‘Arbitrators retain full control over the decision-making process. While AI can provide insights, and assist with certain tasks, arbitrators must retain full control over any decision that might have an impact on the proceedings and shall not delegate any such decision to an AI tool’, and ‘The use of AI tools by the arbitrator shall not substitute their independent analysis of the relevant factual and legal issues. AI tools may only be used to facilitate such analysis, not to replace it’. Not expressly identified However, relevant paragraphs pointing towards the recognition of due process and independence include: paragraph 10.1 which states: ‘Accountability: Tribunals are accountable for all aspects of Proceedings including the outcome. AI Tools do not substitute human judgment and analysis’. Paragraph 14.1 states: ‘Any AI-generated information must undergo independent verification before inclusion in decisions’. Sub-section ‘Precautions for Arbitra Tribunals’ The Guideline states: ‘Before employing an AI tool, a tribunal must assess the necessity and proportionality of its use, weigh efficiency gains against the risks, evaluate the data security and accuracy of the specific AI tool, and understand the legal and regulatory landscape concerning AI in the relevant jurisdiction. The guidelines emphasise that AI use must not infringe upon a party’s right to be heard and that tribunals must independently analyse facts and laws and are required to independently provide reasoning and Decisions Contained In The award’ Disclosure of usage Guideline 7 Guideline 7.1 requires that disclosure is needed ‘to the extent that its use may have an impact on the evidence, the outcome of the arbitration or otherwise involve a delegation of an express duty toward the arbitrators or any other party’. Guideline 7.2 states that the ‘[d]isclosure of the use of AI may be required to ensure transparency over its use and ultimately preserve the integrity of the arbitration and/or the validity and enforceability of the award’. Guideline 7.3 empowers the arbitrator to ‘impose certain AI-related disclosure obligations on the parties including any party-appointed experts or factual witness. In this context, arbitrators may make directions as to the type of AI covered by the obligation to disclose, circumstances in which disclosure is required, to whom disclosure is to be made and within which timeframe’. Please also refer to Guideline 7.4 (duty of continuous disclosure); Guideline 7.5 (arbitrator powers to consider any inconsistencies); Guideline 7.6 (arbitrator power to inquire upon failure to disclose); and Guideline 7.7 (measures to be taken upon failure). Guideline 3 Guideline 3 states: ‘Disclosure that AI tools were used in connection with an arbitration is not necessary as a general matter. Decisions regarding disclosure of the use of AI tools shall be made on a case-by-case basis taking account of the relevant circumstances, including due process and any applicable privilege. Where appropriate, the following details may help reproduce and evaluate the output of an AI tool: 1. the name, version, and relevant settings of the tool used; 2. a short description of how the tool was used; and 3. the complete prompt (including any template, additional context, and conversation thread) and associated output’. Silent Guideline 5 Guideline 5.1 states: ‘Arbitrators shall, within their discretion and where they consider necessary, facilitate the parties’ and any third parties’ (e.g., experts, court reporters) understanding and use of AI tools. They may wish to discuss in the case management conference, the potential use of AI in the proceedings, the requirement of disclosure as well as the potential impact of AI on the arbitration timeline and costs’. Guideline 5.2 allows the arbitrator to disclose their own use of the tool. Guideline 5.3 allows the arbitrators and the parties to come to an agreement on the use of the tool. Paragraph 12.2 Paragraph 12.2 states: ‘The use of AI and AI Tools may be covered in the Agreement or clarified by the Tribunal with the Parties. The Tribunal, the Parties, their representatives or witnesses should consider whether it is appropriate to disclose any other use of AI and AI Tools, and the nature and extent of such use’. Sub-section ‘Basic Principles’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines are built on the three principles of party autonomy, auxiliary, and good faith. With respect to party autonomy, the IA Guidelines provide that the parties are empowered to agree on the rules governing AI use. This includes deciding whether to permit, restrict, or entirely prohibit AI, as well as establishing protocols for disclosure. The auxiliary principle states that AI’s role is assistive, with the tribunal responsible for the final award. The good faith principle ensures parties remain responsible for the truthfulness and legality of their submissions’ Confidentiality Guideline 2.2 Guideline 2.2 states: ‘Confidentiality: The use of third-party AI entails substantial risks in regard to confidentiality, which is a fundamental prerequisite in the realm of arbitration. Inputting confidential data into a third-party AI Tool will inevitably raise concerns about how such data is stored, protected from outside access, used for machine learning purposes and potentially presented to third parties as output from their use of the same AI platform. Real-world AI applications can use multiple tools and platforms, which may make it difficult to identify which data is being used and where it is stored. It’s important to carefully consider the context in which the relevant data was given and whether permission has been obtained to use it for purposes beyond resolving the dispute. The use of confidentiality agreements and a deeper understanding of how such tools protect confidentiality from misuse or cyber attacks will be necessary. When evaluating and considering AI Tools, it is important to consider whether the AI Tool has been vetted, has sufficient privacy and security standards, and whether it is an open or closed model. Not all AI Tools are equal in this regard’. Guideline 2 Guideline 2 states: ‘All participants in international arbitration are responsible for ensuring their use of AI tools is consistent with their obligations to safeguard confidential information (including privileged, private, secret, or otherwise protected data). They should not submit confidential information to any AI tool without appropriate vetting and authorisation. Special attention should be paid to policies on recording, storage, and use of prompt or output histories and of any other confidential data submitted to the AI tool. Only AI tools that adequately safeguard confidentiality should be used with confidential information. Participants should assess the data use and retention policies offered by available AI tools and opt for secure solutions. Where appropriate, participants should redact or anonymise materials submitted to an AI tool’. Guideline at page 3 The Guideline states: ‘Confidentiality: Many users value arbitration’s ability to respect accords on confidentiality. The use of some AI tools may have unintended consequences for the confidentiality of an arbitration. Participants, in particular the Arbitral Tribunal, are therefore encouraged to inform themselves as to how any data input is employed and deployed when using AI’. Guideline 3 Guideline 3 states: ‘3.1. Arbitrators and parties shall respect the confidentiality of the arbitral process as a whole and ensure that AI tools are compliant with any such confidentiality obligations. 3.2. Arbitrators and parties shall thereby take all reasonable steps to prevent unauthorised access to sensitive case-related data’. Paragraph 12.3 Paragraph 12.3 states: ‘Any party using AI Tools should ensure confidentiality and security is maintained throughout the Proceeding and for as long as is legally necessary thereafter. This may be achieved by: 12.3.1 Vetting providers of AI Tools thoroughly, including their privacy policies, information storage, and security measures; 12.3.2 Considering data sovereignty, including storage and processing locations; 12.3.3 Avoiding open and free AI Tools, preferring paid subscription services with enhanced privacy; 12.3.4 Anonymising information before inputting case materials; 12.3.5 Using secure AI Tools with appropriate access controls to prevent unauthorised access to confidential information; 12.3.6 Regularly reviewing updates to the AI Tools and their terms of use such as those relating to privacy’. Silent Arbitrators’ powers Guideline 4 Guideline 4.1 states: ‘The use of AI by parties falls within the general power of arbitrators to conduct the proceedings including giving directions and making procedural rulings, subject to any express prohibition agreed by the parties or flowing from mandatory applicable laws, regulations, policies, or institutional rules, to the extent that it may have an impact on the procedure’. The tribunal is empowered to appoint AI experts to understand tools (see Guideline 4.2), order disclosure of use of the tools (see above as well as Guideline 4.4), and most importantly order consequences to follow (be it the drawing of adverse inferences or costs orders) (see Guideline 4.7). The tribunal is also empowered to regulate the use of AI (see guideline 4.3), and is encouraged to record any decision on the use of AI in a procedural order (see guideline 4.6). Guideline 4.3 states: ‘Arbitrators may regulate the use of AI by parties with a view to preserve the integrity of arbitral proceedings which they oversee and ensure the validity and enforceability of any ensuing awards’. See also Guideline 6, which looks into the arbitrator’s ability to rule on the use of AI and admissibility of AI-generated material in the arbitration record. No specific powers prescribed Commentary to Guideline 5 states: ‘The duty is to protect the integrity of the proceedings, not to mislead the arbitral tribunal, and not to submit false or adulterated evidence. Fraudulent behaviour and misconduct, such as submitting false documents or resorting to so-called “guerilla tactics”, can occur with or without the use of AI. Advancements in AI, however, particularly in generative AI and deep fakes, can heighten the risks of manipulated or false evidence, making it significantly easier to create fake evidence that can appear strikingly convincing to the naked eye or that can sometimes be virtually indistinguishable from authentic versions. It can also make it more costly or difficult to detect any such manipulation through forensic and other means. This Guideline reminds parties to be aware and vigilant of these heightened risks while emphasising the importance of ensuring the fairness and integrity of the proceedings when using AI. Parties, party representatives and experts should simply not use AI tools to fabricate evidence, distort evidence, or compromise the integrity of the proceedings under any circumstances. If the arbitral tribunal determines that a party has violated this Guideline, it may consider, in addition to any other measures available under the governing law and the applicable arbitration rules or the lex arbitri (such as, for example, striking the evidence from the record, or deeming it inadmissible), deriving adverse inferences, and taking the infringing party representatives’ conduct into account in its allocation of the costs of the arbitration’. No specific powers prescribed Guideline 5 Guideline 5.1 states: ‘Arbitrators shall, within their discretion and where they consider necessary, facilitate the parties’ and any third parties’ (e.g., experts, court reporters) understanding and use of AI tools. They may wish to discuss in the case management conference, the potential use of AI in the proceedings, the requirement of disclosure as well as the potential impact of AI on the arbitration timeline and costs’. Guideline 5.2 states: ‘Arbitrators may, within their discretion and where they consider relevant and necessary, inform the parties if they plan to use certain AI tools. The arbitrators may wish to inform the parties of the name of the AI tool and the tasks it will be employed for. The parties shall be provided with an opportunity to comment on such information’. Guideline 5.3 states: ‘Where applicable, the arbitrators and the parties may wish to consider coming to an agreement on provisions related to confidentiality and the transparent use of AI, e.g., in Procedural Order No. 1’. No specific powers prescribed Sub-section ‘Recommended Risk Mitigation Measures’ and ‘Precautions for Arbitral Tribunals’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines provide a checklist of recommended actions for: • Parties to include stipulations regarding AI usage in their arbitration agreements. • Tribunals to invite the parties to express their views on the use of AI in the procedural order or pre-hearing conference. • Using CIETAC’s own platform for services like electronic case filing and AI-powered transcription. • Engaging in training and education to better understand AI’s capabilities, limitations, and the associated legal developments’ At the sub-section titled ‘Precautions for Arbitral Tribunals’ ‘Before employing an AI tool, a tribunal must assess the necessity and proportionality of its use, weigh efficiency gains against the risks, evaluate the data security and accuracy of the specific AI tool, and understand the legal and regulatory landscape concerning AI in the relevant jurisdiction. The guidelines emphasise that AI use must not infringe upon a party’s right to be heard and that tribunals must independently analyse facts and laws and are required to independently provide reasoning and decisions contained in the award’ Parties’ usage Guideline 5 Guideline 5.1 states: ‘Subject to any limitation or prohibition flowing from applicable laws, regulations or policies, institutional rules, or the arbitration agreement, the parties may by their agreement exercise their autonomy to agree whether and how AI may be used by parties in the arbitration. The parties may also propose specific AI Tools that may or may not be utilised or agree on any limits thereof’. Guideline 5.2 states: ‘When the arbitrator receives a request for arbitration, it should ascertain whether and how the parties provided for the use of AI in their arbitration agreement’. Guideline 5.3 states: ‘In case the arbitration agreement is silent or unclear on the use of AI, and the parties have not raised the issue in their initial communications with the institution or the arbitrators, the arbitrators are encouraged to invite the parties to express their views on the subject at the appropriate time (either at the first case management conference or later)’. Guideline 5.4 states: ‘The parties may discuss the subject of the use of AI. Although, the arbitrators may intervene in the discussion to clarify what AI Tools or classes of tools may be available to the parties, how they could be used in the course of the arbitral proceedings, any risks thereof (e.g., as to accuracy, privacy etc. ), and any other issues of that the arbitrators or the institution consider the parties should be aware’. Guidelines 4 and 5 Guideline 4 states: ‘Party representatives shall observe any applicable ethical rules or professional standards of competent or diligent representation when using AI tools in the context of an arbitration. Parties shall review the output of any AI tool used to prepare submissions to verify it is accurate from a factual and legal standpoint. Parties and party representatives on record shall be deemed responsible for any uncorrected errors or inaccuracies in any output produced by an AI tool they use in an arbitration’. Guideline 5 states: ‘Parties, party representatives, and experts shall not use any forms of AI in ways that affect the integrity of the arbitration or otherwise disrupt the conduct of the proceedings. Parties, party representatives and experts shall not use any form of AI to falsify evidence, compromise the authenticity of evidence, or otherwise mislead the arbitral tribunal and/or opposing party(ies)’. Silent Guideline 5 Guideline 5.2 states: ‘The arbitrators may wish to inform the parties of the name of the AI tool and the tasks it will be employed for. The parties shall be provided with an opportunity to comment on such information’. Guideline 5.3 states: ‘Where applicable, the arbitrators and the parties may wish to consider coming to an agreement on provisions related to confidentiality and the transparent use of AI, e.g., in Procedural Order No. 1’. Paragraph 12.4 Paragraph 12.4 states: ‘Formal Agreements: The Tribunal and Parties should consider adopting written agreements to address issues such as: 12.4.1 Permitted uses of AI Tools; 12.4.2 Limitations on the use of AI Tools; 12.4.3 Disclosure obligations; 12.4.4 Confidentiality and other safeguards; 12.4.5 The Tribunal’s rights of investigation and direction in respect to the use of AI Tools’. Sub-section ‘Recommended Risk Mitigation Measures’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines provide a checklist of recommended actions for: • Parties to include stipulations regarding AI usage in their arbitration agreements. • Tribunals to invite the parties to express their views on the use of AI in the procedural order or pre-hearing conference. • Using CIETAC’s own platform for services like electronic case filing and AI-powered transcription. • Engaging in training and education to better understand AI’s capabilities, limitations, and the associated legal developments’ Understanding the risks of AI usage See generally Guideline 2, which deals with issues of: (1) Confidentiality (2) Data Integrity and Security (3) Impartiality and independence (4) Due process (5) ‘black box’ issues (6) Enforceability of arbitral awards; and (6) Impact on energy use and the environment. See generally Guideline 1, which states: ‘All participants involved in arbitration proceedings who use AI tools in preparation for or during an arbitration are responsible for familiarising themselves with the AI tool’s intended uses and should adapt their use accordingly. All participants using AI tools in connection with an arbitration should make reasonable efforts to understand each AI tool’s relevant limitations, biases, and risks and, to the extent possible, mitigate them’. Silent Silent Paragraph 11 Paragraph 11 states: ‘11. The use of AI does introduce risks to Proceedings, the Tribunal and Parties. These include: 11.1 Confidentiality and Data Security Risks: 11.1.1 Data Exposure: AI Tools may store, access or utilise confidential information for machine learning purposes; 11.1.2 Complex Data Flows: Tracking data usage and storage becomes intricate due to the multifaceted nature of AI Tools, which often employ various tools and platforms; 11.1.3 Cybersecurity Vulnerabilities: AI Tools are vulnerable to hacking and phishing threats; 11.2 Technical Limitations and Reliability Concerns; 11.2.1 Hallucinations: AI Tools can generate factually incorrect information, including fabricated legal authorities or cases; 11.2.2 “Black Box” Problem: AI Tools’ decision-making processes are opaque, hindering oversight and accountability; 11.2.3 Bias in Outputs: AI Tools reflect the biases present in their training data, potentially resulting in discriminatory outcomes; 11.2.4 Accuracy Limitations: AI Tools are challenged by nuanced legal reasoning and complex factual scenarios; 11.3 Regulatory and Enforceability Risks: 11.3.1 Inconsistent Regulations: Different jurisdictions adopt varying AI regulations, posing compliance challenges; 11.3.2 Conflict with Mandatory Rules: AI Tools may conflict with applicable laws, regulations, or institutional rules’. Sub-section ‘Benefits and Risks of Using AI’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines present a balanced view of AI’s advantages and disadvantages. Advantages include improved efficiency and quality in text generation, data analysis, evidence review and legal research, resulting in faster and more cost-effective arbitration. Disadvantages demonstrate the substantial risks associated with improper AI use such as confidentiality and data breaches, accuracy and bias, the “Black Box” issue where AI algorithms and decision mechanisms are difficult to understand, and possible enforcement risks for awards where AI has been used in the proceedings’ Benefits of AI See generally Guideline 1, which recognizes: (1) Efficiency and quality (2) Legal research (3) Data analysis (4) Text generation (5) Collecting evidence (6) Translating and interpretation (7) Transcription (8) Detecting the use of AI (9) Case analysis; and (10) Equal treatment of parties. See generally Guideline 1, as above. See Guideline at page 2, which references: (1) Legal research, concept searching (2) Editing and proof-reading (3) Translations and interpretation (4) Transcriptions (5) generating briefing notes, diagrams, and summaries of texts (6) Drafting communications and submissions (7) document production (8) document management (9) reviewing, analysing, and presenting of evidence (10) drafting cross-examination questions (11) arbitrator appointments, and (12) case-value analysis. Silent Paragraph 7 Paragraph 7 states: ‘AI can be used in Proceedings by the Tribunal, the Parties or their representatives in a number of ways, including: 7.1 To analyse and compile facts: AI can be used to extract complex and interrelated facts and summarise them and put them into chronological order; 7.2 To conduct research and analysis: AI Tools can be used to search and analyse legal precedents, statutes, and arbitral awards; 7.3 To review and manage documentation: AI Tools can be used to process case documents, identify relevant materials, and organise them, while highlighting inconsistencies and manipulation; 7.4 To speed up decision making: AI Tools can be used to mine text and objectively identify patterns and inconsistencies to facilitate evidence and data analysis; 7.5 To generate text: AI Tools can be used to draft procedural documents, summarise arguments, collate facts, arrange data in a logical sequence and assist in drafting; 7.6 To facilitate Proceedings: AI Tools can be used to provide real-time and cost-effective translation or transcription of hearings; 7.7 To automate administrative tasks: AI Tools can automate scheduling, communication, and other procedural tasks’. Paragraph 8 also states: ‘8. AI Tools may also assist Parties by: 8.1 Reducing Costs: AI automates document review and labour-intensive tasks, thereby reducing costs associated with Proceedings; 8.2 Levelling the playing field: 8.2.1 Empowering Parties with less resources: AI enables Parties with limited resources to undertake sophisticated legal analysis, expanding their capabilities; 8.2.2 Assisting Unrepresented Parties: AI Tools facilitate the participation of unrepresented or underrepresented Parties’. Sub-section ‘Benefits and Risks of Using AI’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines present a balanced view of AI’s advantages and disadvantages. Advantages include improved efficiency and quality in text generation, data analysis, evidence review and legal research, resulting in faster and more cost-effective arbitration. Disadvantages demonstrate the substantial risks associated with improper AI use such as confidentiality and data breaches, accuracy and bias, the “Black Box” issue where AI algorithms and decision mechanisms are difficult to understand, and possible enforcement risks for awards where AI has been used in the proceedings’

plain_text:
1 THE RISE OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN THE LEGAL INDUSTRY Generative artificial intelligence (AI) models are rapidly evolving, and being increasingly adopted within traditional industry globally. The legal industry is no exception. Today, the legal industry benefits from of over 100 legal AI models that are tailored to meet the needs of lawyers in law firms and corporate legal departments and provide services such as contract drafting and amendment, legal research, and legal analysis.1 See Eric Chin, Legal Generative AI: The New Equation for Lawyers of the Future [2025] SAL Prac 2, at [4]. For example, the American Arbitration Association – International Centre for Dispute Resolution has partnered with Optima Juris to develop an AI-powered transcription service. Naturally, the advent of legal AI has led to an increase in the use of AI in the realm of international arbitration. For example, AI has been used for arbitrator selection and predictive analysis of arbitrators’ decisions and inclinations, which are assessed through a review of past case experience and judgments or published awards,2 See Aditya Singh Chauhan, Future of AI in Arb itration: The Fine Line Between Fiction and Reality (Kluwer Arbitration Blog 26 Sep. 2020), https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2020/09/26/future-of-ai-in-arbitration-the-fine-line-between-fiction-and-reality/ (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). and may extend to reviewing publications of potential tribunal members and even social media posts. In the future, advances in technology may mean that we could even see the use of AI in tribunal deliberations.3 See Maxi Scherer, Artificial Intelligence and Legal Decision-Making: The Wide Open?, 36(5) J. Int’l Arb. 539–574 (2019) (© Maxi Scherer ed., Kluwer Law International; Kluwer Law International 2019), doi: 10.54648/joia2019028. See also How Arbitrators are Harnessing Artificial Intelligence (American Arbitration Association 20 Feb. 2024), https://www.adr.org/blog/how-arbitrators-are-harnessing-artificial-intelligence (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). However, such advancements in technology do not come without risk. Specifically, the unregulated use of AI can pose serious dangers not only to a client’s right to adequate legal representation, but also to their due process rights. As a consequence, we have witnessed the recent development and publication of guidelines for the use of AI in arbitration by a number of leading arbitral institutions and bodies globally. Within this article, we consider some of the risks attaching to the use of AI in international arbitration, whilst also comparing the terms of the leading published guidelines in the field.

2 RISKS OF THE USE OF AI IN INTERNATIONAL ARBITRATION The use of AI as an adjudication tool (either by arbitrators or the judiciary) poses the risk of undermining parties’ rights of due process in the proceedings, as well as posing public policy and potentially ethical concerns, which may be highly relevant to the issue of enforcement of arbitral awards in foreign jurisdictions. Indiscriminate use of generative AI in the course of the determination of disputes might undermine a client’s right to a fair hearing, should they indicate a lack of due attention paid by the tribunal in deliberating on issues, and may in turn result in a tribunal not fully appreciating the nuances of a party’s case, thereby resulting in a miscarriage of justice. As arbitration practitioners must know, a sufficient breach of a party’s due process rights may constitute grounds for setting aside under the UNCITRAL Model Law, on which many nations’ arbitration statutes are founded. As a result, any award rendered may be liable to be set aside by the relevant court (which may or may not be the eventual enforcement court or the setting aside court). Increasing AI usage by parties to arbitration proceedings also poses risk. One of the classic issues that has arisen is the risk of hallucination by legal AI models, particularly by Large Language Models (LLM). A study by Stanford RegLab and the Institute for Human-Centred AI found that the risk of hallucination in LLMs ranges from 69% to 88%, in response to specific legal queries, and that LLMs have a tendency to reinforce ‘incorrect legal assumptions’.4 See Matthew Dahl, Varun Magesh, Mirac Suzgun & Daniel E. Ho, Hallucinating Law: Legal Mistakes With Large Language Models are Pervasive (Legal Aggregate, Stanford Law School 11 Jan. 2024), https://law.stanford.edu/2024/01/11/hallucinating-law-legal-mistakes-with-large-language-models-are-pervasive (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). This development poses an unfortunate consequence for the use of AI in legal work, whereby high-profile cases involving legal counsels’ misplaced reliance on generative AI to their detriment (and in certain cases, public humiliation) has made headlines around the world.5 See Benjamin Weiser, Here’s What Happens When Your Lawyer Uses ChatGPT (The New York Times, 27 May 2023), https://www.nytimes.com/2023/05/27/nyregion/avianca-airline-lawsuit-chatgpt.html (accessed 8 Jul. 2025); Sara Merken, AI ‘Hallucinations’ in Court Papers Spell Trouble for Lawyers (Reuters 19 Feb. 2025) https://www.reuters.com/technology/artificial-intelligence/ai-hallucinations-court-papers-spell-trouble-lawyers-2025-02-18 (accessed 8 Jul. 2025); and Sara Merken, Lawyers in Walmart Lawsuit Admit AI ‘Hallucinated’ Case Citations (Reuters 11 Feb. 2025), https://www.reuters.com/legal/legalindustry/lawyers-walmart-lawsuit-admit-ai-hallucinated-case-citations-2025-02-10 (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). By way of example, a junior barrister was found to have cited multiple fake cases in a hearing before the English Courts to which the Court commented6 See Judge Fury After ‘Fake’ Cases Cited by Rookie Barrister in High Court (AJO Solicitors), https://ajosolicitors.co.uk/judge-fury-after-fake-cases-cited-by-rookie-barrister-in-high-court (accessed 8 Jul. 2025).: On the balance of probabilities, I consider that it would have been negligent for this barrister, if she used AI and did not check it, to put that text into her pleading. However, I am not in a position to determine whether she did use AI. I find as a fact that Ms Forey intentionally put these cases into her statement of facts and grounds, not caring whether they existed or not, because she had got them from a source which I do not know but certainly was not photocopying cases, putting them in a box and tabulating them, and certainly not from any law report. I do not accept that it is possible to photocopy a non-existent case and tabulate it. Although the court did not positively identify that the barrister in question relied on AI, the use of non-existent cases is a hallmark of the hallucinatory consequences of using general off-the-shelf AIs in legal practice. The barrister in question was eventually ordered to pay GBP 2,000 in costs.7 See ibid. Instances such as these represent not only the inherent limitations of AI, but also a threat to a client’s right to adequate legal representation. Against this backdrop, the emergence of guidelines surrounding AI usage in arbitration and disputes is welcomed as a means to manage these aforementioned risks and to prevent similar incidents from arising. The reality is that AI is only going to continue to play a role in arbitration, and to grow in significance going forward. A good example of this may be seen in the Ordinance on the Shenzhen Court of International Arbitration (SCIA) (the SCIA Ordinance) which recognizes, at Article 7, that: The SCIA shall pursue smart arbitration by fully utilizing the internet, big data, artificial intelligence , and other information technologies to provide efficient and convenient dispute resolution services to the parties 8 See Art. 7, Ordinance on the Shenzhen Court of International Arbitration. (emphasis added).In this regard, caution, and the adoption of appropriate guidelines on the use of AI, is and will continue to be critical.

3 WHAT ARE THE EXISTING GUIDELINES ON THE USE OF AI IN ARBITRATION? At present, the institutions and bodies which have led the way in the development and publication of guidelines pertaining to the use of AI in arbitration are the Silicon Valley Arbitration & Mediation Centre (SVAMC); the Chartered Institute of Arbitrators (CIArb); the Stockholm Chamber of Commerce (SCC); the Vienna International Arbitral Centre (VIAC); the American Arbitration Association – International Centre for Dispute Resolution (AAA-ICDR); the Associations of Arbitrators (Southern Africa) NPC (AOASA); and the China International Economic and Trade Arbitration Commission (CIETAC). Given the growing importance of AI in not only the legal field generally, but also specifically in international arbitration, the guidelines published by these institutions already represent a significant step towards providing clear guard rails on AI usage in international arbitration.9 We also note that there are likely to be an increasing number of AI-related disputes going forward where the central issue involves an issue of AI liability (see Dan Perera & Aaron Tan Kai Ran, Legal Liability for AI-Driven Decisions – When AI Gets It Wrong, Who Can You Turn To? (Holman Fenwick Willan LLP, 15 Apr. 2025), https://www.hfw.com/insights/legal-liability-for-ai-driven-decisions-when-ai-gets-it-wrong-who-can-you-turn-to/#:~:text=Given%20the%20rapidly%20increasing%20integration%20of%20artificial%20intelligence,focus%20on%20the%20energy%2C%20resources%20and%20commodities%20sectors (accessed 8 Jul. 2025)). In such cases, specialized rules might be necessary such as the JAMS Artificial Intelligence Disputes Clause, Rules and Protective Order, which governs AI arbitrations. However, this is beyond the current scope of this piece. As the SVAMC Consultation Draft observed:10 See SVMAC Consultation Draft dated 31 Aug. 2023, issued for public consultation prior to the publication of the final guidelines (the SVAMC Consultation Draft). Any person involved in the arbitration proceedings that uses an AI tool must be aware of the risks of such use (breach of confidentiality, cyberattacks, bias, inaccuracies/hallucinations, violation of personal data protection, etc. ) and shall be accountable in case of occurrence of these risks. The guidelines for the use of AI in arbitration published by these organizations in chronological order are as follows: (1) AAA-ICDR AAA-ICDR Principles Supporting the Use of AI in Alternative Dispute Resolution, published on 30 January 2024 (AAA-ICDR Guidelines) (2) SVAMC 2024 SVAMC Guidelines on the Use of AI in Arbitration, published on 30 April 2024 (SVAMC Guidelines); (3) SCC SCC Arbitration Institute Guide to the use of AI in cases administered under the SCC rules, published on 16 October 2024 (SCC Guidelines); (4) CIArb 2025 CIArb Guidelines on the Use of AI in Arbitration, published on 19 March 2025 (CIArb Guidelines); (5) VIAC’s Note on the Use of AI in Arbitration Proceedings, published on 10 April 2025 (VIAC Guidelines); and (6) AOASA Guidelines on the use of AI in Arbitrations and Adjudications on 20 May 2025 (AOASA Guidelines). (7) CIETAC Provisional guidelines on the use of AI in arbitration on 18 July 2025 (CIETAC Guidelines) Together, these are collectively referred to as the Guidelines. The SVAMC Guidelines, CIArb Guidelines, and AOASA Guidelines are intended to be comprehensive and instructive suggestions, capable for general adoption by parties or tribunals in a manner similar to the IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence in International Arbitration, whereas the SCC Guidelines, AAA-ICDR Guidelines, CIETAC Guidelines and VIAC Guidelines are plainly intended to be more general in nature and, as noted by VIAC, ‘intended to facilitate discussions related to the use of AI in the context of VIAC arbitration proceedings’.11 See at 2 of the VIAC Guidelines at the section titled ‘Applicability’. In our view, the guidelines surrounding AI will only continue to grow over the years as governments and international organizations continue to publish AI guidelines for various industries and sectors. By way of example, the European Union (EU) AI Act12 See EU AI Act: First Regulation on Artificial Intelligence (European Parliament 8 Jun. 2023), https://www.europarl.europa.eu/topics/en/article/20230601STO93804/eu-ai-act-first-regulation-on-artificial-intelligence (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). is a comprehensive legislation governing the use of AI in Europe while the International Chamber of Commerce has released its AI governance guidelines for businesses.13 See Overarching Narrative on Artificial Intelligence (International Chamber of Commerce), https://iccwbo.org/global-insights/digital-economy/icc-overarching-narrative-on-artificial-intelligence (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). The prevalence and importance of AI guidelines further extends to areas as diverse as mediation (IBA Guidelines on the use of Gen AI in Mediation), general litigation (Supreme Court of Singapore Guide on the Use of Generative AI Tools by Court Users), and even guidelines created to govern AI disputes themselves (JAMS AI Dispute Rules). Unfortunately, these alternative guidelines are beyond the scope of the present article.

4 EVALUATING THE EFFECTIVENESS OF THE VARIOUS GUIDELINES In our view, the effectiveness of any AI guidelines should be assessed based on the following criteria: (1) whether the guidelines provide sufficient clarity and scope of application; (2) whether the parties and the arbitrators are sufficiently protected and empowered by the guidelines; and (3) whether the integrity, confidentiality of the arbitration process, and party autonomy is adequately safeguarded. In summary, with reference to what we consider to be the most critical elements surrounding the use of AI in arbitration, we set out below (and in tabular form at Annex 1 hereto, with reference to the specific provisions) a comparative analysis of the Guidelines: (1) Definition of AI: Having a carefully considered definition of AI adds clarity to the scope of application of the Guidelines, although this is not present in all of the Guidelines; (2) Due Process and Independence: The CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines provide the greatest degree of due process protection for parties, thereby ensuring the integrity of the arbitration; (3) Disclosure of Use of AI: In relation to party usage, the CIArb Guidelines provide the tribunal with the broadest discretion to order AI-related disclosure. However, the SVAMC Guidelines provide helpful clarity on how such disclosure should be effected. In respect of tribunal usage, the various guidelines provide that arbitrators themselves would need to disclose AI usage where appropriate; (4) Confidentiality: Although the CIArb Guidelines protect parties’ confidentiality, best practices would need to be imported from the SVAMC Guidelines as well as prior CIArb Guidelines; (5) Arbitrator Powers: The CIArb Guidelines provide the clearest guidance and empower the tribunal to exercise their powers to regulate the usage of AI by the parties in the arbitration to the fullest extent; and (6) Parties’ Usage: the SVAMC Guidelines place the onus on parties to respect the integrity of arbitration proceedings in using AI. We examine each of these critical elements in greater detail below: Comparative an aly sis of critical elements of Guidelines 1. Definition of AI: Having a carefully considered definition of AI adds clarity to the scope of application of the Guidelines, although this is not present in all of the Guidelines. In considering the effectiveness of any AI guideline, a starting point is to consider: what constitutes AI? To which question, there does not appear to be any universal consensus.14 Daniel Seng, Jerrold Soh & Lim How Khang, An Introduction to the Relevant Technologies, in Law and Technology in Sin gapore (Singapore Academy of Law Publishing, 2nd ed., Simon Chesterman, Goh Yihan, and Andrew Phang, 2025) at 26 and 27. By way of example, the CIArb Guidelines define AI as a: machine-based system that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments. Different AI systems vary in their levels of autonomy and adaptiveness after deployment.In comparison, the SVAMC Guidelines define AI as: computer systems that perform tasks commonly associated with human cognition, such as understanding natural language, recognising complex semantic patterns, and generating human-like outputs.Another useful comparator is the SCC Guidelines, which adopts the EU’s definition of AI, which is a15 See EU Regulation 2024/1689 (EU AI Act).: machine-based system that is designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy and that may exhibit adaptiveness after deployment, and that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers, from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments. Another comparator is the AOASA Guidelines, which adopts a comparatively unique definition of AI, which: shall mean non-human power capable of performing tasks traditionally requiring human cognitive functions and human intelligence, such as sequencing and collating facts, summarising such facts, reasoning, learning, problem-solving, perception, and decision-making.As is apparent, the definitions of AI are already comparatively different. For example, the CIArb Guidelines and the SCC Guidelines adopt an external-facing approach, which emphasizes the ability of the AI to generate outputs and have an influence on the physical and virtual environment. In contrast, the SVAMC Guidelines place a greater emphasis on internal processes and human cognition, as opposed to any external output. In even sharper contrast to the aforementioned three sets of Guidelines, the VIAC Guidelines deliberately eschew any prior definition. Instead, the VIAC Guidelines make it clear that the ‘VIAC does not subscribe to a single definition’, on the basis that ‘a fixed definition would likely not cover the broadening range of tools that will be used in the near future’. This represents the VIAC’s pragmatic approach in recognizing the evolving technology that is AI, though the lack of a definition might result in an over-inclusive approach. This approach is similar to the AAA-ICDR Guidelines and the CIETAC Guidelines, which do not put forth a definition of AI. While the VIAC Guidelines recognize the speed of development of emerging technology, and consequently expressly a loose description of AI to account for this, in our view this comes with both benefits and drawbacks. It is arguably more important to have a clearly identifiable definition of AI, for the purposes of the application of any rules or guidelines. The CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines, in particular, propose extensive sets of rules for parties to consider adopting, which permit the use of AI to be regulated during arbitral proceedings through their adoption. In circumstances where such guidelines are adopted by the parties, or their application is ordered by a tribunal, parties and their counsel would benefit from the clarity of knowing whether their tools may attract an enhanced level of oversight. Adopting the VIAC Guidelines approach opens the door to a greater degree of uncertainty, insofar that parties will not be aware of which tools they would need to subject to a higher standard of caution. In contrast, the definition proffered by the CIArb Guidelines is in our view likely to see the widest adoption in the realm of international arbitration. In our view, the definition builds on the guidelines that have come before whilst recognizing that various AIs provide different levels of capabilities. In turn, this captures the widest possible scope of AIs of all capabilities, thereby preventing issues over whether the guidelines should apply to less advanced AIs: 2. Due Process and Independence: The CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines provide the greatest degree of due process protection for parties, thereby ensuring the integrity of the arbitration.At its core, due process comprises the parties’ legitimate expectations to receive protection from arbitrary, discriminatory, or unfair treatment. In relation to AI specifically, the CIArb Guidelines recognize that the use of AI tools in arbitration: may give rise to due process issues including inequality of arms and significant discrepancies in parties’ respective ability to understand the case advanced against them. The use of an AI Tool as an aid in factual case analysis might impinge upon the right to present a party’s case and the implicit obligation of arbitrators to consider and address all of the parties’ arguments and exhibits, rather than a limited selection determined by an AI Tool. Use of an AI Tool as an aid to identify the relevant legal framework may also carry such risk.The issue of due process is further compounded by the fact that generative AI tends to face the ‘black box’ problem (a particularly thorny issue when generative AI is being used by the decision maker). By way of context, the ‘black box’ problem arises as a result of AI models that use opaque deep learning methodologies to generate output, which is problematic insofar that the reasoning undertaken by the AI model might not be accessible or comprehensible.16 See Lou Blouin, AI’s Mysterious ‘Black Box’ Problem, Explained (University of Michigan-Dearborn 6 Mar. 2023), https://umdearborn.edu/news/ais-mysterious-black-box-problem-explained (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). This is a point expressly recognized by the AOASA Guidelines which note that the ‘black box’ problem is in essence a situation in which AI Tools’ decision-making processes are opaque, hindering oversight and accountability.Naturally, this leads to questions of whether parties on the receiving end of an AI model’s decision have suffered from arbitrary, discriminatory, or unfair treatment, thereby violating their due process rights – particularly in cases where AI has been used to assist tribunals with the deliberation of issues. To elaborate, the importance of the role of the arbitrator and the reasoning behind the awards granted is critical. As put by philosopher Jurgen Habermas, a decision is only legitimate when it results from a legitimate and deliberative process which must be undertaken in a manner in which the problems have been clearly formulated and the way in which solutions have been assessed and evaluated are made public. The importance of reasoning is highlighted by the fact that: (1) reasoning is evidence of comprehension; (2) reasoning is evidence of care provided to the parties’ fundamental rights of due process; and (3) reasoning is a tool necessary for the relevant court to scrutinize the award. The use of AI within the scope of the various guidelines assists in ensuring that the concept of reasoning is not lost in the process of relying on AI. Although AI might assist the arbitrators with assessing facts or research, it is clear that the use of AI is not a substitute for the arbitrators’ critical thinking, reasoning, and ensuring due process. Fortunately, this is a reality recognized by several of the guidelines, such as the AAA-ICDR Guidelines, which take time to expressly highlight the importance of ADR professionals continuing to exercise independent judgment and the need to continue relying on their expertise, experience, and judgment.17 See Colin Rule, AAA ICDR Principles Supporting the Use of AI in Alternative Dispute Resolution (Arbitrate.com 30 Jan. 2024), https://arbitrate.com/aaa-icdr-principles-supporting-the-use-of-ai-in-alternative-dispute-resolution (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). To that end, the CIArb Guidelines have provided clear guidance on mitigating these potential due process issues surrounding AI usage. In particular, Guideline 9 of the CIArb Guidelines encourages arbitrators to be fully transparent in their use of any AI tool to which a similar position is adopted by the AOASA Guidelines at 10.3, which states that ‘AI usage should be transparent to all Parties involved’ and advises that they should inform parties of the use of any such tool. Crucially, Guideline 8.2 of the CIArb Guidelines highlights that: Arbitrators should not relinquish their decision-making powers to AI but may use AI to support more accurate and efficient processing of submitted information, always ensuring independent judgment … the Tribunal should avoid delegating any tasks to AI Tools, such as legal analysis, research and interpretation of facts and law, or application of the law to the facts, if such use could influence procedural or substantive decisions (emphasis added).In this regard, the CIArb Guidelines make it clear that arbitrators are required to continue applying their minds to the issues pleaded before them, and are not permitted to relinquish key decisions to their AI tools. This is an eminently sensible and clear position that protects parties’ rights to due process. The SVAMC Guidelines adopt a similar position as the CIArb Guidelines. Guideline 7 makes it clear that AI usage by arbitrators must respect due process.
