For reference, Guideline 7 states: An arbitrator shall not rely on AI-generated information outside the record without making appropriate disclosures to the parties beforehand and, as far as practical, allowing the parties to comment on it.Similarly to the CIArb Guidelines, the commentary on the SVAMC Guidelines makes it clear that the arbitrator has a clear duty to disclose reliance on any AI tools that might influence their understanding of the case, and goes a step further to highlight that parties must have the right to comment on decisions and ‘to be heard’. The SVAMC Guidelines also include clear guidance that arbitrators must not ‘delegate any part of their personal mandate to any AI tool’.18 See Guideline 6 of the SVAMC Guidelines. This serves as a helpful reminder that arbitral appointments are indeed personal mandates, which take careful account of the knowledge, experience, and judgment of the appointed arbitrators. Their execution of such mandates is not capable of being delegated and outsourced at will – either to any other individual, or to the collective assumed knowledge of an AI model. Next, both the CIArb Guidelines and the SVAMC Guidelines highlight that arbitrators should take the time to verify the outputs generated by any AI tools. In this regard, this is a sensible and necessary due process protection to safeguard against hallucination and, by extension, an egregious breach of party rights. The consequence of failing to prevent hallucination or overreliance on AI to generate decisions is significant, and may result in applications to set aside awards. A good example of this was highlighted recently in the application of John Lapaglia v. Valve Corporation (Valve), before the United States District Court in the Southern District of California, in which the applicant filed an application to vacate an arbitration award on the basis that the arbitrator had ‘outsourced his adjudicative role to Artificial Intelligence’.19 See en-john-lapaglia-v-valve-corporation-petition-to-vacate-arbitration-award-memorandum-of-points-and-authorities-in-support-thereof-tuesday-8th-april-2025. In that case, following the issuance of the final post-hearing brief on 23 Dec. 2024, the award was issued on 7 Jan. 2025 on the same day that the arbitrator was intended to travel to the Galapagos. In the application, the claimant argued that the arbitrator had used AI to adjudicate and draft the award on the basis that there were telltale signs of AI generation, the fact that the arbitrator was aiming to get the award out before his trip, and the awkward phrasing, incoherence, and generalization of the award that was indicative of AI generation. We foresee an increased number of such challenges going forward, as the use of AI in arbitral proceedings increases exponentially. For completeness, both the VIAC Guidelines (Guideline 2.1) and SCC Guidelines (page 3) likewise make it clear that arbitrators should not delegate key decision-making functions to AI tools, and must retain their independent analysis. A similar position is adopted by the CIETAC Guidelines which notes that: Before employing an AI tool, a tribunal must assess the necessity and proportionality of its use, weigh efficiency gains against the risks, evaluate the data security and accuracy of the specific AI tool, and understand the legal and regulatory landscape concerning AI in the relevant jurisdiction 20 See at 2 of the CIETACT Guidelines at sub-section ‘Precautions for Arbitral Tribunals’.Additionally, the CIETAC Guidelines make it clear that the tribunal must ‘independently analyse facts and laws and are required to independently provide reasoning and decisions’.21 See ibid. Overall, these guidelines adopt a clear stance that tribunals must not secede their independent decision making powers to AI. However, none of these set of guidelines goes as far as the CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines, to directly consider the impact of hallucination and necessary safeguards against it. The lack of clear guidance on this critical element poses the risk of another Valve situation arising. To summarize, the CIArb Guidelines and SVAMC Guidelines are comprehensive in ensuring that the issue of due process rights in the age of AI is recognized and protected. Although the VIAC Guidelines and SCC Guidelines likewise recognize the importance of protecting due process rights, closer attention might need to be paid to issues of hallucination: 3. Disclosure of use of AI: In relation to party usage, the CIArb Guidelines provide the tribunal with the broadest discretion to order AI-related disclosure. However, the SVAMC Guidelines provide helpful clarity on how such disclosure should be effected. In respect of tribunal usage, the various guidelines provide that arbitrators themselves would need to disclose AI usage where appropriate.As a starting point, disclosure in the present case refers to the disclosure of parties’ use of various AI tools. In particular, parties might be required to disclose their use of the tools if there is an impact on the evidence, or outcome of the arbitration.22 See Guideline 7.1 of the CIArb Guidelines. In this regard, the CIArb Guidelines recognizes that the disclosure of an AI might be helpful insofar that such disclosure ensures the transparency of the arbitration. The rationale for such disclosure as noted by the CIArb Guidelines is to provide an opportunity for other arbitration participants to understand the manner and context in which AI has been used or is to be used.The CIArb Guidelines also empower the arbitrator to impose certain AI-related disclosure obligations on the parties, including on any party-appointed experts or factual witnesses, and to make directions as to the type of AI covered by the obligation to disclose.23 See Guideline 7.3 of the CIArb Guidelines. The CIArb Guidelines finally make it clear that in the event of failure to adhere to disclosure orders, the arbitrator is empowered to take steps to inquire. However, beyond a general ability to take steps, the CIArb Guidelines are, at present, only guidelines for parties and do not possess a clear set of consequences for failure to adhere. Nonetheless, the fact of the matter is that the CIArb Guidelines as a general rule seek to empower the tribunal to act where possible to ensure compliance. Although only time will tell whether any pattern of clear consequences for a breach of the CIArb Guidelines would arise, the discretion provided to tribunals would be a good starting point for ensuring proper compliance, seeing as parties are more likely than not to apply for the tribunal to exercise these powers should a breach by the opposing party become evident. In any case, the scope of the tribunal’s powers under the CIArb Guidelines is elaborated on in subsection 5 below. In comparison, the other guidelines are either silent or brief on the issue of disclosure of AI tools. As a starting point, the AAA-ICDR Guidelines are silent on the issue of disclosure. They only go as far as to highlight that ADR professionals should continue to exercise independent judgment. The SCC Guidelines are also noticeably silent on the need to disclose the use of AI tools, and the VIAC Guidelines make only brief mention to it. Guideline 5.1 of the VIAC Guidelines does recognize that: [the parties and/or the arbitrator] may wish to discuss in the case management conference, the potential use of AI in the proceedings, the requirement of disclosure as well as the potential impact of AI on the arbitration timeline and costs.The VIAC Guidelines also recognize the importance of disclosure beyond the mere usage of AI by the parties. The VIAC Guidelines also note that arbitrators should have the power to request disclosure of evidence provided by AI, or with the support of AI.24 See Guideline 6.1 of the VIAC Guidelines. Next, the CIETAC Guidelines likewise recognize the importance of disclosure and adopt a generalist approach towards it25 See at 2 of the CIETAC Guidelines at sub-section ‘Basic Principles’. With respect to party autonomy, the IA Guidelines provide that the parties are empowered to agree on the rules governing AI use. This includes deciding whether to permit, restrict, or entirely prohibit AI, as well as establishing protocols for disclosureIn comparison, the AOASA Guidelines adopt the position that disclosure is an issue to be covered by agreement or by the tribunal. To illustrate, paragraph 12.2 of the AOASA Guidelines state26 See para. 12.2 of the AOASA Guidelines.: The use of AI and AI Tools may be covered in the Agreement or clarified by the Tribunal with the Parties. The Tribunal, the Parties, their representatives or witnesses should consider whether it is appropriate to disclose any other use of AI and AI Tools, and the nature and extent of such useHowever, the SVAMC Guidelines address the issue of disclosure of AI tools in quite some detail. Guideline 3 of the SVAMC Guidelines state that: Disclosure that AI tools were used in connection with an arbitration is not necessary as a general matter. Decisions regarding disclosure of the use of AI tools shall be made on a case-by-case basis taking account of the relevant circumstances, including due process and any applicable privilege. Where appropriate, the following details may help reproduce and evaluate the output of an AI tool: 1. the name, version, and relevant settings of the tool used; 2. a short description of how the tool was used; and 3. the complete prompt (including any template, additional context, and conversation thread) and associated output (emphasis added).Similarly to the CIArb Guidelines, the SVAMC Guidelines recognize that disclosure of AI usage is ultimately to be considered and effected on a case-by-case basis, and does not constitute a mandatory requirement. This decision is justified in the commentary to the SVAMC guidelines, on the basis that in an ‘evolving landscape, defining a set of generally applicable criteria for disclosure of AI does not seem feasible and could create more controversy than it resolves’.27 See Guideline 3 of the SVAMC Guidelines. Another similarity to the CIArb Guidelines is the acknowledgment that disclosure might be appropriate in certain circumstances – in particular, where disclosure is required by professional rules or more broadly to avoid misleading other participants in the arbitration. In fact, the SVAMC Guidelines make it clear that any disputes concerning AI disclosure should be ‘resolved using the existing mechanisms for resolving disclosure disputes in the context of an arbitration, which, in most cases, is done by the tribunal under the procedural rules applicable to the arbitration’.28 See at 17 of the SVAMC Guidelines. Importantly, the commentary also highlights that where the specific outputs of an AI tool are deemed to warrant disclosure, such disclosure should, in principle, cover the information necessary to reproduce and evaluate such output. In the case of generative AI tools, the input parameters typically include the full conversation history and additional text submitted to the AI model along with the query. The same considerations would apply, mutatis mutandis, to the use of AI tools that are not ‘generative’ but rather evaluative, such as recommender or classification systems. The SVAMC Guidelines in fact adopt a pseudo-compromise, as compared with the SVAMC Consultation Draft, in which two options were provided. Option A required that factors such as the reliance on AI outputs ‘instead of primary sources and the material impact of AI on proceedings might necessitate disclosure’.29 See also Rabia Batool & Scarlet Tunney, California International Arbitration Week 2024: AI in ADR: Building Consensus Around The Path Forward (Kluwer Arbitration Blog, 12 May 2024), https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2024/05/12/california-international-arbitration-week-2024-ai-in-adr-building-consensus-around-the-path-forward (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). Option B adopted a stricter approach which required proactive disclosure when ‘AI-generated documents are materially relied upon or when AI has a significant effect on the outcome of the case’. However, there was widespread disagreement on the use of either option. This was because commentary highlighted that both options were unclear in providing for when disclosure would be needed, and would be difficult to apply in practice.30 See SVAMC Consultation Draft dated 31 Aug. 2023. In particular, a number of law firms which had already acted to integrate AI and other legal technology into their daily work practices disagreed with the obligation to disclose the use of AI.31 See Felix Kasseroler, From Draft to Published Version: A Commentary on the Changes Implemented in the SVAMC AI Guidelines (Kluwer Arbitration Blog 21 Jun. 2024), https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2024/06/21/from-draft-to-published-version-a-commentary-on-the-changes-implemented-in-the-svamc-ai-guidelines (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). Following such feedback, the SVAMC reworked the guidelines into the final version, extracts of which are reproduced above, and which emphasize that the need for disclosure is to be addressed on a case-by-case basis. Fortunately, where such disclosure is deemed necessary, the SVAMC Guidelines provide clear requirements for adequate disclosure. In particular, the SVAMC Guidelines make it clear that parties would need to provide information such as the name, version and relevant settings of the tool used; a short description of the specific use; and the complete prompt. In light of the above, we have prepared an illustrative table highlighting the extent of disclosure required, the timing of disclosure, and the form of disclosure. We feel may this table may assist readers in assessing the various disclosure obligations owed under each guideline. Guideline Appropriateness of Disclosure Timing of Disclosure Form of Disclosure AAA-ICDR Silent Silent Silent SVAMC Disclosure is not necessary as a general matter. Instead, disclosure is to be made on a case-by-case basis (see Guideline 3). Arbitrators relying on AI tools would need to make appropriate disclosures and allow parties to comment on it (see Guideline 7). Case-by-case basis (see Guideline 3) Disclosure should include: (1) the name, version, and relevant settings of the tool used; (2) a short description of how the tool was used; and (3) the complete prompt (including any template, additional context, and conversation thread) and associated output (see Guideline 3). SCC Silent Silent Silent CIArb Appropriateness to disclose is required to the extent that its use may have an impact on the evidence or the outcome of the arbitration, or otherwise involve a delegation of an express duty toward the arbitrators or any other party (see Article 7). Silent Silent. The sample Procedural Order on the Use of AI in Arbitration provides timelines for the disclosure of ‘High Risk AI Use’. VIAC Appropriateness has to be brought up by the parties at the case management proceedings (see paragraph 5.1). The arbitrator may request for the parties to disclose the use of AI as well (paragraph 6.1). Case Management Conference (see paragraph 5.1) Silent AOASA Appropriateness of disclosure is determined by way of party agreement or by the Tribunal (see paragraph 12.2). Silent. Timing of disclosure is likely to be determined by way of party agreement or Tribunal direction. Form of disclosure may be issued by way of formal written agreements (see paragraph 12.4). CIETAC Silent. Subject to the party’s agreement (see sub-section ‘Basic Principles’) Silent. Subject to the party’s agreement (see sub-section ‘Basic Principles’) Silent. Subject to the party’s agreement (see sub-section ‘Basic Principles’) 4. Confidentiality: Although the CIArb Guidelines protect parties’ confidentiality, best practices would need to be imported from the SVAMC Guidelines as well as prior CIArb Guidelines. Confidentiality is of central importance in international arbitration, and this element of the arbitral process helps make arbitration so attractive to parties.32 See Avinash Poorooye & Ronán Feehily, Confidentiality and Transparency in International Commercial Arbitration: Finding the Right Balance (Harvard Negotiation Law Review, vol. 22:275 Spring 2017), https://journals.law.harvard.edu/hnlr/wp-content/uploads/sites/91/HNR203_crop-1.pdf (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). However, the use of AI in arbitration poses substantial risks to confidentiality which is a point directly recognized by the CIETAC Guidelines.33 See at 2 of the CIETAC Guidelines at sub-section ‘Benefits and Risks of Using AI’. The reality of AI usage for legal proceedings is the inescapable fact that AIs need to be trained on large data sets which invariably include confidential documents. An example would be in a substantially large arbitration, such as an investor state arbitration, parties might be inundated with a large number of confidential documents. The reliance on an AI to sort those documents inevitably results in the AI being exposed and trained on a large number of sensitive materials. Fortunately, this is a reality that has been recognized by the various guidelines. As a starting point, the CIArb Guidelines at Guideline 2.2 highlights that: Inputting confidential data into a third-party AI Tool will inevitably raise concerns about how such data is stored, protected from outside access, used for machine learning purposes and potentially presented to third parties as output from their use of the same AI platform … it is important to consider whether the AI Tool has been vetted, has sufficient privacy and security standards, and whether it is an open or closed model.In addressing this issue, the CIArb Guidelines suggest that parties should consider the use of confidentiality agreements (see Guideline 2.2), and should redact or anonymize inputs (see Guideline 7.7 in Appendix A of the CIArb Guidelines). However, these suggestions do not go very far in suggesting a comprehensive set of confidentiality protections, designed to mitigate the risk of AI usage. A similar issue of a lack of specificity is highlighted in the SCC Guidelines, AAA-ICDR Guidelines, and VIAC Guidelines, which only recognize that parties and, by extension, the arbitral tribunal, should be ‘encouraged to inform themselves as to how any data input is employed and deployed when using AI’, and that parties ‘shall respect the confidentiality of the arbitral process as a whole and ensure that AI tools are compliant with any such confidentiality obligations … thereby take all reasonable steps to prevent unauthorised access to sensitive case-related data’, respectively. This leaves much to subjective interpretation. In this regard, the SVAMC Guidelines are instructive. Guideline 2 of the SVAMC Guidelines states: All participants in international arbitration are responsible for ensuring their use of AI tools is consistent with their obligations to safeguard confidential information (including privileged, private, secret, or otherwise protected data). They should not submit confidential information to any AI tool without appropriate vetting and authorisation. Special attention should be paid to policies on recording, storage, and use of prompt or output histories and of any other confidential data submitted to the AI tool . Only AI tools that adequately safeguard confidentiality should be used with confidential information. Participants should assess the data use and retention policies offered by available AI tools and opt for secure solutions. Where appropriate, participants should redact or anonymise materials submitted to an AI tool (emphasis added)The SVAMC Guidelines add additional clarity and guidance as to what tools and functions should be maintained in order to preserve confidentiality. In fact, the SVAMC Guidelines go further by highlighting that publicly available tools represent a risk of disclosing confidential information and that, as a matter of best practice, parties should assess the confidentiality policies, features, and limitations of the tool, engaging technical experts as appropriate.34 See SVMAC Guidelines at Commentary to Guideline 2 on, at 17. It is clear that the SVAMC Guidelines adopt a clearer position as to the technical protections that parties should consider. Next, the AOASA Guidelines likewise recognize the impact of AI on parties’ confidentiality. To that end, paragraph 12.3 states:35 See para. 12.3 of the AOASA Guidelines. Any party using AI Tools should ensure confidentiality and security is maintained throughout the Proceeding and for as long as is legally necessary thereafter. This may be achieved by: 12.3.1. Vetting providers of AI Tools thoroughly, including their privacy policies, information storage, and security measures; 12.3.2. Considering data sovereignty, including storage and processing locations; 12.3.3. Avoiding open and free AI Tools, preferring paid subscription services with enhanced privacy; 12.3.4. Anonymising information before inputting case materials; 12.3.5. Using secure AI Tools with appropriate access controls to prevent unauthorised access to confidential information; 12.3.6. Regularly reviewing updates to the AI Tools and their terms of use such as those relating to privacy;The AOASA Guidelines provide one of the most comprehensive overviews of key confidentiality protections for the use of AI in arbitration. In fact, the AOASA Guidelines directly adopt the technical considerations of the AI model and their impact on confidentiality. In a similar vein to the SVAMC Guidelines, the AOASA Guidelines are alive to the issue of relying on open and free AI tools and directly address this concern by recommending reliance on paid services with enhanced security. In this regard, the AOASA Guidelines and their emphasis on the technical specifications relating to confidentiality demonstrate that the drafters are alive to the concerns. Quite similarly to the SVAMC Guidelines, the AOASA Guidelines also place the onus of assessing the quality of tools on the parties themselves. Overall, the AOASA Guidelines on confidentiality are helpful insofar that they highlight key technical specifications that should be taken into account to ensure that AI does not breach confidentiality. Additionally, the 2021 CIArb Framework Guideline on the Use of Technology in International Arbitration (CIArb Technology Framework) is also instructive. The CIArb Technology Framework provides clear guidance on how to protect confidentiality with modern technology, much of which can be adapted to supplement current guidance as a matter of best practices. For example: (1) the use of passwords, encryptions, and multi-factor authentication when transmitting soft copies of confidential documents and data; and (2) the use of secure file sharing services to transfer and store confidential information.These practices are easily integrable in the use of AI tools, and are aligned with the SVAMC Guidelines. They would go the furthest in ensuring that the parties’ confidentiality and security are upheld and protected. The importance of security and confidentiality will continue to be a key focus for arbitration institutions in the digital age and it is clear that other best practices from around the world should also be considered when using AI. A good example is the SCIA Online Arbitration Rules (SCIA Online Rules) at Article 13, which recognizes that: [a]n arbitral tribunal may, in consideration of the results of examination of evidence, review and judge the authenticity of the generation, collection, storage and transmission of the electronic data, with particular focus on: (a) whether the computer systems and other hardware and software environment relied on for the generation, collection, storage, and transmission of the electronic data are secure and reliable ; (b) whether the electronic data have a clear generator and time of generation, and whether the electronic data present clear, objective, and accurate information ; (c) whether the electronic data have a clear storage and safekeeping medium and have been kept safe by appropriate means and methods ; (d) whether the entities, tools, and methods that extract and preserve the electronic data are reliable and whether the extraction process can be reproduced ; (e) whether the electronic data are incomplete or have been altered due to addition, deletion, or modification of information ; and (f) whether the electronic data may be verified through a specified form (emphasis added) In this regard, the SCIA Online Rules go an extra step, by suggesting a clear framework that is needed to ensure the security of data and confidentiality of the parties. The SCIA Online Rules are also forward-looking, insofar as they recognize that a tribunal may request or use its powers to engage third parties to verify the authenticity of the electronic data, or gather other related evidence for cross-check.36 See Art. 13(3) of the SCIA Online Rules. These would be important additional steps that tribunals relying on other guidelines, such as the CIArb Guidelines, may consider when dealing with AI: 5. Arbitrator Powers: The CIArb Guidelines provide the clearest guidance and empower the tribunal to exercise their powers to regulate the usage of AI by the parties in the arbitration to the fullest extent.The scope of a tribunal’s right to issue directions and procedural rulings on the use of AI in arbitration is essential. This is consistent with the maxim that the arbitrator is the master of its own procedure, and Article 19(2) of the Model Law, which allows the tribunal to ‘conduct the arbitration in such manner as it considers appropriate’.37 See Republic of India v. Vedanta Resources plc [2021] 2 SLR 354. In this regard, arbitrators would benefit from clear guidance as to when they may rule on the use of AI in arbitral proceedings. On this matter, the CIArb Guidelines provide arbitrators a great deal of discretion in giving directions and making procedural rulings. This is expressly recognized in Guideline 4.1 of the CIArb Guidelines, which states that: [t]he use of AI by parties falls within the general power of arbitrators to conduct the proceedings including giving directions and making procedural rulings, subject to any express prohibition agreed by the parties or flowing from mandatory applicable laws, regulations, policies, or institutional rules, to the extent that it may have an impact on the procedure (emphasis added).The CIArb Guidelines go further by highlighting that the tribunal itself is empowered to appoint AI experts to understand tools (see Guideline 4.2); order disclosure of use of the tools (see above as well as Guideline 4.4); and, most importantly, order consequences to follow (be it the drawing of adverse inferences or costs orders) (see Guideline 4.7). As a matter of procedure, the CIArb Guidelines also place it beyond doubt that, in the event of a disagreement between the parties, the arbitrator is empowered to make a ruling on the use of AI by the parties.38 See Guideline 6 of the CIArb Guidelines. Arbitrators are encouraged to take into account ‘any benefits of using the relevant AI Tool (including any cost and/or time saving) and any risks associated with such AI Tool including whether it may have an impact on the evidence, fairness including due process and equality of arms, and confidentiality’.39 See Guideline 6.3 of the CIArb Guidelines. In determining the input of AI tools, the arbitrators are also provided with guidance to: assess the extent to which source data for the machine-generated content is on the record. Where the link between evidential source material and the model output is not clear, and where that link is a relevant factor in assessing whether the content should be admitted, arbitrators may seek submissions from the parties to explain how inputs from the record are linked to machine-generated outputs. 40 See Guideline 6.6 of the CIArb Guidelines.Finally, tribunals are also encouraged to consider the training sets used for the AI models that are being relied on by the parties.41 See Guideline 6.5 of the CIArb Guidelines. In this regard, the CIArb Guidelines represent a comprehensive set of guides and best practices that arbitrators should consider when faced with issues of AI usage. The rules are clear, extensive, and empower the tribunal to make critical decisions on their usage. In comparison, other arbitral institutional guidelines have paid considerably less attention to this critical matter. The SVAMC Guidelines do not go as far as the CIArb Guidelines in prescribing an extensive list of powers that arbitrators have to manage the use of AI. Instead, the SVAMC Guidelines take a broad approach, cautioning arbitrators on the use of AI tools, and reiterating the prudent approach that should be taken when considering the use of AI as part of the deliberation process. In this regard, the SVAMC Guidelines do not go as far as empowering the tribunal with the power to rule on the usage of AI. However, the commentary to Guideline 5 of the SVAMC Guidelines does make clear that, in the event that parties breach the guidelines, the tribunal would be empowered to consider taking measures available under the governing law, drawing adverse inferences, or making adverse cost orders. Likewise, the AOASA Guidelines do not delve into as much detail as the CIArb Guidelines. Instead, the AOASA Guidelines are more general in comparison. Specifically, paragraph 12.1 of the AOASA Guidelines places the onus on the tribunal to assess whether the use of AI in the proceedings should be allowed. By way of example, the AOASA Guidelines state that ‘[b]efore using AI Tools … Tribunals should establish whether the use of AI is permissible. Tribunals should review the Agreement, consider applicable law, consult Parties if the Agreement is silent, document consensus if agreed, and ensure AI use is permissible’.42 See para. 12.1 of the AOASA Guidelines. In this regard, the AOASA Guidelines adopt a framework approach which instructs tribunals to review the various written agreements first before consulting the parties. This pseudo framework approach ensures that the tribunal has a principled basis for assessing the use of AI in arbitration. Interestingly, the tribunal under the AOASA Guidelines plays a comparatively facilitative role in the adoption of AI and ultimately places the question of permissibility in the parties’ hands and their ability to agree on its use. Further, the SCC Guidelines do not highlight specific rules allowing the arbitrator to rule on the parties’ use of AI. Similar to the SVAMC Guidelines, the SCC Guidelines only go as far as to highlight that the use of AI must uphold confidentiality, quality, integrity, and non-delegation of decision-making powers. A similar position is adopted by the AAA-ICDR Guidelines and the CIETAC Guidelines. In particular, the CIETAC Guidelines only recognize that the tribunal would need to ‘assess the necessity and proportionality of its use, weigh efficiency gains against the risks, evaluate the data security and accuracy of the specific AI tool, and understand the legal and regulatory landscape concerning AI in the relevant jurisdiction’.43 See ‘Precautions for Arbitral Tribunals’, supra n. 20, at 2. In this regard, the SCC Guidelines, AAA-ICDR Guidelines, and the CIETAC Guidelines are comparatively general and do not delve into any specifics. The VIAC Guidelines go further than the SVAMC Guidelines and the SCC Guidelines, to recognize that the use of AI should be discussed at relevant case management conferences, and agreement amongst the parties as to its usage should be sought and adopted.44 See Guidelines 5.1 and 5.2 of the VIAC Guidelines. In this regard, the VIAC Guidelines are closer to the CIArb Guidelines, as they explicitly recognize the role that arbitrators have to play in determining the use of AI. In particular, the VIAC Guidelines make note of the possibility of needing arbitrators and parties to come to an agreement in the procedural orders for the use of AI in the proceedings,45 See Guidelines 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 of the VIAC Guidelines. which practice we expect will eventually become commonplace, until such time as arbitral institutions adopt express rules to address the use of AI. In our view, the CIArb Guidelines, which provide the most extensive guidance for arbitrators, should be a point of reference for any arbitrator who is asked to consider the usage of any AI. Most importantly, the CIArb Guidelines put beyond doubt the notion that arbitrators have the power to make procedural rulings on the matter and make directions as needed. 6. Parties’ Usage: the SVAMC Guidelines place the onus on parties to respect the integrity of arbitration proceedings in using AI.As a starting point, there is no uniform code of conduct or global standard practice for party conduct in international arbitration.46 See Raid Abu-Manneh & Lisa Dubot, The Conduct of the Parties in International Arbitration: The Current State of Play (International Bar Association 21 Oct. 2022), https://www.ibanet.org/conduct-of-parties-in-international-arbitration (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). Instead, legal representatives’ practices would likely be shaped or governed by a variety of different sources, including the regulatory authorities in the jurisdiction in which they are licensed; any relevant national laws; and any institutional guidelines or rules.47 See Guideline 6.5 of the CIArb Guidelines, supra n. 41. Fortunately, both the CIArb Guidelines and the SVAMC Guidelines provide clear guidance for parties who are considering the use of AI – although the SVAMC Guidelines go further and place it beyond doubt that parties bear the burden of ensuring responsible AI usage. The CIArb Guidelines make it clear that, as a starting point, parties have the right to agree on whether and how AI may be used by parties in the arbitration.48 See Guideline 5.1 of the CIArb Guidelines. This aligns with fundamental tenets of international arbitration as a voluntary, consensual process, recognizing party autonomy and, where relevant, the contractual relationship formed not only with their counterparties in the proceedings, but also the administering institution. Going further, the CIArb Guidelines allow for parties to discuss the use of AI in the proceedings.49 See Guideline 5.4 of the CIArb Guidelines. However, beyond these points, the CIArb Guidelines do not provide further elaboration. Understandably, more attention is placed on the arbitrator’s right to decide whether, and when, AI usage should be permitted. In this regard, the SCC Guidelines likewise do not place a great emphasis on the parties’ usage of AI tools. Instead, the majority of the SCC Guidelines focus heavily on the duty of the tribunal in using AI. In comparison, the VIAC Guidelines do make note of the fact that parties have a role in responsible AI usage. In comparison to the above, a more general approach is adopted by the AAA-ICDR Guidelines, which suggest only a general set of principles for the tribunal and the parties. Quite similarly, the AOASA Guidelines place a greater emphasis on ensuring that both the tribunal and the parties have a duty to consider the core principles of accountability, confidentiality, transparency, and fair decision-making in the use of AI tools. In particular, the AOASA Guidelines recognize that both the tribunal and the parties’ usage of AI tools should be subject to a degree of oversight. As a final note, the decision to use AI under the AOASA Guidelines rests primarily in the parties’ agreement with the tribunal having a comparatively more limited role in allowing the usage of AI. A similar approach to the AOASA Guidelines is also taken by the CIETAC Guidelines. In particular, the CIETAC Guidelines recognize that both parties and tribunals have a role to play in managing AI usage in arbitration. In this regard, the CIETAC Guidelines provide a checklist of recommended actions to mitigate the risk of AI50 See at 2 of the CIETAC Guidelines at sub-section ‘Recommended Risk Mitigation Measures’.: Parties to include stipulations regarding AI usage in their arbitration agreements. Tribunals to invite the parties to express their views on the use of AI in the procedural order or pre-hearing conference.Interestingly, the CIETAC Guidelines eschew a more comprehensive set of principles on party usage. The CIETAC has instead adopted a position that the CIETAC Guidelines are a starting point and should not prescribed a set of detailed and restrictive rules.51 See ibid. Regardless, what is clear is that the majority of the Guidelines place greater emphasis on the tribunal’s usage, as compared to the parties’ usage – most likely, a recognition of the potential for setting aside proceedings, in the event that the tribunal does not act consistently with critical principles of due process and natural justice. However, the SVAMC Guidelines go further by highlighting that parties should have a duty of competence in the use of AI (Guideline 4), and must respect the integrity of the proceedings and the evidence (Guideline 5), to ensure the ethical and responsible use of AI. We elaborate below. First, Guideline 4 of the SVAMC Guidelines states: Party representatives shall observe any applicable ethical rules or professional standards of competent or diligent representation when using AI tools in the context of an arbitration. Parties shall review the output of any AI tool used to prepare submissions to verify it is accurate from a factual and legal standpoint. Parties and party representatives on record shall be deemed responsible for any uncorrected errors or inaccuracies in any output produced by an AI tool they use in an arbitration.Guideline 4 recognizes that party representatives and parties themselves should avoid delegating key tasks without the proper review. In this regard, the commentary to the SVAMC Guidelines makes it clear that, when using AI tools, party representatives still owe a professional and ethical duty to review any work product created by AI.52 See SVAMC Guidelines, at 18. The SVAMC Guidelines make it clear that, ultimately, Guideline 4 does not impose an independent standard of review of party representatives’ conduct. Rather, it contains renvoi to any applicable rules of professional conduct or responsibility, to determine the level of diligence and reasonableness required when using AI tools. Therefore, the party on record will ultimately be deemed responsible for any non-compliance with the SVAMC’s Guideline 4. The position taken by the SVAMC is therefore one of cautious guidance, without seeking to impose a strict set of rules that parties would be required to abide by. Another important piece of guidance is contained in Guideline 5 of the SVAMC Guidelines, which requires that the parties must respect the integrity of the proceedings and the evidence. The SVAMC Guidelines take a firm stance that the parties continue to have a duty not to mislead the tribunal. Specifically, the commentary to the SVAMC Guidelines takes the view that ‘[p]arties, party representatives and experts should simply not use AI tools to fabricate evidence, distort evidence, or compromise the integrity of the proceedings under any circumstances’. In such circumstances, the SVAMC Guidelines take the position that, in the event of a breach, the tribunal may consider, in addition to any other measures available under the governing law and the applicable arbitration rules or the lex arbitri, deriving adverse inferences, and taking the infringing party representatives’ conduct into account, in its allocation of the costs of the arbitration. In our view, the SVAMC Guidelines and their suggestions on the best practices for party usage should be applied and considered by various tribunals where appropriate.

5 THE FUTURE OF AI GUIDELINES IN INTERNATIONAL ARBITRATION Within the space of just under two years, since the time of publication of the SVAMC Consultation Draft, we have seen a significant evolution in the development of AI guidelines for use in international arbitration. This is perhaps unsurprising, given the rapid increase in general usage of AI, and its undoubted increased adoption in the sphere of dispute resolution and international arbitration. We expect to see further innovations and the publication of updated guidelines in this space going forward, and also the adoption of rules around the ethical and responsible use of AI appearing in arbitral institutional rules, and perhaps even national legislation. The CIArb Guidelines represent the culmination of AI guidance in arbitration up to the present date. Both they and the SVAMC Guidelines are extremely helpful tools for both parties and tribunals. The SCC Guidelines and the SVAMC Guidelines were both published in 2024, while the CIArb Guidelines, VIAC Guidelines, and AOASA Guidelines were published in March, April, and May 2025 respectively. It is clear that the CIArb Guidelines have adopted and improved on many of the provisions provided in the predecessor guidelines in several ways. For example, the rules on disclosure of AI usage clearly show a degree of development from the SVAMC Guidelines, through the introduction of clear consequences of failing to disclose. However, the CIArb Guidelines continue to have several gaps that may be filled through reference to other guidelines – for example, on the issue of whether parties bear a burden for ensuring the ethical and responsible use of AI tools, where the SVAMC Guidelines are comprehensive. In any case, the continued development of relevant AI guidelines is a practical requirement, as AI tools themselves develop. Parties will likely only see their technology usage increase as greater and more efficient tools are used. The use of AI tools continues to pose several challenges, which are likely to be exacerbated in the event that guidelines are not adhered to. For example, arbitrators who rely on AI to generate parts of their awards might well find them being set aside on the basis of a breach of natural justice. In particular, situations where the use of AI results in awards which contain incomplete reasoning, omissions of key facts, and hallucinated arguments or sources would likely constitute a breach of natural justice. A close (though imperfect) analogy is in the recent case of DJP & DJO, in which the Singapore Court of Appeal held that an arbitration award prepared by the former Chief Justice of India should be set aside, on the basis that the award had been prepared by his copying and pasting significant chunks from other awards, plainly without consideration of the facts at hand.53 See DJO v. DJO [2025] SGCA(I) 2. In such cases, where an arbitrator has failed to adequately use their mind in preparing awards, the Singapore courts (and other courts around the world) would ‘not hesitate to set aside awards that are proven to fall below the requisite standards of natural justice’.54 See Aaron Tan Kai Ran & Ryan Baptista, The Need to Secure Trust and Accountability in Arbitration: DJO v. DJP or the ‘Copy and Paste’ Case (Kluwer Arbitration Blog 6 Dec. 2024), https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2024/12/06/the-need-to-secure-trust-and-accountability-in-arbitration-djo-v-djp-or-the-copy-and-paste-case (accessed 8 Jul. 2025). This is clearly consistent with the need to ‘enforce minimum standards of conduct’ in arbitration proceedings.55 See Guideline 6.5 of the CIArb Guidelines, supra n. 47. As Chief Justice Sundaresh Menon of the Supreme Court of Singapore noted in his extrajudicial speech at the Singapore International Arbitration Centre Annual India Conference 2024: trust can no longer be taken for granted as an inherent feature of arbitration; […] there is a pressing need for all stakeholders in the arbitration community to collectively and proactively act in the endeavour to secure trust in arbitration. These words are relevant in the age of AI, especially where there are numerous generative AI models that are theoretically capable of generating high-quality submissions, and even arbitral awards. However, the various Guidelines make it clear that, although such innovations are helpful, they must not undermine the critical importance of the integrity of the arbitration process. Thus, the presence of AI guidelines is a welcome and helpful innovation for arbitrations in the age of AI. After all, AI represents both unlimited transformational potential with similarly unlimited risks. Recognizing that both concerns co-exist and how to balance them is key. As the G7 leaders recently highlighted56 See G7 Leaders’ Statement on the Hiroshima AI Process (30 Oct. 2023), https://www.mofa.go.jp/files/100573466.pdf (accessed 8 Jul. 2025).: We, the Leaders of the Group of Seven (G7), stress the innovative opportunities and transformative potential of advanced Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems, in particular, foundation models and generative AI. We also recognize the need to manage risks and to protect individuals, society, and our shared principles including the rule of law and democratic values, keeping humankind at the center.

6 ANNEX 1: COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS OF AI GUIDELINES ON KEY ISSUES57 The AAA-ICDR Guidelines are notably general in nature as compared to the other guidelines. For instance, the AAA-ICDR Guidelines are entirely silent on the issue of disclosure and do not provide specific guidance on the appropriateness, timing, or form of disclosure required when AI is employed. Compared to other guidelines, the AAA-ICDR Guidelines offer a much more generic set of principles for both tribunals and parties, leaving considerable room for subjective interpretation and application by both the parties and the tribunal. Due to the high-level approach adopted by the AAA-ICDR Guidelines, the rules have been omitted from Annex 1 which seeks to distil the key applicable rules in each situation. Category CIArb Guidelines SVAMC Guidelines SCC Guidelines VIAC Guidelines AOASA Guidelines CIETAC Guidelines Definition of AI Preamble The Preamble states: ‘machine-based system that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments. Different AI systems vary in their levels of autonomy and adaptiveness after deployment’. Preliminary Provisions The Preliminary Provisions state: ‘the term “AI” refers to computer systems that perform tasks commonly associated with human cognition, such as understanding natural language, recognising complex semantic patterns, and generating human-like outputs’. Adopting EU AI Act definition Page 1 at the section titled ‘AI and its uses’ states: ‘machine-based system that is designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy and that may exhibit adaptiveness after deployment, and that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers, from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments’ and ‘AI systems are capable of adapting their behaviour to a certain degree by analysing the effects of previous actions and working autonomously’. No definition adopted Page 2 at the section titled ‘Applicability’ states: ‘While there are numerous definitions of AI (including Generative AI) published by various organisations and bodies across various industries, VIAC does not subscribe to a single definition. This is due to the speed at which AI-derived tools are proliferating, not least in the legal field. As such, a fixed definition would likely not cover the broadening range of tools that will be used in the near future’. Paragraph 3 Paragraph 3 at the section titled ‘Definitions’ states: ‘shall mean non-human power capable of performing tasks traditionally requiring human cognitive functions and human intelligence, such as sequencing and collating facts, summarising such facts, reasoning, learning, problem-solving, perception, and decision-making’. Silent Due Process and Independence Guideline 8 and 9 Guideline 8.2 provides that arbitrators should ‘avoid delegating any tasks to AI Tools, such as legal analysis, research and interpretation of facts and law, or application of the law to the facts, if such use could influence procedural or substantive decisions’. Guideline 8.3 further elaborates that ‘[a]rbitrators should also independently verify the accuracy and correctness of information obtained through AI, ensuring their judgment is free from confirmation bias and other distortions. They should conduct their own research, using AI-generated information as a supportive tool, while maintaining a critical perspective to prevent undue influence on their decisions, including through appropriate supervision’. Please also see Guidelines 8.1 and 8.4. Guideline 9.1 requires that arbitrators consult with the parties on the use of any AI tools by the arbitrators. Guideline 7 Guideline 7 requires that the arbitrator respect due process. It states: ‘An arbitrator shall not rely on AI-generated information outside the record without making appropriate disclosures to the parties beforehand and, as far as practical, allowing the parties to comment on it. Where an AI tool cannot cite sources that can be independently verified, an arbitrator shall not assume that such sources exist or are characterised accurately by the AI tool’. Guideline at page 3 The Guideline states: ‘AI tools may be used to support arbitral decision-making but cannot replace it. Arbitral Tribunals cannot delegate the decision or the reasoning leading to it to anyone or anything’ and ‘The integrity of arbitration is essential to its continued success. Transparency and accountability are key components of integrity. Arbitral Tribunals are therefore encouraged to disclose any use of AI in researching and interpreting facts and the law or applying the law to facts. Such disclosure may assist in ensuring the parties’ rights to be heard, and that the Arbitral Tribunal does not exceed its mandate’. Guideline 2 Guideline 2.1 states: ‘Arbitrators retain full control over the decision-making process. While AI can provide insights, and assist with certain tasks, arbitrators must retain full control over any decision that might have an impact on the proceedings and shall not delegate any such decision to an AI tool’, and ‘The use of AI tools by the arbitrator shall not substitute their independent analysis of the relevant factual and legal issues. AI tools may only be used to facilitate such analysis, not to replace it’. Not expressly identified However, relevant paragraphs pointing towards the recognition of due process and independence include: paragraph 10.1 which states: ‘Accountability: Tribunals are accountable for all aspects of Proceedings including the outcome. AI Tools do not substitute human judgment and analysis’. Paragraph 14.1 states: ‘Any AI-generated information must undergo independent verification before inclusion in decisions’. Sub-section ‘Precautions for Arbitra Tribunals’ The Guideline states: ‘Before employing an AI tool, a tribunal must assess the necessity and proportionality of its use, weigh efficiency gains against the risks, evaluate the data security and accuracy of the specific AI tool, and understand the legal and regulatory landscape concerning AI in the relevant jurisdiction. The guidelines emphasise that AI use must not infringe upon a party’s right to be heard and that tribunals must independently analyse facts and laws and are required to independently provide reasoning and Decisions Contained In The award’ Disclosure of usage Guideline 7 Guideline 7.1 requires that disclosure is needed ‘to the extent that its use may have an impact on the evidence, the outcome of the arbitration or otherwise involve a delegation of an express duty toward the arbitrators or any other party’. Guideline 7.2 states that the ‘[d]isclosure of the use of AI may be required to ensure transparency over its use and ultimately preserve the integrity of the arbitration and/or the validity and enforceability of the award’. Guideline 7.3 empowers the arbitrator to ‘impose certain AI-related disclosure obligations on the parties including any party-appointed experts or factual witness. In this context, arbitrators may make directions as to the type of AI covered by the obligation to disclose, circumstances in which disclosure is required, to whom disclosure is to be made and within which timeframe’. Please also refer to Guideline 7.4 (duty of continuous disclosure); Guideline 7.5 (arbitrator powers to consider any inconsistencies); Guideline 7.6 (arbitrator power to inquire upon failure to disclose); and Guideline 7.7 (measures to be taken upon failure). Guideline 3 Guideline 3 states: ‘Disclosure that AI tools were used in connection with an arbitration is not necessary as a general matter. Decisions regarding disclosure of the use of AI tools shall be made on a case-by-case basis taking account of the relevant circumstances, including due process and any applicable privilege. Where appropriate, the following details may help reproduce and evaluate the output of an AI tool: 1. the name, version, and relevant settings of the tool used; 2. a short description of how the tool was used; and 3. the complete prompt (including any template, additional context, and conversation thread) and associated output’. Silent Guideline 5 Guideline 5.1 states: ‘Arbitrators shall, within their discretion and where they consider necessary, facilitate the parties’ and any third parties’ (e.g., experts, court reporters) understanding and use of AI tools. They may wish to discuss in the case management conference, the potential use of AI in the proceedings, the requirement of disclosure as well as the potential impact of AI on the arbitration timeline and costs’. Guideline 5.2 allows the arbitrator to disclose their own use of the tool. Guideline 5.3 allows the arbitrators and the parties to come to an agreement on the use of the tool. Paragraph 12.2 Paragraph 12.2 states: ‘The use of AI and AI Tools may be covered in the Agreement or clarified by the Tribunal with the Parties. The Tribunal, the Parties, their representatives or witnesses should consider whether it is appropriate to disclose any other use of AI and AI Tools, and the nature and extent of such use’. Sub-section ‘Basic Principles’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines are built on the three principles of party autonomy, auxiliary, and good faith. With respect to party autonomy, the IA Guidelines provide that the parties are empowered to agree on the rules governing AI use. This includes deciding whether to permit, restrict, or entirely prohibit AI, as well as establishing protocols for disclosure. The auxiliary principle states that AI’s role is assistive, with the tribunal responsible for the final award. The good faith principle ensures parties remain responsible for the truthfulness and legality of their submissions’ Confidentiality Guideline 2.2 Guideline 2.2 states: ‘Confidentiality: The use of third-party AI entails substantial risks in regard to confidentiality, which is a fundamental prerequisite in the realm of arbitration. Inputting confidential data into a third-party AI Tool will inevitably raise concerns about how such data is stored, protected from outside access, used for machine learning purposes and potentially presented to third parties as output from their use of the same AI platform. Real-world AI applications can use multiple tools and platforms, which may make it difficult to identify which data is being used and where it is stored. It’s important to carefully consider the context in which the relevant data was given and whether permission has been obtained to use it for purposes beyond resolving the dispute. The use of confidentiality agreements and a deeper understanding of how such tools protect confidentiality from misuse or cyber attacks will be necessary. When evaluating and considering AI Tools, it is important to consider whether the AI Tool has been vetted, has sufficient privacy and security standards, and whether it is an open or closed model. Not all AI Tools are equal in this regard’. Guideline 2 Guideline 2 states: ‘All participants in international arbitration are responsible for ensuring their use of AI tools is consistent with their obligations to safeguard confidential information (including privileged, private, secret, or otherwise protected data). They should not submit confidential information to any AI tool without appropriate vetting and authorisation. Special attention should be paid to policies on recording, storage, and use of prompt or output histories and of any other confidential data submitted to the AI tool. Only AI tools that adequately safeguard confidentiality should be used with confidential information. Participants should assess the data use and retention policies offered by available AI tools and opt for secure solutions. Where appropriate, participants should redact or anonymise materials submitted to an AI tool’. Guideline at page 3 The Guideline states: ‘Confidentiality: Many users value arbitration’s ability to respect accords on confidentiality. The use of some AI tools may have unintended consequences for the confidentiality of an arbitration. Participants, in particular the Arbitral Tribunal, are therefore encouraged to inform themselves as to how any data input is employed and deployed when using AI’. Guideline 3 Guideline 3 states: ‘3.1. Arbitrators and parties shall respect the confidentiality of the arbitral process as a whole and ensure that AI tools are compliant with any such confidentiality obligations. 3.2. Arbitrators and parties shall thereby take all reasonable steps to prevent unauthorised access to sensitive case-related data’. Paragraph 12.3 Paragraph 12.3 states: ‘Any party using AI Tools should ensure confidentiality and security is maintained throughout the Proceeding and for as long as is legally necessary thereafter. This may be achieved by: 12.3.1 Vetting providers of AI Tools thoroughly, including their privacy policies, information storage, and security measures; 12.3.2 Considering data sovereignty, including storage and processing locations; 12.3.3 Avoiding open and free AI Tools, preferring paid subscription services with enhanced privacy; 12.3.4 Anonymising information before inputting case materials; 12.3.5 Using secure AI Tools with appropriate access controls to prevent unauthorised access to confidential information; 12.3.6 Regularly reviewing updates to the AI Tools and their terms of use such as those relating to privacy’. Silent Arbitrators’ powers Guideline 4 Guideline 4.1 states: ‘The use of AI by parties falls within the general power of arbitrators to conduct the proceedings including giving directions and making procedural rulings, subject to any express prohibition agreed by the parties or flowing from mandatory applicable laws, regulations, policies, or institutional rules, to the extent that it may have an impact on the procedure’. The tribunal is empowered to appoint AI experts to understand tools (see Guideline 4.2), order disclosure of use of the tools (see above as well as Guideline 4.4), and most importantly order consequences to follow (be it the drawing of adverse inferences or costs orders) (see Guideline 4.7). The tribunal is also empowered to regulate the use of AI (see guideline 4.3), and is encouraged to record any decision on the use of AI in a procedural order (see guideline 4.6). Guideline 4.3 states: ‘Arbitrators may regulate the use of AI by parties with a view to preserve the integrity of arbitral proceedings which they oversee and ensure the validity and enforceability of any ensuing awards’. See also Guideline 6, which looks into the arbitrator’s ability to rule on the use of AI and admissibility of AI-generated material in the arbitration record. No specific powers prescribed Commentary to Guideline 5 states: ‘The duty is to protect the integrity of the proceedings, not to mislead the arbitral tribunal, and not to submit false or adulterated evidence. Fraudulent behaviour and misconduct, such as submitting false documents or resorting to so-called “guerilla tactics”, can occur with or without the use of AI. Advancements in AI, however, particularly in generative AI and deep fakes, can heighten the risks of manipulated or false evidence, making it significantly easier to create fake evidence that can appear strikingly convincing to the naked eye or that can sometimes be virtually indistinguishable from authentic versions. It can also make it more costly or difficult to detect any such manipulation through forensic and other means. This Guideline reminds parties to be aware and vigilant of these heightened risks while emphasising the importance of ensuring the fairness and integrity of the proceedings when using AI. Parties, party representatives and experts should simply not use AI tools to fabricate evidence, distort evidence, or compromise the integrity of the proceedings under any circumstances. If the arbitral tribunal determines that a party has violated this Guideline, it may consider, in addition to any other measures available under the governing law and the applicable arbitration rules or the lex arbitri (such as, for example, striking the evidence from the record, or deeming it inadmissible), deriving adverse inferences, and taking the infringing party representatives’ conduct into account in its allocation of the costs of the arbitration’. No specific powers prescribed Guideline 5 Guideline 5.1 states: ‘Arbitrators shall, within their discretion and where they consider necessary, facilitate the parties’ and any third parties’ (e.g., experts, court reporters) understanding and use of AI tools. They may wish to discuss in the case management conference, the potential use of AI in the proceedings, the requirement of disclosure as well as the potential impact of AI on the arbitration timeline and costs’. Guideline 5.2 states: ‘Arbitrators may, within their discretion and where they consider relevant and necessary, inform the parties if they plan to use certain AI tools. The arbitrators may wish to inform the parties of the name of the AI tool and the tasks it will be employed for. The parties shall be provided with an opportunity to comment on such information’. Guideline 5.3 states: ‘Where applicable, the arbitrators and the parties may wish to consider coming to an agreement on provisions related to confidentiality and the transparent use of AI, e.g., in Procedural Order No. 1’. No specific powers prescribed Sub-section ‘Recommended Risk Mitigation Measures’ and ‘Precautions for Arbitral Tribunals’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines provide a checklist of recommended actions for: • Parties to include stipulations regarding AI usage in their arbitration agreements. • Tribunals to invite the parties to express their views on the use of AI in the procedural order or pre-hearing conference. • Using CIETAC’s own platform for services like electronic case filing and AI-powered transcription. • Engaging in training and education to better understand AI’s capabilities, limitations, and the associated legal developments’ At the sub-section titled ‘Precautions for Arbitral Tribunals’ ‘Before employing an AI tool, a tribunal must assess the necessity and proportionality of its use, weigh efficiency gains against the risks, evaluate the data security and accuracy of the specific AI tool, and understand the legal and regulatory landscape concerning AI in the relevant jurisdiction. The guidelines emphasise that AI use must not infringe upon a party’s right to be heard and that tribunals must independently analyse facts and laws and are required to independently provide reasoning and decisions contained in the award’ Parties’ usage Guideline 5 Guideline 5.1 states: ‘Subject to any limitation or prohibition flowing from applicable laws, regulations or policies, institutional rules, or the arbitration agreement, the parties may by their agreement exercise their autonomy to agree whether and how AI may be used by parties in the arbitration. The parties may also propose specific AI Tools that may or may not be utilised or agree on any limits thereof’. Guideline 5.2 states: ‘When the arbitrator receives a request for arbitration, it should ascertain whether and how the parties provided for the use of AI in their arbitration agreement’. Guideline 5.3 states: ‘In case the arbitration agreement is silent or unclear on the use of AI, and the parties have not raised the issue in their initial communications with the institution or the arbitrators, the arbitrators are encouraged to invite the parties to express their views on the subject at the appropriate time (either at the first case management conference or later)’. Guideline 5.4 states: ‘The parties may discuss the subject of the use of AI. Although, the arbitrators may intervene in the discussion to clarify what AI Tools or classes of tools may be available to the parties, how they could be used in the course of the arbitral proceedings, any risks thereof (e.g., as to accuracy, privacy etc. ), and any other issues of that the arbitrators or the institution consider the parties should be aware’. Guidelines 4 and 5 Guideline 4 states: ‘Party representatives shall observe any applicable ethical rules or professional standards of competent or diligent representation when using AI tools in the context of an arbitration. Parties shall review the output of any AI tool used to prepare submissions to verify it is accurate from a factual and legal standpoint. Parties and party representatives on record shall be deemed responsible for any uncorrected errors or inaccuracies in any output produced by an AI tool they use in an arbitration’. Guideline 5 states: ‘Parties, party representatives, and experts shall not use any forms of AI in ways that affect the integrity of the arbitration or otherwise disrupt the conduct of the proceedings. Parties, party representatives and experts shall not use any form of AI to falsify evidence, compromise the authenticity of evidence, or otherwise mislead the arbitral tribunal and/or opposing party(ies)’. Silent Guideline 5 Guideline 5.2 states: ‘The arbitrators may wish to inform the parties of the name of the AI tool and the tasks it will be employed for. The parties shall be provided with an opportunity to comment on such information’. Guideline 5.3 states: ‘Where applicable, the arbitrators and the parties may wish to consider coming to an agreement on provisions related to confidentiality and the transparent use of AI, e.g., in Procedural Order No. 1’. Paragraph 12.4 Paragraph 12.4 states: ‘Formal Agreements: The Tribunal and Parties should consider adopting written agreements to address issues such as: 12.4.1 Permitted uses of AI Tools; 12.4.2 Limitations on the use of AI Tools; 12.4.3 Disclosure obligations; 12.4.4 Confidentiality and other safeguards; 12.4.5 The Tribunal’s rights of investigation and direction in respect to the use of AI Tools’. Sub-section ‘Recommended Risk Mitigation Measures’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines provide a checklist of recommended actions for: • Parties to include stipulations regarding AI usage in their arbitration agreements. • Tribunals to invite the parties to express their views on the use of AI in the procedural order or pre-hearing conference. • Using CIETAC’s own platform for services like electronic case filing and AI-powered transcription. • Engaging in training and education to better understand AI’s capabilities, limitations, and the associated legal developments’ Understanding the risks of AI usage See generally Guideline 2, which deals with issues of: (1) Confidentiality (2) Data Integrity and Security (3) Impartiality and independence (4) Due process (5) ‘black box’ issues (6) Enforceability of arbitral awards; and (6) Impact on energy use and the environment. See generally Guideline 1, which states: ‘All participants involved in arbitration proceedings who use AI tools in preparation for or during an arbitration are responsible for familiarising themselves with the AI tool’s intended uses and should adapt their use accordingly. All participants using AI tools in connection with an arbitration should make reasonable efforts to understand each AI tool’s relevant limitations, biases, and risks and, to the extent possible, mitigate them’. Silent Silent Paragraph 11 Paragraph 11 states: ‘11. The use of AI does introduce risks to Proceedings, the Tribunal and Parties. These include: 11.1 Confidentiality and Data Security Risks: 11.1.1 Data Exposure: AI Tools may store, access or utilise confidential information for machine learning purposes; 11.1.2 Complex Data Flows: Tracking data usage and storage becomes intricate due to the multifaceted nature of AI Tools, which often employ various tools and platforms; 11.1.3 Cybersecurity Vulnerabilities: AI Tools are vulnerable to hacking and phishing threats; 11.2 Technical Limitations and Reliability Concerns; 11.2.1 Hallucinations: AI Tools can generate factually incorrect information, including fabricated legal authorities or cases; 11.2.2 “Black Box” Problem: AI Tools’ decision-making processes are opaque, hindering oversight and accountability; 11.2.3 Bias in Outputs: AI Tools reflect the biases present in their training data, potentially resulting in discriminatory outcomes; 11.2.4 Accuracy Limitations: AI Tools are challenged by nuanced legal reasoning and complex factual scenarios; 11.3 Regulatory and Enforceability Risks: 11.3.1 Inconsistent Regulations: Different jurisdictions adopt varying AI regulations, posing compliance challenges; 11.3.2 Conflict with Mandatory Rules: AI Tools may conflict with applicable laws, regulations, or institutional rules’. Sub-section ‘Benefits and Risks of Using AI’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines present a balanced view of AI’s advantages and disadvantages. Advantages include improved efficiency and quality in text generation, data analysis, evidence review and legal research, resulting in faster and more cost-effective arbitration. Disadvantages demonstrate the substantial risks associated with improper AI use such as confidentiality and data breaches, accuracy and bias, the “Black Box” issue where AI algorithms and decision mechanisms are difficult to understand, and possible enforcement risks for awards where AI has been used in the proceedings’ Benefits of AI See generally Guideline 1, which recognizes: (1) Efficiency and quality (2) Legal research (3) Data analysis (4) Text generation (5) Collecting evidence (6) Translating and interpretation (7) Transcription (8) Detecting the use of AI (9) Case analysis; and (10) Equal treatment of parties. See generally Guideline 1, as above. See Guideline at page 2, which references: (1) Legal research, concept searching (2) Editing and proof-reading (3) Translations and interpretation (4) Transcriptions (5) generating briefing notes, diagrams, and summaries of texts (6) Drafting communications and submissions (7) document production (8) document management (9) reviewing, analysing, and presenting of evidence (10) drafting cross-examination questions (11) arbitrator appointments, and (12) case-value analysis. Silent Paragraph 7 Paragraph 7 states: ‘AI can be used in Proceedings by the Tribunal, the Parties or their representatives in a number of ways, including: 7.1 To analyse and compile facts: AI can be used to extract complex and interrelated facts and summarise them and put them into chronological order; 7.2 To conduct research and analysis: AI Tools can be used to search and analyse legal precedents, statutes, and arbitral awards; 7.3 To review and manage documentation: AI Tools can be used to process case documents, identify relevant materials, and organise them, while highlighting inconsistencies and manipulation; 7.4 To speed up decision making: AI Tools can be used to mine text and objectively identify patterns and inconsistencies to facilitate evidence and data analysis; 7.5 To generate text: AI Tools can be used to draft procedural documents, summarise arguments, collate facts, arrange data in a logical sequence and assist in drafting; 7.6 To facilitate Proceedings: AI Tools can be used to provide real-time and cost-effective translation or transcription of hearings; 7.7 To automate administrative tasks: AI Tools can automate scheduling, communication, and other procedural tasks’. Paragraph 8 also states: ‘8. AI Tools may also assist Parties by: 8.1 Reducing Costs: AI automates document review and labour-intensive tasks, thereby reducing costs associated with Proceedings; 8.2 Levelling the playing field: 8.2.1 Empowering Parties with less resources: AI enables Parties with limited resources to undertake sophisticated legal analysis, expanding their capabilities; 8.2.2 Assisting Unrepresented Parties: AI Tools facilitate the participation of unrepresented or underrepresented Parties’. Sub-section ‘Benefits and Risks of Using AI’ The Guideline states: ‘The AI Guidelines present a balanced view of AI’s advantages and disadvantages. Advantages include improved efficiency and quality in text generation, data analysis, evidence review and legal research, resulting in faster and more cost-effective arbitration. Disadvantages demonstrate the substantial risks associated with improper AI use such as confidentiality and data breaches, accuracy and bias, the “Black Box” issue where AI algorithms and decision mechanisms are difficult to understand, and possible enforcement risks for awards where AI has been used in the proceedings’

title: How to Teach an Old Dog New Tricks: Appeals and the English Arbitration Law Reform

sections.title: HISTORICAL BACKGROUND OF APPEALS ON POINT OF LAW UNDER ENGLISH LAW
sections.text: 1 HISTORICAL BACKGROUND OF APPEALS ON POINT OF LAW UNDER ENGLISH LAW The court’s review of the merit of an arbitration award is unique to English jurisprudence, as enunciated in the pre-1950 Arbitration Act (AA) regime.1 Lord Hacking, The ‘Stated Case’ Abolished: The United Kingdom Arbitration Act of 1979 14(1) Am. B. Ass’n 95–102, 96 (1980). Such review of the merits of an arbitration award has a distinct history in English law, rooted in the AA 1950, which preserved the stated case procedure. This allowed either party to require the arbitrator to refer a legal question arising during proceedings to the court, enabling wide judicial oversight of arbitral decisions. However, this mechanism was increasingly seen as undermining finality and efficiency. Hence, it is not surprising that section 69 of the ‘AA 1996’, which permits appeals on a question of law, was notably influenced by the previous English AAs and courts’ guidance. The trend of supporting the finality of arbitral awards over judicial reviews first emerged under the English ‘AA 1979’ and courts’ decisions thereafter.2 The Arbitration Act 1979 s. 1(1). See also Pioneer Shipping Ltd. and Others v. B.T.P. Tioxide Ltd. (The Nema) [1981] 3 W.L.R. 292; Antaios Compania Naviera Sa v. Salen Rederierna Ab (‘The Antaios’) [1985] AC 191[1982] A.C. 724. The AA 1979 marked a significant shift by abolishing the stated case procedure under the AA 1950 and prohibiting the court from setting aside an arbitral award on the grounds of errors of fact or law.3 The AA 1979, s. 1(1). However, although this prohibition was mainly restricted to procedural matters, once an award is before the court it may accept the application of either party for a review on the question of law.4 The Nema, supra n. 2, at 730 (Lord Roskill). This shift laid the foundation for section 69 of the AA 1996, which permitted appeals only on a question of law and only with the court’s permission or the parties’ agreement. The evolution from broad judicial review to a more restrained model reflected a deliberate policy choice favouring the finality of arbitral awards over a merit-based court review, a trend that continued under the AA 1996. Furthermore, the House of Lords in the case of Pioneer Shipping Ltd. and Others v. B.T.P. Tioxide Ltd (hereinafter ‘The Nema’),5 Ibid. and in Antaios Compania Naviera Sa v. Salen Rederierna Ab (hereinafter ‘The Antaios’),6 The Antaios, supra n. 2. provided important guidelines regarding appeals on a point of law. In The Nema,7 The Nema, supra n. 2. Lord Roskill emphasized that questions of fact fall exclusively within the tribunal’s jurisdiction and should not be usurped by the court.8 Ibid., at 753. He further held that the High Court in exercising its discretion to grant a leave must balance the finality of arbitral awards against reviewing their merits.9 Ibid., at 733. In addition, Lord Diplock observed a clear parliamentary trend favouring the finality of arbitration over the legal review of arbitral awards.10 Ibid., at 739–40. Under the AA 1979, appealing on a point of law could be brought either with the consent of all the parties to arbitration or with the leave of the High Court.11 The AA 1979, s. 1(3)(a) and (b). However, leave would not be granted unless the court, considering all circumstances12 Ibid., s. 1(4). and exercising its discretion,13 The Nema, supra n. 2, at 730 (Lord Roskill). was satisfied that the ‘determination of the question of law concerned could substantially affect the rights of one or more of the parties to the arbitration agreement’.14 The AA 1979, s. 1(4). The court had the discretion to impose conditions on the grant of the leave if the applicant’s case was weak, such as requiring payment of the whole or part of the award into the court or the provision of security.15 Ibid., s. 1(4); The Nema, supra n. 2, at 739 (Lord Diplock). Moreover, section 1(7) of the Act restricted appeals from the High Court to the Court of Appeal. Leave had to be granted by the High Court or the Court of Appeal, and the High Court was required to certify that the question of law was one of general public importance or one which for some other special reason deserved consideration by the Court of Appeal.16 The AA 1979, s. 1(7)(a) and (b).

sections.title: CHALLENGING ARBITRAL AWARDS
sections.text: 2 CHALLENGING ARBITRAL AWARDS Under English law, the starting point is that an arbitral award is final and binding on the parties unless challenge is possible in accordance with the AA 1996. This principle of ‘finality of the award’ reflects both the text of the AA and the approach of the English courts. In other words, the courts will generally seek to uphold and enforce arbitral awards interpreting them with a degree of deference.17 C. Ambrose, K. Maxwell & M. Collett, London Maritime Arbitration, Ch. 22, §22.1, 371 (4th ed., Informa 2018); MRI Trading AG v. Erdenet Mining Corp LLC [2013] EWCA Civ 156, [2013] Lloyd’s Rep 638, at 23; Zermalt Holdings v. Nu-Life Upholstery Repairs [1985] 2 EGLR 14 (Comm). This is further justified by the recognition that time-consuming and costly challenges in the courts are directly contrary to the parties’ objective in choosing arbitration as an efficient means of dispute resolution by their chosen tribunal. In essence, the AA 1996 aims to strike a balance between the interest of ensuring a right to challenge, in cases where the award or arbitration is plainly outside what the parties could reasonably have expected,18 The 1996 Departmental Advisory Committee (DAC) Report on the English Arbitration Bill, Feb. 1996, paras §§280, 285. and the principle of giving effect to the parties’ agreement to arbitrate.
