In short, section 69 embodies a careful compromise that safeguards the integrity of the arbitral process, whilst upholding the central pillar upon which arbitration is founded, i.e., party autonomy. The Law Commission’s recent review and recommendations demonstrate that sometimes a reform does not need to involve groundbreaking changes but can lie in fine tuning aspects of a system that is already functioning well.

5 THE CASE FOR REFORM OF SECTION 67 OF THE AA 1996 Section 67 of the AA 1996 provides for challenges of an award on the ground that it was made without jurisdiction. The AA 2025 revised section 67 in its Clause 11 to ensure through insertion of Rules of Court that no new evidence or new arguments may be introduced. An exception was proposed, allowing a new ground for challenge if the objecting party did not know or could not with reasonable diligence have discovered the grounds for objection earlier. The concern which the Law Commission had aimed to address with its proposal was that the current approach had ‘the potential to cause delay and increase in costs through repetition’ and raised ‘a basic question of fairness’.155 Law Commission, supra n. 153, paras 9.16–9.17. In other words, it allowed the losing (objecting) party ‘a second bite of the cherry’.156 Ibid., para. 9.18. Awards can be challenged under section 67 on the basis that the arbitral tribunal lacked jurisdiction. Clause 10 of the AA 2025 amends section 67 to provide the remedies, including remittance for reconsideration, and declaring the award to be of no effect. This aligns the remedies available under section 67 with those available under section 68 and section 69. This also reflects the assumption in the case law that these remedies were intended to be available. In Dallah v. Pakistan 157 Dallah v. Pakistan, supra n. 26. the Supreme Court had held that even where the question of the tribunal’s jurisdiction has been fully debated before the tribunal, a challenge under section 67 is a full rehearing by the court. Clause 11 amends section 67 to confer powers for Rules of Court to restrict this rehearing. Where an application is made under section 67 by a party that took part in the arbitration, and relates to an objection on which the tribunal has already ruled, there will be no full rehearing, contrary to Dallah v. Pakistan,158 Ibid. unless it is not reasonably possible to put such objections before the tribunal – but even then, the evidence is not to be reheard by the court.159 Arbitration Bill [HL], Explanatory Notes, https://bills.parliament.uk/publications/55946/documents/4959 (accessed 28 Jan. 2025). The new, more restrictive approach seeks to limit the broad discretion on evidence and challenges which have been witnessed in cases such as Diag Human and Stava v. Czech Republic. 160 Diag & Mr Josef Stava v. Czech Republic, supra n. 69. Nevertheless, there has been some resistance to this proposal, as some argue that the theoretical objectives of these reforms are not substantiated with practical evidence.161 Alexander Gunning, Has A Sufficient Case Been Made For The Law Commission’s Proposals In Respect of Section 67? 40 Arb. Int’l 25–35 (2024), doi: 10.1093/arbint/aiad048; Jacob Grierson, Two Brief Comments on the Law Commission’s Proposed Reform of the Arbitration Act 1996 39(6) J. Int’l Arb. 765–774 (2022), doi: 10.54648/JOIA2022033.

6 CONCLUSIONS This evaluation of section 69 and our analysis of recent case law support the view that section 69 remains good law and continues to serve its intended function. While questions have been raised about its effectiveness, any review of the appeals mechanism under section 69 does not necessarily call for statutory reform. Rather, further judicial guidance could help clarify the types of questions that properly fall within the scope of a section 69 appeal – even when parties attempt to frame factual disputes as legal questions. Clearer parameters would assist parties in assessing whether an appeal is viable.162 Finch, supra n. 146, at 264–266. The point is not to deny appeals because questions of facts are brought and cloaked as questions of law. The practice of bringing questions of facts presented as questions of law is a long-standing one. Appeals should be allowed where there are valid grounds, not least so as to permit arbitration law to come before the courts and thereby also develop. However, it is necessary that detailed and consistent criteria should be applied to distinguish genuine legal questions from factual disputes in disguise to prevent abuse of the appeal mechanism. Such criteria have already been laid down by the judiciary in previous judgments and could be further defined and elaborated in future case law. A clear example of the principles that should guide substantive appeals on arbitration is found in Kershaw Mechanical Services Ltd. v. Kendrick Construction Ltd. 163 Kershaw Mechanical Services Ltd. v. Kendrick Construction Ltd. [2006] EWHC 727, [2006] 4 All ER 79. Jackson J held that courts should read arbitral awards as a whole, in a fair and reasonable way, and not engage in overly technical or minute textual analysis. He also stated that courts should accord some deference to arbitrators’ decisions, particularly where the arbitrators are experts in the relevant field disputed, and to intervene only if they are satisfied that the arbitrator, despite such expertise, reached the wrong conclusion. The development of such criteria will serve two aims: first, to preserve the proper functioning of the right to appeal; and second, to ensure that leave to appeal is granted only on the basis of the existence of objective grounds and criteria. This would safeguard the core principles of arbitration – its finality, autonomy, and flexibility – while maintaining public confidence in the system as a legitimate and effective means of resolving disputes. The justification for retaining section 69 is closely tied to the importance of preserving a final recourse to the courts, which also leads to the progress of the commercial law system. Among those advocating for the abolition of section 69 there is a faction that argues that the reason for abolishing it is the fact that it restricts the development of commercial law, which should be attained by arbitration tribunals; and if the courts are prevented, due to a statutory restriction, from shaping the law through appellate review, the responsibility for legal development must necessarily shift to arbitrators.164 Esposito, supra n. 148, at 435–438. This view reveals many pitfalls. As demonstrated and argued in this article, the reality is quite to the contrary. Having a right of appeal in court means that more arbitration law cases will be heard in court, and this will strengthen commercial law. The approach to the appeals against arbitral awards remains sparse and fragmented, as our discussion has shown. The English Law Commission has codified a high threshold requirement for an appeal under section 69, to preserve the final and binding nature of arbitration, with a limited appellate review option being available, in an effort to ensure that justice prevails where there are obvious errors of law. Any law reform pertaining to arbitration appeals should be carefully considered to uphold the principles of party autonomy and procedural flexibility while guarding against erosion of arbitration’s core feature, finality. The Tricon Energy Ltd v. MTM Trading LLC,165 Tricon, supra n. 74. Alegrow SA v. Yayla Argo Gida San ve Nak A.S.,166 Alegrow, supra n. 75. and Sharp. Corp Ltd v. Viterra BV 167 Sharp, supra n. 83. cases are recent examples of rare success in challenging arbitral awards on points of law. The English High Court did not lay down any new principles but simply overturned the awards in these cases owing to their particular facts due to obvious errors on a point of law. These cases cannot however be considered as reversing the past trend of allowing only a very small number of section 69 appeals on a point of law. Furthermore, the High Court should be cautious when granting a leave to appeal on questions of law that ensures a proper compliance with the strict requirements under section 69, in particular when applying the two-pronged test that requires the question of law to be one of general public importance, and that the decision of the tribunal must at least be open to serious doubt. These requirements are codified to uphold the finality of arbitral awards and to prevent unnecessary leave to appeal in the English High Court. The discussion in this article has shown that there is a crucial distinction between appeals on a point of fact and appeals on a point of law, with only the latter being under the scope of section 69. The interpretation and application of section 69 by the judiciary has demonstrated that there are a number of instances of appeals on points of law, but only a few have actually been successful because of the higher threshold established and maintained by English courts. This reinforces the need to continue to examine the functionality and effectiveness of section 69 in light of the reform enacted by the Law Commission, with a view to preserving arbitration’s integrity while allowing for limited and principled judicial oversight.

title: DAA 2015 Quo Vadis? An Empirical and Substantive Analysis of Decisions on Applications for Set-Aside of Arbitral Awards under the Dutch Arbitration Act of 2015: Has the Dutch Legislator Reached Its Objectives?

sections.title: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: 1 INTRODUCTION Being host to the Permanent Court of Arbitration at the Peace Palace1 Pursuant to Art. 22, para. 1, of the Convention pour le Règlement Pacifique des conflits internationaux of 1899 and Art. 43 of the Convention pour le Règlement Pacifique des conflits internationaux of 1907. and various other international organizations,2 Including the International Criminal Court, Eurojust and The Hague Conference on International Private Law. The Dutch government has made available a list of international organizations in the Netherlands at https://www.government.nl/topics/international-organisations/multilateral-forums (accessed 30 Jul. 2025). the Netherlands is a prime venue both for international arbitration and for recognition and enforcement of arbitral awards. The Netherlands has a long history as a trading nation. It benefits from a strong network of international investment treaties and tax arrangements. International arbitration is a frequently used and broadly accepted method of dispute resolution. This holds true for both general commercial disputes and for more sector-specific matters, such as those concerning construction and in relation to mergers and acquisitions. The Dutch Arbitration Act (DAA) applies to Netherlands-seated arbitrations. It is incorporated in Book 4 of the Dutch Code of Civil Procedure (DCCP). On the back of the Dutch legislature’s considerable support for arbitration, an updated DAA (DAA 2015) entered into force on 1 January 2015.3 As per the publication of the DAA 2015 in the Dutch Bulletin of Acts and Decrees 2014, no. 200. The DAA 2015 applies to arbitral proceedings that commenced on or after 1 January 2015 and related court proceedings.4 On the DAA 2015s transitory provisions, see s. 3.4 below. The DAA 2015 comprehensively updated the DAA 1986, which had undergone only minor changes.5 Sections 2 and 3 below provide a brief overview of the historical development of the DAA. The DAA 2015, since its entry into force ten years ago, has subsequently been the subject of analysis and development by arbitrators, courts and academics alike. This contribution investigates whether the application of the DAA 2015 in set-aside matters6 The term ‘annulment’ is also commonly used as an English translation for the Dutch term ‘vernietiging’. Vacatur appears to be less commonly used. In this article, I shall consistently employ the term ‘set-aside’. meets the objectives communicated by the legislator in the Parliamentary process. It comprises two parts. Part I sets the scene and discusses the genesis of the DAA 2015. Part II analyses the most relevant decided cases in setting-aside proceedings. This part includes both empirical (section 5) and dogmatic analyses (section 6) of published set-aside decisions in Netherlands-seated arbitrations. The research finds that the DAA 2015 appears to meet the legislator’s objective to reduce the number of successful applications for set-aside of an arbitral award – bringing down the number of set-aside applications granted by about two-thirds (from 23% to 6.8% of cases) when compared with the DAA 1986. It is submitted that three key areas remain in which further clarity on the state of Dutch arbitration law is desirable. PART I. SETTING THE SCENE: GENESIS AND KEY OBJECTIVES OF THE DAA 2015

sections.title: HISTORICAL DEVELOPMENT OF THE DAA: TOWARDS THE DAA OF 1986
sections.text: 2 HISTORICAL DEVELOPMENT OF THE DAA: TOWARDS THE DAA OF 1986 Arbitration has been part of the DCCP ever since 1 October 1838. The provisions central to arbitration – originally anchored in Articles 620–657 of the DCCP – remained in place until 1986. During this period spanning almost 150 years, Dutch arbitration law developed through decisions of courts and tribunals, as well as by way of legal scholarship.7 H. J. Snijders, Nederlands Arbitragerecht 59–60 (5th ed., Deventer: Wolters Kluwer 2018). In the early 1980s, it became increasingly apparent that a revision of the existing DCCP provisions on arbitration was needed.8 The arguments for and against a revision of the DAA are conveniently summarized in the contribution by J. P. Franx, Het Ontwerp Boek IV van het Wetboek van Burgerlijke Rechtsvordering in het licht van internationale arbitrage en vergeleken met het Ontwerp UNCITRAL Modelwet en het Franse recht inzake internationale arbitrage 3 et seq. (Deventer: FED 1985). Hence, drafting commenced on the DAA 1986. The DAA 1986 originated around the same time as the UNCITRAL Model Law of 1985. As the auctor intellectualis 9 Snijders, supra n. 7, at 60 (footnote 92). For that matter, Sanders was also one of the founding fathers of the New York Convention, as well as the founder and inaugural editor of the ICCA Publications. See http://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2012/10/02/piet-sanders-1912–2012/ (accessed 30 Jul. 2025) Rightly he is considered at the ‘historic treasures’ website of the ICCA, https://www.arbitration-icca.org/historic-treasures (accessed 30 Jul. 2025). On the work of UNCITRAL on Arbitration and Conciliation, see P. Sanders, The Work of UNCITRAL on Arbitration and Conciliation (Alphen aan den Rijn: Kluwer Law International 2004). of the DAA 1986, the late Prof. Piet Sanders,10 Sanders is considered one of the most influential Dutch arbitration lawyers of all time. See V. Timmerman et al., Piet Sanders: een honderdjarige vernieuwer (The Hague: Boom 2012). shows, the DAA 1986 is strongly influenced by the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules of 1976 and the UNCITRAL Model Law of 1985. The objective of the DAA 1986 was to improve ‘the climate for international arbitration in The Netherlands’.11 A. J. Van den Berg, Internationale arbitrage in Nederland onder de huidige arbitragewet 170 (TvA 4, 1996). A preliminary draft of the DAA 1986 – also inspired by the then recently revised arbitration acts of Switzerland and France12 Snijders, supra n. 7, at 60. – was scrutinized at a 1982 symposium, attended by 140 specialists from the field.13 P. Sanders, Geschiedenis van de Nederlandse Arbitragewet 122 (TvA 4, 1996). A governmental committee subsequently reviewed this preliminary draft, before a bill was submitted to the Dutch parliament in 1984.14 The 1984 draft as submitted to the Dutch parliament is insightfully discussed in P. Sanders, International Commercial Arbitration, vol. iv (Alphen aan den Rijn: Kluwer Law International 1984). This bill was passed into law on 1 July 1986, and entered into force on 1 December of that same year.15 Law of 2 Jul. 1986 (Stb. 1986), at 372. The DAA 1986 is incorporated as Book 4 of the DCCP. Book 4 comprises two sections: Articles 1020–1073 (Arbitration in the Netherlands) and Articles 1074–1076 DAA (Arbitration outside of the Netherlands). The latter section is modest in size and nature. It mainly concerns court assistance in relation to arbitration proceedings abroad, including recognition and enforcement.16 Snijders, supra n. 7, at 621. Arguably, the two most pertinent features of the DAA 1986 are (1) its focus on party autonomy, and (2) its monistic character. With respect to party autonomy, parliamentary records confirm the legislature’s desire that the DAA be largely default in nature, allowing the parties to derogate from such provisions by agreement.17 Memorandum of Reply (Old Act), at 2. The focus on party autonomy served to ensure that the Netherlands retain its position as a go-to jurisdiction for international arbitration.18 A. Marsman, International Arbitration in the Netherlands, With a Commentary on the NAI and PCA Arbitration Rules 26 (Kluwer Law International 2021), supported by Van den Berg, supra n. 11, at 170. Different from the Swiss and French legislatures, Dutch lawmakers opted for a monistic model, thus avoiding a stand-alone arrangement for international arbitrations.19 Snijders, supra n. 7, at 68. Even though a switch to a dualistic system was suggested,20 Somewhat reluctantly by F. de Ly, De herziening van de Arbitragewet en de internationale arbitrage 35 (TvA 2005), yet more forcefully by Peters, after the passing of DAA 2015 into law. See N. Peters, Drie jaar nieuwe arbitragewet: tien suggesties voor verbetering (TCR 4 2018). the DAA 2015 retained the monistic approach.

sections.title: THE DAA 2015
sections.text: 3 THE DAA 2015 3.1 Key objectives Although a 1996 article by Albert Jan van den Berg praised the DAA 1986 as ‘one of the most modern arbitration acts of the time’, the author cautioned the Dutch arbitration community not to be complacent and proposed a number of potential improvements.21 Van den Berg, supra n. 11, at 170. Subsequently, a working group of eminent Dutch arbitration practitioners drafted a preliminary proposal for a modernized version of the DAA 1986. This proposal was published in 2005 (the Van den Berg Proposal).22 Van den Berg, TvA 36 (2005), at 65–121. Notably, the Van den Berg Proposal considered various international developments, including the planned revision of the UNCITRAL Model Law that was ultimately completed in 2006.23 On which: I. Bantekas & P. Ortolani et al., UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration – A Commentary (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press 2020), with further references. The legislature took considerable time to take the Van den Berg Proposal forward. On 31 October 2011,24 Annex to Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, no. 3 (Explanatory Memorandum). the Ministry of Justice issued a draft for consultation as part of a so-called ‘Innovation Agenda’. This led to the introduction of the bill constituting the revision of the DAA 1986 in parliament.25 Ibid., at 1–2. The bill was ultimately approved and converted into law as per 1 January 2015.26 Marsman, supra n. 18, at 26–30, and, in Dutch, Snijders, supra n. 7, at 61–68, both elaborately discuss the process of the induction of the DAA 2015. Where the DAA 1986 recodified approximately 150 years of Dutch and international arbitral practice, the DAA 2015 retained the systematics incorporated in the DAA 1986. The DAA 2015 encompasses a modernization of the DAA 1986, rather than a grassroots revision.27 As provided in Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, supra n. 24, at 4: ‘[A]s noted, the bill modernises the Dutch arbitration rules. In accordance with the advice by the Advisory Committee for civil procedural law, an amending Act, rather than a full-scale provision, is proposed’. (‘Zoals gezegd, moderniseert het wetsvoorstel de Nederlandse arbitrageregels. Hierbij is, in overeenstemming met het advies van de Adviescommissie voor burgerlijk procesrecht, gekozen voor een wijzigingswet in plaats van een integrale herziening’.) Consequently, decided cases and doctrine developed under the DAA 1986 remain relevant in the application of the DAA 2015. Moreover, the DAA 1986 remains applicable to arbitrations commenced before 1 January 2015, including any enforcement of arbitral awards rendered under the DAA 1986.28 Netherlands Supreme Court 24 Dec. 2021, ECLI:NL:HR:2021:1990. Thus, the DAA 2015 constitutes, first and foremost, an effort to modernize Dutch arbitration law through the incorporation of national and international developments.29 This also follows from the DAA 2015s formal name (‘Act of 2 June 2014 for the amendment of Books 3, 6 and 10 of the Civil Code and Book Four of the Code of Civil Procedure in connection with the modernization of arbitration law’). In addition, the DAA 2015 sought to (1) simplify and clarify arbitral procedure,30 Amongst others, through a clarification of terminology, on which see G. J. Meijer & P. E. Ernste, De arbitragewet 2015, bezien in het licht van dwingend en regelend recht, 176(3) Rechtsgeleerd Magazijn Themis, 95–106 (2015). (2) reduce administrative costs associated with arbitration,31 In particular by removing a requirement to deposit an arbitral award with the District Court of the seat. See Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, supra n. 24. (3) limit the involvement of state courts, (4) limit the number of successful set-aside applications and the duration of set-aside proceedings, and (5) increase party autonomy.32 Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, supra n. 24, 1–2; Marsman, supra n. 18, paras 2–009 to 2–010. These objectives follow from the referenced ‘Innovation Agenda’, which sought to remove hurdles for out-of-court dispute settlement, including through arbitration.33 Annex to Parliamentary Documents II 2011/12, 33071, no. 5. 3.2 Key features: General The DAA 2015 was inspired by the 2006 revisions of the UNCITRAL Model Law and the analyses underlying the Van den Berg Proposal.34 Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, supra n. 24, at 2. Marsman references ‘the ordering of interim measures during the pendency of the arbitration (Article 1043b(1) DCCP), the possibility of communicating by electronic means (Article 1072b DCCP) and the ability for remission of the case by the set-aside court to the tribunal to eliminate a set-aside ground (Article 1065a DCCP)’ in particular.35 Marsman, supra n. 18, at 27–28. The DAA 2015 does, however, diverge from the UNCITRAL Model Law in a number of relevant respects. I reference the analysis by Marsman, who usefully notes the following summary of key differences:• A decision by the tribunal to uphold its jurisdiction cannot be challenged before a State court until after the tribunal has rendered a final or partial award (Article 1052(4) DCCP); there is therefore generally no ‘interim’ appeal to a State court on the issue of jurisdiction (as under Article 16(3) UNCITRAL Model Law 2006). • An objection that the tribunal lacks jurisdiction must be raised at the latest when submitting a defence on the merits (Articles 1052(2) and (3) DCCP), without scope for the tribunal to admit an objection that was submitted late (as under Article 16(2) UNCITRAL Model Law 2006). • The statements of claim and defence must be accompanied as far as possible by the documents on which the parties rely (Article 1040(1) DCCP); the parties are not at liberty to suffice with a reference to documents they will submit later (as under Article 23(1) UNCITRAL Model Law 2006). • The tribunal is at liberty to grant any type of interim measure that it deems appropriate in the circumstances (Article 1043b DCCP), and is not limited to certain specific types of measures (as is the case under Article 17(2) UNCITRAL Model Law 2006). • Nor is the tribunal required to apply a standard of irreparable harm (as under Article 17A(1)(a) UNCITRAL Model Law 2006): • The State courts lack jurisdiction to grant an interim measure if that measure can be timely obtained in arbitration (Article 1022c DCCP), and there is no scope for the State courts to become involved if so (as under Articles 9 and 17J UNCITRAL Model Law 2006). • The tribunal is at liberty to directly determine the applicable law in the manner that it deems appropriate in the circumstances (Article 1054(2) DCCP), and is not required to make this determination based on a set of conflicts of laws rules (as under Article 28(2) UNCITRAL Model Law 2006). • The tribunal may summarily award the claimant’s claims if no defence was submitted (Article 1043a(2) and (3) DCCP), and is not required to continue the proceedings (as under Article 25(b) UNCITRAL Model Law 2006).36 Marsman, supra n. 18, at 28–29. A comprehensive overview of the differences between the preliminary proposal by the Van den Berg Commission and the DAA 2015 is available in Dutch. See Snijders, supra n. 7, at 66–68. Marsman also submits that under Art. 1048a DAA 2015, ‘[p]rovisions of both non-mandatory and mandatory law are deemed waived if a party does not timely object in the arbitration when a violation of such provisions occurs (Article 1048a DCCP), rather than merely provisions of non-mandatory law (as under Article 4 UNCITRAL Model Law 2006)’. It is unsettled to what extent Art. 1048a DCCP applies to matters of public policy – such due process principles under Art. 1036(2) DCCP. The key features of the DAA 2015s regime as to set-aside and recognition and enforcement are discussed in greater detail in Part II below. For now, suffice to note that the Dutch legislature’s deviations from the UNCITRAL Model Law resulted from its desire to promote and enhance the Netherlands as a venue for international arbitrations.37 Memorandum in Response to the Report, Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, no. 5, at 2–3. 3.3 Mandatory and default provisions The drafts for the DAA 1986 initially only allowed deviation from the DAA 1986 where ‘specifically provided’.38 Parliamentary Documents II 1985/86, 18464, no. 6 (Explanatory Memorandum), 2. See also Snijders, supra n. 7, at 73; and Meijer & Ernste, supra n. 30, at 95–106. Ultimately, in the DAA 2015, the drafters took a more liberal approach, significantly expanding the number of default provisions.39 Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, supra n. 24, at 5. These provisions are recognized by their particular wording: ‘where agreed between the parties’ (als door de partijen is overeengekomen), ‘by agreement’ (bij overeenkomst), or ‘unless the parties have agreed otherwise’ (tenzij de partijen anders zijn overeengekomen).40 Ibid. A pertinent point on which the DAA 2015 allows for additional autonomy for the parties is the possibility for parties to agree that a request for challenge of an arbitrator is dealt with by an independent third person (other than the provisional relief judge of the district court). This particular provision – proposed by Van den Berg in 199641 Van den Berg, supra n. 11, at 170. and specifically requested to be introduced by the ICC, the Permanent Court of Arbitration (PCA) and the Netherlands Arbitration Institute (NAI)42 Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, supra n. 24, at 6. – is laid down in Article 1035(7) DAA 2015. 3.4 Transitory provisions The DAA 2015 stipulates that it applies to all arbitrations and related court proceedings commenced on or after 1 January 2015. The same applies to court proceedings initiated on or after 1 January 2015 in matters where arbitration proceedings are yet to be commenced - including with respect to court proceedings in aid of arbitration.43 As per Art. 1022a–c DAA 2015 (for Dutch-seated arbitrations) and Art. 1074a–d DAA 2015 (for foreign arbitrations), courts decline jurisdiction in cases where the requested relief may be timely obtained in arbitration. This matter is a subject of intensive debate in Dutch legal literature. In addition to the treatises by Snijders, supra n. 7, Note to Art. 1022c DAA, and Meijer & Ernste, Asser Procesrecht 8 Arbitrage en bindend advies (Deventer: Kluwer 2024), paras 274 et seq., for further reading I refer to T. Stouten & L. Stevens, Het tijdigheidsvereiste van artikel 1022c en artikel 1074d Rv in kort geding (TvA 25, 2016); A. D. Josephus Jitta, Reactie op ‘Het tijdigheidsvereiste van artikel 1022c en artikel 1074d Rv in kort geding (TvA 68, 2016); L. C. M. Berger & s. M. Campmans, Kort geding en arbitrage (Bb 16, 2017); Asser Procesrecht/Boonekamp 6 2020/50; and N. Peters, De vraag naar de uitvoerbaarheid van arbitrale voorlopige voorzieningen in het Koninkrijk mede bezien in het licht van art. 1022c Rv en art. 1074d Rv, (TvA 2, 2022). Conversely, the DAA 1986 remains applicable to all proceedings commenced before 1 January 2015, including related court proceedings. These transitory arrangements appear fairly straightforward. They did, nonetheless, invite discussion in Dutch scholarly writing. Snijders submitted that the DAA 1986 should be applicable to request proceedings (verzoekschriftprocedures) – exequatur proceedings in particular – initiated after 1 January 2015 with respect to foreign arbitral proceedings initiated before 1 January 2015.44 Snijders, supra n. 7, at 70. Meijer was of the view that a foreign arbitral award only enters the Dutch legal sphere at the time when a request for the recognition and/or enforcement of such an award has been submitted to the Dutch courts. Consequently, he submitted, only the DAA applicable at the moment of actual submission of the request for exequatur is applicable to such request.45 G. J. Meijer, T&C Burgerlijke Rechtsvordering (2022), n. 1b. In a similar vein: C. L. Schleijpen, JBPr 2019/19, at 5, and B. R. D. Hoebeke, Arbitragewet 2015: Le roi est mort, vive le roi!, 907 (JBPr 2015). The Netherlands Supreme Court clarified this point in its (widely commented upon) decision of 24 December 2021.46 See Netherlands Supreme Court 24 Dec. 2021, ECLI:NL:HR:2021:1990, JIN 2022/30, case note by I. W. Osch; JBPr 2022/23 case note by I. P. M. Nieuwendijk; and H. J. Snijders, TvA 1 (2022). The decision was confirmed in Netherlands Supreme Court 7 Jul. 2023, ECLI:NL:HR:2023:1072. It ruled that the DAA 1986 applies to request proceedings (verzoekschriftprocedures) initiated after 1 January 2015 with respect to foreign arbitral proceedings initiated before 1 January 2015. The Supreme Court considers that the applicable transitory provisions are ‘exhaustive’ in nature. According to the Supreme Court, such provisions apply equally to national and foreign arbitrations, as neither the wording of the law nor parliamentary records prescribe that the transitory provisions (to the DAA 2015) do not apply to foreign arbitrations.47 See the Netherlands Supreme Court’s decision of 24 Dec. 2021 at paras 4.1.4 and 4.1.5. This discussion further underlines the importance of the DAA 1986, which will likely retain practical relevance for years to come. PART II. SETTING-ASIDE UNDER THE DAA 2015

sections.title: INTRODUCTION: FRAMEWORK AND RELEVANT AMENDMENTS IN THE DAA 2015
sections.text: 4 INTRODUCTION: FRAMEWORK AND RELEVANT AMENDMENTS IN THE DAA 2015 Articles 1064 through 1067 DAA 2015 concern set-aside of arbitral awards rendered in the Netherlands. Article 106448 Article 1064 DCCP is a general provision, noting that a final or a partial final arbitral award is not subject to any remedy except set-aside and revocation. and Article 1068 DAA 2015 provide the framework applicable to revocation. Although set-aside and revocation are both ways to challenge an arbitral award, they differ significantly in scope and grounds.49 See generally, Meijer & Ernste, supra n. 43, paras 476 et seq. (for set-aside) and paras 561 et seq. (for revocation). Set-aside focuses on procedural defects or serious flaws in the arbitral process itself; revocation is a more limited remedy, targeted at situations where the award is based on fraud, forgery of documents, or the surfacing of documents that would have changed the outcome of the arbitration, all discovered after the award was made.50 It is not settled to what extent a causal link is required between the alleged fraud or forgery and the arbitral award. There appears to be no requirement that it must be positively established that the arbitral proceedings would have had a different outcome had the ground for revocation not occurred. See Netherlands Supreme Court, 21 Mar. 1997 (ECLI:NL:HR:1997:ZC2314), NJ 206 (1998) (Benetton/Eco Swiss), para. 3.5. These two forms of recourse against arbitral awards are intended to be exhaustive, apart from any agreed-upon arbitral appeal (Article 1061a DAA 2015).51 On which: G. J. Meijer & P. E. Ernste, Interface perikelen rondom arbitraal hoger beroep (TvA 32 2018). The DAA 2015 does not provide for the possibility for parties to agree to alternative or additional forms of recourse against arbitral awards.52 Peters has proposed introducing the possibility of initiating set-aside proceedings before arbitral tribunals. N. Peters, Vernietiging van arbitrale vonnissen door ad-hocscheidsgerechten, is dat niet vernieuwend?, in Piet Sanders: een honderdjarige vernieuwer 217–223 (V. Timmerman, G. Meijer, P. Storm eds, The Hague: Boom Juridische uitgevers 2012). In exceptional circumstances, a party may be successful in submitting, in enforcement proceedings under Article 438 DCCP,53 Snijders, supra n. 7, Art. 1064 DAA, n. 1, at 540 and references in footnote 7; Snijders, supra n. 7, at 584, Art. 1066 DAA, n. 2, and references in footnote 12. See Netherlands Supreme Court 23 Mar. 2007, ECLI:NL:HR:2007:AZ5441, NJ 2007/177 for the standard of review in enforcement proceedings re the enforcement of an arbitral award with res judicata. In this case, the Netherlands Supreme Court found that in an enforcement dispute concerning an award with res judicata, there is no room for a re-assessment of the dispute (para. 3.6–3.7). The Netherlands Supreme Court annulled the judgments by the Court of Appeal for re-assessing the dispute on the basis of a new expert’s investigation. that it cannot reasonably be expected to fully comply with a final (irrevocable) arbitral award.54 Snijders, supra n. 7, Art. 1064 DCCP, n. 1, at 540 and references in footnote 7. The DAA 2015 brought the following pertinent amendments to the set-aside regime of the DAA 1986:(1) First, Article 1064a(1) DAA 2015 provides that the court of appeal at the seat of the arbitration is competent to hear a claim for set-aside. Article 1068(2) DAA stipulates the same for revocation proceedings. This is in deviation of the DAA 1986, under which set-aside proceedings were to be brought before the District Court – allowing for appeal and Supreme Court appeal. The DAA 2015 limits set-aside proceedings to one factual instance (the Court of Appeal at the seat). Parties acting in a professional capacity55 This is interpreted as parties that are not consumers. See Meijer & Ernste, supra n. 43, para. 489. may opt to exclude the possibility of proceedings before the Netherlands Supreme Court (Article 1064a(5) DAA).56 Such waiver may be included in the applicable arbitration rules. See for instance, ICC Rules 2021 (35(6)), SIAC Rules 2025 (51.6.), LCIA Rules 2014 (26.8). Also, the model arbitration clause for the UNCITRAL Rules 2013 provides for such waiver. They may do so before, during and after the arbitration proceedings.57 G. J. Meijer & A. I. M. van Mierlo, Parlementaire Geschiedenis Arbitragewet (Deventer: Kluwer 2015), para. I.75.5. Before the revision of the DAA in 2015, the district court had subject-matter jurisdiction to hear claims for set-aside (Art. 1064a (2) DAA 1986). The decision not to allow for the possibility to fully waive the right to bring set-aside proceedings was criticized by Strik. See D. A. Strik, Uitsluitingsovereenkomsten en het consultatie-wetsontwerp herziening arbitragewetgeving: een gemiste kans? 20 (TvA 2012). The Dutch legislator introduced the possibility to submit an application to set aside an award to the Netherlands Commercial Court of Appeal (NCCA).58 As per Art. 1064a (1) DAA 2015. This requires that the arbitration is seated in Amsterdam. Proceedings before the NCCA may be conducted in English. The recently revised NAI Rules provide that, where the parties have not agreed on a seat, the seat shall be in Amsterdam. This serves to facilitate a choice of English language proceedings before the NCC(A).59 Van Zelst & Schellaars offer a first English language commentary to the 2024 NAI Rules. See B. van Zelst & R. Schellaars, International Arbitration In The Netherlands – a Counsel’s Guide to Using ICC, LCIA, DIS and UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules in the Netherlands and a High-Level Commentary to the 2024 NAI Rules (Antwerp: Maklu 2025). See also in Dutch, M. Hetterscheidt, NAI Arbitragereglement 2024: op weg naar efficiëntere en snellere beslechting van (internationale) handelsgeschillen (TCR 31, 2024). (2) Second, the DAA 2015 introduced the so-called in favor validitatis principle.60 This rule is inspired by Art. 178(2) of the Swiss Private International Law Act. See Snijders, supra n. 7, Art. 1020 DAA, n. 1.5.2; N. Peters, IPR, Proces & Arbitrage: Over grondslagen en rechtspraktijk 294 (University of Groningen 2015), para. 6.5.3.2(c); in English, B. van Zelst, The Proposed New Dutch Arbitration Act, 18(3) Int’l Arb. L. Rev. 75–80 (2012). Under this rule, an agreement to arbitrate is valid if (a) that agreement is valid under applicable law as agreed by the parties, or under the laws of the seat of the arbitration; or, (b) if the parties have not agreed to an applicable law, then under the laws applicable to the legal relationship to which the agreement to arbitrate pertains. This legal relationship need not be of a contractual nature. Claims not based in contract (e.g., tort, benevolent intervention, unjustified enrichment) are arbitrable under Dutch law.61 Marsman, supra n. 18, para. 5.1.3. (3) Third, under the DAA 2015 an award may not be set aside for breach of mandate if such breach is not of a serious nature, or if the party advancing this ground has failed to make an objection in the matter in accordance with Article 1048a DAA 2015. According to Meijer, the test is whether the award would have been different if the arbitrators had stayed within their mandate (as determined by the court).62 Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, supra n. 24, at 39; G. J. Meijer, T&C Rv, commentaar op art. 1065 Rv, n. 4. This is in line with the standard of restraint that Dutch courts apply in set-aside proceedings.63 On which more elaborately s. 6 below. (4) Fourth, Article 1065(5) DAA 2015 provides for the possibility to set an award aside only in part. Article 1065(5) DAA stipulates that if a ground for setting aside concerns only a part of the arbitral award, then the award shall not be set aside with respect to the remaining part. This, however, is assuming that the remaining part is not inseparably connected with the part to be set aside, bearing in mind the content of the award.64 It may prove challenging to establish whether the remaining part of the arbitral award is inseparable from the part i.e., to be set aside. In 2009, the Netherlands Supreme Court ruled that arbitral decisions on interest and cost are not inseparable from the other decisions in the award. In a case in which an award was partially set aside for violations of the right to be heard having led to the tribunal awarding a higher interest rate than initially claimed, the Netherlands Supreme Court ruled that the Court of Appeal could have partially set aside the award to the effect that only the lower interest rate was compensable. The Netherlands Supreme Court thus found that the post-award interest rate granted by the tribunal is separable. See Netherlands Supreme Court 24 Mar. 2023, JBPr 55 (2023)case note by P. E. Ernste. This decision shows that separability of different elements of arbitral awards is a case-specific issue. The phrasing of the base rule that partial setting-aside is only available where ‘the arbitral decision contains elements that are not inextricably linked’ (verschillende beslissingen bevat die niet onverbrekelijk samenhangen), suggests that the bar for a claim for partial setting aside is relatively high. (5) Fifth, the DAA 2015 introduced the option for the set-aside court to, in certain circumstances, suspend the proceedings and remit the case to the tribunal to remedy the ground for set-aside. The Explanatory Note provides that this is to underline the ultimum remedium character of set-aside.65 Meijer & van Mierlo, supra n. 57, para. I.77.3. The relevant statutory provision, Article 1065a(1) DAA, is based on Article 34(4) UNCITRAL Model Law.66 Ibid., para. I.77.3. The possibility of remission was virtually unknown in the legal systems of continental Europe in 1985 when the Model Law appeared. In common law countries, on the other hand, it was fairly well-established.67 N. Peters & B. van Zelst, Remission in de nieuwe arbitragewet (TCR 4, 2013), under s. 2 above and references contained there. To date, multiple arbitration laws and rules in jurisdictions such as England, Germany and Belgium provide for remission.68 Snijders, supra n. 7, at 574–576. In derogation of Article 34(3) UNCITRAL Model law, Article 1065a (1) DAA 2015 allows the court of appeal to remit the case to the tribunal of its own motion. On the basis of Article 34(4) UNCITRAL Model law, remission is only possible ‘where requested by a party’.69 On which, in comparative perspective, see H. Holtzmann & J. Neuhaus, A Guide to The UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration (Legislative History and Commentary) 986 (Kluwer Law and Taxation Publishers, The Netherlands, in co-operation with the TMC Asser Institute 1989). That said, Article 1065a (1) DAA 2015 requires the court to give the parties an opportunity to express views before remitting a case.70 Parliamentary Documents II 33 6111, no. 3 (Explanatory Memorandum), 40; Peters & van Zelst, supra n. 64 , para. 3.1. It the court fails to do so, such a decision will constitute a surprise decision.71 Peters & van Zelst, supra n. 64, para. 3.1. This Part now proceeds with an empirical analysis comparing the outcome of set-aside proceedings under the DAA 2015 to decided set-aside cases under the DAA 1986 (section 5). Section 6 provides a thematic dogmatic analysis of the most pertinent set-aside decisions rendered under the DAA 2015. Section 7 offers concluding remarks.

sections.title: EMPIRICAL ANALYSIS
sections.text: 5 EMPIRICAL ANALYSIS 5.1 Introduction: Methodology and caveats This section offers an empirical analysis of published court decisions. The analysis focuses on set-aside (and not revocation) to facilitate a comparative assessment between outcomes under the DAA 1986 and the DAA 2015. As a general rule, Dutch courts apply restraint in set-aside and revocation proceedings.72 See in further detail: s. 6.2 below. The available remedies are considered ultimum remedium. 73 Parliamentary Documents II 1985–1986, 18 464, no. 6 (Explanatory Memorandum), at 38; and Parliamentary Documents II 2012–2013, at 33 611, no. 3 (Explanatory Memorandum), at 40–41. See also Netherlands Supreme Court 30 Dec. 1977, ECLI:NL:HR:1977:AC6162, NJ 449 (1978) with note by P. A. Stein; Netherlands Supreme Court 9 Jan. 2004, ECLI:NL:HR:2004:AK8380, NJ 190 (2004) with note by H. J. Snijders, para. 3.5.2; A. J. van den Berg, Wetsontwerp Nieuwe Arbitragewet 215 (TvA 6, 1984). This approach is informed by the finality principle.74 Snijders, supra n. 7, at 55, para. 1.2. This may explain the relatively low number of set-aside cases brought before the Dutch courts. Annually, an average of twenty to thirty set-aside proceedings are conducted before the Dutch courts.75 Marsman, supra n. 18, para. 18–002 with reference to parliamentary records. This also follows from Boevink’s empirical analysis of judgments in set-aside and revocation cases published from 1 January 2010 to 31 December 2023.76 M. Boevink, Successfully Set-Aside Arbitral Awards in the Netherlands – What Are the Odds? 2 (TvA 2024). Boevink’s helpful analysis does not differentiate between judgments applying the DAA 1986 from judgments applying the DAA 2015. See also from the perspective of the Netherlands Arbitration Institute, P. E. Ernste & C. L Schleijpen, De vernietiging van arbitrale vonnissen: lessen voor (NAI-)arbiters?, in Going Dutch. ADR in Nederland, in het bijzonder bij het NAI 651–700 (C. J. M. Klaassen, G.J. Meijer ed., Deventer: Wolters Kluwer 2019). The Dutch judiciary renders about 1.5 million decisions yearly. It publishes about 5% of these cases. Most published cases are anonymized.77 See more generally on the value of legal empiricism in the Dutch context: Eric Tjong Tjin Tai & Paul Verbruggen, Onderzoeksmethoden in de rechtswetenschap (NJB 2 2022). On 12 March 2012, the Council to the Judiciary adopted a decree providing the selection criteria for the publication of court decisions (besluit selectiecriteria uitsprakendatabase Rechtspraak.nl). The decree was updated on 12 February 2024.78 https://www.rechtspraak.nl/Uitspraken/paginas/selectiecriteria.aspx (accessed 30 Jul. 2025). It follows that the relatively low percentage of published cases is due, mostly, to the fact that decisions in cantonal and smaller commercial, administrative and criminal cases are rarely published. These cases make up the bulk of the decisions rendered by Dutch courts.79 Over 2024, of the total of 1,467,080 incoming cases, 1,126,550 cases concerned civil matters. Of those civil matters, 131,570 concerned family matters at the District Courts (of first instance) and 926,320 concerned cantonal court cases. The total number of new commercial cases – those potentially concerning arbitration – in the District Courts (59,290) and the Courts of Appeal (5,880) jointly, amounted to 65,170. See https://www.rechtspraak.nl/SiteCollectionDocuments/Jaarverslag%20Rechtspraak%202024.pdf, at 56 (accessed 30 Jul. 2025). As the Dutch judiciary’s annual reports evidence, the courts most seized in set-aside matters under the DAA 1986 – Amsterdam, Rotterdam, The Hague and Utrecht – have a higher percentage of published cases than other courts. In fact, courts of appeal – which as per the DAA 2015 are the first instance courts for set-aside matters – publish about 33% of all relevant cases. So, notwithstanding a lack of specific numbers, there is evidence to suggest that decisions in matters pertaining to arbitration, guiding decisions in set-aside cases more particularly, are published more regularly – in particular under the DAA 2015. This, it is submitted, allows for meaningful empirical analyses of these decisions.80 In its decision of 21 Apr. 2023, ECLI:NL:HR:2023:658, the Netherlands Supreme Court found that, in summary, courts are to be more forthcoming in publishing decisions. This should further improve the valour of empirical research of Dutch court decisions. The analysis has found a total of 282 individual rulings in set-aside cases. These were rendered after 25 February 2000.81 This number underlines that caution is advised. Parliamentary records show that, in 2013, the Dutch Counsel for the Judiciary estimated that an average of 20 to 30 set-aside cases were filed with the Dutch courts. Taking the low end of this estimate, this would lead to twenty times twenty-three years = 460 published cases. See Memorandum in Response to the Report, Parliamentary Documents II 2013/2014, 33.611, at 5. 190 of these rulings pertain to claims for set-aside under the DAA 1986; the other ninety-two rulings were made under the DAA 2015. An analysis of individual rulings is, however, not of assistance to an empirical analysis. A ruling may have been overturned by a higher court and thus not represent the result (set-aside or no set-aside) in an individual case. In the Yukos saga, for instance, at the date of publication of this contribution, no less than four final court rulings have been rendered (one by The Hague District Court,82 Decisions of 20 Apr. 2016 (ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2016:4229 and ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2016:4230). one by The Hague Court of Appeal,83 Decision of 18 Feb. 2020 (ECLI:NL:GHDHA:2020:234). one by the Supreme Court84 Decision of 5 Nov. 2021 (ECLI:NL:HR:2021:1645). and one by the Amsterdam Court of Appeal after remission by the Supreme Court on one particular point85 Decision of 20 Feb. 2024 (ECLI:NL:GHAMS:2024:351).). To date,86 The research was concluded in 13 Jul. 2025. the matter is not yet finally decided, with Supreme Court proceedings against the Amsterdam Court of Appeal’s 2024 ruling still pending. This example shows that accounting for each individual ruling would pollute the numbers. Thus, the analysis below assesses the result in cases/matters, rather than individual rulings by individual courts. This correction leads to a total number of 73 cases decided under the DAA 2015, and 148 cases under the DAA 1986. 5.2 Key findings and comments In cases decided under the DAA 2015, a total of five (6.8%) was set aside, with one award being partially set aside (1.3%), and four awards being remitted to the tribunal (5.8%). In cases decided under the DAA 1986, twenty-two out of 148 were set aside (14.9%), and twelve were partially set aside (8.1%). The numbers appear in the table below, with rejections on non-substantive grounds (inadmissibility, lack of jurisdiction) included in a separate column for perspective. Number of Decisions Set Aside Rejected Other (Including Inadmissibility /Lack of Jurisdiction) DAA 1986 148 34 (23%) 105 (71%) 9 (6%) DAA 2015 73 5 (6.8%) 63 (86.3%) 5 (6.8%), of which 4 remitted (5.5%) In terms of the grounds invoked87 This refers to a particular ground being relied on by a party. Numbers on grounds that were invoked successfully appear in the table further below. by parties claiming for setting-aside, the research offers the following Ground: Article 1065(1) DAA 1986 (Out of 148 Decisions) DAA 2015 (Out of seventy-three Decisions) (1) Lack of arbitration agreement /Jurisdiction 36 (24.3%) 20 (27.4%) (2) Composition of the tribunal 11 (7.4%) 7 (9.6%) (3) Breach of mandate 101 (68.2%) 49 (67.1%) (4) Not properly signed/lack of reasoning 109 (35.3%) 52 (71.2%) (5) Public policy 114 (73.6%) 50 (68.5%) The research shows that the grounds of breach of mandate, defects in reasoning/signature of the award, and public policy are the grounds invoked most often. There is a spike in reliance on the award being not properly signed or lacking in reasoning under the DAA 2015. This is striking, as defects with respect to the signing of awards in accordance with Article 1057 DAA 2015 may be resolved relatively easily through remission of the award to the tribunal (as per Article 1065a DAA 2015). This suggests that the claims for set-aside under the DAA 2015 relying on ground (d) concern, in large part, complaints as to the reasoning of the award. As the following table provides, complaints over the reasoning of awards are not likely to be successful. In fact, the number of cases in which reliance on a defect in reasoning of an arbitral award was found meritorious declined, percentage-wise, from over 35% of decisions setting aside an award rendered under the DAA 1986, to 20% (one matter) under the DAA 2015. Conversely, percentage-wise there is close to a threefold increase in the number of cases set aside for lack of existence of an agreement to arbitrate. Section 6 will offer further perspective on these numbers, based on a dogmatic analysis of the relevant decisions. Ground: Article 1065(1) DAA 1986 (Out of thirty-four Set-Aside Decisions) 88 Note that in some cases, the award was set aside on multiple grounds, explaining why the grounds for set-aside in this column add up to 38 (in 34 cases). DAA 2015 (Out of five Set-Aside Decisions) (1) Lack of arbitration agreement /Jurisdiction 7 (20.6%) 3 (60%) (2) Composition of the tribunal 1 (2.9%) 0 (0%) (3) Breach of mandate 7 (20.6%) 1 (20%) (4) Not properly signed/lack of reasoning 12 (35.3%) 1 (20%) (5) Public policy 11 (32.4%) 2 (40%) These numbers appear to confirm that the legislature was successful in its curbing the number of set-asides of arbitral awards – including by only allowing for set-aside in case of serious breach of mandate and by offering the possibility of remission of an award to the tribunal.89 For as discussion of the DAA 2015s objectives, see s. 3.1 above. However, by means of contextualization, it is pertinent to note, first, that the Dutch jurisdiction does not apply the doctrine of precedent. In other words, technically, courts are not bound by the rulings of higher courts.90 Hence, this article applies the term ‘decided cases’ rather than ‘case law’. There is, however, an important nuance to this point. See Hester Sanne Taekema, Introduction, in Understanding Dutch Law 20 (Hester Sanne Taekema, Annie de Roo, Carinne Elion-Valter eds, The Hague, Eleven 2020), who explains that ‘Dutch courts are not formally obligated to follow earlier (Netherlands Supreme Court) decisions. This means that there is no principle of precedent or stare decisis as known in common law countries (stare decisis is the idea that courts must abide by the rulings of higher courts and that courts should in principle respect their own previous decisions). In fact, however, Dutch courts do follow Netherlands Supreme Court decisions: when a court’s decision goes against a Netherlands Supreme Court ruling, it is possible to appeal to a higher court and, in the last instance, the Netherlands Supreme Court, which will not usually overrule its own previous decision. Thus, in practice, lower courts use the Netherlands Supreme Court’s decisions as an authoritative source’. Decisions rendered can thus not be assumed to ‘predict’ the outcome of future cases. Second, the published set-aside cases assessed in the context of this research – essentially without exception – pertain to highly case-specific matters. Consequently, an assessment of the likelihood of set-aside of an individual award requires rigorous substantive analysis.91 This is also recognized by Alford, Baltag, Hall, and Sasson, who note – in the context of the recognition and enforcement of set-aside arbitral awards – that Dutch courts ‘take an assessment approach, on a case by case basis’. See Roger P. Alford, Crina Baltag, Matthew E. K. Hall & Monique Sasson, Empirical Analysis of National Courts Vacatur and Enforcement of International Commercial Arbitration Awards, 39(2) J. Int’l Arb. 299–330, 327(2022), doi; 10.54648/JOIA2022013. This is also true, it is submitted by reference to Boevink (supra n. 76), for cases decided by the Dutch courts with respect to claims for set-aside. This may be illustrated by the following points:(1) In the 2000–2025 timeframe, 10 awards were (also) set aside on the basis of the lack of an arbitration agreement. These cases are not comparable. Two cases concern the application of an arbitral clause to a third party.92 Netherlands Supreme Court 20 Jan. 2006, ECLI:NL:HR:2006:AU4523; District Court of Haarlem, 13 Jan. 2010, ECLI:NL:RBHAA:2010:BL1528. Other cases concern the inapplicability of standard terms or the articles of association of a cooperative,93 Den Bosch Court of Appeal, 17 Mar. 2015, ECLI:NL:GHSHE:2015:929, TvA 54 (2015); Amsterdam Court of Appeal, 23 Feb. 2021, ECLI:NL:GHAMS:2021:521. the lack of an agreement for Dutch arbitral summary proceedings,94 Netherlands Supreme Court, 25 May 2007, ECLI:NL:HR:2007:BA1523. or a matter in which the parties had agreed on set-aside being meritorious as part of a settlement agreement.95 The Hague Court of Appeal, 21 Dec. 2021, ECLI:NL:GHDHA:2021:2548. A particularly salient matter underlies the Amsterdam Court of Appeal’s decision of thirty August 2022.96 ECLI:NL:GHAMS:2022:2493. The author was involved in this matter as counsel to the party defending the arbitral award. In this case, the Court of Appeal conducted a full de novo review of the existence of the agreement to arbitrate – offering limited, or no, deference to the tribunal’s analysis. It found that there was no such agreement, and set aside the arbitral award at issue. The Supreme Court subsequently dismissed the appeal without offering reasoning. In doing so, the Supreme Court passed on the opportunity to clarify what degree of deference is due to the arbitral tribunal’s assessment of its jurisdiction.97 Netherlands Supreme Court, 15 Mar. 2024, ECLI:NL:HR:2024:403. This question is the central issue in Prof. van Haersolte-van Hof’s inaugural lecture (unpublished, on file with the author). Osadchiy has also critically assessed the de novo standard in his article in this journal. See M. Osadchiy, Calibrating De Novo: Judicial Review of Arbitral Jurisdiction, J. Int’l Arb. 41(1) 1–28 (2024), doi: 10.54648/JOIA2024001 (accessed 30 Jul. 2025). See also Netherlands Supreme Court, 6 Dec. 2024, ECLI:NL:HR:2024:1812, JBPR 2025/26 case note by Jamalov and Van Zelst. (2) There is no clear explanation for the lack of set-aside decisions on the basis of the composition of the arbitral tribunal violating the applicable rules under the DAA 2015. The decision rendered under the DAA 1986 does not involve the larger and more experienced arbitral institutions.98 Utrecht District Court, 8 Aug. 2007, ECLI:NL:RBUTR:2007:BB1410; Den Bosch Court of Appeal, supra n. 93. In a very recent decision,99 Netherlands Supreme Court, 9 May 2025, ECLI:NL:HR:2025:725. the Supreme Court remitted to the Court of Appeal of’s-Hertogenbosch a matter concerning the composition of the arbitral tribunal.100 Amsterdam Court of Appeal, 28 Nov. 2023, ECLI:NL:GHAMS:2023:3525. This case centres around the applicability of the Netherlands Arbitration Institute’s Rules to a request by an arbitrator to be relieved of his mandate. The arbitrator had submitted such request with the interim relief judge, which – the Supreme Court found – lacked jurisdiction as the NAI was granted such authority as per the parties’ agreement to apply the NAI Rules. The Court of Appeal of’s – Hertogenbosch is now to reassess the matter. Pending this assessment, this matter is registered as an unsuccessful challenge – given that the Amsterdam Court of Appeal’s set-aside ruling was quashed, and thus the arbitral award at issue reinstated. (3) The ground of breach of mandate appears to be less successful under the DAA 2015 (one out of five set-aside cases) when compared to the DAA 1986 (seven out of thirty-four set-aside cases). This may be a consequence of the new provision of Article 1048a DAA 2015, which provides for the obligation of parties to, without reasonable delay, object to, amongst others, objections breaches of mandate, on the pain of waiving the right to rely on the breach of mandate later in the arbitral proceedings or before the court.101 See the discussion of Art. 1048a DAA in footnote 36 above. (4) Under the DAA 1986, three awards were set aside for (amongst others) not being signed in accordance with the requirements of Article 1057.102 See Utrecht District Court, supra n. 98; Leeuwarden District Court, 27 Jul. 2008, ECLI:NL:RBLEE:2008:BD8721; Amsterdam District Court, 29 Jan. 2014, ECLI:NL:RBAMS:2014:793. This ground is unlikely to lead to set-aside under the DAA 2015, as the latter provides for the court of appeal to, at the request of a party or of its own motion, suspend the set-aside proceedings and remit the case to the tribunal.103 See s. 4 above, at Roman numeral v. The one award set aside under the DAA 2015 for lack of reasoning concerns a matter arbitrated before the Disputes Committee on the legal profession (Geschillencommissie Advocatuur) on unacceptably high fee statements issued by a counsel to its client.104 Arnhem-Leeuwarden Court of Appeal, 18 Apr. 2017, ECLI:NL:GHARL:2017:3283, TvA 47 (2017). (5) Decisions setting aside awards for being contrary to public policy (thirteen in total since 2000, yet only two under the DAA 2015), appear to underline that set-aside decisions pertain to specific cases. Examples are cases concerning fraud105 Den Bosch Court of Appeal, 5 Oct. 2021, ECLI:NL:GHSHE:2021:3015. or a violation of the fundamental right to be heard.106 Example, Netherlands Supreme Court 25 May 2007, ECLI:NL:HR:2007:BA2495; The Hague Court of Appeal, 28 Dec. 2010, ECLI:NL:GHSGR:2010:BP7374; The Hague Court of Appeal, 31 Mar. 2015, ECLI:NL:GHDHA:2015:713; District Court of Amsterdam, 19 Dec. 2018, ECLI:NL:RBAMS:2018:8247. Section 6.4 shall explore these matters in greater detail. A final perspective that may be of interest is the relatively swift assessment of set-aside cases by the Dutch courts. The analysis finds that the bulk of first instance rulings on published set-aside applications are rendered within 12–18 months. This is a positive note, given earlier research showing that the length and unpredictability of Dutch commercial court cases is considered a structural weakness of the Dutch judicial system.107 See F. van Tulder, K. Strijbos & S. Koolen, Kwaliteit van rechtspraak: een kijk over de grenzen 1 Raad voor de rechtspraak (Research Memoranda 2021)–, Utrecht: Xerox/OSAGE 2021, para. 8.4. See also the report Doorlooptijden in beweging (Processing Times on the Move), as issued by the Dutch judiciary in 2019, https://www.rechtspraak.nl/SiteCollectionDocuments/eindrapport-doorlooptijden-in-beweging.pdf (accessed 30 Jul. 2025).

sections.title: SUBSTANTIVE ANALYSIS OF KEY DECISIONS UNDER THE DAA 2015
sections.text: 6 SUBSTANTIVE ANALYSIS OF KEY DECISIONS UNDER THE DAA 2015 6.1 Introduction: A note on method Much has been written about the value of empiricism in legal academia.108 Given that this particular question is not central to this article, it suffices to reference Kritzer’s Advanced Introduction to Empirical Legal Research (Cheltenham, UK; Northampton, MA, USA: Edward Elgar Publishing 2021). For current purposes, it is sufficient to recall the cautionary remarks on the publication regime of the Dutch judiciary in section 5.1 above. It follows from these notes that there can be no way of assuring all set-aside cases rendered in the Netherlands have been published and part of the ‘data set’ underlying this analysis.109 This does not necessarily hamper the representativity of the empirical analysis in the above. See Boevink, who notes that most cases under the DAA 2015 are likely published. Boevink, supra n. 76, para. 2 in fine. In addition, the decisions by the Dutch courts in set-aside matters are fact-specific in nature. Thus, an analysis of the merits of such decisions is in order. As this article is not a suitable occasion to discuss each of the over 250 court rulings decisions found in the research, it is necessary to make a selection. Given the fundamental nature of claims for set-aside on the ground of (1) a lack of valid agreement to arbitrate, and (2) violation of public policy, the following focuses on these two grounds. These grounds are furthermore of interest, as Netherlands Supreme Court decisions have consistently held that the applicable standard of ‘review with restraint’ does not unequivocally apply with respect to these grounds. 6.2 Standard of review In the interest of the effectiveness of arbitral proceedings, courts are in principle to apply restraint in reviewing set-aside applications.110 Netherlands Supreme Court, 9 Jan. 2004, ECLI:NL:HR:2004:AK8380, NJ 2005/190, case note by H. J. Snijders, TvA 47(2004); case note by F. J. M. de Ly, AA (2004), at 881; case note by G. R. Rutgers, NbBW (2004), at 40; case note by K. N. Claassens, TPR (2005), 1359; H. J. Snijders, (Nannini/SFT). ‘De terughoudendheidsleer’ van dit arrest is tal van keren bevestigd inde lagere rechtspraak. See e.g., The Hague Court of Appeal, 28 Jan. 2014, ECLI:NL:GHDHA:2014:71, and Amsterdam Court of Appeal, 1 Nov. 2016, ECLI:NL:GHAMS:2016. The Netherlands Supreme Court has made clear, in multiple rulings, that it does not permit the use of set-aside proceedings as a de facto appeal.111 This follows from a long line of Netherlands Supreme Court cases. See Netherlands Supreme Court, 22 Dec. 1978, ECLI:NL:HR:1978:AC6449; NJ 521 (1979) (Zaunbrecher/Muyzert); Netherlands Supreme Court, 23 Dec. 1943, ECLI:NL:HR:1943:201; NJ 164 (1944) (Drost/Schippers), which was recently confirmed in Netherlands Supreme Court, 5 Nov. 2021, ECLI:NL:HR:2021:1645 (Russian Federation/HVY), para. 5.4.9. The set-aside of an award is regarded as an ultimum remedium. 112 Parliamentary Documents II 2012/13, 33611, supra n. 24, at 40. Standards for successfully challenging an award on the basis of breach of mandate or defective reasoning are particularly high. In addition, parties are expected to raise known concerns that may lead to set-aside during the course of arbitral proceedings, failing which they would be deemed to have waived their right to raise the relevant ground as a basis to set aside the award.113 See the discussion of Art. 1048a DAA in footnote 36 above. There are two exceptions to this general rule of restraint. First, the courts do not have to apply restraint for claims that seek to set aside an arbitral award based on lack of a valid arbitration agreement (Article 1065(1)(a) DAA). I note that the term ‘without restraint’ has not been uniformly interpreted in the literature and decided cases. In his note to the Chevron v. Ecuador judgment, Snijders refers to ‘not a marginal, but a complete (…) [review]’.114 NJ 2015/318 case note by H. J. Snijders, under 3(c). Other authors for example refer to ‘the final say’ of the regular court115 G. J. Meijer, T&C Rv, commentaar op art.1065 Rv, n. 2(a). or a review ‘in full’.116 Netherlands Supreme Court, 26 Sep. 2014, ECLI:NL:HR:2014:2837, TvA 6 (2015), note by M. C. van Leyenhorst, at 13. The Explanatory Memorandum to the DAA 1986 – under which Chevron v. Ecuador was delivered – references the arbitral tribunal’s decision on jurisdiction as a ‘preliminary opinion’.117 Parliamentary Documents II 1983/84, 18464, no. 3 (Explanatory Memorandum), at 21. Generally, it is assumed that the courts are allowed to fully assess the positions and evidence put to an arbitral tribunal in the arbitration. The courts may also review evidence that the parties relied upon in set-aside proceedings can come to different assessments, views and conclusions than those of the arbitral tribunal.118 See Netherlands Supreme Court, 26 Sep. 2014, ECLI:NL:HR:2014:2837, NJ 2015/318 (Ecuador/Chevron), note by H. J. Snijders, n. 3(c). That being said, courts in a number of jurisdictions have accorded a substantial degree of deference to arbitrators’ factual determination and legal conclusions regarding the existence or validity of an arbitration agreement in set-aside proceedings.119 Born (2023,) §25.04 [A][8][a]. This is particularly true where the arbitral tribunal has conducted extensive fact-finding (also touching on witness credibility).120 Ibid. In all these circumstances, although the set-aside decision is made on a de novo basis, set-aside courts accord substantial weight to the arbitrator’s jurisdictional fact-finding, analysis and conclusions. It is worth noting that this issue – which is assessed in section 6.3 – remains unsettled in the Dutch jurisdiction. Second, the courts do not have to apply restraint for set-aside claims where a violation of the right to be heard121 Netherlands Supreme Court, 26 Sep. 2014, ECLI:NL:HR:2014:2837, TvA 6 (2015) case note by M. C. van Leyenhorst; NJ 318 (2015) case note by H. J. Snijders, para. 4.2. and/or fundamental EU law is at issue.122 Netherlands Supreme Court, 25 Feb. 2000, ECLI:NL:HR:2000:AA4947, NJ 508 (2000), case note by H. J. Snijders, TvA 131 (2000). For other alleged violations of public policy, the point of departure is that this ground must, by its nature, also be applied with restraint.123 Netherlands Supreme Court, 12 Apr. 2019, ECLI:NL:HR:2019:565, para. 4.3.2; Netherlands Supreme Court, 24 Apr. 2009, NJ 2010, 171 (IMS v. Modsaf), para. 4.3.1; Netherlands Supreme Court, 25 May 2007, ECLI:NL:HR:2007:BA2495; NJ 2007, 294 (Spaanderman v. Anova), para. 3.5; Netherlands Supreme Court, 17 Jan. 2003, NJ 2004, 384 (IMS v. Modsaf), para. 3.3; Netherlands Supreme Court, 5 Nov. 2021, ECLI:NL:HR:2021:1645, JBPr 16 (Yukos) case note by Van Zelst. See also G. J. Meijer, Overeenkomst tot arbitrage. Bezien in het licht van het bewijsvoorschrift van artikel 101 Rv (Deventer: Kluwer 2012), at 939. This will be developed further in section 6.4. 6.3 Lack of a valid agreement to arbitrate (Article 1065(1)(a) DAA 2015) If an arbitral award is not based on a valid arbitration agreement, it may be challenged on the basis of Article 1065(1)(a) DAA. Whilst a tribunal is competent to rule on its jurisdiction,124 Article 1052(1), DAA 2015. the fundamental nature of the right of access to the courts requires that the question of whether a valid arbitration agreement exists is ultimately for the state court:125 Article 1022(1) DAA and Art. 1065(1), under a DAA, as well as Netherlands Supreme Court, 9 Jan. 1981, ECLI:NL:HR:1981:AG4130, NJ 203 (1981); Netherlands Supreme Court, 26 Sep. 2014, ECLI:NL:HR:2014:2837, with case notes by M. C. van Leyenhorst, TvA 6 (2015), and H. J. Snijders, NJ 318 (2015), para. 4.2. In principle, the burden of proof with regard of the facts and circumstances on which a party relies alleging that a valid arbitration agreement exists or not, rests on the party that relies on the existence of the arbitration agreement (i.e., the claimant in the arbitration proceedings).126 G. J. Meijer, T&C Rv, commentaar op art. 1065 Rv, n. 2; Snijders, supra n. 7, Art. 1065 DCCP, n. 2. The Netherlands Supreme Court has held that a claim for set-aside for lack of a valid arbitration agreement is to be assessed without restraint (zonder terughoudendheid). It is unsettled what this entails. Van Haersolte-van Hof posits that this permits a de novo review of the question of validity of the agreement to arbitrate.127 J.J. van Haersolte-van Hof, ‘Arbitral jurisdiction – exploring the boundaries of de novo review’, who notes that ‘court review should be comprehensive and not applied through the prism of the tribunal’s decision’ (unpublished). She submits, however, that it is unclear what degree of deference the courts are to pay to the tribunal’s assessment of its jurisdiction – and thus, by extension, of the validity of the agreement to arbitrate. It appears that a judgment by a Court of Appeal that assesses the tribunal’s jurisdiction afresh and disregards the tribunal’s reasoning would be liable to Supreme Court appeal for being insufficiently reasoned. As noted above, invoking the non-existence of an agreement to arbitrate requires timely reliance on this ground in the arbitration.128 See s. 3.2 above. The purpose of this provision is to allow the tribunal to take a decision on its own jurisdiction at an early stage in the proceedings.129 Netherlands Supreme Court, 27 Mar. 2009 (ECLI:NL:HR:2009:BG4003), NJ 2010/169 (Hendrix Poultry/Burshan), para. 4.3.4; Netherlands Supreme Court, 27 Mar. 2009 (ECLI:NL:HR:2009:BG6443), NJ 2010/170 (Smit Bloembollen c.s./Ruwa Bulbs), para. 3.4.2. Once a jurisdictional objection has been raised, there is only limited scope for making new factual or legal assertions in support of the objection. In this regard, the set-aside court considers whether the new arguments are linked to the arguments earlier submitted, why the party did not raise the arguments at an earlier stage, and whether the party was assisted by a lawyer in the arbitral proceedings. In contrast, the defendant in set-aside proceedings is allowed to submit new arguments that may support the consideration of the tribunal that it has jurisdiction.130 Netherlands Supreme Court, 27 Mar. 2009 (ECLI:NL:HR:2009:BG6443), NJ 2010/170 (Smit Bloembollen c.s./Ruwa Bulbs). It may do so to prevent the court from setting a jurisdictional decision of an arbitral tribunal aside because the arbitrators based this on an incorrect reason. That would lead to the undesirable result that an award is set aside, thus leading to the jurisdiction of the state court, simply because the tribunal based its jurisdictional decision on an incorrect reason.131 Ibid., para. 4.4.3. If the tribunal declines jurisdiction for lack of a valid arbitration agreement, the jurisdiction of the state court revives. It was long unsettled whether a negative jurisdictional award is subject to review on the ground that the tribunal erred in its jurisdictional judgment. In other jurisdictions, courts have held that a negative jurisdictional decision cannot be reviewed on the basis that the tribunal wrongfully denied jurisdiction (or a negative jurisdictional decision is even deemed a non-award, not capable of being set aside or confirmed).132 Born (2023), §25.04 [A][8][b]. Born references the conclusion of a 2002 German Bundesgerichtshof judgment, which reasoned that none of the grounds set forth in the German version of the Model Law for annulment of an award included correcting a mistaken negative jurisdictional award. Some jurisdictions treat negative jurisdictional decisions in the same manner as positive jurisdictional awards.133 Born (2023), §25.04 [A][8][b]. Born refers to Art. 190(2)(b) of the Swiss Law on Private International Law, which provides for judicial review in an annulment action where ‘the arbitral tribunal has wrongly declared itself to have or not to have jurisdiction’. Similarly, he refers to §10(a)(4) of the US FAA, which applies equally to both positive and negative jurisdictional awards, permitting the same degree of judicial review in both scenarios. The main argument for permitting a court to review a negative jurisdictional ruling is that to deny such a review fundamentally subverts the purpose of an arbitration agreement. The parties who have selected arbitration in a neutral territory to resolve disputes are forced to do so via court proceedings instead (ordinarily in the home state of one of the parties – a situation which the parties presumably intended to avoid).134 http://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2016/11/08/a-tale-of-two-cities-an-analysis-of-divergent-approaches-to-negative-jurisdictional-rulings/ (accessed 30 Jul. 2025). In 2021, the Court of Appeal of The Hague held that a negative jurisdictional decision cannot be reviewed under Article 1065(1)(a) DAA (lack of a valid arbitration agreement).135 The Hague Court of Appeal, 19 Jan. 2021, ECLI:NL:GHDHA:2021:14, para. 22. The Court reasoned that, pursuant to the principle of competence-competence, tribunals are to rule on their own jurisdiction and a set-aside court may only verify whether an arbitral tribunal has based its jurisdiction on a valid arbitration agreement.136 Ibid. The Court of Appeal found this incompatible with a substantive review and the potential set-aside of a negative jurisdictional decision.137 Ibid. The Court of Appeal did, however, consider that a negative jurisdictional award may be set aside for a violation of mandate. In the matter at issue, the Court of Appeal found that no such violation had taken place. In the subsequent Supreme Court appeal proceedings, the Netherlands Supreme Court took a strict stance, finding that negative jurisdictional decisions cannot be set-aside under Article 1065(1) DAA.138 Netherlands Supreme Court, 21 Apr. 2023, ECLI:NL:HR:2023:636, para. 3.2.6. The wording of this decision appears to entail that set-aside cannot be claimed, even where the negative jurisdictional award is tainted by due process or other fundamental concerns. On this basis, the Supreme Court’s decision has been subject to scholarly critique.139 See the case notes by H. J. Snijders (NJ 2024/291) and B. van Zelst and B. Keizers (JBPr 2023/57). It is unsettled whether the existence of pre-arbitral requirements such as escalation and mediation agreements not being complied with render a claim in arbitration inadmissible or operate as a bar to the jurisdiction of a tribunal. It was long held that a mediation clause could not lead to the inadmissibility of claims before, or the lack of jurisdiction of, arbitral tribunals – or courts for that matter.140 See E. Schutte, Hoe hard is een mediationclausule?, 2 Contracteren juni 31–32 (2009). This view was based on the Supreme Court’s consideration that, in view of the voluntary nature of mediation, parties are always at liberty to terminate a pending mediation or refuse to cooperate with the mediation.141 Netherlands Supreme Court, 20 Jan. 2006, NJ 75 (2006), para. 3.4. Although this decision concerned a family law matter, lower courts have applied the principle of party autonomy underlying it virtually without exception.142 E. J. M. van Beukering-Rosmuller, De juridische afdwingbaarheid van een mediationclausule. Een bespreking van de procesrechtelijke invalshoek, aan de hand van recente wetgevingsontwikkelingen 7–14 (TvA 1 2017). In its decision of 12 July 2024, the Supreme Court found that an agreement to mediate should be construed as a bar to the admissibility or jurisdiction of a claim in arbitration – or, for that matter, before the state courts – is a matter of interpretation. In this particular case, the Supreme Court ruled that the Court of Appeal had sufficiently reasoned its decision that a clause agreed between two professional parties effectively providing for mediation a condition precedent to arbitration did not bar one of the parties from refusing to mediate and proceeding to arbitration without delay.143 Netherlands Supreme Court, 12 Jul. 2024, ECLI:NL:HR:2024:1078, with case notes by E. Van Beukering-Rosmuller (TvA 2024/77); Van Mourik (JOR 2024/246) and Van Zelst (JBPR 2024/48). This decision has met with some criticism, as it appears to leave the key question – does the failure to proceed with mediation lead to inadmissibility or a lack of jurisdiction for an arbitral tribunal? – unanswered.144 This is also a matter of long-standing debate in the international context. See Jan Paulsson, Jurisdiction and Admissiblity, in Global Reflections on International Law, Commerce and Dispute Resolution, Liber Amicorum in honour of Robert Briner 693 (ICC Publishing, Publication 2005); and, more recently, Raphael Ren, The Dichotomy Between Jurisdiction and Admissibility in International Arbitration, 73(2) ICLQ (2024), doi: 10.1017/S0020589324000022, available at https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/international-and-comparative-law-quarterly/article/dichotomy-between-jurisdiction-and-admissibility-in-international-arbitration/C0B93FC7C31161274E46C4DE13DE764D#fn1 (accessed 30 Jul. 2025). It is unclear whether this line will continue to be applicable, in particular in cases where specific arrangements were agreed upon for the mediation.145 For instance, where the participation in a mediation is agreed as a ‘condition precedent’ for arbitration. This would be in keeping with the Supreme Court’s decision of 19 February 2021. In this matter, the Supreme Court confirmed that a pending mediation interrupts the statute of limitations in cross-border matters.146 Netherlands Supreme Court, 19 Feb. 2021 ECLI:NL:HR:2021:274. It remains to be seen whether the Supreme Court will go so far to attach consequences of pre-arbitral arrangements for the admissibility of claims in arbitration and/or the jurisdiction of the tribunal. 6.4 Public policy violations (Article 1065(1)(e) DAA 2015) Article 1065(1)(e) DAA 2015 stipulates that an award may be set aside if the award, or the manner in which it was made, violates public policy.
