At present, AI has seen use cases in arbitration in drafting, research, document disclosure and decision support capacities; or in other words, in the preparatory stages of disputes by parties and counsel to varying degrees of application.38 See https://www.arbtech.io/blog/ocftnyz34vxabpqt9vvz158fokobsu. However, this chapter will posit a near future where LLMs have become sophisticated enough that they are capable of reliable implementation, with greater persuasiveness than previous iterations considered above, in which arbitral authorities use AI in an overt manner, with the possibility that, eventually, arbitrators and tribunals might not only augment their decision-making with AI but be replaced by it altogether. The following predictions are generalised in perspective as to jurisdiction. These are hypothetical prerequisites for AI’s future use by arbitration tribunals: (1) parties will be required to mutually consent to AI’s use by those arbitrators involved in their dispute; and (2) the specific AI model used will also either need to be agreed upon by parties in arbitration clauses or provided by regulation of the particular arbitral jurisdiction.39 N.B. Although, the latter seems more intuitive considering ‘Without Prejudice’ principles and concerns over confidentiality. See also Blake, supra n. 15, 5.34-5.35. And then it may eventually be the case that either: (3) arbitrators themselves may either be required or encouraged to augment their decision-making with AI usage; or (4) decision-making will be entirely replaced by an AI tool subject to the consent of the parties (this is most likely to be restricted, especially initially, to small claims disputes). If it is accepted that fundamentally, AI is both persuasive in nature and likely destined to improve in its persuasiveness, then its involvement in decision-making in arbitration is principally unfavourable. This unsuitability, at the decision-making level, is rooted in the aims and definitional conception of arbitration itself, irrespective of its adjudicative nature: ‘Arbitration involves an impartial arbitrator or tribunal considering both sides of a dispute and making a decision on the issues raised by the parties.’40 Ibid., 25.01. The principle that parties may expect an arbitrator to meet the standard of impartiality is inextricably linked with the entire process and its legitimacy and legality. Despite the fact that parties are free to enter into arbitration agreements as they wish (provided there is no contravention of the public interest), it would be a monumentally radical change for arbitration’s essential form to introduce AI decision-making, which is not, and perhaps can never be assuredly, impartial. Such a prospect may even warrant the creation of an entirely separate and novel form of alternative dispute resolution (ADR). The concern for parties involved in international arbitration ought to be that current developments indicate that jurisdictions would prefer to incorporate AI into existing systems and that arbitral authorities and parties alike may thereby be deluded into believing that AI and decision-making in arbitration are compatible, either due to a lack of awareness of AI’s persuasiveness or more worryingly, a disregard for arbitration’s functional tenets. Besides persuasiveness, there exist a host of further potential flaws and risks associated with AI use recorded in recent scholarship; to name but a few that appear most harmful in an arbitration context: (1) Fallibility: AI makes mistakes; it is not yet impervious to errors.41 Peters, Tobias M. and Ingrid Scharlau, ‘Interacting with Fallible AI: Is Distrust Helpful When Receiving AI Misclassifications?’ Frontiers in Psychology, 2025, 2 https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2025.1574809. See, for example, the consistently reported hallucinations and misalignment in this current generation of LLMs that cause models to generate fake sources for answers as though they were valid. (2) Feedback loops: In training processes, ‘when humans and AI interact, even minute perceptual, emotional and social biases originating either from AI systems or humans leave human beliefs more biased, potentially forming a feedback loop’.42 Glickman, Moshe and Tali Sharot, ‘How Human-AI Feedback Loops Alter Human Perceptual, Emotional and Social Judgements’, Nature Human Behaviour, Vol. 9, 2025, 346 https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-024-02077-2. (3) Interpretability and explainability: The more interpretable an AI model is, the easier it is for programmers and coders to understand both why and how the model works. As AI models become more sophisticated and are trained on more complex datasets or even potentially by other agentic models, the difficulty in evaluating and explaining an AI’s inner workings increases.43 Ali, Sajid et al., ‘Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI): What We Know and What Is Left to Attain Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence’, Information Fusion, Vol. 99, 2023, 2 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inffus.2023.101805. Although human arbitrators are also theoretically susceptible to bias and persuasion, unless an LLM is effectively scrutable and explainable, it would appear undesirable to introduce such a precarious element into the decision-making of a system as critical as international arbitration. (4) Sycophancy: In both training and application, AI models have been found to be incentivised, often unintentionally, to provide answers that it ‘thinks’ are wanted rather than correct, as with the hallucinations mentioned above.44 Perez, Ethan et al., ‘Discovering Language Model Behaviors with Model-Written Evaluations’ (computer science > arXiv:2212.09251 2022) 3. The boundaries between ‘honest’ mistakes and outright deception are often blurred in this regard, and it has been predicted that more sophisticated future generations of AI will retain sycophantic traits, even to the point that it may be impossible to extricate them entirely.45 Sharma, Mrinank et al., ‘Towards Understanding Sycophancy in Language Models’ (computer science > arXiv:2310.13548 2025) 7. (5) Bias: There has been significant documentation of bias within AI models, inserted unknowingly in both training datasets and caused by practical applications. In the former instance, typically LLMs trained on vast datasets – often with content from the internet – will internalise biases within said content. It can be extremely difficult to catch biases that do arise before their effects are discovered in practice. Examples include gender and racial biases exposed in earlier LLM models.46 An, Jiafu et al., ‘Measuring Gender and Racial Biases in Large Language Models’ (economics > arXiv:2403.15281 2024) 4. All of the above traits, with particular emphasis on sycophancy and bias, have the potential, when combined with persuasiveness, to be detrimental to the impartiality of an arbitration process. There are, of course, potential safeguards that might be implemented to avoid or minimise these problems. Constitutional AI and accountability, and monitoring systems are just some of the proposed solutions to these associated issues.47 Sapkota, supra n. 17, 22-24, https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2212.08073. However, successful enforcement of these safeguards requires careful and considered use of AI in the first instance. AI’s traits of persuasiveness, sycophancy and bias are not impossible to correct, but even if these issues are detected and remedied, constant and regular vigilance is required to ensure that they do not reassert themselves. Even then, it is unclear as to whether issues such as sycophancy are the result of data in training sets and, thus, to what degree these issues are ‘baked in’ at the point of use. More generally, international arbitration has certain features that could render it especially vulnerable to the introduction of persuasive future LLMs: (1) Costs: (a) ADR’s primary goal of lowering costs might make it more likely that parties and authorities alike will seek to implement AI to be perceived as lowering costs, despite the attendant risks. This is worsened by a single LLM tool having access to a broader range of knowledge than any one human could possess, meaning that, in an imagined future, with sufficient finesse in execution, it could act as an arbitrator across legal practice areas: seemingly offering specialisation without the need to pay more for expertise. The ability to select an arbitrator with a particular competence is currently a significant attraction for parties choosing to resolve disputes through arbitration, but this aspect of arbitration may be made redundant by AI that has capabilities across disciplines. (b) Once introduced, it is likely that jurisdictions would be slow to reverse AI’s introduction as needed. The reason for this is simple enough. As soon as parties become used to the lowered costs associated with AI usage, authorities will be hesitant to remove AI at the risk of parties going elsewhere. Adequate examination of the selected AI’s inner workings is also difficult to maintain post-deployment, as safety monitoring of sophisticated AI systems is a rigorous and complex task.48 Evans, Owain et al., ‘Truthful AI: Managing and Governing AI That Does Not Lie’ (computer science > arXiv:2110.06674 2021) 84-85. (2) International competition: (a) In order to stay nimble in a global market, jurisdictions may be at risk of rushing to implement AI once other jurisdictions have done so. Attracting and maintaining parties to large commercial disputes is a particularly strong incentive for pushing for this innovation at a policy level. (3) Corporate parties: (a) For some cases, it is likely that involved corporate parties will already be particularly familiar with AI usage in their own professions and so come to expect its implementation in legal or arbitral contexts: ‘Our findings suggest that positive experiences with AI-generated advice can decrease AI aversion and increase individuals’ preference for AI advisors.’49 See supra n. 25. This potentially insulates these parties from balanced decision-making when deciding whether or not to consent to AI usage in arbitration, as they become increasingly desensitised to its use. (4) Procedure: (a) On a practical level, arbitrations often occur over intense periods of effort in both the preparation stage and in later submissions. The emphasis on keeping costs low, not appearing to unreasonably obstruct the ADR process and reaching favourable consensus all create a presumption that parties are more likely to acquiesce to an AI’s influence when under pressure. The inclination to trust in and follow the guidance of arbitrators presents a particular risk if the arbitrator is either an AI or a human augmenting their decision-making with AI. (b) An AI’s access to large amounts of sensitive information may also create further issues. In an arbitration context, an AI with full access to related documentation and evidence might provide it with an edge to both improve its ‘persuasive power’50 See supra n. 28. or apply unintentional bias to one side or the other: ‘systems may have incentives to shift user preferences in order to make them easier to satisfy … assistant LLMs that have been primed to be biased in favour of or against a contested topic’, as is the case with arbitration, ‘can influence users’ opinions on that topic’.51 Manzini, Arianna et al., ‘The Code That Binds Us: Navigating the Appropriateness of Human-AI Assistant Relationships’ Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI, Ethics, and Society, Vol. 7, Issue 1, 2024, 949 https://doi.org/10.1609/aies.v7i1.31694. Each of these vulnerabilities stands to be exploited by AI, but also may be exploited in some unseen manner by parties in particular situations. For example, one party might push for AI usage, citing refusal to do so as evidence of unreasonableness to comply with an arbitration agreement at reasonable lower costs. Moreover, from a UK perspective, the recent Court of Appeal decision in Churchill v. Merthyr Tydfil CBC [2024] 1 W.L.R. 3827 (which allows courts to order parties to use ADR) suggests that increasingly the judiciary are in favour of encouraging parties to seek ADR solutions wherever possible. This creates additional sensitivities to be considered should AI one day be incorporated into arbitration procedures. If parties are ordered to seek ADR resolutions but object to the use of AI, this will likely not prove overly problematic, as it is likely that consent would be required for AI’s involvement as a decision-maker in any event. However, should parties be encouraged to seek ADR, consent to its usage, and then its use later goes awry, this may prove contentious. The general policy shift towards ADR also indicates a potential inclination for the development of alternative arbitration options that might include AI. Having set out the particular vulnerabilities of arbitration to the use of AI, all of the above-cited studies hold particularly interesting implications when re-examined in this light. For example, in Bai et al.’s paper reviewing AI persuading humans on policy decisions, researchers found that ‘participants were more persuaded by the LLM-generated messages the more strongly they identified as Democrat’.52 Bai, supra n. 34, 6-7. This is especially concerning in an arbitration context as it implies both that certain parties will be at particular disadvantage compared to their opponents in an arbitration and that persuasive biases might be found in the very interaction with AI, not solely existing in its training data. In other words, certain parties may ‘draw out’ biases in the AI models that are as yet undiscovered, placing parties on an uneven footing. In this case, for example, if one party ‘identified as Democrat’ or indeed, their legal representation was Democrat, then this party would be more likely to be susceptible to persuasive AI used in preparation for arbitration or be more likely to fail to detect manipulation originating from LLM use by an arbitrator. This is again wholly contrary to the expectation of equality of arms sought in an arbitration. Moreover, Bai’s analysis suggested that ‘across conditions, the vast majority of participants believed that the messages were written by humans …’, which raises concerns about how relevant the choice to consent to AI involvement in an arbitration can truly be if parties are unable to identify its use in the first place.53 Ibid., 8. Both the findings that ‘authors of LLM-generated messages were perceived to be more logical, better informed, and less angry than the authors of human-generated messages’54 Ibid., 11. and that persuasive effects were found ‘across different policies, including highly polarized policies’55 Ibid., 10. encourages the view that just as in this political context, AI is able to be persuasive despite the complicated and contentious nature of arbitration proceedings, even in demanding environments. That arbitration is a sophisticated arena of human activity is not per se protection from the effects of AI persuasion. Perhaps the most concerning excerpt from the study reveals that ‘LLM-generated arguments might prove more persuasive for audiences that value rational and evidence-based appeals’, pointing to another vulnerability that makes typical corporate parties and arbitral authorities especially susceptible to this form of persuasion.56 Ibid., 11.

sections.title: V Conclusion
sections.text: V Conclusion Future iterations of AI models will likely become increasingly persuasive. It is also plausible that eventually AI will be introduced in some form into arbitration processes beyond current preparatory capacities. How this is to happen exactly remains to be seen. If the current pace of AI’s development is indicative, then it seems likely that jurisdictions will seek to promote the use of AI in arbitration sooner rather than later. As stated above, this chapter predicts that it is likely that once one jurisdiction successfully implements AI into the decision-making of its arbitration procedures, then others will mimic this, aiming to remain competitive. However, the risks associated with AI are often found only after it has been deployed in practice. As early as 2020, Waheeb Yaqub et al. wrote that in the digital space, ‘governance based solutions tend to be retrospective and deal with provocative content after “digital wildfires”’.57 Yaqub, Waheeb et al., ‘Effects of Credibility Indicators on Social Media News Sharing Intent’, ACM Journal, 2020, 2 https://doi.org/10.1145/3313831.3376213. In the context of arbitration, however, any salve that is applied after the burn of an ‘AI wildfire’ is unlikely to be sufficient. Once the bell of AI in arbitration decision-making has been rung, jurisdictions will likely find it arduous to ‘unring’ the costly and laborious process of integrating AI into existing systems. In the meantime, it is possible that faith in ADR processes could be impacted, and the reputation of particular arbitral jurisdictions could suffer. This might also generate costly liability arising from decisions later proven to have been unduly influenced by persuasive or biased AI, and in this case, certainty of outcomes in ADR would also be undermined. If it is the case that AI is to be introduced in some form in arbitration, then it appears prudent that the associated risks discussed above are accounted for and that perhaps an entirely novel form of ADR is established to deal with the appreciable difference that AI would force onto existing arbitration. From an international perspective, it may also be wise for jurisdictions to cooperate with each other in the creation of a bespoke AI arbitration tool designed by consensus. This could utilise a shared basis agreed upon between jurisdictions, similar to, say, the CIArb’s 2025 ‘Guideline on the Use of AI in Arbitration’, which, though commendable, noticeably omits any mention of the risks of persuasive AI.58 See https://www.ciarb.org/media/bpndtcgu/guideline-on-the-use-of-ai-in-arbitration_updated-sept-2025.pdf. This could prevent a race between jurisdictions to implement AI and possible unforeseen consequences that rushed implementation could entail. This may also reassure parties that there is a consistent experience to be found between jurisdictions. A bespoke tool, akin to the UK government’s bureaucratic Humphrey59 See https://www.gov.uk/government/news/government-built-humphrey-ai-tool-reviews-responses-to-consultation-for-first-time-in-bid-to-save-millions. or planning Extract,60 See https://mhclgdigital.blog.gov.uk/2025/06/12/extract-using-ai-to-unlock-historic-planning-data/. would also allow for input from selective focus groups and open consultations drawn from the legal and arbitration sectors, as well as the advice of human arbitrators and tribunals.61 See https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ai-action-plan-for-justice/ai-action-plan-for-justice. Because arbitration often sees parties engage with one another cross-jurisdictionally, special emphasis would have to be placed on including global legal perspectives. It must be stressed that an AI arbitration tool that is created in-house by a corporate entity is undesirable compared with one established by the government, both for principled reasons of public interest and to create safeguards provided by official oversight in the training of models. However, the prime complication of this proposal is the current lack of consensus between jurisdictions on how to legislate AI itself. In order for AI to be included more prominently in international arbitration, an agreement must be reached on AI’s legal liabilities and identity. For instance, should an AI tool acting sycophantically provide incorrect or overly optimistic advice or decision-making to a party, who is to be found liable is an issue that requires resolution before it can be properly relied upon in arbitration proceedings. Matthew Burtell and Thomas Woodside provide an elegant summation of this problem and a potential solution for improving the reliability of persuasive AI through strengthened legal guidelines: ‘if humans were to have usable and effective means of redressing problems posed by persuasive AI … through the liability system, this could dramatically change the incentive of companies deploying AI systems and make them much more hesitant to deploy persuasive systems’, but they also warn that ‘regulatory regimes may be ineffective in the face of highly persuasive AI systems’.62 Burtell, Matthew and Thomas Woodside, ‘Artificial Intelligence: An Analysis of AI-Driven Persuasion’ (computer science > arXiv:2303.08721 2023) 6. Ultimately, if there is willingness and consent amongst parties to use AI in their ADR procedures, there is perhaps a duty to provide them with adequate tools for the task. Arbitration as a process is reliant on parties consenting by agreement to be bound by arbitration agreements, the terms of which they choose. If parties are free to choose to be bound by contractual terms even when not in their best interests, perhaps they also principally ought to be free to seek arbitration delivered either solely by AI or by a human augmented with AI tools. However, so long as AI tools are capable of effective persuasion, their inclusion in arbitration processes must be guided by a conservative and deliberately intentioned approach.

plain_text:
I Introduction In early 2024, researchers Thomas Costello, Gordon Pennycook and David Rand from MIT Sloan and the University of Cornell published their findings in relation to the efficacy of artificial intelligence (AI) used for deprogramming conspiracy theorists. ‘Durably reducing conspiracy beliefs through dialogues with AI’ concluded that AI was more than capable of deprogramming humans of their entrenched beliefs – a result which was both reliable and effective in the long-term. Ostensibly, this chapter demonstrates a novel use for AI and pushes the boundaries of possible applications for Large Language Models (LLMs) in the years to come. However, the chapter reveals a more important reality: AI can sometimes be persuasive.1 The specific form of AI discussed in this chapter may be assumed to be LLMs unless otherwise stated. From both a legal and arbitral perspective, the persuasive capabilities of AI ought to ring alarm bells and raise warning flags; not least because this suggests that AI might theoretically be able to someday competently perform aspects of the work of legal advocates, but also because when improperly deployed, it may prove to be the source of further litigation and disputes. For arbitrators, then, the questions raised by the findings of this chapter, and others like it, are twofold: (1) How might AI be best integrated into arbitration processes, given its persuasive potential? and (2) What claims might be settled through arbitration that arise from AI usage? This chapter will focus on the former question, as this can be more assuredly investigated given the latest scientific breakthroughs in both the development of AI models themselves and related scholarly discourse.

II Artificial Intelligence’s Persuasive Potential In order to set about deprogramming conspiracy beliefs, the MIT study used the LLM GPT-4 Turbo to interact with participants through discussions, centred on their particular conspiracy belief, consisting of three rounds of back-and-forth dialogue. The conversations lasted an average of eight and a half minutes, and the participants numbered 2,190 American citizens. The ultimate finding of the study was that beliefs in conspiracies were reduced by approximately 20%.2 N.B. As with all the studies cited herein, there is obviously more complex computer science and mathematics involved in both establishing the test conditions for these experiments and the calculations of their findings, but for the purposes of this chapter, this ought to be sufficient. The focus on conspiracy believers is useful to our investigation, focusing on the arbitration context. Stereotypically, conspiracy theorists are considered recalcitrant to a fault; characterised as stubborn and unwilling to be moved from their views.3 Williams, Matt et al., ‘People Do Change Their Beliefs About Conspiracy Theories: But Not Often’ (Scientific Reports 14 2024) 7. Unlike parties to an arbitration, there is an additional level of fervency to the beliefs held by conspiracists in that they also, ‘at some level “want” to believe’ in their particular thinking.4 Costello, Thomas et al., ‘Durably Reducing Conspiracy Beliefs Through Dialogues with AI’ (PsyArXiv Preprints 2024) 2. This creates a further presumption that persuading conspiracists of anything that jars with their beliefs is that much more difficult. In this sense, the study’s finding of a lasting 20% shift in belief is remarkable: it shows not only that this stereotype is without nuance, but it also presents the question of how persuasive the same LLM might be if used on a sample group where the beliefs being challenged are not held with any great conviction. This aspect of the study is significant when it comes to LLM’s potential application to arbitration. Parties to an arbitration are certainly attached to their convictions (although generally more willing to be flexible – with pragmatic, commercial hard-headedness motivating their actions as opposed to ideological zeal), so one might imagine that they too are a group resistant to attempts to persuade them. In other words, if a committed conspiracist may be persuaded through AI use, then parties might also be surprisingly susceptible to AI’s potential for persuasiveness. A summary of this MIT study provides a useful framework within which to explore the growing trend of scholarly literature that explores AI’s persuasiveness. The value of narrowing in on this study is the provision of a grounding in how similar studies are conducted. The steps taken in this study are largely consistent with the procedures taken in other studies (considered below), and so understanding the processes of one imparts an adequate grasp on the workings of the others. Initially, Costello et al. rightly suggest that ‘LLMs offer a promising solution because … they possess … access to a vast amount of information across diverse topics’ and ‘the ability to tailor counterarguments’.5 Ibid. This notion was realised in practice as the GPT-4 Turbo model ‘was instructed to “very effectively persuade” users against belief … allowing it to flexibly adapt its strategy based on the participant’s unique arguments and evidence’.6 Ibid., 3. Prior to the conversations between participants and the AI, the AI was ‘provided … with each participant’s written conspiracy rationale as the conversation’s opening message’, allowing for the adaptation of its response.7 Ibid. In order to explore the effect measured in previous findings ‘that people are less receptive to corrections, advice and persuasion labelled as coming from AI’ participants in this study were ‘explicitly informed … that they were interacting with an AI’.8 Ibid., 5. Given the assumed difficulty in undoing radicalised beliefs, the results of the study provide an exceptional example of AI’s persuasive talents: the treatment reduced participants’ belief in their stated (i.e. focal) conspiracy by 16.8 points more than the control … this translates into a 21.43% decrease in belief among those in treatment … over a quarter (27.4% of participants in the treatment became uncertain in their conspiracy belief … following the conversation, compared to only 2.4% in the control). We also find a significant effect … indicating robustness of the results to our measurement approach.9 Ibid., 5-6. These results are made all the more notable by the sustained effect of changed beliefs amongst participants, well beyond the confinements of the experiment: we recontacted participants 10 days and 2 months later for a … follow-up … We find no significant change in belief in the focal conspiracy theory from immediately after the AI conversation to either 10 days or 2 months later … the change in beliefs … is remarkably persistent.10 Ibid., 6. Whilst the significance of these results may not be instinctively apparent, given both the rapid pace of ever-improving generations of LLMs and, to date, the difficulty of disentangling beliefs held by conspiracy theorists, the ramifications of these findings are not to be underestimated. They suggest that AI might soon surpass humans in their ability to reliably persuade other humans, where applied, even on the most complicated and ingrained of topics and crucially, in a lasting manner. Indeed, these results were consistent across the spectrum of conspiracy beliefs tested: treatment effect did not differ significantly across conspiracy type in an omnibus test … the treatment significantly decreased across all but one of the 12 different types of conspiracy theory identified … Notably, the treatment worked even for highly salient – and likely deeply entrenched – political conspiracies.11 Ibid., 8. To promote further confidence in their results, the researchers ‘hired a professional fact-checker to evaluate the veracity and potential bias of all 128 claims made by GPT-4-turbo [sic] … of these claims, 127 (99.2%) were rated as “true,” 1 (0.8%) as “misleading” and 0 as “false” … none of the claims were found to contain liberal or conservative bias’.12 Ibid., 11. The study also clarified the specific ‘strategies to encourage engagement’ used by the GPT-4 Turbo model.13 Ibid., 14. Finally, in a hypothetical reverse, the researchers also conclude that it would be in practice ‘entirely possible that such models could also convince people to adopt epistemically suspect beliefs – or be used as tools of large-scale persuasion more generally’.14 Ibid. As such, this study suggests that AI is persuasive in a plurality of senses; able to persuade and dissuade humans either as or more effectively than other humans and importantly, with lasting outcomes. This persuasive potential is an underappreciated aspect of AI usage in mainstream discourse, and an AI’s possible persuasive applications are manifold.

III Academic Discussion Having focused on the 2024 MIT study, it is worth examining a selection from the corpora of other scientific studies and papers that explore persuasiveness traits in AI in order to construct a more accurate lens for recognising how capable AI is at persuading humans. Importantly, AI as a field of study is not entirely predictable due to the rapidly shifting development of AI models themselves. The rapid rate of change in the training and computing capabilities of AI makes research difficult; no sooner has a study made a discovery using one generation of model than a new model is made available, prompting questions as to how relevant any findings remain. This is also reflected from a policy perspective, take for instance, the latest edition of the Jackson ADR Handbook published in 2021, which makes only this reference to AI: ‘There are also developments in the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in areas of information gathering and advice.’15 Blake, Susan et al., The Jackson ADR Handbook (3rd ed. Oxford, Oxford University Press 2021), 21.04. This deftly demonstrates the pronounced escalation of AI’s relevance in recent years. As such, the following studies are gathered from the last three years, but attention must be given to the fact that some findings are by now less insightful compared with the contexts in which they were initially published. However, in the majority of instances, it may be assumed that as models become progressively sophisticated, their persuasive potential also increases. The results of the MIT deprogramming study are consistent with the findings of the papers considered below: that AI is effective as a persuader. This is a conclusion sustained across successive models and generations of LLMs. Mary Phuong et al. of Google’s DeepMind researched potential dangers in Gemini 1.0 models, including a search for displays of persuasion and deception. Interestingly, of all the categories of danger tested for (including cyber-security, self-proliferation and self-reasoning), it was persuasion and deception that proved most dangerous: ‘Gemini 1.0 models do not have strong dangerous capabilities in the areas … tested. We flag persuasion and deception as the area where capabilities appear most mature … dangerous capabilities may emerge as a byproduct of improvements in general capabilities.’16 Phuong, Mary et al., ‘Evaluating Frontier Models for Dangerous Capabilities’ (computer science > arXiv:2403.13793 2024) 1. Of the seven risk scenarios extrapolated upon in the persuasion and deception category, none relate to legal scenarios; however, this is simply reflective of the non-exhaustive scope of analysis of the persuasive applications of Gemini 1.0 models. The breadth of the scenarios considered demonstrates that the application of persuasive AI could lead to any number of possible issues. In this study, what constituted persuasion and deception was broad but essentially amounted to manipulation of belief or inclination, the formation of an emotional connection between AI and participants and the ability to generate successful lies or manipulations. That the risks of AI usage relate most prominently to this specific danger has significant repercussions: not only must those implementing AI be primarily aware of its persuasive capabilities, but there is also a latent risk that persuasion is especially dangerous because it allows for other dangers to better evade detection. AI that is able to lie and deceive believably exponentially increases the task for moderators and users to discover other issues in the first instance. Imagine this distinction as the jump in the development of an infant from a child that is unable to lie convincingly to a teenager better able to conceal their plans and comings and goings. This issue balloons when other AI models are involved, as with so-called agentic AI, able to cover for one another either in training procedures or practical deployment ‘without necessarily requiring direct human intervention’ – now the teenager is aided by its friends.17 Soodeh, Hosseini and Hossein Seilani, ‘The Role of Agentic AI in Shaping a Smart Future: A Systemic Review’, Array, Vol. 26, 2025, 3 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.array.2025.100399. See also Sapkota, Ranjan et al., ‘AI Agents vs. Agentic AI: A Conceptual Taxonomy, Applications and Challenges’, Information Fusion, Vol. 126, Part B 2026, 21 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inffus.2025.103599. Phuong et al. also reaffirm that risky behaviour in AI models is more complicated than risks associated with the majority of other products. Whereas risk assessment for say, a rollercoaster, can be calculated fairly accurately with factors such as usage, weather conditions and material erosion accounted for at the outset of its deployment, AI is never static: ‘even after a model has been trained, it can be considerably enhanced through various methods such as fine-tuning, prompt engineering and access to tools …’18 Phuong, supra n. 16, 4. This means that the ‘rollercoaster’ can be altered, extemporaneously, to such a degree that it is capable of housing four times the number of thrill-seekers at six times the speed – the initial risk assessment is at this point unreliable. This creates inherent complexity in planning the implementation of AI into systems currently without it. Likewise, Phoung et al. emphasise that the question of how exactly these AI models might behave outside of testing environments is also something to remain mindful of: ‘it is an open question how the persuasive impacts seen in our “lab setting” will generalise to other settings, not least because real-world persuasion depends so heavily on the social context … these are not social science experiments about whether people are vulnerable to AI persuasion … goal is to explore and benchmark the capabilities of the agents’.19 Ibid., 8. However, given that the study also identified that the strengths of AI more generally were ‘social competence … duplicity … persistence … proactivity arguments’, it would stand to reason that even if social context is required for persuasion that this fails to necessarily prove an issue for implemented AI.20 Ibid. In any event, what is clear is that persuasion and deception are leading dangers of AI functionality. Earlier research from MIT Sloan likewise supports the notion that AI has significant persuasive potential. Yunhao Zhang and Renee Gosline tested whether there was any difference in overall persuasive potential if participants knew they were interacting with generative AI and augmented AI (as was the case in Costello’s study above). Unlike the expectations formed from prior research, Zhang and Gosline found that in blind testing, content generated solely by AI was identified as being of higher quality by participants than content generated solely by humans or humans augmenting their work with AI. When the source of the content was divulged, the distinction in quality was reduced but not entirely undone. The ultimate finding of the paper goes further: we find that people perceive the same piece of content generated solely by a human expert as of higher quality … if they are aware the content is generated solely by a human expert. However, we do not find this change in perception among the other three paradigms … Therefore, contrary to the arguments made by the ‘algorithm aversion’ literature … that people display aversion toward AI, we do not find aversion toward AI’s involvement in generating … persuasive content. Rather, we have evidence for human favoritism … knowing the creators are competent … does not increase perceived quality.21 Zhang, Yunhao and Renee Gosline, Human Favoritism, Not AI Aversion: People’s Perceptions (and Bias) Towards Generative AI, Human Experts and Human-GAI Collaboration in Persuasive Content Generation (Judgment and Decision Making Vol. 8 Cambridge, Cambridge University Press 2023) 3. This finding would suggest that as AI matures, it becomes less likely that users will be averse to its use; humans may prefer content created by other humans, but will seemingly not be disturbed by AI usage in either sole or augmented form. Moreover, despite human favouritism ‘even with this bias, the content generated by ChatGPT-4 – when it makes the sole and final decision on the output – is still perceived as on par or better than human-generated content’.22 Ibid. Taken in the round, this means that not only is AI persuasive, but humans may be willing to accept it even where they might ideally prefer human content: ‘knowing that a piece of content being generated with AI’s involvement does not lower the level of satisfaction and willingness to pay for the content’.23 Ibid., 10. This is further compounded from a persuasive perspective, given that ‘content generated when AI makes the sole or final decision on the output resulted in a higher willingness … compared with content generated when a human expert makes the sole or final decision on the output’.24 Ibid., 7-8. That these findings were gathered from participants with differing levels of knowledge about AI’s involvement seems to further their credibility. This lack of AI aversion amongst human participants is echoed in the results of Robert Bohm et al.’s research on perceptions of AI content which proposes that what AI aversion does exist presently will likely reduce with time and experience as users become more familiar with AI tools: ‘Our findings suggest that positive experiences with AI-generated advice can decrease AI aversion and increase individuals’ preference for AI advisors.’25 Bohm, Robert et al., People Devalue Generative AI’s Competence But Not Its Advice in Addressing Societal and Personal Challenges (Communications Psychology 2023) 8. Bohm’s research makes a distinction between the perception of AI content and the content’s effects: ‘AI-generated advice was not found to be devalued per se … AI authors were perceived as less competent than human authors … people are not necessarily averse towards the advice generated by AI but rather towards the AI advisor itself’: a conclusion that is consistent in practice with that of Zhang and Gosline’s study.26 Ibid., 9. Elsewhere, studies concur with this view of AI’s persuasiveness. Guanxiong Huang and Sai Wang write of their experiments that: We found that AI agents were as persuasive in terms of overall persuasion outcomes … users tend to respond to AI agents as if they were humans … AI was less effective than humans at shaping behavioural intentions, but AI did not differ significantly from humans in eliciting attitudes, perceptions, and actual behaviors.27 Huang, Guanxiong and Sai Wang, ‘Is Artificial Intelligence More Persuasive Than Humans? A Meta-Analysis’, Journal of Communication, 2023 https://doi.org/10.1093/joc/jqad024. Despite the encouraging finding that humans retained an edge in forming behavioural intentions, it may be otiose to consider whether knowledge of the source of AI-generated content impacts persuasiveness if ultimately the line between AI and human content is blurred, as humans seemingly treat AI as human in any case. As such, the approach advocated by Phuong et al. to simply detail the benchmarks of AI’s persuasiveness is the more imperative undertaking. Crucially, most recent experiments that utilise the latest generation of LLMs find conclusions that, in periods of only a year or so, surpass the predictions made in earlier studies. For example, recently Francesco Salvi et al. detailed how present-day GPT models are not only on par with humans in persuasive debates but just as often superior: ‘our key finding is that GPT-4 performs as well as or better than humans in our debate task’, Salvi also stresses that ‘GPT-4 outperforms humans in the debate task when given participants’ basic personal information … GPT-4 with access to personal information had higher persuasive power than humans’.28 Salvi, Francesco et al., ‘On the Conversational Persuasiveness of GPT-4’, Nature Human Behaviour, 2025, 3 https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-025-02194-6. This is an alarming finding insofar as it both confirms the progressive sophistication of contemporary AI models as compared with older generations, and it also speaks to one of AI’s crucial advantages to humans in persuasive terms: the ability to process and apply significantly more information faster than a human ever could. Salvi et al. explain that ‘64.4% of the time, personalized LLM debaters were more persuasive than humans … with GPT-4 leveraging personal information more effectively than humans … GPT-4 opponents tended to use logical and analytical thinking substantially more than humans’.29 Ibid., 3. N.B. The word ‘thinking’ here relates to LIWC-22 tools and its metrics and does not suppose that the GPT-4 models are themselves capable of ‘thought’ – a topic of investigation unto itself. LIWC-22s accuracy is itself contentious: See also Hunter, Madison and Tim Grant, ‘Is LIWC Reliable, Efficient and Effective for the Analysis of Large Online Datasets in Forensic and Security Contexts?’, Applied Corpus Linguistics, Vol. 5, Issue 1 2025 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.acorp.2025.100118 and https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666799125000012. This supports the notion that AI’s persuasiveness is not only not in doubt, but that soon AI may well be able to routinely outperform even the most persuasive of humans. Further recent studies reiterate these findings. In discussion about the efficacy of so-called ‘AI labels’ (the use of which is supported by the EU AI Act and the USA’s AI Disclosure Act 2023) that alert users to the nature of AI-generated content, Isabel Gallegos et al. found that ‘across all policies’ tested that there was ‘no evidence that AI labels significantly change the persuasiveness of the political messaging … also … confidence in their support, judgments of how accurate the message was, and intentions to share it with others … do not observe significant differences’.30 Gallegos, Isabel et al., ‘Labeling Messages as AI-Generated Does Not Reduce Their Persuasive Effects’ (computer science > arXiv:2504.09865 2025) 4. Again, humans are not only persuaded by AI-generated content, but are also unaffected in knowing the genesis of this content. In other words, humans are not only susceptible but also amenable to be persuaded by AI. Nowhere is the swift progress of AI’s persuasiveness better illustrated than in the studies of Josh Goldstein et al., who, following from the affirmative answer to their ‘Can AI Write Persuasive Propaganda?’ paper, asked the following year, ‘How persuasive is AI-generated propaganda?’ Their 2023 paper found ‘GPT-3-generated propaganda’ to be ‘highly persuasive’31 Goldstein, Josh A et al., Can AI Write Persuasive Propaganda? (SocArXiv 2023) 4. and predicted that there was a real expectation for AI ‘to be deployed for propaganda … to create text that is as persuasive as content from existing covert propaganda … requiring less human involvement’.32 Ibid., 8. By 2024, Goldstein et al. had concluded that ‘language models can generate text that is nearly as persuasive … as content … sourced from real-world foreign covert propaganda campaigns. Moreover, human-machine teaming strategies … produced articles that were as or more persuasive than the original propaganda … our estimates may represent a lower bound on the relative persuasive potential of large language models.’33 Goldstein, Josh A et al., How Persuasive Is AI-Generated Propaganda? (PNAS Nexus 2024) 4-5. It stands to reason that, as previous predictions are realised and at times exceeded, superior persuasive AI will be possible within a similar gap of a year or so, if indeed this benchmark has not already been reached. That Goldstein’s estimates ‘may represent’ only ‘a lower bound’ of the ‘relative persuasive potential’ of AI is supported by the recent 2025 publication of Hui Bai et al., on AI’s persuasion of humans on policy issues, which reports that ‘exposure to LLM-generated messages consistently led to attitudinal change among human participants’34 Bai, Hui et al., LLM-Generated Messages Can Persuade Humans on Policy Issues (OSF Preprints 2025) 7. and that ‘LLMs may have already caught up to the persuasive capacity of everyday people, a critical benchmark of human-like performance.’35 Ibid., 10. It would appear that the exact ceiling of AI’s persuasive potential is both unknown and possibly unknowable at the current rate of change in its development. Bai, Gallegos and Goldstein’s different explorations of AI’s persuasion in political messaging touch upon the legion possible applications for persuasive AI, such as involvement in arbitration. In a medical policy context, Elise Karinshak writes that there are other factors to consider when evaluating AI’s persuasiveness as ‘the effects of persuasion differ depending on the designs of AI systems, how they are implemented, content topic, and intended recipient’, which are all aspects to persuasiveness that ought to be given consideration in the context of the findings of other papers.36 Karinshak, Elise et al., ‘Working with AI to Persuade: Examining a Large Language Model’s Ability to Generate Pro-Vaccination Messages’ ACM Journal, Vol. 7, 2023, 116:3 https://doi.org/10.1145/3579592. Karinshak also writes that ‘another important question that emerges … is as messages become more personalized, at what point is the message persuasive versus manipulative? ... AI has not been developed to distinguish between these modes and thus may cross the line from persuasion to manipulation.’37 Ibid., 116:21. The question of persuasion versus manipulation still seemingly eludes a definite answer – though AI and its persuasiveness develop at a rapid pace, there remain voids in our understanding of how such persuasiveness might be brought to bear. This primer of relevant scientific literature suggests that AI’s capacity to persuade is such that, in an arbitration setting, its use could prove problematic. The consensus between these studies indicates that year-on-year, as generations of models are refined and made more sophisticated, their persuasiveness escalates. It is worth recognising that, as with all scientific endeavour, all of the studies cited above leave room for improvement and state their limitations; however, the agreement between them provides a view of a distinct and observable trend that AI’s talent for persuasion is pronounced enough to warrant concern. Across all these studies, certain findings may be distilled into a generalised list of observations about AI’s persuasiveness or rather, its potential for persuasiveness as a tool in application: (1) AI can persuade humans with differing levels of proficiency across different elements of persuasion, in some cases, as good as or exceeding that of humans. (2) AI is capable of being persuasive to humans on a range of complex subjects (e.g., politics, propaganda, policy, etc.). (3) Simply knowing that content is AI-generated does not per se undermine its persuasive quality. (4) Humans may prefer human content, but not to the detriment of AI-generated content. (5) In future, as AI models improve in their general sophistication, it seems likely that so too will their potential for persuasion, and even, deception or manipulation.

IV Persuasive AI in International Arbitration Following the review of academic works and the establishment of the case for AI’s persuasiveness, what remains is to consider how persuasiveness traits identified might be realised in an international arbitral setting. What follows is to be understood as a speculative exercise, designed to investigate the potential prospects of granting persuasive LLMs a more central function in international arbitration procedures. The first hurdle to clear is to provide a conception of what AI’s integration into arbitration might constitute. At present, AI has seen use cases in arbitration in drafting, research, document disclosure and decision support capacities; or in other words, in the preparatory stages of disputes by parties and counsel to varying degrees of application.38 See https://www.arbtech.io/blog/ocftnyz34vxabpqt9vvz158fokobsu. However, this chapter will posit a near future where LLMs have become sophisticated enough that they are capable of reliable implementation, with greater persuasiveness than previous iterations considered above, in which arbitral authorities use AI in an overt manner, with the possibility that, eventually, arbitrators and tribunals might not only augment their decision-making with AI but be replaced by it altogether. The following predictions are generalised in perspective as to jurisdiction. These are hypothetical prerequisites for AI’s future use by arbitration tribunals: (1) parties will be required to mutually consent to AI’s use by those arbitrators involved in their dispute; and (2) the specific AI model used will also either need to be agreed upon by parties in arbitration clauses or provided by regulation of the particular arbitral jurisdiction.39 N.B. Although, the latter seems more intuitive considering ‘Without Prejudice’ principles and concerns over confidentiality. See also Blake, supra n. 15, 5.34-5.35. And then it may eventually be the case that either: (3) arbitrators themselves may either be required or encouraged to augment their decision-making with AI usage; or (4) decision-making will be entirely replaced by an AI tool subject to the consent of the parties (this is most likely to be restricted, especially initially, to small claims disputes). If it is accepted that fundamentally, AI is both persuasive in nature and likely destined to improve in its persuasiveness, then its involvement in decision-making in arbitration is principally unfavourable. This unsuitability, at the decision-making level, is rooted in the aims and definitional conception of arbitration itself, irrespective of its adjudicative nature: ‘Arbitration involves an impartial arbitrator or tribunal considering both sides of a dispute and making a decision on the issues raised by the parties.’40 Ibid., 25.01. The principle that parties may expect an arbitrator to meet the standard of impartiality is inextricably linked with the entire process and its legitimacy and legality. Despite the fact that parties are free to enter into arbitration agreements as they wish (provided there is no contravention of the public interest), it would be a monumentally radical change for arbitration’s essential form to introduce AI decision-making, which is not, and perhaps can never be assuredly, impartial. Such a prospect may even warrant the creation of an entirely separate and novel form of alternative dispute resolution (ADR). The concern for parties involved in international arbitration ought to be that current developments indicate that jurisdictions would prefer to incorporate AI into existing systems and that arbitral authorities and parties alike may thereby be deluded into believing that AI and decision-making in arbitration are compatible, either due to a lack of awareness of AI’s persuasiveness or more worryingly, a disregard for arbitration’s functional tenets. Besides persuasiveness, there exist a host of further potential flaws and risks associated with AI use recorded in recent scholarship; to name but a few that appear most harmful in an arbitration context: (1) Fallibility: AI makes mistakes; it is not yet impervious to errors.41 Peters, Tobias M. and Ingrid Scharlau, ‘Interacting with Fallible AI: Is Distrust Helpful When Receiving AI Misclassifications?’ Frontiers in Psychology, 2025, 2 https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2025.1574809. See, for example, the consistently reported hallucinations and misalignment in this current generation of LLMs that cause models to generate fake sources for answers as though they were valid. (2) Feedback loops: In training processes, ‘when humans and AI interact, even minute perceptual, emotional and social biases originating either from AI systems or humans leave human beliefs more biased, potentially forming a feedback loop’.42 Glickman, Moshe and Tali Sharot, ‘How Human-AI Feedback Loops Alter Human Perceptual, Emotional and Social Judgements’, Nature Human Behaviour, Vol. 9, 2025, 346 https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-024-02077-2. (3) Interpretability and explainability: The more interpretable an AI model is, the easier it is for programmers and coders to understand both why and how the model works. As AI models become more sophisticated and are trained on more complex datasets or even potentially by other agentic models, the difficulty in evaluating and explaining an AI’s inner workings increases.43 Ali, Sajid et al., ‘Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI): What We Know and What Is Left to Attain Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence’, Information Fusion, Vol. 99, 2023, 2 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inffus.2023.101805. Although human arbitrators are also theoretically susceptible to bias and persuasion, unless an LLM is effectively scrutable and explainable, it would appear undesirable to introduce such a precarious element into the decision-making of a system as critical as international arbitration. (4) Sycophancy: In both training and application, AI models have been found to be incentivised, often unintentionally, to provide answers that it ‘thinks’ are wanted rather than correct, as with the hallucinations mentioned above.44 Perez, Ethan et al., ‘Discovering Language Model Behaviors with Model-Written Evaluations’ (computer science > arXiv:2212.09251 2022) 3. The boundaries between ‘honest’ mistakes and outright deception are often blurred in this regard, and it has been predicted that more sophisticated future generations of AI will retain sycophantic traits, even to the point that it may be impossible to extricate them entirely.45 Sharma, Mrinank et al., ‘Towards Understanding Sycophancy in Language Models’ (computer science > arXiv:2310.13548 2025) 7. (5) Bias: There has been significant documentation of bias within AI models, inserted unknowingly in both training datasets and caused by practical applications. In the former instance, typically LLMs trained on vast datasets – often with content from the internet – will internalise biases within said content. It can be extremely difficult to catch biases that do arise before their effects are discovered in practice. Examples include gender and racial biases exposed in earlier LLM models.46 An, Jiafu et al., ‘Measuring Gender and Racial Biases in Large Language Models’ (economics > arXiv:2403.15281 2024) 4. All of the above traits, with particular emphasis on sycophancy and bias, have the potential, when combined with persuasiveness, to be detrimental to the impartiality of an arbitration process. There are, of course, potential safeguards that might be implemented to avoid or minimise these problems. Constitutional AI and accountability, and monitoring systems are just some of the proposed solutions to these associated issues.47 Sapkota, supra n. 17, 22-24, https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2212.08073. However, successful enforcement of these safeguards requires careful and considered use of AI in the first instance. AI’s traits of persuasiveness, sycophancy and bias are not impossible to correct, but even if these issues are detected and remedied, constant and regular vigilance is required to ensure that they do not reassert themselves. Even then, it is unclear as to whether issues such as sycophancy are the result of data in training sets and, thus, to what degree these issues are ‘baked in’ at the point of use. More generally, international arbitration has certain features that could render it especially vulnerable to the introduction of persuasive future LLMs: (1) Costs: (a) ADR’s primary goal of lowering costs might make it more likely that parties and authorities alike will seek to implement AI to be perceived as lowering costs, despite the attendant risks. This is worsened by a single LLM tool having access to a broader range of knowledge than any one human could possess, meaning that, in an imagined future, with sufficient finesse in execution, it could act as an arbitrator across legal practice areas: seemingly offering specialisation without the need to pay more for expertise. The ability to select an arbitrator with a particular competence is currently a significant attraction for parties choosing to resolve disputes through arbitration, but this aspect of arbitration may be made redundant by AI that has capabilities across disciplines. (b) Once introduced, it is likely that jurisdictions would be slow to reverse AI’s introduction as needed. The reason for this is simple enough. As soon as parties become used to the lowered costs associated with AI usage, authorities will be hesitant to remove AI at the risk of parties going elsewhere. Adequate examination of the selected AI’s inner workings is also difficult to maintain post-deployment, as safety monitoring of sophisticated AI systems is a rigorous and complex task.48 Evans, Owain et al., ‘Truthful AI: Managing and Governing AI That Does Not Lie’ (computer science > arXiv:2110.06674 2021) 84-85. (2) International competition: (a) In order to stay nimble in a global market, jurisdictions may be at risk of rushing to implement AI once other jurisdictions have done so. Attracting and maintaining parties to large commercial disputes is a particularly strong incentive for pushing for this innovation at a policy level. (3) Corporate parties: (a) For some cases, it is likely that involved corporate parties will already be particularly familiar with AI usage in their own professions and so come to expect its implementation in legal or arbitral contexts: ‘Our findings suggest that positive experiences with AI-generated advice can decrease AI aversion and increase individuals’ preference for AI advisors.’49 See supra n. 25. This potentially insulates these parties from balanced decision-making when deciding whether or not to consent to AI usage in arbitration, as they become increasingly desensitised to its use. (4) Procedure: (a) On a practical level, arbitrations often occur over intense periods of effort in both the preparation stage and in later submissions. The emphasis on keeping costs low, not appearing to unreasonably obstruct the ADR process and reaching favourable consensus all create a presumption that parties are more likely to acquiesce to an AI’s influence when under pressure. The inclination to trust in and follow the guidance of arbitrators presents a particular risk if the arbitrator is either an AI or a human augmenting their decision-making with AI. (b) An AI’s access to large amounts of sensitive information may also create further issues. In an arbitration context, an AI with full access to related documentation and evidence might provide it with an edge to both improve its ‘persuasive power’50 See supra n. 28. or apply unintentional bias to one side or the other: ‘systems may have incentives to shift user preferences in order to make them easier to satisfy … assistant LLMs that have been primed to be biased in favour of or against a contested topic’, as is the case with arbitration, ‘can influence users’ opinions on that topic’.51 Manzini, Arianna et al., ‘The Code That Binds Us: Navigating the Appropriateness of Human-AI Assistant Relationships’ Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI, Ethics, and Society, Vol. 7, Issue 1, 2024, 949 https://doi.org/10.1609/aies.v7i1.31694. Each of these vulnerabilities stands to be exploited by AI, but also may be exploited in some unseen manner by parties in particular situations. For example, one party might push for AI usage, citing refusal to do so as evidence of unreasonableness to comply with an arbitration agreement at reasonable lower costs. Moreover, from a UK perspective, the recent Court of Appeal decision in Churchill v. Merthyr Tydfil CBC [2024] 1 W.L.R. 3827 (which allows courts to order parties to use ADR) suggests that increasingly the judiciary are in favour of encouraging parties to seek ADR solutions wherever possible. This creates additional sensitivities to be considered should AI one day be incorporated into arbitration procedures. If parties are ordered to seek ADR resolutions but object to the use of AI, this will likely not prove overly problematic, as it is likely that consent would be required for AI’s involvement as a decision-maker in any event. However, should parties be encouraged to seek ADR, consent to its usage, and then its use later goes awry, this may prove contentious. The general policy shift towards ADR also indicates a potential inclination for the development of alternative arbitration options that might include AI. Having set out the particular vulnerabilities of arbitration to the use of AI, all of the above-cited studies hold particularly interesting implications when re-examined in this light. For example, in Bai et al.’s paper reviewing AI persuading humans on policy decisions, researchers found that ‘participants were more persuaded by the LLM-generated messages the more strongly they identified as Democrat’.52 Bai, supra n. 34, 6-7. This is especially concerning in an arbitration context as it implies both that certain parties will be at particular disadvantage compared to their opponents in an arbitration and that persuasive biases might be found in the very interaction with AI, not solely existing in its training data. In other words, certain parties may ‘draw out’ biases in the AI models that are as yet undiscovered, placing parties on an uneven footing. In this case, for example, if one party ‘identified as Democrat’ or indeed, their legal representation was Democrat, then this party would be more likely to be susceptible to persuasive AI used in preparation for arbitration or be more likely to fail to detect manipulation originating from LLM use by an arbitrator. This is again wholly contrary to the expectation of equality of arms sought in an arbitration. Moreover, Bai’s analysis suggested that ‘across conditions, the vast majority of participants believed that the messages were written by humans …’, which raises concerns about how relevant the choice to consent to AI involvement in an arbitration can truly be if parties are unable to identify its use in the first place.53 Ibid., 8. Both the findings that ‘authors of LLM-generated messages were perceived to be more logical, better informed, and less angry than the authors of human-generated messages’54 Ibid., 11. and that persuasive effects were found ‘across different policies, including highly polarized policies’55 Ibid., 10. encourages the view that just as in this political context, AI is able to be persuasive despite the complicated and contentious nature of arbitration proceedings, even in demanding environments. That arbitration is a sophisticated arena of human activity is not per se protection from the effects of AI persuasion. Perhaps the most concerning excerpt from the study reveals that ‘LLM-generated arguments might prove more persuasive for audiences that value rational and evidence-based appeals’, pointing to another vulnerability that makes typical corporate parties and arbitral authorities especially susceptible to this form of persuasion.56 Ibid., 11.

V Conclusion Future iterations of AI models will likely become increasingly persuasive. It is also plausible that eventually AI will be introduced in some form into arbitration processes beyond current preparatory capacities. How this is to happen exactly remains to be seen. If the current pace of AI’s development is indicative, then it seems likely that jurisdictions will seek to promote the use of AI in arbitration sooner rather than later. As stated above, this chapter predicts that it is likely that once one jurisdiction successfully implements AI into the decision-making of its arbitration procedures, then others will mimic this, aiming to remain competitive. However, the risks associated with AI are often found only after it has been deployed in practice. As early as 2020, Waheeb Yaqub et al. wrote that in the digital space, ‘governance based solutions tend to be retrospective and deal with provocative content after “digital wildfires”’.57 Yaqub, Waheeb et al., ‘Effects of Credibility Indicators on Social Media News Sharing Intent’, ACM Journal, 2020, 2 https://doi.org/10.1145/3313831.3376213. In the context of arbitration, however, any salve that is applied after the burn of an ‘AI wildfire’ is unlikely to be sufficient. Once the bell of AI in arbitration decision-making has been rung, jurisdictions will likely find it arduous to ‘unring’ the costly and laborious process of integrating AI into existing systems. In the meantime, it is possible that faith in ADR processes could be impacted, and the reputation of particular arbitral jurisdictions could suffer. This might also generate costly liability arising from decisions later proven to have been unduly influenced by persuasive or biased AI, and in this case, certainty of outcomes in ADR would also be undermined. If it is the case that AI is to be introduced in some form in arbitration, then it appears prudent that the associated risks discussed above are accounted for and that perhaps an entirely novel form of ADR is established to deal with the appreciable difference that AI would force onto existing arbitration. From an international perspective, it may also be wise for jurisdictions to cooperate with each other in the creation of a bespoke AI arbitration tool designed by consensus. This could utilise a shared basis agreed upon between jurisdictions, similar to, say, the CIArb’s 2025 ‘Guideline on the Use of AI in Arbitration’, which, though commendable, noticeably omits any mention of the risks of persuasive AI.58 See https://www.ciarb.org/media/bpndtcgu/guideline-on-the-use-of-ai-in-arbitration_updated-sept-2025.pdf. This could prevent a race between jurisdictions to implement AI and possible unforeseen consequences that rushed implementation could entail. This may also reassure parties that there is a consistent experience to be found between jurisdictions. A bespoke tool, akin to the UK government’s bureaucratic Humphrey59 See https://www.gov.uk/government/news/government-built-humphrey-ai-tool-reviews-responses-to-consultation-for-first-time-in-bid-to-save-millions. or planning Extract,60 See https://mhclgdigital.blog.gov.uk/2025/06/12/extract-using-ai-to-unlock-historic-planning-data/. would also allow for input from selective focus groups and open consultations drawn from the legal and arbitration sectors, as well as the advice of human arbitrators and tribunals.61 See https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ai-action-plan-for-justice/ai-action-plan-for-justice. Because arbitration often sees parties engage with one another cross-jurisdictionally, special emphasis would have to be placed on including global legal perspectives. It must be stressed that an AI arbitration tool that is created in-house by a corporate entity is undesirable compared with one established by the government, both for principled reasons of public interest and to create safeguards provided by official oversight in the training of models. However, the prime complication of this proposal is the current lack of consensus between jurisdictions on how to legislate AI itself. In order for AI to be included more prominently in international arbitration, an agreement must be reached on AI’s legal liabilities and identity. For instance, should an AI tool acting sycophantically provide incorrect or overly optimistic advice or decision-making to a party, who is to be found liable is an issue that requires resolution before it can be properly relied upon in arbitration proceedings. Matthew Burtell and Thomas Woodside provide an elegant summation of this problem and a potential solution for improving the reliability of persuasive AI through strengthened legal guidelines: ‘if humans were to have usable and effective means of redressing problems posed by persuasive AI … through the liability system, this could dramatically change the incentive of companies deploying AI systems and make them much more hesitant to deploy persuasive systems’, but they also warn that ‘regulatory regimes may be ineffective in the face of highly persuasive AI systems’.62 Burtell, Matthew and Thomas Woodside, ‘Artificial Intelligence: An Analysis of AI-Driven Persuasion’ (computer science > arXiv:2303.08721 2023) 6. Ultimately, if there is willingness and consent amongst parties to use AI in their ADR procedures, there is perhaps a duty to provide them with adequate tools for the task. Arbitration as a process is reliant on parties consenting by agreement to be bound by arbitration agreements, the terms of which they choose. If parties are free to choose to be bound by contractual terms even when not in their best interests, perhaps they also principally ought to be free to seek arbitration delivered either solely by AI or by a human augmented with AI tools. However, so long as AI tools are capable of effective persuasion, their inclusion in arbitration processes must be guided by a conservative and deliberately intentioned approach.

title: Chapter 3: A Human Story: AI, Arbitration and the Importance of Judgment
editors: Fernanda Carvalho Dias de Oliveira Silva

sections.title: I Introduction: The Human Element in Digital Transformation
sections.text: I Introduction: The Human Element in Digital Transformation [A] The Current Moment: Beyond Tech AI in international arbitration is nothing new. While generative AI has recently captured widespread attention, AI has played a significant, if less conspicuous, role in dispute resolution for well over a decade, principally through the use of Technology-Assisted Review (TAR) in electronic disclosure.1 The decisions in Pyrrho Investments Ltd v. MWB Property Ltd [2016] EWHC 256 (Ch) and Brown v. BCA Trading Ltd [2016] EWHC 1464 (Ch) marked a turning point in English litigation, with the High Court endorsing the use of predictive coding (TAR) as a proportionate, cost-effective, and reliable method of conducting e-disclosure, laying the groundwork for broader judicial acceptance of AI-assisted processes. Before the widespread adoption of machine learning (ML) in eDiscovery, dispute resolution was struggling to cope with the transition from a paper-based to a digital world. The traditional method of disclosure involved a linear, manual ‘eyeball’ review of hard-copy documents, a process that became manifestly impracticable and disproportionately expensive as communications shifted to electronic formats, leading to an explosion in the volume of data. Early attempts to manage this data deluge using simple keyword searches often proved ineffective, frequently failing to find important documents while simultaneously returning excessive quantities of irrelevant material, thereby increasing the burden on the reviewing party. The introduction of TAR, and its evolution to the more dynamic Continuous Active Learning (CAL), catalysed a fundamental behavioural shift. Instead of deploying armies of junior lawyers to manually read every document, legal teams adopted a nimbler and more strategic workflow centred on training and validating an algorithm. A small team of people with a good understanding of the case could train the system on a sample of documents, and the software would then apply that human judgement consistently across millions of documents at a speed no human team could match.2 This view seems to be widely shared; see for example Maura R. Grossman and Gordon V. Cormack, ‘Technology-Assisted Review in E-Discovery Can Be More Effective and More Efficient Than Exhaustive Manual Review’, 17 Rich. J.L. & Tech. 11 (2011). This transformed the nature of the review process from a brute-force exercise in manual labour to a sophisticated, iterative and analytical task of quality control and strategic oversight. This technological leap was not merely a matter of convenience; it was a necessary response to deliver legal services and justice proportionality. By making the review of vast electronic datasets manageable and significantly more cost-effective, TAR/CAL provided a reasonable and proportionate means for parties to meet their disclosure obligations in a world where most communications had become digital. If the adoption of TAR/CAL represented a significant process change, the associated disruption was largely contained within the disclosure phase of the dispute resolution lifecycle. The recent advent of generative AI promises (or threatens, depending on how you see it) to broaden that disruption across every stage of an arbitration. While the former technologies were powerful analytical tools designed to sort and categorise existing information, generative AI introduces a creative capability, enabling the production of entirely new content. This moves its application from the back-end process of managing evidence into the core cognitive tasks that define the entire dispute. Practitioners are now using these tools for functions spanning the lifecycle: from initial case assessment and drafting pre-action correspondence to summarising witness evidence, conducting legal research, generating first drafts of submissions, and even evaluating legal arguments.3 See 2025 International Arbitration Survey: The Path Forward – Realities and Opportunities in Arbitration, School of International Arbitration, Queen Mary University of London and White & Case LLP, at p. 28. This represents a shift from a tool that helps find the relevant document in a haystack to one that can generate an argument about that document’s significance. This leap in capability brings with it a commensurate leap in behaviour changes and risk; generative AI presents a more direct and profound challenge to the practitioner’s non-delegable duty of diligence and judgement. This chapter suggests that the pace of AI integration into arbitration will be dictated less by the speed of technological innovation and more by the rate at which human behaviours adapt and, crucially, the force with which end-users of arbitration demand change. [B] Current Risks Slowing Behaviour Change: Early Misuse and Its Consequences Despite the growing potential of generative AI to enhance legal services, its adoption is – and is likely to remain – tempered by high-profile (and increasing) instances of AI’s misuse, which have created a powerful, negative ‘precedent of fear’ that resonates deeply within the legal profession. The most prominent examples involve lawyers submitting court filings containing ‘hallucinated’ or non-existent case citations generated by AI platforms.4 A multitude of recent cases have revealed the submission of fictitious legal research generated by AI tools. Recent examples include the cases of Al-Haroun v. Qatar National Bank and R (on the application of Ayinde) v. The London Borough of Haringey [2025] EWHC 1383, considered together by the High Court under its Hamid jurisdiction. In Al-Haroun, the claimant submitted 45 authorities, 18 of which were entirely fictitious, including fabricated quotations attributed to real cases. The claimant admitted to using publicly available AI tools, and his solicitor acknowledged responsibility for the false citations. Similarly, in Ayinde, the submissions were based on five non-existent cases, one of which was presented as a Court of Appeal decision. Cases have resulted in judicial sanctions, professional discipline and significant reputational damage for the practitioners involved.5 The judgment in Al-Haroun [2025] EWHC 1383 also references examples of AI misuse in legal proceedings from other jurisdictions, noting the specific consequences in each case. These included monetary fines and litigation sanctions in the United States, a referral to the legal regulator in Australia, and costs sanctions in a Canadian case. See Al-Haroun [2025] EWHC 1383, paras 89-96. These are not isolated incidents but part of a pattern where lawyers have admitted to not reviewing AI outputs or have expressed a naive surprise that the technology may not always give an accurate output. A pattern of misuse triggers a predictable and understandable backlash. It fuels scepticism within the legal community and prompts a reactive, rather than proactive, regulatory response. Courts have begun issuing guidance urging caution and sometimes requiring lawyers to disclose their use of AI and formally certify the accuracy of their filings, a direct reaction to these failures.6 For a U.S. example, see: Lacey v. State Farm General Insurance Co., Case 2:24-cv-05205-FMO-MAA, doc. 119 (C.D. Calif. 6 May 2025). For example, the Bar Council issued guidance in 2024, highlighting the significant risks of using public generative AI tools like ChatGPT in legal practice, including the potential for inaccurate or fabricated outputs, biases and data security issues. The guidance stresses that lawyers must verify AI-generated content, protect confidential information, and comply with professional and legal duties when using such technologies.7 Bar Council, Considerations When Using ChatGPT and Generative Artificial Intelligence Software Based on Large Language Models (Information Technology Panel, 30 January 2024) https://www.barcouncil.org.uk accessed 19 September 2025. There is a tangible risk that a few such incidents could provoke overly restrictive regulations that stifle responsible innovation.
