Arbitration practitioners, like all humans, are vulnerable to cognitive and emotional biases when interacting with AI outputs. The combination of high fluency, speed, and comprehensive presentation can trigger a psychological ‘wow factor’ leading to what scholars term algorithmic appreciation, a tendency to trust algorithmic outputs over human ones, even when both are demonstrably flawed.13 ArbTech, The ‘Wow Factor’ of AI in Judicial Decision-Making (24 September 2025). When AI provides coherent, jargon-appropriate summaries, practitioners may unconsciously defer to its authority, especially under time pressure, resulting in reduced critical scrutiny. This can cause practitioners to treat AI-generated insights as inherently correct, potentially overlooking hidden nuances or embedded biases. These abilities – to assess AI outputs for accuracy, bias and reliability – are rapidly becoming a core competency in arbitration practice. They are not technical skills in isolation, nor are they mere compliance obligations. They represent a hybrid skill set where legal judgment must be exercised in a digital context. The existing gap between the capabilities of AI tools and the skill sets of arbitration practitioners is both measurable and remediable. Addressing it will require investment in education, reform of professional training, and a redefinition of legal competence in the age of AI. At the same time, only part of this challenge sits with individual practitioners. While individual practitioners can be expected to perform checks, spot inconsistencies, and recognise signs of AI hallucination, evaluating an AI system’s overall accuracy, bias, and reliability goes beyond what can be achieved in daily legal work. A robust assessment would involve model benchmarking, auditing, and monitoring. Crucially, the algorithms underpinning AI systems must be explainable and interpretable so that they can be subjected to scrutiny by parties and tribunals. This is particularly important where automated systems might influence case management or decision-making. Meeting this standard will likely necessitate investment from firms or arbitral institutions into technical resources, infrastructure, and computer science expertise. [B] Cultural Barriers in a Precedent-Based Profession Beyond developing the essential hybrid skills needed to work effectively with AI, law firms and arbitral institutions must also cultivate a supportive culture to enable meaningful AI adoption. The legal profession is inherently conservative, grounded in precedent, risk mitigation and expert judgment. While these qualities underpin legal rigour and quality, they can also foster reticence toward experimentation and change. The result is a profession caught in a cultural paradox: aware that AI may enhance efficiency and accuracy, but constrained by the fear of making costly mistakes that could damage professional standing and undermine client interests. That fear is not misplaced: insufficient training and persistent gaps in understanding how to effectively use AI tools, assess their limitations, and mitigate associated risks contribute to legal14 Thomson Reuters Institute, 2024 Generative AI in Professional Services report – as summarized by Caroline Hill in Legal IT Insider (2 December 2024). Available at: https://legaltechnology.com/2024/12/02/just-10-of-law-firms-have-a-genai-policy-new-thomson-reuters-report-shows/. ‘reluctance to adopt them’. According to a Thomson Reuters report, 77% of legal professionals surveyed said they were concerned about risks related to the use of AI.15 Thomson Reuters Institute, 2024 Generative AI in Professional Services report – as summarized by Caroline Hill in Legal IT Insider (2 December 2024). Available at: https://legaltechnology.com/2024/12/02/just-10-of-law-firms-have-a-genai-policy-new-thomson-reuters-report-shows/. To overcome this barrier, legal organisations need to create clear governance frameworks that provide concrete guidance on when and how to use AI and, equally important, when not to. Yet the development of such governance and corresponding training has typically lagged behind AI deployment,16 LexisNexis, New Survey Data from LexisNexis Points to Seismic Shifts in Law Firm Business Models and Corporate Legal Expectations Due to Generative AI (24 September 2024). Available at: https://www.lexisnexis.com/community/pressroom/b/news/posts/new-survey-data-from-lexisnexis-points-to-seismic-shifts-in-law-firm-business-models-and-corporate-legal-expectations-due-to-generative-ai. leaving practitioners uncertain about the risks and cautious about adoption. Legal organisations can also promote AI adoption by creating spaces for careful experimentation with AI tools, enabling controlled trials, iterative learning and cross-disciplinary collaboration. Legal practitioners generally do not buy into the concept of being ‘safe to fail’ (as there is simply no scope for failure given client interests), but they are more likely to engage with new ways of working where they feel ‘safe to experiment’. Leadership plays a pivotal role here; without engaged leaders who endorse and role-model experimentation, adoption efforts risk being stifled.17 Gallup Inc, Culture of AI Benchmark Report: State of AI Adoption and Culture Readiness in Europe, 4 (2024). Available at: https://www.gallup.com/workplace/652784/culture-of-ai-and-adoption-report.aspx. Clients are also a driver of change in arbitration practice. They increasingly expect their legal advisers to be technologically capable, placing pressure on the profession to keep pace with digital advancements.18 For example, evidence from the 2024 Legal Operations and Technology Survey by Bloomberg Law reveals that law firm professionals expect new technologies, including generative AI, to increase the amount of work received from in-house clients, suggesting that technological capability is becoming an important factor in legal service delivery. Legal advisers who fail to respond to these expectations risk falling behind competitors and potentially missing out on opportunities. [C] Commercial Pressures and Misaligned Incentives Finally, the prevailing billable hour model and associated incentive structures in law firms may also discourage effective AI integration in arbitration practice. Despite a growing interest in value-based pricing, most firms globally continue to rely on the billable hour model.19 Brooke MacKenzie, ‘Better Value: Problems with the Billable Hour and the Viability of Value-Based Billing.’ 90 Can. B. Rev. 675 (2011). This model does not allow legal advisers to pass on costs involved in experimentation or learning from trial and error to their clients, and the model itself arguably does not reward practitioners who unlock greater efficiencies through the use of AI tools. Similarly, incentives in law firms are typically linked to hours worked. As a result, some firms and individuals may perceive advanced AI adoption not as an opportunity but as a threat to revenue streams. Indeed, a study conducted on behalf of Thomson Reuters found that 40% of the law firm professionals surveyed believe that the billable hour model hampers the innovation and adoption of generative AI.20 Thomson Reuters, Tech, AI and the Law 19 (2024). Conversely, clients are increasingly demanding greater efficiency and productivity at a lower cost. They are simply unwilling to pay premium rates for work they know could be automated by AI tools. This exposes a fundamental misalignment between the market’s expectations and current law firm commercial models. To bridge this gap and unlock mutual value, clients, practitioners, and arbitral institutions need to align around new pricing frameworks. Such models should incentivise and reward investment in AI technologies, facilitating the sharing of efficiency gains and fostering a sustainable approach to innovation in arbitration services.

III The Guardrails and Redlines for AI in Arbitration [A] The Automation of Professional Judgement The integration of AI into arbitration also requires, fundamentally, a clearer understanding of professional judgment, a quality that has traditionally been the domain of the human lawyer or arbitrator. In arbitration, judgment is not simply the application of legal rules to a set of facts; it is a complex process of thought and ethics. It is the fundamentally human ability to reach a fair and impartial decision, free from bias, based on a deep and contextual understanding of legal principles, commercial realities, and human intentions and motivations. This involves navigating the ‘grey areas’ of the law, balancing competing duties and applying a sense of fairness. It also requires the ability to weigh both objective facts and subjective inputs – including information which may be uncertain or incomplete – and to make decisions that reflect not just legal correctness, but ethical, commercial and practical implications.21 M. Legg, ‘Better Than a Bot: Instilling Ethical Judgement into the Lawyers of the Future in the Age of AI’ 33(3) Griffith L. Rev. 273-293 (2024) https://doi.org/10.1080/10383441.2025.2493493. These qualities of human judgment, and the justice they afford, often stand in contrast to the perception of AI-generated output as purely analytical and mathematical. AI can assist with parts of the process: it can analyse large volumes of data, identify patterns and generate predictions – such as whether documents are relevant in disclosure or how a tribunal might rule based on past decisions. Generative AI, in particular, can produce plausible text by predicting word sequences. In this way, it can, with great speed, mimic the perception of human thinking and judgment. But the importance of human judgement continues, and arguably increases, because AI does not perform the ‘judgement step’. It is still up to the practitioner to decide how to interpret and act on AI-generated outputs, based on the broader context of a case and other circumstances.22 Ibid. Several qualities considered essential to justice and the legitimacy of arbitration remain uniquely human.23 Aziz Z Huq, ‘A Right to a Human Decision’ 106 Va. L. Rev. 611 (2020). Available at: https://virginialawreview.org/articles/right-human-decision/. These include: – Empathy and emotional intelligence: Human arbitrators possess the ability to interpret complex human dynamics, build rapport with parties, and assess witness credibility through subtle cues such as tone, demeanour, and body language. This capacity for empathy and emotional intelligence is critical to understanding what is at stake for the parties and fostering trust in the arbitral process. While some AI systems are being developed to detect emotional cues and simulate empathetic responses using techniques such as language processing and sentiment analysis, these remain limited to pattern recognition. AI cannot genuinely experience or understand emotions; its responses are performative rather than experiential. Although such systems can convincingly signal attentiveness, and in some contexts even provide more consistent, patient, and unbiased interactions than humans, they lack the nuanced, context-dependent empathy that underpins human judgment. – Ethical reasoning: Arbitrators are often called upon to navigate difficult ethical situations and balance competing principles, using moral reasoning that an algorithm does not possess. This requires the exercise of moral reasoning, informed by professional codes, societal values, the obligation to promote the public interest, and the specifics of each case. By contrast, AI operates strictly within the parameters of its programming and training data. It lacks the capacity for independent ethical reflection or the ability to resolve novel moral conflicts. Unlike science and technology, which rely on precise rules, law and principles of justice are shaped by deep human values and require a holistic, context-sensitive judgment that cannot be reduced to code.24 M. Legg, ‘Better Than a Bot: Instilling Ethical Judgement into the Lawyers of the Future in the Age of AI’ 33(3) Griffith L. Rev. 281-282 (2024) https://doi.org/10.1080/10383441.2025.2493493. – Accountability: A defining feature of human arbitrators is their legal and ethical accountability. They can be held answerable for their decisions through appeals, challenges and regulatory or judicial oversight. They can be called upon to explain and justify their decisions. This accountability underpins the legitimacy of the arbitral process and provides recourse for parties who believe they have been treated unjustly. That said, some literature suggests that with the right design, machine systems can be made as accountable as human ones. Technical tools – such as audit trails, record-keeping, and post-hoc analysis – can allow for the reconstruction and review of algorithmic decisions, sometimes even more reliably than with human actors, who may not be able to fully articulate the reasons behind their judgments.25 Aziz Z Huq, ‘A Right to a Human Decision’ 106 Virginia L. Rev. (2020) 611, 640-646. Despite such advances in technical solutions, the ‘black box’ problem remains a significant barrier to true accountability. Many AI systems, particularly those using deep learning, rely on highly complex, non-intuitive processes that even their creators cannot fully interpret. Moreover, responsibility for machine decisions is spread across designers, programmers, deployers and users, making it harder to identify who is answerable when things go wrong. For the time being at least, genuine accountability, a core element of justice, remains uniquely tied to human decision-makers. These distinctions limit – at least in the short term – the roles AI can play in arbitration. The spectrum ranges from assistance, where AI automates routine tasks, and augmentation, where it provides analytical support to a human decision-maker, to replacement, where a core decision-making function is handed over to an AI. A recent qualitative study of UK judges found that while there is broad recognition of AI’s potential to improve efficiency, consistency, and access to justice – particularly for administrative tasks and legal research – judges remain deeply concerned about the reliability of AI-generated outputs, the risk of de-skilling, and the importance of preserving human judgment and oversight, especially in high-stakes or sensitive cases. The study highlights that judges value the uniquely human aspects of legal reasoning, empathy, and collaborative deliberation, and believe that AI should be designed to support, rather than replace, these core judicial functions.26 Erin Solovey, Brian Flanagan and Daniel Chen, Interacting with AI at Work: Perceptions and Opportunities from the UK Judiciary. In CHIWORK ‘25: Proceedings of the 4th Annual Symposium on Human-Computer Interaction for Work (CHIWORK ‘25), (Amsterdam, Netherlands. ACM, New York, NY, USA 23-25 June 2025). Similarly, the arbitration stakeholders appear closely aligned against the final step. The emerging consensus, found in guidelines from bodies like the Silicon Valley Arbitration & Mediation Center (SVAMC),27 The tech-focused set of principles published by the Silicon Valley Arbitration & Mediation Center (SVAMC) in April 2024, specifically Guideline 6. Chartered Institute of Arbitrators (CIArb)28 The CIArb Guideline on the Use of AI in Arbitration, published in April 2025, specifically Part IV, sections 8 and 9. and others,29 Other guidelines include the SCC Guide to the Use of AI in Cases Administered under the SCC Rules in October 2024, and the Vienna International Arbitral Centre's Note on the use of AI in arbitral proceedings in April 2025. is that an arbitrator’s personal mandate to decide a case cannot be delegated to an AI tool. This principle that humans must remain in control is reinforced by regulations like the EU AI Act,30 The EU AI Act came into force on 1 August 2024. which requires effective human oversight for high-risk AI systems to mitigate associated risks. The EU AI Act goes further by requiring AI providers to deliver their systems in a way that keeps users aware of potential pitfalls, such as automation bias,31 J. Laux and H. Ruschemeier, ‘Automation Bias in the AI Act: On the Legal Implications of Attempting to De-Bias Human Oversight of AI.’ European Journal of Risk Regulation 1-16 Published online (2025). Available at: https://www.cambridge.org/core/services/aop-cambridge-core/content/view/C97C85015056C09326944DE55CBC4D2C/S1867299X25100330a.pdf/automation_bias_in_the_ai_act_on_the_legal_implications_of_attempting_to_debias_human_oversight_of_ai.pdf. the known human tendency to over-rely on machine-generated answers. English law mandates that arbitration awards be the product of human judgment. The Arbitration Act 2025 imposes a duty on tribunals to act fairly and impartially, ensuring each party has a reasonable opportunity to present their case.32 These duties are set out in section 33(1)(a) of the Arbitration Act 1996 and were reaffirmed and expanded in the Arbitration Act 2025, which introduced a new duty of disclosure under section 23A. This duty reflects the principles of natural justice and underscores the importance of human discretion in assessing evidence, reasoning decisions, and maintaining procedural fairness. Judicial supervision under sections 68 and 69 of the Arbitration Act 2025 further reinforces that awards must be reasoned and the product of genuine deliberation, not automation. The principle that an arbitrator’s judgment must remain a human function is already being tested in court. In LaPaglia v. Valve Corp.,33 John LaPaglia v. Valve Corporation, Petition to Vacate Arbitration Award, Case No 3:25-cv-00833-RBM-DDL (US Dist Ct, SD Cal, 8 April 2025). a case recently filed in a US federal court, the claimant seeks to vacate an arbitral award on the grounds that the arbitrator allegedly relied on AI to such an extent that he ‘outsourced his adjudicative role’. The petition alleges that the arbitrator used generative AI to draft the award without proper oversight, resulting in factual assertions not supported by the record and stylistic features indicative of machine-generated text. While at the time of writing, the outcome remains pending, the case highlights the legal and ethical boundaries of AI use in arbitration. It serves as a timely example of how courts may begin to grapple with the implications of AI-assisted decision-making and where the line between analytical support and impermissible delegation must be drawn. In many ways, this principle is not unique to AI. Similar ethical concerns and restrictions arise when arbitrators delegate substantive tasks to tribunal secretaries. For example, the LCIA only permits secretaries to prepare ‘first drafts of awards or sections of awards’,34 LCIA 2017 Notes for Arbitrators, section 71.c. while the ICC restricts this to ‘factual portions of an award, such as the summary of the proceedings, the chronology of facts, and the summary of the parties’ positions’.35 CC 2019 ‘Note to Parties and Arbitral Tribunals on the Conduct of the Arbitration under the ICC Rules of Arbitration’, section 185. In both cases, such assistance is permissible only under the tribunal’s proper direction and supervision. The analogy is clear: whether the assistance comes from a human or AI, the critical issue is not delegation itself but ensuring that the arbitrator retains ultimate control and exercises adequate oversight. [B] Accountability and Professional Responsibility The opaque nature of many advanced AI systems creates a challenge: when a mistake occurs, it is difficult to assign responsibility. The simple response to this challenge has been to establish a clear and simple principle: the human user is always held fully responsible for any reliance on the AI’s output. Judicial guidance and court decisions in England and Wales have been uncompromising on this point, confirming that legal professionals are personally accountable for all material produced in their name. This applies to both legal representatives, who have an obligation to ensure the material they put before a court/tribunal is accurate and appropriate, as well as judicial office holders, who are personally responsible for material, i.e., orders and judgments, which is produced in their name.36 These professional duties are noted in the Guidance for Judicial Office Holders on AI, published on 12 December 2023, at p. 5. Reliance on an AI tool does not in any way reduce their professional duties of competence, diligence, and honesty. This principle of ultimate human responsibility has direct consequences for professional liability. The uncritical use of generative AI creates substantial risks of being sued for malpractice, whether for submitting flawed legal arguments containing fabricated cases or for breaching client confidentiality by entering sensitive data into public AI platforms.37 Daniel E. Tranen, ‘Imagining Lawyer Malpractice in the Age of Artificial Intelligence’, XV(226) Nat. L. Rev. (5 June 2025). Available at: https://natlawreview.com/article/imagining-coverage-litigation-age-artificial-intelligence. The risk is now so recognised that some professional indemnity insurers are introducing specific policy exclusions for liabilities arising from the use of generative AI.38 As of May 2025, insurers are taking divergent approaches to AI-related risk. Start-up insurer Armilla, in partnership with Lloyd’s, as well as Munich Re have introduced dedicated AI insurance products offering affirmative coverage for certain AI exposures. Meanwhile, other insurers have adopted broad exclusionary language. For example, Berkley’s new ‘absolute’ AI exclusion purports to exclude coverage for any actual or alleged use, deployment, or development of artificial intelligence (Geoffrey B. Fehling et al., ‘The Continued Proliferation of AI Exclusions,’ Hunton Insurance Recovery Blog (28 May 2025)). These new risks require a rethinking of the professional duty of competence. This duty now includes technological literacy. Professional conduct rules require lawyers to stay informed about the benefits and risks of relevant technology.39 In the US, the American Bar Association’s Model Rule 1.1 explicitly include technological competence as part of a lawyer’s duty. In the UK, the SRA Code of Conduct requires solicitors to deliver competent, informed, and accountable service – even when using technology like AI. Law Society guidance further stresses transparency, oversight, and retaining professional judgment when deploying technology in legal services. In practice, this means having a sufficient understanding of an AI tool’s capabilities and limitations to use it responsibly. Crucially, it also imposes a duty to treat all AI-generated content with scepticism, as if it were a first draft from an unreliable source that requires rigorous, independent verification before it can be used.

IV The Critical Path: Education [A] Regulatory Framework Development The global regulatory landscape for AI is fragmented and rapidly evolving, creating significant compliance challenges for international arbitration practitioners. There is currently no uniform international approach; instead, key jurisdictions are pursuing distinct and sometimes conflicting models. The European Union has adopted the EU AI Act, a comprehensive, risk-based legal framework that is the first of its kind. It classifies AI systems based on their potential for harm, designating many legal applications as ‘high-risk’ and subjecting them to strict, legally binding obligations regarding data quality, human oversight, and documentation. From August 2026, this legislation will apply to arbitrations with an EU nexus.40 See EU AI Act 2025, Art. 6(2), Recital 61 and Art. 113. In contrast, the United Kingdom has opted for a more flexible, principles-based model that avoids new legislation, empowering existing sectoral regulators to interpret and apply five core principles – Safety, Transparency, Fairness, Accountability, and Contestability – within their specific domains. Similarly, Singapore’s Model AI Governance Framework offers a practical, voluntary set of guidelines focused on ethical AI deployment, emphasising transparency, fairness, and data responsibility, designed to help organisations operationalise responsible AI use across sectors. The United States currently lacks a comprehensive federal law, relying on a decentralised patchwork of state laws and sector-specific rules. This divergence means that firms operating internationally must manage significant regulatory differences.41 Pouya Kashefi, Yasaman Kashefi and AmirHossein Ghafouri Mirsaraei, ‘Shaping the Future of AI: Balancing Innovation and Ethics in Global Regulation’, 29(3) Uniform L. Rev. 524-548 (August 2024) https://doi.org/10.1093/ulr/unae040. Available at: https://academic.oup.com/ulr/article/29/3/524/7904690. This environment highlights a tension between proactive and reactive regulatory approaches. The EU AI Act is an example of a proactive model, seeking to establish a comprehensive set of rules before the technology is fully embedded. The UK’s approach is deliberately more adaptive and iterative, avoiding what it considers potentially cumbersome rules that could hold back innovation and reduce the ability to respond quickly to future advances. At the same time, a reactive pattern is emerging from the courts in response to early misuse of AI. High-profile incidents where lawyers have submitted court filings containing ‘hallucinated’ case citations have prompted some courts to issue standing orders requiring attorneys to disclose their use of AI and certify the accuracy of their filings.42 For example, in 2023 Judge Michael Baylson of the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania gave a standing order which provided that any party that has used AI in the preparation court documentation must provide a factual statement that they have done so and certify that each citation to the law has been verified as accurate. Available at: https://www.paed.uscourts.gov/rules-orders/standing-order-re-artificial-intelligence-ai-cases-assigned-judge-baylson. This shows how specific failures can provoke a targeted regulatory response, creating an ad hoc layer of rules in the absence of a comprehensive framework. Beyond the above-mentioned regulatory models, a growing number of jurisdictions are taking steps to integrate GenAI into their legal systems. These governments are actively embedding the technology into judicial and legislative processes. For example, the United Arab Emirates recently became the first jurisdiction globally to deploy AI as a ‘co-legislator’ to draft, review, and amend legislation. This initiative, led by the UAE’s Office of Regulatory Intelligence, aims to increase legislative efficiency by up to 70%.43 Boodle Hatfield, AI & Lawmaking in the UAE: A Bold Step Forward (28 April 2025). Available at: https://www.boodlehatfield.com/insights/102k9k9/ai-lawmaking-in-the-uae-a-bold-step-forward. Brazil offers another compelling example. According to the National Council of Justice (CNJ), 62 Brazilian courts had implemented or were developing AI-based projects by the end of 2023, a 17% increase from the previous year.44 National Council of Justice, CNJ Starts Collecting Information on the Use of Artificial Intelligence by Courts (17 December 2023). Available at: https://www.cnj.jus.br/cnj-inicia-coleta-de-informacoes-sobre-uso-de-inteligencia-artificial-por-tribunais/. These initiatives focus on automating routine tasks such as document classification, case indexing, and process monitoring, helping to alleviate Brazil’s historically high litigation volumes. The Supreme Federal Court’s ‘Victor’ system, for example, streamlines the classification of extraordinary appeals, aiming to reduce clerical workload and accelerate case management. Other courts are leveraging AI to detect patterns in litigation, identify abusive practices, and promote more rational judicial administration.45 Finocchio and Ustra, Artificial Intelligence and the Brazilian Judiciary: Challenges and Opportunities in the Litigation Sphere (15 April 2025). Available at: https://www.fius.com.br/en/artificial-intelligence-and-the-brazilian-judiciary-challenges-and-opportunities-in-the-litigation-sphere/. Given these international coordination challenges, regulatory gaps and divergent national approaches, ‘soft law’ and guidance developed by arbitral institutions have become critically important for setting standards. Rather than overriding national rules, these bodies are creating a form of industry-wide consensus on best practices and a coherent set of expectations where legislation may be inconsistent or silent. The CIArb Guideline (2025), the SVAMC Guidelines (2024) and the SCC Arbitration Institute Guide (2024) are examples, providing a harmonised set of principles for practitioners. These model frameworks converge on several core tenets: the ultimate accountability of the human user for all outputs; the need for transparency where appropriate; the professional duty of competence, which now includes understanding the capabilities and limitations of AI tools; and, most fundamentally, the non-delegation of an arbitrator’s core decision-making mandate. [B] Educational Revolution: Reimagining Legal Training To equip legal professionals in the digital era, professional education must be radically transformed. Law schools and continuing education programmes are already responding by incorporating AI content and practical labs into their curriculums.46 For example, the University of Oxford offers the ‘AI University Foundations’ module, a 10‑week online programme for legal professionals covering the computer science, legal, and ethical foundations of AI, including global regulation and the EU AI Act. Brochure available at: https://www.oltep.ox.ac.uk/courses/ai-university-foundations (accessed 24 September 2025). Law firms, client organisations and arbitral institutions must also find ways to upskill and reskill their workforces at pace. By combining the use of embedded AI coaches, dedicated communities of practice, and AI-driven personalised learning, the legal profession can shift from abstract, theoretical instruction to continuous, contextual, and collaborative learning embedded in real work. The primary way professionals learn new skills on the job is by seeking out colleagues who can share their informal learning experiences, role model positive behaviours and provide guidance and advice.47 John Cunningham and Emilie Hillier, ‘Informal Learning in the Workplace: Key Activities and Processes,’ 55(1) Education + Training 37-51 (February 2013) https://doi.org/10.1108/00400911311294960. Available at: 7/Informal-learning-in-the-workplace-key-activities?redirectedFrom=fulltext. Legal organisations can encourage this form of peer learning through empowering early adopters to act as AI coaches or champions.48 Rhodes, Nathan, 7 Ways to Ensure AI Adoption at Your Organisation (techUK, 16 May 2025). Available at: https://www.techuk.org/resource/7-ways-to-ensure-ai-adoption-at-your-organisation.html. These are individuals within teams who can support, model, and socialise effective AI use across the organisation. To effectively support arbitration practitioners, these coaches need to understand the legal context while being comfortable exploring how AI can be applied to everyday workflows, such as case management, document review, and evidentiary analysis. The role of an AI coach is not to answer technical questions, but rather to coach colleagues in the hybrid skill set essential for responsible adoption. Arbitral organisations should consider establishing networks of arbitration-focused AI coaches who are supported and incentivised to promote AI fluency across teams. These coaches can play a vital role in reinforcing good AI practices and supporting culture change.49 J.P. Gownder, Ground Your Workforce AI Strategy in Human Experience (Forrester Report, 6 March 2025). Available at: https://reprint.forrester.com/reports/ground-your-workforce-ai-strategy-in-human-experience-d161c8a9/index.html. Another way to enable peer-to-peer learning is through communities of practice that foster experimentation, shared learning and psychological safety. For example, an arbitration team might form a working group to test AI tools for document summarisation, share feedback, and refine workflows. These communities create shared cultural norms that support continuous learning, allowing members to explore new tools without reputational risk and to co-create best practices. They can also enable cross-functional collaboration, bringing together legal professionals, technologists and other specialists, to collectively explore and shape how AI can best be applied in practice. This enables applied learning for professionals and strengthens adoption. Formal training and communications are also essential,50 Noah Waisberg and Dr Alexander Hudek, AI For Lawyers: How Artificial Intelligence Is Adding Value, Amplifying Expertise and Transforming Careers (Wiley), 226. including the sharing of success stories and practical-high impact use cases.51 Oliver Roberts, Driving AI Adoption at Top Law Firms: Candid Insights from Innovation Leaders (National Law Review, 31 March 2025). Available at:https://natlawreview.com/article/driving-ai-adoption-top-law-firms-candid-insights-innovation-leaders. AI-driven learning can be leveraged to make learning more personalised, efficient and embedded in daily work. AI-powered platforms can tailor development to individual roles and workflows, and surface nudges, short reflections, or scenario-based prompts in real time, helping to embed new behaviours directly into the flow of work.52 Josh Bersin, A New Paradigm for Corporate Training: Learning in the Flow of Work. Deloitte Insight (2018). Available at: https://joshbersin.com/2018/06/a-new-paradigm-for-corporate-training-learning-in-the-flow-of-work. Conversational AI assistants extend this capability by offering organisation-specific guidance, modelling responsible use, and providing instant, context-specific feedback.53 Wayne Holmes, Maya Bialik and Charles Fadel, Artificial Intelligence in Education: Promises and Implications for Teaching and Learning (Boston, MA: Center for Curriculum Redesign, 2019). Available at: https://curriculumredesign.org/wp-content/uploads/AIED-Book-Excerpt-CCR.pdf. For arbitration professionals, where credibility and judgment are central, this approach ensures that hybrid skills such as bias awareness, accuracy and reliability are not taught as abstract concepts but are reinforced in daily practice. Through combining the use of AI-driven learning with active networks of peer coaches and communities of practice, arbitration organisations can build a dynamic learning ecosystem – one that is adaptive, inclusive, and embedded in the flow of work. This is not just a shift in training; it’s a shift in mindset. To encourage adoption and responsible use of AI, organisations must invest not only in tools, but in the behavioural infrastructure that enables people to use them well.

V Evidence in the Digital Age: Rethinking Authenticity and Weight [A] The Challenge of Digital Evidence Proliferation Alongside questions of judgment, accountability and competence, evidence has emerged as a critical consideration – both reflecting and shaping how AI is integrating into legal practice and arbitration. The digital era has transformed arbitration by dramatically increasing the volume and velocity of evidence creation. From emails and messaging apps to video calls and cloud-based documents, the evidentiary landscape is expanding at an unprecedented rate. Some e-discovery platforms reported a 130% year-on-year growth in video content alone.54 JD Supra, Deepfakes in Legal Proceedings: A Strategic Framework for Collaborative Solutions (4 September 2024). Available at: https://www.jdsupra.com/legalnews/deepfakes-in-legal-proceedings-a-9816082/. Human review is no longer feasible at scale – AI-powered tools are now essential for filtering, analysing, and synthesising vast datasets, enabling counsel and tribunals to focus on substance over logistics. This places the legal process in a technological arms race, where it must rely on advanced tools to address the evidentiary challenges that are themselves a byproduct of technological advancement. This shift also reframes the long-standing issue of equality of arms in arbitration. As AI tools become central to document review and evidence analysis, parties with access to advanced technologies may gain an advantage, not through legal argument but through superior evidentiary capabilities. To address this, tribunals may require disclosure of AI tools used during document production, promoting transparency, although this is decided on a case-by-case basis.55 Under the various arbitral institutions’ guidelines discussed above, disclosure of AI use is not necessary as a general matter but is mandated by the arbitrators on a case-by-case basis. Over time, however, AI may become a leveller. As tools grow more affordable and accessible, even resource-constrained parties may benefit. As early as 2018, a Canadian court noted that the use of AI tools could have significantly reduced counsel’s preparation time and, accordingly, reduced the adverse costs order.56 Cass v. 1410088 Ontario Inc., 2018 ONSC 6959. Such judicial recognition and encouragement suggest that AI, while currently seen as a disruptor of fairness, may ultimately help democratise arbitral processes. [B] Redefining Authenticity and Reliability While AI helps analyse evidence, it also introduces new threats to evidentiary integrity. Generative AI can produce hyper-realistic but entirely fabricated content – deepfakes – that challenge foundational assumptions about authenticity in legal proceedings. Synthetic audio, video and images can be weaponised to create false evidence, discredit witnesses or support baseless claims. Also troubling is the erosion of trust in authentic evidence. The mere possibility of deepfakes has enabled parties to cast doubt on legitimate media. In some cases, litigants have claimed that compromising footage ‘might’ be fake. These claims were dismissed only because the content could be verified as real.57 For example, in a criminal case in Pennsylvania, when a prosecutor claimed that a video was a fake that was created to victimize a teen cheerleader, the defendant used expert testimony to prove it was authentic: J-S18007-23 2023 PA Super 238 Commonwealth of Pennsylvania v. Spone. This phenomenon is often referred to as the ‘Liars Dividend’, a concept introduced by legal scholars to highlight how the rise of convincing digital forgeries is eroding trust in authentic evidence.58 Rebecca Delfino, The Deepfake Defense: Exploring the Limits of the Law and Ethical Norms in Protecting Legal Proceedings from Lying Lawyers (12 February 2023). Loyola Law School, Los Angeles Legal Studies Research Paper No. 2023-02, 84.5 Ohio St. L.J. 1068 (2024), Available at: SSRN: https://ssrn.com/abstract=4355140 or http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.4355140. Such arguments point to a future where manipulation may be harder to detect and harder to refute. Traditional authentication methods, such as metadata analysis, are no longer sufficient. New frameworks are needed to assess AI-processed and AI-generated evidence. The burden may shift toward requiring stronger corroboration, especially for digital media.59 John P. LaMonaga, ‘A Break from Reality: Modernizing Authentication Standards for Digital Video Evidence in the Era of Deepfakes,’ 69(6) Am. Univ. L. Rev. (2020), Art. 5. Available at: https://digitalcommons.wcl.american.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2221&context=aulr. Technologies like blockchain, hash verification and digital watermarking offer promising solutions for establishing provenance and integrity, but they are not yet widely adopted or standardised.60 Cuatrecasas, Unlocking Blockchain Evidence in International Arbitration (2022). [C] Procedural Adaptations To preserve fairness and transparency, arbitration must adapt procedurally to the realities of AI-enhanced evidence. These adaptations are not optional; they are essential to maintaining the integrity of the process. Tribunals may increasingly rely on pre-hearing evidentiary reviews to assess the admissibility of contested digital evidence. Where AI tools have been used to analyse or generate content, expert testimony may be required to establish reliability and origin. These hearings can help ensure that tribunals are not relying on compromised or unverifiable material. Case management orders will evolve to reflect these challenges, albeit a balance will need to be struck between party autonomy as to how it prosecutes/defends a claim versus the transparency required to ensure due process and fairness. Discovery procedures may also need to be updated to reflect the challenges of authenticating digital and synthetic media. Tribunals may require stringent corroboration or expert validation of file integrity. For example, under the CIArb Guideline (2025), arbitrators may give directions or make procedural rulings with regards to parties’ use of AI, including (i) appointing an ‘AI expert’; (ii) whether AI-generated or AI-assisted content should be admitted to the record; and (iii) in deciding whether such content should be admitted to the record, requesting that parties make submissions as to how any inputs based on source data from the evidentiary record are linked to the machine-generated outputs of the AI tool.61 Part III of the CIArb Guideline (2025). That said, guidelines on the use of AI in arbitration currently offer only broad principles on this point. For example, the SVAMC Guidelines (2024) prohibit falsifying evidence using AI, a rule already existing in general legal ethics, but leave the specifics of disclosure and verification to case-by-case discretion.62 Guidelines 3 and 5. More detailed procedural standards are likely to be developed to address risks posed by AI in arbitration and, in doing so, enable the safe deployment of these technologies at scale to improve the process of arbitration for the benefit of its users. A further concern is the technical competence of arbitrators. The 2023 BCLP Arbitration Survey found that most practitioners (79%) and arbitrators themselves (73%) rated arbitrators’ technical skills at 5 out of 10 or lower.63 BCLP Arbitration Survey 2023: AI in IA – The Rise of Machine Learning (Bryan Cave Leighton Paisner, 9 November 2023). This skills gap affects the tribunal’s ability to evaluate AI-assisted submissions and manage disputes involving complex digital issues. It suggests that technological literacy may become an increasingly relevant criterion in arbitrator selection and training. In the interim, tribunals may need to rely more heavily on expert input – a necessary but costly and time-consuming solution.

VI Two Futures: Evolution Versus Revolution Set against the risks, pressures and promises of AI in arbitration – and the legal field more broadly – two broad paths are emerging. The first is evolutionary: AI is used to streamline existing workflows, helping practitioners manage complexity, reduce costs and improve speed. In this model, arbitration remains fundamentally the same, with technology acting as a tool to assist rather than replace. The other path is more disruptive. In the revolutionary model, AI begins to take on more substantive roles: assessing claims, shaping strategy and potentially influencing or even generating decisions. [A] The Vision of Efficiency Gains: AI Improving Current Processes The evolutionary vision, which represents the current state and near-term trajectory of AI in arbitration, views technology as an assistive tool for incremental improvement. In this future, AI automates the more laborious aspects of arbitration – document review, legal research, contract analysis and transcription – making the process faster, cheaper and more accurate. The traditional structures of arbitration, including the roles of counsel and arbitrators, are maintained. The core principle is that AI assists with tasks, but strategic thinking and final decision-making authority remain exclusively human domains. This approach is lower risk, aligns with the conservative culture of the profession, and delivers clear, measurable efficiency gains. It supports cost reduction and procedural streamlining, particularly in complex cases with large volumes of evidence. However, its limitation lies in its scope. By focusing on refining existing processes, it may fail to unlock AI’s full transformative potential. [B] The Vision of Revolution: Fundamental Transformation The revolutionary vision imagines a future where AI fundamentally reshapes arbitration, not just by assisting, but by transforming the dispute resolution process as we know it (or indeed reducing the quantity and nature of disputes dramatically). This goes beyond automation: AI could analyse large volumes of case data to assess claims, predict likely outcomes and guide settlement strategies upfront. More radically, algorithmic dispute resolution mechanisms could adjudicate disputes directly, using either predefined rules or machine learning models trained on historical decisions.64 Jeremy Barnett and Philip Treleaven, ‘Algorithmic Dispute Resolution: The Automation of Professional Dispute Resolution Using AI and Blockchain Technologies’, 61(3) Comp. J. 399-408 (March 2018). These mechanisms are especially well-suited to low-value, high-volume cases such as consumer or small commercial claims where traditional arbitration is often too expensive or slow to be practical. AI platforms could deliver near-instant, low-cost decisions, making it economically viable to resolve disputes that currently go unaddressed.65 Iain Quirk KC, Re-imagining DR with AI (Counsel Magazine, 10 March 2025). We may also see the rise of virtual tribunals: fully digital environments that combine predictive analytics, natural language processing, and real-time data to manage disputes from start to finish. Such platforms would likely operate on license, subscription or pay-per-use models, replacing the traditional billable hour. In these models, lawyers shift from advocates to architects, designing, maintaining, and overseeing the systems that resolve disputes, rather than arguing individual cases. Together, these innovations could significantly expand access to justice. By reducing costs, simplifying procedures, and removing logistical barriers, AI-powered systems could open arbitration to individuals and small businesses who are currently unable to afford formal dispute resolution. [C] Drivers and Barriers Despite its promise, the revolutionary vision of AI in arbitration faces significant structural, cultural and legal barriers. Indeed, the future of AI in the legal industry, and in arbitration, will be shaped not only by technological potential but by the interplay of market forces, institutional culture, client expectations and regulatory frameworks. Individual and institutional resistance to change plays a significant role. The legal profession (including within in-house teams) is steeped in tradition, and its conservative culture tends to favour incremental change over disruption. Deep-rooted norms, concerns about fairness and discomfort with delegating judgment to machines all contribute to the pace of change. Emerging legal frameworks, including ethical guidelines and laws restricting automated decision-making, reflect and systematise a current consensus that certain aspects of legal reasoning must remain ‘human-only’. These safeguards are likely to become more formalised as AI capabilities advance. Client acceptance is another critical factor. While some users may welcome AI-driven tools for their speed and, especially, their cost-efficiency, others remain wary of automated justice. Trust, transparency and perceived legitimacy are essential, especially in high-stakes disputes. Market readiness varies widely across sectors and jurisdictions, and adoption will depend on whether AI systems can demonstrate reliability, fairness, and value. Other limitations, such as confidentiality and cybersecurity risks, or concerns about bias and accountability, also temper enthusiasm. Another real barrier is structural. The billable hour remains the dominant commercial model in legal practice, and full automation threatens its foundation by reducing the time-based value of legal work. This can create a disincentive for private practice to adopt technologies that could significantly cut hours, even if those technologies improve efficiency. The evolutionary model, which preserves traditional roles while streamlining tasks, is more commercially palatable and therefore more widely adopted. That said, the opportunities for those firms or lawyers which embrace the change and price for value rather than time spent stand significantly to benefit from this disruption, as do their clients. Yet despite these barriers, there are clear drivers of change. Rising caseloads, cost pressures and the need for faster resolution are pushing institutions and practitioners to explore new tools. Early adopters and market leaders will play a decisive role in shaping the trajectory. Tech-forward arbitral institutions, innovative lawyers and firms – together with tech providers – are already experimenting with how far AI-enhanced processes can be pushed. Their success will influence broader market confidence. Pilot programmes, sandbox initiatives, and cross-disciplinary collaborations can help build the evidence base needed to support responsible innovation. Ultimately, the future of AI in arbitration is not a binary choice between evolution and revolution, but a spectrum of possibilities. The degree of automation will vary depending on the nature of the dispute, the preferences of the parties, and the regulatory environment. The challenge is to strike the right balance, embracing innovation where it enhances efficiency and access, while preserving the human judgment that remains central to the legitimacy of legal decision-making.

VII A Path to Success [A] Core Competencies for the AI-Enhanced Practitioner Legal professionals need to extend their technical capabilities beyond being able to use AI tools to understanding how to apply them responsibly, effectively, and ethically. This requires a hybrid skillset at the intersection of law, technology, and human judgment. These hybrid competencies include the ability to critically assess AI outputs, verify them against authoritative sources, and recognise when errors, hallucinations, or gaps may arise. Practitioners must be able to evaluate the consistency and reliability of AI-generated insights across different contexts and apply them proportionally, with appropriate caveats. They also need to understand how algorithmic bias can shape legal analysis, evidence review, or procedural decisions – and how to mitigate its influence. Together, these skills position arbitration practitioners not as passive users of technology, but as active custodians of its responsible integration into arbitral practice. [B] The Art of AI Orchestration Legal professionals do not need to compete with AI; instead, they need to know how to orchestrate its capabilities. This is a strategic ability, which involves knowing what tasks to delegate, how to structure workflows, and where to intervene with human judgment. AI orchestration requires the ability to identify which parts of a case are suitable for AI assistance (e.g., document sorting, timeline construction, or case law research), and which require human-led analysis (including credibility assessments, legal reasoning, and discretionary judgement). It also involves embedding AI into legal workflows so that outputs feed seamlessly into case preparation, rather than existing isolated or as parallel processes. To develop this orchestration capability, legal professionals should pursue training that goes beyond surface-level AI overviews. Effective learning experiences should not only demystify core AI functionalities, such as natural language processing, machine learning workflows, and data structuring, but also cultivate the human skills that complement AI. This includes sharpening critical thinking, ethical reasoning, and the ability to interpret AI outputs within nuanced legal contexts. Learning experiences that blend legal scenarios with foundational computer science concepts and that offer hands-on experimentation with legal tech tools are especially valuable. These should include collaborative exercises where teams assess AI-generated outputs, debate their implications, and decide how to integrate them into case strategy. This form of experiential learning, reflection and peer review will help professionals learn to integrate AI insights with human judgment. When arbitration professionals master the art of orchestration, they unlock AI’s potential to transform how cases are prepared and how teams work – transforming the legal practice from manual processes into a strategically coordinated system of human and machine collaboration. [C] Serving Justice in the Digital Age The integration of AI into arbitration must ultimately be in service of justice. This requires a vigilant commitment to ensuring that technology enhances, rather than undermines, the principles of fairness and due process. The promise of AI lies in its potential to deliver outcomes with the speed and efficiency that users of the system increasingly expect, while also addressing the time and cost burdens that can render arbitration inaccessible or disproportionate. When guided by skilled practitioners, AI can automate labour-intensive tasks, such as reviewing vast volumes of documents or summarising complex evidence, that do not require core legal reasoning. By ‘taking the robot out of the lawyer’, these tools allow practitioners to focus on the high-value work essential to a just outcome: strategic thinking, creative problem-solving, and persuasive advocacy. This pursuit of efficiency, however, reinforces rather than diminishes the primacy of human judgment. The benefits of AI are only unlocked when its power is guided by human wisdom and accountability. It is the practitioner’s judgment that mitigates the risks of algorithmic bias and error, ensuring that technological tools are used in a manner consistent with the principles of fairness and due process. It is their judgment that transforms the raw analytical output of an AI into a coherent and compelling case strategy. In this model, the practitioner becomes a conductor of systems – verifying outputs, safeguarding professional values and ethical obligations, and ensuring that the integrity of the process remains intact. Far from being diminished, the AI-enhanced practitioner is empowered: better equipped to serve clients and uphold the rule of law in the digital age.

VIII Conclusion [A] Reaffirming the Role of Human Judgement The safe and productive integration of AI into the arbitration process requires us to recognise and reaffirm the role of human judgment in the process. While AI offers powerful tools to streamline legal processes, the risks of unreliable outputs and systemic bias demand that we not lose sight of the central role of human discretion and oversight. The challenge is not technological, it is behavioural and institutional, rooted in how AI is used, understood and regulated. The true threat to the integrity of the process is not AI itself, but rather its misuse, over-reliance and a lack of clear boundaries that would otherwise preserve the primacy of human intellect and empathy. The successful adoption of AI demands a redefinition of legal competence and a shift in professional culture that continually reaffirms the central role of human decision-makers. It demands the development of safeguards that ensure AI is used ethically and transparently. Ultimately, the path forward requires a firm belief that human judgment, especially in tasks that require empathy, discretion and accountability, remains the cornerstone of the process. [B] The Path Forward The future of AI in arbitration is not a binary choice between tradition and disruption. It is a spectrum of possibilities, shaped by the needs of users and the values of the arbitral community. Practitioners must become strategic conductors of AI, deploying it usefully for tasks like document review and case preparation while maintaining control over core legal functions that require human insight. Institutions must support this shift by investing in training and adapting procedural frameworks to meet new challenges. By approaching this integration thoughtfully, AI can become a powerful ally in the pursuit of more efficient and effective justice, while ensuring that human judgment remains the driving force behind fair and reasoned outcomes. [C] Final Reflection: Technology in the Service of Effective, Efficient and Enforceable Justice Arbitration is ultimately a human-centred process. While AI is already indispensable for modernising processes and improving efficiency, the greatest potential lies in the partnership between technology and human judgment. The future lies in a dynamic collaboration where AI supports, rather than supplants, human decision-making, enabling arbitrators to deliver fair, well-reasoned and timely decisions. This balance ensures that arbitration evolves without compromising its foundational principles.

title: Chapter 4: When Decentralised Justice Meets Artificial Intelligence: Law in the Age of Crowds and Code
editors: Fernanda Carvalho Dias de Oliveira Silva

sections.title: I When Blockchain Meets Arbitration: The Birth of Decentralised Justice
sections.text: I When Blockchain Meets Arbitration: The Birth of Decentralised Justice The digital economy introduces new ways for users to engage in financial and commercial transactions. For instance, imagine Alice hires Bob to create a promotional video. They agree on terms, but when Bob delivers, Alice is unhappy and claims he did not follow specifications. Bob insists he did. Such low-value disputes, often cross-border in nature, are typically not easy to solve through traditional courts or arbitration. These mechanisms tend to be costly, slow, and jurisdictionally complex, making them impractical for small claims that emerge in global digital marketplaces. Decentralised justice, first proposed by Ast and Lesaege,1 See generally, Federico Ast and Clément Lesaege, Kleros, a Protocol for a Decentralized Justice System: Building a Judicial System for the Internet Age, Medium (11 September 2017) https://medium.com/kleros/kleros-a-decentralized-justice-protocol-for-the-internet-38d596a6300d. is a novel dispute resolution approach that combines blockchain, game theory, and collective intelligence. When a dispute arises, it is resolved by a panel of randomly selected jurors, users who stake a cryptographic token to participate in the process. Jurors review the evidence and vote on the outcome, receiving rewards or penalties depending on whether their decision aligns with the majority. This mechanism creates strong incentives to rule honestly. In a seminal article published at the Stanford Journal of Blockchain Law & Policy, Ast and Deffains2 See generally, Federico Ast and Bruno Deffains, When Online Dispute Resolution Meets Blockchain: The Birth of Decentralized Justice, Stan. J. Blockchain L. & Pol’y (2021). defined decentralised justice by three core features: (1) a Decentralised Autonomous Organisation (DAO) structure, (2) a mechanism design based on cryptoeconomics, and (3) a perception of fairness in its decisions.3 A Decentralised Autonomous Organisation (DAO) is a blockchain-based governance system that operates through smart contracts rather than traditional management structures. Decisions are made collectively by token holders, who can propose and vote on actions such as funding, rule changes, or project development. This allows the organisation to function transparently and autonomously, without relying on centralised leadership. The concept first gained prominence in 2016 with ‘The DAO’, an early experiment on the Ethereum network that, despite its technical failure, inspired today’s thriving DAO ecosystem. The DAO structure ensures that the process runs exactly as programmed, with governance in the hands of the community (see Figure 4.1). The immutability of the blockchain guarantees that evidence, juror selection, and decisions cannot be tampered with, fostering trust and neutrality. The mechanism design relies on cryptoeconomic incentives (rather than moral expectations) to guide behaviour. Jurors vote and receive rewards or penalties based on their alignment with the collective outcome. Finally, the system must be perceived as fair by users, observers, and the intended beneficiaries of the process.4 Ibid., 247. Figure 4.1 Decentralised Autonomous Organisation Structure Source: Federico Ast and Bruno Deffains, When Online Dispute Resolution Meets Blockchain: The Birth of Decentralized Justice, Stan. J. Blockchain L. & Pol’y (2021). Features of decentralised justice as defined by Ast and Deffains (2021). While decentralised justice is still an emerging field of dispute resolution, it has already resolved a significant number of claims across various categories. Kleros seeks to be a general purpose decentralised justice platform.5 See generally, Clément Lesaege, Federico Ast and William George, Kleros Whitepaper v. 1.0.7 (September 2019), https://kleros.io/whitepaper.pdf. Data from the Kleros Cases6 Kleros Cases Dashboard, KlerosBoard, https://klerosboard.com/1/cases (accessed 28 October 2025). and Kleros v2 Cases7 Kleros v2 Cases Dashboard, Kleros, https://v2.kleros.builders/#/cases/display/1/desc/all (accessed 28 October 2025). dashboards show that, as of 28 October 2025, there have been cases arising from Identity and Verification Disputes (1,000+ cases),8 These disputes are related to the eligibility of profiles submitted to the Proof of Humanity (PoH) network. In order to register for PoH, a user must submit a photo and video of themself satisfying certain constraints that are used to detect duplicate submissions with the aim of limiting each user to at most one profile. Then such a registry can be used by applications that want to screen out certain forms of spam or bot attacks. Kleros has been used to judge disputes related to whether a profile submission satisfies the necessary requirements, particularly that it should not be from a user with an existing profile. See generally, Stuart James, Proof of Humanity: An Explainer, Kleros Blog (12 March 2021), https://blog.kleros.io/proof-of-humanity-an-explainer/. Curation and Registry Disputes (850+ cases),9 This notably includes disputes related to placing labels on smart contracts addresses that can be used to verify that a user of a smart contract is not interacting with a malicious imitation of a well-known smart contract. Submissions of such a smart contract label can be challenged by users who place a deposit, creating a Kleros case between the submitter and the challenger to determine if the label is approved. These labels are then displayed on various blockchain explorers, such as Etherscan, see generally, Coinbase, What Is Etherscan and How to Use It, Coinbase Learn, https://www.coinbase.com/learn/crypto-glossary/what-is-etherscan-and-how-to-use-it (accessed 29 October 2025). See section [2] below. Commercial Transactions (170+ cases),10 This category includes disputes arising from customer service on the Argentina-based cryptocurrency exchange Lemon related to chargebacks and transaction errors. In this setup, the company Lemon recognises the decisions of Kleros in disputes between them and their users while nonetheless not restricting the users’ right to take disputes to Small Claims Courts if Kleros rules in favour of the company. See generally, Federico Ast and Agustín Jamele, La justicia digital se expande en la Argentina: cómo vas a poder usarla, Forbes Argentina (13 June 2024), https://www.forbesargentina.com/innovacion/la-justicia-digital-expande-argentina-como-vas-poder-usarla-n54438. See section III below. Content and Information Disputes (160+ cases),11 This category includes a variety of disputes related to content moderation. For example, Telegram bots were created that allowed users to flag messages in a Telegram channel that they believed to be inappropriate, creating a Kleros dispute between the poster of the message and the user who flagged it. See generally, Federico Ast, A Decentralized Infrastructure for the Future of Social Media, Kleros Blog (6 September 2023), https://blog.kleros.io/decentralized-infrastructure-for-the-future-of-social-media/. Also included in this category are a number of disputes related to whether to community submissions to communications campaigns by various blockchain projects met minimum standards to be given a reward, see generally, Damjan Malbašić, Kleros Storytelling Reward Program, Kleros Blog (19 December 2019), https://blog.kleros.io/kleros-storytelling-reward-program/. Governance and Prediction Markets Disputes (30+ cases),12 Blockchains only natively have access to limited information, generally only including information from transactions that are issued on-chain and the computations involved in the consensus algorithm of the blockchain. Any other information about the external world must be provided in one or more transactions. Mechanisms designed to provide such information are known as blockchain ‘oracles’. Then, the disputes in this category arose through the Realitio tool, which is a blockchain oracle mechanism to which users can ask arbitrary questions about the external world. When there are disagreements between users on the answer to a question, these are resolved by Kleros jurors. See generally, Federico Ast, The Kleros-Realit.io Oracle, Kleros Blog (May 2019), https://blog.kleros.io/the-kleros-realit-io-oracle/. Notably, this tool has been used to provide information to DAO smart contracts related to how the community of that DAO voted through its off-chain governance process, see generally Guangmian Kung, Introducing the Kleros Snapshot Module, Kleros Blog (October 2024), https://blog.kleros.io/introducing-the-kleros-snapshot-module/ and to provide prediction market smart contracts information about whether the events the market considered ultimately were realised. and Pilot Programs for Government Integrations (14 cases)13 This category includes disputes arising from pilot programs with governmental agencies in various jurisdictions, including Lavalle Department in Mendoza, Argentina. See generally, Avanza la experiencia de Kleros en el departamento de Lavalle, Poder Judicial Mendoza (6 October 2025), https://jusmendoza.gob.ar/avanza-la-experiencia-de-kleros-en-el-departamento-de-lavalle/. In these cases, Kleros jurors judge questions arising from these communities such as whether a driver in a minor traffic accident should receive a fine, see Kleros v2 Court, Case # 111 ‘¿El hecho es una contravención?’, Kleros, https://v2.kleros.builders/#/cases/111/overview (accessed 25 November 2025), or whether a debt that a bank attempts to recover from a client is valid, see Kleros v2 Court, Case # 80 ‘¿Debe aceptarse el reclamo del demandante?’, Kleros, https://v2.kleros.builders/#/cases/80/overview (accessed 25 November 2025). This process acts as a non-binding, advisory tool for the public authorities tasked with resolving these disputes. on the Kleros platform. The UMA Optimistic Oracle platform is primarily aimed at providing blockchain applications with information about the real-world that might be required for smart contracts to execute; when there are disagreements about this information, decentralised justice principles are used to determine the correct information.14 See supra n. 12 for a discussion of blockchain oracles. Furthermore, see generally, Hart Lamburt, Introducing UMA’s Optimistic Oracle, Medium (12 May 2021), https://medium.com/uma-project/introducing-umas-optimistic-oracle-d92ce5d1a4bc. On the UMA platform, as of 28 October 2025, there have been cases15 Risk Labs, UMA OO Metrics Dashboard, Dune, http://dune.com/risk_labs/uma-oo-metrics (accessed 28 October 2025). related to Prediction Markets Disputes (1,250+ cases) and Content and Information Disputes (100+ cases).16 Content and Information Disputes include, notably, verification of information that is being ‘bridged’ from one blockchain to another. Namely, these disputes are part of an oracle mechanism that provides information about blockchain B for use in applications on blockchain A as blockchain A does not natively have access to this information, see supra n. 12. In recent years, the rapid advancement of AI has sparked growing interest in exploring how these tools can complement or streamline parts of the dispute resolution processes, both in traditional, offline settings as well as emerging dispute resolution settings such as decentralised justice. For example, these tools can be used for evidence classification, juror assistance, or even full automation in certain types of cases. Various authors have explored potential interactions between decentralised justice and AI tools,17 See generally, Amy J. Schmitz, Picking the Proper Problem-Solving Tool in Arbitration, in Transforming Arbitration (Maud Piers and Sean McCarthy eds, Radboud Univ. Press 2025). , 18 See generally, Nathalie M.-P. Potin, The Digital Revolution: Blockchain and Artificial Intelligence in International Arbitration, Rev. Confluence: Sci. & Humanités 5 (2024): 165-188, https://shs.cairn.info/journal-confluence-2024-1-page-165?lang=en. particularly related to how decentralised justice platforms can be complemented with Natural Language Processing tools,19 See generally, Alesia Zhuk, Enhancing Online Dispute Resolution Through Natural Language Processing: A Case Study of Kleros, Int’l Cybersecur. L. Rev. (2025) (at press), https://doi.org/10.1365/s43439-025-00161-5. explored potential interactions between AI tools and decentralised justice in the handling of intellectual property,20 See generally, Christos A. Makridis and Joshua D. Ammons, Governing the Large Language Model Commons: Using Digital Assets to Endow Intellectual Property Rights, J. Inst. Econ. 21 (2025): e19, https://doi.org/10.1017/S1744137425000165. and ethical considerations arising from these interactions.21 See generally, Benjamin Davies, Ethics in Artificial Intelligence and Alternative Dispute Resolution: Generating AI/Human Reviewed Ethical Guidelines for ADR Practitioners and the Legal Profession, U. Mass. L. Rev. 20 (2025): 149. This article explores the current state of research at the intersection of AI and decentralised justice. We analyse the use of AI for dispute preparation, examine how AI, decentralised justice, and traditional dispute resolution methods can coexist, review current research on the use of AI models as jurors, and envision what the flow of decentralised dispute resolution may look like in the future.22 The bibliographic review combined systematic and narrative approaches. Relevant literature was identified through searches in academic databases (including Google Scholar and SSRN) and specialised sources in law and technology. Priority was given to peer-reviewed articles, conference papers, and reports. The selection aimed to capture both academic research and practical developments relevant to the convergence of AI and decentralised justice.

sections.title: II AI in Dispute Preparation
sections.text: II AI in Dispute Preparation A first area where AI can collaborate with decentralised justice is in the onboarding of disputes. AI can help to streamline this stage by standardising how cases are submitted, simplifying the work for jurors, and supporting compliance with party and regulatory requirements, particularly with regard to the preservation of confidentiality. [A] Streamlining the Dispute Resolution Process One of the core value propositions of decentralised justice systems is their speed, affordability, and ability to deliver quick decisions. However, certain disputes involve extensive documentation: hundreds of pages of contracts, emails, logs, and other evidence. Without assistance, preparing such cases would be time-consuming and costly. AI can help by transforming complex, multi-document bundles into concise, neutral summaries that highlight only the most relevant elements. This process, however, presents challenges: if AI condenses a ten-page document into half a page, it must ensure that no critical evidence is lost and that the essential points are preserved for jurors. The goal is to prevent AI from ‘overstepping’ and effectively pre-determining the outcome. Dean and Ast23 See generally, Robert Dean and Federico Ast, Kleros Mediation Bridge: A Cohesive Approach Blending Traditional Mediation and Kleros Blockchain Arbitration, Kleros Blog (16 June 2023) https://blog.kleros.io/innovating-dispute-resolution-a-cohesive-approach-blending-traditional-mediation-and-kleros-blockchain-arbitration/. proposed the Kleros Mediation Bridge, a cohesive approach that blends traditional mediation with Kleros arbitration. Under this methodology, parties agree to a hybrid dispute resolution process in which they first engage in traditional mediation with the assistance of a human mediator. If the dispute remains unresolved, Kleros then intervenes to provide a transparent, efficient, and cost-effective resolution, with a final judgment delivered by a panel of jurors. In a follow-up article, Dean and Ast24 See generally, Robert Dean and Federico Ast, Harmony, the Kleros Mediator Bot, LexisNexis Legal News (30 January 2024), https://www.lexisnexis.co.uk/legal/news/harmony-the-kleros-mediator-bot. proposed Harmony, a bot based on GPT-4 designed to implement the principles of the Kleros Mediation Bridge.25 This tool can be accessed at: https://chat.openai.com/g/g-Bfl7BCw9e-harmony. The process involves both top-down and bottom-up steps. First, the AI thoroughly reviews all documents and establishes a clear factual chronology and narrative of the dispute. Each party is then given the opportunity to confirm that its position and arguments are accurately represented. This ensures that both sides’ pleadings are summarised as precisely and concisely as possible. Next, the AI reviews these party-approved summaries to identify the cause-and-effect chains underlying the dispute. The isolation process works as follows: the AI first maps the chronological sequence of events and identifies where the parties’ accounts diverge. It then examines each point of divergence to determine whether it can be resolved through objective verification (such as checking a timestamp or confirming a contractual term) or whether it requires subjective judgment about reasonableness, intent, or interpretation. For instance, in the Alice-Bob video dispute, the AI might identify that both parties agree Bob delivered the video on 15 March, but disagree on whether it met specifications. The AI would flag ‘compliance with specifications’ as requiring human judgment, while treating the delivery date as an established fact. The AI would then extract only the specific specification requirements from the contract and the relevant portions of the delivered video for juror review, filtering out tangential correspondence or unrelated project history. This approach highlights the essential points that truly require human judgment (such as whether creative work meets subjective quality standards or whether a party acted reasonably under the circumstances), ensuring that Kleros jurors receive only the most relevant information in an easily digestible format. The objective is not to allow AI to decide the outcome. Instead, AI assists in reducing unnecessary complexity and presenting a lean, fact-driven summary. Jurors can then apply their reasoning and judgment to reach a fair decision. If an unclear element arises (e.g., an unreadable date or a suspicious stamp on a document), the AI flags it for human review before finalising the evidence pack. This preserves the human element in the dispute resolution process. If a case is too complex to be decided in one step, the AI may recommend ‘chunking’ it into several smaller sub-disputes, each requiring its own set of documents. Through this modular approach, the dispute can be resolved step by step, ensuring that jurors remain focused and are able to deliver meaningful verdicts. Beyond simply organising evidence, AI offers advantages in maintaining procedural consistency throughout the preparation process. When human reviewers handle case preparation, parties must trust that these intermediaries won’t inadvertently or deliberately bias the presentation through selective emphasis or framing choices. However, AI neutrality should not be overstated. AI systems inevitably carry biases from their training data, design choices, and the objectives encoded by their developers. Research has repeatedly demonstrated that algorithmic systems can perpetuate or even amplify existing biases in legal contexts. The advantage AI offers is more limited but still valuable: procedural uniformity. An AI system instructed to extract factual information according to predetermined rules will apply those same rules to both parties’ evidence without favouring either side. Unlike human preparers who may unconsciously favour one party’s narrative or framing, AI maintains consistent treatment across submissions. This procedural uniformity is particularly crucial when restructuring evidence for ‘chunked disputes’, cases that are split into sequential sub-disputes for separate resolution (e.g., first determining liability, then separately determining damages). The AI can be instructed to extract only factually relevant information for each chunk without making evaluative judgments, a task that would require extraordinary discipline from human reviewers who have seen all evidence. By automating this process, the system reduces both costs and the trust burden on parties, as they can audit the AI’s instructions rather than having to trust in human impartiality. That said, parties and system designers must remain vigilant about AI bias. The pre-processing rules, summarisation algorithms, and classification systems must be carefully designed and regularly audited to ensure they do not systematically disadvantage certain types of claims, evidence formats, or argumentative styles. Transparency in how AI systems process evidence becomes essential for maintaining legitimacy. The Harmony implementation of the Kleros Mediation Bridge illustrates a simple and practical way in which a decentralised justice mechanism can be combined with AI to build hybrid dispute resolution processes. A practical example is Mirrorfall,26 Jay Buidl, Robert Dean and Federico Ast, Mirrorfall, https://mirrorfall.ai/ (accessed 24 November 2025). a proof of concept developed by Kleros to test this integration in intellectual property disputes. In Mirrorfall, different AI models conduct an initial analysis to detect potential copyright or trademark infringements by comparing two images when a user suspects that one may infringe upon the other.
