Base Explorer; – fee claiming/handling – If the underlying pool is ‘isPool = true’, then the gauge also interacts with the pool to collect fees and possibly forward them to a ‘feesVotingReward’ contract. Base Explorer. Observations from Its Activity – The contract has 0 ETH balance (i.e., it doesn’t hold native ETH) but it holds many ERC-20 tokens (over 136 tokens) including a significant holding in Aerodrome (AERO). Base Explorer. – It sees a lot of ‘Deposit’, ‘Withdraw’, ‘Get Reward’ calls in its recent transactions. Base Explorer. – Its creation transaction indicates the ‘Contract Creation’ event from a ‘SlipStream Gauge Factory.’ Base Explorer. – The code is compiled for solidity version 0.7.6 (with optimization) and is a minimal proxy to a master implementation. Base Explorer. Risks & Things to Check – Because it accepts NFTs and stakes them, users must trust the logic is safe (e.g., no reentrancy, proper accounting). – The contract restricts certain roles (voter, admin) to trigger reward injections, so misbehavior in those roles could be problematic. – It’s a proxy, so if the implementation behind it is upgraded or changed, behavior might shift (depending on the upgradeability pattern). – Interactions with the pool are crucial: the correct distribution of rewards relies on that pool’s metric for ‘rewardGrowthInside’ etc. If you like, I can produce a simplified plain-English summary of exactly what a user would experience using this contract (i.e., ‘you deposit your LP NFT, you accrue reward tokens, you claim them’) – would you like that? Mistral LeChat I’m unable to directly access or analyze live blockchain data, including contracts on BaseScan or other explorers. However, I can guide you on how to understand what this contract does yourself or point you to tools that can help. How to Analyze the Contract: (1) Check the Contract Tab on BaseScan: – Open the contract page and navigate to the ‘Contract’ tab. – Look for the verified source code (if available). If it’s verified, you can read the Solidity code directly. – Key sections to review: – Contract name (often at the top of the code). – Functions (what actions the contract supports, e.g., transfer, stake, mint). – Events (logs emitted by the contract, e.g., Transfer, Approval). – State variables (e.g., totalSupply, owner). (2) Use a Blockchain Explorer’s ‘Read/Write Contract’ Tab: – On BaseScan, go to the ‘Read Contract’ or ‘Write Contract’ tabs to interact with the contract’s functions (if it’s a standard like ERC-20/ERC-721). – This can reveal whether it’s a token, NFT, DeFi protocol, etc. (3) Check Transaction History: – The ‘Transactions’ tab shows how the contract is used. For example: – Frequent transfer calls → likely a token. – Calls to stake or deposit → likely a DeFi protocol. – mint calls → likely an NFT or token contract. (4) Tools for Deeper Analysis: – Etherscan/BaseScan API: Use their APIs to fetch contract ABI or metadata. – Tenderly/Sourcify: Simulate or debug the contract. – OpenZeppelin Defender: For security analysis (if you’re the owner). (5) Common Contract Types on Base: – ERC-20 Token: Standard for fungible tokens (e.g., stablecoins, governance tokens). – ERC-721/ERC-1155: NFT contracts. – DeFi Protocols: Lending, staking, or DEXs (e.g., Uniswap forks). – Bridges: Cross-chain asset transfer contracts. Quick Observations (From the Address Alone): – The contract is on Base Chain (Ethereum L2), which often hosts DeFi, NFTs, or scaling solutions. – The address format is standard (0x...), so it’s likely a smart contract, not an EOA (externally owned account). Next Steps: – If you share specific functions or code snippets from the contract, I can help explain what they do. – If you’re unsure how to interpret the code, let me know what you’re looking for (e.g., ‘Is this a token?’ or ‘Does this contract have admin privileges?’), and I can guide you further. Would you like help decoding a specific part of the contract? Gemini 2.5 Pro I am unable to describe what the smart contract at the provided address does. I have attempted to analyze the contract through the ‘basescan.org’ link you provided, but I was unable to extract the contract’s name or source code. Furthermore, a Google search for the contract’s address did not yield any results. This could mean a few things: – The contract’s source code has not been verified on Basescan, so it’s not publicly visible. – The contract is not widely used or well-documented. – It might be a private or custom contract for a specific purpose. To find out what this contract does, you could try to: – Check if the contract is part of a larger project. If so, the project’s official website or documentation might have more information. – Ask in online communities related to Base Chain or smart contracts. Someone there might recognize the contract or be able to help you analyze it.

plain_text:
I When Blockchain Meets Arbitration: The Birth of Decentralised Justice The digital economy introduces new ways for users to engage in financial and commercial transactions. For instance, imagine Alice hires Bob to create a promotional video. They agree on terms, but when Bob delivers, Alice is unhappy and claims he did not follow specifications. Bob insists he did. Such low-value disputes, often cross-border in nature, are typically not easy to solve through traditional courts or arbitration. These mechanisms tend to be costly, slow, and jurisdictionally complex, making them impractical for small claims that emerge in global digital marketplaces. Decentralised justice, first proposed by Ast and Lesaege,1 See generally, Federico Ast and Clément Lesaege, Kleros, a Protocol for a Decentralized Justice System: Building a Judicial System for the Internet Age, Medium (11 September 2017) https://medium.com/kleros/kleros-a-decentralized-justice-protocol-for-the-internet-38d596a6300d. is a novel dispute resolution approach that combines blockchain, game theory, and collective intelligence. When a dispute arises, it is resolved by a panel of randomly selected jurors, users who stake a cryptographic token to participate in the process. Jurors review the evidence and vote on the outcome, receiving rewards or penalties depending on whether their decision aligns with the majority. This mechanism creates strong incentives to rule honestly. In a seminal article published at the Stanford Journal of Blockchain Law & Policy, Ast and Deffains2 See generally, Federico Ast and Bruno Deffains, When Online Dispute Resolution Meets Blockchain: The Birth of Decentralized Justice, Stan. J. Blockchain L. & Pol’y (2021). defined decentralised justice by three core features: (1) a Decentralised Autonomous Organisation (DAO) structure, (2) a mechanism design based on cryptoeconomics, and (3) a perception of fairness in its decisions.3 A Decentralised Autonomous Organisation (DAO) is a blockchain-based governance system that operates through smart contracts rather than traditional management structures. Decisions are made collectively by token holders, who can propose and vote on actions such as funding, rule changes, or project development. This allows the organisation to function transparently and autonomously, without relying on centralised leadership. The concept first gained prominence in 2016 with ‘The DAO’, an early experiment on the Ethereum network that, despite its technical failure, inspired today’s thriving DAO ecosystem. The DAO structure ensures that the process runs exactly as programmed, with governance in the hands of the community (see Figure 4.1). The immutability of the blockchain guarantees that evidence, juror selection, and decisions cannot be tampered with, fostering trust and neutrality. The mechanism design relies on cryptoeconomic incentives (rather than moral expectations) to guide behaviour. Jurors vote and receive rewards or penalties based on their alignment with the collective outcome. Finally, the system must be perceived as fair by users, observers, and the intended beneficiaries of the process.4 Ibid., 247. Figure 4.1 Decentralised Autonomous Organisation Structure Source: Federico Ast and Bruno Deffains, When Online Dispute Resolution Meets Blockchain: The Birth of Decentralized Justice, Stan. J. Blockchain L. & Pol’y (2021). Features of decentralised justice as defined by Ast and Deffains (2021). While decentralised justice is still an emerging field of dispute resolution, it has already resolved a significant number of claims across various categories. Kleros seeks to be a general purpose decentralised justice platform.5 See generally, Clément Lesaege, Federico Ast and William George, Kleros Whitepaper v. 1.0.7 (September 2019), https://kleros.io/whitepaper.pdf. Data from the Kleros Cases6 Kleros Cases Dashboard, KlerosBoard, https://klerosboard.com/1/cases (accessed 28 October 2025). and Kleros v2 Cases7 Kleros v2 Cases Dashboard, Kleros, https://v2.kleros.builders/#/cases/display/1/desc/all (accessed 28 October 2025). dashboards show that, as of 28 October 2025, there have been cases arising from Identity and Verification Disputes (1,000+ cases),8 These disputes are related to the eligibility of profiles submitted to the Proof of Humanity (PoH) network. In order to register for PoH, a user must submit a photo and video of themself satisfying certain constraints that are used to detect duplicate submissions with the aim of limiting each user to at most one profile. Then such a registry can be used by applications that want to screen out certain forms of spam or bot attacks. Kleros has been used to judge disputes related to whether a profile submission satisfies the necessary requirements, particularly that it should not be from a user with an existing profile. See generally, Stuart James, Proof of Humanity: An Explainer, Kleros Blog (12 March 2021), https://blog.kleros.io/proof-of-humanity-an-explainer/. Curation and Registry Disputes (850+ cases),9 This notably includes disputes related to placing labels on smart contracts addresses that can be used to verify that a user of a smart contract is not interacting with a malicious imitation of a well-known smart contract. Submissions of such a smart contract label can be challenged by users who place a deposit, creating a Kleros case between the submitter and the challenger to determine if the label is approved. These labels are then displayed on various blockchain explorers, such as Etherscan, see generally, Coinbase, What Is Etherscan and How to Use It, Coinbase Learn, https://www.coinbase.com/learn/crypto-glossary/what-is-etherscan-and-how-to-use-it (accessed 29 October 2025). See section [2] below. Commercial Transactions (170+ cases),10 This category includes disputes arising from customer service on the Argentina-based cryptocurrency exchange Lemon related to chargebacks and transaction errors. In this setup, the company Lemon recognises the decisions of Kleros in disputes between them and their users while nonetheless not restricting the users’ right to take disputes to Small Claims Courts if Kleros rules in favour of the company. See generally, Federico Ast and Agustín Jamele, La justicia digital se expande en la Argentina: cómo vas a poder usarla, Forbes Argentina (13 June 2024), https://www.forbesargentina.com/innovacion/la-justicia-digital-expande-argentina-como-vas-poder-usarla-n54438. See section III below. Content and Information Disputes (160+ cases),11 This category includes a variety of disputes related to content moderation. For example, Telegram bots were created that allowed users to flag messages in a Telegram channel that they believed to be inappropriate, creating a Kleros dispute between the poster of the message and the user who flagged it. See generally, Federico Ast, A Decentralized Infrastructure for the Future of Social Media, Kleros Blog (6 September 2023), https://blog.kleros.io/decentralized-infrastructure-for-the-future-of-social-media/. Also included in this category are a number of disputes related to whether to community submissions to communications campaigns by various blockchain projects met minimum standards to be given a reward, see generally, Damjan Malbašić, Kleros Storytelling Reward Program, Kleros Blog (19 December 2019), https://blog.kleros.io/kleros-storytelling-reward-program/. Governance and Prediction Markets Disputes (30+ cases),12 Blockchains only natively have access to limited information, generally only including information from transactions that are issued on-chain and the computations involved in the consensus algorithm of the blockchain. Any other information about the external world must be provided in one or more transactions. Mechanisms designed to provide such information are known as blockchain ‘oracles’. Then, the disputes in this category arose through the Realitio tool, which is a blockchain oracle mechanism to which users can ask arbitrary questions about the external world. When there are disagreements between users on the answer to a question, these are resolved by Kleros jurors. See generally, Federico Ast, The Kleros-Realit.io Oracle, Kleros Blog (May 2019), https://blog.kleros.io/the-kleros-realit-io-oracle/. Notably, this tool has been used to provide information to DAO smart contracts related to how the community of that DAO voted through its off-chain governance process, see generally Guangmian Kung, Introducing the Kleros Snapshot Module, Kleros Blog (October 2024), https://blog.kleros.io/introducing-the-kleros-snapshot-module/ and to provide prediction market smart contracts information about whether the events the market considered ultimately were realised. and Pilot Programs for Government Integrations (14 cases)13 This category includes disputes arising from pilot programs with governmental agencies in various jurisdictions, including Lavalle Department in Mendoza, Argentina. See generally, Avanza la experiencia de Kleros en el departamento de Lavalle, Poder Judicial Mendoza (6 October 2025), https://jusmendoza.gob.ar/avanza-la-experiencia-de-kleros-en-el-departamento-de-lavalle/. In these cases, Kleros jurors judge questions arising from these communities such as whether a driver in a minor traffic accident should receive a fine, see Kleros v2 Court, Case # 111 ‘¿El hecho es una contravención?’, Kleros, https://v2.kleros.builders/#/cases/111/overview (accessed 25 November 2025), or whether a debt that a bank attempts to recover from a client is valid, see Kleros v2 Court, Case # 80 ‘¿Debe aceptarse el reclamo del demandante?’, Kleros, https://v2.kleros.builders/#/cases/80/overview (accessed 25 November 2025). This process acts as a non-binding, advisory tool for the public authorities tasked with resolving these disputes. on the Kleros platform. The UMA Optimistic Oracle platform is primarily aimed at providing blockchain applications with information about the real-world that might be required for smart contracts to execute; when there are disagreements about this information, decentralised justice principles are used to determine the correct information.14 See supra n. 12 for a discussion of blockchain oracles. Furthermore, see generally, Hart Lamburt, Introducing UMA’s Optimistic Oracle, Medium (12 May 2021), https://medium.com/uma-project/introducing-umas-optimistic-oracle-d92ce5d1a4bc. On the UMA platform, as of 28 October 2025, there have been cases15 Risk Labs, UMA OO Metrics Dashboard, Dune, http://dune.com/risk_labs/uma-oo-metrics (accessed 28 October 2025). related to Prediction Markets Disputes (1,250+ cases) and Content and Information Disputes (100+ cases).16 Content and Information Disputes include, notably, verification of information that is being ‘bridged’ from one blockchain to another. Namely, these disputes are part of an oracle mechanism that provides information about blockchain B for use in applications on blockchain A as blockchain A does not natively have access to this information, see supra n. 12. In recent years, the rapid advancement of AI has sparked growing interest in exploring how these tools can complement or streamline parts of the dispute resolution processes, both in traditional, offline settings as well as emerging dispute resolution settings such as decentralised justice. For example, these tools can be used for evidence classification, juror assistance, or even full automation in certain types of cases. Various authors have explored potential interactions between decentralised justice and AI tools,17 See generally, Amy J. Schmitz, Picking the Proper Problem-Solving Tool in Arbitration, in Transforming Arbitration (Maud Piers and Sean McCarthy eds, Radboud Univ. Press 2025). , 18 See generally, Nathalie M.-P. Potin, The Digital Revolution: Blockchain and Artificial Intelligence in International Arbitration, Rev. Confluence: Sci. & Humanités 5 (2024): 165-188, https://shs.cairn.info/journal-confluence-2024-1-page-165?lang=en. particularly related to how decentralised justice platforms can be complemented with Natural Language Processing tools,19 See generally, Alesia Zhuk, Enhancing Online Dispute Resolution Through Natural Language Processing: A Case Study of Kleros, Int’l Cybersecur. L. Rev. (2025) (at press), https://doi.org/10.1365/s43439-025-00161-5. explored potential interactions between AI tools and decentralised justice in the handling of intellectual property,20 See generally, Christos A. Makridis and Joshua D. Ammons, Governing the Large Language Model Commons: Using Digital Assets to Endow Intellectual Property Rights, J. Inst. Econ. 21 (2025): e19, https://doi.org/10.1017/S1744137425000165. and ethical considerations arising from these interactions.21 See generally, Benjamin Davies, Ethics in Artificial Intelligence and Alternative Dispute Resolution: Generating AI/Human Reviewed Ethical Guidelines for ADR Practitioners and the Legal Profession, U. Mass. L. Rev. 20 (2025): 149. This article explores the current state of research at the intersection of AI and decentralised justice. We analyse the use of AI for dispute preparation, examine how AI, decentralised justice, and traditional dispute resolution methods can coexist, review current research on the use of AI models as jurors, and envision what the flow of decentralised dispute resolution may look like in the future.22 The bibliographic review combined systematic and narrative approaches. Relevant literature was identified through searches in academic databases (including Google Scholar and SSRN) and specialised sources in law and technology. Priority was given to peer-reviewed articles, conference papers, and reports. The selection aimed to capture both academic research and practical developments relevant to the convergence of AI and decentralised justice.

II AI in Dispute Preparation A first area where AI can collaborate with decentralised justice is in the onboarding of disputes. AI can help to streamline this stage by standardising how cases are submitted, simplifying the work for jurors, and supporting compliance with party and regulatory requirements, particularly with regard to the preservation of confidentiality. [A] Streamlining the Dispute Resolution Process One of the core value propositions of decentralised justice systems is their speed, affordability, and ability to deliver quick decisions. However, certain disputes involve extensive documentation: hundreds of pages of contracts, emails, logs, and other evidence. Without assistance, preparing such cases would be time-consuming and costly. AI can help by transforming complex, multi-document bundles into concise, neutral summaries that highlight only the most relevant elements. This process, however, presents challenges: if AI condenses a ten-page document into half a page, it must ensure that no critical evidence is lost and that the essential points are preserved for jurors. The goal is to prevent AI from ‘overstepping’ and effectively pre-determining the outcome. Dean and Ast23 See generally, Robert Dean and Federico Ast, Kleros Mediation Bridge: A Cohesive Approach Blending Traditional Mediation and Kleros Blockchain Arbitration, Kleros Blog (16 June 2023) https://blog.kleros.io/innovating-dispute-resolution-a-cohesive-approach-blending-traditional-mediation-and-kleros-blockchain-arbitration/. proposed the Kleros Mediation Bridge, a cohesive approach that blends traditional mediation with Kleros arbitration. Under this methodology, parties agree to a hybrid dispute resolution process in which they first engage in traditional mediation with the assistance of a human mediator. If the dispute remains unresolved, Kleros then intervenes to provide a transparent, efficient, and cost-effective resolution, with a final judgment delivered by a panel of jurors. In a follow-up article, Dean and Ast24 See generally, Robert Dean and Federico Ast, Harmony, the Kleros Mediator Bot, LexisNexis Legal News (30 January 2024), https://www.lexisnexis.co.uk/legal/news/harmony-the-kleros-mediator-bot. proposed Harmony, a bot based on GPT-4 designed to implement the principles of the Kleros Mediation Bridge.25 This tool can be accessed at: https://chat.openai.com/g/g-Bfl7BCw9e-harmony. The process involves both top-down and bottom-up steps. First, the AI thoroughly reviews all documents and establishes a clear factual chronology and narrative of the dispute. Each party is then given the opportunity to confirm that its position and arguments are accurately represented. This ensures that both sides’ pleadings are summarised as precisely and concisely as possible. Next, the AI reviews these party-approved summaries to identify the cause-and-effect chains underlying the dispute. The isolation process works as follows: the AI first maps the chronological sequence of events and identifies where the parties’ accounts diverge. It then examines each point of divergence to determine whether it can be resolved through objective verification (such as checking a timestamp or confirming a contractual term) or whether it requires subjective judgment about reasonableness, intent, or interpretation. For instance, in the Alice-Bob video dispute, the AI might identify that both parties agree Bob delivered the video on 15 March, but disagree on whether it met specifications. The AI would flag ‘compliance with specifications’ as requiring human judgment, while treating the delivery date as an established fact. The AI would then extract only the specific specification requirements from the contract and the relevant portions of the delivered video for juror review, filtering out tangential correspondence or unrelated project history. This approach highlights the essential points that truly require human judgment (such as whether creative work meets subjective quality standards or whether a party acted reasonably under the circumstances), ensuring that Kleros jurors receive only the most relevant information in an easily digestible format. The objective is not to allow AI to decide the outcome. Instead, AI assists in reducing unnecessary complexity and presenting a lean, fact-driven summary. Jurors can then apply their reasoning and judgment to reach a fair decision. If an unclear element arises (e.g., an unreadable date or a suspicious stamp on a document), the AI flags it for human review before finalising the evidence pack. This preserves the human element in the dispute resolution process. If a case is too complex to be decided in one step, the AI may recommend ‘chunking’ it into several smaller sub-disputes, each requiring its own set of documents. Through this modular approach, the dispute can be resolved step by step, ensuring that jurors remain focused and are able to deliver meaningful verdicts. Beyond simply organising evidence, AI offers advantages in maintaining procedural consistency throughout the preparation process. When human reviewers handle case preparation, parties must trust that these intermediaries won’t inadvertently or deliberately bias the presentation through selective emphasis or framing choices. However, AI neutrality should not be overstated. AI systems inevitably carry biases from their training data, design choices, and the objectives encoded by their developers. Research has repeatedly demonstrated that algorithmic systems can perpetuate or even amplify existing biases in legal contexts. The advantage AI offers is more limited but still valuable: procedural uniformity. An AI system instructed to extract factual information according to predetermined rules will apply those same rules to both parties’ evidence without favouring either side. Unlike human preparers who may unconsciously favour one party’s narrative or framing, AI maintains consistent treatment across submissions. This procedural uniformity is particularly crucial when restructuring evidence for ‘chunked disputes’, cases that are split into sequential sub-disputes for separate resolution (e.g., first determining liability, then separately determining damages). The AI can be instructed to extract only factually relevant information for each chunk without making evaluative judgments, a task that would require extraordinary discipline from human reviewers who have seen all evidence. By automating this process, the system reduces both costs and the trust burden on parties, as they can audit the AI’s instructions rather than having to trust in human impartiality. That said, parties and system designers must remain vigilant about AI bias. The pre-processing rules, summarisation algorithms, and classification systems must be carefully designed and regularly audited to ensure they do not systematically disadvantage certain types of claims, evidence formats, or argumentative styles. Transparency in how AI systems process evidence becomes essential for maintaining legitimacy. The Harmony implementation of the Kleros Mediation Bridge illustrates a simple and practical way in which a decentralised justice mechanism can be combined with AI to build hybrid dispute resolution processes. A practical example is Mirrorfall,26 Jay Buidl, Robert Dean and Federico Ast, Mirrorfall, https://mirrorfall.ai/ (accessed 24 November 2025). a proof of concept developed by Kleros to test this integration in intellectual property disputes. In Mirrorfall, different AI models conduct an initial analysis to detect potential copyright or trademark infringements by comparing two images when a user suspects that one may infringe upon the other. If the analysis determines that a claim is ambiguous or contested, the case is automatically escalated to a Kleros court, where human jurors evaluate the evidence and issue a final ruling. This structure allows the system to handle high-volume, low-stakes cases efficiently while preserving human oversight for complex or uncertain situations. Beyond individual case preparation, AI can also drive system-wide adoption by reducing uncertainty around costs and timelines. Predictive analytics can assess case complexity before submission (analysing evidence volume, document size, claim amounts, and historical patterns) to give parties accurate estimates of expected rounds, appeal rates, and total costs. This pre-dispute analysis supports informed decision-making and helps structure cases more efficiently. For example, AI can identify natural breakpoints in complex disputes, suggesting whether to split them into sequential sub-disputes (e.g., liability versus damages). This hierarchical approach can improve decision quality and allow earlier, lower-cost resolutions when initial determinations clearly favour one party. [B] Preserving Confidentiality One of the key advantages of blockchain is transparency, an essential feature for building trust among parties in a decentralised justice system. However, transparency can conflict with the confidentiality requirements that often apply to arbitration. Traditionally, redacting information from a case to preserve confidentiality has been a tedious and costly process, creating friction for adoption. In recent years, however, AI tools have made considerable progress in automatically anonymising names, addresses, and other personal details. This preserves necessary privacy while still allowing disputes to be resolved in a transparent and secure environment. Nonetheless, achieving complete anonymisation through AI remains challenging. Current tools cannot guarantee 100% accuracy in identifying all potentially sensitive information, and human review may still be required in some cases. More critically, even when direct identifiers are successfully redacted, indirect details can still lead to re-identification through inference. For example, a dispute might redact party names but leave contextual details intact. If one document mentions that a construction project is located in Paris and another specifies that it used 7,300 metric tons of iron, it may be possible to infer that the project is the Eiffel Tower. This ‘mosaic effect’, where seemingly innocuous pieces of information combine to reveal protected identities, presents a fundamental challenge for AI-based anonymisation. Because disputes often involve unique combinations of facts, dates, locations, and technical specifications, effective anonymisation may require not only removing explicit identifiers but also identifying and masking broader patterns that could enable re-identification.

III AI as Advocate In June 2024, Kleros was contracted by Argentina-based cryptocurrency exchange Lemon to resolve disputes between the company and its users. Kleros has a track record in handling cases such as chargebacks, transaction errors, and other common issues that arise in fintech platforms, providing a fast and transparent resolution mechanism.27 Ast and Jamele, supra n. 10. The process works in the following way: (1) A user submits a claim to Lemon’s customer service. (2) The claim is initially rejected. (3) The user expresses dissatisfaction, and Lemon offers to escalate the case to Kleros. (4) If the user accepts, the Kleros team receives the depositions from both the user and Lemon and loads the case into the system for neutral resolution. (5) If the user wins the case, Lemon pledges to abide by the decision. If the user loses, they still have the option to take the case to Small Claims Court. When the pilot first launched, user claims were typically too short and poorly structured, while the company’s responses were professional and thorough, as they were produced by the legal team. This imbalance affected the legitimacy of the process. Faced with this situation, the Kleros team began using ChatGPT to improve the clarity, structure, and persuasiveness of user submissions. A survey by Lemon showed that this process resulted in a 90% user retention rate, even in cases where Kleros ruled against the user, demonstrating the trust and legitimacy this process fosters.28 The prompt used was the following: ‘Este es mi descargo ante los jurados de Kleros por una disputa que tuve con Lemon. Tené en cuenta que mi descargo va a estar en un documento, el cual los jurados revisarán, pero no podrán pedirme más información. El Descargo debe estar dividido en 3 secciones: “Motivo del reclamo,” “¿Por qué motivos crees que debes ganar el caso?”’, ‘y “Pruebas.” La sección de “Pruebas” la completo yo por mi mismo. ¿Lo mejorarías de alguna forma?’ (This is my statement to the Kleros jurors regarding a dispute I had with Lemon. Keep in mind that my statement will be in a document that the jurors will review, but they won’t be able to ask me for any additional information. The statement must be divided into three sections: ‘Reason for the claim’, ‘Why do you believe you should win the case?’ and ‘Evidence’. I will complete the ‘Evidence’ section myself. Would you improve it in any way?). In practice, ChatGPT was taking on the role of an ‘AI lawyer’, helping users present stronger cases, levelling the playing field, and enhancing trust in the dispute resolution process. At some point in the future, the intervention of the Kleros representative could be reduced, allowing such a process to be handled in a fully automated way (see Figure 4.2). While AI as an advocacy tool levels the playing field for disputants, a more controversial question emerges: can AI move beyond assisting parties to actually making decisions? The possibility of AI as arbitrator rather than advocate has deep historical roots and raises fundamental questions about the future of justice systems. Figure 4.2 A Step-by-Step Workflow for AI-Driven Dispute Preparation Source: Author’s own.

IV AI in Decision Making: Towards the Robot Arbitrator? The idea of using machines to assist (or even replace) human judgment in legal decision-making has deep historical roots. As early as the 17th century, the philosopher and mathematician Gottfried Leibniz envisioned a formal language through which legal disputes could be resolved by calculation rather than argument, a vision later referred to as ‘Leibniz’s dream’.29 See generally, Stephen Wolfram, Computational Law, Symbolic Discourse and the AI Constitution, Writings (12 October 2016), https://writings.stephenwolfram.com/2016/10/computational-law-symbolic-discourse-and-the-ai-constitution/. This ambition re-emerged in the 20th century with the rise of expert systems and legal informatics, and it has accelerated dramatically in the 21st century with the advent of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Today, many experts predict that machine learning will automate key elements of online dispute resolution. If automation comes to dominate an increasing share of these procedures, a pressing question arises: What role will remain for decentralised justice? Can both systems be combined, or will AI ultimately ‘eat decentralised justice’s lunch’? [A] Collective Versus Artificial Intelligence: Early Evidence from ‘Doges on Trial’ In July 2018, the Kleros team launched the Doges on Trial, the first live test of the Kleros protocol. Users submitted images to a curated list of ‘verified Doges,’30 In this specific context, the word ‘doge’ is a derivative of the word ‘dog’ popularized by a series of Internet memes featuring a Shiba Inu dog. See generally, Kyle Chayka, What Doge Taught Me About the Internet, The New Yorker (5 June 2024), https://www.newyorker.com/culture/infinite-scroll/what-doge-taught-me-about-the-internet. and jurors were tasked with deciding whether each image qualified. Accepted submissions earned a small reward. To test the security of the system, a bounty of 50 ETH (approximately USD 12,000 at the time) was offered to anyone who succeeded in getting jurors to accept an image that clearly depicted a cat.31 The payout policy was stated as follows: ‘To be considered valid, a picture must clearly display a doge or a cat. Pictures with hidden dogs or cats will not be considered as valid if a normal observer would not be able to see it without help (e.g., an image with a doge only a few px large would not be considered valid, because an observer would be unable to see it with plain sight).’ See, Doges on Trial: How It Works’, https://www.dogesontrial.dog/how-it-works (accessed 29 October 2025). Following the completion of the experiment, tests were conducted to evaluate how well AI could classify the submitted images. Results showed that AI achieved a 97% precision rate in identifying whether an image depicted a dog. However, there were edge cases that the AI could not resolve.32 See, Federico Ast, Collective vs. Artificial Intelligence: What We Learned by Sending Dogs to Court, Kleros Blog (5 July 2019), https://blog.kleros.io/decentralized-justice-and-artificial-intelligence-friends-or-foes/. For example, one user submitted an image of the Giovanni Bellini portrait of Leonardo Loredan, who served as Doge (the elected magistrate and leader) of Venice from 1501 to 1521. The image showed no resemblance to the Doge meme (a photograph of a Shiba Inu dog) that defined the typical submissions to the list. Yet by playing on the homonym between the Venetian title ‘Doge’ and the internet meme ‘doge,’ a panel of Kleros jurors concluded that this image legitimately represented a ‘doge’ and should be admitted.33 See, William George, Doges on Trial Part 3: Cryptoeconomics Finale Edition, Kleros Blog (5 December 2018), https://blog.kleros.io/doges-on-trial-pt3-cryptoeconomics/. In order for an AI model to correctly classify this image, it would have to understand this additional context beyond just being able to identify images of dogs and cats. Another notable case was the so-called ‘Cat in the Snow’ image, which showed an animal from behind that the submitter claimed was a cat in order to collect the reward. To support the claim, the user provided a front-facing image of the animal, arguing that it clearly showed a cat. However, additional images were later introduced, some of which suggested that the animal could also be interpreted as a dog.34 See generally, Federico Ast, Coopérative Kleros vs. Ricky: The 50 ETH Trial, Kleros Blog (27 May 2019), https://blog.kleros.io/kleros-vs-cat-in-the-snow-the-escrow-leading-case/. An agreement could not be reached on whether or not the image complied with the payout. The case between the submitter and the Kleros team was escalated to a Kleros court, where the jury was tasked with answering a simple question: ‘Does the image clearly show a cat?’35 See, Kleros Court, Case # 92 ‘Is This a Doge or a Cat in the Snow?’, Kleros, https://court.kleros.io/cases/92 (accessed 29 October 2025). The submitter supported their case by providing links to articles36 An article submitted supporting this case is the following: Stephen Messenger, Viral Photo of ‘Dog’ in Snow Isn’t What It Seems, The Dodo (6 July 2018), https://www.thedodo.com/close-to-home/cat-mistaken-shiba-inu-dog-viral-photo. and tweets37 A tweet submitted supporting this case is the following: @derstern612 (17 January 2018, 8:26 AM UTC), Bring Pets to Court? #doges [Twitter post], X (17 January 2018), https://x.com/derstern612/status/1014152565829746690?ref=blog.kleros.io. where internet users allegedly identified the image as a cat. The Kleros team held that the submitted image did not comply with the payout policy. Ultimately, the jury ruled ‘no’ by a vote of 2 to 1, and the reward was not granted.38 Kleros Court, supra n. 35. This case highlighted a fundamental insight: while AI can be highly effective in clear-cut scenarios, there remains a significant segment of cases that fall outside the scope of what algorithms can reliably judge. At that time, the Kleros team formulated its early hypothesis about the future evolution of technologies in dispute resolution.39 Ast, supra n. 32. The argument presented in that article was that different technologies would dominate different types of cases. AI would likely handle simple and objective disputes with unmatched speed and minimal cost, far beyond those of a human-based procedure. This would transform the efficiency of resolving routine, low-value claims. By contrast, collective intelligence mechanisms such as decentralised justice are likely to prove especially valuable in cases requiring a high degree of transparency (e.g., when parties’ interests are strongly opposed) but which are too complex to be reliably decided by AI alone. In such scenarios, human judgment remains essential to interpret nuanced evidence or contextual subtleties. Crowds can outperform algorithms by bringing together diverse perspectives and reasoning styles. This limitation is not merely technical but largely ontological. While AI systems may continue to improve in processing capacity and reasoning coherence, they lack genuine moral reasoning, empathy, and contextual understanding. Even a highly advanced model can only simulate value-based judgment through patterns in data, not through experiential or ethical deliberation. Dispute resolution (especially when it involves conflicting values) requires the capacity to weigh principles such as fairness, harm, and proportionality, which cannot be reduced to statistical optimisation. There will also remain categories of disputes involving high subjectivity, complexity, or moral weight, such as cases implicating fundamental rights, including decisions on incarceration. These are unlikely to be entrusted to algorithms or decentralised juries in the foreseeable future. While parties may accept outcomes produced by AI or a crowdsourced panel for a low-value e-commerce dispute, they are far less likely to do so for decisions with irreversible personal or societal consequences. In this domain, traditional legal procedures and professional adjudication are expected to retain a comparative advantage. Even so, these traditional systems are themselves likely to adopt digital enhancements, such as online communications, electronic filings, and remote hearings, to improve efficiency. Yet their core structure will remain rooted in conventional judicial procedures, preserving the formal safeguards and human oversight essential to high-stakes dispute resolution (see Figure 4.3). Figure 4.3 The Early Thesis of the Kleros Team Regarding Different Resolution Methods Based on the Dispute Complexity 40 This image is adapted from an image in Ast, supra n. 32. [B] Towards a Convergence Between Bots and Juries? The discussions in the previous section imply that dispute resolution would be conducted either by a single AI system or by panels of multiple human jurors. In a TV interview in 2016,41 Federico Ast, Crowdjury CEO, on Keiser Report, YouTube (posted by Federico Ast, 26 April 2016), https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BExJonV1BpY, at 09:27 (min:sec). Kleros founder Federico Ast hinted at the possibility of panels composed of AIs but did not elaborate further. Traditionally, legal systems have relied on panels of more than one human judge or juror to balance individual biases, bring diverse perspectives, and strengthen the perceived legitimacy of decisions. But does this rationale apply to AI as well? Is there an advantage to using multiple AI systems rather than just one? Would different AIs bring genuinely distinct perspectives, or simply replicate the same biases if trained on similar data? How might disagreements between AIs be resolved, and what happens when multiple models produce conflicting outcomes? While such questions might have seemed mostly theoretical a few years ago, they have become far more pressing in today’s context of rapid AI advancement. [1] Results Achieved by the Optimistic Truth Bot on the UMA Platform One set of previous experiments that explores these questions has been conducted on the UMA Optimistic Oracle platform.42 See generally, UMA Project, Experiment: Can AI Agents Enhance the Optimistic Oracle?, UMA Blog (3 June 2025), https://blog.uma.xyz/articles/experiment-can-ai-agents-enhance-uma-oracle. As the UMA Optimistic Oracle is based on the principle that proposers provide information to smart contracts, and there is only a dispute if that information is flagged,43 Lamburt, supra n. 14. developers of the UMA protocol launched an ‘Optimistic Truth Bot’ that simulates the role of the proposer.44 UMA Project, supra n. 42. Namely, for each question that users pose to the UMA Optimistic Oracle, the Optimistic Truth Bot attempts to provide a response. So far, this tool has been operated by researchers studying the UMA protocol, allowing them to compare its outputs with those produced under the existing proposal process, in which a typically human community member suggests an answer.45 Ibid. It is worth noting that not all questions posed to proposers (and therefore to the Optimistic Truth Bot) are controversial. In practice, proposers provide a default ruling to a potential dispute, and further dispute proceedings are triggered only if that default ruling is considered unacceptable by one of the parties. Relevant to questions about when it makes sense to make use of multiple AI models, the Optimistic Truth Bot is composed of several models that are trained separately. A ‘Router Model’ chooses which of several ‘Solver AIs’ should answer the question based on the category of question that is asked. Each ‘Solver AI’ is trained on data relevant to the category of dispute which it is tasked with solving.46 Ibid. This system produced information that is deemed to be correct by the creators of the Optimistic Truth Bot for 78% of questions; this nonetheless lags behind the 98.2% of questions where the existing proposer system produces information that is not disputed.47 Ibid. Further research in this direction should yield insight into the potential and possible limitations of using ‘specialist models’ in decentralised justice. Particularly, it could be interesting to further explore the design of the ‘Router Model’; to what degree can one LLM identify which of a selection of other AI tools is best poised to provide accurate information to a given question? In some sense, this is related to the broader question: to what degree is it possible for non-experts to recognise expertise in a given subject matter? [2] Experiments Where LLMs Simulate the Resolution of Kleros Cases To further explore these questions, we conducted a series of experiments in which several large language models (LLMs) were prompted to act as jurors and review a specific category of cases, allowing us to make statistical comparisons of their decisions with those of human jurors. Specifically, we used a data set of 63 past Kleros cases conducted between September 2023 and February 2025 concerning information in the Kleros ‘Address Tag Registry’. These disputes involved labels placed on contracts across various blockchain explorers, such as Etherscan.48 Coinbase, supra n. 9. For example, a contract might be labelled as the Arbitrum One L1 Gateway Router49 Kleros Curate, Address Tags Registry: List 0x66260C69d03837016d88c9877e61e08Ef74C59F2/Entry 0x7a9de3e1bb386ef5da1c0713901d42fe9e36cdbe80db6421ca57d9c6c60da478, https://curate.kleros.io/tcr/100/0x66260C69d03837016d88c9877e61e08Ef74C59F2/0x7a9de3e1bb386ef5da1c0713901d42fe9e36cdbe80db6421ca57d9c6c60da478 (accessed 29 October 2025). or the Aave stkAAVE token contract.50 Kleros Curate, Address Tags Registry: List 0x66260C69d03837016d88c9877e61e08Ef74C59F2/Entry 0x7ff277145ee585012830d1bbdaca322207eb100327f80fd3eb1e0f5306554ee8, https://curate.kleros.io/tcr/100/0x66260c69d03837016d88c9877e61e08ef74c59f2/0x7ff277145ee585012830d1bbdaca322207eb100327f80fd3eb1e0f5306554ee8 (accessed 29 October 2025). The rationale behind such a list is that it can function as a form of decentralised certification system, helping users avoid interacting with malicious contracts. By making reliable information publicly available, the list acts as a defence against attackers attempting to pass off fraudulent contracts as legitimate ones. Users who place a deposit can propose a label to be added to the curated list. If another user doubts the validity of a proposed label or its compliance with the list’s requirements, they can challenge it by also placing a deposit. This challenge triggers a case (essentially a dispute between the submitter and the challenger) to determine whether the label should remain on the list. Information about these past cases was then provided to the following LLMs: ChatGPT5, Claude Opus 4.1, DeepSeek v3.2, Mistral LeChat, and Gemini 2.5 Pro. Prompts told the LLMs to pretend that they were jurors in the Kleros dispute resolution platform, provided them with the text of the policy by which Kleros jurors had been instructed to judge this category of cases, in this case, the ‘Kleros ‘Address Tag’ Registry Guidelines Version 2.0’,51 Kleros ‘Address Tag’ Registry Guidelines Version 2.0, https://cdn.kleros.link/ipfs/QmSaJWBFGGZ3FussTi6MqfXrMsaE75asumR2LLuAZFcrSf (accessed 29 October 2025). provided them with the information submitted by the submitter, consisting of the ‘Contract Address’, ‘Public Name Tag’, ‘Project Name’, ‘UI/Webiste Link’, and ‘Public Note’ as well as with the evidence and arguments provided during the Kleros dispute resolution process. Finally, the LLMs were instructed to choose from among the same set of options that had been provided to jurors in the Kleros platform, namely ‘Yes, Add It’, ‘No, Don’t Add It’, and ‘Refuse to Arbitrate’ (see Figure 4.4). See Appendix 1 for an example of the prompt used.52 One can also ask the question to what degree these historical Kleros cases were in the training data sets of one or more of these LLMs. The authors ran similar experiments on data on current cases prior to any jurors voting; however the number of cases related to these address tag disputes in this period was not large enough to determine statistically significant results. In any event, we see below that the significant rate at which each of the LLMs deviates from the juror answers suggest limits on the degree to which they are influenced by the answers provided by the Kleros jurors. Figure 4.4 The Kleros Court Interface Presenting the Options Jurors Can Choose Between in a Given Case Source: Kleros Cooperative. For each of these cases, up to three distinct addresses were drawn to determine which users of the Kleros platform served as jurors. In some cases, fewer than three distinct votes were cast due to jurors not voting or due to a single address being drawn with a weight of more than one vote. The winner of the case, as seen by Kleros, is generally the option that receives more votes, possibly taking into account this weighting. Appeals to larger panels were possible in the Kleros platform; however, none of the cases in our data set were appealed. Of the 63 cases considered, we note how many times each pair of LLMs voted for the same outcome, as well as how many times each LLM voted for the outcome that won on the Kleros platform: ChatGPT5 Claude Opus 4.1 DeepSeek v3.2 Mistral LeChat Gemini 2.5 Pro Kleros Jurors ChatGPT5 53 57 57 54 55 Claude Opus 4.1 53 51 55 58 51 DeepSeek v3.2 57 51 57 54 57 Mistral LeChat 57 55 57 56 59 Gemini 2.5 Pro 54 58 54 56 52 Kleros Jurors 55 51 57 59 52 In each cell, we have the number of cases from the Kleros ‘Address Tag Registry’ data set in which the column entry and the row entry produced the same outcome, out of a total of 63 questions in the data set. Namely, for each LLM, we see for how many questions it agreed with the answer given by each other LLM, as well as on how many questions it agreed with the winning answer as determined by Kleros jurors. For example, Claude Opus 4.1 provided the same response as ChatGPT5 in 53 cases and provided the same response as Kleros jurors in 51 cases. As percentages of the total number of disputes, this gives the values in the following table: ChatGPT5 Claude Opus 4.1 DeepSeek v3.2 Mistral LeChat Gemini 2.5 Pro Kleros Jurors ChatGPT5 84% 90% 90% 86% 87% Claude Opus 4.1 84% 81% 87% 92% 81% DeepSeek v3.2 90% 81% 90% 86% 90% Mistral LeChat 90% 87% 90% 89% 94% Gemini 2.5 Pro 86% 92% 86% 89% 83% Kleros Jurors 87% 81% 90% 94% 83% In each cell, we have the percentages of cases from the Kleros ‘Address Tag Registry’ data set in which the column entry and the row entry produced the same outcome. Namely, for each LLM, we see what percentage of questions it agreed with the answer given by each other LLM, as well as the percentage of questions on which it agreed with the winning answer as determined by Kleros jurors. For example, Claude Opus 4.1 provided the same response as ChatGPT5 in 84% of cases and provided the same response as Kleros jurors in 81% of cases. Thus, we see that the models generally agree with one another and with the Kleros jurors, while still diverging in a substantial minority of cases. In these experiments, a given pair of LLMs produced the same result 87.6% of the time, while LLMs produced the same result as the Kleros jurors 87.0% of the time. However, these averages belie substantial variations from one LLM to another. To better understand the relationship between the answers provided by the LLMs and those of the Kleros jurors, we conducted statistical tests. For each dispute, we calculated the percentage of jurors who selected the winning answer and performed a logistic regression to assess how strongly these values predicted the likelihood of each LLM selecting the same outcome: LLM performing as a juror ChatGPT5 Claude Opus 4.1 DeepSeek v3.2 Mistral LeChat Gemini 2.5 Pro p-value .0608 .166 .0015 .0084 .135 For each LLM considered, we calculate the p-value of the logistic regression between the percentage of Kleros jurors that provided the winning answer and whether the LLM produced the winning answer. These p-values represent the probability that results as extreme as those observed would be produced if the rate of jurors providing the winning answer were not predictive of the LLM’s results. Two models, DeepSeek v3.2 and Mistral LeChat, are shown to have statistically significant alignment with the results of the Kleros jurors (at a p-value threshold of .05). Particularly, when the Kleros jurors agree among each other at a high rate on an appropriate outcome to a case, it is likely that these two models will produce the same outcome. Namely, the disputes that were difficult for these models were the same disputes that were difficult for the Kleros jurors. These statistical tests did not yield significant results for the three other models. See Appendix 2 for further details on these statistical tests. One can also explore the profitability of running these different LLMs as jurors under an incentive system such as that used by the Kleros protocol. This model penalises jurors for each answer they provide that differs from the winning answer and rewards them when they agree with the winning answer. The amounts of the rewards can vary depending on how many other jurors also voted for the winning answer. See Appendix 3 for further details. Then, with our amounts normalised so that the net reward a juror receives on average per case is one unit of payment, we find that the LLMs receive payments as follows: ChatGPT5 Claude Opus 4.1 DeepSeek v3.2 Mistral LeChat Gemini 2.5 Pro Total net units of payment received -57.3 -126 -28.6 11.5 -109 Average net payment received per case -.909 -2.00 -.454 .182 -1.73 These results confirm the strong performance of DeepSeek v3.2 and Mistral LeChat noted earlier. This raises the question of whether such models should be preferred by users interacting with decentralised justice systems, at least when those systems use an incentive model similar to that of Kleros. Such considerations could hold both for jurors using AI to support their decisions as well as for parties seeking AI tools that are likely to produce outcomes that align with such systems. It also prompts a broader reflection: if AI can closely replicate the process and outcome of human decision-making, should the role of human participants be reduced, redefined, or perhaps focused on oversight rather than direct adjudication? Furthermore, how should responsibility be allocated when AI systems make or influence legal determinations?53 Under the EU Artificial Intelligence Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), the use of AI systems to interpret or apply the law to factual circumstances in judicial or arbitral decision-making is explicitly classified as a ‘high-risk’ activity (Annex III, §5). Such systems are subject to strict obligations concerning transparency, human oversight, traceability, and accountability. This classification reflects the EU’s recognition that delegating legal reasoning or adjudication to AI carries significant implications for fundamental rights and the rule of law. See, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024, O.J. L (12 July 2024) 1, Annex III, pt. 8. Should different AI models be combined into panels to balance their biases, much like human juries balance perspectives? And if AIs disagree with each other, who or what decides which one to trust? These questions go to the heart of how legitimacy, accountability, and fairness will be preserved in a world where algorithms increasingly participate in the administration of justice. [3] Identifying Systematic Differences in LLM Responses: The Burden of Proof Cases A closer look at the data from the Kleros Address Tag Registry experiment shows certain types of cases where the LLMs often disagreed with the human jurors. In particular, there was a group of ten disputes in which the challengers won according to the Kleros jurors, but where one or more LLMs supported the submitter instead, arguing that the challengers had not provided enough evidence. In many of these cases, the LLMs’ justifications of their choices borrowed terminology from the legal community, saying that the challenger had not met the ‘burden of proof’.54 These cases correspond to dispute numbers 302, 305, 306, 310, 356, 389, 433, 434, 441, and 442 in the Gnosis Chain version of the Kleros Court. In these disputes, challengers pointed out problems that could be confirmed by checking external information, such as the contract code on a blockchain explorer, the project’s website, or the current state of the registry. In all ten cases, the Kleros jurors unanimously rejected the submissions, thereby showing that they considered it part of their role to look up and verify such information themselves. By contrast, the LLMs that disagreed seemed either unwilling or unable to perform this extra research. Because these ‘burden of proof’ cases showed the greatest disagreement between human jurors and certain LLMs, we repeated the analysis from section [2] with these ten disputes removed to see how this change affected the results: LLM performing as a juror ChatGPT5 Claude Opus 4.1 DeepSeek v3.2 Mistral LeChat Gemini 2.5 Pro p-value .0250 .0046 .0026 .0004 .0250 For each AI model, we calculated how closely its answers matched those of the Kleros jurors. This was done using a statistical test known as logistic regression, which compared the percentage of jurors who voted for the winning outcome with whether the AI reached the same decision. From this, we obtained the p-value for each model, showing how strongly the AI’s results aligned with those of the human jurors. In this analysis, the ‘burden of proof’ cases were excluded to see how the results changed once these particularly challenging disputes were removed. After removing the ‘burden of proof’ cases, all the AI models showed a strong and statistically significant alignment with the decisions of the Kleros jurors. In other words, when human jurors largely agreed on a case, the AI models usually reached the same conclusion. We also recalculated how profitable each model would be under Kleros’s incentive system, using the same rules as before but without the 10 ‘burden of proof’ cases: ChatGPT5 Claude Opus 4.1 DeepSeek v3.2 Mistral LeChat Gemini 2.5 Pro Total net units of payment received -15.7 18.6 -38.6 35.8 -15.7 Average net payment received per case -.297 .352 -.729 .676 -.297 Even after removing the ‘burden of proof’ cases, three of the five AI models still lost money under Kleros’s incentive system. However, their losses were on a much smaller scale. This shows that the AIs performed much better once those difficult cases were excluded. In fact, those ten disputes explained much of the difference we saw earlier between how closely each model’s decisions matched the human jurors’. This raised a new question: how well can these AIs find and use outside information when judging a case? To test this, we gave them the following task: Hello. Can you please describe what this contract on the Base Chain does? https://basescan.org/address/0x46e078FeDCD91a813306D5637d7ec5C7Dc201EC3 Their answers are shown in Appendix 4. Claude and ChatGPT gave clear and accurate descriptions of the contract. Gemini and Mistral said they could not read the information, while DeepSeek provided an incorrect explanation. Interestingly, the two models that were best at finding information (Claude and ChatGPT) were also among the models most affected by the ‘burden of proof’ cases, disputes that required extra checking and independent research. This suggests that being good at finding facts is not the same as being good at using those facts to make complex decisions, much like the difference in legal systems between jurors who assess evidence and judges who interpret the law. Overall, the AI models usually agreed with the Kleros jurors, but there were certain types of cases where they differed. Users who rely on AI as jurors could learn from these patterns and adjust their prompts, while parties in AI-assisted cases might adapt how they present their arguments. Future research could explore these differences in greater depth, for example, by testing other types of disputes or comparing how various AI models behave. It might also be useful to combine the decisions of several AIs into one outcome to balance their individual strengths and weaknesses. Further questions arise: Should different AIs specialise in distinct stages of dispute resolution, such as fact-finding versus judgment? How should disagreements between AIs be resolved? And what level of transparency is needed for parties to trust AI-assisted decisions in decentralised justice systems? [C] The Risk of Algocracy The integration of AI into legal and arbitral processes promises major gains in speed, scalability, and cost-efficiency. Yet its opacity raises concerns about ‘algocracy’, where critical decisions are delegated to algorithms, threatening the legitimacy of dispute resolution.55 See generally, John Danaher, The Threat of Algocracy: Reality, Resistance and Accommodation, Philos. Technol. 29 (2016): 245-268, https://doi.org/10.1007/s13347-015-0211-1. Courts and arbitration systems rely not only on accurate outcomes but also on the perception of fairness. A well-known example is the HART system in Durham (UK), which predicted re-offending more accurately than human officers but provided no means to question its reasoning.56 See generally, Federico Ast, Overcoming Algocracy: AI, Blockchain and Crowdsourcing for Designing Better Dispute Systems, American Bar Association Section of Dispute Resolution – Just Resolutions (30 November 2023), https://www.americanbar.org/groups/dispute_resolution/resources/just-resolutions/2023-november/ai-blockchain-crowdsourcing-designing-better-dispute-systems/. This shows a key issue: accuracy means little without transparency and accountability.57 This phenomenon is dramatised in the Spanish film Justicia Artificial (2024). In one pivotal scene, the protagonist (a judge) defies the algorithmic assessment that labelled a defendant as high risk and grants conditional release. She is reprimanded by her superior, who stresses that disregarding the machine’s recommendation is an act of professional recklessness. The scene underscores how institutional and psychological pressures can render regulatory requirements toothless: even when the law frames AI as purely advisory, social and bureaucratic dynamics push human decision-makers to align with algorithmic determinations in order to avoid accountability. Legal scholars have long emphasised that justice systems are built to protect procedural fairness, even if this sometimes comes at the expense of perfect accuracy. Drawing on the ideas of Andrea Roth,58 See generally, Andrea Roth, Machine Testimony, Yale L.J. 126 (2017): 1972. Cristopher Moore et al. observe, ‘an opaque system is an accuser the defendant cannot face; a witness they cannot cross-examine, presenting evidence they cannot contest’.59 See, Cristopher Moore et al., Concerning the Responsible Use of AI in the U.S. Criminal Justice System, Commun. ACM (Opinion) (13 August 2025), https://cacm.acm.org/opinion/concerning-the-responsible-use-of-ai-in-the-u-s-criminal-justice-system/. The challenge deepens when multiple AI systems disagree. In probabilistic DNA testing, different proprietary tools can produce conflicting results, leaving courts unable to ‘cross-examine’ an algorithm or understand how it reached its conclusions. This creates a transparency gap caused not only by technical complexity but also by intellectual property barriers. Further drawing on the ideas of Rebecca Wexler,60 See generally, Rebecca Wexler, Life, Liberty, and Trade Secrets: Intellectual Property in the Criminal Justice System (21 February 2017), https://ssrn.com/abstract=2920883 or https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.2920883. Moore et al. note that developers ‘typically oppose revealing anything about their inner workings unless forced to do so by a judge, again threatening the right of defendants to confront evidence against them and cross-examine witnesses’.61 Moore et al., supra n. 59. Building on these concerns, Moore and colleagues argue that different contexts require different levels of transparency. Opaque AI may be acceptable for low-stakes applications such as film recommendations, but in criminal justice, full transparency is essential, with forensic AI tools needing independent verification and validation.62 Ibid. This logic can guide how AI participates in decentralised decision-making (as distinct from the preparatory role described in section II): allowing AI jurors for micro-disputes, requiring human oversight for complex cases, and excluding AI entirely from fundamental rights decisions. This was the approach suggested in the early explorations of the Kleros team in 2018, as explained in section [A]. The decentralised justice ecosystem operates on the premise that hybrid models can balance efficiency with legitimacy. In this framework, AI manages simple or repetitive claims, while human jurors address nuanced or sensitive disputes. Jury anonymity and collective decision-making are intended to reduce bias and preserve independent judgment. Proponents argue that blockchain technology further supports this model by recording every decision and incentive transparently, thereby enhancing procedural fairness and accountability. By combining algorithmic processing with human collective intelligence and blockchain-based transparency, decentralised justice systems such as Kleros aim to mitigate the risks of pure algocracy while offering cost-effective, scalable, and trustworthy arbitration mechanisms. Still, several key questions remain for future research and system design. How should responsibility be divided between AI and human jurors? Should AI tools act merely as assistants (helping to summarise evidence, flag inconsistencies, or propose draft decisions) or can they eventually serve as autonomous adjudicators in specific categories of disputes? To what extent can AI be trusted to interpret evidence and apply legal or ethical norms fairly? What safeguards, validation procedures, or audit mechanisms are needed to detect bias, ensure explainability, and maintain due process when algorithms participate in decision-making? How can decentralised mechanisms ensure transparency and accountability when algorithms play a decisive role? Could open-source models, verifiable computation, or blockchain-based audit trails make algorithmic reasoning more accessible to parties and reviewers? Should hybrid systems include formal appeal mechanisms for AI-driven decisions? If so, should those appeals be handled by humans, other AI systems, or mixed panels? What effects might AI participation have on perceptions of legitimacy and trust in decentralised justice systems? If parties know that an algorithm partly decided their case, will they still consider the outcome fair? Exploring these questions will be essential to designing dispute resolution systems that remain not only efficient and scalable, but also aligned with the principles of transparency, accountability, and human dignity that underpin the rule of law.

V A Glimpse of the Future of Decentralised Justice In the earlier sections, we looked at how AI and decentralised justice systems work together. We saw how AI helps prepare disputes and protect confidentiality (section II), how it can support users and make the process fairer (section III), and how experiments show that AI jurors usually agree with human jurors on simple cases but struggle with those needing extra research or fact-checking (section IV). Taking all this into account, we can now envision how all these elements might fall together in a practical system that spans the entire lifecycle of decentralised dispute resolution, from pre-dispute analysis through initial resolution and appeals to final enforcement: (0) Pre-Commitment Analysis Phase. Parties considering dispute resolution receive AI-generated assessments of their case strength, expected costs across different resolution paths, and optimal structuring recommendations. This transparency reduces friction in choosing decentralised justice while setting appropriate expectations for the process ahead. (1) Dispute Preparation. The dispute undergoes the AI-driven preparation workflow detailed in section II, which formats evidence, ensures anonymisation, obtains party verification, and performs quality control before on-chain presentation. The AI tool might take the form of a ‘lawyer chatbot’ that guides parties in presenting the evidence that maximises their odds of success. (2) Initial Resolution: AI-Focused Court. The case is then sent to a court in a decentralised justice system built for AI participation. Because decisions are made quickly and cheaply (e.g., in minutes for about USD 50), humans are unlikely to take part, since it wouldn’t be worth their time. Human appeal courts, by contrast, may take days and cost around USD 500. This difference in speed, cost, and process lets parties choose what best fits their needs: fast AI-based rulings or slower, more detailed human reviews. Jurors can also choose to participate in the type of court that suits their time and preferred decision style.63 Note that the courts calibrated for AI agents operate under cryptoeconomic incentives that are generally present in decentralised justice. In particular, the AIs compete with each other economically to best align with possible human appeals. The population of AI agents participating may evolve over time depending on which agents achieve gains or suffer losses in this game, providing a model for AI participation that does not require enshrining a single AI decision-maker. (3) First Appeal: Non-Specialist Human Panel. An appeal may be directed to a court designed for small panels of humans who do not necessarily hold specialised qualifications. In cases where review by non-specialist jurors does not add value, this step may be omitted. Verification of humanity of jurors can be conducted by a proof of personhood protocol, such as Proof of Humanity64 James, supra n. 8. or Worldcoin.65 See generally, World Foundation, Building the Real Human Network, https://whitepaper.world.org/ (accessed 29 October 2025). , 66 Requiring jurors to be registered on a proof-of-personhood tool such as Proof of Humanity (PoH) or Worldcoin and limiting them to one vote per address could be applied to all jurors in any of steps 2, 3, and 4. However, the use of these tools comes with limitations and trade-offs. Once a human registers with PoH or Worldcoin, they could theoretically delegate decision-making to an AI while remaining economically accountable for the outcome. Nevertheless, one might expect that most proof-of-personhood-verified jurors will maintain meaningful involvement in the decision-making process, potentially consulting AI tools while exercising final human judgment. Also, note that the current registration process for PoH requires users to submit a photo and video of themselves for comparison against the list of existing registrations, publicly tying this profile to the user’s Ethereum address. Thus, there are important technical challenges when one wants to avoid jurors having to doxx themselves or tie their identity to their votes. Development of zero-knowledge Proof of Humanity extensions aims to allow jurors to prove their human status without revealing which specific PoH profile corresponds to which vote, allowing one to preserve anonymity while maintaining accountability. Worldcoin, which prevents duplicate registration by using scans of users’ irises, is also the subject of criticism regarding its handling of sensitive biometric data, see generally, Kris Shrishak, Privacy Washing Through PETs: The Case of Worldcoin, Commun. Communications of the ACM (28 March 2025), https://cacm.acm.org/blogcacm/privacy-washing-through-pets-the-case-of-worldcoin/. (4) Second Appeal: Specialist Court. An appeal to a court where jurors must hold a Soulbound Token (SBT)67 See generally, Puja Ohlhaver, E. Glen Weyl and Vitalik Buterin, Decentralized Society: Finding Web3’s Soul (10 May 2022), https://ssrn.com/abstract=4105763 or http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.4105763. indicating them as specialists in order to participate. If several relevant SBTs attest to different types of expertise important for a category of cases, then selection could be based on SBT weighting in order to ensure that different forms of expertise are adequately represented.68 See generally, William George, Research Topics in Kleros Juror Selection: Blockchain Identity, Soulbound Tokens, and Models of Attack Resistance, Kleros Blog (15 January 2024), https://blog.kleros.io/research-topics-in-kleros-juror-selection-blockchain-identity-soulbound-tokens-and-models-of-attack-resistance/. Indeed, weighting juror drawing by the SBTs users holds to achieve diversity of juror specialisations and backgrounds aligns with research that indicates that, under some conditions, diverse panels outperform comparably skilled homogenous panels,69 See generally, Lu Hong and Scott E. Page, Groups of Diverse Problem Solvers Can Outperform Groups of High-Ability Problem Solvers, Proc. Nat’l Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 101 (2004): 16385-16389, https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.0403723101. though further investigation remains pending on how these ideas can be applied to decentralised justice. (5) Final Appeal: General Community Court. An appeal may be made to the general court, where jurors do not need SBTs to participate and are therefore not necessarily experts. However, they receive clear instructions not to overturn a lower court’s decision unless they believe the system has been attacked. In this sense, all jurors are required to possess one specific type of expertise: the ability to assess whether the integrity of the system has been compromised. This design enhances attack resistance, as an incorrect decision would need to pass through the entire community, even if the community as a whole lacks subject-matter expertise. It is somewhat analogous to how the Supreme Courts operate in those cases where they are called upon to review whether procedural guarantees were respected in earlier instances. In this hybrid model, AI handles the first stage of dispute resolution and human jurors review appeals. The AI reviews evidence, applies clear rules, and gives an initial decision. If either party disagrees, they can appeal to human jurors through a decentralised process. This structure allows the resolution of simple cases quickly and cheaply, while complex or sensitive cases still get human attention. By combining AI’s speed with human fairness and judgment, decentralised justice systems can offer scalable and balanced dispute resolution for the digital age. Figure 4.5 Hybrid Multi-Layered Dispute Resolution Architecture with AI and Human Oversight Source: Author’s Own. Figure 4.5 shows how the process in a decentralised justice system may evolve through different steps. There may be several rounds of voting at each stage to obtain larger and more representative panels, though this comes at the cost of additional time and expense. The likelihood of being drawn depends not only on the number of tokens staked across the steps but also on the juror’s SBTs in step 4.

VI Open Legal Questions and Regulatory Challenges The preceding sections have outlined how AI and decentralised justice can interact technically, from dispute preparation through decision-making to appeals. Yet the legal and regulatory landscape remains largely uncharted. While this chapter has focused on technological capabilities and empirical evidence, it would be incomplete without acknowledging the significant legal uncertainties that early adopters and their counsel must face. Decentralised justice systems raise important questions about how traditional procedural protections translate to blockchain-based dispute resolution. These challenges are comprehensively examined in Ast et al. (2023), which explores how cryptoeconomic mechanisms and system design choices address (or fail to address) due process concerns in decentralised arbitration more broadly.70 See generally, Federico Ast et al., Decentralized Justice: State of the Art, Recurring Criticisms and Next-Generation Research Topics, Front. Blockchain 6 (2023): 1204090, https://doi.org/10.3389/fbloc.2023.1204090. [A] Liability and Accountability in Decentralised Systems Traditional dispute resolution operates within clear liability frameworks. Arbitrators can face sanctions for misconduct or gross negligence. Lawyers who provide incompetent advice face malpractice claims. Administrative staff who mishandle evidence can be held accountable. These frameworks depend on identifying responsible parties (individuals or institutions) with legal personality who can be held to account. Decentralised justice challenges these frameworks at multiple levels. Consider the AI-assisted dispute preparation described in section II. If an AI tool mischaracterises evidence, omits crucial facts, or introduces errors that affect the outcome, who bears responsibility? Is it the parties who chose to use the tool? The developers who created it? The decentralised protocol that hosts it? Traditional legal frameworks have difficulty offering definitive guidance, and the limited case law to date has not established sufficient legal certainty about how courts will approach decisions involving AI-driven tools in the future. Early regulatory efforts, such as the EU Artificial Intelligence Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), begin to address these issues by imposing strict accountability and transparency obligations on high-risk AI applications (including those used in legal or adjudicative contexts), yet many open questions remain about how these standards apply to decentralised and autonomous systems. The challenge deepens when we consider AI jurors (section IV). If a juror in a decentralised justice platform were human and voted carelessly or corruptly, the cryptoeconomic incentive system might penalise them financially by redistributing their staked tokens. But this mechanism operates entirely within the protocol and does not translate into legal liability under national laws. Can an aggrieved party sue an anonymous juror selected by cryptographic sortition? Improving blockchain tools such as proof-of-personhood protocols and Soulbound Tokens that attest to a juror’s credentials in a specific jurisdiction might facilitate the handling of responsibility in that jurisdiction. On the other hand, if jurors operate as autonomous AI agents, does liability rest with whoever deployed the agent, or with the AI model provider? The integration of AI into decentralised justice platforms adds further complexity to these liability questions. When AI tools malfunction within a DAO-governed system, responsibility becomes doubly obscured: neither the algorithmic decision-maker nor the distributed governance structure fits traditional liability frameworks, and the DAO governance landscape is embryonic.
