While some jurisdictions are beginning to recognise DAOs as legal entities, these emerging frameworks do not yet address AI-specific harms. As courts and regulators develop approaches to both DAO liability and AI accountability, the intersection of these two technologies presents a unique frontier where new legal frameworks may emerge organically through early precedents and regulatory experimentation. [B] Enforceability and the Recognition of Awards Traditional arbitration benefits from well-established enforcement regimes, most notably the 1958 New York Convention, adhered to by over 170 countries, providing for the recognition and enforcement of foreign arbitral awards by the national courts of the signatory countries.71 Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (New York, 10 June 1958), United Nations, https://uncitral.un.org/sites/uncitral.un.org/files/media-documents/uncitral/en/new-york-convention-e.pdf (accessed 29 October 2025). National arbitration laws similarly favour enforcement, with courts permitted to vacate awards only on narrow grounds such as excess of authority, lack of due process, or violation of public policy. Decentralised arbitration awards, however, sit uneasily within these frameworks. Treaties such as the New York Convention operate on the basis of a nation State’s geographical sovereignty. The New York Convention itself contemplates awards issued by identifiable arbitrators following agreed procedures. Kleros and similar systems instead offer awards determined by anonymous jurors selected through cryptographic mechanisms and recorded immutably on a blockchain. While the award itself may be clear, courts accustomed to traditional arbitration may question whether such systems satisfy off-chain legal requirements. Several specific issues arise. First, many arbitration statutes require arbitrators to be identified and to meet certain qualifications. Anonymous jurors selected by token staking do not fit this model. Second, courts reviewing arbitration awards typically assess whether arbitrators exceeded their authority or violated procedural requirements. How should courts evaluate the conduct of a decentralised jury that cannot be individually examined? Third, blockchain’s immutability creates tension with the courts’ power to vacate awards: if an award is permanently recorded on-chain, how can it be ‘set aside’? Early judicial decisions and practical innovations suggest that these challenges may be more manageable than they first appear. In a landmark 2021 case, a Mexican court in Jalisco enforced an arbitral award based on Kleros’s blockchain arbitration protocol. The parties had agreed to a hybrid process in which the arbitrator issued a procedural order to Kleros, whose decentralised jurors rendered a decision. The arbitrator then incorporated this outcome into a formal award under Mexican law, satisfying requirements such as date, place, and signature. This ‘arbitrator of record’ model successfully bridged blockchain arbitration with the national legal system, preserving the benefits of decentralised decision-making while ensuring enforceability.72 See generally, Maxime Chevalier, Arbitration Tech Toolbox: Is a Mexican Court Decision the First Stone to Bridging the Blockchain Arbitral Order with National Legal Orders?, Kluwer Arbitration Blog (4 March 2022), https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2022/03/04/arbitration-tech-toolbox-is-a-mexican-court-decision-the-first-stone-to-bridging-the-blockchain-arbitral-order-with-national-legal-orders/. Building on this precedent, Kleros has developed hybrid models that combine traditional dispute resolution with AI. The Kleros Mediation Bridge, described earlier, blends conventional mediation with decentralised arbitration, while its AI-powered mediator, Harmony, helps to structure disputes and suggest settlements. Human mediators provide oversight and empathy, and Kleros jurors deliver enforceable decisions when needed. Other models may involve creating legal entities to act as institutional arbitration providers while decision-making remains decentralised, easing recognition under existing arbitration frameworks. Moreover, regulatory frameworks are evolving to accommodate these innovations. Mexico’s passage of the MASC (Ley General de Mecanismos Alternativos de Solución de Controversias) law in 2024 reflects growing legislative recognition of new forms of dispute resolution, including those conducted through blockchain systems and even AI-based procedures.73 México, Ley General de Mecanismos Alternativos de Solución de Controversias en Materia Penal, Diario Oficial de la Federación [DOF], 29 January 2014 (as amended), available at https://www.dof.gob.mx/nota_detalle.php?codigo=5715307&fecha=26/01/2024#gsc.tab=0. As more jurisdictions gain experience with decentralised justice and develop targeted regulations, the enforceability landscape continues to mature. The legal questions outlined in this section are neither exhaustive nor insurmountable. They reflect the natural growing pains of a field at the intersection of law, technology, and governance. As decentralised justice systems build track records, courts set precedents, and regulators create clearer frameworks, many of today’s uncertainties will subside. For now, these systems are best suited to low-value disputes, cases involving blockchain-savvy parties, or contexts where speed and transparency outweigh legal uncertainty. This points to a tiered approach in which decentralised mechanisms handle appropriate cases, while traditional systems remain available for high-stakes disputes requiring maximum legal certainty. The convergence of AI and decentralised justice brings both new opportunities and legal puzzles. How these are resolved will determine whether such systems remain niche or evolve into mainstream mechanisms for the digital economy.

VII Conclusion The rise of AI marks a profound shift in how societies resolve disputes. While praised for its efficiency, AI also raises concerns about bias and algocracy. Decentralised justice offers transparency and incentive structures that can counter these risks. Together, AI and decentralised justice introduce new forms of dispute resolution: faster, more accessible, and potentially more trustworthy than traditional mechanisms. The goal is not to replace human judgment, but to redefine the balance between humans and machines. AI excels at scale: it can summarise evidence, anonymise data, and assist parties in preparing claims. Decentralised juries, meanwhile, preserve fairness, legitimacy, and the interpretive nuance that algorithms lack. Hybrid models combining both can offset each other’s weaknesses: AI’s opacity through collective intelligence, and human processes’ slowness through automation. Challenges remain: bias, explainability, and the acceptability of algorithmic rulings all demand attention. Divergent AI outcomes raise questions of trust and accountability, while evolving regulatory frameworks must address issues of liability, transparency, and data protection in AI-assisted justice. Here, decentralised protocols like Kleros provide a valuable model, embedding transparency and diversity into the adjudication process. Ultimately, the path forward is not to choose between AI and decentralised justice, but to combine them into systems that are both efficient and legitimate. As these technologies mature, their convergence may mark not the end of justice as we know it, but the beginning of a new era where crowds and code collaborate to deliver justice for the digital age.

Appendix 2 In this appendix, we provide greater detail on the statistical results presented in section IV. For each dispute of the 63 past Kleros disputes regarding information in the Kleros ‘Address Tag Registry’ from the period from September 2023 and February 2025, one can calculate the percentage of Kleros jurors that provided the winning answer, which we denote by X. Then, we can calculate whether each LLM used in our experiments, ChatGPT5, Claude Opus 4.1, DeepSeek v3.2, Mistral LeChat, and Gemini 2.5 Pro, provided this outcome or not, giving us the binary variable Y. For each LLM, we perform a logistic regression between these two lists,74 Fundamentally, we want to learn about the ability of the models to obtain the ‘true’ outcomes to the questions they are asked. One might model both the LLMs and the Kleros jurors as receiving some underlying signal with noise. Then, the rates at which the jurors vote for the winning answer, as interpreted by the Kleros platform, will capture both which outcome corresponds to the true signal as well as how much noise the jurors observe this signal with except in cases where the noise the jurors receive is so extreme that they collectively vote for an answer different from the signal. Thus, the regression considered above should be capable of capturing the true relationship between the noises in the two groups’ observations except possibly at extreme values of juror noise where the relationship might break down. yielding a best fit to the model: We find the following statistics for the different models. Appendix 3 In this appendix, we discuss how we determined the values for the penalties and rewards attributed to the LLMs for their votes in the tables of section [2] and section [3]. This is based on the incentive system that is used by the Kleros protocol, which is given as follows: – Net fees are paid into the protocol that are linear in the number of jurors. If there are N jurors and the fee per juror is f, the total net payment made is N*f. – If a juror does not vote for the winning answer, she loses a value of d. – Suppose there are N total jurors, k of whom vote for the winning answer and N-k of whom do not vote for the winning answer. Then each juror who votes for the winning answer receives: We normalise by taking f=1; namely, the fee the protocol charges per juror is one unit of payment. Then one can calibrate d so that the payment one receives by always voting for the most common answer to a category of disputes, in this case by always voting ‘No, Don’t Add It’ which won 58 of the 63 disputes in this data set, is zero. We call the strategy of always voting for the same outcome the ‘lazy’ strategy; this approach of calibrating parameters so that the lazy strategy is unprofitable is used in the Kleros protocol.75 See generally, William George, Parameterization for Kleros Courts, Kleros Blog (13 March 2023), https://blog.kleros.io/parameterization-of-kleros-courts/. Then, for dispute j, we take Nj to be the number of jurors who participated in case j and kj to be the number of jurors who voted for the winning answer. We think of a juror executing the lazy strategy or ruling based on one of the LLMs as being added to the existing jury, so the total number of jurors would then be Nj+1. Choosing d so that the lazy strategy has a return of exactly zero corresponds to: where: is the indicator function that takes a value of one for values of j such that ‘No, Don’t Add It ‘ wins and zero otherwise. For this data set, this calculation yields d=16.18 units of payment.

Can you please describe what this contract on the Base Chain does? https://basescan.org/address/0x46e078FeDCD91a813306D5637d7ec5C7Dc201EC3 Claude Opus 4.1 I’ll fetch and analyze that smart contract from BaseScan for you. Récupéré Address: 0x46e078Fe...7Dc201EC3 | BaseScan basescan.org This contract is a CLGauge (Concentrated Liquidity Gauge), which is part of a decentralised exchange (DEX) ecosystem. Here’s what it does: Main Purpose This is a staking/rewards contract for liquidity providers in a concentrated liquidity pool (similar to Uniswap V3). It allows users to stake their liquidity position NFTs to earn reward tokens instead of trading fees. Key Functions Staking Mechanism – Users can deposit their liquidity position NFTs (represented as ERC721 tokens) into the gauge. – When staked, positions earn reward tokens (likely governance/protocol tokens) instead of the trading fees they would normally collect. – Users can withdraw their NFTs at any time and claim accumulated rewards. Reward Distribution – Rewards are distributed based on the amount of liquidity provided and the time staked. – The contract receives periodic reward distributions through notifyRewardAmount(). – Rewards are calculated using a time-based emission rate (rewardRate). – Each epoch (weekly periods) has its own reward rate. Fee Collection – If this is a pool gauge (isPool = true), it collects trading fees from the underlying pool. – These fees are forwarded to a separate feesVotingReward contract for distribution to voters. Key Features (1) NFT-based positions: Works with Nonfungible Position Manager NFTs from concentrated liquidity pools. (2) Reward tracking: Tracks rewards per position based on liquidity and time in range. (3) Multi-position support: Users can stake multiple NFT positions. (4) Reentrancy protection: Uses ReentrancyGuard for security. (5) WETH support: Can handle positions involving wrapped ETH. Technical Details – Built on Solidity 0.7.6. – Implements a minimal proxy pattern (clone) for gas-efficient deployment. – Uses fixed-point math (Q128) for precise reward calculations. – Integrates with a broader ecosystem including: – Voter contract (manages emissions). – Pool contract (the underlying liquidity pool). – Gauge Factory (creates these gauges). – NFT Position Manager (manages liquidity positions). This is essentially an incentive layer on top of a concentrated liquidity DEX, allowing the protocol to direct rewards to specific pools to incentivize liquidity provision in desired trading pairs. Deepseek v3.2 Of course. I have analyzed the contract at address 0x46e078FeDCD91a813306D5637d7ec5C7Dc201EC3 on the Base blockchain. This is a staking and rewards contract for a token called Puff. The core function is to allow users to lock up their Puff tokens for a set period of time in exchange for rewards, which are also paid in Puff. Here is a detailed breakdown of its purpose and mechanics. 1. Core Purpose: Time-Locked Staking The contract is designed for staking with a commitment. Unlike flexible staking where you can withdraw anytime, this contract requires you to lock your tokens for a specific duration. In return for this commitment, you earn a higher reward rate. 2. Key User Actions – Stake (deposit): A user can lock their Puff tokens for a pre-defined period (e.g., 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, or 180 days). When they do this, the contract mints and assigns them a special NFT (a ‘Staked NFT’ or sNFT). This NFT is the proof of their stake. – Claim Rewards (getReward): Users can claim their accumulated Puff token rewards at any time without ending their stake. – Withdraw (withdraw): Once the lock-up period for their stake has ended, the user can ‘burn’ their NFT and withdraw their original staked principal plus all remaining unclaimed rewards. 3. Main Components & Mechanics – Staked NFT (sNFT): This is a crucial part of the contract. It’s an ERC-721 token (a non-fungible token) that represents the user’s stake. Each stake creates a unique NFT with metadata stored on-chain that records: – amount: The number of Puff tokens staked. – lockPeriod: The chosen lock-up duration. – startTime: The timestamp when the stake began. – rewardDebt: An internal accounting value to track rewards. – Reward Calculation: Rewards are calculated based on three factors: – The amount of Puff staked. – The lock period (longer locks typically yield higher rewards). – A global reward rate set by the contract owner. – Tiers and Multipliers: The contract has a system of tiers (from 1 to 6) corresponding to the different lock periods. A longer lock period places you in a higher tier, which grants a higher multiplier on your reward rate. This incentivizes long-term staking. – Fee Structure: The contract includes mechanisms for fees, which are likely used to support the project’s treasury and operations. The treasuryWallet address receives these fees. – Ownable and Pausable: The contract is owned by a deployer address, which has the ability to: – Set the reward rates. – Change the treasury wallet. – Pause staking in case of an emergency. Summary in Simple Terms Think of this contract as a Certificate of Deposit (CD) for Puff tokens: (1) You buy a ‘CD’ by locking your Puff tokens for a fixed term (e.g., 90 days). (2) You receive a ‘certificate’ (the NFT) as your receipt. (3) You earn a higher interest rate (rewards in Puff) for choosing a longer term. (4) You can collect the interest anytime. (5) When the term is over, you cash in your certificate to get your original tokens plus any remaining interest. Important Note: Always exercise caution when interacting with DeFi contracts. Ensure you understand the lock-up periods and the risks involved, including the potential for the project’s token value to change or for the contract to have undiscovered vulnerabilities. It’s a best practice to verify the contract’s code through a third-party audit, which is not visible from the Basescan page alone. Base Explorer It is deployed as a minimal proxy pointing to a master implementation (i.e., a proxy instantiation). Base Explorer The idea is: – Users deposit NFTs (representing LP positions) into this gauge. – The gauge tracks how much liquidity (in those NFT positions) is staked. – The gauge accrues rewards over time, which users can later claim. – It interacts with a pool contract (CLPool) to coordinate reward growth and staking. – It also handles ‘fees’ from the underlying pool (if it’s a pool) and distributes them via a ‘voting rewards’ mechanism. So, in summary: It’s a contract that allows staking of NFT-based liquidity positions and distributing rewards over time to stakers. Key Components & Functions Here are some of the important pieces and behaviors: Part/Variable Role/Meaning nft (INonfungiblePositionManager) The NFT contract that represents LP positions (e.g., Uniswap-style liquidity NFTs) Base Explorer+1 voter A contract used for governance/controlling whether this gauge is ‘alive’ and allowed to operate Base Explorer pool (ICLPool) The liquidity pool contract that tracks global reward growth etc. Base Explorer+1 rewardToken The token used as reward (ERC-20) that stakers will receive Base Explorer rewardRate, periodFinish Track the rate at which rewards accrue and when the current reward period ends Base Explorer+1 rewardGrowthInside For each NFT (tokenId), the stored ‘growth’ of rewards inside its tick range, used to compute incremental rewards Base Explorer lastUpdateTime, rewards[tokenId] Used to maintain how much is accrued but not yet claimed for each NFT Base Explorer Major Functions/Actions – initialize(...) – This is called once when the proxy is set up to point it to the appropriate pool, NFT manager, reward token, etc. Base Explorer; – deposit(uint256 tokenId) – A user deposits their NFT (liquidity position): – The NFT must be owned by the depositor; – It is transferred into the gauge; – The corresponding liquidity is ‘staked’ into the pool; – Reward accounting is initialized. Base Explorer+1. – withdraw(uint256 tokenId) – A user withdraws their NFT (getting it back), while also claiming rewards. It handles updating reward state, transferring the NFT, and adjusting the pool stake. Base Explorer; – getReward(...) – Allows a user to claim the accumulated rewards for their staked positions. There are versions for claiming across all NFTs or a specific one. Base Explorer; – notifyRewardAmount(uint256 _amount) & notifyRewardWithoutClaim(uint256 _amount) – Functions to inject new reward tokens into the gauge (so new reward periods begin). They are restricted to certain roles (voter or admin). Base Explorer; – internal accounting (_updateRewards, _earned) – These are used to track how much reward each NFT has earned since last updates. They use the global reward growth data from the pool and liquidity in each NFT’s tick range. Base Explorer; – fee claiming/handling – If the underlying pool is ‘isPool = true’, then the gauge also interacts with the pool to collect fees and possibly forward them to a ‘feesVotingReward’ contract. Base Explorer. Observations from Its Activity – The contract has 0 ETH balance (i.e., it doesn’t hold native ETH) but it holds many ERC-20 tokens (over 136 tokens) including a significant holding in Aerodrome (AERO). Base Explorer. – It sees a lot of ‘Deposit’, ‘Withdraw’, ‘Get Reward’ calls in its recent transactions. Base Explorer. – Its creation transaction indicates the ‘Contract Creation’ event from a ‘SlipStream Gauge Factory.’ Base Explorer. – The code is compiled for solidity version 0.7.6 (with optimization) and is a minimal proxy to a master implementation. Base Explorer. Risks & Things to Check – Because it accepts NFTs and stakes them, users must trust the logic is safe (e.g., no reentrancy, proper accounting). – The contract restricts certain roles (voter, admin) to trigger reward injections, so misbehavior in those roles could be problematic. – It’s a proxy, so if the implementation behind it is upgraded or changed, behavior might shift (depending on the upgradeability pattern). – Interactions with the pool are crucial: the correct distribution of rewards relies on that pool’s metric for ‘rewardGrowthInside’ etc. If you like, I can produce a simplified plain-English summary of exactly what a user would experience using this contract (i.e., ‘you deposit your LP NFT, you accrue reward tokens, you claim them’) – would you like that? Mistral LeChat I’m unable to directly access or analyze live blockchain data, including contracts on BaseScan or other explorers. However, I can guide you on how to understand what this contract does yourself or point you to tools that can help. How to Analyze the Contract: (1) Check the Contract Tab on BaseScan: – Open the contract page and navigate to the ‘Contract’ tab. – Look for the verified source code (if available). If it’s verified, you can read the Solidity code directly. – Key sections to review: – Contract name (often at the top of the code). – Functions (what actions the contract supports, e.g., transfer, stake, mint). – Events (logs emitted by the contract, e.g., Transfer, Approval). – State variables (e.g., totalSupply, owner). (2) Use a Blockchain Explorer’s ‘Read/Write Contract’ Tab: – On BaseScan, go to the ‘Read Contract’ or ‘Write Contract’ tabs to interact with the contract’s functions (if it’s a standard like ERC-20/ERC-721). – This can reveal whether it’s a token, NFT, DeFi protocol, etc. (3) Check Transaction History: – The ‘Transactions’ tab shows how the contract is used. For example: – Frequent transfer calls → likely a token. – Calls to stake or deposit → likely a DeFi protocol. – mint calls → likely an NFT or token contract. (4) Tools for Deeper Analysis: – Etherscan/BaseScan API: Use their APIs to fetch contract ABI or metadata. – Tenderly/Sourcify: Simulate or debug the contract. – OpenZeppelin Defender: For security analysis (if you’re the owner). (5) Common Contract Types on Base: – ERC-20 Token: Standard for fungible tokens (e.g., stablecoins, governance tokens). – ERC-721/ERC-1155: NFT contracts. – DeFi Protocols: Lending, staking, or DEXs (e.g., Uniswap forks). – Bridges: Cross-chain asset transfer contracts. Quick Observations (From the Address Alone): – The contract is on Base Chain (Ethereum L2), which often hosts DeFi, NFTs, or scaling solutions. – The address format is standard (0x...), so it’s likely a smart contract, not an EOA (externally owned account). Next Steps: – If you share specific functions or code snippets from the contract, I can help explain what they do. – If you’re unsure how to interpret the code, let me know what you’re looking for (e.g., ‘Is this a token?’ or ‘Does this contract have admin privileges?’), and I can guide you further. Would you like help decoding a specific part of the contract? Gemini 2.5 Pro I am unable to describe what the smart contract at the provided address does. I have attempted to analyze the contract through the ‘basescan.org’ link you provided, but I was unable to extract the contract’s name or source code. Furthermore, a Google search for the contract’s address did not yield any results. This could mean a few things: – The contract’s source code has not been verified on Basescan, so it’s not publicly visible. – The contract is not widely used or well-documented. – It might be a private or custom contract for a specific purpose. To find out what this contract does, you could try to: – Check if the contract is part of a larger project. If so, the project’s official website or documentation might have more information. – Ask in online communities related to Base Chain or smart contracts. Someone there might recognize the contract or be able to help you analyze it.

title: Chapter 5: Reflexive Justice: The Paradox of Predictive Systems in Arbitral Environments
editors: Fernanda Carvalho Dias de Oliveira Silva
copyright: 2024 Kluwer Law International BV, and/or its subsidiaries, licensors, and contributors. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

sections.title: I Introduction: Predictive Systems and the Fragility of Anticipated Order
sections.text: I Introduction: Predictive Systems and the Fragility of Anticipated Order The progressive deployment of predictive artificial intelligence in arbitration has been marked by expectations of rationalization, efficiency, and enhanced consistency.1 Gizem Halis Kasap, “Can Artificial Intelligence (‘AI’) Replace Human Arbitrators? Technological Concerns and Legal Implications” (2021) 2021(2) Journal of Dispute Resolution, available at https://scholarship.law.missouri.edu/jdr/vol2021/iss2/5 (accessed December 18, 2025). Arbitral proceedings, while institutionally designed to mitigate unpredictability through expert decision-making, remain subject to the uncertainty inherent to human judgment. In this context, algorithmic tools capable of mapping patterns, estimating probabilities of success, and recommending potentially optimal procedural choices are increasingly perceived as instruments capable of stabilizing strategic environments. These systems, grounded in extensive databases of past decisions and statistical learning, are designed to operate as epistemic infrastructures: they promise to transform indeterminacy into manageable risk, offering parties a way to anticipate outcomes and optimize strategies in light of prior behavioral patterns. However, this promise must be understood in light of a deeper structural tension: the very act of prediction reshapes the system it seeks to observe. Predictive systems do not operate in a vacuum. The recursive nature of predictive AI means that its outputs influence the behavior of the actors to whom they are addressed. That behavior, in turn, becomes part of the input of subsequent predictive cycles, introducing a structural instability into the very conditions of the system’s reliability.2 Brent Mittelstadt et al., “The Ethics of Algorithms: Mapping the Debate” (2016) 3(2) Big Data & Society 1, 3-5 https://doi.org/10.1177/2053951716679679. As parties internalize3 In the social sciences, internalization refers to the process by which external cues, norms, or informational signals become incorporated into an agent’s own reasoning or behavior—not merely as external data, but as part of their decision-making framework. For example, when individuals internalize a social norm, they adopt it as a guide for action even in the absence of external enforcement. See Internalization (sociology), Wikipedia (defining the integration of external influences into one’s own cognitive and behavioral structures) https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internalization_%28sociology%29 (accessed November 15, 2025); see also Cristina Bicchieri, Social Norms (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy) (internalization leads actors to perceive norm-abiding behavior as appropriate) https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/social-norms/ (accessed November 15, 2025). predictive outputs and adjust their conduct accordingly, the behavioral patterns that previously grounded the model’s predictive reliability, understood as its alignment with relatively stable behavioral regularities, are progressively eroded. As the referential base of the system increasingly derives from the behavioral effects produced by the model itself, a recursive dynamic emerges in which the growing adoption of, and reliance on, predictive outputs gradually reshapes the very ecosystem of behaviors and interactions the model is designed to describe and anticipate. Consequently, the stable behavioral patterns that initially supported the model’s capacity to generate reliable predictions begin to dissolve. This paradox does not emerge as a mere empirical irregularity but reflects a deeper epistemological tension concerning the conditions under which predictive knowledge can be produced and relied upon in institutional decision-making systems, such as dispute resolution, where observation, prediction, and strategic behavior are tightly interwoven. Predictive outputs do not merely describe an external reality; they circulate within an ecosystem of strategic agents, legal norms, and institutional incentives. Once such forecasts become known and acted upon, the conditions that sustained their claim to neutrality and objectivity are progressively undermined. Importantly, this recursive structure is not to be mistaken for an isolated malfunction of predictive logic. Legal reasoning itself is reflexive, evolving through iterative cycles of interpretation, precedent formation, and behavioral adjustment. What distinguishes predictive systems is not that they introduce reflexivity, but that they accelerate and reorient it. Their logics of optimization—privileging efficiency, cost-minimization, statistical coherence, and procedural convergence—increasingly define the terms under which legal reflexivity unfolds. This redirection may compress or marginalize dimensions traditionally central to juridical rationality, such as normative contestation, abstraction, and the interpretive rearticulation of legal meaning. This chapter foregrounds that tension as its central argument, proposing that the epistemic consequences of predictive modeling extend beyond the technical domain into the institutional foundations of legal judgment. Game theory offers a useful analytical lens for this discussion. In what follows, this chapter draws on game theory and related frameworks to examine how predictive analysis interacts with strategic behavior in arbitration, and how adaptive responses by parties complicate assumptions of stability and predictability in decision-making. In games of incomplete information, equilibria are rarely stable once actors begin to adapt their strategies in response to perceived patterns or expected behavior. Arbitration exemplifies this dynamic in a particularly acute form, given the combination of party-driven procedure, strategic choice of decision-makers, and limited ex ante visibility over outcomes.4 Drew Fudenberg and Jean Tirole, Game Theory (MIT Press, 1991). A model that accurately predicts certain procedural or substantive outcomes may cease to do so once such predictions are internalized by litigants and arbitrators, precisely because they are incorporated into strategic assessments and decision-making processes, thereby prompting a recalibration of the expectations and tactical assumptions that previously structured party behavior. The dynamics described above can be more clearly understood through established theoretical frameworks that address the interaction between prediction, behavior, and uncertainty in complex systems. One such framework is the theory of reflexivity advanced by George Soros,5 George Soros, The Alchemy of Finance, Chapter 1 (John Wiley & Sons, 2003). which emphasizes how feedback loops between perception and reality blur the distinction between model and world. In reflexive environments, predictive statements do not merely anticipate future states; once disclosed, they may influence behavior in ways that feed back into the system they seek to describe.6 Mike Ananny and Kate Crawford, “Seeing Without Knowing: Limitations of the Transparency Ideal and Its Application to Algorithmic Accountability” (2018) 20(3) New Media & Society 973, 976 https://doi.org/10.1177/1461444816676645. From a complementary perspective, Nassim Nicholas Taleb’s critique of model fragility7 Nassim Nicholas Taleb, The Black Swan: The Impact of the Highly Improbable (2nd edn, Random House 2010). highlights how apparent statistical robustness under normal conditions can foster excessive confidence and mask latent sources of instability. Applied to predictive systems, this critique draws attention to the risks associated with treating probabilistic outputs as stable reference points. This phenomenon is observed across multiple decision-making contexts and acquires particular institutional relevance in arbitration, given the strategic use of prediction in shaping party behavior and procedural choices, especially when underlying assumptions and limits8 This expression refers both to the assumptions embedded in the model’s construction—such as which data are selected, which variables are prioritized, and how performance is measured—and to the assumptions tacitly made by human users who interpret these outputs as meaningful. Both dimensions must be considered because epistemic risk arises not only from the technical limitations of the model itself, but also from the uncritical way its outputs are often received and acted upon within legal and strategic environments.—both those encoded in the model’s training process and those tacitly accepted by human users regarding the nature and trustworthiness of the predictions—are insufficiently scrutinized.9 Mike Ananny and Kate Crawford, “Seeing Without Knowing: Limitations of the Transparency Ideal and Its Application to Algorithmic Accountability” (2018) 20(3) New Media & Society 973, 976 https://doi.org/10.1177/1461444816676645. Throughout the chapter, arbitration is treated as a case study that allows the broader epistemic issues posed by predictive AI to become particularly visible. This is not to suggest that arbitration uniquely intensifies reflexivity, but that its procedural features, especially party autonomy, flexible procedural design, and limited public scrutiny, make it a productive forum for examining how predictive systems interact with legal reasoning in practice. Taken together, these perspectives provide a conceptual lens through which the recursive dynamics identified above can be analyzed. Rather than functioning as neutral instruments of observation, predictive tools operate within institutional environments shaped by strategic actors, legal norms, and incentive structures. The following discussion applies these insights to arbitration, examining how the increasing availability of predictive analysis may reshape strategic interaction, expectations, and the epistemic role of prediction within arbitral proceedings. This article situates this paradox—henceforth referred to as the predictability paradox—at the center of a structural analysis of artificial intelligence in arbitration. The analysis developed here is technical in a conceptual and epistemic sense, rather than in an engineering or implementation-oriented one. It does not pursue empirical validation or system design questions, but draws on epistemology, decision theory, and the philosophy of technology to examine how predictive systems generate, stabilize, and disrupt knowledge claims within institutional decision-making contexts. Predictive AI is conceptualized not as an external tool applied to a pre-existing institutional decision-making process, but as an epistemic agent embedded within a self-referential ecosystem drawing on critical accounts that emphasize the constitutive role of algorithmic systems within institutional practices10 Frank Pasquale, New Laws of Robotics: Defending Human Expertise in the Age of AI (Harvard University Press, 2020). and endowed with the capacity to alter the environment it seeks to optimize. Under this lens, the integration of predictive systems in arbitration is not merely a question of efficiency or accuracy, but a matter of institutional design, cognitive limitation, and dynamic stability. The argument proceeds in five parts. The next section examines the technical architecture and epistemological assumptions of current predictive systems in arbitration, with attention to their data foundations, model dynamics, and structural limitations. The third section articulates the theoretical foundations of the predictability paradox, drawing from game theory, reflexivity, and epistemic fragility. The fourth section explores the juridical and institutional consequences of recursive adaptation in arbitration, advancing a normative and analytical assessment of how the performativity of AI predictions may distort party incentives and procedural integrity. The fifth section outlines design-oriented responses that aim to stabilize predictive ecosystems without sacrificing epistemic rigor. Finally, the article concludes by reframing the role of artificial intelligence in arbitration as one that is moving toward constitutive interdependence—not merely as an instrument of foresight, but as a force that, once embedded in arbitral design and expectations, reshapes the very conditions of predictability it promises to deliver.

sections.title: II Predictive Infrastructures and the Architecture of Legal Forecasting
sections.text: II Predictive Infrastructures and the Architecture of Legal Forecasting Artificial intelligence systems applied to arbitration increasingly embody a class of technologies known as predictive infrastructures. These tools are currently used by parties to assess case strategies, by law firms to forecast likely outcomes, and by institutions to analyze patterns in procedural behavior.11 Julie E. Cohen, Between Truth and Power: The Legal Constructions of Informational Capitalism, 1-5; 75-107; 143-169 (Oxford University Press, 2019). These systems are built upon architectures that collect, organize, and process data from past arbitral proceedings in order to produce probabilistic inferences about future outcomes. They rely on a pipeline that integrates data acquisition, preprocessing, feature engineering, model training, and output interpretation.12 Standard machine learning pipelines are described in the literature as comprising stages such as input data, preprocessing, feature selection, and model training, which collectively transform raw data into predictive models. See: A. Narayanan et al., “Ten Technical and Legal Strategies to Mitigate AI Risks,” (June 2024) 67(6) Communications of the ACM, available at: https://dl.acm.org/doi/fullHtml/10.1145/3641525.3663630. Their technical core frequently combines natural language processing with statistical learning, using structured and unstructured inputs to identify behavioral patterns across multiple dimensions of the arbitral process. While the deployment of such infrastructures is not exclusive to arbitration, this chapter treats arbitration as a developed case study through which the epistemic consequences of predictive reasoning in law can be examined with particular clarity. The objective is not to claim that arbitration is uniquely susceptible to reflexivity, but to show how its structural features render the acceleration and redirection of legal reflexivity by predictive tools especially observable in practice. At the foundational level, predictive systems in arbitration operate through the construction of training datasets drawn from the limited corpus of publicly accessible arbitral awards, institutional rosters, and associated procedural information. Platforms such as Jus Mundi13 Jus Mundi, “Institutional Partnerships,” available at: https://jusmundi.com/en/arbitral-institutions-and-associations (accessed December 24, 2025). combine curated collections of awards, rules, treaties, and metadata with specialized AI architectures designed for legal research,14 “Data Wars: How Legal Data & Specialized AI Are the New Competitive Advantage in Arbitration,” Daily Jus, October 17, 2025, available at: https://dailyjus.com/news/2025/10/data-wars (accessed December 24, 2025). enabling analysis of variables—including seat of arbitration, tribunal composition, applicable law, procedural milestones, relief sought, and outcome—to identify patterns across that corpus.15 Daniyal Shoukat, “Using AI in International Arbitration: From Predictive Analytics to Automated Awards” (2025) 3(2) Legal Research & Analysis 613, available at https://doi.org/10.69971/lra.3.2.2025.87. The inherently fragmented and confidential nature of most commercial arbitrations, however, means that the available datasets are comparatively thin; this scarcity constrains the scope and reliability of any predictive inference. Importantly, the epistemic limitation here is not merely quantitative. Even where datasets are carefully curated and statistically robust for retrospective analysis, they remain limited in their capacity to sustain forwardlooking knowledge claims once predictive tools are integrated into the strategic environment they describe. The data capture past regularities under prior institutional and strategic conditions, but offer limited traction over how behavior evolves when predictions themselves become decisionrelevant. To address data scarcity, some systems integrate document retrieval and semantic search mechanisms akin to retrievalaugmented generation (RAG) or hybrid AI designs,16 Patrick Lewis et al., “Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks,” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2020, available at: https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401. whereby relevant documents are retrieved from a specialized repository and used to ground model outputs in observable data rather than relying solely on generic LLM training.17 For scholarly discussion of machine learning workflows applied to legal corpora and the use of structured and unstructured legal text as data inputs, see Daniel Martin Katz and John J. Nay, “Machine Learning and Law,” in Legal Informatics, Daniel Martin Katz, Ron Dolin and Michael J. Bommarito (eds) 94-98 (Cambridge University Press, 2021). Once features are extracted, models are trained to identify statistical associations between input vectors and target variables. Depending on the system’s objective, the output may be binary, such as predicting whether a party will prevail, or continuous, such as estimating the quantum of damages. Commonly-used algorithms include logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, support vector machines, and increasingly, neural networks with shallow or deep architectures. These models are evaluated through cross-validation techniques and tuned for performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and area under the ROC curve [the ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve represents the relationship between a model’s true positive rate and false positive rate, helping assess its classification performance].18 Trevor Hastie, Robert Tibshirani and Jerome Friedman, The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction (2nd edn, Springer, 2009). The optimization process does not seek to explain legal outcomes in normative terms, but to improve the predictive performance over unseen instances.19 Cary Coglianese and David Lehr, “Transparency and Algorithmic Governance” (2019) 71(1) Administrative Law Review 1. Prediction, rather than interpretation, becomes the organizing epistemic objective. This architecture presumes that arbitration exhibits patterns stable enough over time to support generalization.20 Generalization refers to a model’s ability to apply patterns learned during training to new, previously unseen data. See Google Developers, “Generalization,” Machine Learning Crash Course, available at: https://developers.google.com/machine-learning/crash-course/overfitting/generalization (accessed December 29, 2025). It assumes that the behavior of arbitrators and parties follows detectable regularities with sufficient temporal consistency to allow predictive modeling. Furthermore, it assumes that these regularities are not merely incidental but carry predictive and operational significance. This presumption is both technical and epistemological.21 Mireille Hildebrandt, Smart Technologies and the End(s) of Law: Novel Entanglements of Law and Technology (Edward Elgar Publishing, 2015). It mirrors that Jennifer Cobbe22 Jennifer Cobbe, “Legal Singularity and the Reflexive Turn in Law” in Algorithmic Regulation, Karen Yeung and Martin Lodge (eds) (Oxford University Press 2021) https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198838494.003.0011. refers to as the “legal singularity” hypothesis, whereby law is reimagined as a domain of measurable patterns and optimizable predictions, rather than deliberative reasoning. It implies that legal decisions, though formally grounded in discretionary23 In this context, the term “discretionary judgment” should not be interpreted as suggesting arbitrariness or a departure from legal certainty. Rather, it refers to the bounded interpretive space that arbitrators occupy when applying pre-existing legal norms to complex or novel factual scenarios. While this discretion is constrained by rules, precedent, and institutional expectations, it nonetheless permits variation in legal reasoning, evidentiary assessment, and procedural design. In civil law jurisdictions such as Brazil, this notion is aligned with the idea of judicial interpretation within a legally delimited field, rather than in opposition to predictability. The reference here seeks to highlight the epistemological tension between such situated, context-sensitive judgments and the inductive assumptions of predictive AI models, which abstract from variability to infer stable, generalizable patterns. judgment, can be modeled as the output of repeatable functions applied to fact-pattern configurations and procedural variables. Here, “discretionary” does not denote decisionmaking detached from law or institutional constraint. Rather, it refers to the bounded interpretive space within which legal actors apply preestablished norms to concrete facts, weigh evidence, and exercise procedural judgment. This space of interpretation, while constrained, remains irreducible to purely statistical regularities—a tension that predictive systems tend to obscure by treating variability as noise rather than as a constitutive feature of legal reasoning. However, this presumption is fragile. Unlike domains such as credit scoring or disease prediction, arbitration is a strategic, adversarial, and interpretive ecosystem. Its actors are not merely responsive to predictions but operate within a recursive loop, where adaptations are made in anticipation of how those adaptations will themselves shape future predictions. The challenge is not that law changes—legal systems are inherently reflexive—but that predictive infrastructures accelerate this reflexivity while channeling it through rationalities of efficiency, cost reduction, consistency, and convergence, rather than through deliberative justification, abstraction, or creative legal reasoning. As a result, models trained on historical data may perform well on temporally adjacent test sets but experience degradation when confronted with actors who adjust their strategies in response to the model’s inferred patterns. This degradation is not primarily a matter of technical lag or insufficient retraining, but of epistemic endogeneity: the system increasingly models a reality partially produced by its own outputs. Moreover, the integration of predictive models into legal decision-making processes entails not merely a methodological innovation, but an epistemic realignment of significant depth. Traditional legal reasoning is grounded in justificatory rationality, where conclusions are supported by articulated premises, normative coherence, and interpretative fidelity to legal sources. Decisions are legitimized through discursive justification, which organizes facts and norms within structured canons of argumentation, such as analogy, distinction, and proportionality.24 Stefano Bertea, Law and Legal Reasoning (Noting the Foundational Role of Argumentation in Legal Decision-Making), available at: https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-94-007-1078-8_17 (accessed January 5, 2026); see also S. Kloepfer, Legal Reasoning and Legal Theory (Discussing Legal Reasoning as Structured and Rationalized), available at: https://digitalcommons.osgoode.yorku.ca/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2036&context=ohlj (accessed January 5, 2026). Predictive models, by contrast, are governed by an inferential logic that privileges statistical association over normative justification.25 Sandra Wachter, Brent Mittelstadt and Chris Russell, “Why Fairness Cannot Be Automated: Bridging the Gap Between EU Non-Discrimination Law and AI” (2021) 41(1) Computer Law & Security Review 105567 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clsr.2021.105567. Their outputs emerge not from a process of legal reasoning, but from correlations extracted from past data.26 Daniel Becker and Ludmilla Campos, “Artificial Intelligence and Arbitration: A Brief Study on the Instrumentalization of Technology in Arbitral Proceedings” Privacy and Data Protection Magazine, Issue 008, https://www.europeia.pt/resources/media/documents/Para_revis%C3%A3o_2025_Ebook_DIREITO_DA_SOCIEDADE_DO_CONHECIMENTO_CMGM_n8_corre%C3%A7%C3%A3o_31jul.pdf (accessed December 20, 2025). The internal opacity of these systems often precludes any reconstruction of a reasoning path compatible with legal discourse. What arises, then, is not a failure of interpretability alone, but a deeper epistemological rupture: the substitution of conceptual understanding with pattern recognition. In this regime, the proximity between inputs and outputs is treated as a sufficient proxy for knowledge, even in the absence of normative intelligibility or argumentative consistency. Although some recent initiatives and efforts to integrate explicit reasoning traces aim to reduce this gap and bring LLM behavior closer to forms of justifiable reasoning,27 See, e.g., Anthropic, “Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback” Anthropic, 2023, https://www.anthropic.com/index/2023/05/9/constitutional-ai (accessed December 29, 2025). On broader efforts to approximate reasoning capabilities in LLMs through prompt engineering and model architecture, see Jason Wei et al., “Chain of Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models” (2022) arXiv:2201.11903; Xuezhi Wang et al., “Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models” (2022) arXiv:2203.11171; Shinn Yao et al., “ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models” (2023) arXiv:2210.03629; Oyvind Tafjord et al., “ProofWriter: Generating Implications, Proofs, and Abductive Statements over Natural Language” (2021) arXiv:2012.13048. these systems remain primarily predictive in nature, not genuinely justificatory. This reliance on predictive tools signals the rise of what may be termed an epistemology of correlation,28 The expression “epistemology of correlation” is used here as an analytic shorthand for the shift toward statistical associationbased reasoning in predictive systems. For discussions of correlational and patternbased epistemic regimes in algorithmic contexts, see Matteo Pasquinelli and Vladan Joler, “The Nooscope Manifested: AI as Instrument of Knowledge Extractivism” (2020) https://nooscope.ai (accessed January 5, 2026). in which the capacity to predict shifts the threshold of what is considered an adequate justification. Such a move is not trivial. It challenges the very structure of legal rationality, which does not merely seek outcomes but aims to produce decisions that can be contested, defended, and situated within a normative order. In such a configuration, statistical plausibility risks becoming a surrogate for legal persuasiveness, subtly reconfiguring the standards by which arguments, strategies, and procedural choices are evaluated. This misalignment has concrete consequences. While the epistemological shift reshapes the justificatory structure of legal reasoning, its effects are not merely discursive. Variables that are statistically significant within a model may be normatively irrelevant and yet end up being persuasive in practice. This effect resonates with concerns in recent empirical studies, such as McGinnis and Wasick, who caution against the risk of “spurious salience” in automated legal analytics, where statistically relevant terms or structures gain undue epistemic weight.29 John O McGinnis and Russell G Wasick, “Law’s Algorithm” (2015) 66 Florida Law Review 991, https://scholarship.law.ufl.edu/flr/vol66/iss4/2/. For instance, the use of certain legal terms, the structure of a memorial, or the nationality of the respondent may improve predictive accuracy without bearing any intrinsic relation to legal merit. As the optimization process favors features that improve performance, irrelevant or even spurious variables may be reinforced. This raises serious concerns regarding the opacity of model architecture, the interpretability of outputs, and the potential for reinforcing latent biases embedded in the historical record. A further layer of complexity arises from the way predictive outputs influence strategic decision-making. Once model recommendations become available to parties, they are no longer merely descriptive artifacts. They become behavioral signals that shape the very phenomena they aim to represent. This recursive interaction transforms the predictive system into a participant in the arbitration ecosystem.30 Holli Sargeant, Ahmed Izzidien and Felix Steffek, Topic Classification of Case Law Using a Large Language Model and a New Taxonomy for UK Law: AI Insights into Summary Judgment (University of Cambridge Faculty of Law Research Paper No. 21/2024, May 21, 2024) https://ssrn.com/abstract=4836558 (accessed December 20, 2025). Its presence introduces second-order effects that alter party behavior, tribunal expectations, and procedural choices. For example, a party informed that its case has a low predicted probability of success may choose to settle or refrain from initiating proceedings. Conversely, a favorable prediction may embolden litigation strategies that are marginal in substantive legal terms. In both cases, the predictive output generates incentives that reshape the structure of dispute resolution. Over time, as multiple actors converge upon similar tools and outputs, the behavioral field becomes increasingly oriented around shared expectations, eroding the referential independence of the predictive system—understood here not merely as a matter of data recency, but as the epistemic condition under which predictive models can operate over patterns that are externally grounded rather than reflexively constituted. This erosion alters the model’s function from describing an external behavioral field to co-producing it, thereby diminishing its reliability as an independent source of foresight. Such dynamic, central to what this chapter terms the “predictability paradox,” reflects a recursive shift in which the instrument of observation alters the object it observes—a hallmark of reflexive systems. This risk is particularly salient in arbitration, not because reflexivity is unique to this forum, but because limited data availability and the high strategic responsiveness of its actors may amplify feedback effects more rapidly than in higher-volume adjudicative settings. This recursive erosion of referential independence can be analytically captured through the architecture of feedback loops. Rather than mapping a pre-existing behavioral field, the model becomes entangled in its own effects: outputs shape inputs, and predictive inferences loop back as behavioral incentives. Over successive iterations, the system no longer passively reflects patterns—it co-produces them. In control systems theory, feedback occurs when the output of a system becomes an input that affects future outputs. In the context of arbitration, the model’s forecasts inform behavior, which alters the distribution of cases—for instance, by encouraging settlements, discouraging low-probability claims, or prompting procedural adaptations. Over time, these behavioral shifts can affect what kinds of disputes are brought forward and, consequently, what gets included in future training datasets. The concern here is not scale or ubiquity, but structure: even localized or bilateral use of predictive tools can generate recursive effects once actors adapt strategically. This does not presume widespread dissemination of model outputs or feedback loops operating at scale, but rather identifies a theoretical risk: that as predictive tools become embedded in arbitral workflows, particularly in institutional or high-volume settings, their outputs may begin to subtly reshape the behavioral field they aim to represent. In such cases, the system risks becoming endogenous to its own effects.31 Sonia Katyal, “Private Accountability in the Age of Artificial Intelligence” (2019) 66(1) UCLA Law Review. In legal traditions such as common law, normative shifts are consciously reasoned and embedded through justificatory discourse. Yet this feature is not exclusive to common law systems. The broader point concerns a fundamental characteristic of legal reasoning itself—its commitment to deliberative justification as the vehicle through which interpretive change is stabilized and rendered legitimate. Legal rationality, across different traditions, relies on discursive articulation, not merely statistical regularity, to embed shifts in meaning and precedent. In this configuration, model drift—that is, the degradation of predictive accuracy over time—becomes not an incidental problem, but a structural feature of recursive environments. The consequence is a shift from a realistic epistemology, in which the model seeks to approximate a stable external reality, to a reflexive epistemology, in which the model operates within a world that responds to its own outputs. Traditional performance metrics—such as accuracy or F1 scores measured on static test sets—remain useful tools for assessing statistical precision under controlled conditions. However, they were not designed to evaluate the normative validity of predictive outputs in legal contexts. Their function is to measure formal accuracy, not the epistemic or institutional coherence of the predictions they validate. As predictive models become endogenous to the systems they aim to represent, these metrics may continue to indicate technical success while failing to capture the erosion of normativity and deliberative structure within the legal environment.32 These metrics are designed to assess statistical correctness in terms of alignment between predicted and actual outcomes within a predefined test set. However, they do not evaluate whether the output is normatively grounded, interpretatively coherent, or epistemically valid in legal terms. In reflexive systems like arbitration, where the model’s outputs influence the very behaviors they aim to predict, a model may preserve high statistical accuracy while diverging from the deliberative standards and justificatory structures that constitute the legal domain. In this sense, performance metrics continue to measure what they were built to measure, but that measurement may become progressively disconnected from the kinds of normative validity that legal institutions are meant to uphold. A model may achieve high accuracy within a closed test set but fail to preserve its utility once deployed in an ecosystem where agents optimize against its logic. In that sense, the concern is not with the metrics themselves, but with the epistemic inferences drawn from them when models are treated as authoritative sources of legal knowledge. The challenge is not merely statistical, but ontological. The model participates in the construction of the system it aims to observe. What renders this reflexive dynamic problematic is not reflexivity itself—a feature also intrinsic to legal reasoning—but the rationalities through which prediction operates. Predictive tools tend to accelerate reflexivity along vectors of efficiency, cost-reduction, speed, and statistical convergence, rather than through deliberative practices of legal justification, normative abstraction, or creative reasoning. This shifts the axis of epistemic authority from argumentative persuasion to probabilistic alignment. As Jennifer Cobbe observes in her critique of legal singularity, such systems risk recoding law as an optimized pattern of correlations rather than a discursive, interpretive practice anchored in contestability and normative reasoning.33 Jennifer Cobbe, “Legal Singularity and the Reflexivity of Law” (2023) https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4321502 (accessed January 15, 2026). In this light, the concern is not that AI cannot handle legal reflexivity, but that it channels it through epistemologies alien to law’s justificatory commitments. Arbitration presents specific challenges in this regard. Although formally structured as a private dispute resolution mechanism, it often produces decisions that extend beyond the interests of the parties and contribute to the formation of expectations and standards in broader legal or commercial communities. This hybrid positioning—between contractual adjudication and norm-producing effects—means that the impacts of predictive modeling are not confined to efficiency concerns but extend to procedural justice, legal intentionality, and institutional legitimacy. If predictive outputs become central to strategic planning, the arbitration process may progressively absorb algorithmic rationalities that reshape human deliberation. Arbitrators, aware of how their decisions are modeled, may adjust their behavior to escape prediction or conform to what is expected according to predictive tools.34 Fabio Morosini and Michelle Ratton Sanchez Badin (eds), Reconceptualizing International Investment Law from the Global South (Cambridge University Press 2018). Legal counsel may structure arguments not for persuasive force but for model alignment.35 Ben Green and Yiling Chen, “Algorithmic Risk Assessments Can Alter Human Decision-Making Processes in High-Stakes Government Contexts” (2021) 5 Proc ACM Hum-Comput Interact CSCW2, Art. 418, 1-33 https://doi.org/10.1145/3479562. In this controlled experiment, participants exposed to risk assessment tools became significantly more risk-averse and aligned their decisions with the logic implicit in the algorithmic output—even when this conflicted with broader equity or policy goals. This dynamic illustrates how predictive systems may reshape the epistemic frames within which decisions are made, not by removing humans from the loop, but by altering the criteria perceived as rational or legitimate. A parallel concern may arise in arbitration, where arbitrators might internalize predictive outputs as proxies for institutional legitimacy or peer alignment. Similarly, legal counsels might adapt litigation strategies not primarily for persuasive force before human adjudicators, but to align filings with features known to optimize model favorability—reorienting advocacy from legal substance to statistical optimization. These behaviors introduce strategic opacity and normative distortion into what is presumed to be a deliberative legal space. The concern is not merely theoretical. In France, for instance, Loi n° 2019-222 du 23 mars 2019 inserted Article 33 into the Code de l’organisation judiciaire,36 Loi n° 2019-222 du 23 mars 2019 de programmation 2018-2022 et de réforme pour la justice (France), Journal officiel de la République française, Art. 33, available at: https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/jorf/id/JORFTEXT000038261631 (accessed January 5, 2026). prohibiting the public dissemination of statistical analyses that identify magistrates based on their rulings. This legislative measure reflects a systemic effort to preserve the autonomy of legal reasoning in the face of predictive systems that may standardize or influence adjudicative behavior. While the provision applies to the judiciary rather than to arbitration, it reveals broader anxieties about the epistemic authority of predictive analytics in legal contexts. In arbitration, where institutional safeguards are looser, and transparency is often limited, the influence of predictive tools on behavior may be even more pronounced, producing recursive effects that challenge the integrity of procedural justification. The enduring transformation lies not in the replacement of arbitrators by predictive systems, but in the slow erosion of epistemic autonomy that sustains legal decision-making. As predictive infrastructures become embedded in the procedural texture of arbitration, they do not merely support reasoning—they begin to contour the very shape of what counts as rational deliberation. This is not a matter of external interference but of internal recalibration. The arbitral process, once grounded in justificatory practices of interpretation, coherence, and normative reasoning, is progressively absorbed into a regime where probabilistic convergence supplants argumentative depth. The notion of rationality itself is re-coded: it becomes indexed not to the persuasiveness of reasons, but to the predictive alignment with prior outcomes. This shift does not imply that probabilistic outcomes are inherently unsound; rather, it raises concerns about the erosion of deliberative autonomy and the transformation of adjudication into an exercise of pattern recognition. When arbitrators anticipate or internalize modelled outcomes as benchmarks for correctness, the space for genuine disagreement, normative dissent, or context-sensitive reasoning is narrowed. Even when a prediction aligns with a reasonable outcome, the fact that it becomes epistemically privileged prior to deliberation alters the function of justification—from a process of reason-giving to a performance of model conformity. What is at stake is not only fairness but also the integrity of judgment as an independent legal act. The model thereby acquires a quasi-normative force, not by issuing binding commands, but by delimiting the plausible contours of legal rationality through statistical sedimentation. Crucially, this shift should not be misunderstood as merely a technocratic enhancement. It represents, from our perspective, an epistemic re-foundation. The arbitrator remains formally empowered yet operates within a decision space increasingly shaped by what this chapter characterizes as a technocratic configuration. In this arrangement, expert systems are not openly imposed, but rather embedded into procedural architectures that subtly constrain legal reasoning through patterns of statistical familiarity. The model risks disciplinating judgment probabilistically, shaping the perceived space of plausible decisions not through coercion, but through the silent authority of optimized regularities. This influence is neither absolute nor determinative: it operates alongside evidentiary contingencies—such as the unpredictability of witness testimony—and interacts with, rather than overrides, the discretionary and procedural fluidity that characterizes arbitration practice. Recognizing this transformation requires more than a critique of model accuracy. It requires a shift in analytical perspective, treating predictive systems as technologically mediated epistemic agents shaping the environment in which law is interpreted and applied. As argued by Latour and others, non-human actants can participate in the stabilization of knowledge within institutional fields.37 See Bruno Latour, Reassembling the Social: An Introduction to Actor-Network-Theory (OUP 2005); see also Mireille Hildebrandt, Smart Technologies and the End(s) of Law: Novel Entanglements of Law and Technology (Edward Elgar 2015). Their outputs do not merely reflect the law but constitute part of the environment in which the law is interpreted and applied. This demands new methodologies for assessing model validity, including adversarial testing, reflexivity mapping, and institutional impact analysis. It also demands a rethinking of legal epistemology in the age of artificial intelligence. The question is no longer whether machines can predict outcomes, but how their predictions reshape the very conditions under which law operates.

sections.title: III Theoretical Foundations of the Predictability Paradox
sections.text: III Theoretical Foundations of the Predictability Paradox The structural instability identified above regarding the deployment of predictive systems within arbitration does not emerge as a technical flaw or implementation failure. Rather, it reflects a deeper epistemological configuration whose logic has been critically explored across distinct, yet heuristically converging, theoretical domains. Game theory, reflexivity theory, and epistemic fragility each illuminate aspects of self-referential dynamics that help conceptualize the predictability paradox38 Brian Skyrms, The Dynamics of Rational Deliberation (Harvard University Press, 1990).—a recursive erosion of predictive validity triggered not by model failure, but by its internalization within strategic behavior. While these fields do not mirror the specific empirical traits of arbitration—such as its ad hoc structure and the privacy of outcomes, which constrain data availability and uniformity—they provide analytical tools to understand how expectations recursively shape decision environments. This allows us to frame arbitration not as an exceptionally vulnerable forum, but as a crystallized instance through which these recursive effects become particularly legible. Taken together, they expose the limits of instrumental rationality in systems where prediction modifies behavior, and delineate a critical framework through which the integration of artificial intelligence into legal ecosystems must be rethought. This section therefore articulates the theoretical underpinnings of the predictability paradox, understood here as the tension between the statistical rationalities introduced by predictive tools and the reflexive, interpretive, and strategic qualities that characterize legal reasoning. While law is reflexive by nature—evolving through feedback between doctrine, institutions, and social expectations—predictive systems accelerate this reflexivity, anchoring it in metrics of optimization such as speed, efficiency, and consistency. These logics sit in tension with the more pluralistic features of legal thought, including abstraction, dissensus, and original insight. [A] The Collapse of Classical Equilibria under ModelDriven Strategic Adaptation The use of predictive systems in arbitration inserts algorithmic reasoning into an environment already defined by strategic interdependence. Unlike contexts governed by exogenous probabilities—such as meteorological forecasting or epidemiological modeling—arbitration is composed of rational, adversarial agents whose behavior is intrinsically shaped by second-order expectations. Parties rarely make decisions in isolation; their procedural and argumentative choices are informed by anticipations of the opposing party’s strategy, the tribunal’s interpretive posture, and increasingly, the outputs of machine learning systems trained on prior disputes. Arbitrators, in turn, are not immune to these dynamics: their reasoning may be influenced by the perceived plausibility of outcomes modeled by predictive tools. What distinguishes this dynamic is not the existence of reflexivity— which is inherent to legal reasoning—but its algorithmic acceleration and channeling through statistical rationalities such as optimization, cost-efficiency, and outcome likelihood. This configuration invites a reconceptualization of prediction not as the passive extrapolation of past behavior, but as an intervention within a recursive game structure. This transformation reflects the broader theoretical shift described by scholars such as Jennifer Cobbe in the context of the “legal singularity,” in which law is reframed not as a deliberative process, but as an object of predictive optimization.39 Jennifer Cobbe, “Legal Singularity and the Reflexive Turn in Law” in Algorithmic Regulation, Karen Yeung and Martin Lodge (eds) (Oxford University Press 2021) https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198838494.003.0011. In formal terms, arbitration approximates a sequential, incomplete-information game, in which players possess asymmetrical knowledge about each other’s positions, payoffs, and procedural preferences.40 Drew Fudenberg and Jean Tirole, Game Theory (MIT Press, 1991). Predictive systems function in this context as epistemic amplifiers, reducing uncertainty by supplying probabilistic estimates on likely outcomes or favorable strategies. In classical game theory, strategic decisions are shaped by agents’ beliefs about others’ behavior under conditions of incomplete information. When probabilistic forecasts are internalized by the parties, they alter these beliefs, becoming part of the informational environment of the game. As a result, prediction ceases to be a neutral extrapolation of past behavior and becomes an active variable within the recursive structure of expectations that define strategic interaction. Such forecasts may initially appear as merely informational; however, once internalized by rational agents operating within a game-theoretic setting, they feed back into second-order beliefs, influencing strategies and, potentially, the overall dynamics of equilibrium. It is not the mere availability of information that produces this effect, but the endogenous role it plays once it enters the actors’ anticipatory reasoning. This phenomenon disturbs the classical notion of equilibrium, understood as a state in which no player has an incentive to unilaterally deviate from their chosen strategy given the strategies of others. While such an idealized state rarely materializes fully in arbitration, it remains a theoretical baseline. The incorporation of predictive outputs into the strategic environment introduces new feedback dynamics that complicate, rather than stabilize, this equilibrium logic. This recursive entanglement disturbs the classical notion of equilibrium, understood as a state in which no player has an incentive to unilaterally deviate from their chosen strategy given the strategies of others. While such an idealized state rarely materializes fully in arbitration, it remains a theoretical baseline against which distortions become analytically visible. A forecast that signals a high probability of success under a certain procedural configuration—for example, requesting bifurcation or proposing a specific arbitrator—may lead one party to pursue that path preemptively. Anticipating this, the opposing party may attempt to block such moves, challenge the composition of the tribunal, or restructure its claim. This recursive responsiveness is particularly visible in arbitration because of its strategic flexibility and the relatively smaller universe of repeat actors, such as multinational corporations or experienced counsel, who learn to optimize behavior based not only on legal norms, but also on inferred model outputs.41 Wolfgang Alschner and Dmitriy Skougarevskiy, “Mapping the Universe of International Investment Agreements” (2016) 19(3) Journal of International Economic Law https://doi.org/10.1093/jiel/jgw056. The game evolves from one centered primarily on legal argumentation to one increasingly shaped by the tactical use of forecasts. While strategic anticipation has always played a role—whether through doctrinal analysis or manually compiled case law—the integration of predictive models shifts the scale, speed, and epistemic architecture of such anticipations. The point here is not to claim that strategic anticipation is novel in arbitration—legal actors have always responded to precedent, reputation, and doctrine. Rather, what changes is the formalization of anticipation into real-time predictive infrastructure, which transforms forecasting from a legal craft into an automated layer of influence. Forecasts no longer serve merely as background information; they become operative components in the recursive adaptation of strategies, enabling actors to anticipate anticipations at a new level of intensity and precision. As strategic behavior converges around perceived patterns, the predictive model ceases to function as an instrument of observation and begins to participate actively in the configuration of the game’s payoffs. In game-theoretical terms, this process modifies the utility functions of the players. The value of a given strategy is no longer intrinsic to its legal strength, but contingent on how it aligns with the behavior induced by the model across the population of users. The system thereby loses the independence of its parameters. The rules of the game become entangled with the anticipatory logic introduced by prediction, generating endogeneity within the strategic architecture itself. Furthermore, the presence of AI-driven forecasts introduces what is often referred to in economic game theory as higher-order beliefs—beliefs about the beliefs of others. While such recursive reasoning is not exclusive to arbitration, its structural configuration—including confidentiality, procedural discretion, and limited transparency—facilitates the concrete operationalization of higher-order anticipations. The chapter thus treats arbitration as a paradigmatic case study, not because it uniquely intensifies reflexive dynamics, but because it renders them computationally tractable and institutionally legible. If a party believes that its opponent has access to the same model and will act upon it, then it must adjust its own strategy not only in response to the model’s prediction but also in anticipation of the opponent’s counter-adaptation. The recursive depth of this reasoning rapidly exceeds what traditional equilibrium concepts can accommodate. Nash equilibria, for instance, are premised on static best-response dynamics under fixed utility structures. Once those utilities become responsive to model outputs, the stability conditions collapse. The system enters a regime of dynamic reflexivity, wherein each player’s move recalibrates the model’s referential reliability42 George Soros, The Alchemy of Finance (John Wiley & Sons, 2003). and alters the strategic topography of the game. Such dynamics are not merely theoretical. In practice, large arbitration users already engage in strategy optimization informed by data analytics,43 Catherine A. Rogers and Roger P. Alford (eds), The Future of Investment Arbitration (Oxford University Press 2009). including clustering of arbitrator behavior, tribunal responsiveness to specific procedural requests, and outcome correlations based on dispute types. The insertion of predictive AI formalizes and scales this logic, offering a structured architecture for expectation management. Yet it does so at the cost of systemic volatility.
