As more actors conform to model-based reasoning, their aggregate behavior feeds back into the dataset, destabilizing the regularities that initially enabled prediction. In this sense, arbitration functions as an empirical lens through which broader epistemic concerns—such as the loss of model referential independence and the erosion of pluralistic legal reasoning—become observable. Importantly, this is not a case of noise corrupting a signal. It is a case of the signal reorganizing the system in which it operates. The predictability paradox thus manifests not because prediction fails, but because it succeeds. The model accurately captures patterns in a previous equilibrium. Once its output becomes a shared point of reference, it triggers strategic migration away from that equilibrium, which in turn dissolves the validity of the model’s premises. What was once predictive knowledge becomes a variable of distortion. The game board changes in response to the analysis of its own layout. The implication is not limited to efficiency loss or model bias. It extends to the epistemic basis of legal strategy formation itself. Predictive systems do not merely observe strategic environments—they co-construct them. Insofar as arbitration operates through adaptive interaction, the inclusion of AI forecasts adds a new, endogenous layer of transformation that resists equilibrium stabilization. This layer cannot be neutralized through better data or improved algorithms, because its effects are not contingent on technical limitations but on the recursive nature of strategic reasoning itself. [B] Reflexive Prediction and the Dissolution of the Observer–System Divide At the heart of the predictability paradox lies a distinctive epistemological tension: while law is inherently reflexive, the use of predictive tools accelerates and reorients this reflexivity through rationalities of optimization—privileging efficiency, consistency, and speed over the interpretive creativity, abstraction, and normative contestation that characterize legal reasoning. The epistemological architecture of predictive systems presumes the existence of an object of analysis that can be observed, modeled, and anticipated without fundamentally altering its nature. This presumption holds in natural systems governed by physical regularities, but falters in social and institutional environments characterized by intentionality, strategic adaptation, and the capacity for reflexive behavior. Although this dynamic can manifest across multiple domains of legal practice, arbitration—due to its strategic flexibility, limited publicity, and high data variability—serves as a particularly revealing case study. Once predictive models become embedded in the ecosystem, their outputs do not merely describe patterns. They participate in their reconstruction and, in doing so, predictive systems accelerate the reflexive dynamics already latent in legal environments, transforming them into circuits of optimization where strategic adaptation is shaped less by normative discourse and more by alignment with inferred behavioral regularities. The shift is not simply toward faster or more consistent adjudication, but toward a reconfiguration of the epistemic field itself, in which algorithmic legibility begins to supersede legal reasoning as the dominant logic of persuasion. This recursive influence is not problematic in itself—reflexivity is a core feature of legal reasoning—but becomes destabilizing when it is guided by statistical optimization rather than deliberative judgment. Such condition finds a precise conceptual framework in George Soros’ theory of reflexivity, originally developed in critique of equilibrium-based economic models—frameworks that assume market participants operate on the basis of correct expectations and that prices tend toward a stable, self-correcting balance.44 K.J. Arrow & G. Debreu, “Existence of an Equilibrium for a Competitive Economy” (1954) 22(3) Econometrica, 265-290 https://doi.org/10.2307/1907353. Soros posits that in systems involving human cognition and action, perceptions and realities are mutually constitutive. This framework helps articulate the shift from a descriptive epistemology to a generative one: prediction does not merely reflect legal reality but intervenes in its production. Crucially, this applies with particular acuity in settings like arbitration, where parties enjoy strategic autonomy and procedural discretion. Actors form expectations based on their interpretation of reality, and act upon those expectations, thereby influencing the very state of affairs they seek to comprehend. The result is a breakdown of the classical epistemic divide between subject and object. In reflexive systems, there is no external vantage point from which to observe the system without simultaneously intervening in it. This collapse of the epistemic divide entails that the very act of observing the system becomes a form of participation. This condition reveals how the stability of a model’s referential basis cannot be assumed once its outputs circulate within the decision environment. Even high-performing systems become entangled in the behaviors they seek to predict, raising new questions about institutional resilience and epistemic integrity. In such reflexive settings, any attempt to understand or predict outcomes inevitably alters the dynamics being studied, since predictive observation acquires performative force—the observer becomes part of the observed. Much like equilibrium-based economic models, which assume that market actors adjust their behavior in response to expectations that, in turn, reshape market conditions, predictive arbitration, this logic is instantiated through the operational cycle of model development, dissemination, and adoption. Once predictive outputs are disseminated and incorporated into party strategy, they alter procedural decisions,45 Daniel Becker and Ludmilla Campos, “Artificial Intelligence and Arbitration: A Brief Study on the Instrumentalization of Technology in Arbitral Proceedings” Privacy and Data Protection Magazine, Issue 008, https://www.europeia.pt/resources/media/documents/Para_revis%C3%A3o_2025_Ebook_DIREITO_DA_SOCIEDADE_DO_CONHECIMENTO_CMGM_n8_corre%C3%A7%C3%A3o_31jul.pdf (accessed December 20, 2025). claim structures, and expectations about adjudicative behavior. These altered behaviors subsequently enter the data stream, feeding future training sets and refining the model’s internal representations.46 Sonia Katyal, “Private Accountability in the Age of Artificial Intelligence” (2019) 66(1) UCLA Law Review. The system becomes reflexive not in the weak sense of being responsive to external change, but in the strong sense of recursively shaping the empirical basis upon which its own validity depends. This reflexivity dissolves the condition of observational neutrality.47 Mireille Hildebrandt, “Law as Computation in the Era of Artificial Legal Intelligence” (2018) 68 University of Toronto Law Journal. The model ceases to function as a tool of epistemic extraction and becomes a component of the environment it purports to measure. Its outputs acquire performative force. When parties rely on a model’s assessment that a particular arbitrator has historically favored early procedural bifurcation, and adjust their procedural requests accordingly, they are not merely adapting. They are also transforming the context in which such bifurcation is evaluated, interpreted, and potentially granted. The model no longer operates on stable terrain. Its referential coordinates are in flux, contingent on the recursive effects of its own predictions. Rather than treating the system as a black box to be decoded, we must acknowledge that model outputs reshape the epistemic field within which legal reasoning unfolds—a dynamic Jennifer Cobbe and others have identified as characteristic of algorithmic legal environments.48 Jennifer Cobbe, “Legal Singularity and the Reflexive Turn in Law” in Algorithmic Regulation, Karen Yeung and Martin Lodge (eds) (Oxford University Press 2021) https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198838494.003.0011. The legal ramifications of this dynamic are significant. Arbitration is premised on the autonomy of legal reasoning, which in turn depends on a distinction between substantive merit and instrumental behavior. The introduction of reflexive prediction blurs this boundary. Legal arguments are increasingly crafted not only to persuade the tribunal but also to align with perceived model logic.49 Felix Steffek, Dispute Resolution Transformed by Technology (SSRN, October 7, 2024) https://ssrn.com/abstract=4978521 (accessed December 20, 2025). While such alignment may enhance persuasiveness by increasing consistency with prevailing patterns of adjudication, it also shifts the locus of rationality. Argumentative strength becomes less a matter of normative coherence and more a matter of statistical conformity. The result is not a degeneration of the process in a moral sense, but a reconstitution of its epistemic infrastructure—one where the persuasive value of a claim is filtered through its compatibility with model-informed expectations. Reflexivity also challenges the epistemic status of model outputs themselves. In traditional inferential logic, a prediction’s value lies in its correspondence with future events. Under reflexive conditions, this correspondence becomes unstable. The act of prediction contributes to the realization or frustration of the predicted event. A forecast that a party is unlikely to succeed may discourage litigation, thereby confirming the model’s accuracy through a pathway that undermines its normative significance. This is not a trivial artifact. It introduces selection bias into the system’s empirical foundations, reinforcing outcomes that owe their occurrence to the very act of forecasting. While legal systems are themselves reflexive—evolving through interaction between rules, interpretation, and social practice—the reflexivity induced by predictive models is of a different kind. It is governed not by contestation, abstraction, or normative reasoning, but by optimization logics geared toward efficiency, speed, and statistical alignment. This orientation toward optimization—central to algorithmic rationality—is what fundamentally distinguishes this form of reflexivity from the deliberative self-correction intrinsic to law. This problem compounds over time. As models are updated and retrained on datasets shaped by earlier iterations of their own logic, they generate a form of epistemic inbreeding. The diversity of strategic behavior shrinks. The system becomes increasingly self-referential, recycling its own assumptions through successive waves of strategic conformity. In statistical terms, this can be analogized to overfitting at the level of system behavior, not model parameters. What emerges is not a failure of prediction per se, but a narrowing of epistemic horizons—a version of reflexivity that privileges alignment with past behavior over critical reassessment of legal meaning. The model performs well on data shaped by its own influence but loses relevance when confronted with novel patterns or unmodeled incentives. The result is a regime of reflexive, where epistemic reliability is mistaken for consistency with model-informed behavior, masking the progressive closure of legal imagination. Crucially, this overfitting effect does not result from a mere repetition of identical decisions, but from the recursive structure of behavior induced by prediction. As parties adjust their claims and procedural tactics to align with the patterns recognized by the model, tribunals are increasingly presented with homogenized forms of legal reasoning and procedural posture. Even when tribunals render new awards, these decisions are shaped by a narrowed field of strategic variation, reflecting a filtered version of legal pluralism. Over time, the feedback loop privileges forms of argumentation and conduct that conform to model expectations, while marginalizing unconventional or novel approaches. The model thus performs well within the closed epistemic circuit it helped to shape, but its predictive power deteriorates when faced with exogenous shifts in legal culture, emerging fact patterns, or strategies that fall outside its trained repertoire. This reflexivity has institutional implications as well. Arbitral bodies that integrate predictive tools into case management or tribunal formation processes risk entrenching feedback loops that erode the pluralism of adjudicative reasoning. If arbitrator appointments are influenced by predictive clustering of decision patterns, and arbitrators respond by modifying their behavior to escape such clustering, the system enters a phase of anticipatory distortion. Arbitrators become reflexive agents within a model-governed field, responding to reputational metrics that are in turn shaped by model-sensitive conduct.50 Mirjam Gicquello, “Artificial Intelligence and Arbitration” in The Oxford Handbook of International Arbitration, Thomas Schultz and Federico Ortino (eds) (Oxford University Press 2020). The model’s authority over the epistemic contours of the field becomes difficult to disaggregate from the normative authority of law. These recursive effects are not exclusive to arbitration, but arbitration—due to its party-driven structure, limited transparency, and high strategic malleability—renders them more empirically observable and technically tractable. It thus functions not as a uniquely exacerbated forum, but as an analytically fertile site through which the broader logics of algorithmic reflexivity can be studied. Crucially, this dynamic cannot be resolved through transparency alone. While interpretability and explainability are essential for understanding model behavior, they do not neutralize reflexivity. Even perfectly explainable models, once integrated into strategic environments, generate feedback effects that compromise the independence of their referential basis. Addressing reflexivity requires a conceptual shift from viewing prediction as a descriptive enterprise to understanding it as a constitutive force. In legal contexts, where meaning is co-produced through argument, precedent, and institutional discourse, this shift is not ancillary. It is foundational. Ultimately, the reflexive collapse of epistemic separation exposes a core vulnerability in the normative project of algorithmic integration. This vulnerability is not intrinsic to the reflexive nature of law, but to the specific modality of reflexivity fostered by algorithmic infrastructures—one that displaces interpretive variance with convergent behavioral modeling. It reveals that predictive systems do not merely operate within legal environments. They co-author the strategic and epistemic parameters of those environments. Arbitration, as a mode of legal decision-making predicated on the asymmetry of claims and the plurality of reasoning, becomes increasingly flattened under the gravitational pull of reflexive prediction. Without institutional safeguards that reintroduce noise, variability, or deliberative discontinuity, the system risks entering a regime of self-reinforcing homogeneity, in which law is displaced by algorithmically legible behavior. In this sense, arbitration serves not as an exceptional case, but as a crystallized instance of a broader phenomenon: the performativity of predictive infrastructures in shaping legal rationality itself. The challenge is to design environments where such reflexivity can be moderated, rather than blindly amplified. This does not mean that legal reasoning is ever fully detached or static. Law itself is reflexive, evolving through feedback between interpretation and institutional practice. The concern is that predictive systems intensify this reflexivity through rationalities focused less on normative justification and more on computational efficiency, thereby reconfiguring the epistemic boundaries of what counts as legally reasonable. [C] Fragility, Antifragility, and the Structural Limits of Prediction The operational success of predictive systems in arbitration—frequently quantified through internal metrics such as accuracy, precision, or model confidence—often conceals an underlying epistemic fragility. This fragility does not stem from the fact that law changes—indeed, legal evolution is a central feature of jurisprudence. It arises from the recursive entanglement between prediction and behavior, wherein AI systems intensify the reflexive character of legal decision-making while simultaneously reorienting it toward statistical coherence, efficiency, and optimization, rather than normative reasoning. The concern, therefore, is not change as such, but the structural conditions under which prediction accelerates reflexivity while narrowing the epistemic space of legal judgment. Rather, it stems from a deeper structural vulnerability to discontinuity, strategic perturbation, and model-induced instability.51 The concepts of discontinuity, strategic perturbation, and model-induced instability refer to distinct but interconnected risks emerging from the use of predictive systems in recursive legal environments. Discontinuity captures how apparently stable model performance can collapse in the face of institutional or behavioral shifts that violate structural assumptions. Strategic perturbation refers to actors who, by understanding how a model influences procedural behavior, intentionally adapt to or game the model’s outputs. Model-induced instability describes feedback loops where the model shapes the system it observes, gradually eroding the validity of its own predictions. While these terms are not directly employed in Taleb’s work, the underlying epistemic critique finds resonance in his analysis of probabilistic failure under uncertainty. The conceptual groundwork for this critique is articulated in Nassim Nicholas Taleb’s body of work, particularly in The Black Swan 52 Nassim Nicholas Taleb, The Black Swan: The Impact of the Highly Improbable (2nd edn, Random House 2010). and Antifragile,53 Nassim Nicholas Taleb, Antifragile: Things That Gain from Disorder (Random House 2012). which address the failure of probabilistic models when applied to dynamic, adversarial, and recursively conditioned environments. Taleb’s core insight lies in the distinction between robustness and fragility under conditions of uncertainty. Systems that perform reliably under expected variance are often mistaken for being resilient. However, this apparent reliability is a byproduct of optimization for known distributions. Once the system is subjected to inputs outside its calibration zone—particularly inputs generated in response to the model itself—its reliability degrades in nonlinear, and often disruptive, fashion. In Taleb’s terms, these are domains of fat-tailed risk, where extreme deviations are rare in the historical record but structurally latent in the system’s architecture. In such environments, success under normal conditions becomes a source of hidden vulnerability rather than a guarantee of stability. This epistemic fragility takes on a distinct form in legal contexts where prediction interacts with strategic behavior, institutional design, and normative fluidity. While law is reflexive by nature, predictive AI accelerates that reflexivity and channels it through logics of efficiency, cost-saving, and standardization—often in tension with the interpretive creativity and abstraction that characterize legal reasoning. The risk is not merely that prediction influences outcomes, but that it subtly recalibrates what counts as rational, persuasive, or institutionally acceptable within legal practice. The result is not merely technical brittleness but a transformation in the cognitive and procedural environment in which legal arguments unfold. Predictive models in arbitration, such as those embedded in Arbitrator Intelligence, Solomonic, or GAR Data Analytics, are typically trained on retrospective datasets comprised of resolved disputes, procedural records, and award texts. These data points reflect institutional stability, legal continuity, and strategic behavior adapted to prior normative expectations. They are, by construction, filtered through survivorship,54 Survivorship bias arises when analytical conclusions are drawn exclusively from outcomes that “survive” the selection process, while ignoring cases that fall outside the observable sample. In the context of predictive systems in arbitration, this bias manifests when models are trained only on published or accessible awards, treating them as representative of arbitral decision-making as a whole. This overlooks the fact that many disputes either do not generate awards or generate awards that remain confidential, thereby producing a distorted picture of legal reasoning, procedural trajectories, and success patterns. Although the term originates in statistical and methodological literature, its relevance to legal and arbitral datasets is well established. See David J. Hand, The Improbability Principle, Chapter 3 (Scientific American/Farrar, Straus and Giroux 2014); see also Michael R. Levy, “Big Data and Predictive Analytics in Law,” (2019) 68 American University Law Review 107, at 132-134, discussing survivorship effects in legal prediction based on incomplete datasets. resolution,55 Resolution bias refers to the systematic distortion arising from the fact that only disputes that culminate in a formal decision or award enter the observable dataset, while cases that are settled, withdrawn, or otherwise terminated prior to adjudication remain invisible. In arbitration, a significant proportion of disputes are resolved through settlement before a final award is rendered, meaning that datasets built on awards disproportionately reflect disputes that resisted settlement, involved higher stakes, or presented particular procedural or strategic characteristics. Although the term “resolution bias” is not always explicitly employed, this structural limitation is widely acknowledged in empirical arbitration scholarship. See, e.g., Tomer Broude and Anne van Aaken, “Introduction: Empirical Perspectives on International Adjudication” (2018) 31 Leiden Journal of International Law 585, at 589-591, discussing how reliance on awards systematically excludes non-adjudicated disputes and skews empirical inference. and disclosure biases.56 Disclosure bias denotes the selective visibility of arbitral awards resulting from confidentiality obligations, institutional publication policies, and party consent. Even among disputes that reach a final award, only a subset becomes publicly available or accessible through databases, often reflecting the preferences of repeat players, institutional practices, or regulatory contexts. This leads to datasets that overrepresent certain sectors, claim types, or parties, while underrepresenting others. While the expression “disclosure bias” is not always used verbatim in arbitration literature, the phenomenon is extensively documented as a core limitation of arbitral data availability. See, e.g., Gary Born, International Commercial Arbitration, Vol. II, Chapter 26, §§26.01-26.03 (3rd edn, Kluwer Law International 2021), noting that publication of awards is exceptional rather than representative, and that empirical generalization from disclosed awards must therefore be approached with caution. Although such datasets are valuable for capturing historical patterns, they are epistemically thin57 By “epistemically thin,” this chapter does not refer to datasets that are quantitatively small, incomplete, or technically deficient. The notion is not statistical, but epistemological. The point is that such datasets, while often robust for retrospective description of past regularities under prior institutional and strategic conditions, have limited capacity to ground knowledge claims about future system behavior once predictive models are deployed. In reflexive and adversarial environments, predictions themselves may reshape strategic conduct, procedural choices, and case selection, thereby altering the very conditions under which future data are generated. In this sense, the data remain descriptively rich yet forwardlooking epistemically thin, as they offer limited traction over how the system will evolve once prediction becomes endogenous to decisionmaking. with respect to future states of a system undergoing recursive transformation due to model adoption. Internal performance metrics—such as those published by platforms like Solomonic—may demonstrate statistical accuracy over retrospective datasets, but offer limited insight into how these models behave once deployed in environments that respond to their outputs. Therefore, the assumption that past correlations will generalize across time is not only empirically untested but also conceptually inadequate when applied to adversarial and self-referential settings such as arbitration. This tension is not exclusive to arbitration. However, arbitration provides a particularly salient case study for observing how recursive dynamics and strategic behavior interact with predictive outputs. The procedural flexibility, private nature, and party-driven structure of arbitration make it a revealing—though not uniquely exacerbated—forum for examining the consequences of algorithmic forecasting in legal practice. Fragility becomes particularly acute when models appear most successful. A predictive system that reliably estimates success probabilities for certain claim types or arbitrator behaviors may lead users to converge on those strategies. This convergence homogenizes procedural behavior, reduces variance, and reinforces the model’s apparent accuracy. However, this phase of high performance is precisely the point at which the system becomes most exposed to collapse. Strategic actors may identify vulnerabilities in model logic, regulators may intervene, or the tribunal may reassert discretionary authority in response to perceived instrumentalization. In each case, the model’s structural assumptions are violated, triggering failures that are not gradual degradations but sharp breakdowns in predictive coherence. What appears as epistemic strength thus masks an accumulation of latent instability. In adversarial systems such as arbitration, the effect is compounded by intentional behavior. Parties are not passive recipients of model guidance but strategic actors capable of generating adversarial inputs. Model-aware counsel may learn to frame arguments, structure pleadings, or engage in procedural maneuvering designed to exploit model sensitivities.58 Cary Coglianese and David Lehr, “Regulating by Robot: Administrative Decision Making in the Machine-Learning Era” (2017) 105 Georgetown Law Journal. This introduces a layer of adversarial drift, analogous to adversarial attacks in machine learning, where small perturbations in input data lead to significant misclassifications. In legal settings, such perturbations are not accidental. They are the product of legal engineering designed to exploit the statistical biases of predictive systems. This fragility is not solely technical. It reveals a deeper epistemological tension in the deployment of AI in legal reasoning. Model optimization operates under the logic of inductive closure: it assumes that the structure of the future will resemble the regularities of the past. However, in legal systems, interpretive creativity, institutional innovation, and strategic behavior constantly reconfigure the landscape. Arbitration, in particular, amplifies these dynamics due to its structural features. Unlike domestic courts, arbitration allows for considerable procedural flexibility, enabling parties and arbitrators to shape the conduct of proceedings, select applicable rules, and even design bespoke procedural pathways. Institutional rules are frequently updated, evidencing continuous innovation in areas such as transparency, digital tools, and emergency relief. Strategic behavior is likewise intensified, as parties engage in forum shopping, influence arbitrator appointments, and structure arguments in ways that maximize procedural advantage. These characteristics make arbitration especially susceptible to recursive effects, model-induced drift, and adversarial perturbations. A model that thrives on past data cannot, by design, anticipate structural breaks, novel legal arguments, or shifts in normative priorities. These are precisely the domains where legal judgment, as opposed to prediction, becomes indispensable. Moreover, the continued reliance on quantitative performance indicators reinforces a sense of epistemic security that is itself unfounded. Accuracy metrics, confusion matrices, or loss functions provide an illusion of control, which may, under certain circumstances, be inversely proportional to systemic resilience.59 Sandra Wachter, Brent Mittelstadt and Luciano Floridi, “Why a Right to Explanation of Automated Decision-Making Does Not Exist in the General Data Protection Regulation” (2017) 7(2) International Data Privacy Law https://doi.org/10.1093/idpl/ipx005 . As Taleb notes, the more efficient a system appears under controlled conditions, the more vulnerable it becomes to rare but devastating failures. In arbitration, such failures may take the form of procedural miscarriages, reputational erosion, or the systemic marginalization of outlier claims that fall outside the model’s scope of intelligibility. Another dimension of fragility concerns the loss of diversity within the system. As actors converge around model-sanctioned strategies, the procedural and argumentative landscape becomes narrower. This monoculture increases model confidence while reducing systemic adaptability. When unmodeled contingencies emerge, such as regulatory reforms, novel treaty interpretations, or shifts in arbitral doctrine, the predictive infrastructure lacks the cognitive flexibility to recalibrate. The epistemic ecology becomes brittle, unable to absorb shock without losing validity. Taleb’s notion of antifragility—the property of systems that gain from disorder—is instructive here, not as a prescriptive model for AI design, but as a critique of systems engineered for performance under equilibrium conditions. In a field like arbitration—and, by extension, other legal fora—resilience depends not on accuracy under past distributions but on the system’s ability to absorb surprise without epistemic collapse. Arbitration does not require systems that merely avoid failure under known risk. It requires epistemic infrastructures capable of withstanding strategic manipulation, institutional shifts, and normative redefinition. Sadly, predictive AI, in its current formulation, is not antifragile. It accumulates epistemic debt with each cycle of self-confirmation, increasing the risk and scope of future systemic breakdowns. The very mechanisms that enhance short-term predictive performance thus undermine long-term institutional resilience. This diagnosis suggests that the integration of predictive systems into arbitration cannot be guided solely by technical sophistication or empirical validation. It must include a theory of epistemic risk, attentive to the structural conditions under which prediction ceases to be a mode of insight and becomes a generator of systemic fragility. Without this perspective, the introduction of AI into arbitration may enhance local efficiencies while undermining global resilience. What begins as a project of rationalization may culminate in an epistemic monoculture—one that undermines the pluralism, creative insight, and normative fluidity that give legal reasoning its adaptive power. This sets the stage for the institutional analysis that follows, where the focus shifts from epistemic diagnosis to questions of governance, design, and normative containment. The next section turns to the institutional consequences of this shift.

sections.title: IV Institutional Mutation and the Architecture of Predictive Arbitration
sections.text: IV Institutional Mutation and the Architecture of Predictive Arbitration The integration of predictive systems into arbitration does not merely introduce new tools. It accelerates and reorients the reflexivity inherent to legal practice, channeling it through optimization-driven rationalities that restructure the institutional fabric of the procedure itself. Rather than displacing arbitrators or rewriting procedural rules, predictive infrastructures reconfigure the underlying logic of legal reasoning and decision-making. This mutation is not always visible in formal terms, but manifests through subtle shifts in how choices are made, arguments are framed, and authority is enacted. Note that while law itself is a reflexive practice—evolving through interpretation, feedback, and doctrinal innovation—the introduction of predictive systems amplifies this reflexivity through rationalities of efficiency, speed, cost reduction, and optimization. In this sense, arbitration provides a particularly illustrative case study for examining how predictive tools can channel legal reflexivity through optimization-oriented logics—including cost-efficiency, procedural speed, and outcome consistency—which may undermine the more creative and pluralistic dimensions of legal deliberation. One of the most profound effects is the procedural ossification induced by predictive modeling. Arbitration has traditionally drawn legitimacy from its procedural flexibility, allowing parties to tailor the process according to the needs of the dispute. This elasticity, however, is placed under strain when predictive systems begin to suggest optimal paths based on inferred efficiencies or statistical patterns. Parties and tribunals alike become inclined to follow what the model identifies as most probable or cost-effective. In doing so, they internalize algorithmic outputs as procedural defaults. The very space of procedural discretion becomes overcoded by prior inferences, narrowing the range of legitimate deviation. What begins as optional guidance quickly solidifies into tacit constraint.60 Mireille Hildebrandt, Smart Technologies and the End(s) of Law: Novel Entanglements of Law and Technology (Edward Elgar Publishing, 2015). This shift undermines procedural autonomy by discouraging parties from exercising legitimate procedural entitlements that deviate from model-aligned expectations. For example, a party may forgo requesting document production, the appointment of a particular arbitrator, or the application of a certain procedural rule—not because such options lack legal grounding, but because the model suggests they are unlikely to succeed or may entail reputational or financial costs. Over time, this dynamic suppresses procedural diversity and entrenches a form of strategic conformity that limits the system’s responsiveness to the needs of individual disputes. This transformation is not neutral. It redistributes epistemic power within the arbitration ecosystem. Actors with privileged access to predictive systems gain an asymmetrical advantage. Their ability to anticipate procedural developments, optimize legal arguments, and model outcome probabilities enhances their strategic positioning. This advantage is not merely tactical. It reshapes the nature of legal competition, introducing a layer of technocratic control that is not equally distributed. Parties without access to such infrastructures must operate under conditions of opacity and epistemic dependence, rendering the ideal of parity of arms increasingly fragile. This phenomenon parallels broader concerns in legal AI about informational asymmetries and epistemic monopolies, which Jennifer Cobbe and others have linked to risks of “legal singularity”—a regime where system-wide predictions begin to define legal truth. At a deeper level, predictive systems restructure the rationality of arbitration. Traditional modes of legal justification rely on structured argumentation, balancing norms and facts under a framework of deliberative reasoning. In contrast, predictive reasoning optimizes for correlation. It does not explain why an outcome is appropriate, but why it is likely. This shift from justification to prediction entails an epistemic reorientation that reverberates institutionally. Arbitrators may still issue reasoned awards, but their sense of what counts as plausible, persuasive or legitimate is shaped upstream by statistical infrastructures that delimit the field of acceptable reasoning. The tribunal is no longer the sole locus of rational deliberation. It becomes the endpoint of a process already partially determined by algorithmic guidance. This is not a matter of technological determinism. Rather, it is the product of architectural choices that embed predictive norms into legal infrastructures. These norms are rarely public or contestable in the traditional sense. Even when some degree of transparency is offered, the influence they exert often manifests in the design of upstream procedural architectures, operating beneath the threshold of juridical deliberation. They are encoded into interfaces, dashboards and automated workflows that silently influence procedural design and legal argumentation. Authority, once negotiated through interpretation and procedural equality, is displaced into architectures that do not invite deliberation.61 Tarleton Gillespie, “The Relevance of Algorithms” in Media Technologies: Essays on Communication, Materiality, and Society, Tarleton Gillespie, Pablo Boczkowski and Kirsten Foot (eds) (MIT Press, 2014). The topology of the arbitral process is thus reconfigured. What remains visible is the tribunal, the hearing, the award. What remains invisible is the predictive scaffolding that subtly governs what is likely to be argued, accepted, or decided. Responding to this transformation requires more than technical audits or ex post explanations. It demands the institutionalization of epistemic safeguards capable of reintroducing contestability and discretion into predictive environments. Arbitration must retain its capacity to surprise the model, to reject optimization in favor of fairness, and to reinterpret past patterns in light of new facts or values.62 Thomas Burri, Machine Learning and the Law: Five Theses (SSRN, January 3, 2017) https://ssrn.com/abstract=2927625 (accessed December 20, 2025). These capacities are not incidental: they are the very conditions under which legal creativity, dissent, and normative evolution remain possible in the face of accelerating optimization logics. Absent such safeguards, the risk is that predictive systems will not merely support the arbitral process but gradually redefine its very identity. In this sense, the risk is not that contestation becomes impossible—procedural and normative frameworks remain in place. Rather, it is that contestation becomes epistemically dispensable: displaced by the apparent authority of high-performing systems whose statistical reliability masks their lack of justificatory grounding. Legal reasoning, traditionally oriented toward coherence with normative sources and deliberative canons, may gradually defer to predictive outputs whose persuasive force derives not from argumentative strength, but from optimization against prior regularities.

sections.title: V Designing for Stability: Toward a Sustainable Predictive Ecosystem in Arbitration
sections.text: V Designing for Stability: Toward a Sustainable Predictive Ecosystem in Arbitration If predictive systems embedded in arbitration risk triggering recursive instability, epistemic fragility, and normative distortion, then their integration cannot proceed under the logic of pure optimization. Rather, the challenge becomes one of institutional design under epistemic tension. Sustainable deployment of artificial intelligence in arbitration requires frameworks that explicitly recognize—and strategically accommodate—the recursive, performative, and normative dynamics these systems engender.63 Felix Steffek, Dispute Resolution Transformed by Technology (SSRN, October 7, 2024) https://ssrn.com/abstract=4978521 (accessed December 20, 2025). Such a framework must preserve interpretive openness against algorithmic convergence, preventing prediction from becoming the default horizon of procedural and argumentative possibility. This is not a call for technophobic retrenchment, but for a recalibrated approach to predictive integration grounded in reflexive jurisprudence and epistemic pluralism. One axis of intervention lies in the deliberate introduction of epistemic friction into predictive infrastructures. Contrary to dominant AI development paradigms, which seek to reduce noise and maximize signal clarity, fragile legal ecosystems often benefit from controlled opacity. The inclusion of randomized variability, delayed feedback loops, or rotating model architectures can serve to dampen the effects of recursive adaptation. In game-theoretical terms, these mechanisms prevent convergence on a single dominant strategy, preserving diversity in party behavior and interpretive innovation. While this recommendation applies broadly to legal prediction, it becomes especially tractable in arbitration, where procedural flexibility allows for experimental design of this nature. In practice, this might involve suppressing visibility of certain statistical outputs, injecting synthetic perturbations into model forecasts, or staggering access across procedural stages. A second axis concerns the institutional governance of model logic. Predictive systems currently deployed in legal environments often operate as black boxes, their internal architecture opaque even to users, let alone to arbitrators or institutions. A sustainable predictive ecosystem must be structured around epistemic transparency with normative accountability. 64 Brent Mittelstadt, “Principles Alone Cannot Guarantee Ethical AI” (2019) 1(1) Nature Machine Intelligence https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-019-0114-4. This requires the establishment of model oversight bodies composed not only of data scientists and legal technologists but also of arbitrators, legal theorists, and party representatives. Their task would be to scrutinize feature selection processes, assess embedded normative assumptions, and authorize or suspend model iterations in light of procedural distortions. This kind of governance structure can act as a buffer against excessive internalization of model outputs, helping to reintroduce reflexive awareness into institutional decisions. In this configuration, the model ceases to be a unilateral source of insight and becomes a deliberative object within the broader juridical ecosystem. Equally crucial is the reassertion of interpretive autonomy within arbitral reasoning. This does not mean denying the utility of probabilistic tools but rather embedding them within institutional practices that resist over-dependence. Arbitral tribunals must remain capable of justifying departures from model-aligned outcomes without incurring reputational or procedural penalty. Awards should reflect the logic of contextual judgment, not statistical conformity.65 Joshua Kroll et al., “Accountable Algorithms” (2017) 165 University of Pennsylvania Law Review. Institutions might formalize this autonomy through procedural codes or soft law instruments that reaffirm the primacy of reasoning over optimization. In doing so, they preserve the space for original legal insight and dissensus—dimensions central to the legal imagination, yet often suppressed by predictive convergence. A fourth avenue of design involves the redistribution of epistemic power across the arbitration community. As things stand, access to advanced predictive systems is concentrated in a small subset of actors—well-resourced firms, legal tech providers, and repeat players. If these tools continue to shape procedural expectations and argumentative patterns, their asymmetrical availability raises questions of fairness, transparency, and procedural equality. A sustainable regime must ensure either broader access to such systems or procedural neutralization of their strategic advantage. This may involve institutional provisioning of base-level predictive analytics, the development of model-neutral procedural frameworks, or even the formal disclosure of algorithm-influenced strategic choices during proceedings. Absent such mechanisms, arbitration risks reinforcing systemic asymmetries that contradict its own normative commitments to party equality and deliberative fairness. Perhaps most importantly, any sustainable integration of predictive systems into arbitration must be anchored in a reconceptualization of the role of technology in legal epistemology.66 Mireille Hildebrandt, “Discrimination, DataDriven AI Systems and Practical Reason” (2021), 7(3) European Data Protection Law Review https://doi.org/10.21552/edpl/2021/3/4. Predictive AI should not be treated as an external enhancer of a pre-given process, but as a co-constitutive agent within a hybrid system of reasoning, decision-making, and strategic negotiation. This recognition enables a move beyond binary debates over automation versus human judgment. It opens space for a jurisprudence of interaction—one that treats algorithmic systems as elements of a normative ecosystem, subject to contestation, modulation, and institutional counterweight. Such a shift also demands pedagogical and cultural change. Arbitrators, counsel, and institutional actors must be equipped not merely with technical literacy but with epistemic literacy: the ability to interrogate the assumptions, limitations, and behavioral consequences of predictive systems. This form of literacy, as suggested by emerging literature on algorithmic governance, is essential to avoid the passive normalization of prediction as normative truth. Without this capacity, even the most carefully designed models risk being naturalized into the decision-making process, their outputs mistaken for evidence rather than orientation, for legal inevitability rather than statistical inference. The goal is not to restore a mythical pre-digital purity to arbitration, nor to erect regulatory firewalls that isolate it from technological evolution. Rather, it is to cultivate a stable instability or, in other words, a dynamic equilibrium in which predictive tools can operate without undermining the adaptive, plural, and interpretive foundations of the arbitral process. In such a system, prediction does not dictate outcomes but informs judgment. It does not replace deliberation but enriches it. And most importantly, it does not establish a new epistemic orthodoxy but remains subject to the legal imagination it was meant to support. Reflexivity must remain institutionally channeled through deliberative norms—not algorithmic closure.

sections.title: VI Conclusion
sections.text: VI Conclusion The rise of predictive artificial intelligence in arbitration has been accompanied by a powerful promise: that of rendering uncertainty manageable, strategy optimizable, and adjudication more consistent. This promise, however, contains within it a structural contradiction. As predictive systems become increasingly embedded in the architecture of arbitral practice, they begin to reshape the very behaviors, expectations, and epistemic conditions upon which their utility depends. Their presence alters strategic conduct, modifies procedural choices, and reframes the interpretive logic of decision-making. What was once a tool of foresight becomes a participant in the system it sought to observe. The consequence is a paradox of predictability—a self-disrupting cycle in which algorithmic success undermines its own conditions of validity. At the heart of this transformation lies a fundamental epistemological tension: law is reflexive by nature, but predictive systems accelerate this reflexivity and channel it through optimization logics that often stand at odds with the creative, plural, and deliberative character of legal reasoning. Arbitration, as this chapter has argued, serves as a particularly illustrative case study of this intensification. Its structural features—flexibility, privacy, party autonomy—render it especially transparent to the recursive dynamics introduced by algorithmic infrastructures. Yet the concern raised here is not exclusive to arbitration; it reflects a broader reconfiguration of legal epistemology under predictive rationalities. Through the lenses of game theory, reflexivity, and epistemic fragility, this article has examined how predictive models, once internalized by legal actors, generate recursive feedback loops that destabilize equilibria, collapse epistemic separation, and render systems optimized for regularity increasingly vulnerable to perturbation. The analysis has shown that the problem is not reducible to technical error or algorithmic bias. It is ontological in nature, rooted in the relational entanglement between model, behavior, and institutional form. Beyond these dynamics, the article has argued that predictive systems must be recognized as normative actors. Their outputs do not merely inform but structure the field of legal possibilities. They generate diffuse expectations, incentivize epistemic conformity, and alter the topology of procedural rationality. In doing so, they exert a form of algorithmic governance that reconfigures the foundations of arbitral legitimacy. No longer external tools, these systems become internal mechanisms of orientation, filtering what counts as plausible, efficient, or persuasive within the adjudicative process. Crucially, the intensification of reflexivity under predictive guidance displaces interpretive variance with statistical conformity. Argumentation contracts into legible templates; tribunal reasoning risks being perceived as confirmatory rather than deliberative; procedural elasticity gives way to optimization inertia. These shifts are not incidental side effects—they are manifestations of an epistemic narrowing that reshapes the conditions under which legal judgment is rendered. Ultimately, the future of arbitration in the age of predictive intelligence will not be defined solely by technical sophistication, but by the capacity of its institutions to remain epistemically plural. Prediction must remain an input, not a verdict. A heuristic, not a horizon. A companion to legal reasoning, not its surrogate. Only by maintaining this distinction—and by institutionalizing safeguards that modulate rather than amplify reflexivity—can arbitration continue to function as a forum of principled disagreement, normative experimentation, and adaptive justice within a computationally saturated legal ecosystem.

plain_text:
I Introduction: Predictive Systems and the Fragility of Anticipated Order The progressive deployment of predictive artificial intelligence in arbitration has been marked by expectations of rationalization, efficiency, and enhanced consistency.1 Gizem Halis Kasap, “Can Artificial Intelligence (‘AI’) Replace Human Arbitrators? Technological Concerns and Legal Implications” (2021) 2021(2) Journal of Dispute Resolution, available at https://scholarship.law.missouri.edu/jdr/vol2021/iss2/5 (accessed December 18, 2025). Arbitral proceedings, while institutionally designed to mitigate unpredictability through expert decision-making, remain subject to the uncertainty inherent to human judgment. In this context, algorithmic tools capable of mapping patterns, estimating probabilities of success, and recommending potentially optimal procedural choices are increasingly perceived as instruments capable of stabilizing strategic environments. These systems, grounded in extensive databases of past decisions and statistical learning, are designed to operate as epistemic infrastructures: they promise to transform indeterminacy into manageable risk, offering parties a way to anticipate outcomes and optimize strategies in light of prior behavioral patterns. However, this promise must be understood in light of a deeper structural tension: the very act of prediction reshapes the system it seeks to observe. Predictive systems do not operate in a vacuum. The recursive nature of predictive AI means that its outputs influence the behavior of the actors to whom they are addressed. That behavior, in turn, becomes part of the input of subsequent predictive cycles, introducing a structural instability into the very conditions of the system’s reliability.2 Brent Mittelstadt et al., “The Ethics of Algorithms: Mapping the Debate” (2016) 3(2) Big Data & Society 1, 3-5 https://doi.org/10.1177/2053951716679679. As parties internalize3 In the social sciences, internalization refers to the process by which external cues, norms, or informational signals become incorporated into an agent’s own reasoning or behavior—not merely as external data, but as part of their decision-making framework. For example, when individuals internalize a social norm, they adopt it as a guide for action even in the absence of external enforcement. See Internalization (sociology), Wikipedia (defining the integration of external influences into one’s own cognitive and behavioral structures) https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internalization_%28sociology%29 (accessed November 15, 2025); see also Cristina Bicchieri, Social Norms (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy) (internalization leads actors to perceive norm-abiding behavior as appropriate) https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/social-norms/ (accessed November 15, 2025). predictive outputs and adjust their conduct accordingly, the behavioral patterns that previously grounded the model’s predictive reliability, understood as its alignment with relatively stable behavioral regularities, are progressively eroded. As the referential base of the system increasingly derives from the behavioral effects produced by the model itself, a recursive dynamic emerges in which the growing adoption of, and reliance on, predictive outputs gradually reshapes the very ecosystem of behaviors and interactions the model is designed to describe and anticipate. Consequently, the stable behavioral patterns that initially supported the model’s capacity to generate reliable predictions begin to dissolve. This paradox does not emerge as a mere empirical irregularity but reflects a deeper epistemological tension concerning the conditions under which predictive knowledge can be produced and relied upon in institutional decision-making systems, such as dispute resolution, where observation, prediction, and strategic behavior are tightly interwoven. Predictive outputs do not merely describe an external reality; they circulate within an ecosystem of strategic agents, legal norms, and institutional incentives. Once such forecasts become known and acted upon, the conditions that sustained their claim to neutrality and objectivity are progressively undermined. Importantly, this recursive structure is not to be mistaken for an isolated malfunction of predictive logic. Legal reasoning itself is reflexive, evolving through iterative cycles of interpretation, precedent formation, and behavioral adjustment. What distinguishes predictive systems is not that they introduce reflexivity, but that they accelerate and reorient it. Their logics of optimization—privileging efficiency, cost-minimization, statistical coherence, and procedural convergence—increasingly define the terms under which legal reflexivity unfolds. This redirection may compress or marginalize dimensions traditionally central to juridical rationality, such as normative contestation, abstraction, and the interpretive rearticulation of legal meaning. This chapter foregrounds that tension as its central argument, proposing that the epistemic consequences of predictive modeling extend beyond the technical domain into the institutional foundations of legal judgment. Game theory offers a useful analytical lens for this discussion. In what follows, this chapter draws on game theory and related frameworks to examine how predictive analysis interacts with strategic behavior in arbitration, and how adaptive responses by parties complicate assumptions of stability and predictability in decision-making. In games of incomplete information, equilibria are rarely stable once actors begin to adapt their strategies in response to perceived patterns or expected behavior. Arbitration exemplifies this dynamic in a particularly acute form, given the combination of party-driven procedure, strategic choice of decision-makers, and limited ex ante visibility over outcomes.4 Drew Fudenberg and Jean Tirole, Game Theory (MIT Press, 1991). A model that accurately predicts certain procedural or substantive outcomes may cease to do so once such predictions are internalized by litigants and arbitrators, precisely because they are incorporated into strategic assessments and decision-making processes, thereby prompting a recalibration of the expectations and tactical assumptions that previously structured party behavior. The dynamics described above can be more clearly understood through established theoretical frameworks that address the interaction between prediction, behavior, and uncertainty in complex systems. One such framework is the theory of reflexivity advanced by George Soros,5 George Soros, The Alchemy of Finance, Chapter 1 (John Wiley & Sons, 2003). which emphasizes how feedback loops between perception and reality blur the distinction between model and world. In reflexive environments, predictive statements do not merely anticipate future states; once disclosed, they may influence behavior in ways that feed back into the system they seek to describe.6 Mike Ananny and Kate Crawford, “Seeing Without Knowing: Limitations of the Transparency Ideal and Its Application to Algorithmic Accountability” (2018) 20(3) New Media & Society 973, 976 https://doi.org/10.1177/1461444816676645. From a complementary perspective, Nassim Nicholas Taleb’s critique of model fragility7 Nassim Nicholas Taleb, The Black Swan: The Impact of the Highly Improbable (2nd edn, Random House 2010). highlights how apparent statistical robustness under normal conditions can foster excessive confidence and mask latent sources of instability. Applied to predictive systems, this critique draws attention to the risks associated with treating probabilistic outputs as stable reference points. This phenomenon is observed across multiple decision-making contexts and acquires particular institutional relevance in arbitration, given the strategic use of prediction in shaping party behavior and procedural choices, especially when underlying assumptions and limits8 This expression refers both to the assumptions embedded in the model’s construction—such as which data are selected, which variables are prioritized, and how performance is measured—and to the assumptions tacitly made by human users who interpret these outputs as meaningful. Both dimensions must be considered because epistemic risk arises not only from the technical limitations of the model itself, but also from the uncritical way its outputs are often received and acted upon within legal and strategic environments.—both those encoded in the model’s training process and those tacitly accepted by human users regarding the nature and trustworthiness of the predictions—are insufficiently scrutinized.9 Mike Ananny and Kate Crawford, “Seeing Without Knowing: Limitations of the Transparency Ideal and Its Application to Algorithmic Accountability” (2018) 20(3) New Media & Society 973, 976 https://doi.org/10.1177/1461444816676645. Throughout the chapter, arbitration is treated as a case study that allows the broader epistemic issues posed by predictive AI to become particularly visible. This is not to suggest that arbitration uniquely intensifies reflexivity, but that its procedural features, especially party autonomy, flexible procedural design, and limited public scrutiny, make it a productive forum for examining how predictive systems interact with legal reasoning in practice. Taken together, these perspectives provide a conceptual lens through which the recursive dynamics identified above can be analyzed. Rather than functioning as neutral instruments of observation, predictive tools operate within institutional environments shaped by strategic actors, legal norms, and incentive structures. The following discussion applies these insights to arbitration, examining how the increasing availability of predictive analysis may reshape strategic interaction, expectations, and the epistemic role of prediction within arbitral proceedings. This article situates this paradox—henceforth referred to as the predictability paradox—at the center of a structural analysis of artificial intelligence in arbitration. The analysis developed here is technical in a conceptual and epistemic sense, rather than in an engineering or implementation-oriented one. It does not pursue empirical validation or system design questions, but draws on epistemology, decision theory, and the philosophy of technology to examine how predictive systems generate, stabilize, and disrupt knowledge claims within institutional decision-making contexts. Predictive AI is conceptualized not as an external tool applied to a pre-existing institutional decision-making process, but as an epistemic agent embedded within a self-referential ecosystem drawing on critical accounts that emphasize the constitutive role of algorithmic systems within institutional practices10 Frank Pasquale, New Laws of Robotics: Defending Human Expertise in the Age of AI (Harvard University Press, 2020). and endowed with the capacity to alter the environment it seeks to optimize. Under this lens, the integration of predictive systems in arbitration is not merely a question of efficiency or accuracy, but a matter of institutional design, cognitive limitation, and dynamic stability. The argument proceeds in five parts. The next section examines the technical architecture and epistemological assumptions of current predictive systems in arbitration, with attention to their data foundations, model dynamics, and structural limitations. The third section articulates the theoretical foundations of the predictability paradox, drawing from game theory, reflexivity, and epistemic fragility. The fourth section explores the juridical and institutional consequences of recursive adaptation in arbitration, advancing a normative and analytical assessment of how the performativity of AI predictions may distort party incentives and procedural integrity. The fifth section outlines design-oriented responses that aim to stabilize predictive ecosystems without sacrificing epistemic rigor. Finally, the article concludes by reframing the role of artificial intelligence in arbitration as one that is moving toward constitutive interdependence—not merely as an instrument of foresight, but as a force that, once embedded in arbitral design and expectations, reshapes the very conditions of predictability it promises to deliver.

II Predictive Infrastructures and the Architecture of Legal Forecasting Artificial intelligence systems applied to arbitration increasingly embody a class of technologies known as predictive infrastructures. These tools are currently used by parties to assess case strategies, by law firms to forecast likely outcomes, and by institutions to analyze patterns in procedural behavior.11 Julie E. Cohen, Between Truth and Power: The Legal Constructions of Informational Capitalism, 1-5; 75-107; 143-169 (Oxford University Press, 2019). These systems are built upon architectures that collect, organize, and process data from past arbitral proceedings in order to produce probabilistic inferences about future outcomes. They rely on a pipeline that integrates data acquisition, preprocessing, feature engineering, model training, and output interpretation.12 Standard machine learning pipelines are described in the literature as comprising stages such as input data, preprocessing, feature selection, and model training, which collectively transform raw data into predictive models. See: A. Narayanan et al., “Ten Technical and Legal Strategies to Mitigate AI Risks,” (June 2024) 67(6) Communications of the ACM, available at: https://dl.acm.org/doi/fullHtml/10.1145/3641525.3663630. Their technical core frequently combines natural language processing with statistical learning, using structured and unstructured inputs to identify behavioral patterns across multiple dimensions of the arbitral process. While the deployment of such infrastructures is not exclusive to arbitration, this chapter treats arbitration as a developed case study through which the epistemic consequences of predictive reasoning in law can be examined with particular clarity. The objective is not to claim that arbitration is uniquely susceptible to reflexivity, but to show how its structural features render the acceleration and redirection of legal reflexivity by predictive tools especially observable in practice. At the foundational level, predictive systems in arbitration operate through the construction of training datasets drawn from the limited corpus of publicly accessible arbitral awards, institutional rosters, and associated procedural information. Platforms such as Jus Mundi13 Jus Mundi, “Institutional Partnerships,” available at: https://jusmundi.com/en/arbitral-institutions-and-associations (accessed December 24, 2025). combine curated collections of awards, rules, treaties, and metadata with specialized AI architectures designed for legal research,14 “Data Wars: How Legal Data & Specialized AI Are the New Competitive Advantage in Arbitration,” Daily Jus, October 17, 2025, available at: https://dailyjus.com/news/2025/10/data-wars (accessed December 24, 2025). enabling analysis of variables—including seat of arbitration, tribunal composition, applicable law, procedural milestones, relief sought, and outcome—to identify patterns across that corpus.15 Daniyal Shoukat, “Using AI in International Arbitration: From Predictive Analytics to Automated Awards” (2025) 3(2) Legal Research & Analysis 613, available at https://doi.org/10.69971/lra.3.2.2025.87. The inherently fragmented and confidential nature of most commercial arbitrations, however, means that the available datasets are comparatively thin; this scarcity constrains the scope and reliability of any predictive inference. Importantly, the epistemic limitation here is not merely quantitative. Even where datasets are carefully curated and statistically robust for retrospective analysis, they remain limited in their capacity to sustain forwardlooking knowledge claims once predictive tools are integrated into the strategic environment they describe. The data capture past regularities under prior institutional and strategic conditions, but offer limited traction over how behavior evolves when predictions themselves become decisionrelevant. To address data scarcity, some systems integrate document retrieval and semantic search mechanisms akin to retrievalaugmented generation (RAG) or hybrid AI designs,16 Patrick Lewis et al., “Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks,” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2020, available at: https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401. whereby relevant documents are retrieved from a specialized repository and used to ground model outputs in observable data rather than relying solely on generic LLM training.17 For scholarly discussion of machine learning workflows applied to legal corpora and the use of structured and unstructured legal text as data inputs, see Daniel Martin Katz and John J. Nay, “Machine Learning and Law,” in Legal Informatics, Daniel Martin Katz, Ron Dolin and Michael J. Bommarito (eds) 94-98 (Cambridge University Press, 2021). Once features are extracted, models are trained to identify statistical associations between input vectors and target variables. Depending on the system’s objective, the output may be binary, such as predicting whether a party will prevail, or continuous, such as estimating the quantum of damages. Commonly-used algorithms include logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, support vector machines, and increasingly, neural networks with shallow or deep architectures. These models are evaluated through cross-validation techniques and tuned for performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and area under the ROC curve [the ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve represents the relationship between a model’s true positive rate and false positive rate, helping assess its classification performance].18 Trevor Hastie, Robert Tibshirani and Jerome Friedman, The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction (2nd edn, Springer, 2009). The optimization process does not seek to explain legal outcomes in normative terms, but to improve the predictive performance over unseen instances.19 Cary Coglianese and David Lehr, “Transparency and Algorithmic Governance” (2019) 71(1) Administrative Law Review 1. Prediction, rather than interpretation, becomes the organizing epistemic objective. This architecture presumes that arbitration exhibits patterns stable enough over time to support generalization.20 Generalization refers to a model’s ability to apply patterns learned during training to new, previously unseen data. See Google Developers, “Generalization,” Machine Learning Crash Course, available at: https://developers.google.com/machine-learning/crash-course/overfitting/generalization (accessed December 29, 2025). It assumes that the behavior of arbitrators and parties follows detectable regularities with sufficient temporal consistency to allow predictive modeling. Furthermore, it assumes that these regularities are not merely incidental but carry predictive and operational significance. This presumption is both technical and epistemological.21 Mireille Hildebrandt, Smart Technologies and the End(s) of Law: Novel Entanglements of Law and Technology (Edward Elgar Publishing, 2015). It mirrors that Jennifer Cobbe22 Jennifer Cobbe, “Legal Singularity and the Reflexive Turn in Law” in Algorithmic Regulation, Karen Yeung and Martin Lodge (eds) (Oxford University Press 2021) https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198838494.003.0011. refers to as the “legal singularity” hypothesis, whereby law is reimagined as a domain of measurable patterns and optimizable predictions, rather than deliberative reasoning. It implies that legal decisions, though formally grounded in discretionary23 In this context, the term “discretionary judgment” should not be interpreted as suggesting arbitrariness or a departure from legal certainty. Rather, it refers to the bounded interpretive space that arbitrators occupy when applying pre-existing legal norms to complex or novel factual scenarios. While this discretion is constrained by rules, precedent, and institutional expectations, it nonetheless permits variation in legal reasoning, evidentiary assessment, and procedural design. In civil law jurisdictions such as Brazil, this notion is aligned with the idea of judicial interpretation within a legally delimited field, rather than in opposition to predictability. The reference here seeks to highlight the epistemological tension between such situated, context-sensitive judgments and the inductive assumptions of predictive AI models, which abstract from variability to infer stable, generalizable patterns. judgment, can be modeled as the output of repeatable functions applied to fact-pattern configurations and procedural variables. Here, “discretionary” does not denote decisionmaking detached from law or institutional constraint. Rather, it refers to the bounded interpretive space within which legal actors apply preestablished norms to concrete facts, weigh evidence, and exercise procedural judgment. This space of interpretation, while constrained, remains irreducible to purely statistical regularities—a tension that predictive systems tend to obscure by treating variability as noise rather than as a constitutive feature of legal reasoning. However, this presumption is fragile. Unlike domains such as credit scoring or disease prediction, arbitration is a strategic, adversarial, and interpretive ecosystem. Its actors are not merely responsive to predictions but operate within a recursive loop, where adaptations are made in anticipation of how those adaptations will themselves shape future predictions. The challenge is not that law changes—legal systems are inherently reflexive—but that predictive infrastructures accelerate this reflexivity while channeling it through rationalities of efficiency, cost reduction, consistency, and convergence, rather than through deliberative justification, abstraction, or creative legal reasoning. As a result, models trained on historical data may perform well on temporally adjacent test sets but experience degradation when confronted with actors who adjust their strategies in response to the model’s inferred patterns. This degradation is not primarily a matter of technical lag or insufficient retraining, but of epistemic endogeneity: the system increasingly models a reality partially produced by its own outputs. Moreover, the integration of predictive models into legal decision-making processes entails not merely a methodological innovation, but an epistemic realignment of significant depth. Traditional legal reasoning is grounded in justificatory rationality, where conclusions are supported by articulated premises, normative coherence, and interpretative fidelity to legal sources. Decisions are legitimized through discursive justification, which organizes facts and norms within structured canons of argumentation, such as analogy, distinction, and proportionality.24 Stefano Bertea, Law and Legal Reasoning (Noting the Foundational Role of Argumentation in Legal Decision-Making), available at: https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-94-007-1078-8_17 (accessed January 5, 2026); see also S. Kloepfer, Legal Reasoning and Legal Theory (Discussing Legal Reasoning as Structured and Rationalized), available at: https://digitalcommons.osgoode.yorku.ca/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2036&context=ohlj (accessed January 5, 2026). Predictive models, by contrast, are governed by an inferential logic that privileges statistical association over normative justification.25 Sandra Wachter, Brent Mittelstadt and Chris Russell, “Why Fairness Cannot Be Automated: Bridging the Gap Between EU Non-Discrimination Law and AI” (2021) 41(1) Computer Law & Security Review 105567 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clsr.2021.105567. Their outputs emerge not from a process of legal reasoning, but from correlations extracted from past data.26 Daniel Becker and Ludmilla Campos, “Artificial Intelligence and Arbitration: A Brief Study on the Instrumentalization of Technology in Arbitral Proceedings” Privacy and Data Protection Magazine, Issue 008, https://www.europeia.pt/resources/media/documents/Para_revis%C3%A3o_2025_Ebook_DIREITO_DA_SOCIEDADE_DO_CONHECIMENTO_CMGM_n8_corre%C3%A7%C3%A3o_31jul.pdf (accessed December 20, 2025). The internal opacity of these systems often precludes any reconstruction of a reasoning path compatible with legal discourse. What arises, then, is not a failure of interpretability alone, but a deeper epistemological rupture: the substitution of conceptual understanding with pattern recognition. In this regime, the proximity between inputs and outputs is treated as a sufficient proxy for knowledge, even in the absence of normative intelligibility or argumentative consistency. Although some recent initiatives and efforts to integrate explicit reasoning traces aim to reduce this gap and bring LLM behavior closer to forms of justifiable reasoning,27 See, e.g., Anthropic, “Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback” Anthropic, 2023, https://www.anthropic.com/index/2023/05/9/constitutional-ai (accessed December 29, 2025). On broader efforts to approximate reasoning capabilities in LLMs through prompt engineering and model architecture, see Jason Wei et al., “Chain of Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models” (2022) arXiv:2201.11903; Xuezhi Wang et al., “Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models” (2022) arXiv:2203.11171; Shinn Yao et al., “ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models” (2023) arXiv:2210.03629; Oyvind Tafjord et al., “ProofWriter: Generating Implications, Proofs, and Abductive Statements over Natural Language” (2021) arXiv:2012.13048. these systems remain primarily predictive in nature, not genuinely justificatory. This reliance on predictive tools signals the rise of what may be termed an epistemology of correlation,28 The expression “epistemology of correlation” is used here as an analytic shorthand for the shift toward statistical associationbased reasoning in predictive systems. For discussions of correlational and patternbased epistemic regimes in algorithmic contexts, see Matteo Pasquinelli and Vladan Joler, “The Nooscope Manifested: AI as Instrument of Knowledge Extractivism” (2020) https://nooscope.ai (accessed January 5, 2026). in which the capacity to predict shifts the threshold of what is considered an adequate justification. Such a move is not trivial. It challenges the very structure of legal rationality, which does not merely seek outcomes but aims to produce decisions that can be contested, defended, and situated within a normative order. In such a configuration, statistical plausibility risks becoming a surrogate for legal persuasiveness, subtly reconfiguring the standards by which arguments, strategies, and procedural choices are evaluated. This misalignment has concrete consequences. While the epistemological shift reshapes the justificatory structure of legal reasoning, its effects are not merely discursive. Variables that are statistically significant within a model may be normatively irrelevant and yet end up being persuasive in practice. This effect resonates with concerns in recent empirical studies, such as McGinnis and Wasick, who caution against the risk of “spurious salience” in automated legal analytics, where statistically relevant terms or structures gain undue epistemic weight.29 John O McGinnis and Russell G Wasick, “Law’s Algorithm” (2015) 66 Florida Law Review 991, https://scholarship.law.ufl.edu/flr/vol66/iss4/2/. For instance, the use of certain legal terms, the structure of a memorial, or the nationality of the respondent may improve predictive accuracy without bearing any intrinsic relation to legal merit. As the optimization process favors features that improve performance, irrelevant or even spurious variables may be reinforced. This raises serious concerns regarding the opacity of model architecture, the interpretability of outputs, and the potential for reinforcing latent biases embedded in the historical record. A further layer of complexity arises from the way predictive outputs influence strategic decision-making. Once model recommendations become available to parties, they are no longer merely descriptive artifacts. They become behavioral signals that shape the very phenomena they aim to represent.
