This recursive interaction transforms the predictive system into a participant in the arbitration ecosystem.30 Holli Sargeant, Ahmed Izzidien and Felix Steffek, Topic Classification of Case Law Using a Large Language Model and a New Taxonomy for UK Law: AI Insights into Summary Judgment (University of Cambridge Faculty of Law Research Paper No. 21/2024, May 21, 2024) https://ssrn.com/abstract=4836558 (accessed December 20, 2025). Its presence introduces second-order effects that alter party behavior, tribunal expectations, and procedural choices. For example, a party informed that its case has a low predicted probability of success may choose to settle or refrain from initiating proceedings. Conversely, a favorable prediction may embolden litigation strategies that are marginal in substantive legal terms. In both cases, the predictive output generates incentives that reshape the structure of dispute resolution. Over time, as multiple actors converge upon similar tools and outputs, the behavioral field becomes increasingly oriented around shared expectations, eroding the referential independence of the predictive system—understood here not merely as a matter of data recency, but as the epistemic condition under which predictive models can operate over patterns that are externally grounded rather than reflexively constituted. This erosion alters the model’s function from describing an external behavioral field to co-producing it, thereby diminishing its reliability as an independent source of foresight. Such dynamic, central to what this chapter terms the “predictability paradox,” reflects a recursive shift in which the instrument of observation alters the object it observes—a hallmark of reflexive systems. This risk is particularly salient in arbitration, not because reflexivity is unique to this forum, but because limited data availability and the high strategic responsiveness of its actors may amplify feedback effects more rapidly than in higher-volume adjudicative settings. This recursive erosion of referential independence can be analytically captured through the architecture of feedback loops. Rather than mapping a pre-existing behavioral field, the model becomes entangled in its own effects: outputs shape inputs, and predictive inferences loop back as behavioral incentives. Over successive iterations, the system no longer passively reflects patterns—it co-produces them. In control systems theory, feedback occurs when the output of a system becomes an input that affects future outputs. In the context of arbitration, the model’s forecasts inform behavior, which alters the distribution of cases—for instance, by encouraging settlements, discouraging low-probability claims, or prompting procedural adaptations. Over time, these behavioral shifts can affect what kinds of disputes are brought forward and, consequently, what gets included in future training datasets. The concern here is not scale or ubiquity, but structure: even localized or bilateral use of predictive tools can generate recursive effects once actors adapt strategically. This does not presume widespread dissemination of model outputs or feedback loops operating at scale, but rather identifies a theoretical risk: that as predictive tools become embedded in arbitral workflows, particularly in institutional or high-volume settings, their outputs may begin to subtly reshape the behavioral field they aim to represent. In such cases, the system risks becoming endogenous to its own effects.31 Sonia Katyal, “Private Accountability in the Age of Artificial Intelligence” (2019) 66(1) UCLA Law Review. In legal traditions such as common law, normative shifts are consciously reasoned and embedded through justificatory discourse. Yet this feature is not exclusive to common law systems. The broader point concerns a fundamental characteristic of legal reasoning itself—its commitment to deliberative justification as the vehicle through which interpretive change is stabilized and rendered legitimate. Legal rationality, across different traditions, relies on discursive articulation, not merely statistical regularity, to embed shifts in meaning and precedent. In this configuration, model drift—that is, the degradation of predictive accuracy over time—becomes not an incidental problem, but a structural feature of recursive environments. The consequence is a shift from a realistic epistemology, in which the model seeks to approximate a stable external reality, to a reflexive epistemology, in which the model operates within a world that responds to its own outputs. Traditional performance metrics—such as accuracy or F1 scores measured on static test sets—remain useful tools for assessing statistical precision under controlled conditions. However, they were not designed to evaluate the normative validity of predictive outputs in legal contexts. Their function is to measure formal accuracy, not the epistemic or institutional coherence of the predictions they validate. As predictive models become endogenous to the systems they aim to represent, these metrics may continue to indicate technical success while failing to capture the erosion of normativity and deliberative structure within the legal environment.32 These metrics are designed to assess statistical correctness in terms of alignment between predicted and actual outcomes within a predefined test set. However, they do not evaluate whether the output is normatively grounded, interpretatively coherent, or epistemically valid in legal terms. In reflexive systems like arbitration, where the model’s outputs influence the very behaviors they aim to predict, a model may preserve high statistical accuracy while diverging from the deliberative standards and justificatory structures that constitute the legal domain. In this sense, performance metrics continue to measure what they were built to measure, but that measurement may become progressively disconnected from the kinds of normative validity that legal institutions are meant to uphold. A model may achieve high accuracy within a closed test set but fail to preserve its utility once deployed in an ecosystem where agents optimize against its logic. In that sense, the concern is not with the metrics themselves, but with the epistemic inferences drawn from them when models are treated as authoritative sources of legal knowledge. The challenge is not merely statistical, but ontological. The model participates in the construction of the system it aims to observe. What renders this reflexive dynamic problematic is not reflexivity itself—a feature also intrinsic to legal reasoning—but the rationalities through which prediction operates. Predictive tools tend to accelerate reflexivity along vectors of efficiency, cost-reduction, speed, and statistical convergence, rather than through deliberative practices of legal justification, normative abstraction, or creative reasoning. This shifts the axis of epistemic authority from argumentative persuasion to probabilistic alignment. As Jennifer Cobbe observes in her critique of legal singularity, such systems risk recoding law as an optimized pattern of correlations rather than a discursive, interpretive practice anchored in contestability and normative reasoning.33 Jennifer Cobbe, “Legal Singularity and the Reflexivity of Law” (2023) https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4321502 (accessed January 15, 2026). In this light, the concern is not that AI cannot handle legal reflexivity, but that it channels it through epistemologies alien to law’s justificatory commitments. Arbitration presents specific challenges in this regard. Although formally structured as a private dispute resolution mechanism, it often produces decisions that extend beyond the interests of the parties and contribute to the formation of expectations and standards in broader legal or commercial communities. This hybrid positioning—between contractual adjudication and norm-producing effects—means that the impacts of predictive modeling are not confined to efficiency concerns but extend to procedural justice, legal intentionality, and institutional legitimacy. If predictive outputs become central to strategic planning, the arbitration process may progressively absorb algorithmic rationalities that reshape human deliberation. Arbitrators, aware of how their decisions are modeled, may adjust their behavior to escape prediction or conform to what is expected according to predictive tools.34 Fabio Morosini and Michelle Ratton Sanchez Badin (eds), Reconceptualizing International Investment Law from the Global South (Cambridge University Press 2018). Legal counsel may structure arguments not for persuasive force but for model alignment.35 Ben Green and Yiling Chen, “Algorithmic Risk Assessments Can Alter Human Decision-Making Processes in High-Stakes Government Contexts” (2021) 5 Proc ACM Hum-Comput Interact CSCW2, Art. 418, 1-33 https://doi.org/10.1145/3479562. In this controlled experiment, participants exposed to risk assessment tools became significantly more risk-averse and aligned their decisions with the logic implicit in the algorithmic output—even when this conflicted with broader equity or policy goals. This dynamic illustrates how predictive systems may reshape the epistemic frames within which decisions are made, not by removing humans from the loop, but by altering the criteria perceived as rational or legitimate. A parallel concern may arise in arbitration, where arbitrators might internalize predictive outputs as proxies for institutional legitimacy or peer alignment. Similarly, legal counsels might adapt litigation strategies not primarily for persuasive force before human adjudicators, but to align filings with features known to optimize model favorability—reorienting advocacy from legal substance to statistical optimization. These behaviors introduce strategic opacity and normative distortion into what is presumed to be a deliberative legal space. The concern is not merely theoretical. In France, for instance, Loi n° 2019-222 du 23 mars 2019 inserted Article 33 into the Code de l’organisation judiciaire,36 Loi n° 2019-222 du 23 mars 2019 de programmation 2018-2022 et de réforme pour la justice (France), Journal officiel de la République française, Art. 33, available at: https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/jorf/id/JORFTEXT000038261631 (accessed January 5, 2026). prohibiting the public dissemination of statistical analyses that identify magistrates based on their rulings. This legislative measure reflects a systemic effort to preserve the autonomy of legal reasoning in the face of predictive systems that may standardize or influence adjudicative behavior. While the provision applies to the judiciary rather than to arbitration, it reveals broader anxieties about the epistemic authority of predictive analytics in legal contexts. In arbitration, where institutional safeguards are looser, and transparency is often limited, the influence of predictive tools on behavior may be even more pronounced, producing recursive effects that challenge the integrity of procedural justification. The enduring transformation lies not in the replacement of arbitrators by predictive systems, but in the slow erosion of epistemic autonomy that sustains legal decision-making. As predictive infrastructures become embedded in the procedural texture of arbitration, they do not merely support reasoning—they begin to contour the very shape of what counts as rational deliberation. This is not a matter of external interference but of internal recalibration. The arbitral process, once grounded in justificatory practices of interpretation, coherence, and normative reasoning, is progressively absorbed into a regime where probabilistic convergence supplants argumentative depth. The notion of rationality itself is re-coded: it becomes indexed not to the persuasiveness of reasons, but to the predictive alignment with prior outcomes. This shift does not imply that probabilistic outcomes are inherently unsound; rather, it raises concerns about the erosion of deliberative autonomy and the transformation of adjudication into an exercise of pattern recognition. When arbitrators anticipate or internalize modelled outcomes as benchmarks for correctness, the space for genuine disagreement, normative dissent, or context-sensitive reasoning is narrowed. Even when a prediction aligns with a reasonable outcome, the fact that it becomes epistemically privileged prior to deliberation alters the function of justification—from a process of reason-giving to a performance of model conformity. What is at stake is not only fairness but also the integrity of judgment as an independent legal act. The model thereby acquires a quasi-normative force, not by issuing binding commands, but by delimiting the plausible contours of legal rationality through statistical sedimentation. Crucially, this shift should not be misunderstood as merely a technocratic enhancement. It represents, from our perspective, an epistemic re-foundation. The arbitrator remains formally empowered yet operates within a decision space increasingly shaped by what this chapter characterizes as a technocratic configuration. In this arrangement, expert systems are not openly imposed, but rather embedded into procedural architectures that subtly constrain legal reasoning through patterns of statistical familiarity. The model risks disciplinating judgment probabilistically, shaping the perceived space of plausible decisions not through coercion, but through the silent authority of optimized regularities. This influence is neither absolute nor determinative: it operates alongside evidentiary contingencies—such as the unpredictability of witness testimony—and interacts with, rather than overrides, the discretionary and procedural fluidity that characterizes arbitration practice. Recognizing this transformation requires more than a critique of model accuracy. It requires a shift in analytical perspective, treating predictive systems as technologically mediated epistemic agents shaping the environment in which law is interpreted and applied. As argued by Latour and others, non-human actants can participate in the stabilization of knowledge within institutional fields.37 See Bruno Latour, Reassembling the Social: An Introduction to Actor-Network-Theory (OUP 2005); see also Mireille Hildebrandt, Smart Technologies and the End(s) of Law: Novel Entanglements of Law and Technology (Edward Elgar 2015). Their outputs do not merely reflect the law but constitute part of the environment in which the law is interpreted and applied. This demands new methodologies for assessing model validity, including adversarial testing, reflexivity mapping, and institutional impact analysis. It also demands a rethinking of legal epistemology in the age of artificial intelligence. The question is no longer whether machines can predict outcomes, but how their predictions reshape the very conditions under which law operates.

III Theoretical Foundations of the Predictability Paradox The structural instability identified above regarding the deployment of predictive systems within arbitration does not emerge as a technical flaw or implementation failure. Rather, it reflects a deeper epistemological configuration whose logic has been critically explored across distinct, yet heuristically converging, theoretical domains. Game theory, reflexivity theory, and epistemic fragility each illuminate aspects of self-referential dynamics that help conceptualize the predictability paradox38 Brian Skyrms, The Dynamics of Rational Deliberation (Harvard University Press, 1990).—a recursive erosion of predictive validity triggered not by model failure, but by its internalization within strategic behavior. While these fields do not mirror the specific empirical traits of arbitration—such as its ad hoc structure and the privacy of outcomes, which constrain data availability and uniformity—they provide analytical tools to understand how expectations recursively shape decision environments. This allows us to frame arbitration not as an exceptionally vulnerable forum, but as a crystallized instance through which these recursive effects become particularly legible. Taken together, they expose the limits of instrumental rationality in systems where prediction modifies behavior, and delineate a critical framework through which the integration of artificial intelligence into legal ecosystems must be rethought. This section therefore articulates the theoretical underpinnings of the predictability paradox, understood here as the tension between the statistical rationalities introduced by predictive tools and the reflexive, interpretive, and strategic qualities that characterize legal reasoning. While law is reflexive by nature—evolving through feedback between doctrine, institutions, and social expectations—predictive systems accelerate this reflexivity, anchoring it in metrics of optimization such as speed, efficiency, and consistency. These logics sit in tension with the more pluralistic features of legal thought, including abstraction, dissensus, and original insight. [A] The Collapse of Classical Equilibria under ModelDriven Strategic Adaptation The use of predictive systems in arbitration inserts algorithmic reasoning into an environment already defined by strategic interdependence. Unlike contexts governed by exogenous probabilities—such as meteorological forecasting or epidemiological modeling—arbitration is composed of rational, adversarial agents whose behavior is intrinsically shaped by second-order expectations. Parties rarely make decisions in isolation; their procedural and argumentative choices are informed by anticipations of the opposing party’s strategy, the tribunal’s interpretive posture, and increasingly, the outputs of machine learning systems trained on prior disputes. Arbitrators, in turn, are not immune to these dynamics: their reasoning may be influenced by the perceived plausibility of outcomes modeled by predictive tools. What distinguishes this dynamic is not the existence of reflexivity— which is inherent to legal reasoning—but its algorithmic acceleration and channeling through statistical rationalities such as optimization, cost-efficiency, and outcome likelihood. This configuration invites a reconceptualization of prediction not as the passive extrapolation of past behavior, but as an intervention within a recursive game structure. This transformation reflects the broader theoretical shift described by scholars such as Jennifer Cobbe in the context of the “legal singularity,” in which law is reframed not as a deliberative process, but as an object of predictive optimization.39 Jennifer Cobbe, “Legal Singularity and the Reflexive Turn in Law” in Algorithmic Regulation, Karen Yeung and Martin Lodge (eds) (Oxford University Press 2021) https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198838494.003.0011. In formal terms, arbitration approximates a sequential, incomplete-information game, in which players possess asymmetrical knowledge about each other’s positions, payoffs, and procedural preferences.40 Drew Fudenberg and Jean Tirole, Game Theory (MIT Press, 1991). Predictive systems function in this context as epistemic amplifiers, reducing uncertainty by supplying probabilistic estimates on likely outcomes or favorable strategies. In classical game theory, strategic decisions are shaped by agents’ beliefs about others’ behavior under conditions of incomplete information. When probabilistic forecasts are internalized by the parties, they alter these beliefs, becoming part of the informational environment of the game. As a result, prediction ceases to be a neutral extrapolation of past behavior and becomes an active variable within the recursive structure of expectations that define strategic interaction. Such forecasts may initially appear as merely informational; however, once internalized by rational agents operating within a game-theoretic setting, they feed back into second-order beliefs, influencing strategies and, potentially, the overall dynamics of equilibrium. It is not the mere availability of information that produces this effect, but the endogenous role it plays once it enters the actors’ anticipatory reasoning. This phenomenon disturbs the classical notion of equilibrium, understood as a state in which no player has an incentive to unilaterally deviate from their chosen strategy given the strategies of others. While such an idealized state rarely materializes fully in arbitration, it remains a theoretical baseline. The incorporation of predictive outputs into the strategic environment introduces new feedback dynamics that complicate, rather than stabilize, this equilibrium logic. This recursive entanglement disturbs the classical notion of equilibrium, understood as a state in which no player has an incentive to unilaterally deviate from their chosen strategy given the strategies of others. While such an idealized state rarely materializes fully in arbitration, it remains a theoretical baseline against which distortions become analytically visible. A forecast that signals a high probability of success under a certain procedural configuration—for example, requesting bifurcation or proposing a specific arbitrator—may lead one party to pursue that path preemptively. Anticipating this, the opposing party may attempt to block such moves, challenge the composition of the tribunal, or restructure its claim. This recursive responsiveness is particularly visible in arbitration because of its strategic flexibility and the relatively smaller universe of repeat actors, such as multinational corporations or experienced counsel, who learn to optimize behavior based not only on legal norms, but also on inferred model outputs.41 Wolfgang Alschner and Dmitriy Skougarevskiy, “Mapping the Universe of International Investment Agreements” (2016) 19(3) Journal of International Economic Law https://doi.org/10.1093/jiel/jgw056. The game evolves from one centered primarily on legal argumentation to one increasingly shaped by the tactical use of forecasts. While strategic anticipation has always played a role—whether through doctrinal analysis or manually compiled case law—the integration of predictive models shifts the scale, speed, and epistemic architecture of such anticipations. The point here is not to claim that strategic anticipation is novel in arbitration—legal actors have always responded to precedent, reputation, and doctrine. Rather, what changes is the formalization of anticipation into real-time predictive infrastructure, which transforms forecasting from a legal craft into an automated layer of influence. Forecasts no longer serve merely as background information; they become operative components in the recursive adaptation of strategies, enabling actors to anticipate anticipations at a new level of intensity and precision. As strategic behavior converges around perceived patterns, the predictive model ceases to function as an instrument of observation and begins to participate actively in the configuration of the game’s payoffs. In game-theoretical terms, this process modifies the utility functions of the players. The value of a given strategy is no longer intrinsic to its legal strength, but contingent on how it aligns with the behavior induced by the model across the population of users. The system thereby loses the independence of its parameters. The rules of the game become entangled with the anticipatory logic introduced by prediction, generating endogeneity within the strategic architecture itself. Furthermore, the presence of AI-driven forecasts introduces what is often referred to in economic game theory as higher-order beliefs—beliefs about the beliefs of others. While such recursive reasoning is not exclusive to arbitration, its structural configuration—including confidentiality, procedural discretion, and limited transparency—facilitates the concrete operationalization of higher-order anticipations. The chapter thus treats arbitration as a paradigmatic case study, not because it uniquely intensifies reflexive dynamics, but because it renders them computationally tractable and institutionally legible. If a party believes that its opponent has access to the same model and will act upon it, then it must adjust its own strategy not only in response to the model’s prediction but also in anticipation of the opponent’s counter-adaptation. The recursive depth of this reasoning rapidly exceeds what traditional equilibrium concepts can accommodate. Nash equilibria, for instance, are premised on static best-response dynamics under fixed utility structures. Once those utilities become responsive to model outputs, the stability conditions collapse. The system enters a regime of dynamic reflexivity, wherein each player’s move recalibrates the model’s referential reliability42 George Soros, The Alchemy of Finance (John Wiley & Sons, 2003). and alters the strategic topography of the game. Such dynamics are not merely theoretical. In practice, large arbitration users already engage in strategy optimization informed by data analytics,43 Catherine A. Rogers and Roger P. Alford (eds), The Future of Investment Arbitration (Oxford University Press 2009). including clustering of arbitrator behavior, tribunal responsiveness to specific procedural requests, and outcome correlations based on dispute types. The insertion of predictive AI formalizes and scales this logic, offering a structured architecture for expectation management. Yet it does so at the cost of systemic volatility. As more actors conform to model-based reasoning, their aggregate behavior feeds back into the dataset, destabilizing the regularities that initially enabled prediction. In this sense, arbitration functions as an empirical lens through which broader epistemic concerns—such as the loss of model referential independence and the erosion of pluralistic legal reasoning—become observable. Importantly, this is not a case of noise corrupting a signal. It is a case of the signal reorganizing the system in which it operates. The predictability paradox thus manifests not because prediction fails, but because it succeeds. The model accurately captures patterns in a previous equilibrium. Once its output becomes a shared point of reference, it triggers strategic migration away from that equilibrium, which in turn dissolves the validity of the model’s premises. What was once predictive knowledge becomes a variable of distortion. The game board changes in response to the analysis of its own layout. The implication is not limited to efficiency loss or model bias. It extends to the epistemic basis of legal strategy formation itself. Predictive systems do not merely observe strategic environments—they co-construct them. Insofar as arbitration operates through adaptive interaction, the inclusion of AI forecasts adds a new, endogenous layer of transformation that resists equilibrium stabilization. This layer cannot be neutralized through better data or improved algorithms, because its effects are not contingent on technical limitations but on the recursive nature of strategic reasoning itself. [B] Reflexive Prediction and the Dissolution of the Observer–System Divide At the heart of the predictability paradox lies a distinctive epistemological tension: while law is inherently reflexive, the use of predictive tools accelerates and reorients this reflexivity through rationalities of optimization—privileging efficiency, consistency, and speed over the interpretive creativity, abstraction, and normative contestation that characterize legal reasoning. The epistemological architecture of predictive systems presumes the existence of an object of analysis that can be observed, modeled, and anticipated without fundamentally altering its nature. This presumption holds in natural systems governed by physical regularities, but falters in social and institutional environments characterized by intentionality, strategic adaptation, and the capacity for reflexive behavior. Although this dynamic can manifest across multiple domains of legal practice, arbitration—due to its strategic flexibility, limited publicity, and high data variability—serves as a particularly revealing case study. Once predictive models become embedded in the ecosystem, their outputs do not merely describe patterns. They participate in their reconstruction and, in doing so, predictive systems accelerate the reflexive dynamics already latent in legal environments, transforming them into circuits of optimization where strategic adaptation is shaped less by normative discourse and more by alignment with inferred behavioral regularities. The shift is not simply toward faster or more consistent adjudication, but toward a reconfiguration of the epistemic field itself, in which algorithmic legibility begins to supersede legal reasoning as the dominant logic of persuasion. This recursive influence is not problematic in itself—reflexivity is a core feature of legal reasoning—but becomes destabilizing when it is guided by statistical optimization rather than deliberative judgment. Such condition finds a precise conceptual framework in George Soros’ theory of reflexivity, originally developed in critique of equilibrium-based economic models—frameworks that assume market participants operate on the basis of correct expectations and that prices tend toward a stable, self-correcting balance.44 K.J. Arrow & G. Debreu, “Existence of an Equilibrium for a Competitive Economy” (1954) 22(3) Econometrica, 265-290 https://doi.org/10.2307/1907353. Soros posits that in systems involving human cognition and action, perceptions and realities are mutually constitutive. This framework helps articulate the shift from a descriptive epistemology to a generative one: prediction does not merely reflect legal reality but intervenes in its production. Crucially, this applies with particular acuity in settings like arbitration, where parties enjoy strategic autonomy and procedural discretion. Actors form expectations based on their interpretation of reality, and act upon those expectations, thereby influencing the very state of affairs they seek to comprehend. The result is a breakdown of the classical epistemic divide between subject and object. In reflexive systems, there is no external vantage point from which to observe the system without simultaneously intervening in it. This collapse of the epistemic divide entails that the very act of observing the system becomes a form of participation. This condition reveals how the stability of a model’s referential basis cannot be assumed once its outputs circulate within the decision environment. Even high-performing systems become entangled in the behaviors they seek to predict, raising new questions about institutional resilience and epistemic integrity. In such reflexive settings, any attempt to understand or predict outcomes inevitably alters the dynamics being studied, since predictive observation acquires performative force—the observer becomes part of the observed. Much like equilibrium-based economic models, which assume that market actors adjust their behavior in response to expectations that, in turn, reshape market conditions, predictive arbitration, this logic is instantiated through the operational cycle of model development, dissemination, and adoption. Once predictive outputs are disseminated and incorporated into party strategy, they alter procedural decisions,45 Daniel Becker and Ludmilla Campos, “Artificial Intelligence and Arbitration: A Brief Study on the Instrumentalization of Technology in Arbitral Proceedings” Privacy and Data Protection Magazine, Issue 008, https://www.europeia.pt/resources/media/documents/Para_revis%C3%A3o_2025_Ebook_DIREITO_DA_SOCIEDADE_DO_CONHECIMENTO_CMGM_n8_corre%C3%A7%C3%A3o_31jul.pdf (accessed December 20, 2025). claim structures, and expectations about adjudicative behavior. These altered behaviors subsequently enter the data stream, feeding future training sets and refining the model’s internal representations.46 Sonia Katyal, “Private Accountability in the Age of Artificial Intelligence” (2019) 66(1) UCLA Law Review. The system becomes reflexive not in the weak sense of being responsive to external change, but in the strong sense of recursively shaping the empirical basis upon which its own validity depends. This reflexivity dissolves the condition of observational neutrality.47 Mireille Hildebrandt, “Law as Computation in the Era of Artificial Legal Intelligence” (2018) 68 University of Toronto Law Journal. The model ceases to function as a tool of epistemic extraction and becomes a component of the environment it purports to measure. Its outputs acquire performative force. When parties rely on a model’s assessment that a particular arbitrator has historically favored early procedural bifurcation, and adjust their procedural requests accordingly, they are not merely adapting. They are also transforming the context in which such bifurcation is evaluated, interpreted, and potentially granted. The model no longer operates on stable terrain. Its referential coordinates are in flux, contingent on the recursive effects of its own predictions. Rather than treating the system as a black box to be decoded, we must acknowledge that model outputs reshape the epistemic field within which legal reasoning unfolds—a dynamic Jennifer Cobbe and others have identified as characteristic of algorithmic legal environments.48 Jennifer Cobbe, “Legal Singularity and the Reflexive Turn in Law” in Algorithmic Regulation, Karen Yeung and Martin Lodge (eds) (Oxford University Press 2021) https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198838494.003.0011. The legal ramifications of this dynamic are significant. Arbitration is premised on the autonomy of legal reasoning, which in turn depends on a distinction between substantive merit and instrumental behavior. The introduction of reflexive prediction blurs this boundary. Legal arguments are increasingly crafted not only to persuade the tribunal but also to align with perceived model logic.49 Felix Steffek, Dispute Resolution Transformed by Technology (SSRN, October 7, 2024) https://ssrn.com/abstract=4978521 (accessed December 20, 2025). While such alignment may enhance persuasiveness by increasing consistency with prevailing patterns of adjudication, it also shifts the locus of rationality. Argumentative strength becomes less a matter of normative coherence and more a matter of statistical conformity. The result is not a degeneration of the process in a moral sense, but a reconstitution of its epistemic infrastructure—one where the persuasive value of a claim is filtered through its compatibility with model-informed expectations. Reflexivity also challenges the epistemic status of model outputs themselves. In traditional inferential logic, a prediction’s value lies in its correspondence with future events. Under reflexive conditions, this correspondence becomes unstable. The act of prediction contributes to the realization or frustration of the predicted event. A forecast that a party is unlikely to succeed may discourage litigation, thereby confirming the model’s accuracy through a pathway that undermines its normative significance. This is not a trivial artifact. It introduces selection bias into the system’s empirical foundations, reinforcing outcomes that owe their occurrence to the very act of forecasting. While legal systems are themselves reflexive—evolving through interaction between rules, interpretation, and social practice—the reflexivity induced by predictive models is of a different kind. It is governed not by contestation, abstraction, or normative reasoning, but by optimization logics geared toward efficiency, speed, and statistical alignment. This orientation toward optimization—central to algorithmic rationality—is what fundamentally distinguishes this form of reflexivity from the deliberative self-correction intrinsic to law. This problem compounds over time. As models are updated and retrained on datasets shaped by earlier iterations of their own logic, they generate a form of epistemic inbreeding. The diversity of strategic behavior shrinks. The system becomes increasingly self-referential, recycling its own assumptions through successive waves of strategic conformity. In statistical terms, this can be analogized to overfitting at the level of system behavior, not model parameters. What emerges is not a failure of prediction per se, but a narrowing of epistemic horizons—a version of reflexivity that privileges alignment with past behavior over critical reassessment of legal meaning. The model performs well on data shaped by its own influence but loses relevance when confronted with novel patterns or unmodeled incentives. The result is a regime of reflexive, where epistemic reliability is mistaken for consistency with model-informed behavior, masking the progressive closure of legal imagination. Crucially, this overfitting effect does not result from a mere repetition of identical decisions, but from the recursive structure of behavior induced by prediction. As parties adjust their claims and procedural tactics to align with the patterns recognized by the model, tribunals are increasingly presented with homogenized forms of legal reasoning and procedural posture. Even when tribunals render new awards, these decisions are shaped by a narrowed field of strategic variation, reflecting a filtered version of legal pluralism. Over time, the feedback loop privileges forms of argumentation and conduct that conform to model expectations, while marginalizing unconventional or novel approaches. The model thus performs well within the closed epistemic circuit it helped to shape, but its predictive power deteriorates when faced with exogenous shifts in legal culture, emerging fact patterns, or strategies that fall outside its trained repertoire. This reflexivity has institutional implications as well. Arbitral bodies that integrate predictive tools into case management or tribunal formation processes risk entrenching feedback loops that erode the pluralism of adjudicative reasoning. If arbitrator appointments are influenced by predictive clustering of decision patterns, and arbitrators respond by modifying their behavior to escape such clustering, the system enters a phase of anticipatory distortion. Arbitrators become reflexive agents within a model-governed field, responding to reputational metrics that are in turn shaped by model-sensitive conduct.50 Mirjam Gicquello, “Artificial Intelligence and Arbitration” in The Oxford Handbook of International Arbitration, Thomas Schultz and Federico Ortino (eds) (Oxford University Press 2020). The model’s authority over the epistemic contours of the field becomes difficult to disaggregate from the normative authority of law. These recursive effects are not exclusive to arbitration, but arbitration—due to its party-driven structure, limited transparency, and high strategic malleability—renders them more empirically observable and technically tractable. It thus functions not as a uniquely exacerbated forum, but as an analytically fertile site through which the broader logics of algorithmic reflexivity can be studied. Crucially, this dynamic cannot be resolved through transparency alone. While interpretability and explainability are essential for understanding model behavior, they do not neutralize reflexivity. Even perfectly explainable models, once integrated into strategic environments, generate feedback effects that compromise the independence of their referential basis. Addressing reflexivity requires a conceptual shift from viewing prediction as a descriptive enterprise to understanding it as a constitutive force. In legal contexts, where meaning is co-produced through argument, precedent, and institutional discourse, this shift is not ancillary. It is foundational. Ultimately, the reflexive collapse of epistemic separation exposes a core vulnerability in the normative project of algorithmic integration. This vulnerability is not intrinsic to the reflexive nature of law, but to the specific modality of reflexivity fostered by algorithmic infrastructures—one that displaces interpretive variance with convergent behavioral modeling. It reveals that predictive systems do not merely operate within legal environments. They co-author the strategic and epistemic parameters of those environments. Arbitration, as a mode of legal decision-making predicated on the asymmetry of claims and the plurality of reasoning, becomes increasingly flattened under the gravitational pull of reflexive prediction. Without institutional safeguards that reintroduce noise, variability, or deliberative discontinuity, the system risks entering a regime of self-reinforcing homogeneity, in which law is displaced by algorithmically legible behavior. In this sense, arbitration serves not as an exceptional case, but as a crystallized instance of a broader phenomenon: the performativity of predictive infrastructures in shaping legal rationality itself. The challenge is to design environments where such reflexivity can be moderated, rather than blindly amplified. This does not mean that legal reasoning is ever fully detached or static. Law itself is reflexive, evolving through feedback between interpretation and institutional practice. The concern is that predictive systems intensify this reflexivity through rationalities focused less on normative justification and more on computational efficiency, thereby reconfiguring the epistemic boundaries of what counts as legally reasonable. [C] Fragility, Antifragility, and the Structural Limits of Prediction The operational success of predictive systems in arbitration—frequently quantified through internal metrics such as accuracy, precision, or model confidence—often conceals an underlying epistemic fragility. This fragility does not stem from the fact that law changes—indeed, legal evolution is a central feature of jurisprudence. It arises from the recursive entanglement between prediction and behavior, wherein AI systems intensify the reflexive character of legal decision-making while simultaneously reorienting it toward statistical coherence, efficiency, and optimization, rather than normative reasoning. The concern, therefore, is not change as such, but the structural conditions under which prediction accelerates reflexivity while narrowing the epistemic space of legal judgment. Rather, it stems from a deeper structural vulnerability to discontinuity, strategic perturbation, and model-induced instability.51 The concepts of discontinuity, strategic perturbation, and model-induced instability refer to distinct but interconnected risks emerging from the use of predictive systems in recursive legal environments. Discontinuity captures how apparently stable model performance can collapse in the face of institutional or behavioral shifts that violate structural assumptions. Strategic perturbation refers to actors who, by understanding how a model influences procedural behavior, intentionally adapt to or game the model’s outputs. Model-induced instability describes feedback loops where the model shapes the system it observes, gradually eroding the validity of its own predictions. While these terms are not directly employed in Taleb’s work, the underlying epistemic critique finds resonance in his analysis of probabilistic failure under uncertainty. The conceptual groundwork for this critique is articulated in Nassim Nicholas Taleb’s body of work, particularly in The Black Swan 52 Nassim Nicholas Taleb, The Black Swan: The Impact of the Highly Improbable (2nd edn, Random House 2010). and Antifragile,53 Nassim Nicholas Taleb, Antifragile: Things That Gain from Disorder (Random House 2012). which address the failure of probabilistic models when applied to dynamic, adversarial, and recursively conditioned environments. Taleb’s core insight lies in the distinction between robustness and fragility under conditions of uncertainty. Systems that perform reliably under expected variance are often mistaken for being resilient. However, this apparent reliability is a byproduct of optimization for known distributions. Once the system is subjected to inputs outside its calibration zone—particularly inputs generated in response to the model itself—its reliability degrades in nonlinear, and often disruptive, fashion. In Taleb’s terms, these are domains of fat-tailed risk, where extreme deviations are rare in the historical record but structurally latent in the system’s architecture. In such environments, success under normal conditions becomes a source of hidden vulnerability rather than a guarantee of stability. This epistemic fragility takes on a distinct form in legal contexts where prediction interacts with strategic behavior, institutional design, and normative fluidity. While law is reflexive by nature, predictive AI accelerates that reflexivity and channels it through logics of efficiency, cost-saving, and standardization—often in tension with the interpretive creativity and abstraction that characterize legal reasoning. The risk is not merely that prediction influences outcomes, but that it subtly recalibrates what counts as rational, persuasive, or institutionally acceptable within legal practice. The result is not merely technical brittleness but a transformation in the cognitive and procedural environment in which legal arguments unfold. Predictive models in arbitration, such as those embedded in Arbitrator Intelligence, Solomonic, or GAR Data Analytics, are typically trained on retrospective datasets comprised of resolved disputes, procedural records, and award texts. These data points reflect institutional stability, legal continuity, and strategic behavior adapted to prior normative expectations. They are, by construction, filtered through survivorship,54 Survivorship bias arises when analytical conclusions are drawn exclusively from outcomes that “survive” the selection process, while ignoring cases that fall outside the observable sample. In the context of predictive systems in arbitration, this bias manifests when models are trained only on published or accessible awards, treating them as representative of arbitral decision-making as a whole. This overlooks the fact that many disputes either do not generate awards or generate awards that remain confidential, thereby producing a distorted picture of legal reasoning, procedural trajectories, and success patterns. Although the term originates in statistical and methodological literature, its relevance to legal and arbitral datasets is well established. See David J. Hand, The Improbability Principle, Chapter 3 (Scientific American/Farrar, Straus and Giroux 2014); see also Michael R. Levy, “Big Data and Predictive Analytics in Law,” (2019) 68 American University Law Review 107, at 132-134, discussing survivorship effects in legal prediction based on incomplete datasets. resolution,55 Resolution bias refers to the systematic distortion arising from the fact that only disputes that culminate in a formal decision or award enter the observable dataset, while cases that are settled, withdrawn, or otherwise terminated prior to adjudication remain invisible. In arbitration, a significant proportion of disputes are resolved through settlement before a final award is rendered, meaning that datasets built on awards disproportionately reflect disputes that resisted settlement, involved higher stakes, or presented particular procedural or strategic characteristics. Although the term “resolution bias” is not always explicitly employed, this structural limitation is widely acknowledged in empirical arbitration scholarship. See, e.g., Tomer Broude and Anne van Aaken, “Introduction: Empirical Perspectives on International Adjudication” (2018) 31 Leiden Journal of International Law 585, at 589-591, discussing how reliance on awards systematically excludes non-adjudicated disputes and skews empirical inference. and disclosure biases.56 Disclosure bias denotes the selective visibility of arbitral awards resulting from confidentiality obligations, institutional publication policies, and party consent. Even among disputes that reach a final award, only a subset becomes publicly available or accessible through databases, often reflecting the preferences of repeat players, institutional practices, or regulatory contexts. This leads to datasets that overrepresent certain sectors, claim types, or parties, while underrepresenting others. While the expression “disclosure bias” is not always used verbatim in arbitration literature, the phenomenon is extensively documented as a core limitation of arbitral data availability. See, e.g., Gary Born, International Commercial Arbitration, Vol. II, Chapter 26, §§26.01-26.03 (3rd edn, Kluwer Law International 2021), noting that publication of awards is exceptional rather than representative, and that empirical generalization from disclosed awards must therefore be approached with caution. Although such datasets are valuable for capturing historical patterns, they are epistemically thin57 By “epistemically thin,” this chapter does not refer to datasets that are quantitatively small, incomplete, or technically deficient. The notion is not statistical, but epistemological. The point is that such datasets, while often robust for retrospective description of past regularities under prior institutional and strategic conditions, have limited capacity to ground knowledge claims about future system behavior once predictive models are deployed. In reflexive and adversarial environments, predictions themselves may reshape strategic conduct, procedural choices, and case selection, thereby altering the very conditions under which future data are generated. In this sense, the data remain descriptively rich yet forwardlooking epistemically thin, as they offer limited traction over how the system will evolve once prediction becomes endogenous to decisionmaking. with respect to future states of a system undergoing recursive transformation due to model adoption. Internal performance metrics—such as those published by platforms like Solomonic—may demonstrate statistical accuracy over retrospective datasets, but offer limited insight into how these models behave once deployed in environments that respond to their outputs. Therefore, the assumption that past correlations will generalize across time is not only empirically untested but also conceptually inadequate when applied to adversarial and self-referential settings such as arbitration. This tension is not exclusive to arbitration. However, arbitration provides a particularly salient case study for observing how recursive dynamics and strategic behavior interact with predictive outputs. The procedural flexibility, private nature, and party-driven structure of arbitration make it a revealing—though not uniquely exacerbated—forum for examining the consequences of algorithmic forecasting in legal practice. Fragility becomes particularly acute when models appear most successful. A predictive system that reliably estimates success probabilities for certain claim types or arbitrator behaviors may lead users to converge on those strategies. This convergence homogenizes procedural behavior, reduces variance, and reinforces the model’s apparent accuracy. However, this phase of high performance is precisely the point at which the system becomes most exposed to collapse. Strategic actors may identify vulnerabilities in model logic, regulators may intervene, or the tribunal may reassert discretionary authority in response to perceived instrumentalization. In each case, the model’s structural assumptions are violated, triggering failures that are not gradual degradations but sharp breakdowns in predictive coherence. What appears as epistemic strength thus masks an accumulation of latent instability. In adversarial systems such as arbitration, the effect is compounded by intentional behavior. Parties are not passive recipients of model guidance but strategic actors capable of generating adversarial inputs. Model-aware counsel may learn to frame arguments, structure pleadings, or engage in procedural maneuvering designed to exploit model sensitivities.58 Cary Coglianese and David Lehr, “Regulating by Robot: Administrative Decision Making in the Machine-Learning Era” (2017) 105 Georgetown Law Journal. This introduces a layer of adversarial drift, analogous to adversarial attacks in machine learning, where small perturbations in input data lead to significant misclassifications. In legal settings, such perturbations are not accidental. They are the product of legal engineering designed to exploit the statistical biases of predictive systems. This fragility is not solely technical. It reveals a deeper epistemological tension in the deployment of AI in legal reasoning. Model optimization operates under the logic of inductive closure: it assumes that the structure of the future will resemble the regularities of the past. However, in legal systems, interpretive creativity, institutional innovation, and strategic behavior constantly reconfigure the landscape. Arbitration, in particular, amplifies these dynamics due to its structural features. Unlike domestic courts, arbitration allows for considerable procedural flexibility, enabling parties and arbitrators to shape the conduct of proceedings, select applicable rules, and even design bespoke procedural pathways. Institutional rules are frequently updated, evidencing continuous innovation in areas such as transparency, digital tools, and emergency relief. Strategic behavior is likewise intensified, as parties engage in forum shopping, influence arbitrator appointments, and structure arguments in ways that maximize procedural advantage. These characteristics make arbitration especially susceptible to recursive effects, model-induced drift, and adversarial perturbations. A model that thrives on past data cannot, by design, anticipate structural breaks, novel legal arguments, or shifts in normative priorities. These are precisely the domains where legal judgment, as opposed to prediction, becomes indispensable. Moreover, the continued reliance on quantitative performance indicators reinforces a sense of epistemic security that is itself unfounded. Accuracy metrics, confusion matrices, or loss functions provide an illusion of control, which may, under certain circumstances, be inversely proportional to systemic resilience.59 Sandra Wachter, Brent Mittelstadt and Luciano Floridi, “Why a Right to Explanation of Automated Decision-Making Does Not Exist in the General Data Protection Regulation” (2017) 7(2) International Data Privacy Law https://doi.org/10.1093/idpl/ipx005 . As Taleb notes, the more efficient a system appears under controlled conditions, the more vulnerable it becomes to rare but devastating failures. In arbitration, such failures may take the form of procedural miscarriages, reputational erosion, or the systemic marginalization of outlier claims that fall outside the model’s scope of intelligibility. Another dimension of fragility concerns the loss of diversity within the system. As actors converge around model-sanctioned strategies, the procedural and argumentative landscape becomes narrower. This monoculture increases model confidence while reducing systemic adaptability. When unmodeled contingencies emerge, such as regulatory reforms, novel treaty interpretations, or shifts in arbitral doctrine, the predictive infrastructure lacks the cognitive flexibility to recalibrate. The epistemic ecology becomes brittle, unable to absorb shock without losing validity. Taleb’s notion of antifragility—the property of systems that gain from disorder—is instructive here, not as a prescriptive model for AI design, but as a critique of systems engineered for performance under equilibrium conditions. In a field like arbitration—and, by extension, other legal fora—resilience depends not on accuracy under past distributions but on the system’s ability to absorb surprise without epistemic collapse. Arbitration does not require systems that merely avoid failure under known risk. It requires epistemic infrastructures capable of withstanding strategic manipulation, institutional shifts, and normative redefinition. Sadly, predictive AI, in its current formulation, is not antifragile. It accumulates epistemic debt with each cycle of self-confirmation, increasing the risk and scope of future systemic breakdowns. The very mechanisms that enhance short-term predictive performance thus undermine long-term institutional resilience. This diagnosis suggests that the integration of predictive systems into arbitration cannot be guided solely by technical sophistication or empirical validation. It must include a theory of epistemic risk, attentive to the structural conditions under which prediction ceases to be a mode of insight and becomes a generator of systemic fragility. Without this perspective, the introduction of AI into arbitration may enhance local efficiencies while undermining global resilience. What begins as a project of rationalization may culminate in an epistemic monoculture—one that undermines the pluralism, creative insight, and normative fluidity that give legal reasoning its adaptive power. This sets the stage for the institutional analysis that follows, where the focus shifts from epistemic diagnosis to questions of governance, design, and normative containment. The next section turns to the institutional consequences of this shift.

IV Institutional Mutation and the Architecture of Predictive Arbitration The integration of predictive systems into arbitration does not merely introduce new tools. It accelerates and reorients the reflexivity inherent to legal practice, channeling it through optimization-driven rationalities that restructure the institutional fabric of the procedure itself. Rather than displacing arbitrators or rewriting procedural rules, predictive infrastructures reconfigure the underlying logic of legal reasoning and decision-making. This mutation is not always visible in formal terms, but manifests through subtle shifts in how choices are made, arguments are framed, and authority is enacted. Note that while law itself is a reflexive practice—evolving through interpretation, feedback, and doctrinal innovation—the introduction of predictive systems amplifies this reflexivity through rationalities of efficiency, speed, cost reduction, and optimization. In this sense, arbitration provides a particularly illustrative case study for examining how predictive tools can channel legal reflexivity through optimization-oriented logics—including cost-efficiency, procedural speed, and outcome consistency—which may undermine the more creative and pluralistic dimensions of legal deliberation. One of the most profound effects is the procedural ossification induced by predictive modeling. Arbitration has traditionally drawn legitimacy from its procedural flexibility, allowing parties to tailor the process according to the needs of the dispute. This elasticity, however, is placed under strain when predictive systems begin to suggest optimal paths based on inferred efficiencies or statistical patterns. Parties and tribunals alike become inclined to follow what the model identifies as most probable or cost-effective. In doing so, they internalize algorithmic outputs as procedural defaults. The very space of procedural discretion becomes overcoded by prior inferences, narrowing the range of legitimate deviation. What begins as optional guidance quickly solidifies into tacit constraint.60 Mireille Hildebrandt, Smart Technologies and the End(s) of Law: Novel Entanglements of Law and Technology (Edward Elgar Publishing, 2015). This shift undermines procedural autonomy by discouraging parties from exercising legitimate procedural entitlements that deviate from model-aligned expectations. For example, a party may forgo requesting document production, the appointment of a particular arbitrator, or the application of a certain procedural rule—not because such options lack legal grounding, but because the model suggests they are unlikely to succeed or may entail reputational or financial costs. Over time, this dynamic suppresses procedural diversity and entrenches a form of strategic conformity that limits the system’s responsiveness to the needs of individual disputes. This transformation is not neutral. It redistributes epistemic power within the arbitration ecosystem. Actors with privileged access to predictive systems gain an asymmetrical advantage. Their ability to anticipate procedural developments, optimize legal arguments, and model outcome probabilities enhances their strategic positioning. This advantage is not merely tactical. It reshapes the nature of legal competition, introducing a layer of technocratic control that is not equally distributed. Parties without access to such infrastructures must operate under conditions of opacity and epistemic dependence, rendering the ideal of parity of arms increasingly fragile. This phenomenon parallels broader concerns in legal AI about informational asymmetries and epistemic monopolies, which Jennifer Cobbe and others have linked to risks of “legal singularity”—a regime where system-wide predictions begin to define legal truth. At a deeper level, predictive systems restructure the rationality of arbitration. Traditional modes of legal justification rely on structured argumentation, balancing norms and facts under a framework of deliberative reasoning. In contrast, predictive reasoning optimizes for correlation. It does not explain why an outcome is appropriate, but why it is likely. This shift from justification to prediction entails an epistemic reorientation that reverberates institutionally. Arbitrators may still issue reasoned awards, but their sense of what counts as plausible, persuasive or legitimate is shaped upstream by statistical infrastructures that delimit the field of acceptable reasoning. The tribunal is no longer the sole locus of rational deliberation. It becomes the endpoint of a process already partially determined by algorithmic guidance. This is not a matter of technological determinism. Rather, it is the product of architectural choices that embed predictive norms into legal infrastructures. These norms are rarely public or contestable in the traditional sense. Even when some degree of transparency is offered, the influence they exert often manifests in the design of upstream procedural architectures, operating beneath the threshold of juridical deliberation. They are encoded into interfaces, dashboards and automated workflows that silently influence procedural design and legal argumentation. Authority, once negotiated through interpretation and procedural equality, is displaced into architectures that do not invite deliberation.61 Tarleton Gillespie, “The Relevance of Algorithms” in Media Technologies: Essays on Communication, Materiality, and Society, Tarleton Gillespie, Pablo Boczkowski and Kirsten Foot (eds) (MIT Press, 2014). The topology of the arbitral process is thus reconfigured. What remains visible is the tribunal, the hearing, the award. What remains invisible is the predictive scaffolding that subtly governs what is likely to be argued, accepted, or decided. Responding to this transformation requires more than technical audits or ex post explanations. It demands the institutionalization of epistemic safeguards capable of reintroducing contestability and discretion into predictive environments. Arbitration must retain its capacity to surprise the model, to reject optimization in favor of fairness, and to reinterpret past patterns in light of new facts or values.62 Thomas Burri, Machine Learning and the Law: Five Theses (SSRN, January 3, 2017) https://ssrn.com/abstract=2927625 (accessed December 20, 2025). These capacities are not incidental: they are the very conditions under which legal creativity, dissent, and normative evolution remain possible in the face of accelerating optimization logics. Absent such safeguards, the risk is that predictive systems will not merely support the arbitral process but gradually redefine its very identity. In this sense, the risk is not that contestation becomes impossible—procedural and normative frameworks remain in place. Rather, it is that contestation becomes epistemically dispensable: displaced by the apparent authority of high-performing systems whose statistical reliability masks their lack of justificatory grounding. Legal reasoning, traditionally oriented toward coherence with normative sources and deliberative canons, may gradually defer to predictive outputs whose persuasive force derives not from argumentative strength, but from optimization against prior regularities.

V Designing for Stability: Toward a Sustainable Predictive Ecosystem in Arbitration If predictive systems embedded in arbitration risk triggering recursive instability, epistemic fragility, and normative distortion, then their integration cannot proceed under the logic of pure optimization. Rather, the challenge becomes one of institutional design under epistemic tension. Sustainable deployment of artificial intelligence in arbitration requires frameworks that explicitly recognize—and strategically accommodate—the recursive, performative, and normative dynamics these systems engender.63 Felix Steffek, Dispute Resolution Transformed by Technology (SSRN, October 7, 2024) https://ssrn.com/abstract=4978521 (accessed December 20, 2025). Such a framework must preserve interpretive openness against algorithmic convergence, preventing prediction from becoming the default horizon of procedural and argumentative possibility. This is not a call for technophobic retrenchment, but for a recalibrated approach to predictive integration grounded in reflexive jurisprudence and epistemic pluralism. One axis of intervention lies in the deliberate introduction of epistemic friction into predictive infrastructures. Contrary to dominant AI development paradigms, which seek to reduce noise and maximize signal clarity, fragile legal ecosystems often benefit from controlled opacity. The inclusion of randomized variability, delayed feedback loops, or rotating model architectures can serve to dampen the effects of recursive adaptation. In game-theoretical terms, these mechanisms prevent convergence on a single dominant strategy, preserving diversity in party behavior and interpretive innovation. While this recommendation applies broadly to legal prediction, it becomes especially tractable in arbitration, where procedural flexibility allows for experimental design of this nature. In practice, this might involve suppressing visibility of certain statistical outputs, injecting synthetic perturbations into model forecasts, or staggering access across procedural stages. A second axis concerns the institutional governance of model logic. Predictive systems currently deployed in legal environments often operate as black boxes, their internal architecture opaque even to users, let alone to arbitrators or institutions. A sustainable predictive ecosystem must be structured around epistemic transparency with normative accountability. 64 Brent Mittelstadt, “Principles Alone Cannot Guarantee Ethical AI” (2019) 1(1) Nature Machine Intelligence https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-019-0114-4. This requires the establishment of model oversight bodies composed not only of data scientists and legal technologists but also of arbitrators, legal theorists, and party representatives. Their task would be to scrutinize feature selection processes, assess embedded normative assumptions, and authorize or suspend model iterations in light of procedural distortions. This kind of governance structure can act as a buffer against excessive internalization of model outputs, helping to reintroduce reflexive awareness into institutional decisions. In this configuration, the model ceases to be a unilateral source of insight and becomes a deliberative object within the broader juridical ecosystem. Equally crucial is the reassertion of interpretive autonomy within arbitral reasoning. This does not mean denying the utility of probabilistic tools but rather embedding them within institutional practices that resist over-dependence. Arbitral tribunals must remain capable of justifying departures from model-aligned outcomes without incurring reputational or procedural penalty. Awards should reflect the logic of contextual judgment, not statistical conformity.65 Joshua Kroll et al., “Accountable Algorithms” (2017) 165 University of Pennsylvania Law Review. Institutions might formalize this autonomy through procedural codes or soft law instruments that reaffirm the primacy of reasoning over optimization. In doing so, they preserve the space for original legal insight and dissensus—dimensions central to the legal imagination, yet often suppressed by predictive convergence. A fourth avenue of design involves the redistribution of epistemic power across the arbitration community. As things stand, access to advanced predictive systems is concentrated in a small subset of actors—well-resourced firms, legal tech providers, and repeat players. If these tools continue to shape procedural expectations and argumentative patterns, their asymmetrical availability raises questions of fairness, transparency, and procedural equality. A sustainable regime must ensure either broader access to such systems or procedural neutralization of their strategic advantage. This may involve institutional provisioning of base-level predictive analytics, the development of model-neutral procedural frameworks, or even the formal disclosure of algorithm-influenced strategic choices during proceedings. Absent such mechanisms, arbitration risks reinforcing systemic asymmetries that contradict its own normative commitments to party equality and deliberative fairness. Perhaps most importantly, any sustainable integration of predictive systems into arbitration must be anchored in a reconceptualization of the role of technology in legal epistemology.66 Mireille Hildebrandt, “Discrimination, DataDriven AI Systems and Practical Reason” (2021), 7(3) European Data Protection Law Review https://doi.org/10.21552/edpl/2021/3/4. Predictive AI should not be treated as an external enhancer of a pre-given process, but as a co-constitutive agent within a hybrid system of reasoning, decision-making, and strategic negotiation. This recognition enables a move beyond binary debates over automation versus human judgment. It opens space for a jurisprudence of interaction—one that treats algorithmic systems as elements of a normative ecosystem, subject to contestation, modulation, and institutional counterweight. Such a shift also demands pedagogical and cultural change. Arbitrators, counsel, and institutional actors must be equipped not merely with technical literacy but with epistemic literacy: the ability to interrogate the assumptions, limitations, and behavioral consequences of predictive systems. This form of literacy, as suggested by emerging literature on algorithmic governance, is essential to avoid the passive normalization of prediction as normative truth. Without this capacity, even the most carefully designed models risk being naturalized into the decision-making process, their outputs mistaken for evidence rather than orientation, for legal inevitability rather than statistical inference. The goal is not to restore a mythical pre-digital purity to arbitration, nor to erect regulatory firewalls that isolate it from technological evolution. Rather, it is to cultivate a stable instability or, in other words, a dynamic equilibrium in which predictive tools can operate without undermining the adaptive, plural, and interpretive foundations of the arbitral process. In such a system, prediction does not dictate outcomes but informs judgment. It does not replace deliberation but enriches it. And most importantly, it does not establish a new epistemic orthodoxy but remains subject to the legal imagination it was meant to support. Reflexivity must remain institutionally channeled through deliberative norms—not algorithmic closure.

VI Conclusion The rise of predictive artificial intelligence in arbitration has been accompanied by a powerful promise: that of rendering uncertainty manageable, strategy optimizable, and adjudication more consistent. This promise, however, contains within it a structural contradiction. As predictive systems become increasingly embedded in the architecture of arbitral practice, they begin to reshape the very behaviors, expectations, and epistemic conditions upon which their utility depends. Their presence alters strategic conduct, modifies procedural choices, and reframes the interpretive logic of decision-making. What was once a tool of foresight becomes a participant in the system it sought to observe. The consequence is a paradox of predictability—a self-disrupting cycle in which algorithmic success undermines its own conditions of validity. At the heart of this transformation lies a fundamental epistemological tension: law is reflexive by nature, but predictive systems accelerate this reflexivity and channel it through optimization logics that often stand at odds with the creative, plural, and deliberative character of legal reasoning. Arbitration, as this chapter has argued, serves as a particularly illustrative case study of this intensification. Its structural features—flexibility, privacy, party autonomy—render it especially transparent to the recursive dynamics introduced by algorithmic infrastructures. Yet the concern raised here is not exclusive to arbitration; it reflects a broader reconfiguration of legal epistemology under predictive rationalities. Through the lenses of game theory, reflexivity, and epistemic fragility, this article has examined how predictive models, once internalized by legal actors, generate recursive feedback loops that destabilize equilibria, collapse epistemic separation, and render systems optimized for regularity increasingly vulnerable to perturbation. The analysis has shown that the problem is not reducible to technical error or algorithmic bias. It is ontological in nature, rooted in the relational entanglement between model, behavior, and institutional form. Beyond these dynamics, the article has argued that predictive systems must be recognized as normative actors. Their outputs do not merely inform but structure the field of legal possibilities. They generate diffuse expectations, incentivize epistemic conformity, and alter the topology of procedural rationality. In doing so, they exert a form of algorithmic governance that reconfigures the foundations of arbitral legitimacy. No longer external tools, these systems become internal mechanisms of orientation, filtering what counts as plausible, efficient, or persuasive within the adjudicative process. Crucially, the intensification of reflexivity under predictive guidance displaces interpretive variance with statistical conformity. Argumentation contracts into legible templates; tribunal reasoning risks being perceived as confirmatory rather than deliberative; procedural elasticity gives way to optimization inertia. These shifts are not incidental side effects—they are manifestations of an epistemic narrowing that reshapes the conditions under which legal judgment is rendered. Ultimately, the future of arbitration in the age of predictive intelligence will not be defined solely by technical sophistication, but by the capacity of its institutions to remain epistemically plural. Prediction must remain an input, not a verdict. A heuristic, not a horizon. A companion to legal reasoning, not its surrogate. Only by maintaining this distinction—and by institutionalizing safeguards that modulate rather than amplify reflexivity—can arbitration continue to function as a forum of principled disagreement, normative experimentation, and adaptive justice within a computationally saturated legal ecosystem.

title: Chapter 6: Artificial Intelligence in International Arbitration: From Tool to Adjudicator
editors: Fernanda Carvalho Dias de Oliveira Silva
copyright: 2024 Kluwer Law International BV, and/or its subsidiaries, licensors, and contributors. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

sections.title: I Introduction
sections.text: I Introduction The transformative impact of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) on legal decision-making processes is increasingly evident. Artificial intelligence is transforming the epistemic foundations of adjudication. While its use as a productivity instrument is widely accepted, its potential role in decision making remains contentious. The arbitral community, traditionally cautious in adopting procedural innovations, now faces a paradigm in which algorithms could assist—or possibly replace—human reasoning in selected functions of dispute resolution. This chapter situates AI within arbitration’s evolving information infrastructure and highlights the legal, institutional, and ethical challenges of automation in adjudicative contexts. Three perspectives are examined: (1) a focus on the Brazilian context as a large civil-law jurisdiction already experiencing concrete deployments of AI in the judicial system; (2) a tentative development of the concept of technological due process as a framework for safeguarding transparency, accountability, and human oversight; and (3) a comparative examination of the AAA-ICDR experience, which illustrates how an arbitral institution is practically integrating AI into its procedural architecture. Together, these elements move the discussion beyond abstract speculation and ground it in both empirical observation and institutional practice.

sections.title: II The Current State of AI Evolution
sections.text: II The Current State of AI Evolution Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is a relatively recent development of the AI evolution process. Examining the evolution of AI systems and tools and their current state is essential to understanding the importance of these tools, their use, and identifying future trends. The current stage of AI systems’ development reflects a long (though not uninterrupted) evolution. Historically, AI tools and systems have gone through several generations. Although AI research began as early as the 1950s, progress was uneven, with long “AI winters.”1 The conference that took place in Dartmouth College in 1956 coined the term artificial intelligence. According to Peter J. Bentley, “The event took place in 1956, consisting of eight weeks of talks and discussions amongst many of the leading pioneers at the time. The event formed the catalyst for the field of artificial intelligence and laid the foundations for many of the key ideas that were to dominate the field for decades to follow. It was a time of great excitement and optimism, with many new AIs being created that followed different principles” (Artificial Intelligence in Byte-sized Chunks, Michael O’Mara Books Limited, London, 2024, p. 19). [A] From Purpose-Built Systems to Data Recognition At the outset, AI systems were designed to address specific, well-defined problems. They were developed to perform particular tasks and to carry out very limited functions. At the same time, these systems depended on the provision of data that was processed and organized specifically for their operation.2 A view on the expert systems and their different uses could be found in Toby Walsh (The Shortest History of AI.
