sections.text: V Institutional Integration: The AAA–ICDR Experience In late 2024, the American Arbitration Association (AAA) and its International Centre for Dispute Resolution (ICDR) announced the deployment of AI-based case-management tools to automate parts of the procedural workflow.63 https://www.adr.org/press-releases/aaa-icdr-to-launch-ai-native-arbitrator-transforming-dispute-resolution/, accessed on December 10, 2025. This represents a turning point: the institutionalization of AI as procedural infrastructure in arbitration rather than optional aid. Such developments suggest that participation in arbitral proceedings will increasingly require interaction with AI systems, whether by counsel or tribunal. The AAA–ICDR has been building a genuine institutional ecosystem of artificial intelligence, going beyond the occasional or optional use of technological tools and becoming a part of the very procedural infrastructure of arbitration and other ADR mechanisms. AI thus ceases to be an external, ancillary resource and instead becomes embedded in the institutional environment in which cases are administered, from the pre-dispute stage through case management and support for decision-making activities. Among the tools already announced, particular attention should be given to AAAi Panelist Search, a semantic search mechanism applied to the institution’s roster of arbitrators and mediators, used by case managers to generate broader and more inclusive lists, with a significant reduction in preparation time.64 https://www.adr.org/press-releases/aaa-icdr-launches-new-aaai-panelist-search-to-enhance-panelist-selection-with-ai-technology/, accessed on December 10, 2025. This strategy is complemented by initiatives set out in the AAA–ICDR’s AI roadmap, such as the AAAi Chat Book65 https://www.adr.org/chatbook/caseprep, accessed on December 10, 2025. and the rules chatbot, designed to answer questions on applicable rules and to guide case preparation and conduct, as well as by the AAA ClauseBuilder AI, a generative-AI tool for drafting arbitration clauses, which has received LegalTech awards and anticipates the early use of AI at the contractual stage. The integration of AI into case management further reinforces this structural logic. The WebFile platform now includes an AI assistant that allows users to summarize documents and consult the case file directly within the system. In addition, the partnership with Clearbrief enables arbitrators to organize large volumes of evidence, create hyperlinked timelines, and verify citations, subject to disclosure obligations when informing the parties that the tribunal intends to use the tool.66 https://www.adr.org/news-and-insights/advancing-efficiency-and-quality-in-adr-how-the-aaa-icdr-s-partnership-with-clearbrief-revolutionizes-arbitration-and-mediation/, accessed on December 10, 2025. This is complemented by pilot projects and the adoption of tools such as Vincent (vLex) and CoCounsel, which are used to accelerate case intake and screening, as well as legal research and contract analysis, particularly in the pre-procedural phase.67 According to one report on the event, the panelists “highlighted recent AI pilots with CoCounsel (now part of Thomson Reuters), VLex’s Vincent, and Clearbrief. Following successful trials, AAA now offers Clearbrief—an AI tool that generates hyperlinked timelines and deposition summaries—for free to arbitrators on AAA cases. Meanwhile, AAA’s intake teams have adopted Vincent to speed up contract review for new filings, reporting significant time savings” (Sarah Chojecki, From Today to Tomorrow: AAA-ICDR and ArbTech Panel Discussion on Tech-Driven Arbitration, available at https://legalblogs.wolterskluwer.com/arbitration-blog/from-today-to-tomorrow-aaa-icdr-and-arbtech-panel-discussion-on-tech-driven-arbitration/, accessed on December 10, 2025). Taken together, these initiatives point to a clear evolution toward an institutionalized “AI infrastructure,” explicitly articulated by the AAA–ICDR in official communications and on a dedicated webpage outlining its technological strategy. Reports indicate that AI systems are already routinely used by case managers, effectively making interaction with algorithms a normal part of the user experience at the AAA–ICDR, albeit accompanied by transparency safeguards and, in some cases, opt-out mechanisms. This context opens the door to a deeper normative debate, encompassing disclosure obligations, impacts on due process, standards of review for AI-supported decisions, and potential adjustments to arbitration clauses in light of this new reality of an institutional algorithmic infrastructure. The AAA–ICDR is a pioneer in setting up this type of infrastructure, but it is possible to anticipate a trend—also reflected in empirical research on the use of AI in arbitration mentioned above—that other leading institutions are likely to adopt a similar approach and consolidate the use of AI in their operations. This represents an important step toward the broader normalization of AI in arbitral proceedings. Once the largest and most influential arbitration institutions and chambers embrace this type of technology, its use is likely to proliferate among arbitrators, counsel, law firms active in arbitration, and professionals who support arbitral tribunals.

sections.title: VI Three Perspectives: Different Approaches
sections.text: VI Three Perspectives: Different Approaches Just as it is not possible to generalize the concept of artificial intelligence68 Artificial intelligence is a broad term used to describe a variety of systems that are often quite different from one another and perform diverse functions. As shown at the beginning of this text, there are multiple types of AI and a wide range of systems derived from them. As a result, the use of the term artificial intelligence inevitably produces a generalization that tends to oversimplify the multiple realities encompassed by this category of technology. or to consider all existing AI systems as equivalent, it is also not possible to approach every use of AI in arbitration in the same manner. It is necessary to identify the specific functions performed by AI systems within arbitration, particularly in relation to the roles exercised by arbitrators, the parties, and counsels. In this respect, at least three distinct functions of AI in the context of arbitration can be identified. This tripartite framework draws on functional typologies developed in the AI literature, which distinguish, for example, between assistive, augmented, and autonomous AI systems,69 Jieqiong Cao et al., Not All Forms of Artificial Intelligence Are Perceived Equal: AI Functions and Work Outcomes, 11(2) Journal of Open Innovation: Technology, Market, and Complexity (2025), available at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joitmc.2025.100521, accessed on January 26, 2026. as well as on analyses of legal AI systems that emphasize the diversity of functions and risks associated with different legal applications of AI.70 Gizem Gültekin-Várkonyi, AI Literacy for Legal AI Systems: A Practical Approach, 2025. Available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.18006, accessed on January 26, 2026. In the field of arbitration, several contributions similarly distinguish between the use of AI as a tool for procedural or analytical support and proposals in which AI assists—or potentially replaces—the arbitrator in the adjudicatory function.71 Ankit Malhoutra and Faizan Ahmad, Artificial Intelligence and International Arbitration. Novos Estudos Jurídicos, 2022. Available at https://doi.org/10.14210/nej.v27n2.p258-281, accessed on January 26, 2026. The classification adopted here—tool, assistant, and decision-maker—systematizes these debates within a single analytical framework. [A] Arbitration as a Tool AI as a tool serves primarily technical purposes: document management, translation, scheduling, or data visualization. It increases efficiency without affecting adjudicative reasoning. In this respect, AI represents a functional evolution comparable to the replacement of typewriters by computers. [B] Arbitration as an Assistant AI as an assistant exhibits enhanced, but still limited, autonomy. It drafts sections of awards, suggests reasoning structures, or identifies precedents. Its role is to augment cognitive capacity, not to substitute it. Institutional initiatives such as the AAA–ICDR pilot fall within this category. [C] Arbitration as Decision-Maker In narrowly defined contexts—such as disputes arising from smart contracts or low-value online arbitration—AI systems may be capable of performing forms of rule-based adjudication.72 See, for example, P. Han, AI-Powered Digital Arbitration Framework Leveraging Smart Contracts and Electronic Evidence Authentication, 15 Scientific Reports 37327 (2025). Available at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-21313-x, accessed on January 26, 2026 and Luís de Lima Pinheiro, Arbitration, Blockchain and Artificial Intelligence, May 28, 2025. Available at: SSRN: https://ssrn.com/abstract=5272787 or http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5272787, accessed on January 26, 2026. These settings are typically characterized by standardized inputs, clearly defined legal or contractual rules, and limited factual or legal discretion, which makes automation more feasible. By contrast, complex disputes often involve open-textured legal standards, evidentiary assessment, credibility judgments, and normative balancing, all of which remain poorly suited to fully autonomous decision making by AI. For this reason, while the complete replacement of human adjudicators in complex cases remains unlikely, current technological and institutional developments point toward a gradual increase in algorithmic participation in determinative functions, particularly in structured and repetitive decision-making tasks. [D] Comparison The three functions of artificial intelligence (AI) in arbitration can be structured in the following analytical framework: AI Role Description & Function Practical Examples Primary Objective (1) Tool AI is used to perform mechanical tasks or provide technical and operational support. Case law search, translation, document review/analysis, and deadline management. To increase workflow efficiency without influencing the substance of the decision. (2) Assistant Acts as a co-pilot for the arbitrator or the parties (AI agent), with limited autonomy. Proposing award or report drafts, suggesting lines of reasoning, and assisting with evidence assessment. To extend human cognitive capacity (“augmented cognition”). (3) Adjudicator AI assumes the decision-making role, either fully or partially. Automated arbitration in standardized or parameterized disputes (e.g., smart contract execution, when needed). To decide disputes based on strictly predefined parameters. These categories reflect different degrees of technological interaction, requiring differentiated levels of care, safeguards, and regulation depending on the degree of autonomy and the risks involved in each function. While the use of AI as a tool is primarily aimed at efficiency and speed, its use as an assistant or as a decision-maker calls for much stricter safeguards in order to preserve the integrity of the legal process. For example, the EU AI Act classifies certain AI systems used for decision making as high-risk primarily under Article 6(2) and Annex III, where they materially influence outcomes in sensitive areas like employment, education, public services, law enforcement, and justice. According to the AI Act, these systems pose significant risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights, triggering strict obligations unless derogations apply. Article 6(2) deems AI systems listed in Annex III as high-risk, including those for decision making that affect access to education (e.g., admissions), employment (e.g., recruitment, promotions, terminations), essential services (e.g., eligibility for benefits, creditworthiness), law enforcement (e.g., risk assessments), and administration of justice (e.g., interpreting facts and law). Article 6(3) provides some derogations: such systems are not high-risk if they perform narrow procedural tasks, improve prior human activities, detect patterns without replacing human assessment (with review), or handle preparatory tasks, provided they do not pose significant risk or materially influence decisions—except for profiling, which remains high-risk. In the case of high-risk AI systems, providers must implement a risk management system (Article 9), ensure high-quality data governance (Article 10), maintain technical documentation (Article 11), provide transparency (Article 13), ensure accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity (Article 15), and conduct conformity assessments (Article 43). Article 16 requires providers to establish a quality management system (Article 17), keep logs (Article 19), issue EU declarations of conformity (Article 47), affix CE marking, register systems, and take corrective actions. To facilitate understanding, one may think of AI in arbitration as analogous to different levels of vehicle automation: when used as a tool, it operates like a GPS that merely guides the itinerary; as an assistant, it resembles an autopilot that maintains speed and suggests maneuvers under human supervision; and as a decision-maker, it functions like a fully autonomous car, in which the system makes all driving decisions from start to finish. [E] AI in Arbitration: Risks, Regulation, and Governance Taking into account the three different functions, it is also possible to identify distinct levels of measures and safeguards that should be adopted in relation to different AI systems. As a system’s degree of autonomy increases, so too must the scope and intensity of the limits and controls imposed upon it. Systems that operate merely as tools involve lower risks and therefore require less intensive oversight by professionals involved in arbitration. In a sense, this approach is akin to the risk-based regulation that underpins the European AI Act. However, it is not simply a matter of adopting risk-based regulatory techniques. The differing risk perceptions arising from the various functions of AI systems shall trigger different responses at the regulatory and governance levels, as well as in the development of concrete safeguards governing the use of such systems in arbitration. The table below provides an objective and concise summary of this idea: Function Primary Risks Required Safeguards Tool Data leakage, bias propagation Access controls, validation Assistant Over-reliance, automation bias, cognitive bias Human-in-the-loop oversight Adjudicator Legitimacy crisis, opacity, lack of accountability Explainability, appealability, system certification [F] Moving Beyond Conventional Understanding Recognizing that AI systems perform different functions, entail different levels of risk, and require function-specific safeguards must be taken into account when seeking to regulate or establish guidelines for the use of AI in arbitration. Generic or overly broad provisions may lead to requirements that impose excessive constraints on systems used merely as tools. At the same time, taking into consideration the specific function performed by an AI system makes it possible to optimize its use while minimizing the risks associated with each of the functions exercised by AI systems. It should be noted that, at this stage, this analysis does not address the general constraints applicable to all AI systems, such as the risks of hallucinations or the limitations arising from biases and flaws in AI training datasets. These (and other) concerns that are common to all three functions performed by AI systems must also be assessed in light of the importance of the task at hand and the risks involved.

sections.title: VII Due Process in a Digital Legal Order
sections.text: VII Due Process in a Digital Legal Order The integration of AI into adjudicative systems, as in arbitration, imposes a re-examination of the concept of due process. Procedural fairness, explainability, and transparency must be preserved within algorithmic environments. [A] Technological Due Process The evolution toward a digital society grounded in data and artificial intelligence is reshaping the law’s information infrastructure, calling for the recognition of what we can refer to as technological due process.73 Richard Susskind, Tomorrow’s Lawyers, 3rd ed., Oxford University Press, Oxford, 2023, p. 264. As arbitration increasingly absorbs artificial intelligence into its workflow, the traditional understanding of due process must evolve to keep pace. This concept extends traditional due process guarantees to ensure that, in digital environments, the rights of the parties are protected through three core pillars: (1) transparency, (2) fairness, and (3) accountability in technology-assisted decision making. Sophie Nappert examines the case of blockchain dispute resolution and mentions that social expectations of fairness, satisfaction and trust are evolving under ecommerce and social media dynamics, and that this shift toward confidence in technology poses disruptive questions for traditional, timeintensive due process in arbitration.74 Sophie Nappert, Twenty‑First Century Arbitration: The Question of Trust, 2021, SSRN working paper, available at https://ssrn.com/abstract=3956155, accessed on January 3, 2026. While this may hold true for certain categories of dispute—such as those arising in social media or e-commerce contexts—a purely technological model of trust does not appear feasible across all types of disputes. In the context of platforms and social networks, the debate on technological due process also arises. In this regard, Frederick Mostert argues that “the fundamental principles of ‘procedural and substantive due process’ were developed to protect an individual from the coercive power of government. And, as we have seen, social media platforms, at least for now, are not recognized in the eyes of the law as ‘governments’ because they operate in privately owned spaces. The distinction is preserved despite the fact that in those very same privately controlled spaces, individuals and users are quite often subjected to the arbitrary exercise of power when it comes to (i) free speech and (ii) the abuse and manipulation of private data, especially in the US.”75 Frederick Mostert, “Digital Due Process”: A Need for Online Justice, 15(5) Journal of Intellectual Property Law & Practice (May 2020), pp. 378-389, https://doi.org/10.1093/jiplp/jpaa024, accessed on February 2, 2026. According to the author, “‘Digital due process’ forged on global customary norms, usually include among other principles: 1. a fair and public review by an independent and impartial panel or competent court within a reasonable time; 2. a proper prior notification of the review; 3. an opportunity for a user or notifier to respond and present evidence in respect of a takedown or a stay-up inaction by a platform; 4. The right to legal representation; 5. the right to appeal to an appeals panel, alternative dispute resolution tribunal or competent court; 6. notifiers may at any stage in the process seek access to competent courts; 7. the right to receive a decision which clearly articulates the reason for that decision; and, 8. the right to an effective remedy including, for example, stay-up or takedown.” In this new digital landscape, protecting the parties’ rights requires more than just neutral arbitrators; it demands a structural commitment to transparency and accountability regarding how technology influences the decision-making process.76 In this respect, the EU AI Act seeks to address these concerns by imposing active obligations and safeguards where AI systems are used for decision-making purposes. As noted above, high-risk AI systems are subject to stringent requirements, including the implementation of a risk management system (Art. 9), the assurance of high-quality data governance (Art. 10), the maintenance of technical documentation (Art. 11), transparency obligations (Art. 13), guarantees of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity (Art. 15), and the performance of conformity assessments (Art. 43). In addition, providers are required to establish a quality management system (Art. 17), keep logs (Art. 19), issue EU declarations of conformity (Art. 47), affix CE marking, register AI systems, and adopt corrective measures where necessary (Art. 16). Without this update, the efficiency gains of AI could inadvertently compromise the legitimacy of the entire arbitral framework. The core rationale for applying this concept rests on distinguishing the role played by AI and the risks associated with each role: – Graduated Need for Protection: The stringency of technological due process must be proportionate and compatible to the degree of autonomy exercised by AI. When AI is used merely as a tool (such as for precedent searches or deadline management), the aim is efficiency. By contrast, its use as an assistant (suggesting draft decisions or lines of reasoning) or as a decision-maker (fully automated decisions) requires far more robust safeguards, so that undetected errors or embedded biases do not compromise the integrity of the proceedings. – The “Human-in-the-Loop” Model: To enable this new paradigm without undermining legal trust, there is a need for mandatory integration of human arbitrators who review, assess, and validate AI-generated outputs before any decision is finalized. This mechanism makes it possible to harness the technology’s predictive power while preserving human values and the ability to explain the recommendations produced by machines. – Mitigation of Procedural Risks: The current absence of clear and specific regulation on technological due process—except for more general rules, as the AI Act mentioned before—is seen as a major drawback. There is a risk that awards may be challenged in court on the grounds of violations of due process when arbitrators rely on AI. The existence of a general regulatory framework alone, such as the AI Act, does little to mitigate these risks, as it neither encompasses the full range of potential uses of AI in arbitration nor is tailored to the specificities of dispute resolution, thereby leaving room for broad and potentially inconsistent interpretations in the event of a challenge. Accordingly, technological due process is not merely a technical adjustment, but rather a continuous dialogue and the development of regulatory frameworks designed to ensure that automation does not come at the expense of ethics and legal certainty, especially in complex arbitration disputes. [B] Grounds for Challenging Awards This evolving concept of due process is likely to become a primary battleground for the enforcement of arbitral awards. If a tribunal relies on opaque “black box” AI tools, losing parties may have valid grounds to argue that they were denied a fair hearing. Specific challenges could arise from a lack of explainability, where parties cannot decipher how an algorithm reached a conclusion, or from algorithmic bias, where the training data skews the result against a specific demographic or jurisdiction. Furthermore, the absence of meaningful human oversight, whereby an arbitrator merely “rubber-stamps” an AI system’s recommendation without genuine critical scrutiny, may be characterized as an abdication of the arbitrator’s adjudicative mandate, with potential consequences for the enforceability of the award. As highlighted by Sophie Nappert, the central challenge lies not in recognizing the need for human oversight, but in articulating how such oversight is to be exercised in practice, particularly in light of the well-documented “wow factor” associated with AI outputs.77 According to the author, “the ‘wow factor’ refers to a cognitive-emotional bias that arises when we are confronted by outputs that improve upon our own capacity—especially when they come from non-human agents like AI. There’s growing research showing that humans have a default tendency to attribute authority and even epistemic superiority to machines that present information with high fluency and speed—especially if that information appears comprehensive” (The “WOW” Factor: Human Cognition in the Age of Algorithmic Summarisation. Available at https://www.arbtech.io/blog/the-wow-factor-human-cognition-in-the-age-of-algorithmic-summarisation, accessed on January 2, 2026). The Brazilian CNJ surveys mentioned above also confirm that there is concern among law professionals (judges and law clerks) related to the “automation bias,” that is similar to the idea of the wow factor. See section III.C.7 above. This cognitive bias can induce undue deference to algorithmic suggestions, even among experienced decision-makers. Effective human oversight therefore requires more than the formal presence of a human in the loop: it demands structured procedural safeguards, such as an explicit duty to independently reason through the issues, documented justification when relying on AI-assisted analysis, transparency as to the role played by AI tools, and, where appropriate, institutional guidance or training aimed at mitigating automation bias. In the context of arbitration and dispute resolution, these measures are essential to ensure that AI remains a decision-support instrument rather than a de facto decision-maker, thereby preserving both the legitimacy of the arbitral process and the enforceability of its outcomes.78 Sophie Nappert proposes that this requires an ethical approach to cognitive design grounded in four complementary strategies. First, AI systems should be designed for skepticism, presenting outputs in ways that invite critical scrutiny rather than passive acceptance, including the explicit indication of uncertainties, data limitations, and omissions. Second, cognitive literacy training should be incorporated into judicial and arbitral practice, equipping decision-makers not only to understand how AI tools function, but also how they shape human reasoning, including well-documented biases such as automation bias and fluency effects. Third, transparent summarisation techniques should be adopted, requiring AI-generated outputs to disclose the underlying priorities, methods, and exclusions that informed the result, thereby enabling contestability and informed review. Finally, human–AI collaboration models should frame AI as a subordinate cognitive aid—similar to a junior clerk or research assistant—designed to augment, rather than replace, independent human judgment. Together, these measures contribute to preserving meaningful human oversight and safeguarding the legitimacy of AI-assisted decision making in arbitration and dispute resolution (The “WOW” Factor: Human Cognition in the Age of Algorithmic Summarisation. Available at https://www.arbtech.io/blog/the-wow-factor-human-cognition-in-the-age-of-algorithmic-summarisation, accessed on January 2, 2026). [C] Implementing Procedural Safeguards To preempt these challenges, the arbitration community must operationalize this new due process through concrete procedural safeguards. This involves more than just theoretical guidelines; it requires mandating independent audits of AI tools to verify their neutrality and accuracy before they are deployed in high-stakes disputes. Institutions must also establish clear protocols for informed consent, ensuring all parties are aware of which technologies are being used and to what extent. By creating formal channels for parties to raise concerns about technological irregularities, the system ensures that efficiency does not come at the cost of justice, providing parties with necessary recourse if the digital tools compromise the integrity of the proceedings. [D] Constitutional AI Constitutional AI can also be understood as a form of (procedural and substantive) safeguard. Some academics advocate for “constitutional AI”—the embedding of legal principles and rights into AI model architecture itself. According to Sophie Nappert, Benjamin Malek, and Fernanda Carvalho Dias de Oliveira Silva, Constitutional AI “offers a different path. It trains AI models to be helpful, harmless, and honest without relying extensively on human feedback at every step of the training process.”79 What is Constitutional AI and Why Does it Matter for International Arbitration? Available at https://legalblogs.wolterskluwer.com/arbitration-blog/what-is-constitutional-ai-and-why-does-it-matter-for-international-arbitration/, accessed on December 12, 2025. The authors mention that “CAI replaces much of the human oversight required during training with a written set of normative principles—the ‘constitution’—which the AI uses to guide its behaviour. These principles, explained in plain language (like ‘avoid harmful content’ or ‘be respectful’), serve as internal guardrails. Rather than being told what is right or wrong in thousands of individual examples, the model learns to assess its own responses in light of these overarching principles.” When it comes to regulating or guiding the use of a given technology, conventional regulatory and governance instruments may prove insufficiently effective. The inherent difficulties in delimiting the use of certain technologies—such as artificial intelligence, which encompasses heterogeneous characteristics and diverse systems developed by multiple vendors and developers—recommend the adoption of innovative approaches. The notion of embedded regulation has already been discussed in some fields, such as the regulation of decentralized finance and blockchain regulation.80 See Nicolas Vasse et al., Embedded Supervision of Decentralized Finance: Final Report (European Commission, DirectorateGeneral for Financial Stability, Financial Services and Capital Markets Union 2025). Available at: https://euagenda.eu/publications/embedded-supervision-of-decentralized-finance, accessed on January 2, 2026. A previous working paper by the Bank for International Settlements—BIS also mention the concept of embedded regulation (Raphael Auer, Embedded Supervision: How to Build Regulation into Decentralised Finance (BIS Working Papers No. 811, Bank for International Settlements, first published September 2019, revised May 2022)), available at https://www.bis.org/publ/work811.htm, accessed on January 2, 2026. The concept of constitutional AI goes further. It is not merely a matter of embedding regulation through the very technological instrument being regulated. Rather, such embedded regulation must promote the use of technology in accordance with principles and values enshrined in the legal order and shared by society. In essence, this approach is concerned not only with setting external limits on the use of technology and AI, but with establishing, within the technology itself, paradigms of use and operation oriented toward the promotion and concrete realization of those values and principles. From this perspective, the proposal of constitutional AI, as articulated by the authors, has the potential to constitute an innovative and effective approach for delimiting the use of AI systems in the context of arbitration. Constitutional AI is a language-model alignment technique that incorporates a set of normative principles (a kind of constitution) directly into the training process, with the aim of making the system more useful, safe, and explainable—an approach with particularly relevant applications in regulated fields such as international arbitration. Rather than relying primarily on case-by-case human feedback (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)), the model learns to critique and revise its own outputs on the basis of these principles, thereby bringing AI operation closer to legal values such as fundamental rights, due process, and impartiality. [1] Concept and Technical Origins The term Constitutional AI was introduced by Anthropic in 2022 in the paper “Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback,” as a method for training assistants to be “helpful, honest, and harmless” using feedback from another AI rather than human harm labels.81 Yuntao Bai, Saurav Kadavath, Sandipan Kundu, Amanda Askell, Jackson Kernion, Andy Jones, Anna Chen, Anna Goldie, Azalia Mirhoseini, Cameron McKinnon, Carol Chen, Catherine Olsson, Christopher Olah, Danny Hernandez, Dawn Drain, Deep Ganguli, Dustin Li, Eli Tran-Johnson, Ethan Perez, Jamie Kerr, Jared Mueller, Jeffrey Ladish, Joshua Landau, Kamal Ndousse, Kamile Lukosuite, Liane Lovitt, Michael Sellitto, Nelson Elhage, Nicholas Schiefer, Noemi Mercado, Nova DasSarma, Robert Lasenby, Robin Larson, Sam Ringer, Scott Johnston, Shauna Kravec, Sheer El Showk, Stanislav Fort, Tamera Lanham, Timothy Telleen-Lawton, Tom Conerly, Tom Henighan, Tristan Hume, Samuel R. Bowman, Zac Hatfield-Dodds, Ben Mann, Dario Amodei, Nicholas Joseph, Sam McCandlish, Tom Brown, and Jared Kaplan, 2022, Available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.08073, accessed on December 10, 2025. The process begins by defining in advance a written set of principles (such as avoiding hate speech, respecting privacy, and not encouraging illegal activities) and using this “constitutional text” to enable the model itself to evaluate and correct its responses to challenging prompts.82 An updated version of Claude’s Constitution is available at https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/cffd979fd050fbc0d8874b8c58b24cc10554e208/claudes-constitution_webPDF_26-01.26a.pdf, accessed on January 30, 2026. [2] The Method Training starts with adversarial or potentially harmful prompts (“red teaming”), to which the model generates initial responses; this is followed by a self-critique phase (or a second model) that compares the response against the constitutional principles and proposes a revised version that is more closely aligned. From numerous comparisons between original response versus revised response, the model is fine-tuned using techniques such as supervised learning and reinforcement learning (e.g., RL from AI feedback and Proximal Policy Optimization), reducing reliance on thousands of human annotations while improving scalability. [3] Variations and Extensions Anthropic has developed the notion of Collective Constitutional AI, in which the set of principles is constructed with public input or the participation of multiple stakeholders, in order to reflect broader social values and enhance the democratic legitimacy of alignment.83 Collective Constitutional AI: Aligning a Language Model with Public Input, 2023. Available at https://www.anthropic.com/research/collective-constitutional-ai-aligning-a-language-model-with-public-input, accessed on December 9, 2025. In parallel, part of the literature uses Constitutional AI to describe the convergence between AI and state constitutional principles, aimed at ensuring that AI systems respect fundamental rights, equality, due process, and other pillars of the rule of law. [4] Critiques and Theoretical Limits Scholars in digital constitutional law argue that Anthropic’s “constitution” is normatively thin: a list of general safety and civility values lacking the institutional, hierarchical, and procedural density of a true, legal constitution. There is also concern that the constitutional metaphor may obscure power asymmetries (e.g., private companies unilaterally defining the principles) and confer an appearance of constitutional-legal legitimacy on choices that are essentially technical or commercial.84 D. Leslie et al., Artificial Intelligence, Human Rights, Democracy, and the Rule of Law: A Primer, 2021, The Council of Europe. Available at https://arxiv.org/pdf/2104.04147, accessed on December 15, 2025. According to the authors, “To summarise, any approach to effectively ensuring that AI safeguards democracy, human rights, and the rule of law is likely to require a combination of the horizontal (binding and nonbinding) approaches outlined here and more sector-specific principles, standards, and requirements” (p. 29). In some sense, social and political disputes and tensions that were present in society could be transplanted into AI models. The dispute, if not well resolved, could lead to distortions. Anthropic has addressed some of these criticisms by indicating that the idea of a Constitutional AI encompasses a long-term goal that “isn’t trying to get our systems to represent a specific ideology, but rather to be able to follow a given set of principles. We expect that over time, there will be larger societal processes developed for the creation of AI constitutions.”85 https://www.anthropic.com/news/claudes-constitution, accessed on January 26, 2026. It recognizes that “Constitutions aren’t a panacea and CAI-trained systems will continue to generate difficult questions about what they are and aren’t allowed to do—for example, whether they be allowed to produce text that contains violence or harmful language” and that “AI models will have value systems, whether intentional or unintentional.” According to Anthropic, “One of our goals with Constitutional AI is to make those goals explicit and easy to alter as needed. We are exploring ways to more democratically produce a constitution for Claude, and also exploring offering customizable constitutions for specific use cases.” The ideas advanced by Anthropic in relation to the notion of Constitutional AI could be applied in specific domains, such as law, dispute resolution, and arbitration. By arguing that there could be “customizable constitutions for specific use cases,” Anthropic points toward the development of tailored models designed for particular domains. In any event, this confirms that models deployed in specialized fields, such as law or arbitration, could be deliberately designed and trained in accordance with a specific set of values and rules to be observed. [5] Applications in Arbitration and Adjudication In the context of international arbitration, the idea of constitutional AI could internalize principles such as impartiality, neutrality, transparency, respect for the tribunal’s jurisdiction, and the separation between facts and value judgments. Examples include evidence summaries that avoid assigning credibility or drawing conclusions about intent; party-defined constitutions governing confidentiality, the treatment of evidentiary inconsistencies, and non-accusatory language; and training models on institutional rules (ICC, LCIA, UNCITRAL) to harmonize procedural standards. [6] Practical Advantages and Challenges Key advantages include greater interpretability (the system can justify refusals or choices by explicit reference to principles), reduced costs of human annotation, scalability to larger models, and greater predictability of behavior. The main challenges involve the participatory and legitimate definition of the constitution (who selects the principles), the risk of overblocking or evasive responses on controversial topics, difficulties in encoding complex notions such as justice and proportionality, and the continued need for human judgment in substantive legal decisions, especially in arbitration and constitutional adjudication. [E] Education and Technological Literacy Technological literacy also becomes a prerequisite for procedural fairness. Lawyers and arbitrators must understand AI’s logic sufficiently to challenge, interpret, or constrain it. Sophie Nappert examines the issue and argues that international arbitrators will increasingly need technological and ethical literacy to work effectively with AI while preserving the legitimacy of arbitration.86 Sophie Nappert, The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Duties, Qualifications and Selection of International Arbitrators (Oxford Law Pro, Thought Leadership series, 2025), available at https://academic.oup.com/info-oxford-law-pro/pages/artificial-intelligence-arbitrators, accessed on January 2, 2026. Nappert insists that arbitrators can no longer say “I am just not a tech person”: technological literacy becomes part of diligence and competence, and cannot be delegated to tribunal secretaries without maintaining informed oversight. According to the author, practitioners should grasp basic features of algorithmic systems (supervised/unsupervised learning, probabilistic versus rules‑based tools, training data, bias, overfitting, “black box” opacity) to interrogate outputs rather than accept them at face value. This literacy is tied to emerging professional standards (e.g., Bar Council guidance on generative AI, CIArb and Silicon Valley guidelines on AI in arbitration), which frame familiarity with AI and its limitations as part of professional competence. Besides pure technological literacy, according to Nappert, arbitrators must develop “cognitive ethics” to recognize automation bias, confirmation bias amplified by AI suggestions, and anchoring effects from algorithmic summaries or predictions. Ethical literacy also covers data provenance, algorithmic accountability, cybersecurity and the risk that proprietary tools embed hidden biases affecting parties from specific jurisdictions or backgrounds. Practitioners need awareness of how their digital footprint, institutional feedback and social media presence feed AI systems, raising questions about reputation management, privacy and fairness in appointments. Nappert frames the “modern” arbitrator as a human–AI integrator: someone who uses AI to enhance efficiency and insight, but preserves independent judgment, transparency of reasoning and due process. As with the introduction of automobiles, regulation and training transformed a potentially dangerous technology into a managed utility. The same principle must apply to AI.

sections.title: VIII Beyond Chatbots: The Emergence of AI Agents
sections.text: VIII Beyond Chatbots: The Emergence of AI Agents Generative systems are evolving into AI agents—autonomous entities capable of executing tasks and communicating with external data sources. These AI agents represent a step ahead of systems based on chat. [A] The Concept of AI Agents and Agentic Systems Unlike traditional AI or simple large language models (LLMs), which operate passively by generating text or images on demand, AI agents are autonomous, goal-oriented systems.87 “Unlike traditional AI, which operates within the confines of predefined rules, AI agents are empowered by large language models (LLMs) and other AI frameworks that are capable of interacting, reasoning, and adapting in real-time to achieve specific goals” (Laurie Hughes, Yogesh K. Dwivedi, Tegwen Malik, Mazen Shawosh, Mousa Ahmed Albashrawi, Il Jeon, Vincent Dutot, Mandanna Appanderanda, Tom Crick, Rahul De, Mark Fenwick, Senali Madugoda Gunaratnege, Paulius Jurcys, Arpan Kumar Kar, Nir Kshetri, Keyao Li, Sashah Mutasa, Spyridon Samothrakis, Michael Wade, and Paul Walton) 2025: AI Agents and Agentic Systems: A Multi-Expert Analysis, Journal of Computer Information Systems. Available at https://doi.org/10.1080/08874417.2025.2483832, accessed on December 10, 2025. An agent is an entity that has a perception of its environment, reasons about it, and acts independently to achieve long-term objectives with minimal human intervention. Agentic systems are characterized by six core properties: (1) goal, (2) perception, (3) action, (4) iteration, (5) adaptation, and (6) termination. Whereas a conventional chatbot functions as a “copilot” that generates content, an agent operates as an “autopilot,” capable of using external tools (APIs, browsers, databases) to execute complex, end-to-end workflows autonomously. According to Joe Kwon, “Unlike chatbots, these systems take a highlevel goal, plan their own steps, call tools, and keep iterating until the task is finished. This shift matters because agents automate decisions, not just steps.”88 AI Agents: Governing Autonomy in the Digital Age, 2025. Available at https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65af2088cac9fb1fb621091f/682f96d6b3bd5a3e1852a16a_AI_Agents_Report.pdf, accessed on December 12, 2025. [B] AI Agents Versus Agentic AI It is important to identify the differences between AI agents and Agentic AI. According to Ranjan Sapkota, Roumeliotis Konstantinos I., and Manoj Karkee, “As the field progresses from Generative Agents toward increasingly autonomous systems, it becomes critically important to delineate the technological and conceptual boundaries between AI Agents and Agentic AI. While both paradigms build upon large LLMs and extend the capabilities of generative systems, they embody fundamentally different architectures, interaction models, and levels of autonomy.”89 Ranjan Sapkota, Konstantinos I. Roumeliotis and Manoj Karkee, AI Agents vs. Agentic AI: A Conceptual Taxonomy, Applications and Challenges, arXiv preprint arXiv:2505.10468, 2025, available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.10468, accessed on December 9, 2025. AI Agents are characterized as modular systems, typically single entities, designed to automate specific and narrowly defined tasks. They rely on sequential reasoning and the use of external tools to perform well-delimited functions. Agentic AI represents a paradigmatic leap and an architectural evolution. It refers to systems composed of multiple specialized agents that collaborate, communicate, and dynamically decompose goals within a broader workflow. Such systems exhibit features such as persistent memory and orchestrated autonomy.90 “AI Agents are typically designed as single-entity systems that perform goal-directed tasks by invoking external tools, applying sequential reasoning, and integrating real-time information to complete well-defined functions. In contrast, Agentic AI systems are composed of multiple, specialized agents that coordinate, communicate, and dynamically allocate sub-tasks within a broader workflow” (Ranjan Sapkota, Konstantinos I. Roumeliotis, and Manoj Karkee. AI Agents vs. Agentic AI: A Conceptual Taxonomy, Applications and Challenges,” arXiv preprint arXiv:2505.10468, 2025, available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.10468, accessed on December 9, 2025). [C] Use of AI Agents to Protect Human Rights and Ensure Due Process The autonomy of AI agents and Agentic AI can be deployed in defense of individuals. This depends on how the agent is programmed and trained to perform specific tasks in accordance with defined guidelines and rules. In the field of consumer law, there are already experiences of this kind, such as those reported by Dazza Greenwood, who mentions the concept of loyal agents in consumer relationships.91 Defining “Loyalty” for AI Agents: Insights from the Stanford AI Agents x Law Workshop. May 2025. Available at https://innovation.consumerreports.org/defining-loyalty-for-ai-agents-insights-from-the-stanford-ai-agents-x-law-workshop/, accessed on December 12, 2025. Dazza Greenwood and Diana Stern also point out that “This compliance-centric approach requires embedding checkpoints within the AI agent’s workflow. Before executing a transaction, the agent would cross-reference its planned actions against applicable legal terms, flagging potential non-compliance and, if necessary, prompting human review or adjusting its course of action. This creates a system of internal controls, ensuring that the agent operates within defined legal boundaries.”92 From Fine Print to Machine Code: How AI Agents are Rewriting the Rules of Engagement: Part 3 of 3, Stanford SLS Blog. Available at https://law.stanford.edu/2025/03/26/from-fine-print-to-machine-code-how-ai-agents-are-rewriting-the-rules-of-engagement-part-3-of-3/, accessed on December 13, 2025. Accordingly, if it is possible to establish specific parameters for AI agents tasked with monitoring and assessing the compliance of other AI systems with a given set of rules and principles, it is likewise feasible to design and deploy AI agents focused on defending constitutional principles and human rights, or even on ensuring the full observance of due process in arbitral proceedings. [D] Some Caveats to Consider: Quis Custodiet? It is evident that this is neither a simple nor a straightforward matter. The same problems and limitations that affect AI systems also apply to AI agents. Their development must therefore proceed with caution, so that they can perform the functions for which they were designed in the most efficient and secure manner possible. In a sense, this gives rise to a form of circular reasoning that requires particular care. If rule-based AI systems are needed to safeguard rights, and AI agents are then employed to ensure that such rules and principles are effectively complied with, the question arises: who will control the AI agents themselves? One possible answer lies in the use of multiple agents operating in different layers, monitoring and supervising one another. While this solution is technically feasible, it may introduce additional complexity and make it more difficult to identify situations in which previously established safeguards are violated, undermining efforts to ensure that AI systems do not operate contrary to the law. Nevertheless, it remains a mechanism that can be deployed and holds significant potential for the effective implementation of the concept of constitutional AI and to ensure compliance with technological due process.

sections.title: IX Conclusion
sections.text: IX Conclusion The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into decision making, particularly in the context of arbitration, appears inevitable and is already underway. The decisive question is not whether AI will be used, but how it can be governed in a manner consistent with fairness, transparency, and human dignity. A structured framework combining technical standards, institutional oversight, and education is essential to prevent an erosion of trust in arbitration. AI will not replace arbitrators; rather, it will redefine the very meaning of adjudication, transforming arbitration from a purely human process into an environment of “augmented justice.” In this context, the development of the concept of technological due process (or digital due process) is fundamental, as it may impose stringent obligations of transparency, explainability, and the mandatory presence of a “human in the loop.” This new paradigm requires traditional procedural guarantees to evolve in order to protect party rights in digital environments, structured around three central pillars: transparency, fairness, and accountability. The application of technological due process must be incremental and proportionate to the level of AI autonomy involved. While the use of an AI system as a mere tool calls for basic technical oversight, its role as an assistant—or especially as a decision-maker—demands far more robust safeguards to mitigate the risks of algorithmic bias and insufficient reasoning. In addition, the emergence of “Constitutional AI” offers a promising pathway by enabling the incorporation of legal principles—such as impartiality and adversarial process—directly into the architecture of AI models. Ultimately, the legitimacy of arbitration in the algorithmic age will depend on our ability to ensure that technological efficiency does not come at the expense of legal certainty or the empathy inherent in human judgment. The arbitrator of the future must therefore be equipped with technological literacy, not only to use these tools but also to question, interpret, and constrain their influence when necessary.

plain_text:
I Introduction The transformative impact of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) on legal decision-making processes is increasingly evident. Artificial intelligence is transforming the epistemic foundations of adjudication. While its use as a productivity instrument is widely accepted, its potential role in decision making remains contentious. The arbitral community, traditionally cautious in adopting procedural innovations, now faces a paradigm in which algorithms could assist—or possibly replace—human reasoning in selected functions of dispute resolution. This chapter situates AI within arbitration’s evolving information infrastructure and highlights the legal, institutional, and ethical challenges of automation in adjudicative contexts. Three perspectives are examined: (1) a focus on the Brazilian context as a large civil-law jurisdiction already experiencing concrete deployments of AI in the judicial system; (2) a tentative development of the concept of technological due process as a framework for safeguarding transparency, accountability, and human oversight; and (3) a comparative examination of the AAA-ICDR experience, which illustrates how an arbitral institution is practically integrating AI into its procedural architecture. Together, these elements move the discussion beyond abstract speculation and ground it in both empirical observation and institutional practice.

II The Current State of AI Evolution Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is a relatively recent development of the AI evolution process. Examining the evolution of AI systems and tools and their current state is essential to understanding the importance of these tools, their use, and identifying future trends. The current stage of AI systems’ development reflects a long (though not uninterrupted) evolution. Historically, AI tools and systems have gone through several generations. Although AI research began as early as the 1950s, progress was uneven, with long “AI winters.”1 The conference that took place in Dartmouth College in 1956 coined the term artificial intelligence. According to Peter J. Bentley, “The event took place in 1956, consisting of eight weeks of talks and discussions amongst many of the leading pioneers at the time. The event formed the catalyst for the field of artificial intelligence and laid the foundations for many of the key ideas that were to dominate the field for decades to follow. It was a time of great excitement and optimism, with many new AIs being created that followed different principles” (Artificial Intelligence in Byte-sized Chunks, Michael O’Mara Books Limited, London, 2024, p. 19). [A] From Purpose-Built Systems to Data Recognition At the outset, AI systems were designed to address specific, well-defined problems. They were developed to perform particular tasks and to carry out very limited functions. At the same time, these systems depended on the provision of data that was processed and organized specifically for their operation.2 A view on the expert systems and their different uses could be found in Toby Walsh (The Shortest History of AI. Editor Black Incorporated, 2025, ISBN 1760645133, kindle edition). The use of unstructured data or data in formats not anticipated by the systems constrained their efficiency and, in some cases, even prevented them from functioning properly. Another distinguishing feature of these early AI systems in comparison to the ones available today is how they operated. They relied on specific, predefined algorithms. The processing of the data fed into the systems followed clear and determinate procedures, expressed through explicit rules and algorithmic functions. In a sense, these systems can be described as being based on deterministic algorithmic rules. This means that data was processed in a linear fashion, following rules and patterns that had been established in advance by the designers of algorithmic programming. In very simplified terms, they followed a binary logic: once a given condition “A” was met or identified, a consequence or a conclusion “B” was produced or reached. This is, of course, an oversimplification of the processes used by these pioneering AI systems. It does not imply that they did not already involve hundreds or even thousands of lines of code to operate. These initial limitations were reflected in their everyday use. Early AI systems were highly specialized, purpose-built and suited to very specific tasks, such as meteorological data processing or the analysis of population-level mathematical data, for example. They were not general-purpose systems capable of use across a wide range of tasks or functions. Another defining feature of these early systems concerned how they were used. Because they were specialized, they depended on specialized operators and typically required the use of specific programming languages. Each system had its own particularities and required knowledge of the programming language employed and of how data had to be fed into the system. Any use that failed to follow the prescribed methods for data input and system instruction would result in errors or malfunction. For all these reasons, these early AI systems were highly constrained, and their use was largely limited to specific niches and activities. However, these systems evolved over the decades. Given the scope limitations of this chapter and its focus on current AI systems based on large language models, it does not undertake a detailed analysis of the evolution of expert systems. Rather, the purpose of referring to them is to highlight the differences among distinct AI systems and tools, in order to situate the generative-AI-based tools currently under development within the broader trajectory of artificial intelligence as a technological paradigm. In this sense, Toby Walsh notes that AI developed through two distinct eras: the symbolic era, in which expert systems flourished, and the learning era, in which approaches to training and feeding data into AI systems changed fundamentally.3 The Shortest History of AI. Editor Black Incorporated, 2025, ISBN 1760645133, kindle edition. From the perspective of the technologies and techniques employed, AI systems began to incorporate increasingly complex forms of data recognition and processing. Several of these techniques can be identified as laying the foundations for contemporary AI systems. [B] Machine Learning Techniques Machine learning may be described as an AI technique that underpins a wide range of methods and tools forming the basis of current developments in the field. It relates to “the development and evaluation of algorithms that enable a computer to extract (or learn) functions from a dataset (sets of examples).”4 John D. Kelleher, Deep Learning, Cambridge, The MIT Press, 2019, p. 6. Martin Ford mentions that “Machine learning is the branch of AI that involves creating algorithms that can learn from data. Another way to put this is that machine learning algorithms are computer programs that program themselves by looking at information.”5 Martin Ford, Architects of Intelligence: The Truth About AI from the People Building It, Birmingham, Packt Publishing, 2018. p. 10. Researchers or developers train machine learning systems on a specific (and large) set of data to extract functions (relations detected between the data). They then translate this function into computer code to form the model. The model consists of an algorithm derived from the function identified in the processed data.6 John D. Kelleher refers that “the three key ingredients in machine learning” are “1. Data (a set of historical examples). 2. A set of functions that the algorithm will search through to find the best match with the data. 3. Some measure of fitness that can be used to evaluate how well each candidate function matches the data” (Deep Learning, Cambridge, The MIT Press, 2019, p. 22). Andriy Burkov mentions that “Machine learning can also be defined as the process of solving a practical problem by 1) gathering a dataset, and 2) algorithmically building a statistical model based on that dataset. That statistical model is assumed to be used somehow to solve the practical problem.”7 Andriy Burkov, The Hundred-page Machine Learning Book, ISBN 978-1-9995795-0-0, 2019, p. 1. In machine learning, the patterns extracted from the data used to train AI models are validated in order to verify whether the algorithm automatically generated from those data corresponds to the logic that can be inferred from them. In this training process, it is also possible to refer to techniques for confirming and reinforcing the learning performed by the AI system. Considering the various techniques employed, one may refer to supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning, which are the most common types of machine learning. John D. Kelleher mentions that “In supervised machine learning, each example in the dataset is labeled with the expected output (or target) value. (…) In order to use supervised machine learning, our dataset must list the value of the target feature for every example in the dataset.”8 Deep Learning, Cambridge, The MIT Press, 2019, p. 26. Kelleher indicates that “These target feature values can sometimes be very difficult, and expensive, to collect. In some cases, we must pay human experts to label each example in a dataset with the correct target value. However, the benefit of having these target values in the dataset is that the machine learning algorithm can use these values to help the learning process. It does this by comparing the outputs a function produces with the target outputs specified in the dataset, and using the difference (or error) to evaluate the fitness of the candidate function, and use the fitness evaluation to guide the search for the best function” (Deep Learning, supra, p. 26). As the author explains, “It is because of this feedback from the target labels in the dataset to the algorithm that this type of machine learning is considered supervised.”9 Deep Learning, supra, p. 26. In its turn, unsupervised learning refers to a technique that is mainly used for clustering data. According to Kelleher, “In unsupervised machine learning, there are no target values in the dataset. Consequently, the algorithm cannot directly evaluate the fitness of a candidate function against the target values in the dataset. Instead, the machine learning algorithm tries to identify functions that map similar examples into clusters, such that the examples in a cluster are more similar to the other examples in the same cluster than they are to examples in other clusters. Note that the clusters are not prespecified, or at most they are initially very underspecified.”10 Deep Learning, supra, p. 27. Reinforced learning consists of other type of machine learning that involves an agent that “needs to learn a policy for how it should act in an environment in order to be rewarded.”11 Deep Learning, supra, p. 28. In reinforcement learning, the goal of the agent is to learn a mapping from its current observation of the environment and its own internal state (its memory) to what action it should take: for instance, should the robot move forward or backward or should the computer program move the pawn or take the queen. The output of this policy (function) is the action that the agent should take next, given the current context.12 John D. Kelleher, Deep Learning, Cambridge, The MIT Press, 2019, p. 28. “In these types of scenarios, it is difficult to create historic datasets, and so reinforcement learning is often carried out in situ: an agent is released into an environment where it experiments with different policies (starting with a potentially random policy) and over time updates its policy in response to the rewards it receives from the environment, If an action results in a positive reward, the mapping from the relevant observations and state to that action is reinforced in the policy, whereas if an action results in a negative reward, the mapping is weakened. Unlike in supervised and unsupervised machine learning, in reinforcement learning, the fact that learning is done in situ means that the training and inference stages are interleaved and ongoing. The agent infers what action it should do next and uses the feedback from the environment to learn how to update its policy. A distinctive aspect of reinforcement learning is that the target output of the learned function (the agent’s actions) is decoupled from the reward mechanism” (Deep Learning, supra, pp. 28-29). Machine learning techniques provided the foundation for the development of other approaches, such as deep learning and neural networks. These will be examined in greater detail below. [1] Neural Networks Neural networks are a subtype of machine learning technique that uses a different architectural approach to try to replicate the human brain structure inside an AI system. According to Kai-Fu Lee, “Instead of trying to teach the computer the rules that had been mastered by a human brain, these practitioners tried to reconstruct the human brain itself. Given that the tangled webs of neurons in animal brains were the only thing capable of intelligence as we knew it, these researchers figured they’d go straight to the source. This approach mimics the brain’s underlying architecture, constructing layers of artificial neurons that can receive and transmit information in a structure akin to our networks of biological neurons.”13 Kai-Fu Lee, Ai Superpowers, China, Silicon Valley and New World Order, Boston, Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, 2018, p. 8. Kai-Fu Lee highlights that “Unlike the rule-based approach, builders of neural networks generally do not give the networks rules to follow in making decisions. They simply feed lots and lots of examples of a given phenomenon—pictures, chess games, sounds—into the neural networks and let the networks themselves identify patterns within the data. In other words, the less human interference, the better.”14 Kai-Fu Lee, AI Superpowers, China, Silicon Valley and New World Order, Boston, Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, 2018, p. 8. [2] Deep Learning Deep learning is a specific field of machine learning that “focuses on deep neural network models.”15 John D. Kelleher, Deep Learning, Cambridge, The MIT Press, 2019, p. 8. According to Martin Ford, “deep learning is a type of machine learning that uses deep (or many layered) artificial neural networks software that roughly emulates the way neurons operate in the brain,” having been “the primary driver of the revolution in AI that we have seen in the last decade or so.”16 Martin Ford, Architects of Intelligence: The Truth About AI from the People Building It. Birmingham, Packt Publishing, 2018. p. 10. John D. Kelleher mentions that “Deep learning is the subfield of artificial intelligence that focuses on creating large neural network models that are capable of making accurate data-driven decisions.”17 Deep Learning, Cambridge, The MIT Press, 2019, p. 1. The author indicates that “The term deep learning describes a family of neural network models that have multiple layers of simple information processing programs, known as neurons, in the network” (Deep Learning, supra, p. 65). The author continues, “Deep learning is particularly suited to contexts where the data is complex and where there are large datasets available.” It is a kind of AI method used for image searching, image translation, face detection, and speech recognition. [C] Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is “a class of machine learning technologies that can generate new content—such as text, images, music, or video—by analyzing patterns in existing data.”18 Erik Brynjolfsson, Danielle Li and Lindsey R. Raymond, Generative AI at Work, NBER Working Paper No. 31161, April 2023, pp. 3-4. Available at http://www.nber.org/papers/w31161, accessed on December 2, 2025. Brynjolfsson, Li and Raymond add that “Unlike traditional programming, generative AI does not require explicit instructions as inputs. Instead, it uses ML to mine vast amounts of human-generated data to recognize patterns, allowing it to generate, summarize, and make inferences based on those patterns.”19 Erik Brynjolfsson, Danielle Li and Lindsey R. Raymond, Generative AI at Work, NBER Working Paper No. 31161, April 2023, p. 6. Available at http://www.nber.org/papers/w31161, accessed on December 2, 2025. [D] Large Language Models (LLMs) Large Language Models are a type of generative AI tool that combines the techniques of machine learning and deep learning and uses neural networks. Stephen Wolfram affirms that “ChatGPT is based on the concept of neural nets—originally invented in the 1940s as an idealization of the operations of brains.”20 What Is ChatGPT Doing … and Why Does It Work? Wolfram Media, 2023, kindle edition, p. 6. It also involves natural language processing (NLP) technology. Natural language processing is a subfield of artificial intelligence that comprehends techniques used to recognize and process human language. Initially, natural language processing (NLP) relied on rule-based processing, with specific rules defining syntax. The evolution of AI systems—particularly their shift toward machine learning and deep learning techniques—also enabled advances in NLP, allowing it to move beyond predefined rules and instead rely on the processing of large volumes of data and specific statistical inferences that make it possible to understand natural language and to generate responses in natural language as well.21 A comprehensive view of NLP techniques is found in Z. Krutilla and A. Kovari, “The origin and primary areas of application of natural language processing,” 2022 IEEE 22nd International Symposium on Computational Intelligence and Informatics and 8th IEEE International Conference on Recent Achievements in Mechatronics, Automation, Computer Science and Robotics (CINTI-MACRo), Budapest, Hungary, 2022, pp. 000293-000298, http://dx.doi.org/10.1109/CINTI-MACRo57952.2022.10029432, accessed on February 18, 2026. Available at: https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10029432, accessed on January 2, 2026. Jens Frankenreiter and Julian Nyarko cite that “like much other AI research, NLP has been a topic of inquiry (in one form or another) since at least the 1950s. In earlier decades, and in line with the then-governing paradigm guiding much research in AI, NLP operated on the basis of a “top down” approach: Computers extracted information from texts following complex sets of hand-coded rules drawn up by linguists and other experts. Since the 1990s, NLP has undergone a profound transition that mirrors similar developments in other areas of AI. Today, most NLP follows a “bottom up” approach, relying heavily on ML and similar statistical techniques. These approaches allow computers to “learn” how to process language on the basis of large amounts of training data.”22 Jens Frankenreiter and Julian Nyarko, Natural Language Processing in Legal Tech, in Engstrom DF ed., Legal Tech and the Future of Civil Justice, Cambridge University Press, 2023, p. 75 https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009255301. LLMs also combine a transformer machine learning model in the context of a deep neural network. According to David Nield, “[a]ll of this text data, wherever it comes from, is processed through a neural network, a commonly used type of AI engine made up of multiple nodes and layers. These networks continually adjust the way they interpret and make sense of data based on a host of factors, including the results of previous trial and error. Most LLMs use a specific neural network architecture called a transformer, which has some tricks particularly suited for language processing. (the ‘GPT’ from ChatGPT stands for Generative Pretrained Transformer).”23 Wired, April 30, 2023, available at https://www.wired.com/story/how-chatgpt-works-large-language-model/?bxid=5cec219efc942d3adaf2a4d2&bxid=5cec219efc942d3adaf2a4d2&cndid=57125742&cndid=57125742&esrc=DailyNLPromo&esrc=DailyNLPromo&hasha=630c7a79420177e20bd10fc204866f1f&hasha=630c7a79420177e20bd10fc204866f1f&hashb=576d6780b9322ffca1c6e70533507975362e91aa&hashb=576d6780b9322ffca1c6e70533507975362e91aa&source=Email_0_EDT_WIR_NEWSLETTER_0_BACKCHANNEL_ZZ&source=Email_0_EDT_WIR_NEWSLETTER_0_BACKCHANNEL_ZZ&utm_brand=wired&utm_brand=wired&utm_campaign=aud-dev&utm_campaign=aud-dev&utm_content=WIR_Backchannel_050523&utm_content=WIR_Backchannel_050523&utm_mailing=WIR_Backchannel_050523&utm_mailing=WIR_Backchannel_050523&utm_medium=email&utm_medium=email&utm_source=nl&utm_source=nl&utm_term=WIR_Backchannel&utm_term=WIR_Backchannel, accessed on December 5, 2025. The transformer models in large language models process data and define relationships based on an “attention model.”24 “Attention is all you need. Advances in neural information processing systems” is considered the pioneer work on transformers and attention models (A. Vaswani et al., Attention Is All You Need. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 2017, available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762v7, accessed on December 4, 2025). Stephen Wolfram summarizes the idea this way: “The idea of transformers is to do something at least somewhat similar for sequences of tokens that make up a piece of text. But instead of just defining a fixed region in the sequence over which there can be connections, transformers instead introduce the notion of ‘attention’ and the idea of ‘paying attention’ more to some parts of the sequence than others.”25 What Is ChatGPT Doing … and Why Does It Work? Wolfram Media, 2023, kindle edition, p. 68. By combining NLP with machine learning (particularly deep learning), LLMs enable the processing of large volumes of written or spoken language and make it possible to generate text (written language) and even spoken language. [E] Computing Power and Data: Two Essential Elements to the AI (R)evolution There is no doubt that the evolution of the techniques and methods employed in artificial intelligence systems has been essential to reaching the current stage of AI development. Besides research advances in computer science and related fields, two important elements must be considered when examining this evolution. The first is the expansion of computing power. In 1956, when the Dartmouth College Conference was held, computing systems were extremely rudimentary. From the 1970s to the 1980s onwards, there was an exponential evolution in computing systems. The computational capacity of a modern high-end smartphone now exceeds that of the early mainframe-based systems. As a result, both the increase in computing power and the interconnection of computing systems—combined with advances in miniaturisation and energy efficiency—allow us to carry, in our pockets, a computer capable of running AI systems. The second element is data, which is essential for training AI systems. Whereas early systems relied on small, carefully curated datasets that were highly structured and tailored to specific tasks, today AI systems and models are trained on vast and diverse collections of data, much of it drawn from the web, spanning multiple domains and formats. This shift from narrow, structured inputs to heterogeneous and largely unstructured data has been critical in enabling the flexibility and performance of today’s AI technologies. Three circumstances related to the data available to train AI systems deserve special attention. First, there is an abundance of data, the phenomenon commonly referred to as big data.26 “The term big data has been in use since the 1990s, with some giving credit to John Mashey for popularizing the term” (Wikipedia Contributors, Big Data. In Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia, December 29, 2025. Retrieved January 3, 2026, from https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Big_data&oldid=1330156857, accessed on February 18, 2026). The concept of big data emerged especially after the 1990s, when the volume of data generated by corporate data centers, the internet, e-commerce, and later by mobile devices and internet of things (IoT) devices, increased dramatically, exceeding the capacity of traditional data-processing systems. According to a 2014 White-House report entitled “Big Data: Seizing Opportunities, Preserving Values,” “The collection, storage, and analysis of data is on an upward and seemingly unbounded trajectory, fueled by increases in processing power, the cratering costs of computation and storage, and the growing number of sensor technologies embedded in devices of all kinds. In 2011, some estimated the amount of information created and replicated would surpass 1.8 zettabytes. In 2013, estimates reached 4 zettabytes of data generated worldwide.” Available at https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/sites/default/files/docs/big_data_privacy_report_may_1_2014.pdf, accessed on January 2, 2026. The volume of available data is enormous.27 Darren S. Tucker and Hill B. Wellford mention that “generally, ‘big data’ refers to a collection of data sets so large and complex that traditional database systems cannot effectively manage or process the information. Information technology research company Gartner defines big data as ‘high-volume, high-velocity and high-variety information assets that demand cost-effective, innovative forms of information processing for enhanced insight and decision making.’ As this widely adopted ‘3V’ definition suggests, big data is not defined only by volume, but also by the complexity of the data (e.g., different types of structured or unstructured data, including text, image, audio, and video files) and the need for the data to be collected and analyzed rapidly” (Big Mistakes Regarding Big Data, Antitrust Source, American Bar Association, December 1, 2014, p. 2. Available at SSRN: https://ssrn.com/abstract=2549044, accessed on January 2, 2026). Second, a large share of these data is digitized or is born digital. Finally, this data is distributed across multiple domains of the web and is accessible to a large portion of the population. The combination of significant and growing computational capacity with an immense volume of data available for training and feeding AI systems and models enables the rapid evolution of these systems. As discussed below, these two elements are equally relevant to assessing the evolution of the use of AI in the legal field. [F] Using AI in the Legal Domain The use of AI systems in the legal field followed the pace of their evolution in other domains. In law, as elsewhere, the earliest AI systems developed for legal applications took the form of expert systems—systems designed to emulate the decision-making processes of human specialists in narrow domains.28 Richard Susskind says, “When I began work in the field of AI and law, one approach dominated—the knowledge and reasoning processes of legal experts were mined from their heads through an interview process known as ‘knowledge elicitation’. That knowledge was then codified in complex decision trees, and dropped into computer systems, around which non-expert users could navigate. We called them rule-based expert systems. They put questions to users and were able to provide legal answers and draft legal documents, often at a higher standard than human experts” (Tomorrow’s Lawyers, 3rd ed., Oxford University Press, Oxford, 2023, pp. 256-257). A longstanding example is the use of the earlier dedicated e-discovery systems by legal professionals.29 Currently, E-discovery platforms have largely moved beyond traditional expert systems. Instead, they employ predictive coding, continuous active learning (CAL), and natural language processing (NLP), all rooted in machine learning rather than static rule sets. Another example of AI use in the legal domain is related to predictive systems. These systems, once trained on information drawn from a large number of court decisions, provide an estimate of the likely outcome of a given case. They rely on the processing of concrete data and statistical correlations to assess the probability that a particular legal issue will be decided in one way or another, taking into account judicial precedents and prior rulings.
