More recently, with the evolution of artificial intelligence systems themselves and the growing use of LLMs, the potential applications of AI in the legal field have multiplied. There is now a profusion of AI systems designed for legal use. From legal process automation, contract automation and review, and the management of precedents and case dockets, to legal research and the drafting of legal submissions, what can be observed today is a rapid expansion in the range of systems built on AI technologies.30 F. Dehghani et al. provide a broad view of the different systems and use of AI systems in the legal field (Large Language Models in Legal Systems: A Survey, Humanities and Social Science Communications, 12, 1977, 2025, https://doi.org/10.1057/s41599-025-05924-3, accessed on January 2, 2026). [G] The Turning Point: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) However, there is a particularly significant milestone in the use of AI systems in the legal field.31 This milestone actually represents a radical shift in the use of AI tools across various domains and by the general public. As will be seen, it is equally significant for the law, considering the epistemological foundations of law and the way legal professionals work. This milestone dates from November 2022, when ChatGPT-3.5 was publicly released, marking the beginning of an exponential expansion of readily-available, accessible generative AI tools and large language models (LLMs). Generative artificial intelligence significantly enhanced the tools for dealing with legal activities. The processing of written and spoken language, as well as text generation, are core functions of large language models within the GenAI paradigm. Although natural Language Processing (NLP) technology already existed prior to these developments, its use was quite limited, and it was not widely adopted. The advent of LLMs and generative AI has altered this landscape. What was once confined to highly specialized systems with limited applications has become broadly accessible. LLMs are transformative precisely because they constitute an evolution of the AI tools that can operate on human language. In some sense, LLM systems added a new layer to the NLP systems. They improved the capabilities of language processing within AI systems. Note that law is, at its core, a linguistic profession. Contracts, briefs, arbitral pleadings, awards, hearings and submissions are all constructed through human language. Accordingly, when machines become capable of processing, generating, and “interpreting” text and language in general at scale, they inevitably have a profound impact on legal practice.32 The capacity of large language models (LLMs) to interpret and to engage in reasoning has been questioned in a growing body of literature. See, for example, Andrew Coan and Harry Surden, Artificial Intelligence and Constitutional Interpretation, 96 University of Colorado Law Review 413 (February 12, 2025), Arizona Legal Studies Discussion Paper No. 24-30, University of Colorado Law Legal Studies Research Paper No. 24-39. Available at SSRN: https://ssrn.com/abstract=5018779, accessed on January 26, 2026. In the same sense, Stevan Harnad, Language Writ Large: LLMs, ChatGPT, Meaning and Understanding, 7 Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence 1490698 (2024), available https://doi.org/10.3389/frai.2024.1490698, accessed on January 26, 2026. In this vein, Zirui Ren and Ziming Liu conclude that “HRM does not ‘reason’ in the commonsense way of approaching the solution gradually, despite using recursive architecture to mimic human reasoning behavior. If one insists on making an analogy to human intelligence, it resembles ‘guessing’ more than ‘reasoning’” (Z. Ren and Z. Liu (n.d.), Are Your Reasoning Models Reasoning or Guessing? A Mechanistic Analysis of Hierarchical Reasoning Models, Shanghai Qi Zhi Institute & Tsinghua University, available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.10679v1, accessed on January 26, 2026). By contrast, some scholars argue that large language models may exhibit a limited degree of rationality and interpretative capacity. That is the case of H.T. Nguyen et al., LLMs for Legal Reasoning: A Unified Framework and Future Perspectives, 58 Computer Law & Security Review: The International Journal of Technology Law and Practice 106165 (2025), available at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clsr.2025.106165, accessed on January 26, 2025). The authors adopt an intermediate, pragmatic position. While acknowledging that LLMs operate through pattern completion, they argue that such models nonetheless exhibit evidence of reasoning capabilities that can be leveraged and further enhanced when embedded within a logical framework (including rules, abductive reasoning, and case-based structures). The scale, context awareness, and generative capabilities of LLMs provide many features that could transform legal workflows, including drafting and semantic search. There are many examples of the capabilities of LLMs in the legal field. First, LLMs can “interpret” legal language with a high degree of semantic sensitivity, including complex syntax, legal terminology, and contextual nuance. This improves tasks such as legal research, issue spotting, and document review. Second, modern LLMs can process and retain context across long documents (contracts, pleadings, awards, statutes), enabling coherent analysis, cross-referencing, and consistency checks within and across legal texts. Third, LLMs can generate first drafts of legal documents (contracts, briefs, memoranda, procedural submissions), rephrase arguments, adapt tone and style, and produce multilingual versions, significantly reducing drafting time. Fourth, unlike keyword-based systems, LLM-powered tools enable semantic search, allowing users to retrieve relevant cases, statutes, or clauses based on meaning rather than exact wording. Fifth, LLMs can condense long legal documents into structured summaries, extract key arguments, holdings, risks, or obligations, and present them at different levels of detail. Sixth, by analyzing large volumes of legal data, LLMs can identify recurring patterns in precedents, contractual clauses, or procedural outcomes, supporting comparative analysis and strategy formulation. Seventh, LLMs can translate and align legal concepts across languages and jurisdictions, facilitating comparative law analysis and cross-border legal work. Eighth, LLMs enable conversational interaction, allowing lawyers to refine queries, test hypotheses, explore alternative formulations, and iteratively improve legal reasoning and outputs. Taken together, these features could shift legal work from purely document-centric processes to more data-driven, semantic, and assisted workflows, with efficiency gains concentrated in research, drafting, review, and knowledge management. [H] A True AI (R)evolution in the Legal Domain and Legal Decision Making? One might argue that the current boom in AI systems—including in the legal field—is merely a passing trend. However, there are concrete elements that must be taken into account, and they point to a genuine turning point in the use of AI systems in the law. As Richard Susskind observes, it is possible to identify what he defines as the changing infrastructure of information. He argues that every major transformation in law follows a shift in the information infrastructure of the society: from oral to written traditions; from print to digital systems; and now to data-driven and AI-mediated environments.33 According to Susskind: “For now, we are coming to the end of the transitional phase between the third and fourth stages of development, between a print-based industrial society and an AI-based digital society. The key point here is that the information sub-structure in society determines to a large extent how much law we have, how complex it is, how regularly it changes, and those who are able, responsibly and knowledgeably, to advise upon it. If we examine the manner in which the law has evolved throughout history, we can understand the shifts in terms of changes in information sub-structure. At its core, then, law is information-based. And we are in the middle of an information revolution. It is not making a wild leap to suggest that the law and the work of lawyers will not emerge unscathed” (Tomorrow’s Lawyers, 3rd ed., Oxford University Press, Oxford, 2023, p. 264). This transition—from paper to digital, and from documents to data—has the potential to revolutionize legal processes, including arbitration. The scale of the introduction of generative AI systems into the legal field is capable of bringing about a revolution in the practice of law, both in how lawyers and legal professionals formulate their claims and arguments, and in how courts and arbitral tribunals decide the matters submitted to them. This issue will be explained in greater detail later in this chapter, where concrete data and trends will be presented to support this assertion. This transformation raises a number of questions and presents significant challenges for a field that has traditionally not been at the forefront of adopting technological systems. The same is true in the context of arbitration and in the decision-making sphere. [I] The Current (Legal) AI Landscape At present, the evolution of AI systems more broadly is also evident in the legal domain. As generative AI models advance, a range of dedicated and specialized systems for legal tasks are developed using these same technologies. The use of AI tools has expanded significantly and has been increasingly adopted both by legal professionals and by state courts. There are numerous reports of experiments with and applications of AI in legal proceedings. In Brazil, for example, the National Council of Justice (Conselho Nacional de Justiça—CNJ), the body responsible for coordinating and overseeing all branches of the Judiciary (including state courts, federal courts, and specialized courts such as labor courts), recently regulated, in 2025, the use of AI by judges and courts.34 This refers to Resolution No. 615 of March 11, 2025. The resolution sets out guidelines for the development, use, and governance of solutions developed with artificial intelligence resources within the Judiciary. Available at https://atos.cnj.jus.br/atos/detalhar/6001, accessed on December 2, 2025. This does not mean that AI systems were not already being used by judges and courts prior to the adoption of the regulatory framework. Rather, after extensive debate, the CNJ set out a number of principles and rules governing the use of AI by Brazilian judges and courts.35 Prior to Resolution No. 615, the CNJ had already issued, in 2020, Resolution No. 332/2020, which set out guidelines on ethics, transparency, and governance in the development and use of artificial intelligence within the Judiciary. Available at https://atos.cnj.jus.br/files/original191707202008255f4563b35f8e8.pdf, accessed on December 2, 2025. One should also not overlook the relevance of off-the-shelf, commercial artificial intelligence systems, whose capabilities expand and evolve on a daily basis, to the broader adoption of AI tools in the legal field.36 As some studies referenced later in this chapter show, Brazilian judges and courts make extensive use of off-the-shelf commercial AI systems, without any customization. Among the most widely used commercial systems are ChatGPT (OpenAI), Gemini (Google), Claude (Anthropic), Copilot (Microsoft), LLaMA (Meta), Perplexity, DeepSeek (China), Notebook LM (Google), and Grok (xAI). Both specific tools developed by courts and general commercial tools differ in their design and capabilities, but they share a common feature: they are rapidly expanding and transforming the way lawyers, arbitrators, and legal institutions operate. Notably, even earlier models such as GPT-4 performed at or above the passing threshold in several U.S. Bar Examinations, demonstrating a significant level of legal reasoning capability.37 Daniel Martin Katz et al., GPT-4 Passes the Bar Exam, March 15, 2023, 382 Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A (2024), Available at SSRN: https://ssrn.com/abstract=4389233 or http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.4389233, accessed on February 18, 2026. Today, generative AI tools are used to summarize and search documents, videos, and audio content; to draft, revise, translate, and review texts and documents; to support brainstorming and the generation of legal argument; to assist with predictive analytics and decision making; to help select arbitrators and identify potential conflicts of interest; and to automate a wide range of procedural tasks.

III The Use of AI by Brazilian Courts and Judges [A] Initial Remarks The analysis of two successive reports produced by the National Council of Justice, the body responsible for the administration and coordination of the Brazilian Judiciary, provides highly relevant data for understanding the use of AI in the legal field. Brazil is a country of vast territorial size and a large population, and its justice system is among the busiest in the world in terms of the number of cases and decisions. In addition, Brazil exerts significant influence in Latin America due to its commercial weight and its geopolitical relevance in the region. These data are therefore particularly relevant for identifying trends in a country whose experience may offer broader insights into developments across the region as a whole. However, although these reports concern the use of AI by the judiciary, the trends they capture may also be useful for examining similar developments in arbitration. In general, in most countries, the judiciary tends to be more conservative than arbitration practitioners, who are generally more inclined to adopt new technologies. The Brazilian Courts’ experience with AI, on the contrary, inverted this tendency. The pioneering role of the Brazilian judiciary in experimenting with AI tools could provide some guidance for the use of AI in arbitration.38 Note that this was not a planned process. The use of AI in the Brazilian judiciary is essentially fragmentary and noncoordinated. These was one of the problems diagnosed by the CNJ reports and that are identified by other studies, as the study The Future of AI in the Brazilian Judicial System, by the School of International Public Administration of the Columbia University and the Institute of Technology and Society of Rio de Janeiro—ITS-Rio (available at https://itsrio.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/SIPA-Capstone-The-Future-of-AI-in-the-Brazilian-Judicial-System-1.pdf, accessed on January 26, 2026). As will be demonstrated below, the fragmented experience of Brazilian courts in the use of AI tools, as well as the attempts to establish coordination by the National Council of Justice (CNJ), are relevant because the results achieved and the difficulties encountered in Brazil may be taken into account in the effective implementation of AI tools in arbitration and by arbitral institutions. At the same time, another relevant factor must be taken into account: the stark difference between the volume of cases handled by the courts and those submitted to arbitration. It is therefore reasonable to expect that the judiciary may turn to AI tools, at least in part, as a means of coping with the sheer volume of judicial proceedings. [B] The 2024 National Council of Justice (CNJ) Report on the Use of Generative AI In 2024, the National Council of Justice (CNJ) published a report on the use of Generative AI tools and systems by Brazilian Courts and Judges,39 Available at https://www.cnj.jus.br/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/cnj-relatorio-de-pesquisa-iag-pj.pdf, accessed on December 12, 2025. titled “The Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence in the Brazilian Judiciary.” The report presents key conclusions on the governance of generative AI (GAI) and the main findings of an empirical survey conducted with judges and judicial staff. The poll was completed by 1,681 judges (9.1% of the total) and 16,844 judicial staff members (6.0% of the total), drawn from a total population of 18,464 judges and 278,755 judicial staff members. [C] Main Empirical Findings on the Use of Generative AI in Brazilian Courts The survey of judges and judicial staff produced the following key findings. [1] Use and frequency The use of generative AI is significant, with approximately half of the judges and judicial staff reporting prior experience with such tools, although not necessarily in the context of court-related activities. Professional use within judicial functions is meaningful but still infrequent, with most respondents describing it as rare or occasional. Usage levels and frequency are higher among judges and staff who are also engaged in academic activities, whether as students or faculty. [2] Tools and Interest Most respondents rely on open or freely available tools accessible on the internet for court work—approximately 75% of judges and 90% of judicial staff among those who use generative AI.40 The report mentions that “Among those who reported having experience with GenAI, the majority indicated that they had used it in their personal lives. However, a significant proportion—around one-third of GenAI users, corresponding to approximately one-sixth of judges and court staff overall—reported having already used GenAI tools in their professional activities” (p. 57). By contrast, the use of generative AI tools officially provided by the courts remains very limited. Interest in the potential usefulness of generative AI is high, suggesting a trend toward increased adoption. More than 90% of the respondents expressed interest in training and capacity-building programs related to the use of these technologies. [3] Types of Use and Associated Risks Most of the reported uses of generative AI in professional activities are considered appropriate by the report.41 According to the report, “The central concern of the survey was to investigate the practices of users themselves—rather than those of the institutions or courts that make up the Judiciary. In other words, the study sought to understand the extent to which these tools are actually used by judges and court staff, the tasks or purposes for which they are used (so as to assess whether the practices adopted are appropriate), the frequency of use, and users’ degree of familiarity with GenAI. It also examined the main advantages, disadvantages, and perceived limitations of their use, the level of transparency surrounding the use of these tools, and the views of court staff on whether such use should be encouraged or prohibited within the Judiciary and for which purposes, as well as on the desirability of training and instruction on these tools” (p. 49). A significant proportion of the respondents reported using these tools for general research, and notably for researching precedents and case law. While this practice is not inherently problematic, the report emphasizes the need for guidance on verifying results, given the known limitations of generative AI systems. [4] Perceived Difficulties The main challenges reported by users include the lack of familiarity with the technology (approximately 51.9%), inaccuracies and imprecision in generated content, and uncertainty regarding the legality and ethical propriety of its use. Importantly, awareness of these limitations is viewed positively by the report, as it suggests a greater propensity to review AI-generated content and thereby mitigate automation bias.42 The report mentions that “As the second most frequently cited reason, 48.9% of judges pointed to a lack of confidence in the results generated, and in 32.6% of cases the results were reported to contain many errors. From a governance and ethical perspective, this is a positive finding, as it distances courts from automation bias and indicates greater care in reviewing outputs generated by machines” (p. 63). [5] Transparency (A Critical Issue) A particularly concerning finding is that a majority of the respondents do not disclose their use of generative AI to peers or hierarchical superiors. This lack of transparency undermines internal document review processes and may increase the risk of inaccuracies or errors in the delivery of judicial services.43 When dealing with the issue of the transparency in use, the report indicates that “It is concerning that most users do not disclose the use of GenAI tools to their peers or superiors, which may hinder internal review and lead to inaccuracies or errors in the results” and that “There is a need to promote transparency, including through the official provision of tools and the adoption of awareness-raising measures” (p. 81). The report therefore highlights the need for awareness-raising initiatives and for the official provision of AI tools by the courts as mechanisms to foster transparency. [6] General Perceptions Overall, the respondents express a strong belief in the potential of generative AI to enhance efficiency and improve the quality of judicial services. At the same time, judges and judicial staff voice concerns regarding the social impacts of these technologies and their effects on human relations in the workplace. They emphasize the importance of gradual adaptation, structured training programs, and the continued recognition of the value of public servants in the context of technological transformation. [7] Governance and Ethical Issues The report concludes that existing measures designed to mitigate the risks associated with traditional artificial intelligence systems—such as errors, opacity, discrimination, and privacy violations—are insufficient to address the specific challenges posed by generative artificial intelligence. This assessment supports the need for a revision of governance policies and of the current regulatory framework adopted by the National Council of Justice (CNJ). Generative AI systems are capable of producing new content—text, audio, images, and video—that may be indistinguishable from human-generated material. However, such outputs may contain errors, inaccuracies, and so-called hallucinations (including references to nonexistent facts or documents), particularly in technical domains, which makes expert review indispensable. The main ethical concerns relate to automation bias—that is, the excessive reliance on AI-generated outputs—and lack of transparency. To mitigate these risks, the report recommends a set of institutional measures, including the official provision of AI tools by the courts, training and capacity-building for judicial staff, and the systematic assessment of GAI providers, particularly with respect to quality, privacy protection, and sustainability. [D] The 2025 National Council of Justice (CNJ) Report on the Use of AI Later, in 2025, the report “Artificial Intelligence in the Judiciary 2024” mapped artificial intelligence (AI) projects within the Brazilian Judiciary and was conducted by the National Council of Justice (CNJ) in partnership with the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP).44 Available at https://www.cnj.jus.br/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/relatorio-ia-2024-resumo-executivo-set2025.pdf, accessed on December 10, 2025. The main conclusions and findings of the study are revealing and can be summarized as follows: [1] Consolidation and Overview of AI Projects First, the report confirms the consolidation of AI-based solutions within the Brazilian Judiciary. In 2024, 178 AI initiatives were recorded across courts and judicial councils. This figure includes 98 new projects developed during the year and 80 projects initiated in previous years that remained active. The survey covered 92 courts and councils, representing 96.8% of all judicial bodies. Of these, 58 developed AI projects in 2024, although the number of institutions with active projects declined slightly compared to 2023, when 62 bodies reported AI initiatives. According to CNJ, the primary motivation for developing AI projects remains the pursuit of greater efficiency and procedural speed, followed by innovation in internal processes. Most projects in 2024 focused on judicial decision support (35.8%) and case-management solutions (25.7%). [2] Use of Generative AI The 2024 edition of the study introduced a dedicated section on generative AI, confirming that these tools are already present in courts and councils, although not yet predominant. According to the findings, 45.8% of courts and councils reported using generative AI tools in their operations.45 “Among the bodies that use generative AI, the data show that, in 57.6% of cases, professionals access these tools through personal email accounts—30.4% using paid versions of the services, individually contracted, and 27.2% using the free versions. In 23.9% of cases, the institutions themselves acquire licenses and distribute them to judges, court staff, and outsourced personnel. In addition, in 18.5% of cases professionals use their institutional email accounts to access these tools, with 10.9% using the free version and 7.6% using the paid version, acquired at their own expense” (p. 26). Among the institutions that do not yet use such tools (54.2%), 81.2% expressed an interest in adopting them in the coming years. Current applications are mainly related to text-based tasks, such as the generation, improvement, and summarization of texts or documents, as well as spelling and language review. [3] Challenges and Ethical Concerns The study confirms that the implementation of AI requires careful and transparent procedures, closely aligned with ethical and legal considerations. One significant concern identified by the report is the lack of institutional coordination in the use of generative AI. Among professionals who use these tools, 57.6% do so through personal email accounts, raising concerns about data privacy and procedural security. In addition, more than half of the courts and councils (50%) reported that they do not have specific internal guidelines or policies governing the use of generative AI. Within this group, 26.3% have no guidelines at all, 23.7% are in the process of developing them, and 21.2% rely on CNJ Resolution No. 332/2020 as a reference. The lack of qualified personnel was identified as a major challenge across several sections of the study, alongside insufficient infrastructure for AI development and difficulties in obtaining data of adequate quantity, quality, and diversity. The principal ethical concerns highlighted include the risk of violation of party privacy, discrimination and bias in AI outputs, and issues of accountability in the event of erroneous decisions. [4] Sinapses Platform and Data Use Integration with the national AI platform Sinapses remained limited in 2024.46 Sinapses is the national platform for the storage, supervised training, version control, distribution, and auditing of artificial intelligence models for the Judiciary, as well as setting out the parameters for its implementation and operation. It was established in August 2020, by the CNJ Resolution No. 332/2020. A total of 80.6% of AI projects were not integrated with the platform, while only 19.4% were partially or fully integrated. The platform received an average rating of 6.01 in 2024, representing a decline from 6.41 in 2023 and 8.06 in 2022. The existence of a unified national platform to manage AI services and systems is an objective pursued by the National Council of Justice (CNJ). The use of the Sinapses platform is intended to unify the understanding and use of AI across the Brazilian Judiciary (that has a two-tiered structure, comprehending the federal Judiciary—including specialized Courts, as labor and military Courts—and 27 different State Courts), expand knowledge about these tools and systems, and, ultimately, ensure the appropriate use of AI tools by the judiciary, judges, and court staff.47 According to the report, “The second challenge identified by the teams is the lack of institutional infrastructure for the development of AI projects. Training robust AI models requires large volumes of data and computational equipment with processing capacity beyond that of ordinary computers. The Sinapses platform could address this need; however, the data show that its adoption was not significant in 2024: only 19.4% of projects were partially or fully integrated into the platform. With an average score of 6.01 in 2024 and 6.41 in 2023, the tool appears to have room for improvement, although it is not unfamiliar to the teams: 70.4% reported that at least one team member had participated in training on Sinapses in 2024 or in previous years. Infrastructure limitations for developing AI projects ultimately make the use of cloud services more attractive, which in turn revives concerns related to the privacy of sensitive data and budgetary constraints” (p. 43). With regard to data sources, most AI projects in 2024 (68.2%) relied on internal data from the institution that developed them, whereas only 5.4% used data provided by other judicial bodies. [5] Main Conclusions Overall, the pursuit of efficiency and procedural speed remains the primary driver of AI development within the Judiciary. However, the fact that 59% of AI initiatives were developed without external partnerships may limit the broader dissemination and scaling of their benefits across the Brazilian judicial system.48 The report also mentions that “Among the courts and councils that use generative AI tools, 37% report having institutional partnerships with companies that provide these services, while 63% indicate that they do not have such agreements. Among the partnerships established, the main providers are Microsoft, creator of Copilot, in 44.1% of cases; Google, with Gemini, in 20.6%; and OpenAI, responsible for ChatGPT, in 11.8% of cases” (p. 27). [E] The Current Use of Generative AI by Judges and Courts in Brazil The two CNJ reports empirically confirm a central finding: AI tools and systems are already being used by judges and courts in Brazil. Recognizing this fact is essential in order to adopt the appropriate safeguards and precautions for the responsible use of AI technologies. More importantly, this is not a matter of a distant trend or speculative forecasts. Artificial intelligence has already become part of the everyday reality of judicial proceedings in Brazil. [F] The Enactment of CNJ’s Resolution No. 615 of 2025 In March 2025, the National Council of Justice (CNJ) enacted Resolution No. 615, establishing a comprehensive regulatory framework for the governance, development, and auditing of artificial intelligence within the Brazilian Judiciary.49 Available at https://atos.cnj.jus.br/atos/detalhar/6001, accessed on December 2, 2025. It replaced the previous 2020 regulation to address the rapid emergence of generative AI and large language models (LLMs). Grounded in the protection of fundamental rights and the centrality of the human person, the resolution mandates that all AI initiatives be registered in the Sinapses platform—a centralized repository for training and auditing models—and establishes a National Committee on Artificial Intelligence of the Judiciary to oversee compliance and update risk classifications. The text emphasizes that AI systems must remain transparent, explainable, and nondiscriminatory, requiring continuous algorithmic impact assessments to detect and mitigate bias throughout the system’s life cycle. The regulation adopts a strict risk-based classification system, explicitly prohibiting AI applications that pose excessive threats to civil liberties, such as predictive policing tools based on personality traits, social scoring systems, and biometric emotion recognition. Permissible applications are divided into high- and low-risk categories. High-risk uses, including those that assist in sentencing, evidence valuation, or facial recognition, require rigorous impact assessments and public summaries of their algorithmic logic. Conversely, low-risk classifications are reserved for administrative efficiency tasks, such as document summarization, transcription, and precedent organization, provided these tools do not replace human evaluation. Regarding the specific use of generative AI, the resolution establishes that such tools are strictly auxiliary, forbidding their use as autonomous decision-makers and enforcing a “privacy by design” approach to data governance. Magistrates and civil servants are prohibited from inputting sealed judicial records or sensitive personal data into private or external LLMs unless the data has been anonymized at the source, ensuring that confidential information is not used to train commercial models. Furthermore, in order to safeguard accountability, the text codifies the principle of human supremacy, affirming that judges remain fully liable for judicial acts and must scrutinize and confirm any AI-generated inputs, which are expressly nonbinding. [G] Some Lessons from the Brazilian Judiciary’s Experience Applicable to Arbitration Processes The decentralized and, to some extent, unplanned implementation of AI tools within the Brazilian Judiciary offers several useful lessons for the field of arbitration. While the experience has yielded positive outcomes, it has also produced negative effects, which were identified in both reports issued by the National Council of Justice (CNJ). [1] Planning and Oversight On the one hand, the Brazilian experience demonstrates that introducing new technologies into traditional procedural frameworks is neither simple nor straightforward. The effective use of AI tools may emerge in an organic and uncontrolled manner; however, such an approach can give rise to certain side effects, including a lack of oversight and limited transparency in their deployment. When transposed to arbitration, this experience suggests that the use of AI in arbitral proceedings should be introduced with caution and governed by explicit rules and procedures. Leaving the definition of AI use solely to arbitrators—or even to the parties—risks reproducing the same structural shortcomings identified in the CNJ’s reports. Such governance measures are more readily implemented in institutional arbitration, where proceedings are administered by arbitral institutions such as the American Arbitration Association (AAA) and its International Centre for Dispute Resolution (ICDR), the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC), or the Silicon Valley Arbitration and Mediation Center (SVAMC). By contrast, in ad hoc arbitration, the adoption and enforcement of comparable safeguards are considerably more challenging. In such cases, the parties and arbitrators must define these safeguards by drawing on experience developed in institutional arbitration or in more structured ad hoc settings. Evidently, the articulation of such conditions and premises on a case-by-case basis is far from ideal. [2] Interoperability and Transparency The lack of interoperability among systems and the consequent lack of transparency in the use of AI tools—one of the issues identified in the CNJ reports—is an evident consequence of the absence of planning in the introduction of AI tools by Brazilian courts and judges. The problem is identified by CNJ as an issue to be addressed through “the official provision of generative AI tools by the organisation, through dedicated interfaces, tends to enhance transparency among peers regarding their use, which is a valuable element of the responsible deployment of generative AI systems.”50 https://www.cnj.jus.br/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/relatorio-ia-2024-resumo-executivo-set2025.pdf, accessed on December 10, 2025, p. 53. The study The Future of AI in the Brazilian Judicial System recognized that this challenge must be tackled through the adoption of certain strategies, such as “having a common architecture and standard is essential to proceed a smooth integration process.”51 School of International Public Administration of the Columbia University and the Institute of Technology and Society of Rio de Janeiro—ITS-Rio (https://itsrio.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/SIPA-Capstone-The-Future-of-AI-in-the-Brazilian-Judicial-System-1.pdf, accessed on January 26, 2026, p. 32). In arbitral proceedings, the interoperability of AI systems and tools does not appear to be as critical as in the context of a national judiciary, where courts are expected to operate in a uniform manner in order to ensure equality of treatment and consistency across AI-assisted decisions and procedures. Nevertheless, the notion of interoperability extends beyond this narrow comparison. It also encompasses situations in which arbitral institutions prescribe or endorse the use of specific AI tools for case administration and, in some instances, for use within the arbitral proceedings themselves. In such scenarios, planning for interoperability—and for the potential use of data derived from prior arbitral proceedings—becomes essential. In this respect, the recommendations issued by the CNJ for the Brazilian judiciary are likewise relevant to arbitration proceedings more generally. [3] A Common Platform in Arbitration? A further relevant factor highlighted in the CNJ’s 2025 report concerns the need to expand both the use and the functionalities of the Sinapses platform. Sinapses is designed to standardize the use of AI tools and to enable interoperability among the various systems developed by courts and employed by judges and judicial staff. This need for interoperability and for a certain degree of uniformity in the treatment of AI tools and systems constitutes a necessary imperative for the governance of AI use within the Brazilian Judiciary itself. Excessive fragmentation of such systems hampers not only their effective use but also the evaluation and oversight of their deployment by courts and judges. In this sense, the effective implementation of the Sinapses portal would allow for greater scrutiny by the CNJ and by the courts themselves, as well as a more planned and coordinated development of AI tools. Transposing this experience to arbitral proceedings entails a more centralized definition of the AI tools and systems to be used by arbitrators and, where appropriate, by the parties. To some extent, this is precisely the approach being advanced by the AAA-ICDR (as it will be shown below) through the establishment of specific technological infrastructures for the conduct of arbitrations administered by the institution. The adoption of a dedicated, AI-enabled platform for the administration and conduct of arbitral proceedings enables enhanced control and greater transparency regarding the use of AI tools. The assessment and auditing of such tools may then be carried out in a unified manner and applied across an indeterminate number of arbitral proceedings. This illustrates how the absence of an institutionally defined tool may introduce additional layers of complexity in verifying and assessing the operation of disparate AI systems employed by arbitrators. An open model—under which each arbitrator and each party may rely on any AI tool of their choosing—creates a level of complexity that may ultimately undermine transparency and complicate the evaluation of the proper functioning of such tools and their effects on the arbitral process. The availability of an institutionalized AI system for the conduct of arbitral proceedings may ultimately become a factor—whether attractive or deterrent—in the parties’ decision to select a particular arbitral institution and its rules to govern the resolution of a dispute. This is a relevant consideration, as such institutionalization may enhance legal certainty, predictability, and transparency with respect to the use of AI tools. All these attributes are likely to be of increasing importance in the arbitral field. [4] AI Literacy and Training Another issue identified in the reports—which is, to some extent, common to the adoption of AI tools in other professions and activities—concerns the lack of training and technical knowledge among the relevant operators regarding the technology and its actual functioning. AI literacy is a fundamental factor not only for the effective implementation and use of these tools, but also to ensure that they do not produce detrimental effects on judicial proceedings or undermine the very notion of a fair and adequate judicial decision. As in the judicial context, arbitration likewise requires that arbitrators possess an adequate understanding of the AI tools employed in a given arbitral proceeding. Similarly, counsel acting in arbitration must have sufficient knowledge of such tools, including their functions, risks, and limitations. This requirement is commonly referred to as AI literacy and constitutes an essential element for both state judges and arbitral actors. In this sense, Sophie Nappert mentions that “arbitrators may soon be expected, and even required, to use AI tools to meet an emerging standard of technological competence, if only because the users of arbitration and their legal teams themselves use these tools as a time- and cost-saving measure.”52 The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Duties, Qualifications, and Selection of International Arbitrators, December 2025, Available at https://academic.oup.com/info-oxford-law-pro/pages/artificial-intelligence-arbitrators?login=false, accessed on January 26, 2026. According to the author, “Arbitrators can no longer afford to say, ‘I am just not a tech person.’ It is no solution to delegate this aspect of the arbitrator’s role to a tribunal secretary; delegation does not absolve arbitrators from understanding, in broad terms, how AI systems work, what data they rely on, and how they might introduce bias or error, because these questions go to the substantive decision-making that is at the centre of the arbitrator’s role.”

IV Perceptions Within the Arbitral Community Empirical observation by the author during informal polls among practitioners shows that practitioners readily embrace AI when used as a tool (for research, translation, or scheduling) or as an assistant (for drafting or summarization). Resistance intensifies, however, when AI is envisioned as a decision-maker. This tension reflects the broader normative dilemma of technological substitution in law: efficiency gains versus legitimacy concerns. [A] Empirical Evidence: The White & Case/Queen Mary Study (2025) A survey conducted by White & Case in collaboration with Queen Mary University of London (2025) points to a marked expansion in the use of digital technologies in arbitration.53 Available at https://www.qmul.ac.uk/arbitration/media/arbitration/docs/White-Case-QMUL-2025-International-Arbitration-Survey-report.pdf, accessed on December 10, 2025. According to the survey, it was based on the broadest empirical dataset collected to date, comprising 2,402 questionnaire responses and 117 in-depth interviews—almost twice the level of participation recorded in the previous survey.54 Although the total number of survey participants may not be particularly large when compared to the global population of practitioners involved in arbitration, it nonetheless provides relevant empirical evidence drawn from a significant cohort of professionals. Moreover, there is no known comparable study that has undertaken a survey of practitioners actively engaged in arbitration. The research drew on perspectives from a notably diverse group of stakeholders, including in-house counsel from both the public and private sectors, arbitrators, private practitioners, representatives of arbitral institutions and interest groups, academics, tribunal secretaries, experts, and third-party funders.55 “The respondent group consisted of private practitioners (35%), full-time arbitrators (17%), practitioners who split their time equally between acting as arbitrators and counsel (14%), arbitral institution staff (9%), academics (8%), in-house counsel from the private sector and government entities (7% and 3%, respectively), tribunal secretaries, experts (over 1% each), and other professional roles” (p. 37). According to the respondents, the main benefits of AI systems include faster document review and data analysis, improved discovery and drafting processes, a reduction in procedural costs and human error, and greater accessibility for parties with limited resources. Overall, the findings confirm that AI is no longer a peripheral tool but that it has become structurally embedded in the arbitral procedure. [1] Current Use and Future Expectations The survey reveals an expectation among participants of significant growth in the adoption of AI tools56 The survey does not mention the types of AI tools that were mentioned by participants. It takes this use in a broad and generic perspective, encompassing many types of AI tools. But there it identified a concern related to the “Impediments to accessing AI due to cost or other limitations were cited in 18% of responses. Participants noted that small law firms and businesses, as well as developing countries, might struggle to meet the price of premium legal AI products. They warned that higher costs of accessing AI may create divisions between the ‘haves and the have-nots’” (p. 31). over the next five years, driven primarily by its potential to increase efficiency and reduce costs. There is a broad consensus that international arbitration and its users will adopt and adapt to AI over the coming five years. At present, the main uses of AI include factual and legal research (reported by 64% of the respondents), as well as data analysis and document review. Looking ahead, adoption is expected to expand across all surveyed tasks. For instance, 91% of the respondents anticipate using AI for research and data analysis within the next five years. AI-assisted drafting and evaluation of legal arguments are also expected to grow substantially, with 75% expecting to use AI for drafting correspondence and 66% for drafting submissions, despite relatively limited use in the past.57 The great majority of respondents (72%) reported never using AI for drafting submissions, citing concerns about accuracy and reasoning. [2] Motivations for the Use of AI The primary drivers of increased AI use in international arbitration are time savings for parties and counsel (54%), cost reduction (44%), and the potential to reduce human error and inconsistencies (39%). Additional motivations include time savings for arbitrators (36%) and the perception that access to AI tools may enable participants with unequal resources to become more competitive (26%), thereby promoting a degree of “equality of arms” in complex disputes. [3] Obstacles and Concerns Enthusiasm for AI is tempered by significant concerns, which represent the main obstacles to its wider adoption. The most frequently cited concern is the risk of undetected errors and biases in AI systems (51%). Confidentiality and data security risks are also prominent (47%). Furthermore, a lack of knowledge or experience with AI (44%) and the absence of clear regulation or guidance on its use and disclosure (38%) are viewed as substantial barriers. Ethical concerns persist, particularly regarding AI’s ability to handle complex legal reasoning. In this context, 28% of respondents point to the risk of challenges to arbitral awards on due process grounds if arbitrators rely on AI. [4] Use of AI by Arbitrators The survey also assessed the perceived appropriateness of AI use by arbitrators across different tasks. There is strong approval for the use of AI in administrative, procedural, and mathematical tasks. This includes assistance with the calculation of damages, costs, and interest (77% approval) and the summarization of submissions or evidence (66% approval).58 The study mentions that “AI usage was also viewed with great scepticism when it came to assessing the merits or accuracy of party submissions or evidence, although 31% were in favour. Many respondents were concerned that AI would interfere with the arbitrator’s fundamental role to fulfil their mandate” (p. 32). By contrast, there is marked resistance to the use of AI in tasks that require the exercise of discretion and judgment, which the respondents viewed as central to the arbitrator’s mandate. Only 23% of the respondents approve of AI being used to draft the legal reasoning sections of awards or decisions. [5] Expected Impacts on Arbitral Practice Respondents foresee several impacts of AI on arbitration over the next five years. More than half (52%) expect arbitrators to increasingly rely on AI. Nearly half (48%) believe that arbitral proceedings will become faster, while 25% expect them to become less costly. In addition, 40% anticipate the creation of new roles dedicated to managing and implementing AI, while 24% predict a reduced need for tribunal secretaries. Taken together, these findings suggest that AI is increasingly perceived as a powerful enabling technology within arbitration, capable of accelerating routine and data-intensive tasks, while leaving core functions of legal judgment and discretion firmly in human hands. [B] Limits of the Study and General Trends Although specific and limited to the use of AI in arbitration, the Queen Mary University study reveals a broader trend that can be observed in arbitration more generally. In a sense, this trend is universal among those working in domestic and international arbitration. It is supported by concrete empirical data demonstrating that the use of AI tools is becoming increasingly widespread and may entail significant changes in how arbitration is practiced, as well as in how professionals involved in it (lawyers, arbitrators, and tribunal secretaries) work. Eliana Rodríguez-Salcedo, María Molina-Torres, Carlos Chagcha-Solís, Viviana Naranjo-Ruiz and Lidia Naranjo-Llerena, authors of the systematic review entitled “Rethinking Mediation and Arbitration in the Age of Artificial Intelligence,” examined the integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into the core mechanisms of Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR), such as mediation and arbitration.59 E. Rodríguez-Salcedo et al., Rethinking Mediation and Arbitration in the Age of Artificial Intelligence: A Systematic Review, Salud, Ciencia y Tecnología [Internet], October 15, 2025. Available from: https://sct.ageditor.ar/index.php/sct/article/view/2316 (https://doi.org/10.56294/saludcyt20252316), accessed on December 12, 2025. The purpose of the study was to systematically analyze the integration of AI by identifying its technological applications, ethical and legal implications, and practical impacts on ADR mechanisms. To this end, the review synthesized 30 peer-reviewed journal articles published between 2010 and 2025. The findings revealed that AI has improved the accessibility and operational performance of ADR, primarily through the automation of administrative tasks and assistance in document analysis and the generation of preliminary settlement proposals. In contexts such as arbitration, algorithms based on Natural Language Processing (NLP) and predictive analytics assist in managing extensive legal documentation. However, the implementation of these technologies exposes unresolved challenges, including algorithmic bias, data confidentiality, and the lack of regulatory frameworks capable of ensuring transparency and fairness. Practitioners tend to reject the use of AI in critical final decision making, preferring to employ it as a complementary tool for administrative support. According to the authors, the most sustainable path forward for justice lies in the concept of “augmented justice,” in which AI supports human expertise by increasing procedural efficiency while preserving human oversight. In their conclusions, the authors emphasize that although AI strengthens the operational performance and accessibility of ADR, it cannot replace the ethical judgment or empathy inherent in human participation in the administration of justice.60 According to the authors, “The main finding indicates that AI increases the efficiency and transparency of mediation and arbitration processes by reducing resolution times and minimizing errors associated with documentation management. Nevertheless, its application raises fundamental concerns regarding confidentiality, algorithmic bias mitigation, and the protection of procedural rights. This underscores that technology, despite its power, cannot replace the ethical judgment or empathy that are intrinsic to human participation in the administration of justice” (Rethinking Mediation and Arbitration in the Age … supra, p. 10). The authors affirm that human ethical judgment remains irreplaceable, particularly in disputes requiring moral and contextual discernment.61 The paper mentions that “While AI contributes to efficiency and transparency, the reviewed studies consistently highlight that human ethical judgment remains irreplaceable in resolving disputes requiring empathy and moral discernment” (Rethinking Mediation and Arbitration in the Age … supra, p. 9). Accordingly, the key implication identified is the urgent need to establish a coherent and globally harmonized regulatory framework governing the use of AI, prioritizing transparency, accountability, and fairness. The authors also recommend interdisciplinary collaboration among legal scholars, computer scientists, and ethicists to guide the responsible adoption of AI. Ultimately, the central challenge is not whether AI can be integrated, but how to do so in a responsible, fair, and sustainable manner.62 The authors point out that “It is recommended to foster interdisciplinary collaboration among legal professionals, computer scientists, and ethicists to design human-centered AI systems that are complemented rather than replace—judicial decision making. In this regard, AI should be understood as a component of ‘augmented justice,’ where technology enhances efficiency without undermining the moral and contextual sensibility that defines conflict resolution” (Rethinking Mediation and Arbitration in the Age … supra, p. 10).

V Institutional Integration: The AAA–ICDR Experience In late 2024, the American Arbitration Association (AAA) and its International Centre for Dispute Resolution (ICDR) announced the deployment of AI-based case-management tools to automate parts of the procedural workflow.63 https://www.adr.org/press-releases/aaa-icdr-to-launch-ai-native-arbitrator-transforming-dispute-resolution/, accessed on December 10, 2025. This represents a turning point: the institutionalization of AI as procedural infrastructure in arbitration rather than optional aid. Such developments suggest that participation in arbitral proceedings will increasingly require interaction with AI systems, whether by counsel or tribunal. The AAA–ICDR has been building a genuine institutional ecosystem of artificial intelligence, going beyond the occasional or optional use of technological tools and becoming a part of the very procedural infrastructure of arbitration and other ADR mechanisms. AI thus ceases to be an external, ancillary resource and instead becomes embedded in the institutional environment in which cases are administered, from the pre-dispute stage through case management and support for decision-making activities. Among the tools already announced, particular attention should be given to AAAi Panelist Search, a semantic search mechanism applied to the institution’s roster of arbitrators and mediators, used by case managers to generate broader and more inclusive lists, with a significant reduction in preparation time.64 https://www.adr.org/press-releases/aaa-icdr-launches-new-aaai-panelist-search-to-enhance-panelist-selection-with-ai-technology/, accessed on December 10, 2025. This strategy is complemented by initiatives set out in the AAA–ICDR’s AI roadmap, such as the AAAi Chat Book65 https://www.adr.org/chatbook/caseprep, accessed on December 10, 2025. and the rules chatbot, designed to answer questions on applicable rules and to guide case preparation and conduct, as well as by the AAA ClauseBuilder AI, a generative-AI tool for drafting arbitration clauses, which has received LegalTech awards and anticipates the early use of AI at the contractual stage. The integration of AI into case management further reinforces this structural logic. The WebFile platform now includes an AI assistant that allows users to summarize documents and consult the case file directly within the system. In addition, the partnership with Clearbrief enables arbitrators to organize large volumes of evidence, create hyperlinked timelines, and verify citations, subject to disclosure obligations when informing the parties that the tribunal intends to use the tool.66 https://www.adr.org/news-and-insights/advancing-efficiency-and-quality-in-adr-how-the-aaa-icdr-s-partnership-with-clearbrief-revolutionizes-arbitration-and-mediation/, accessed on December 10, 2025. This is complemented by pilot projects and the adoption of tools such as Vincent (vLex) and CoCounsel, which are used to accelerate case intake and screening, as well as legal research and contract analysis, particularly in the pre-procedural phase.67 According to one report on the event, the panelists “highlighted recent AI pilots with CoCounsel (now part of Thomson Reuters), VLex’s Vincent, and Clearbrief. Following successful trials, AAA now offers Clearbrief—an AI tool that generates hyperlinked timelines and deposition summaries—for free to arbitrators on AAA cases. Meanwhile, AAA’s intake teams have adopted Vincent to speed up contract review for new filings, reporting significant time savings” (Sarah Chojecki, From Today to Tomorrow: AAA-ICDR and ArbTech Panel Discussion on Tech-Driven Arbitration, available at https://legalblogs.wolterskluwer.com/arbitration-blog/from-today-to-tomorrow-aaa-icdr-and-arbtech-panel-discussion-on-tech-driven-arbitration/, accessed on December 10, 2025). Taken together, these initiatives point to a clear evolution toward an institutionalized “AI infrastructure,” explicitly articulated by the AAA–ICDR in official communications and on a dedicated webpage outlining its technological strategy. Reports indicate that AI systems are already routinely used by case managers, effectively making interaction with algorithms a normal part of the user experience at the AAA–ICDR, albeit accompanied by transparency safeguards and, in some cases, opt-out mechanisms. This context opens the door to a deeper normative debate, encompassing disclosure obligations, impacts on due process, standards of review for AI-supported decisions, and potential adjustments to arbitration clauses in light of this new reality of an institutional algorithmic infrastructure. The AAA–ICDR is a pioneer in setting up this type of infrastructure, but it is possible to anticipate a trend—also reflected in empirical research on the use of AI in arbitration mentioned above—that other leading institutions are likely to adopt a similar approach and consolidate the use of AI in their operations. This represents an important step toward the broader normalization of AI in arbitral proceedings. Once the largest and most influential arbitration institutions and chambers embrace this type of technology, its use is likely to proliferate among arbitrators, counsel, law firms active in arbitration, and professionals who support arbitral tribunals.

VI Three Perspectives: Different Approaches Just as it is not possible to generalize the concept of artificial intelligence68 Artificial intelligence is a broad term used to describe a variety of systems that are often quite different from one another and perform diverse functions. As shown at the beginning of this text, there are multiple types of AI and a wide range of systems derived from them. As a result, the use of the term artificial intelligence inevitably produces a generalization that tends to oversimplify the multiple realities encompassed by this category of technology. or to consider all existing AI systems as equivalent, it is also not possible to approach every use of AI in arbitration in the same manner. It is necessary to identify the specific functions performed by AI systems within arbitration, particularly in relation to the roles exercised by arbitrators, the parties, and counsels. In this respect, at least three distinct functions of AI in the context of arbitration can be identified. This tripartite framework draws on functional typologies developed in the AI literature, which distinguish, for example, between assistive, augmented, and autonomous AI systems,69 Jieqiong Cao et al., Not All Forms of Artificial Intelligence Are Perceived Equal: AI Functions and Work Outcomes, 11(2) Journal of Open Innovation: Technology, Market, and Complexity (2025), available at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joitmc.2025.100521, accessed on January 26, 2026. as well as on analyses of legal AI systems that emphasize the diversity of functions and risks associated with different legal applications of AI.70 Gizem Gültekin-Várkonyi, AI Literacy for Legal AI Systems: A Practical Approach, 2025. Available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.18006, accessed on January 26, 2026. In the field of arbitration, several contributions similarly distinguish between the use of AI as a tool for procedural or analytical support and proposals in which AI assists—or potentially replaces—the arbitrator in the adjudicatory function.71 Ankit Malhoutra and Faizan Ahmad, Artificial Intelligence and International Arbitration. Novos Estudos Jurídicos, 2022. Available at https://doi.org/10.14210/nej.v27n2.p258-281, accessed on January 26, 2026. The classification adopted here—tool, assistant, and decision-maker—systematizes these debates within a single analytical framework. [A] Arbitration as a Tool AI as a tool serves primarily technical purposes: document management, translation, scheduling, or data visualization. It increases efficiency without affecting adjudicative reasoning. In this respect, AI represents a functional evolution comparable to the replacement of typewriters by computers. [B] Arbitration as an Assistant AI as an assistant exhibits enhanced, but still limited, autonomy. It drafts sections of awards, suggests reasoning structures, or identifies precedents. Its role is to augment cognitive capacity, not to substitute it. Institutional initiatives such as the AAA–ICDR pilot fall within this category. [C] Arbitration as Decision-Maker In narrowly defined contexts—such as disputes arising from smart contracts or low-value online arbitration—AI systems may be capable of performing forms of rule-based adjudication.72 See, for example, P. Han, AI-Powered Digital Arbitration Framework Leveraging Smart Contracts and Electronic Evidence Authentication, 15 Scientific Reports 37327 (2025). Available at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-21313-x, accessed on January 26, 2026 and Luís de Lima Pinheiro, Arbitration, Blockchain and Artificial Intelligence, May 28, 2025. Available at: SSRN: https://ssrn.com/abstract=5272787 or http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5272787, accessed on January 26, 2026. These settings are typically characterized by standardized inputs, clearly defined legal or contractual rules, and limited factual or legal discretion, which makes automation more feasible. By contrast, complex disputes often involve open-textured legal standards, evidentiary assessment, credibility judgments, and normative balancing, all of which remain poorly suited to fully autonomous decision making by AI. For this reason, while the complete replacement of human adjudicators in complex cases remains unlikely, current technological and institutional developments point toward a gradual increase in algorithmic participation in determinative functions, particularly in structured and repetitive decision-making tasks. [D] Comparison The three functions of artificial intelligence (AI) in arbitration can be structured in the following analytical framework: AI Role Description & Function Practical Examples Primary Objective (1) Tool AI is used to perform mechanical tasks or provide technical and operational support. Case law search, translation, document review/analysis, and deadline management. To increase workflow efficiency without influencing the substance of the decision. (2) Assistant Acts as a co-pilot for the arbitrator or the parties (AI agent), with limited autonomy. Proposing award or report drafts, suggesting lines of reasoning, and assisting with evidence assessment. To extend human cognitive capacity (“augmented cognition”). (3) Adjudicator AI assumes the decision-making role, either fully or partially. Automated arbitration in standardized or parameterized disputes (e.g., smart contract execution, when needed). To decide disputes based on strictly predefined parameters. These categories reflect different degrees of technological interaction, requiring differentiated levels of care, safeguards, and regulation depending on the degree of autonomy and the risks involved in each function. While the use of AI as a tool is primarily aimed at efficiency and speed, its use as an assistant or as a decision-maker calls for much stricter safeguards in order to preserve the integrity of the legal process. For example, the EU AI Act classifies certain AI systems used for decision making as high-risk primarily under Article 6(2) and Annex III, where they materially influence outcomes in sensitive areas like employment, education, public services, law enforcement, and justice. According to the AI Act, these systems pose significant risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights, triggering strict obligations unless derogations apply. Article 6(2) deems AI systems listed in Annex III as high-risk, including those for decision making that affect access to education (e.g., admissions), employment (e.g., recruitment, promotions, terminations), essential services (e.g., eligibility for benefits, creditworthiness), law enforcement (e.g., risk assessments), and administration of justice (e.g., interpreting facts and law). Article 6(3) provides some derogations: such systems are not high-risk if they perform narrow procedural tasks, improve prior human activities, detect patterns without replacing human assessment (with review), or handle preparatory tasks, provided they do not pose significant risk or materially influence decisions—except for profiling, which remains high-risk. In the case of high-risk AI systems, providers must implement a risk management system (Article 9), ensure high-quality data governance (Article 10), maintain technical documentation (Article 11), provide transparency (Article 13), ensure accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity (Article 15), and conduct conformity assessments (Article 43). Article 16 requires providers to establish a quality management system (Article 17), keep logs (Article 19), issue EU declarations of conformity (Article 47), affix CE marking, register systems, and take corrective actions. To facilitate understanding, one may think of AI in arbitration as analogous to different levels of vehicle automation: when used as a tool, it operates like a GPS that merely guides the itinerary; as an assistant, it resembles an autopilot that maintains speed and suggests maneuvers under human supervision; and as a decision-maker, it functions like a fully autonomous car, in which the system makes all driving decisions from start to finish. [E] AI in Arbitration: Risks, Regulation, and Governance Taking into account the three different functions, it is also possible to identify distinct levels of measures and safeguards that should be adopted in relation to different AI systems. As a system’s degree of autonomy increases, so too must the scope and intensity of the limits and controls imposed upon it. Systems that operate merely as tools involve lower risks and therefore require less intensive oversight by professionals involved in arbitration. In a sense, this approach is akin to the risk-based regulation that underpins the European AI Act. However, it is not simply a matter of adopting risk-based regulatory techniques. The differing risk perceptions arising from the various functions of AI systems shall trigger different responses at the regulatory and governance levels, as well as in the development of concrete safeguards governing the use of such systems in arbitration. The table below provides an objective and concise summary of this idea: Function Primary Risks Required Safeguards Tool Data leakage, bias propagation Access controls, validation Assistant Over-reliance, automation bias, cognitive bias Human-in-the-loop oversight Adjudicator Legitimacy crisis, opacity, lack of accountability Explainability, appealability, system certification [F] Moving Beyond Conventional Understanding Recognizing that AI systems perform different functions, entail different levels of risk, and require function-specific safeguards must be taken into account when seeking to regulate or establish guidelines for the use of AI in arbitration. Generic or overly broad provisions may lead to requirements that impose excessive constraints on systems used merely as tools. At the same time, taking into consideration the specific function performed by an AI system makes it possible to optimize its use while minimizing the risks associated with each of the functions exercised by AI systems. It should be noted that, at this stage, this analysis does not address the general constraints applicable to all AI systems, such as the risks of hallucinations or the limitations arising from biases and flaws in AI training datasets. These (and other) concerns that are common to all three functions performed by AI systems must also be assessed in light of the importance of the task at hand and the risks involved.

VII Due Process in a Digital Legal Order The integration of AI into adjudicative systems, as in arbitration, imposes a re-examination of the concept of due process. Procedural fairness, explainability, and transparency must be preserved within algorithmic environments. [A] Technological Due Process The evolution toward a digital society grounded in data and artificial intelligence is reshaping the law’s information infrastructure, calling for the recognition of what we can refer to as technological due process.73 Richard Susskind, Tomorrow’s Lawyers, 3rd ed., Oxford University Press, Oxford, 2023, p. 264. As arbitration increasingly absorbs artificial intelligence into its workflow, the traditional understanding of due process must evolve to keep pace. This concept extends traditional due process guarantees to ensure that, in digital environments, the rights of the parties are protected through three core pillars: (1) transparency, (2) fairness, and (3) accountability in technology-assisted decision making. Sophie Nappert examines the case of blockchain dispute resolution and mentions that social expectations of fairness, satisfaction and trust are evolving under ecommerce and social media dynamics, and that this shift toward confidence in technology poses disruptive questions for traditional, timeintensive due process in arbitration.74 Sophie Nappert, Twenty‑First Century Arbitration: The Question of Trust, 2021, SSRN working paper, available at https://ssrn.com/abstract=3956155, accessed on January 3, 2026. While this may hold true for certain categories of dispute—such as those arising in social media or e-commerce contexts—a purely technological model of trust does not appear feasible across all types of disputes. In the context of platforms and social networks, the debate on technological due process also arises. In this regard, Frederick Mostert argues that “the fundamental principles of ‘procedural and substantive due process’ were developed to protect an individual from the coercive power of government. And, as we have seen, social media platforms, at least for now, are not recognized in the eyes of the law as ‘governments’ because they operate in privately owned spaces. The distinction is preserved despite the fact that in those very same privately controlled spaces, individuals and users are quite often subjected to the arbitrary exercise of power when it comes to (i) free speech and (ii) the abuse and manipulation of private data, especially in the US.”75 Frederick Mostert, “Digital Due Process”: A Need for Online Justice, 15(5) Journal of Intellectual Property Law & Practice (May 2020), pp. 378-389, https://doi.org/10.1093/jiplp/jpaa024, accessed on February 2, 2026. According to the author, “‘Digital due process’ forged on global customary norms, usually include among other principles: 1. a fair and public review by an independent and impartial panel or competent court within a reasonable time; 2. a proper prior notification of the review; 3. an opportunity for a user or notifier to respond and present evidence in respect of a takedown or a stay-up inaction by a platform; 4. The right to legal representation; 5. the right to appeal to an appeals panel, alternative dispute resolution tribunal or competent court; 6. notifiers may at any stage in the process seek access to competent courts; 7. the right to receive a decision which clearly articulates the reason for that decision; and, 8. the right to an effective remedy including, for example, stay-up or takedown.” In this new digital landscape, protecting the parties’ rights requires more than just neutral arbitrators; it demands a structural commitment to transparency and accountability regarding how technology influences the decision-making process.76 In this respect, the EU AI Act seeks to address these concerns by imposing active obligations and safeguards where AI systems are used for decision-making purposes. As noted above, high-risk AI systems are subject to stringent requirements, including the implementation of a risk management system (Art. 9), the assurance of high-quality data governance (Art. 10), the maintenance of technical documentation (Art. 11), transparency obligations (Art. 13), guarantees of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity (Art. 15), and the performance of conformity assessments (Art. 43). In addition, providers are required to establish a quality management system (Art. 17), keep logs (Art. 19), issue EU declarations of conformity (Art. 47), affix CE marking, register AI systems, and adopt corrective measures where necessary (Art. 16). Without this update, the efficiency gains of AI could inadvertently compromise the legitimacy of the entire arbitral framework. The core rationale for applying this concept rests on distinguishing the role played by AI and the risks associated with each role: – Graduated Need for Protection: The stringency of technological due process must be proportionate and compatible to the degree of autonomy exercised by AI. When AI is used merely as a tool (such as for precedent searches or deadline management), the aim is efficiency. By contrast, its use as an assistant (suggesting draft decisions or lines of reasoning) or as a decision-maker (fully automated decisions) requires far more robust safeguards, so that undetected errors or embedded biases do not compromise the integrity of the proceedings. – The “Human-in-the-Loop” Model: To enable this new paradigm without undermining legal trust, there is a need for mandatory integration of human arbitrators who review, assess, and validate AI-generated outputs before any decision is finalized. This mechanism makes it possible to harness the technology’s predictive power while preserving human values and the ability to explain the recommendations produced by machines. – Mitigation of Procedural Risks: The current absence of clear and specific regulation on technological due process—except for more general rules, as the AI Act mentioned before—is seen as a major drawback. There is a risk that awards may be challenged in court on the grounds of violations of due process when arbitrators rely on AI. The existence of a general regulatory framework alone, such as the AI Act, does little to mitigate these risks, as it neither encompasses the full range of potential uses of AI in arbitration nor is tailored to the specificities of dispute resolution, thereby leaving room for broad and potentially inconsistent interpretations in the event of a challenge. Accordingly, technological due process is not merely a technical adjustment, but rather a continuous dialogue and the development of regulatory frameworks designed to ensure that automation does not come at the expense of ethics and legal certainty, especially in complex arbitration disputes. [B] Grounds for Challenging Awards This evolving concept of due process is likely to become a primary battleground for the enforcement of arbitral awards. If a tribunal relies on opaque “black box” AI tools, losing parties may have valid grounds to argue that they were denied a fair hearing. Specific challenges could arise from a lack of explainability, where parties cannot decipher how an algorithm reached a conclusion, or from algorithmic bias, where the training data skews the result against a specific demographic or jurisdiction. Furthermore, the absence of meaningful human oversight, whereby an arbitrator merely “rubber-stamps” an AI system’s recommendation without genuine critical scrutiny, may be characterized as an abdication of the arbitrator’s adjudicative mandate, with potential consequences for the enforceability of the award. As highlighted by Sophie Nappert, the central challenge lies not in recognizing the need for human oversight, but in articulating how such oversight is to be exercised in practice, particularly in light of the well-documented “wow factor” associated with AI outputs.77 According to the author, “the ‘wow factor’ refers to a cognitive-emotional bias that arises when we are confronted by outputs that improve upon our own capacity—especially when they come from non-human agents like AI. There’s growing research showing that humans have a default tendency to attribute authority and even epistemic superiority to machines that present information with high fluency and speed—especially if that information appears comprehensive” (The “WOW” Factor: Human Cognition in the Age of Algorithmic Summarisation. Available at https://www.arbtech.io/blog/the-wow-factor-human-cognition-in-the-age-of-algorithmic-summarisation, accessed on January 2, 2026). The Brazilian CNJ surveys mentioned above also confirm that there is concern among law professionals (judges and law clerks) related to the “automation bias,” that is similar to the idea of the wow factor. See section III.C.7 above. This cognitive bias can induce undue deference to algorithmic suggestions, even among experienced decision-makers. Effective human oversight therefore requires more than the formal presence of a human in the loop: it demands structured procedural safeguards, such as an explicit duty to independently reason through the issues, documented justification when relying on AI-assisted analysis, transparency as to the role played by AI tools, and, where appropriate, institutional guidance or training aimed at mitigating automation bias. In the context of arbitration and dispute resolution, these measures are essential to ensure that AI remains a decision-support instrument rather than a de facto decision-maker, thereby preserving both the legitimacy of the arbitral process and the enforceability of its outcomes.78 Sophie Nappert proposes that this requires an ethical approach to cognitive design grounded in four complementary strategies.
