Sect. 15 of the Act provides that a party to any civil proceedings relating to an arbitration (with certain exceptions) may apply to the court for an order prohibiting the disclosure of the identity of a party to the arbitration in any report of the proceedings. Subject to certain exceptions, the court must grant the order. The relevant provisions are contained within Chapter 100 of the Rules of the Court of Session, which provides that the petition or note lodged in respect of the arbitration is not to be open for inspection, except by court staff and the parties, that the case is to be referred to as “Arbitration Application” or “Arbitration Appeal” as the case may be and by reference to a number and the year in which it was lodged, and the court proceedings are to be held in private. An example of the nomenclature referred to above can be found in Arbitration Application No 2 of 2011 2011 G.W.D. 38-785. In that case, Lord Glennie, who was at the time one of the Court's designated arbitration judges illustrated the Court's approach to anonymity in proceedings: “It was agreed at the end of the hearing that in the first instance I should issue my Opinion to the parties without publishing it more widely, to enable them to make representations as to whether there should be publication and, if so, whether any details could be omitted without removing from my decision such sense as it might otherwise have.” It should be noted that petitions for enforcement of an award under Sect. 12 of the Act are excluded from the scope of Sect. 15, but that petitions under Sect. 19 for the enforcement of New York Convention awards, are not excluded. The logic of this distinction is not entirely clear. Taken together, the effect of these provisions is that where there is an incidental application to the court during the course of an arbitration, to deal with, for example, a jurisdiction challenge, or the extension of a time limit, an application for anonymity is permitted by Sect. 15. Any petition challenging the validity of an award is also covered by Sect. 15. An action for enforcement of a ‘Convention Award’ (an award made in a New York Convention territory other than the UK) is enforced via Sect. 19 and is not excluded from the anonymity provision. The enforcement of any award seated in the UK, or in a non-New York Convention territory is excluded from the anonymity provisions of Sect. 15.

sections.title: ARBITRAL AWARD
sections.text: CHAPTER V ARBITRAL AWARD 1. Types of Award The Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) provides for provisional awards, part awards and draft awards as well as final awards. The tribunal has power in terms of Rule 53 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Act to make a provisional award in which it may grant any relief on a provisional basis which it has the power to grant permanently. Such an award is enforceable in the Scottish courts, but whether it would be enforceable in other jurisdictions must be doubtful given its interim status. It is not possible to appeal against a provisional award (see Rule 71(3)). In terms of Rule 54 (a mandatory rule) the tribunal has power to make a part award, which is an award that disposes (on a final, not provisional basis) of some, but not all of the matters which the tribunal is to decide in the arbitration. Rule 55 (a default rule) gives the tribunal power to issue a draft award to the parties, but if it does so, it must consider any representations from the parties about the draft received within the timescale set by the tribunal. 2. Making of the Award a. Decision-making Rule 30 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) provides that where the tribunal is unable to make a decision unanimously, a decision made by the majority of the arbitrators is sufficient, and that where there is neither unanimity nor a majority in favour of or opposed to making any decision the decision is to be made by the arbitrator nominated to chair the tribunal, or where no person has been so nominated, the decision is to be made where the tribunal consists of three or more arbitrators, by the last arbitrator to be appointed. b. Time limits There is no set time limit for the making of the award within the Act, although parties do commonly adopt rules which set a time limit. The tribunal has a general duty to avoid unnecessary delay. Where the parties have agreed a time limit, the tribunal, or any party may apply to the court to vary it (Rule 43). Whilst the terms of Rule 43 are broad, an application to vary the time limit should be brought before the expiry of the time limit. Applicable institutional rules will also be relevant. If no award has been made, and no extension has been granted (or perhaps at least applied for) by the expiry of the time limit, the jurisdiction of the tribunal to make an award falls.1 Cunninghame v. Drummond (1491) Mor. 635; Earl of Linlithgow v. Hamilton (1610) Mor. 636. c. Dissenting opinions It is possible for an arbitrator to issue a dissenting opinion. This may be incorporated into the award, in which case if forms part of it, or it may be issued separately, in which case it does not. The dissenting arbitrator might simply record their dissent within the award without issuing an opinion. 3. Form of the Award The award must be signed by all arbitrators (or all those assenting to the award). It must state the seat of the arbitration, when the award is made and when it takes effect, the tribunal's reasons for the award, and whether any previous provisional or part award has been made (and the extent to which any previous provisional award is superseded or confirmed). Rule 51 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto), which sets out the required form of an award is a default rule, so it is open to parties to alter it as they see fit. For example, they may dispense with the requirement to provide reasons. There is no defined standard of the level of reasoning required, but if there is a challenge to the award, the court may require the tribunal to give reasons, or further reasons for its award (see Rule 71). 4. Jurisdiction a. Power of arbitrators In terms of Rule 19 (a mandatory rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto), the tribunal may rule on its own jurisdiction, and in particular the tribunal may rule on whether there is a valid arbitration agreement, whether the tribunal is properly constituted, and what matters have been submitted to arbitration in accordance with the arbitration agreement. Rule 20 provides that any party may object to the tribunal that it does not have jurisdiction or has exceeded its jurisdiction in relation to any matter, and subject to the agreement of the parties, the tribunal may either rule on an objection independently from dealing with the subject matter, or delay ruling on an objection until it makes its award on the merits of the dispute, including its award on jurisdiction within the award on the merits. b. Form of jurisdictional decision The Act does not specify the form that a determination on jurisdiction must take. c. Appeal It is open to parties to appeal against the tribunal's ruling on jurisdiction (whether for or against the objection). Such an appeal is made to the Outer House of the Court of Session within fourteen days of the tribunal's decision on the objection. The decision of the Outer House is final. d. Timing An objection to jurisdiction should be raised before, or as soon as reasonably practicable after the matter to which the objection relates is first raised in the arbitration. It may be raised later where that is considered justified by the tribunal, but an objection must be made before the issue of the final award. Rule 20 governs this issue. Rule 76 provides that a party who participates in an arbitration without making a timeous objection on the ground that that the tribunal does not have jurisdiction may not raise the objection later before the tribunal or the court. Rules 20 and 76 are mandatory rules. e. Role of court Rule 22 (a default rule) provides that the court may determine any question of the tribunal's jurisdiction. This allows for a direct application to the court. However, in terms of Rule 23, such an application is only competent where the parties have agreed that it should be made, or where the tribunal consents, and the court is satisfied that determining the question is likely to produce substantial savings in expenses, that the application was made without delay, and that there is a good reason why the question should be determined by the court. The tribunal may continue with the arbitration pending the determination of the application to the court. 5. Applicable Law Rule 47 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) governs the rules applicable to the substance of the dispute. It applies to both domestic and international arbitration. As a default rule, parties are free to amend it. In terms of Rule 47, the tribunal is obliged to decide the dispute in accordance with the law chosen by the parties as applicable to the substance of the dispute, or failing that, the law determined by the conflict of law rules which the tribunal considers applicable. The tribunal is not permitted to decide the dispute on the basis of general considerations of justice, fairness or equity unless they form part of the law concerned or the parties otherwise agree. It is possible to give the tribunal power to decide as amiables compositeurs or ex aequo et bono by express agreement. The law governing the arbitration agreement is a separate question. In terms of Sect. 6 of the Act, where the parties to an arbitration agreement agree that an arbitration under that agreement is to be seated in Scotland, but the arbitration agreement does not specify the law which is to govern it, then, unless the parties otherwise agree, the arbitration agreement is to be governed by Scots law. 6. Settlement In terms of Rule 57 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto), on the request of the parties, the tribunal may make an award reflecting the terms of an agreed settlement. The tribunal has a discretion as to whether to make such an award, and may refuse to do so where, for example, an award in agreed terms would breach public policy, or where the arbitration has been a sham. In terms of the rule, the tribunal is not required to state reasons in a settlement award, and such awards cannot be challenged under Part 8 of the Act (lack of jurisdiction, serious irregularity or legal error). Settlement Awards under Rule 57 can be enforced in the same way as any other award under the Act. Of course it is possible that the courts of some jurisdictions may take the view that such an award is not a true award within the meaning of the New York Convention. Generally speaking, arbitral tribunals in Scottish seated arbitrations will not attempt to assist parties in negotiating a settlement for fear of a challenge on the grounds of apparent bias. However, where that is the express wish of parties, and the necessary caveats are put in place, the tribunal may try to assist settlement. 7A. Correction and Interpretation of the Award a. Correction In terms of Rule 58 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto) the tribunal may correct an award so as to correct a clerical, typographical or other error in the award arising by virtue of accident or omission, or clarify or remove any ambiguity in the award. The tribunal may make such a correction on its own initiative, or on an application by any party. An application by a party to make such a correction must be made within twenty-eight days of the award being made (or such later date as the court allows). If the tribunal decides to correct that award of its own volition, it must give the parties a reasonable opportunity to make representations about the proposed correction. Where one party applies for a correction, the other party must be given a reasonable opportunity to comment on the proposed correction. b. Interpretation The tribunal is empowered under Rule 58 to issue a correction to clarify or remove any ambiguity in the award. Accordingly, where there is ambiguity, the tribunal can provide interpretation, but it probably cannot do so where the award is clear. c. Form The correction is achieved by issuing a corrected award. The corrected award is to be treated as if it was made in its corrected form on the day the award was made. d. Timing A correction made of the tribunal's own volition must be made within twenty-eight days of the award. Where it is made on the application of a party, the correction must be made within twenty-eight days of the application, and the application must be made within twenty-eight days of the award. 7B. Additional Award In terms of Rule 57 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto), an arbitration ends when the last award to be made in the arbitration is made (and no claim, including any claim for expenses or interest, is outstanding). Therefore, the tribunal retains jurisdiction to issue an award whilst such matters remain outstanding. There is no equivalent under the Act to the “additional award” under the Arbitration Act 1996 applicable in England and Wales. If interest and/or expenses, have not been dealt with, and a further award is required to deal with these issues, then this simply takes the form of an award in the usual away. It will be for the tribunal to determine liability for the additional costs arising from the further award. 8. Fees and Costs i. Costs in General Part 7 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto) deals with “arbitration expenses”. The term “arbitration expense” is defined by Rule 59 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Act as: “(a) the arbitrators' fees and expenses for which the parties are liable under rule 60, (b) any expenses incurred by the tribunal when conducting the arbitration for which the parties are liable under rule 60, (c) the parties' legal and other expenses, and (d) the fees and expenses of— (i) any arbitral appointments referee, and (ii) any other third party to whom the parties give powers in relation to the arbitration, for which the parties are liable under rule 60.” The phrase “other expenses” may include witness costs, travel and accommodation costs, expert witness fees, and other costs incidental to the process such as copying, translation and transcription charges. Rule 59 is a default rule, so it is open to parties to modify the definition of arbitrator expenses. ii. Deposit It is common for arbitrators to require a deposit or security for their fees, and of course this is almost always the case with institutional arbitration. The relevant institutional rules will determine how the deposit is paid and whether it is split equally between the parties. Where the arbitration is ad hoc, it is quite common for the tribunal to require a deposit. iii. Fees of Arbitrators Rule 60, a mandatory rule, governs arbitrators' fees and expenses. The amount of fees and expenses payable under Rule 60 are to be agreed by the parties and the arbitrators or, as the case may be, the arbitral appointments referee or other third party (such as an arbitral institution). Failing such agreement, the fees and expenses payable are to be determined by the Auditor of the Court of Session on the basis of a reasonable commercial rate of charge. Generally speaking, arbitrators' fees are charged on an hourly rate basis, although some fixed fee schemes to apply for smaller scale disputes (certain consumer schemes for example). If an institutional arbitration is used, the system of the institution will apply. iv. Costs of Legal Assistance to Parties The general rule is in Scotland is that costs follow the event. Rule 62(2) provides that when making an award for recoverable arbitral expense (which includes legal costs) the tribunal must have regard to the principle that expenses should follow a decision made in favour of a party except where that would be inappropriate in the circumstances. Rule 61(3) provides that the amount of expenses recoverable must be determined on the basis that a reasonable amount is to be allowed in respect of all reasonably incurred expenses. v. Award on Costs The principle that costs follow the event is applied on the theory that the party who has caused the expense ought to be liable to pay it. So in general terms, where a claimant is unsuccessful, costs are awarded against the claimant on the basis that the claimant has caused the expense by bringing a bad claim. Where the claimant is successful, the respondent is held liable, because the respondent has caused the expense by wrongly resisting the claim. Very often there is split success, and the tribunal is entitled to allocate costs according to its assessment of overall success. Other circumstances may justify a departure from the usual rule. For example, it may be the claimant has failed to obtain an award in a sum greater than an offer contained in a tender (sealed offer), in which case the tribunal might determine that it ought to be liable for expenses incurred after the date of the tender/offer. 9. Notification of the Award and Registration There is no requirement to register an award with the court. The award is made by delivery of the awards to the parties in accordance with the requirements of Rule 83 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto). This may be hand delivery, first class post, or some other method, including email which the sender reasonably considers likely to cause it to be delivered the same or next day. 10. Enforcement of Domestic and International Awards Rendered In Scotland a. General Sect. 12 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) deals with the enforcement of arbitration awards and provides that the court may order that a tribunal's award may be enforced as if it were a decree of the court. This rule applies to domestic and international awards. The court cannot make such an order if there is an ongoing appeal under Part 8 of the Act, or an arbitral process of appeal or review, or a process of correction under Rule 58 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Act, which has not been finally determined. A peculiarity of the Scottish system is that where the arbitration agreement is registered in the Books of Council and Session (the official register of documents), an award in respect of that arbitration agreement can itself be registered in the Books of Council and Session, allowing it to be executed without having to refer to the court. In practice, contracts such as commercial leases are often registered. However, other commercial contracts tend not to be, since the register is public. b. Leave for enforcement If an application under Sect. 12 is successful, no further order for leave to enforce is required, since the award will gain the status of a registered court decree. c. Enforcement The court cannot make an order if the tribunal did not have jurisdiction to make the award, and can restrict the order to exclude any part of the award in respect of which jurisdiction was lacking. However, a party cannot object on jurisdictional grounds if it has lost the right to do so in terms of the rules. 11. Publication of the Award Awards are not published in Scotland. On the contrary, the default position is that awards are confidential. It is possible for parties to waive confidentiality. For example, parties may adopt rules that provide for publication of the award. In that case, the conditions attached to such publication will be determined by the applicable rules. There are a number of exceptions to the rule on confidentiality, and in particular it is permissible to disclose confidential information relating to the arbitration where it would be in the public interest to do so. However, this exception is likely to be interpreted strictly by the court. Given that a breach of the rule against disclosure of confidential information is actionable if it causes loss, caution must be exercised.

sections.title: ENFORCEMENT OF FOREIGN ARBITRAL AWARDS
sections.text: CHAPTER VI ENFORCEMENT OF FOREIGN ARBITRAL AWARDS 1. Enforcement under Conventions and Treaties a. Conventions in force Scotland does not have capacity to enter into international treaties in its own right. However, Scotland is one of the constituent countries of the United Kingdom, and the UK is a signatory to various international conventions relating to the enforcement of foreign arbitral awards. In particular the UK is a signatory to the 1923 Geneva Protocol on Arbitration Clauses, the 1927 Geneva Convention on the Execution of Foreign Arbitral Awards and the 1958 Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (the “New York Convention”). The UK has also ratified the 1965 Convention on the Settlement of Investment Disputes between States and Nationals of Other States (the “ICSID Convention” or “Washington Convention”). b. Procedure Where an award has been made in the territory of a state other than the United Kingdom which is a party to the New York Convention under a written arbitration agreement (a “Convention award”), it is to be treated as binding on the persons as between whom it was made, and the court may order it to be enforced as if it were a registered court decree (Sects. 18, 19 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto)). Recognition or enforcement of a Convention award may only be refused if certain circumstances apply. It may be refused if a party was under some incapacity under the applicable law, the arbitration agreement was invalid, the person against whom it is invoked was not given proper notice of the arbitral process or was otherwise unable to present its case, or if the tribunal was constituted or the arbitration conducted otherwise than in accordance with the agreement of the parties or the law of the country in which it took place. It may also be refused if proved that the award deals with a dispute outside the submission to arbitration or is not binding or has been set aside. In addition, recognition or enforcement may be refused if the award relates to matters not capable of being settled by arbitration, or to grant it would be contrary to public policy (Sect. 20 of the Act). These grounds closely follow the grounds for refusal of enforcement set out in Art. V of the New York Convention. Enforcement of convention awards is governed by Part IX of Chapter 62 of the Rules of the Court of Session. To enforce a Convention award, it is necessary to lodge a petition, and with it the duly authenticated original award or a certified copy of it; the original agreement referred to in Art. II of the Convention or a certified copy of it; a translation of any award or agreement which is in a language other than English, certified by an official or sworn translator or by a diplomatic or consular agent; an affidavit stating the full name, title, trade or business and the usual or last known place of residence or, where appropriate, of the business of the petitioner or noter and the party against whom the Convention award was made; the amount of the Convention award which is unsatisfied; and that the Convention award has become binding on the parties and has not been set aside or suspended by a court of the country which, or under the law of which, the award was made. 2. Enforcement Where No Convention or Treaty Applies It remains possible to enforce a foreign award at common law. However, as noted above, the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto), in Sect. 12, provides a basis for enforcement of domestic and foreign awards, whether or not covered by the Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards of 10 June 1958 (the “New York Convention”). Enforcement proceeds by way of a petition to the court in terms of Chapter 100.10 of the Rules of the Court of Session. The petition procedure under this chapter is a shortened procedure, and is dealt with efficiently by the court. The rule of court sets out the documents which are to be lodged in support of the petition (including the original award or a certified copy) and the matters that must be set out in the petition. 3. Rules of Public Policy Recognition of a Convention award may be refused if to do so would be contrary to public policy (Sect. 20.4(b) of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto). This section has not yet been tested in the Scottish courts, but it is assumed that a Scottish court would take a narrow approach to the interpretation of public policy, and would find English authority persuasive on this point.

sections.title: MEANS OF RECOURSE
sections.text: CHAPTER VII MEANS OF RECOURSE 1. Appeal on the Merits from an Arbitral Award i. Appeal to a Second Arbitral Instance There is nothing in the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) preventing an appeal to a second arbitral instance if that is provided for in the applicable rules. The enforcement provisions of the Act recognize that such procedures may apply in that enforcement is delayed until any applicable arbitral process of repeal or review is completed (see Sect. 12(2)(b)). ii. Appeal to a Court An award may also be challenged on the ground that the tribunal erred on a point of Scots law (Rule 69 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Act). Such an appeal may, however, only be made with the agreement of the parties or leave of the court. Leave may only be given if the court is satisfied that the point will substantially affect a party's rights and that the decision of the tribunal was obviously wrong on a matter of general importance, open to serious doubt (Rule 70). A party may only appeal to the court if any available arbitral appeal or recourse has been exhausted, and must do so within twenty-eight days of the notification of the award, or if there has been appeal or recourse, the determination of that matter (Rule 71). Where the court orders the tribunal to reconsider its award it must do so within time limits, generally three months from the date of the court's decision (Rule 72). Parties can agree to exclude the right to appeal on the basis of an error of Scots law. If the contract is governed by another law, such as English law, no legal error appeal is possible. 2. Setting Aside of the Arbitral Award (Action for Annulment, Vacation of the Award) i. Grounds for Setting Aside A party may appeal to the Court of Session against an award on the ground that the tribunal had no jurisdiction to make it, and the court may confirm, vary or set aside the award. If there is an important point of principle or practice involved, the court may allow leave to appeal. However, appeal thereafter to the Supreme Court is excluded (Rule 67 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto)). A party may also appeal on the grounds of serious irregularity. A serious irregularity is a challenge to the award in respect of the manner in which or the process by which the tribunal made its award, whereby substantial injustice has been or will be caused to the appellant. Several particular instances are set out in the Rules such as an arbitrator not being impartial and independent, not treating parties fairly, acting outside his or her powers, etc. Where the court does not confirm the award but orders the tribunal to reconsider the award, or part thereof, or sets it aside in whole or in part, the court may make such order as it thinks fit as to the fees and expenses of the arbitrator (Rule 68). ii. Procedure A challenge to an award is brought by way of a petition to the court and must be made no later than twenty-eight days after the later of the date on which the award was made, or if there is a correction process, from the date on which the tribunal decides whether or not to correct its award, or where there is an arbitral appeal process, the date on which the appellant is notified of the result of that process. The court has power to order the tribunal to reconsider the award, or part of it. iii. Waivers Rule 67 sets out the right to bring a challenge on the ground that the tribunal has exceeded its jurisdiction, and Rule 68, which sets out the right to bring a challenge on the ground that there has been a serious irregularity are mandatory rules. Accordingly, it is not possible to exclude such a challenge in the arbitration clause or the agreement to arbitrate, or by any subsequent agreement. Sect. 8 of the Act provides: “The following rules, called “mandatory rules”, cannot be modified or disapplied (by an arbitration agreement, by any other agreement between the parties or by any other means) in relation to any arbitration seated in Scotland - … Rules 67, 68, 70, 71 and 72 (challenging awards)” By contrast, Rule 69, which provides for challenges on the ground that the tribunal has made an error of Scots law, is a default rule and can therefore (in terms of Sect. 9) be modified or disapplied in the arbitration agreement, or by any other means, before or after the arbitration begins. iv. Effect of an Award that Has Been Set Aside When the court is presented with a jurisdiction challenge, the court has three options open to it. It may confirm the award; vary the award (or part of it); or set aside the award or part of it. When presented with a serious irregularity or legal error challenge, the court also has three options open to it. It may confirm the award; it may order the tribunal to reconsider the award (or part of it); or if it considers reconsideration inappropriate, it may aside the award (or part of it). An award that has been varied by the court is enforced as varied. An award that has been set aside in its entirety is a nullity. Where an award has been set aside in part, it has effect, except to the extent that it has been set aside. Where the award has been remitted to the tribunal in whole or in part, in respect of a serious irregularity or legal error challenge, the tribunal must make a new award (or confirm its original award) within three months of the decision of the court (unless appealed in which the three month period runs from the decision on the appeal, or earlier abandonment), or such other date as the court specifies. To the extent that the award has been remitted, it is suspended, and to the extent that it is revised, the previous version falls away and is null and void. 3. Other Means of Recourse There are no other means of recourse against the award.

sections.title: CONCILIATION / MEDIATION
sections.text: CHAPTER VIII CONCILIATION / MEDIATION 1. General The UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Conciliation has not been adopted in Scotland. There is, however, considerable use made of conciliation and mediation services. In some cases the courts will suggest this to parties as an appropriate step, only proceeding to litigation or arbitration if no settlement results. Some commercial contracts provide expressly that before resorting to arbitration or litigation parties should attempt to resolve their disputes by conciliation or mediation. Both branches of the legal profession hold lists of members appropriately trained and experienced in mediation. The current lists of such members may be obtained in the case of advocates from: Faculty of Advocates Advocates' Library Parliament Square Edinburgh EH1 1RF Scotland Telephone: +44 (0)131 226 5071 Website: <www.advocates.org.uk> In the case of solicitors, the current list may be obtained from: The Law Society of Scotland Atria One 144 Morrison Street Edinburgh EH3 8EX Scotland Telephone: +44 (0)131 226 7411 Facsimile: +44 (0)131 225 2934 E-mail: lawscot@lawscot.org.uk Website: <www.lawscot.org.uk/> In addition, a number of other organizations can provide mediators, including the Scottish Branch of the Chartered Institute of Arbitrators, RICS Scotland, and the Scottish Mediation Network. 2. Legal Provisions Scotland does not have mediation legislation. Mediation is treated as a matter of contract. A mediation agreement may be enforced through the court in the same way as any other contract. Mediation is encouraged in the pre-action protocol of the Commercial Court, but is not required by the Court.

sections.title: INVESTMENT TREATY ARBITRATION
sections.text: CHAPTER IX INVESTMENT TREATY ARBITRATION 1. Conventions and Treaties Scotland is not a party in its own right to conventions and treaties, but the United Kingdom, of which Scotland is a constituent country, is a party to various international conventions and treaties. 2. Investment Arbitration For investment arbitration decisions involving the United Kingdom, see the ICCA Handbook National Report on England and Wales. 3. National Investment Legislation In Scotland, despite the coming into force of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto), international investment disputes continue to be arbitrated under the pre-existing statutory regime, contained in the Arbitration (International Investment Disputes) Act 1966. In relation to Scotland it gives effect to the 1965 Convention on the Settlement of Investment Disputes between States and Nationals of Other States (the “Washington Convention” or “ICSID Convention”).

plain_text:
CHAPTER I INTRODUCTION 1. Law on Arbitration Scotland has a long history of arbitration with recorded cases dating back to the thirteenth century. The Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) governs all arbitrations seated in Scotland not subject to another statutory regime. All references hereafter to the Act or Rules are to the 2010 Act and the Rules forming Schedule 1 to the Act. The Act is not based on the UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration (the “Model Law”) but instead, subject to a number of modifications, follows the approach of the Arbitration Act 1996, applicable in England and Wales (“the 1996 Act”). Anyone familiar with the 1996 Act will feel instantly at home with the Scottish Act. The courts in Scotland have gone so far as to say that cases determined under the 1996 Act, and its predecessor may be cited in respect of the equivalent provision in the Scottish Act as a useful source of reference (albeit the court in Scotland is not bound by the decisions in English cases) (see Arbitration Application No 3 of 2011 (2012) SLT 150). Accordingly, although the Act is relatively new, there is a wealth of authority that can be used as an aid to interpretation, and it is reasonable to expect that the Scottish courts will follow the approach adopted by the English courts in respect of the equivalent provision of the 1996 Act, unless there is a good reason to take a different approach. Whilst the provisions of the Act reflect the 1996 Act, the structure is significantly different, with provisions governing the conduct of an arbitration extracted, and gathered together in logical order to form a set of arbitration rules (“the Scottish Arbitration Rules”) which form Schedule 1 to the Act. It is important to remember that whilst Scotland has been in a political union with England and Wales since 1707, Scots law and the Scottish courts were preserved by the Acts of Union, and the distinction between Scots law and that of England and Wales has persisted. With the reestablishment of the Scottish Parliament in 1999, that distinction has perhaps been reinforced and extended. Whilst there are many areas of similarity, it can be said that Scotland is a mixed common law/civil system. It is perhaps ironic that whilst the Romans never held sway in Scotland, their legal codes were adopted by the Scots in the Middle Ages due to Scots legal scholars attending Dutch universities and returning to Scotland to spread the influence on Roman-Dutch law. In practical terms, this is unlikely to affect parties to an arbitration unless Scots law is the substantive law of the contract. However, the civil law roots of Scots law have had a lasting effect on some aspects of procedure. Whilst, as explained below, procedure is a matter for the tribunal, if the members of the tribunal are Scots lawyers, they will come from a background where, for example, there is no standard discovery, and the production of documents is only ordered where it can be shown that this is necessary for the development of an existing case. ‘Fishing’ exercises are not permitted. There are no current proposals to reform the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010. However, the legislation has not yet been implemented in full in that the provisions relating to statutory arbitration have not yet been brought into force. b. Mandatory provisions The following rules are mandatory, and cannot be modified or disapplied (by an arbitration agreement, by any other agreement between the parties or by any other mean) in relation to any arbitration seated in Scotland: – Rule 3 provides that only an individual may be an arbitrator. – Rule 4 sets out the provision that an arbitrator cannot be aged sixteen or under, and cannot be suffering from mental incapacity. – Rule 7 provides what is to happen where the appointment procedure fails, and allows either party to refer to one of the statutory arbitral appointments referees to make an appointment. – Rule 8 contains the duty to disclose any conflict of interest. – Rule 12 provides for removal of an arbitrator by the court if satisfied that certain criteria have been met. – Rule 13 provides for dismissal of the tribunal by the court in certain limited circumstances. – Rule 14 sets out preconditions for the removal of an arbitrator or the tribunal. – Rule 15 provides sets out the circumstances in which an arbitrator may resign. – Rule 16 sets out the consequences of an arbitrator resigning. – Rule 19 provides that the tribunal may rule upon its own jurisdiction. – Rule 20 gives parties a right to object to the tribunal that it does not have jurisdiction. – Rule 21 allows an appeal to the court against the tribunal's ruling on its own jurisdiction. – Rule 23 sets out the procedure to be followed if a party makes a direct reference to the court on jurisdiction (assuming that right has not been excluded). – Rule 24 sets out the general duty of the tribunal to be impartial and independent, to treat the parties fairly, and to conduct the arbitration without unnecessary delay and without incurring unnecessary expense. – Rule 25 places an obligation on the parties to ensure that the arbitration is conducted without unnecessary delay and without incurring unnecessary expense. – Rule 42 sets out a mandatory procedure for referrals to the court on points of Scots law (assuming that parties have not deleted that default provision). – Rule 44 sets out the procedure where an application has been made to the court to vary a time limit (assuming that power has not been excluded by the parties). – Rule 45 gives the court power to order attendance of witnesses and disclosure of evidence. – Rule 48 gives the tribunal power to award payment and damages. – Rule 50 provides the tribunal with power to award interest. – Rule 54 allows the tribunal to make part awards. – Rule 56 gives the tribunal power to withhold its award if fees or expenses have not been paid. – Rule 60 makes the parties severally liable to pay the arbitrators their fees and expenses. – Rule 63 places a ban on pre-dispute agreements about the liability for arbitration expenses. – Rule 67 allows challenges to an award on the basis of substantive jurisdiction. – Rule 68 provides for challenges to an award on the basis of serious irregularity. – Rule 70 sets out the procedure to apply where a legal error appeal has been made under Rule 69 (a default rule). – Rule 71 sets out supplementary rules on challenges to awards. – Rule 72 provides for reconsideration of an award by the tribunal where the court has ordered the tribunal to reconsider its award following an appeal. – Rule 73 sets out the immunity rules, and makes it clear that neither the tribunal nor any arbitrator is liable for anything done or omitted in performance of the tribunal's functions except where there has been bad faith, or where there has been an improper resignation. The rule also covers clerks, agents, employees, or other persons assisting (such as tribunal secretaries). – Rule 74 provides for immunity of arbitral institutions. – Rule 75 provides that experts, witnesses, and legal representatives enjoy the same degree of immunity as they would in court. – Rule 76 sets out the circumstances in which a right to object to the tribunal or the court on certain grounds, such as jurisdiction and fair treatment can be lost if not made timeously. – Rule 77 lists the circumstances in which an arbitrator may be held not to be independent. – Rule 79 provides that an arbitrator's authority ends on the death of the arbitrator. – Rule 82 sets out the position in relation to umpires. The remaining rules are default rules and may be modified by the parties in the arbitration agreement or by any other means at any time before or after the arbitration begins (Sect. 9). 2. Practice on Arbitration a. General Prior to the coming into force of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto), the arbitration regime in Scotland was not fit for purpose. The applicable law was scattered over various Acts of the UK Parliament, the pre-Union Scottish Parliament, and various cases. Whilst institutions such as the Chartered Institute of Arbitrators prepared rules, which if adopted, overcame the shortfalls in the law, arbitration was not widely used, and was confined to construction contracts, leases, and the odd partnership agreement. The introduction of statutory adjudication for construction disputes in 1998 greatly reduced the use arbitration for those types of dispute. With the introduction of the 2010 Act, Scotland now has one of the most modern systems of arbitration in the world, and there has been a gradual uplift in the use of arbitration. The construction industry is rediscovering arbitration, and it is now the default dispute mechanism in the SBCC (Scottish standard form) building contracts. There has also been an increase in the use of arbitration in energy contracts, including upstream oil and gas, and renewable energy contracts. As a native English-speaking jurisdiction with a legal system noted for its consistency and incorruptibility, and with a regime modelled on the English Act of 1996, but within a mixed common law/civil law system, Scotland should be fertile territory for the growth of international arbitration. b. Arbitral institutions Scottish Arbitration Centre Level 3 125 Princes Street Edinburgh Scotland, United Kingdom Telephone: +44 (0) 131 226 4686 Facsimile: +44 (0) 131 240 0830 Email: info@scottisharbitrationcentre.org Website: <www.scottisharbitrationcentre.org> The Scottish Arbitration Centre makes ad hoc arbitration appointments via its domestic and international arbitral appointments committee. It does not have its own rules, and does not currently administer arbitrations. In addition to the Scottish Arbitration Centre, a number of bodies have been made “Arbitral Appointment Referees” within the meaning of the 2010 Act. Whilst none of these bodies operate institutional rules, they can make ad hoc appointments in terms of the legislation. Agricultural Industries Confederation Limited Confederation House East of England Showground Peterborough, PE2 6XE United Kingdom Telephone: +44 (0)1733 385230 E-mail: enquiries@agindustries.org.uk Website: <www.agindustries.org.uk> Chartered Institute of Arbitrators 12 Bloomsbury Square London, WC1A 2LP United Kingdom Telephone: +44 (0)20 7421 7441 E-mail: das@ciarb.org Website: <https://www.ciarb.org/our-network/great-britain/scotland/> Dean of the Faculty of Advocates The Faculty of Advocates Parliament Square Edinburgh EH1 1RF Scotland, United Kingdom Telephone: +44 (0)131 260 5689 E-mail: deans.secretariat@advocates.org.uk Website: <http://fdrs.advocates.org.uk/> Institution of Civil Engineers 1 Great George Street, Westminster, London SW1P 3AA United Kingdom E-mail: disputes@ice.org.uk Website: <https://www.ice.org.uk/knowledge-and-resources/professional-practice/ice-dispute-services> Law Society of Scotland Atria One, 144 Morrison Street Edinburgh EH3 8EX Scotland, United Kingdom Telephone: +44 (0)131 226 7411 Facsimile: +44 (0)131 225 2934 E-mail: lawscot@lawscot.org.uk Website: <www.lawscot.org.uk/members/business-support/alternative-dispute-resolution-service/> Royal Incorporation of Architects in Scotland The Royal Incorporation of Architects in Scotland 15 Rutland Square Edinburgh EH1 2BE Scotland, United Kingdom Telephone: +44 (0)131 229 7545 Facsimile: +44 (0)131 228 2188 E-mail: info@rias.org.uk Website: <www.rias.org.uk> Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors RICS Dispute Resolution Service 125 Princes Street Edinburgh, EH2 4AD Scotland, United Kingdom Telephone: +44 (0)131 240 0832 E-mail: drsscotland@rics.org Website: <www.rics.org/drsscotland> Scottish Agricultural Arbiters and Valuers Association (SAAVA) C/o Seed & Co Cothill Duns, Berwickshire, TD10 6YW Scotland, United Kingdom Telephone: +44 (0)1361 882920 Facsimile: +44 (0)1361 882866 Website: <www.saava.org.uk> 3. Bibliography a. Books Davidson, Fraser Arbitration, 2nd edn. (W. Green 2012) 905 pages; ISBN: 9780414017764 Dundas, Hew and Bartos David Dundas & Bartos on the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010, 2nd edn. (W. Green 2014) 550 pages; ISBN: 9780414019270 Parratt, David and Foreman, Peter Scottish Arbitration Handbook (Avizandum 2011); ISBN: 9781904968443 b. Articles Dingwall, Joanna “International arbitration in Scotland: a bold, new future”, 13 International Arbitration Law Review (2010, no. 4) pp. 138-147 Dundas, Hew “Recent cases in the Scottish and English courts”, 80 Arbitration (2014, no. 4) pp. 450-458 Dundas, Hew “The Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010: converting vision into reality”, Arbitration 76 (2010, no. 1) pp. 2-15 Dowers, Neil “First published decision under the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010”, 16 International Arbitration Law Review (2013, no. 1) N3-N5 Hope, James “International arbitration in Scotland: a view from abroad”, 76 Arbitration (2010, no. 1) pp. 16-20 Yu, Hong-Lin “Three may not be a crowd: who is in the driver's seat under s.9 of the Contract (Third Party Rights) Act 2017”, Journal of Business Law (2018, no. 7) pp. 539-558 c. Journals and periodicals There are no Scottish periodicals dedicated exclusively to arbitration, but reports on arbitration cases in the Scottish courts, and articles on Scottish arbitration can be found in the Scots Law Times and the Juridical Review, both published by W. Green. d. Publication of awards Awards are not generally published in Scotland. There is a presumption of confidentiality created by Rule 26 (an opt-out confidentiality regime) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010, see Annex I).

CHAPTER II ARBITRATION AGREEMENT 1. Form and Contents of the Agreement a. Arbitration clause or submission agreement As a general rule arbitration does not arise as a means of resolving disputes unless the parties have agreed that it is by that means that the dispute be decided. Such an agreement may be contained in the contract itself, or may be a separate agreement. Thus, parties may agree in advance that any disputes arising between them of whatever nature shall be determined by arbitration; after a dispute has arisen they may agree it should be determined by arbitration, even if they are already involved in litigation in respect of the matter. b. Form requirements One question that is not determined by the legislation is whether the agreement to arbitrate must be made in writing (and, if so, precisely what is necessary to constitute writing) or may be made orally. It seems clear that the agreement may be made orally, although it is unlikely that such agreements will occur frequently. Where the UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration (the “Model Law”) applies, it is clear that the agreement is to be in writing, but that writing includes any form of record of the agreement (see Art. 7(2) of the Model law). c. Model arbitration clause Where parties wish the Scottish Arbitration Centre to make an ad hoc appointment via the Centre's Arbitral Appointments Committee, they may use the following clause: “Any dispute or difference arising out of or in connection with this contract, including any question regarding its existence, validity or termination, shall be determined by the appointment of a single arbitrator to be agreed between the parties, or failing agreement within fourteen days after either party has given to the other a written request to concur in the appointment of an arbitrator, by an arbitrator to be appointed by the Scottish Arbitration Centre on the written application of either party. The seat of arbitration shall be Scotland. The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English.” The Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto) is compatible with the rules of the major arbitral institutions and to benefit from the advantages of using the Scottish Act, all that is necessary is for parties to specify Scotland, or a place within Scotland, such as Edinburgh, as the seat of arbitration. 2. Parties to the Agreement a. Generally/capacity Any natural or legal person with capacity to contract may enter into an agreement to arbitrate. The capacity of a legal person will normally be determined by the law of the state in which it is incorporated. Where a person lacks legal capacity, the court has power in terms of Rule 46(1)(a) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010, (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I) to appoint a person to safeguard the interests of that person. b. Bankruptcy A natural person, who is sequestrated may enter into arbitration agreements and arbitrate. However, such a person cannot bind their insolvent estate. The real issue is therefore whether the appointed trustee adopts the contract of which the arbitration clause forms part. The trustee has power to refer a claim or other matters capable of being arbitrated arising under the sequestration to arbitration. c. State or State agencies The 2010 Act binds the Crown (which for practical purposes means the UK State and its emanations). Foreign states may be party to arbitration agreements. The State Immunity Act of 1978 (an Act of the UK Parliament) applies to Scotland. That Act restricted the immunity that states can claim from the jurisdiction of courts and tribunals in the UK in respect of their commercial obligations. In terms of Sect. 9 of the 1978 Act, where a State has agreed in writing to submit a dispute which has arisen, or may arise, to arbitration, the State is not immune as respects proceedings in the courts of the United Kingdom (including the courts of Scotland) which relate to the arbitration. This section does not apply to arbitrations between states. d. Multi-party arbitration Rule 40, which is a default rule provides that the parties may agree to consolidate the arbitration with another arbitration or hold concurrent hearings. However, the tribunal has no power to make such orders of its own initiative. 3. Domain of Arbitration a. Arbitrability Sect. 30 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) provides “Nothing in this Act makes any dispute capable of being arbitrated if, because of its subject-matter, it would not otherwise be capable of being arbitrated.” In other words, the Act does not amend the existing law regarding arbitrability. There is no list of matters that are capable of being arbitrated, nor is there any list of matters that cannot be arbitrated. The general principle, stated by Lord Bankton, in the eighteenth century (Institute I, 23, 17) is that “Whatever can be transacted may be determined by arbitration.” As a general rule, matters of public law and personal status are not capable of arbitration. Thus, questions of criminal liability cannot be determined by arbitration. Questions of parentage and marriage cannot be arbitrated (although the financial implications of divorce can be). b. Filling gaps In terms of Rule 47 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules (see Annex I hereto) the tribunal must decide the dispute in accordance with the law chosen by the parties as applicable to the substance of the dispute, or where no such choice is made, or a purported choice is unlawful, in accordance with the law determined by the conflict of law rules which the tribunal considers to be applicable. The tribunal must also have regard to normal commercial or trade usage of any undefined terms in the provision of any contract, any established commercial or trade customs or practices relevant to the substance of the dispute, and any other matter which the parties agree is relevant to the circumstances. Accordingly, there is considerable scope for the tribunal to fill in gaps in the contract where the parties have expressly authorized it to do so. If parties have not given express authority, the position is more complicated. There is English authority (F&G Sykes (Wessex) Ltd v. Fine Fare Ltd [1967] 1 Lloyd's Rep. 53) to the effect that a dispute arising in respect of an agreement to agree can be treated as falling within an arbitration clause. It seems likely that this authority would be followed in Scotland. c. Adapting contracts There is nothing in the 2010 Act which prevents the tribunal from adapting a contract to fundamentally changed circumstances, such as price adjustments in long term contracts if it is authorized to do so by the parties. 4. Separability of Arbitration Clause The Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto) provides that where an arbitration agreement forms only part of an agreement, it is to be treated as a distinct agreement. It is not void, voidable or otherwise unenforceable only because the agreement of which it is part is void, voidable or otherwise unenforceable. Further, a dispute as to the validity of such an agreement containing the arbitration agreement may be arbitrated in accordance with that arbitration agreement (Sect. 5). 5. Effect of the Agreement a. Duty of court It is said that Scots law has always “permitted private parties to exclude the merits if any dispute between them from the consideration of the Court by simply naming their arbiter” (Hamlyn & Co v. Talisker Distillery (1894) 21R (HL)). This approach was summed up by Lord Dunedin in the case of Sanderson & Son v. Armour & Co 1922 SC (HL) 117 where he said, “If the parties have contracted to arbitrate, to arbitration they must go.” This duty of the court to stay (or ‘sist’ to use Scottish terminology) an action now has a statutory basis in Sect. 10 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto), which provides that the court must, on application by a party to legal proceedings concerning any matter under dispute, sist those proceedings in so far as they concern that matter if there is an arbitration agreement covering the disputed matter, the proper procedural steps have been followed, and the applicant has not taken steps to respond to the substance of the dispute or otherwise acted in a manner indicating a desire to have the dispute resolved by legal proceedings rather than arbitration. The court is entitled to consider whether there is a valid arbitration agreement in place, but unless there has been active participation in the litigation, the court is obliged to put the proceedings on hold. This provision applies regardless of whether the arbitration is seated in Scotland. b. Court examination of arbitration agreement The Scottish courts have not yet had to determine whether an inquiry under Sect. 10 of the Act into the arbitration agreement ought to be carried out on a prima facie basis, or on the basis of a full review. However, it has been argued persuasively that the court should take a prima facie approach to determining whether there is an arbitration agreement, and whether the applicant is party to it. (See Dundas and Bartos paras. S10-17 to S10-43). An application to sist the action for arbitration should be made prior to time for lodging defences in the action. The lodging of defences is likely to be construed by the court as a response to the substance of the claim. c. Kompetenz-kompetenz Rule 19 (a mandatory rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules (see Annex I hereto) empowers the tribunal to rule on its own jurisdiction, including the question of whether there is a valid arbitration agreement, whether the tribunal is properly constituted, and what matters have been submitted to arbitration in accordance with the arbitration agreement. Where the tribunal receives such an application, it must either uphold or reject it. If it upholds the objection in whole or in part, it must end the arbitration in so far as it relates to a matter over which the tribunal has ruled it does not have jurisdiction, and set aside any provisional or part award already made in so far as the award relates to such a matter. The tribunal may rule on an objection independently from dealing with the subject matter of the dispute, or delay ruling on an objection until it makes its award on the merits of the dispute (and include its ruling in that award), but, where the parties agree which of these courses the tribunal should take, the tribunal must proceed accordingly.

CHAPTER III ARBITRATORS 1. Qualifications a. Requirements It is a mandatory rule under the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) that an arbitrator is to be an individual (Rule 3). Only a person aged sixteen or over is eligible to act as an arbitrator, and that person must not be an incapable adult within the meaning of Sect. 1(6) of the Adults with Incapacity (Scotland) Act 2000. b. Restrictions There are no minimum qualifications to be appointed as an arbitrator. However, in practice, Scottish arbitrators tend to belong to professional bodies with stringent requirements for admittance to their panels. There is no requirement that an arbitrator is admitted to the Scottish bar or solicitor profession, and there is no restriction based on nationality or residence. Foreigners can act as arbitrator or counsel in an arbitration. It is possible, although rare, for Scottish judges to be appointed as arbitrators. c. Disclosure Since the arbitral tribunal must be impartial and independent (Rule 24), it is incumbent upon any arbitrator or any individual asked to be an arbitrator but not yet appointed to disclose to the parties and to other appointing bodies any circumstances known to that individual which might reasonably be considered relevant in considering whether the individual is impartial and independent. Neither of these words is defined. The obligation to disclose is a continuing one throughout the course of the arbitration. 2. Appointment of Arbitrators Rule 6 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules (see Annex I hereto) provides (in the absence of agreement upon a different approach) that if this is to be a sole arbitrator, the parties must appoint the arbitrator jointly and within a fixed period. If more arbitrators are to be appointed, each party must appoint an eligible individual and the arbitrators so appointed appoint the remainder. When the parties cannot agree upon the proposed appointment, the parties together, or either of them, may refer the matter to an Arbitral Appointments Referee, and failing that the court may on application by any party make the appointment. In any such case the referee or the court must have regard to the nature of the dispute, the terms of the arbitration agreement and the skills and experience required to make the arbitrator suitable to determine that dispute (Rule 7). 3. Number of Arbitrators Where there is no agreement as to the number of arbitrators, then the tribunal is to consist of a sole arbitrator. This is to be contrasted with the UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration, which provides that in such cases there shall be three. It should at this stage be mentioned that in some circumstances, e.g., rent reviews, it is common practice for each party to appoint one arbitrator and these two to conduct the arbitration; but if they cannot agree on matters an umpire replaces them and makes the relevant decisions. The umpire may have been chosen by the parties or failing that by the arbitrators. 4. Challenge to Arbitrators a. Grounds By Rule 10 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules (see Annex I hereto) a party may object to the tribunal about the appointment of an arbitrator on the ground that he is not impartial and independent. The tribunal may then revoke his appointment. The court may remove an arbitrator if satisfied on the application of a party that he is not impartial and independent (Rule 12(a)). In addition, an arbitrator may be removed if the parties jointly agree to do so (Rule 11(1)(a)), or a third party to whom the parties have given power to remove does so (Rule 11(1)(b)). The court has further powers to remove an arbitrator if satisfied that he has not treated the parties fairly, is incapable of acting as arbitrator, lacks a qualification which parties agreed (pre-appointment) that he must have or that substantial injustice is or will be caused to the party applying because he has failed to conduct the arbitration in accordance with the Rules or the agreement of the parties relating to the arbitration (Rules 12, 13). However, the court may only do so if the arbitrator has been notified and given an opportunity to make representations and it is satisfied that any recourse available to the parties has been exhausted (Rule 14). b. Procedure Where a challenge has been brought against the arbitrator under Rules 10 and 12, he may resign. He may also do so if the parties' consent, if he has a contractual right to resign or if the court authorizes his resignation, which it may only do if satisfied that it is reasonable for him to do so. A challenge under Rule 10 must be made within fourteen days of the objector becoming aware of the facts on which it is based. It must be dealt with by the tribunal by confirming or revoking the appointment within fourteen days. If no decision is issued within fourteen days of a competent objection being made, the appointment is revoked. Removal by the parties under Rule 11 is effected by notice to the arbitrator. The procedure for removal by the court under Rule 12 is commenced by a petition to the Outer House of the Court of Session (the court of first instance) in terms of Chapter 100 of the Rules of the Court of Session. 5. Termination of the Arbitrator's Mandate An arbitrator's tenure ends if the arbitrator becomes ineligible to act as an arbitrator (see Rule 4 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules (Annex I hereto)), the tribunal revokes the arbitrator's appointment (see Rule 10), the arbitrator is removed by the parties, a third party or the Outer House (see Rules 11 and 12), the Outer House dismisses the tribunal of which the arbitrator forms part (see Rule 13), the arbitrator resigns (see Rule 15) or dies (see Rule 79). In terms of Rule 13, the Outer House may dismiss the tribunal if satisfied on the application by a party that substantial injustice has been or will be caused to that party because the tribunal has failed to conduct the arbitration in accordance with the arbitration agreement, the Scottish Arbitration Rules (in so far as they apply), or any other agreement by the parties relating to conduct of the arbitration. In terms of Rule 14, the Outer House may remove an arbitrator, or dismiss the tribunal, only if the arbitrator or, as the case may be, tribunal has been notified of the application for removal or dismissal, and given the opportunity to make representations, and the Outer House is satisfied that any recourse available under Rule 10 (objection to the tribunal) has been exhausted, and that any available recourse to a third party (such as an arbitral institution) who the parties have agreed is to have power to remove an arbitrator (or dismiss the tribunal) has been exhausted. Rule 17 provides that where an arbitrator's tenure ends, the tribunal must be reconstituted in accordance with the procedure used to constitute the original tribunal, or where that procedure fails, in accordance with the provisions of the rules outlined in Chapter III above. 6. Liability of Arbitrators An arbitrator has immunity in respect of the performance of his functions save where an act or omission of the arbitrator is shown to have been in bad faith (Rule 73 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto)). This provision has not yet been tested by the courts. Where the arbitrator's tenure ends, the court may make such order as it thinks fit as to the fees and expenses of the arbitrator, either as to entitlement or repayment. It may also make an order as to his liability in respect of acting as an arbitrator. Where the arbitrator has resigned, the court is bound to have regards to whether or not the arbitrator has resigned in accordance with the provisions of the 2010 Act.

CHAPTER IV ARBITRAL PROCEDURE 1. Place of Arbitration a. Determination The seat is a matter for agreement of the parties. If they have not agreed, and have not given power to a third party (such as an institution) to determine the seat, the tribunal has power to designate the seat (Sect. 3 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto)). In the absence of such designation, the court is empowered to designate the seat. b. Legal consequences The legal consequence of Scotland (or a place within Scotland) being designated as the seat of the arbitration is that the arbitration is subject to the 2010 Act. The 2010 Act makes a distinction between the seat or legal place on the one hand, and the venue or geographical place on the other. Rule 29 provides that the tribunal may meet, and otherwise conduct the arbitration, anywhere it chooses (within or outside Scotland). There is no requirement for the award to be signed within Scotland. 2. Arbitral Proceedings in General a. Mandatory provisions In terms of Rule 28 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto)), procedure is a matter for the tribunal. Rule 28 is a default rule, so it is open to parties to alter it, and make provision for a set procedure to apply, either expressly, or by adopting rules inconsistent with the provisions of Rule 28. However, even where the default position applies, and the tribunal has discretion over procedure, that discretion must be exercised in accordance with the applicable mandatory rules, and in particular, the general duties of the tribunal under mandatory Rule 24. Rule 24 provides that the tribunal must be impartial and independent, treat the parties fairly, and conduct the arbitration without unnecessary delay, and without incurring unnecessary expense. “Treating the parties fairly” is defined by the Act as including giving each party a reasonable opportunity to put its case and to deal with the other party's case. b. Determining procedure It is for the tribunal to determine the procedure in the arbitration. If the parties have agreed on a specific timetable or procedure, altering the general power in Rule 28, then the tribunal is obliged to comply with that, as are the parties. However, in terms of Rule 43 (a default rule) the court has power, on an application by the tribunal or any party to vary or set aside a time limited relating to the arbitration which is imposed in the arbitration agreement or by virtue of any agreement between the parties. c. Exchange of written pleadings It is usual for there to be an exchange of pleadings in advance of any substantive hearing, so that the parties are fully aware of each other's position. However, as noted above, procedure is a question for the tribunal (and the agreement of the parties), and it is quite possible to have a form of procedure that does not include written pleadings. d. Oral hearing An oral hearing is not required by the Rules, and the arbitration can be determined on the basis of written evidence if the parties so agree. Even where there is no such agreement, so long as the tribunal complies with its duties to treat the parties fairly, the tribunal can still decide the issues without an oral hearing. An exception to this would be where the parties have agreed to have a right to an oral hearing as part of an procedure which varies the Rules. 3. Evidence a. General The tribunal has full freedom to determine the admissibility, relevance, materiality and weight of the evidence submitted by the parties. Arbitrators are not bound by the rules of evidence prevailing in the Scottish courts. b. Evidence of fact witnesses Witnesses can be sworn in before the arbitrators. In terms of Rule 45 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto), the court has power to compel a witness to attend a hearing for the purposes of giving evidence to the tribunal, and may order the production of documentary or other material evidence (subject to the rules of privileged confidentiality). Whether or not cross-examination is allowed is a matter for the tribunal to determine. Some arbitrators will allow cross-examination, whilst others may preclude it, or insist that questions are directed through the tribunal. c. Evidence of party-appointed expert witnesses (for tribunal-appointed expert witnesses, see Chapter IV.4 below) It is common for parties to appoint their own expert witnesses. It is for the tribunal to determine the form of expert evidence. It is common for experts to adopt their report, and for there to be cross-examination, but there are no set rules. It is also possible for both experts to give evidence at the same time, the process known as “hot-tubbing”. In such a case, the tribunal will normally lead the questioning, adopting a more inquisitorial role. d. Documentary evidence The question of the admissibility of evidence, including written evidence, is a matter for the tribunal under its general power in Rule 28 (a default rule). e. Document disclosure Parties are free to seek the disclosure of documents, and even full discovery, and it is a matter for the tribunal to determine whether, and to what extent such disclosure/discovery it to be allowed. In the Scottish courts, there is no duty of disclosure. Discovery in the Anglo/American style is not allowed, and an order for the recovery of documents is to be justified with reference to the case in the pleadings. That is to say that ‘fishing exercises’ are not allowed. There is no equivalent rule in the Act, and it is a matter for the tribunal to determine how disclosure of documents is to be dealt with. However, an arbitrator from a Scotland is likely to be less inclined to grant open discovery than one from common law jurisdictions. 4. Tribunal-Appointed Experts a. Power of the tribunal In terms of Rule 34 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto) the tribunal may obtain an expert opinion on any matter arising in the arbitration. The consent of the parties is not required, although it is obviously good practice to obtain the parties' views before proceeding. The tribunal must also have regard to its duty to avoid incurring unnecessary expense. b. Role of the parties There is no requirement on the part of the tribunal to consult with the parties regarding the questions that will be submitted to the expert, although it is sensible for the tribunal to do this. c. Rules applicable to the tribunal-appointed expert There are no distinct rules for tribunal-appointed experts, other than in respect of the rights of the parties noted below. d. Procedure There is no requirement for the expert's report to be in writing. The parties must be given a reasonable opportunity to make representations about any written expert report, and where the expert is to give an oral expert opinion, they must be permitted to hear that opinion, and to ask questions of the expert giving it. 5. Interim Measures of Protection a. Categories of interim measures – tribunal By virtue of Rule 53 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) the tribunal is empowered to make a provisional award granting any relief on a provisional basis which it has the power to grant permanently. This is a broad power, and of course it must be exercised with caution, since, ultimately, any provisional award might be reversed in the final award. However, there are clear advantages of such a rule, as it allows the tribunal to deal with, for example, the question of what is to happen to perishable goods pending the outcome of the arbitration. Separately, in terms of Rule 64 (a default rule) the tribunal has the power to order a party making a claim (principal claim or counter claim) to provide security for the recoverable arbitration expenses or any part of them. However, the tribunal does not have power to order arrestment/attachment of property, and an order from the court is required to achieve that. b. Comparison with 2006 Model Law The Act does not expressly adopt Chapter IV.A of the UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration (the “Model Law”) (with 2006 amendments) regarding interim measures. The Act does not restrict the power to make provisional awards, and does not set out the definition of an interim measure as appears in Art. 17(2) of the Model Law. The Act does not specify conditions for granting interim measures such as those set out in Art. 17A, although the tribunal will normally wish to be addressed on the issues identified in Art. 17A. c. Form There is no requirement for an interim measure to take the form of an award, but if it is necessary to enforce it, a provisional award is necessary. A provisional award is an award within the meaning of the Act and may be enforced as such. It is, however, doubtful that a provisional award would be regarded as an award for the purposes of the Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards of 10 June 1958 (the “New York Convention”). Therefore, whilst a Scottish court has discretion to enforce a provisional award, it is not certain that such an award would be enforceable in other jurisdictions. d. Categories of interim measures – court Rule 46 provides that the court has the same power in an arbitration as it has in civil proceedings to appoint a person to safeguard the interests of any party lacking capacity, to order the sale of any property in dispute in the arbitration, to make an order securing any amount in dispute in the arbitration, to make an order under Sect. 1 of the Administration of Justice (Scotland) Act 1972 (c. 59) (for the production of documents), to grant warrant for arrestment or inhibition, to grant interdict (estoppel) (or interim interdict), to grant any other interim or permanent order. 6. Representation and Legal Assistance There are no restrictions as to who may represent a party in an arbitration. However, in terms of Rule 33 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto), the party must give notice of that representative to the tribunal and the other party before the representation begins. There is no requirement for the representative to lodge a power of attorney, although the tribunal could order this if there were doubt as to the representative's authority. There is no requirement that the representative is legally qualified in Scotland or any other jurisdiction. 7. Default Default of the parties is dealt with in Rules 37 to 39 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto), which are all default rules. If a party unnecessarily delays in submitting or otherwise pursuing a claim and the tribunal considers that there is no good reason for this and the delay is giving risk to a risk that it will not be possible to resolve the claim fairly or that it has caused or is likely to cause prejudice to the other party, the tribunal must end the arbitration so far as it relates to the subject matter of the claim and may make such award as it considers appropriate. If a party unnecessarily delays in submitting a defence to a claim without good reason, the tribunal must proceed with the arbitration, although the delay is not in itself to be treated as an admission of anything. The rule applies to a ‘claim’ rather than to the claimant or respondent as such, and therefore applies to claims and counterclaims. If a party fails to attend a hearing or produce a document or other evidence requested by the tribunal without good reason, the tribunal can proceed to make its award on the basis of the evidence before it. Rule 39 gives the tribunal powers where there has been a failure to comply with tribunal directions, or an obligation imposed in the arbitration agreement. This may include drawing adverse inferences. 8. Confidentiality of the Award and Proceedings i. Confidentiality of the Arbitral Award and Proceedings Arbitration in Scotland is a private process, and confidentiality has a statutory basis in the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto). Rule 26 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Act provides an opt-out regime in terms of which disclosure by the tribunal, any arbitrator or a party of confidential information is actionable as a breach of confidence unless the disclose can be justified on certain defined grounds. Confidential information is defined as information which is not and has never been in the public domain. ii. Confidentiality of Arbitration-related Court Proceedings To preserve the confidentiality of the process, court proceedings relating to arbitration are anonymized. Sect. 15 of the Act provides that a party to any civil proceedings relating to an arbitration (with certain exceptions) may apply to the court for an order prohibiting the disclosure of the identity of a party to the arbitration in any report of the proceedings. Subject to certain exceptions, the court must grant the order.
