The relevant provisions are contained within Chapter 100 of the Rules of the Court of Session, which provides that the petition or note lodged in respect of the arbitration is not to be open for inspection, except by court staff and the parties, that the case is to be referred to as “Arbitration Application” or “Arbitration Appeal” as the case may be and by reference to a number and the year in which it was lodged, and the court proceedings are to be held in private. An example of the nomenclature referred to above can be found in Arbitration Application No 2 of 2011 2011 G.W.D. 38-785. In that case, Lord Glennie, who was at the time one of the Court's designated arbitration judges illustrated the Court's approach to anonymity in proceedings: “It was agreed at the end of the hearing that in the first instance I should issue my Opinion to the parties without publishing it more widely, to enable them to make representations as to whether there should be publication and, if so, whether any details could be omitted without removing from my decision such sense as it might otherwise have.” It should be noted that petitions for enforcement of an award under Sect. 12 of the Act are excluded from the scope of Sect. 15, but that petitions under Sect. 19 for the enforcement of New York Convention awards, are not excluded. The logic of this distinction is not entirely clear. Taken together, the effect of these provisions is that where there is an incidental application to the court during the course of an arbitration, to deal with, for example, a jurisdiction challenge, or the extension of a time limit, an application for anonymity is permitted by Sect. 15. Any petition challenging the validity of an award is also covered by Sect. 15. An action for enforcement of a ‘Convention Award’ (an award made in a New York Convention territory other than the UK) is enforced via Sect. 19 and is not excluded from the anonymity provision. The enforcement of any award seated in the UK, or in a non-New York Convention territory is excluded from the anonymity provisions of Sect. 15.

CHAPTER V ARBITRAL AWARD 1. Types of Award The Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) provides for provisional awards, part awards and draft awards as well as final awards. The tribunal has power in terms of Rule 53 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Act to make a provisional award in which it may grant any relief on a provisional basis which it has the power to grant permanently. Such an award is enforceable in the Scottish courts, but whether it would be enforceable in other jurisdictions must be doubtful given its interim status. It is not possible to appeal against a provisional award (see Rule 71(3)). In terms of Rule 54 (a mandatory rule) the tribunal has power to make a part award, which is an award that disposes (on a final, not provisional basis) of some, but not all of the matters which the tribunal is to decide in the arbitration. Rule 55 (a default rule) gives the tribunal power to issue a draft award to the parties, but if it does so, it must consider any representations from the parties about the draft received within the timescale set by the tribunal. 2. Making of the Award a. Decision-making Rule 30 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) provides that where the tribunal is unable to make a decision unanimously, a decision made by the majority of the arbitrators is sufficient, and that where there is neither unanimity nor a majority in favour of or opposed to making any decision the decision is to be made by the arbitrator nominated to chair the tribunal, or where no person has been so nominated, the decision is to be made where the tribunal consists of three or more arbitrators, by the last arbitrator to be appointed. b. Time limits There is no set time limit for the making of the award within the Act, although parties do commonly adopt rules which set a time limit. The tribunal has a general duty to avoid unnecessary delay. Where the parties have agreed a time limit, the tribunal, or any party may apply to the court to vary it (Rule 43). Whilst the terms of Rule 43 are broad, an application to vary the time limit should be brought before the expiry of the time limit. Applicable institutional rules will also be relevant. If no award has been made, and no extension has been granted (or perhaps at least applied for) by the expiry of the time limit, the jurisdiction of the tribunal to make an award falls.1 Cunninghame v. Drummond (1491) Mor. 635; Earl of Linlithgow v. Hamilton (1610) Mor. 636. c. Dissenting opinions It is possible for an arbitrator to issue a dissenting opinion. This may be incorporated into the award, in which case if forms part of it, or it may be issued separately, in which case it does not. The dissenting arbitrator might simply record their dissent within the award without issuing an opinion. 3. Form of the Award The award must be signed by all arbitrators (or all those assenting to the award). It must state the seat of the arbitration, when the award is made and when it takes effect, the tribunal's reasons for the award, and whether any previous provisional or part award has been made (and the extent to which any previous provisional award is superseded or confirmed). Rule 51 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto), which sets out the required form of an award is a default rule, so it is open to parties to alter it as they see fit. For example, they may dispense with the requirement to provide reasons. There is no defined standard of the level of reasoning required, but if there is a challenge to the award, the court may require the tribunal to give reasons, or further reasons for its award (see Rule 71). 4. Jurisdiction a. Power of arbitrators In terms of Rule 19 (a mandatory rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto), the tribunal may rule on its own jurisdiction, and in particular the tribunal may rule on whether there is a valid arbitration agreement, whether the tribunal is properly constituted, and what matters have been submitted to arbitration in accordance with the arbitration agreement. Rule 20 provides that any party may object to the tribunal that it does not have jurisdiction or has exceeded its jurisdiction in relation to any matter, and subject to the agreement of the parties, the tribunal may either rule on an objection independently from dealing with the subject matter, or delay ruling on an objection until it makes its award on the merits of the dispute, including its award on jurisdiction within the award on the merits. b. Form of jurisdictional decision The Act does not specify the form that a determination on jurisdiction must take. c. Appeal It is open to parties to appeal against the tribunal's ruling on jurisdiction (whether for or against the objection). Such an appeal is made to the Outer House of the Court of Session within fourteen days of the tribunal's decision on the objection. The decision of the Outer House is final. d. Timing An objection to jurisdiction should be raised before, or as soon as reasonably practicable after the matter to which the objection relates is first raised in the arbitration. It may be raised later where that is considered justified by the tribunal, but an objection must be made before the issue of the final award. Rule 20 governs this issue. Rule 76 provides that a party who participates in an arbitration without making a timeous objection on the ground that that the tribunal does not have jurisdiction may not raise the objection later before the tribunal or the court. Rules 20 and 76 are mandatory rules. e. Role of court Rule 22 (a default rule) provides that the court may determine any question of the tribunal's jurisdiction. This allows for a direct application to the court. However, in terms of Rule 23, such an application is only competent where the parties have agreed that it should be made, or where the tribunal consents, and the court is satisfied that determining the question is likely to produce substantial savings in expenses, that the application was made without delay, and that there is a good reason why the question should be determined by the court. The tribunal may continue with the arbitration pending the determination of the application to the court. 5. Applicable Law Rule 47 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) governs the rules applicable to the substance of the dispute. It applies to both domestic and international arbitration. As a default rule, parties are free to amend it. In terms of Rule 47, the tribunal is obliged to decide the dispute in accordance with the law chosen by the parties as applicable to the substance of the dispute, or failing that, the law determined by the conflict of law rules which the tribunal considers applicable. The tribunal is not permitted to decide the dispute on the basis of general considerations of justice, fairness or equity unless they form part of the law concerned or the parties otherwise agree. It is possible to give the tribunal power to decide as amiables compositeurs or ex aequo et bono by express agreement. The law governing the arbitration agreement is a separate question. In terms of Sect. 6 of the Act, where the parties to an arbitration agreement agree that an arbitration under that agreement is to be seated in Scotland, but the arbitration agreement does not specify the law which is to govern it, then, unless the parties otherwise agree, the arbitration agreement is to be governed by Scots law. 6. Settlement In terms of Rule 57 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto), on the request of the parties, the tribunal may make an award reflecting the terms of an agreed settlement. The tribunal has a discretion as to whether to make such an award, and may refuse to do so where, for example, an award in agreed terms would breach public policy, or where the arbitration has been a sham. In terms of the rule, the tribunal is not required to state reasons in a settlement award, and such awards cannot be challenged under Part 8 of the Act (lack of jurisdiction, serious irregularity or legal error). Settlement Awards under Rule 57 can be enforced in the same way as any other award under the Act. Of course it is possible that the courts of some jurisdictions may take the view that such an award is not a true award within the meaning of the New York Convention. Generally speaking, arbitral tribunals in Scottish seated arbitrations will not attempt to assist parties in negotiating a settlement for fear of a challenge on the grounds of apparent bias. However, where that is the express wish of parties, and the necessary caveats are put in place, the tribunal may try to assist settlement. 7A. Correction and Interpretation of the Award a. Correction In terms of Rule 58 (a default rule) of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto) the tribunal may correct an award so as to correct a clerical, typographical or other error in the award arising by virtue of accident or omission, or clarify or remove any ambiguity in the award. The tribunal may make such a correction on its own initiative, or on an application by any party. An application by a party to make such a correction must be made within twenty-eight days of the award being made (or such later date as the court allows). If the tribunal decides to correct that award of its own volition, it must give the parties a reasonable opportunity to make representations about the proposed correction. Where one party applies for a correction, the other party must be given a reasonable opportunity to comment on the proposed correction. b. Interpretation The tribunal is empowered under Rule 58 to issue a correction to clarify or remove any ambiguity in the award. Accordingly, where there is ambiguity, the tribunal can provide interpretation, but it probably cannot do so where the award is clear. c. Form The correction is achieved by issuing a corrected award. The corrected award is to be treated as if it was made in its corrected form on the day the award was made. d. Timing A correction made of the tribunal's own volition must be made within twenty-eight days of the award. Where it is made on the application of a party, the correction must be made within twenty-eight days of the application, and the application must be made within twenty-eight days of the award. 7B. Additional Award In terms of Rule 57 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto), an arbitration ends when the last award to be made in the arbitration is made (and no claim, including any claim for expenses or interest, is outstanding). Therefore, the tribunal retains jurisdiction to issue an award whilst such matters remain outstanding. There is no equivalent under the Act to the “additional award” under the Arbitration Act 1996 applicable in England and Wales. If interest and/or expenses, have not been dealt with, and a further award is required to deal with these issues, then this simply takes the form of an award in the usual away. It will be for the tribunal to determine liability for the additional costs arising from the further award. 8. Fees and Costs i. Costs in General Part 7 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto) deals with “arbitration expenses”. The term “arbitration expense” is defined by Rule 59 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Act as: “(a) the arbitrators' fees and expenses for which the parties are liable under rule 60, (b) any expenses incurred by the tribunal when conducting the arbitration for which the parties are liable under rule 60, (c) the parties' legal and other expenses, and (d) the fees and expenses of— (i) any arbitral appointments referee, and (ii) any other third party to whom the parties give powers in relation to the arbitration, for which the parties are liable under rule 60.” The phrase “other expenses” may include witness costs, travel and accommodation costs, expert witness fees, and other costs incidental to the process such as copying, translation and transcription charges. Rule 59 is a default rule, so it is open to parties to modify the definition of arbitrator expenses. ii. Deposit It is common for arbitrators to require a deposit or security for their fees, and of course this is almost always the case with institutional arbitration. The relevant institutional rules will determine how the deposit is paid and whether it is split equally between the parties. Where the arbitration is ad hoc, it is quite common for the tribunal to require a deposit. iii. Fees of Arbitrators Rule 60, a mandatory rule, governs arbitrators' fees and expenses. The amount of fees and expenses payable under Rule 60 are to be agreed by the parties and the arbitrators or, as the case may be, the arbitral appointments referee or other third party (such as an arbitral institution). Failing such agreement, the fees and expenses payable are to be determined by the Auditor of the Court of Session on the basis of a reasonable commercial rate of charge. Generally speaking, arbitrators' fees are charged on an hourly rate basis, although some fixed fee schemes to apply for smaller scale disputes (certain consumer schemes for example). If an institutional arbitration is used, the system of the institution will apply. iv. Costs of Legal Assistance to Parties The general rule is in Scotland is that costs follow the event. Rule 62(2) provides that when making an award for recoverable arbitral expense (which includes legal costs) the tribunal must have regard to the principle that expenses should follow a decision made in favour of a party except where that would be inappropriate in the circumstances. Rule 61(3) provides that the amount of expenses recoverable must be determined on the basis that a reasonable amount is to be allowed in respect of all reasonably incurred expenses. v. Award on Costs The principle that costs follow the event is applied on the theory that the party who has caused the expense ought to be liable to pay it. So in general terms, where a claimant is unsuccessful, costs are awarded against the claimant on the basis that the claimant has caused the expense by bringing a bad claim. Where the claimant is successful, the respondent is held liable, because the respondent has caused the expense by wrongly resisting the claim. Very often there is split success, and the tribunal is entitled to allocate costs according to its assessment of overall success. Other circumstances may justify a departure from the usual rule. For example, it may be the claimant has failed to obtain an award in a sum greater than an offer contained in a tender (sealed offer), in which case the tribunal might determine that it ought to be liable for expenses incurred after the date of the tender/offer. 9. Notification of the Award and Registration There is no requirement to register an award with the court. The award is made by delivery of the awards to the parties in accordance with the requirements of Rule 83 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto). This may be hand delivery, first class post, or some other method, including email which the sender reasonably considers likely to cause it to be delivered the same or next day. 10. Enforcement of Domestic and International Awards Rendered In Scotland a. General Sect. 12 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) deals with the enforcement of arbitration awards and provides that the court may order that a tribunal's award may be enforced as if it were a decree of the court. This rule applies to domestic and international awards. The court cannot make such an order if there is an ongoing appeal under Part 8 of the Act, or an arbitral process of appeal or review, or a process of correction under Rule 58 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Act, which has not been finally determined. A peculiarity of the Scottish system is that where the arbitration agreement is registered in the Books of Council and Session (the official register of documents), an award in respect of that arbitration agreement can itself be registered in the Books of Council and Session, allowing it to be executed without having to refer to the court. In practice, contracts such as commercial leases are often registered. However, other commercial contracts tend not to be, since the register is public. b. Leave for enforcement If an application under Sect. 12 is successful, no further order for leave to enforce is required, since the award will gain the status of a registered court decree. c. Enforcement The court cannot make an order if the tribunal did not have jurisdiction to make the award, and can restrict the order to exclude any part of the award in respect of which jurisdiction was lacking. However, a party cannot object on jurisdictional grounds if it has lost the right to do so in terms of the rules. 11. Publication of the Award Awards are not published in Scotland. On the contrary, the default position is that awards are confidential. It is possible for parties to waive confidentiality. For example, parties may adopt rules that provide for publication of the award. In that case, the conditions attached to such publication will be determined by the applicable rules. There are a number of exceptions to the rule on confidentiality, and in particular it is permissible to disclose confidential information relating to the arbitration where it would be in the public interest to do so. However, this exception is likely to be interpreted strictly by the court. Given that a breach of the rule against disclosure of confidential information is actionable if it causes loss, caution must be exercised.

CHAPTER VI ENFORCEMENT OF FOREIGN ARBITRAL AWARDS 1. Enforcement under Conventions and Treaties a. Conventions in force Scotland does not have capacity to enter into international treaties in its own right. However, Scotland is one of the constituent countries of the United Kingdom, and the UK is a signatory to various international conventions relating to the enforcement of foreign arbitral awards. In particular the UK is a signatory to the 1923 Geneva Protocol on Arbitration Clauses, the 1927 Geneva Convention on the Execution of Foreign Arbitral Awards and the 1958 Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (the “New York Convention”). The UK has also ratified the 1965 Convention on the Settlement of Investment Disputes between States and Nationals of Other States (the “ICSID Convention” or “Washington Convention”). b. Procedure Where an award has been made in the territory of a state other than the United Kingdom which is a party to the New York Convention under a written arbitration agreement (a “Convention award”), it is to be treated as binding on the persons as between whom it was made, and the court may order it to be enforced as if it were a registered court decree (Sects. 18, 19 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto)). Recognition or enforcement of a Convention award may only be refused if certain circumstances apply. It may be refused if a party was under some incapacity under the applicable law, the arbitration agreement was invalid, the person against whom it is invoked was not given proper notice of the arbitral process or was otherwise unable to present its case, or if the tribunal was constituted or the arbitration conducted otherwise than in accordance with the agreement of the parties or the law of the country in which it took place. It may also be refused if proved that the award deals with a dispute outside the submission to arbitration or is not binding or has been set aside. In addition, recognition or enforcement may be refused if the award relates to matters not capable of being settled by arbitration, or to grant it would be contrary to public policy (Sect. 20 of the Act). These grounds closely follow the grounds for refusal of enforcement set out in Art. V of the New York Convention. Enforcement of convention awards is governed by Part IX of Chapter 62 of the Rules of the Court of Session. To enforce a Convention award, it is necessary to lodge a petition, and with it the duly authenticated original award or a certified copy of it; the original agreement referred to in Art. II of the Convention or a certified copy of it; a translation of any award or agreement which is in a language other than English, certified by an official or sworn translator or by a diplomatic or consular agent; an affidavit stating the full name, title, trade or business and the usual or last known place of residence or, where appropriate, of the business of the petitioner or noter and the party against whom the Convention award was made; the amount of the Convention award which is unsatisfied; and that the Convention award has become binding on the parties and has not been set aside or suspended by a court of the country which, or under the law of which, the award was made. 2. Enforcement Where No Convention or Treaty Applies It remains possible to enforce a foreign award at common law. However, as noted above, the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto), in Sect. 12, provides a basis for enforcement of domestic and foreign awards, whether or not covered by the Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards of 10 June 1958 (the “New York Convention”). Enforcement proceeds by way of a petition to the court in terms of Chapter 100.10 of the Rules of the Court of Session. The petition procedure under this chapter is a shortened procedure, and is dealt with efficiently by the court. The rule of court sets out the documents which are to be lodged in support of the petition (including the original award or a certified copy) and the matters that must be set out in the petition. 3. Rules of Public Policy Recognition of a Convention award may be refused if to do so would be contrary to public policy (Sect. 20.4(b) of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto). This section has not yet been tested in the Scottish courts, but it is assumed that a Scottish court would take a narrow approach to the interpretation of public policy, and would find English authority persuasive on this point.

CHAPTER VII MEANS OF RECOURSE 1. Appeal on the Merits from an Arbitral Award i. Appeal to a Second Arbitral Instance There is nothing in the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto) preventing an appeal to a second arbitral instance if that is provided for in the applicable rules. The enforcement provisions of the Act recognize that such procedures may apply in that enforcement is delayed until any applicable arbitral process of repeal or review is completed (see Sect. 12(2)(b)). ii. Appeal to a Court An award may also be challenged on the ground that the tribunal erred on a point of Scots law (Rule 69 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Act). Such an appeal may, however, only be made with the agreement of the parties or leave of the court. Leave may only be given if the court is satisfied that the point will substantially affect a party's rights and that the decision of the tribunal was obviously wrong on a matter of general importance, open to serious doubt (Rule 70). A party may only appeal to the court if any available arbitral appeal or recourse has been exhausted, and must do so within twenty-eight days of the notification of the award, or if there has been appeal or recourse, the determination of that matter (Rule 71). Where the court orders the tribunal to reconsider its award it must do so within time limits, generally three months from the date of the court's decision (Rule 72). Parties can agree to exclude the right to appeal on the basis of an error of Scots law. If the contract is governed by another law, such as English law, no legal error appeal is possible. 2. Setting Aside of the Arbitral Award (Action for Annulment, Vacation of the Award) i. Grounds for Setting Aside A party may appeal to the Court of Session against an award on the ground that the tribunal had no jurisdiction to make it, and the court may confirm, vary or set aside the award. If there is an important point of principle or practice involved, the court may allow leave to appeal. However, appeal thereafter to the Supreme Court is excluded (Rule 67 of the Scottish Arbitration Rules set out in Schedule 1 of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (the “2010 Act” or “Act”, see Annex I hereto)). A party may also appeal on the grounds of serious irregularity. A serious irregularity is a challenge to the award in respect of the manner in which or the process by which the tribunal made its award, whereby substantial injustice has been or will be caused to the appellant. Several particular instances are set out in the Rules such as an arbitrator not being impartial and independent, not treating parties fairly, acting outside his or her powers, etc. Where the court does not confirm the award but orders the tribunal to reconsider the award, or part thereof, or sets it aside in whole or in part, the court may make such order as it thinks fit as to the fees and expenses of the arbitrator (Rule 68). ii. Procedure A challenge to an award is brought by way of a petition to the court and must be made no later than twenty-eight days after the later of the date on which the award was made, or if there is a correction process, from the date on which the tribunal decides whether or not to correct its award, or where there is an arbitral appeal process, the date on which the appellant is notified of the result of that process. The court has power to order the tribunal to reconsider the award, or part of it. iii. Waivers Rule 67 sets out the right to bring a challenge on the ground that the tribunal has exceeded its jurisdiction, and Rule 68, which sets out the right to bring a challenge on the ground that there has been a serious irregularity are mandatory rules. Accordingly, it is not possible to exclude such a challenge in the arbitration clause or the agreement to arbitrate, or by any subsequent agreement. Sect. 8 of the Act provides: “The following rules, called “mandatory rules”, cannot be modified or disapplied (by an arbitration agreement, by any other agreement between the parties or by any other means) in relation to any arbitration seated in Scotland - … Rules 67, 68, 70, 71 and 72 (challenging awards)” By contrast, Rule 69, which provides for challenges on the ground that the tribunal has made an error of Scots law, is a default rule and can therefore (in terms of Sect. 9) be modified or disapplied in the arbitration agreement, or by any other means, before or after the arbitration begins. iv. Effect of an Award that Has Been Set Aside When the court is presented with a jurisdiction challenge, the court has three options open to it. It may confirm the award; vary the award (or part of it); or set aside the award or part of it. When presented with a serious irregularity or legal error challenge, the court also has three options open to it. It may confirm the award; it may order the tribunal to reconsider the award (or part of it); or if it considers reconsideration inappropriate, it may aside the award (or part of it). An award that has been varied by the court is enforced as varied. An award that has been set aside in its entirety is a nullity. Where an award has been set aside in part, it has effect, except to the extent that it has been set aside. Where the award has been remitted to the tribunal in whole or in part, in respect of a serious irregularity or legal error challenge, the tribunal must make a new award (or confirm its original award) within three months of the decision of the court (unless appealed in which the three month period runs from the decision on the appeal, or earlier abandonment), or such other date as the court specifies. To the extent that the award has been remitted, it is suspended, and to the extent that it is revised, the previous version falls away and is null and void. 3. Other Means of Recourse There are no other means of recourse against the award.

CHAPTER VIII CONCILIATION / MEDIATION 1. General The UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Conciliation has not been adopted in Scotland. There is, however, considerable use made of conciliation and mediation services. In some cases the courts will suggest this to parties as an appropriate step, only proceeding to litigation or arbitration if no settlement results. Some commercial contracts provide expressly that before resorting to arbitration or litigation parties should attempt to resolve their disputes by conciliation or mediation. Both branches of the legal profession hold lists of members appropriately trained and experienced in mediation. The current lists of such members may be obtained in the case of advocates from: Faculty of Advocates Advocates' Library Parliament Square Edinburgh EH1 1RF Scotland Telephone: +44 (0)131 226 5071 Website: <www.advocates.org.uk> In the case of solicitors, the current list may be obtained from: The Law Society of Scotland Atria One 144 Morrison Street Edinburgh EH3 8EX Scotland Telephone: +44 (0)131 226 7411 Facsimile: +44 (0)131 225 2934 E-mail: lawscot@lawscot.org.uk Website: <www.lawscot.org.uk/> In addition, a number of other organizations can provide mediators, including the Scottish Branch of the Chartered Institute of Arbitrators, RICS Scotland, and the Scottish Mediation Network. 2. Legal Provisions Scotland does not have mediation legislation. Mediation is treated as a matter of contract. A mediation agreement may be enforced through the court in the same way as any other contract. Mediation is encouraged in the pre-action protocol of the Commercial Court, but is not required by the Court.

CHAPTER IX INVESTMENT TREATY ARBITRATION 1. Conventions and Treaties Scotland is not a party in its own right to conventions and treaties, but the United Kingdom, of which Scotland is a constituent country, is a party to various international conventions and treaties. 2. Investment Arbitration For investment arbitration decisions involving the United Kingdom, see the ICCA Handbook National Report on England and Wales. 3. National Investment Legislation In Scotland, despite the coming into force of the Arbitration (Scotland) Act 2010 (see Annex I hereto), international investment disputes continue to be arbitrated under the pre-existing statutory regime, contained in the Arbitration (International Investment Disputes) Act 1966. In relation to Scotland it gives effect to the 1965 Convention on the Settlement of Investment Disputes between States and Nationals of Other States (the “Washington Convention” or “ICSID Convention”).

sections.title: CHAPTER I: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: CHAPTER I: INTRODUCTION 1. Law on Arbitration a. Legislation The Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I) was enacted on 18 April 1994 and is based on the 1985 UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration (the “Model Law”), with some variations. The differences between the EAL and the UNCITRAL Model Law, as amended in 2006, consist in the following: the applicability of the EAL to both domestic and international arbitrations;1 Art. 1 of the EAL. the possible extraterritorial application of the EAL to proceedings seated abroad only if the parties have agreed to such extraterritorial application;2 Ibid. the requirement that an arbitration agreement in an administrative contract is approved by the competent minister or whoever assumes his or her authority with respect to public entities, and delegation in this regard is prohibited;3 Ibid. the EAL introduces several criteria for the establishment of the international nature of an arbitration including, amongst others, whether the arbitration is institutional, whether it involves parties whose principal places of business are in different states or alternatively if the place of the arbitration determined by the arbitration agreement, the place of performance of the obligations or the place with the closest connection to the dispute is abroad;4 Art. 3 of the EAL. the EAL does not expressly include the possibility to enter into an arbitration agreement by way of electronic means. However, it does not exclude it and therefore nothing prohibits the conclusion of arbitration agreements by electronic means and insofar as the electronic communication fulfils the requirement of writing, the arbitration agreement shall be valid. In brief, the writing requirement under the EAL is a condition for the validity of the arbitration agreement and is not simply a mere evidentiary requirement. According to the EAL, an agreement is in writing if it is contained in a document signed by the parties or contained in an exchange of letters, telegrams or other means of communication. Absence of an arbitration agreement in writing results in the nullity of the arbitration agreement and the writing requirement under the arbitration law is stricter than the one under the UNCITRAL Model Law;5 Art. 12 of the EAL. in the case of incorporation by reference, the reference to the arbitration agreement must be explicit in order for the arbitration agreement to form an integral part of the main contract;6 Art. 10 of the EAL. the EAL does not provide for the “referral exception” whereby a state court may accept to decide over jurisdiction if it finds that the arbitration agreement is null and void, inoperative or incapable of being performed.7 Art. 13 of the EAL. However, in practice, some Egyptian courts have considered the validity and operability of the arbitration agreement before an arbitral tribunal rendered its award; the EAL requires an odd number of arbitrators for purposes of constitution of the arbitral tribunal, the violation of which leads to the nullity of the award;8 Art. 15 of the EAL. the threshold used by the EAL for the challenge of arbitrators is relatively higher than the UNCITRAL Model Law counterpart; the doubts as to the arbitrator’s impartiality and independence must be serious;9 Art. 18 of the EAL. a preliminary arbitral award on jurisdiction cannot be the subject of a court review prior to the tribunal’s rendering of the final award deciding on the entire dispute which must be rendered for purposes of the competent court’s review or annulment;10 Art. 22 of the EAL. the arbitral tribunal may only issue orders for interim relief if the parties bestow this power upon it,11 Art. 24 of the EAL. however the EAL does not provide for a list of the types of relief available to arbitrators or any further details in relation thereto; if the parties do not agree on the language of the arbitration, the latter shall be conducted in Arabic;12 Art. 29 of the EAL. if the parties do not agree on the applicable law, the arbitral tribunal may apply the law having the closest connection to the dispute;13 Art. 39 of the EAL. the EAL adds a ground for annulment based on the non-application by the arbitral tribunal of the lex causae chosen by the parties;14 Art. 53 of the EAL. and the EAL introduces a further condition for purposes of exequatur (leave for enforcement) that is not listed in the UNCITRAL Model Law, namely: the award does not contradict a prior judgment rendered by the Egyptian courts on the merits of the dispute.15 Art. 58 of the EAL. As to the proposals for a reform of the EAL, there are ongoing discussions for reform and possible foreseeable amendments to the EAL. A committee in charge of discussing such possible amendments to the EAL was established by virtue of Decree No. 8 of 2022 of the Deputy Minister of Justice for Arbitration and International Disputes issued on 22 March 2022. The said committee comprises members of the Ministry of Justice Arbitration and International Disputes’ department in addition to named Egyptian arbitration practitioners. The scope of the amendments is limited, and the committee’s work is still ongoing. b. Mandatory provisions Many of the procedural rules governing the conduct of the proceedings are not mandatory and the parties may derogate from by agreement. However, certain rules qualify as mandatory and this includes the following: the arbitration agreement in an administrative contract must be approved by the competent minister or whoever assumes his/her authority with respect to public entities, and delegation in this regard is prohibited;16 Art. 1 of the EAL; see also State Council Challenge No. 8256 of JY 56, Hearing session dated 5 March 2016, where the State Council ruled that the arbitration agreement is void when the competent minister, or whoever assumes his/her authority with respect to public entities, has not approved it and that such requirement is a matter of public policy. the form of the arbitration agreement, which includes the writing requirement of the arbitration agreement,17 Art. 12 of the EAL. as well as when the arbitration agreement is concluded after the dispute has arisen, must indicate the issues subject to arbitration, otherwise the arbitration agreement is null and void;18 Art. 10(2) of the EAL; see also State Council Challenge No. 8256 of JY 56, Hearing session dated 5 March 2016, where the State Council ruled that in the case of a submission agreement, the parties must identify the dispute subjected to the arbitral proceedings or the agreement would be null and void. non-arbitrability of disputes that cannot be subject to a compromise;19 Art. 11 of the EAL. arbitral tribunals must be constituted from an odd number of arbitrators;20 Art. 15(2) of the EAL. the same arbitrator cannot be challenged by the same party more than once in the same arbitration proceedings;21 Art. 19(2) of the EAL. equality between the parties and rights of defence;22 Art. 26 of the EAL. witnesses and experts may not be heard under oath;23 Art. 33(4) of the EAL. arbitral awards may not be challenged by any means of recourse;24 Art. 52(1) of the EAL. and parties may not agree to exclude the right to apply for setting aside of an award prior to the rendering of the said award.25 Art. 54(1) of the EAL. 2. practice of arbitration a. General Arbitration is the prominent mechanism for the settlement of investment and commercial disputes in Egypt. With the growing number of investors in the country and the parties to commercial transactions ultimately resorting to arbitration, Egypt adopts, by the year, measures and reforms that aim at aligning Egypt with best practices in international arbitration. By enacting the EAL, Egypt took a colossal step towards supporting arbitration and becoming an arbitration-friendly jurisdiction. b. Arbitral institutions CRCICA The leading arbitral institution in Egypt is the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”), which is an independent non-profit international organization established in 1979 under the auspices of the Asian African Legal Consultative Organisation. CRCICA administers domestic and international arbitral proceedings as well as other forms of Alternative Dispute Resolution (“ADR”) such as mediation, conciliation, mini-trials and dispute boards. CRCICA promotes arbitration and other ADR techniques in the Afro-Asian region through organizing international conferences and seminars as well as training programs and workshops in cooperation with other international institutions, and provides the necessary technical and administrative assistance at the request of the parties to ad hoc arbitration. CRCICA also acts as an appointing authority and generally adopts the “list procedure” in making its appointments. CRCICA can be contacted at: 1 Al-Saleh Ayoub Street 11211 Zamalek, Cairo Egypt Telephone: +20 2 2735 1333/5/7 Facsimile: +20 2 2735 1336 E-mail: info@crcica.org Website: <https://crcica.org> CRCICA has its own set of arbitration, mediation and dispute board rules. Firstly, with regard to arbitration, since its establishment CRCICA has based its rules on the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules (1976) with minor modifications. The Arbitration Rules have been amended several times, namely in 1998, 2000, 2002, 2007, 2011, and most recently in 2024. The latest version of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules came into force on 15 January 2024 and is available in Arabic, English and French.26 The CRCICA Arbitration Rules are available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/arbitration/crcica-arbitration-rules/>. In this regard, it is worth noting that the CRCICA Arbitration Rules were only available in Arabic and English until 2017, when CRCICA published the French version of its 2011 Arbitration Rules, aiming to reach francophone users and encourage them to choose CRCICA as the arbitral institution administering their dispute in the French language. The new 2024 Arbitration Rules introduce new provisions addressing, inter alia, consolidation, multiparty and multiple contracts arbitrations, third-party funding, emergency arbitrator and expedited procedures. Secondly, as to mediation, CRCICA’s Mediation Rules were issued in March 1990 and were amended in 2001 and 2013. The latest version of the CRCICA Mediation Rules came into force on 1 January 2013, and are available in Arabic and English. Furthermore, it is worth noting that in August 2001, the Mediation and ADR Centre was inaugurated as a branch of CRCICA to administer mediation and other ADR such as conciliation, technical expertise, mini-trials and claim review boards.27 The CRCICA Mediation Rules are available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/mediation/crcica-mediation-rules/>. Thirdly, CRCICA adopted its first Dispute Board Rules on 1 August 2021, offering the international business and construction community a cost-effective and impartial dispute resolution method. These rules were drafted by experts in the field guided by existing institutional rules, and are available in Arabic, English and French.28 .The CRCICA Dispute Board Rules are available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/crcica-dispute-board-rules/>. According to the statistics published by CRCICA,29 See CRCICA Newsletter 2022 available at: <https://crcica.org/news/caseload-2022-number-of-cases-registered-remains-steady/>. the total number of cases filed with CRCICA as at 31 December 2022 was 1618 (domestic and international). In 2022, the total number of cases filed was eighty-three cases, among which were twelve ad hoc proceedings; five mediations referred to CRCICA; and three dispute board cases were registered, equal to the number of dispute board cases registered in 2021, following the issuance of the Dispute Board Rules on 1 August 2021. CRCICA’s caseload in 2022 involved disputes relating to various sectors including construction (17% of the total number of cases); tourism and hospitality (14.5% of the total number of cases); banking, finance and real estate development disputes (8.5% of the total number of cases); oil and gas (7% of the total number of cases); corporate restructuring (6% of the total number of cases); media and entertainment (6% of the total number of cases); sports (5% of the total number of cases); medical and hospital (5% of the total number of cases); and retail (5% of the total number of cases). Also, 2022 witnessed the appointment of 163 arbitrators (149 Egyptians and fourteen non-Egyptians, from France, UK, Lebanon, Tunisia, Belgium, Italy and South Africa), among which four were female arbitrators, representing 2.5% of appointments, compared to twelve female arbitrators appointed in 2021, representing 7% of appointments; and fifteen arbitrators were under the age of forty, representing 9% of appointments in 2022, which equals the same number and percentage of arbitrators under forty appointed in 2021. Among the eighty-three cases filed in 2022, twenty cases (representing 24.5%) were conducted in English whilst sixty-two cases were conducted in Arabic (representing 74.5%) and one case was conducted in French (representing 1%), which was registered in January 2022, constituting the first arbitration case registered in French since the issuance of the French version of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules in 2017. Throughout 2022, 129 hearings were held at CRCICA (eighty-nine related to CRCICA cases; thirty related to ad hoc proceedings; eight related to non-CRCICA proceedings, including ICC proceedings; and two related to mediation), among which fifteen hearings (representing 12%) were held remotely, three hearings (representing 2%) were held in a hybrid format (physical and remote attendance), and the remaining 111 hearings (representing 86%) were held with physical attendance of a limited number of persons in compliance with CRCICA’s social distancing guidelines adopted since the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020.30 Ibid. In 2021, following the COVID-19 pandemic and during the gradual shift to a normal pre-pandemic work environment, more hearings were held remotely and in a hybrid format than in 2022, where most hearings were held with physical attendance. Throughout 2021, 131 hearings were held at CRCICA, among which thirty-eight hearings were held entirely remotely, seventeen hearings were held in a hybrid format and the remaining thirty-eight hearings were held with physical attendance of a limited number of persons in compliance with CRCICA’s social distancing guidelines adopted in 2020.31 See CRCICA Annual Report 2021-2022 available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/Annual-Report-2021-2022.pdf>. In this context it is worth mentioning that in 2020, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, CRCICA had adopted some measures and adapted some of its services by offering guidance notes to arbitration users to ensure smooth administration of ongoing proceedings and the filing of new cases. CRCICA strongly recommended and encouraged arbitration users to file notices of arbitrations, written submissions and exhibits online via email, and also, to the extent possible, hold hearings online. In this respect, physical hearings at CRCICA’s premises were suspended during the period of closure of CRCICA’s premises (from 20 March to 30 May 2020). Furthermore, it is also worthy of note that CRCICA entered into an agreement with the International Court of Arbitration for Sport (“ICAS”) in 2012, nominating it as a host of an Alternative Hearing Centre (“AHC”) for the Court of Arbitration for Sport (“CAS”) based in Switzerland. In May 2017, CRCICA signed the Belt and Road Arbitration Initiative Cooperation Agreement with the Beijing Arbitration Commission/Beijing International Arbitration Centre (“BAC/BIAC”) and the Kuala Lumpur Regional Centre for Arbitration (“KLRCA”). In December 2017, CRCICA also signed a cooperation agreement with the Permanent Court of Arbitration (“PCA”) during the conference celebrating the 50th anniversary of UNCITRAL: Global Inclusion and Dispute Resolution: Harmonizing Trends in International Arbitration, which enables PCA hearings to take place at CRCICA’s premises and vice versa. In 2019, CRCICA signed three cooperation agreements, respectively with (1) the Nairobi Centre for International Arbitration (“NCIA”) in August 2019; (2) the Lagos Court of Arbitration (“LCA”) in September 2019; and (3) the Abu Dhabi Global Market (“ADGM”) on 30 September 2019. The cooperation agreements with NCIA and LCA involve, inter alia, promoting and developing arbitration techniques through joint capacity-building events, cooperation in the achievement of the goals of the African Arbitration Association (“AfAA”), and that NCIA and LCA shall be alternative hearing centres of CRCICA in Kenya and Nigeria and vice versa. Similarly, the ADGM Arbitration Centre (“ADGMAC”) shall be CRCICA’s alternative hearing centre for its arbitrations and mediations to be conducted in the United Arab Emirates. Furthermore, the ADGM can be chosen as the seat of arbitration for cases administered under the CRCICA Rules. More recently, on 29 May 2020, CRCICA concluded a Memorandum of Understanding (“MoU”) with the China Guangzhou Arbitration Commission (“GZAC”), an online arbitration institution founded in response to the rapid growth of electronic business in China, with focus on mutual cooperation to promote online arbitration. The scope of this MoU includes sharing information, organizing joint arbitration and mediation activities, and mutual recommendation of arbitrators. Other arbitral institutions In addition to CRCICA, there is a specialized arbitration centre within the Financial Regulatory Authority named the Egyptian Center for Arbitration and Settlement of Non-Banking Financial Disputes (“ECAS”),32 The official website for the Egyptian Center for Arbitration and Settlement of Non-Banking Financial Disputes is available at: <https://ecas.org.eg/en>. established by virtue of the Presidential Decree No. 335 of 2019. ECAS offers conciliation, mediation and arbitration services. The members of the Board of Trustees have been appointed by virtue of the Financial Regulatory Authority Decision No. 133 of 2019. The statutes of ECAS as well as its arbitration and mediation rules were issued by virtue of the Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2597 of 2020 on 10 December 2020. 3. Bibliography a. Books Aboul-Enien, Mohamed Peaceful Settlement of Commercial Disputes, Commercial Arbitration and other ADR Techniques (CRCICA 2005) (in English) Abou el Wafa, Ahmed Arbitration in Arab Laws (Maktabet Al Wafaa Al Qanouneya 2015) (in Arabic) Allam El Din, Mohi El Din CRCICA Awards, CRCICA (2002) (in Arabic) Al Qalyoubi, Samiha Legal Foundations for Commercial Arbitration in accordance with Law No. 27 of 1994 (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya, 2012) (in Arabic) Barakat, Ali Challenge of Arbitral Awards (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2003) (in Arabic) Barakat, Ali The Control of the Constitutionality of Arbitration Law Provisions (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2003) (in Arabic) Berreri, Mokhtar Commercial International Arbitration (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2004) (in Arabic) El Rifaie, Ashraf Abdel Alim The Law Applicable to the Subject Matter of the Dispute (Dar Al Fikr Al Game’ee 2003) (in Arabic) El Rifaie, Ashraf Abdel Alim The Arbitration Agreement (Dar Al Fikr Al Game’ee 2003) (in Arabic) El Sharkawy, Samir International Commercial Arbitration (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2011) (in Arabic) Hadad, Hamza Arbitration in Arab Laws (Dar Al Sakafa Lel Nashr wa Al Tawzī’ 2007) (in Arabic) Ibrahim, Ibrahim Ahmed Private International Arbitration, 4th ed. (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya) (in Arabic) Mabrouk, Ashour Legal Regime for the Enforcement of Arbitral Awards (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya, 2002) (in Arabic) Rachid, Samia Arbitration in Private International Relationships (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya, 1984) (in Arabic) Salama, Ahmed Abdel Karim Commercial International and Domestic Arbitration Law (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya, 2004) (in Arabic) Saleh, Samir Commercial Arbitration in the Arab Middle East: Shari'a, Lebanon, Syria and Egypt (Hart Publishing 2006) (in English) Shafik, Mohsen International Commercial Arbitration (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 1997) (in Arabic) Waly, Fathi Arbitration in Domestic and International Commercial Disputes in Theory and Practice (Munsha’at Al Ma’aref 2014) (in Arabic) b. Articles Abdel Wahab, Mohamed “Investment Arbitration: An Update on Egypt” in J. Fouret (ed.) Enforcement of Investment Treaty Arbitration Awards: A Global Guide (Globe Business Publishing 2021) “The Egyptian Court of Cassation Sets Standards and Affirms Arbitration-Friendly Principles and Trends in a Ground-Breaking Judgment”, Kluwer Arbitration Blog (22 December 2020) <http://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2020/12/22/the-egyptian-court-of-cassation-sets-standards-and-affirms-arbitration-friendly-principles-and-trends-in-a-ground-breaking-judgment/> (in English) “Supreme Court Strikes a Chord: Establishing Support to Credible Institutional Arbitration”, ICC Bulletin (2020, no. 1) pp. 12-16 (in English) “What if parties don’t agree on a virtual hearing? A pandemic pathway by Mohamed S. Abdel Wahab”, Arbitration News, Global Arbitration Review (6 May 2020) (in English) “‘The “Deemed’ Internationalisation of Arbitration under Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 – Considerations Beyond Hope and Fear”, 3 BCDR International Arbitration Review (2016, no. 1) pp. 47-64 (in English) “Extension of Arbitration Agreements to Third Parties 2.0: Deconstruction, Evolution and Reconsideration”, Chapter 2, Conflict of Laws in International Commercial Arbitration, 2019 (in English) “The Nuts and Bolts of Construction Arbitration in the MENA: an Update”, in Part IV – Regional Construction Arbitration, The Guide to Construction Arbitration, second edition (2018) pp. 292-313 (in English) Aboul-Enein, Mohamed “Reflections on the New Egyptian Law on Arbitration”, 11 Arbitration International (1995, no. 1) pp. 75-84 (in English) El Ahdab, Abdel Hamid “Enforcement of Arbitral Awards in the Arab countries”, 11 Arbitration International (1995, no. 2) pp. 169-182 (in English) “The New Egyptian Arbitration Act in Civil and Commercial Matters”, 12 Journal of International Arbitration (1995, no. 2) pp. 65-102 (in English) El Kosheri, Ahmed Sadek “Main Characteristics of the International Arbitration Case Law Rendered by Egyptian Courts”, 3 BCDR International Arbitration Review (2016, no. 1) pp. 5-22 (in English) El Sharkawy, Samir “Different Forms of Arbitration in Investment Disputes in the Arab World”, 8 Journal of Arab Arbitration (August 2005) pp.19-33 (in Arabic). Mahmoud, Ahmed Sedki “Conservatory Measures Necessary to Resolve Arbitration Proceedings”, 8 Journal of Arab Arbitration (August 2005) p. 64 (in Arabic). c. Journals and periodicals The Journal of Arab Arbitration. (in Arabic) The World Journal of Arbitration, El Ahdab ed. (in Arabic) International Journal of Arab Arbitration, El Ahdab ed. (in English) BCDR International Arbitration Review, N. Ziadé ed. (in English) d. Publication of awards Excerpts of arbitral awards may be published in the above-cited journals, and the CRCICA has published eight volumes of excerpts of its arbitral awards in Arabic and four volumes of its arbitral awards in English, while maintaining confidentiality of these awards.33 CRCICA official website, available at <https://crcica.org/crcica-arbitral-awards/>.

sections.title: CHAPTER II: ARBITRATION AGREEMENT
sections.text: CHAPTER II: ARBITRATION AGREEMENT 1. Form and Contents of the Agreement a. Arbitration clause and submission agreement The Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I hereto) defines the arbitration agreement as “an agreement by which the two parties agree to submit to arbitration in order to resolve all or certain disputes which have arisen or which may arise between them in connection with a defined legal relationship, whether contractual or not”.34 Art. 10(1) of the EAL. According to the EAL, an arbitration agreement may be concluded prior to the existence of the dispute or after it has arisen. In this respect, Art. 10(2) of the EAL specifically provides that arbitration clauses which are concluded prior to the existence of the dispute can be “either in the form of a separate agreement or as a clause in a given contract”, and its scope may extend to all or certain disputes which may arise between the parties, provided that the subject matter of the dispute must be determined in the statement of claim when submitted.35 Art. 10(2) of the EAL. Whereas submission agreements, i.e., arbitration agreements that are concluded after the dispute has arisen, may be concluded even after an action has already been brought before the judiciary, the arbitration agreement must indicate the issues subject to arbitration, otherwise such agreement would be null and void.36 Ibid. The mandatory requirement to identify the issues subject to arbitration in submission agreements has been confirmed through Egyptian court decisions. In this regard, the Cairo Court of Appeal has set aside an arbitral award on the ground that the arbitration agreement failed to specify the subject matter of the dispute to be settled by arbitration and was accordingly invalid.37 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 28 of JY 124, Hearing session dated 20 November 2007. In the same vein, in administrative contracts, the State Council confirmed that in the case of a submission agreement, the parties must identify the dispute subjected to the arbitral proceedings or the agreement would be null and void.38 State Council, Challenge No. 8256 of JY 56, Hearing session dated 5 March 2016. b. Form requirements Whether being an arbitration clause (in the form of a separate arbitration agreement or included in a given contract) or a submission agreement, the validity requirements of an arbitration agreement under the EAL are the following: the parties must have capacity to enter into the arbitration agreement39 Art. 11 of the EAL. and their consent to arbitrate must not tainted by any defects in consent, i.e., error, duress or misrepresentation; the subject matter of the arbitration must be arbitrable;40 Ibid. the subject of the dispute to be resolved by arbitration must be specified in the submission agreement, or in the statement of claim in case of a prior agreement to arbitrate;41 Art. 10(2) of the EAL. and the arbitration agreement must be in writing or else it is null. The writing requirement includes a document signed by the parties, an agreement by exchange of correspondences (letters and telegrams) or other means of communication,42 Art. 12 of the EAL. and/or when the parties subject their legal relationship to the provisions of a model contract, an international convention or another document containing provisions related to arbitration,43 Art. 6 of the EAL. and/or an incorporation into the contract by reference to a document containing an arbitration agreement insofar as the reference is explicit in considering the arbitration agreement part of the parties’ contract.44 Art. 10(3) of the EAL. However, it is worth noting that Art. 750(4) of the Egyptian Civil Code expressly provides that the arbitration agreement included in the general conditions of an insurance policy is null and void. Furthermore, as to the writing requirement, it is worth noting that the EAL does not expressly include the possibility to enter into an arbitration agreement by way of electronic means. However, it does not exclude it and therefore nothing prohibits the conclusion of arbitration agreements by electronic means and insofar as the electronic communication fulfils the requirement of writing, the arbitration agreement shall be valid. c. Model arbitration clause The Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”) proposes the following three model arbitration clauses:45 The CRCICA model arbitration clauses are available at: <https://crcica.org/arbitration/model-clauses/>. CRCICA Model Arbitration Clause for Future Disputes “For contracting parties who wish to have future disputes referred to arbitration under the CRCICA Arbitration Rules, the following clause is recommended: Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this contract, including but not limited to its interpretation, execution, the termination or invalidity thereof, shall be settled by arbitration in accordance with the Arbitration Rules of the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration. Note: Parties should consider adding: a. The number of arbitrators shall be …(1) b. The place of arbitration shall be … (city and country); and c. The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be… Note: Parties may consider adding: – The time limit within which the arbitral tribunal shall make its final award shall be… ; – The governing law of the arbitration agreement; and – The governing law of the contract [is/shall be] the substantive law of (country or state).” (1) State an odd number, such as one or three. CRCICA Model Arbitration Agreement for Existing Disputes “If a dispute has arisen, but there is no agreement between the parties to arbitrate, they may find an agreement to resolve their dispute by arbitration as follows: “The undersigned parties hereby agree to submit their dispute arising out of [name/date of the Contract, if any] and relating to [the description of the disputed issues], to final determination by arbitration in accordance with the Arbitration Rules of the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration. Note: Parties should consider adding: a. The number of arbitrators shall be …(2) b. The place of arbitration shall be [city and country]; and c. The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be … Note: Parties may consider adding: – The time limit within which the arbitral tribunal shall make its final award shall be… ; – The governing law of the arbitration agreement; and – The governing law of the contract [is/shall be] the substantive law of … ” (2) State an odd number, such as one or three. CRCICA Model Arbitration Clause under CRCICA Expedited Arbitration Rules “Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this contract, including but not limited to interpretation, execution, termination or invalidity thereof, shall be settled by arbitration in accordance with the CRCICA Expedited Arbitration Rules. Note: Parties should consider adding: a. The place of arbitration shall be [city and country]; and b. The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be … Note: Parties may consider adding: – The governing law of the arbitration agreement; and – The governing law of the contract [is/shall be] the substantive law of …” CRCICA also proposes the following multi-tiered process model clause:46 The CRCICA model multi-tiered process arbitration clause is available at: <https://crcica.org/mediation/model-clauses/>. “If any dispute arises in connection with this agreement, directors or other senior representatives of the parties with authority to settle the dispute will, within [___] days of a written request from one party to the other, meet in a good faith effort to resolve the dispute. If the dispute is not resolved at that meeting, it shall be settled by mediation in accordance with the Rules of Mediation of the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (CRCICA). Unless otherwise agreed between the parties, the mediator will be nominated by CRCICA. No party may commence any court proceedings or arbitration in relation to any dispute arising out of this agreement until the dispute has been referred to mediation and either the mediation has terminated or the other party has failed to participate in the mediation, provided that the right to issue court proceedings or arbitration is not prejudiced by a delay. In case of failure of the mediation process, the parties agree to refer the dispute to arbitration in accordance with the Arbitration Rules of the CRCICA. Parties may consider adding precise time limits for the negotiation and mediation phases.” We also suggest the following arbitration clause: “The parties hereby irrevocably agree that all disputes arising out of or in connection with the present contract shall be finally settled by arbitration [under the Rules of Arbitration of an Arbitral Institution or pursuant to the provisions of the EAL] by [one or more arbitrators] appointed in accordance with the said [Rules or Law]. The seat of the arbitration shall be [city and country] and the language of the arbitration shall be [Arabic or English or other]. The Arbitral Tribunal has the power to grant precautionary, interim, provisional and/or conservative measures whether in the form of orders or awards and the parties undertake to comply with such orders or awards.” 2. Parties to the Agreement a. Generally/capacity An arbitration agreement may only be concluded by a physical person or legal entity with the capacity to enter into the arbitration agreement.47 Art. 11 of the EAL. It must also satisfy the standard contractual requirements such as consent, capacity and the existence of a legal relationship. In this respect, the Egyptian Civil Code (“ECC”) specifies that the capacity of a physical person is governed by the law of the state of his/her nationality, whilst the capacity of a legal entity is governed by the law of the state where its principal seat of management is located or by Egyptian law if the entity conducts its main activity in Egypt.48 Art. 11 of the ECC. According to the ECC and the Egyptian Code of Civil and Commercial Procedures (“CCCP”) a party may only conclude an arbitration agreement pursuant to a specific power of attorney, which must satisfy the same form requirements of the legal act subject to the power of attorney, hence it must be in writing.49 Art. 700 of the ECC and Art. 76 of the CCCP. The power of attorney must be notarized and attested before the Notary Public in Arabic language only, even if the principal is a non-Egyptian and/or the power of attorney is to be used in Egypt and abroad. In the latter case, the power of attorney shall be translated by a certified translator before the Egyptian Ministry of Justice and then ratified at the Egyptian Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Egyptian Consulate located at the foreign state of the principal and/or the place of use of the power of attorney. As to minors and persons placed under guardianship,50 Arts. 46 and 47 of the ECC. the guardian is empowered to conclude an arbitration agreement on their behalf subject to leave from the court.51 Art. 39 of the Law No. 119 of 1952 on Guardianship of Property. b. Bankruptcy Upon declaration of bankruptcy, the bankrupt party itself, whether being a physical person or a legal entity, may no longer conclude an arbitration agreement relating to its property or enforce an existing arbitration agreement. However, subject to leave from the bankruptcy court, the trustee may conclude such agreement. In this respect it is worth noting that the rules of insolvency are of public policy nature and that the insolvency/bankruptcy court retains jurisdiction to decide all disputes arising out of the insolvency and shall supervise and scrutinize all aspects thereof.52 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 3125 of JY 58, Hearing session dated 6 May 1996. Thus, the Law regulating Restructuring, Preventive Reconciliation, and Bankruptcy No. 11 of 2018 provides that upon declaration of bankruptcy and the appointment of a trustee, the bankruptcy court may, upon consultation of the trustee and upon hearing the insolvent party, allow enforceability of the arbitration agreement. However, if the amount in dispute is not determined or if its value exceeds EGP 20,000 (approximately US$ 665),53 Based on the exchange rate in February 2023 declared by the Central Bank of Egypt, provided that such rate is fluctuating due to the free float rate of US$. arbitration cannot take place, unless validated by the bankruptcy judge.54 Art. 162 of the Insolvency Law No. 11 of 2018. c. State or state agencies The State, as well as state agencies may resort to arbitration and conclude arbitration agreements. In this regard, the EAL requires that arbitration agreements relating to administrative contracts must be approved by the competent minister, or whoever assumes his/her authority with respect to public entities, and expressly prohibits delegation of this power.55 Art. 1 of the EAL. This has been confirmed by a judgment of the State Council ruling that the arbitration agreement is void when the competent minister, or whoever assumes his or her authority with respect to public entities, has not approved it and that such requirement is a matter of public policy.56 State Council, Challenge No. 8256 of JY 56, Hearing session dated 5 March 2016. The aforementioned judgment upholds the previous position taken by Egyptian courts, which consistently held that the failure to satisfy the requirement of signature of the arbitration agreement by the competent minister, or whoever assumes his/her authority with respect to public entities, results in the nullity of the arbitration agreement.57 Cairo Administrative Court, Case No. 18628 of JY 59, Minister of Civil Aviation v. Malicorp Limited, Hearing session dated 19 February 2006; and Cairo Administrative Court dated 31 October 2009 commented upon in M. Amin El Mahdi and M. Fawzi Abdel Bary, “Actions for the Nullity of Arbitration Agreements in Administrative Contracts”, 14 Journal of Arab Arbitration (2010) p. 99. Furthermore, in 2019 a new committee was established within the Council of Ministers called the “High Committee for Studying and Opining on International Arbitration Disputes”, which was renamed to the “High Committee for Arbitration and International Disputes”.58 Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019 and Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 (amending Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019). This Committee’s role is to examine and opine on all arbitration disputes, whether commercial or investment arbitrations, to which the State or any ministry, or public state entities, or governmental bodies and their affiliated entities, or an affiliated company to the State or whereby the State is a shareholder to said company, is party.59 Art. 2 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 (amending Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019). The decisions of this Committee are binding upon the concerned state entities, once approved by the Council of Ministers.60 Art. 1 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019. Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019 was amended twice. In December 2020, the first amendment was introduced by virtue of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020, which, among other things, instructs state entities, which include any administrative body, ministry, public entities, governmental bodies and their affiliated bodies, companies affiliated to the State or to which the State is a shareholder (without any minimum shareholding required), not to conclude or sign any contract with a foreign investor which includes an arbitration agreement, or to make any amendment to such contract, or to even take any measure related to any arbitral dispute, without having this first reviewed by the ministerial committee in charge of examining all arbitration disputes involving any state entity, i.e., the High Committee for Arbitration and International Disputes.61 Art. 6 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 (amending Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019). Later in September 2022, a second single amendment was introduced by virtue of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022, instructing the State, its organs, public entities, state-owned companies and companies in which the State participates in any form (i.e., no minimum shareholding threshold is required), not to take any measure that would lead to the rescission or the termination of any contract including an arbitration agreement, without having this first reviewed by the High Committee for Arbitration and International Disputes. 62 Art. 1 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022 (amending Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019). By introducing these new requirements, the approval of the competent minister, or whoever assumes his or her authority with respect to public entities, will be subject to a prior review, i.e., the review of the High Committee for Arbitration and International Disputes, otherwise the arbitration agreement would risk to be void. These novel requirements are yet to be tested in future investment contracts containing an arbitration agreement. d. Multiparty arbitration There are no specific rules regarding multiparty arbitration, joinder of third parties and consolidation of arbitrations under the EAL. However, the Egyptian courts and the arbitral tribunals applying Egyptian law have clarified and distilled the principles governing extension of arbitration agreements and joinder of third parties. These include group of companies, group of contracts, assignment, universal succession, mergers, implied consent, etc. In brief, Egyptian court decisions have generally deferred to arbitral tribunals’ determinations on joinder requests and upheld same, unless there were no arbitration agreement in writing or principles of Egyptian public policy militated against upholding the arbitral tribunals’ determinations. Group of companies The Court of Cassation has decisively ruled that an arbitration agreement included in a contract does not automatically extend to a company that forms part of a larger group of companies entering into the said contract. The company must have actively contributed in the performance of the contract or there must have been a confusion between the intents of the two relevant companies.63 Court of Cassation, Challenge Nos. 4729 and 4730 of JY 72, Hearing session dated 22 June 2004. In other words, the doctrine of group of companies is accepted by the courts for purposes of extension of the arbitration agreement in the presence of an implication in the performance process of the contract.64 Court of Cassation, Challenges Nos. 2698, 3100 and 3299 of JY 86, Hearing session dated 13 March 2018, whereby the Court of Cassation held that an arbitration agreement may extend to third parties on the basis of several doctrines which include the doctrine of group of companies. Moreover, in CRCICA proceedings, arbitral tribunals have consistently found, on the same grounds, that a parent company was bound by the arbitration agreement concluded by its subsidiary.65 CRCICA Case No. 109/1998, award dated 11 March 1999, 2 Journal of Arab Arbitration (January 2000) p. 224 (in Arabic); CRCICA Case No. 631/2009, award dated 5 May 2010, 15 Journal of Arab Arbitration (December 2010) p. 226 (in Arabic); CRCICA Case No. 652/2009, award dated 14 June 2010, 16 Journal of Arab Arbitration (June 2011) p. 201 (in Arabic). The doctrine of economic unity The doctrine of economic unity is not sufficient, in and of itself, for purposes of extension of the arbitration agreement if the third party has not exhibited consent to arbitration.66 Cairo Court of Appeal, Commercial Circuit No. 62, Challenge No. 83 of JY 118, Hearing session dated 5 August 2002. However, Egyptian courts have shown flexibility regarding extension to third parties and would normally defer to the tribunal’s reasoning in this respect, unless a clear principle of public policy is compromised. Group of contracts It is well established by Egyptian court decisions that an arbitration clause included in a contract may extend to other contracts arising out of the principal contract encompassing the arbitration clause and connected or concluded in relation thereto.67 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 1640 of JY 54, Hearing session dated 14 February 1988; and Cairo Court of Appeal, Commercial Circuit No. 91, Challenge No. 37 of JY 122, Hearing session dated 29 March 2006. For example, an arbitration clause extends to memoranda of understanding concluded in relation to the contract containing such arbitration clause.68 Cairo Court of Appeal, Commercial Circuit No. 91, Challenge No. 37 of JY 122, Hearing session dated 29 March 2006. The doctrine of group of contracts is expressly recognized by the Court of Cassation, which held that an arbitration agreement may extend to third parties on the basis of a non-exhaustive list of several doctrines, among which figures the doctrine of group of contracts.69 Court of Cassation, Challenges Nos. 2698, 3100 and 3299 of JY 86, Hearing session dated 13 March 2018. Assignment When the rights and obligations under a contract containing an arbitration agreement are validly assigned, the transfer of the arbitration agreement is effective and binds the assignee.70 Cairo Court of Appeal, Commercial Circuit No. 7, Challenges Nos. 1294 and 2037 of JY 117, Hearing session dated 9 April 2002, whereby the Court of Appeal held that the arbitration agreement is ancillary to the assigned rights, and is thus binding on the assignee. This decision confirms the ruling of the Court of Cassation, prior to the enactment of the EAL, whereby the Court of Cassation ruled that arbitration agreements may, in principle, be assigned with the contract containing the arbitration clause, such that the Court refused to bind the assignee by the arbitration agreement, owing to the invalidity of the assignment. (Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 289 of JY 30, Hearing session dated 11 January 1966. The Court of Cassation confirmed in a more recent decision the possibility of extension of the arbitration clause on the basis of assignment when its conditions are met.71 Court of Cassation, Challenges Nos. 2698, 3100 and 3299 of JY 86, Hearing session dated 13 March 2018. Joinder and intervention of third parties The EAL is silent regarding the joinder, intervention of third parties and consolidation of arbitral proceedings.
