In this respect, extension of arbitration agreements to third parties may be exceptionally permitted if it can be demonstrated that there is express or implied consent by such third party, or if the factual and legal matrix of the proceedings warrant such extension. However, Egyptian courts, while acknowledging that consent remains a cornerstone of the arbitral process and that extension to third parties is exceptional, are yet to develop a clear trend on the applicability/ inapplicability of certain doctrines of extension. For example, in a decision rendered on 13 February 2014, the Egyptian Court of Cassation had the opportunity to discuss the procedural and formal aspects of joining a third party to the arbitration proceedings and explained as follows:72 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 7595 of JY 81, Hearing session dated 13 February 2014. “Although the arbitration dispute is generally limited to the parties to the arbitration agreement, however either party may request the joining of a person not a party to the arbitration agreement but to which the arbitration agreement may be extended, provided that the request shall be submitted by one of the parties in the form of a joining request or intervention request.” In the same vein, in 2018 the Egyptian Court of Cassation upheld the principle that an arbitration agreement cannot exist without consent of the parties, but added that an arbitration agreement may extend to third parties and to other contracts connected to the principal contract on the basis of several doctrines and principles including: group of companies, group of contracts, universal succession, mergers or assignment, if their conditions are met.73 Court of Cassation, Challenges Nos. 2698, 3100 and 3299 of JY 86, Hearing session dated 13 March 2018. As to consolidation of separate arbitral proceedings under one or more contracts, this is possible, but conditional upon the parties’ agreement, whether directly, or indirectly through the choice of institutional rules that permit consolidation of proceedings. Accordingly, it is possible that applicable institutional rules may include pertinent provisions in relation to multiparty arbitration, joinder of third parties or consolidation of multiple arbitrations. Absent such regulation under institutional rules, if any are applicable, it is preferable that a multiparty arbitration agreement explicitly states whether several parties shall jointly appoint one or more arbitrators. In this regard, the arbitration clause must be clearly drafted in order to determine the role of the parties in the choice of arbitrators. In this regard, the new CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024) introduce provisions in relation to consolidation,74 Art. 50 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). multiparty arbitration,75 Art. 11 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). multiple contracts,76 Art. 51 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). as well as emergency arbitrator rules77 Annex 2 Emergency Arbitrator Rules of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). and expedited procedure rules,78 Annex 3 Expedited Arbitration rules of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). which were not present in the previous version of its Arbitration Rules (2011). In the previous version of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2011), only the joinder of third parties to arbitration proceedings, including the conditions upon which such joinder can be made, was addressed.79 Art. 17(6) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2011). 3. Domain of Arbitration a. Arbitrability The EAL provides that any matter that cannot be settled by compromise is not arbitrable.80 Art. 11 of the EAL. In this regard, matters that cannot be settled by compromise principally pertain to matters of personal or family status,81 Art. 551 of the ECC. public policy,82 Ibid. criminal matters, or rights in rem relating to immovables such as registration of real estate mortgages. Otherwise, the EAL requires that the right subject to arbitration be of an economic nature.83 Art. 2 of the EAL. b. Filling gaps Arbitrators may fill gaps in contracts only when the parties have expressly authorized them to do so. c. Adapting contracts The issue of adapting contracts is subject to the applicable law to the substance of the dispute. In this respect, the EAL recognizes the principle of party autonomy where the parties are free to determine the law applicable to the substance of their dispute, subject to exceptional legislative constraints (as in technology transfer contracts and remuneration of Egyptian commercial agents, where application of Egyptian law is mandatory). This is confirmed by Art. 39(1) of the EAL which provides that the arbitral tribunal shall apply to the substance of the dispute the rules chosen by the parties. Accordingly, when the substantive applicable law to the dispute is Egyptian law, the arbitrators may adapt a contract to the extent permitted under Art. 147(2) of the ECC. This provision applies when unforeseen circumstances render the performance of the contract materially onerous.84 Art. 147 of the ECC reads: “(1) The Contract is the law of the parties. A party cannot unilaterally revoke the contract or amend its provisions except within the limits permitted by the agreement or unless dictated otherwise by the law. (2) However, if unforeseen exceptional circumstances of a general character occurred and rendered the performance of a contractual obligation, though not impossible, excessively onerous to the debtor and threatening same with exorbitant loss, the Judge may, after considering the circumstances and weighing the interests of both parties, restore to reasonable limits the obligation that has become excessively onerous. Any agreement to the contrary shall be void.” 4. Separability of Arbitration Clause According to the EAL, the arbitration clause is considered separable from the main contract and is not affected by the latter’s invalidity, termination and/or rescission insofar as the arbitration agreement itself is valid.85 Art. 23 of the EAL. The principle of separability of the arbitration clause from the main contract has been confirmed by Egyptian courts and is considered as one of the fundamental pillars of arbitration in Egypt.86 Court of Cassation, Challenges Nos. 824 and 933 of JY 71, Hearing session dated 24 May 2007. The arbitration agreement is subject to the same legal requirements as a contract, i.e., consent, capacity and existence of a legal relationship. The arbitration agreement in itself is a distinct agreement from the main contract, as each has its own subject matter and cause.87 Ahmed Abdel Karim Salama, Commercial International and Domestic Arbitration Law (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2004) p. 471; and Fathi Waly, Arbitration in Domestic and International Commercial Disputes in Theory and Practice (Munsha’at Al Ma’aref 2014) p. 105. The subject matter of the arbitration agreement is the settlement of the dispute between the parties through arbitration, which is a procedural matter.88 Id. at Ahmed Abdel Karim Salama, p. 471. Whilst the subject matter of the main contract consists in defining the rights and obligations of the parties of the contract, which is a substantive matter.89 Ibid. In the same vein, the cause for concluding the arbitration agreement is to grant jurisdiction to the arbitral tribunal to decide over the dispute that may arise between the parties instead of being referred to and decided upon by national courts, conversely to the cause of the main contract which consists in the parties’ intention for contracting.90 Ibid. Therefore, being separable from the main contract, the arbitration agreement remains valid so long as it fulfils the above-mentioned contractual requirements and cannot be affected by the validity or existence of the main contract, even if the latter is alleged to have been never concluded or an essential element is missing therein. It is also worth mentioning that the arbitration agreement may be governed by a law that is different from the law governing the main contract.91 Ahmed Abdel Karim Salama, op. cit., p. 473; and Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 108. 5. Effect of the Agreement a. Duty of court Egyptian courts are under a legal obligation to dismiss litigation with respect to disputes subject to an arbitration agreement if the defendant, at the commencement of the proceedings, advances a plea pertaining to the existence of an arbitration agreement (Art. 13(1) of the EAL). The relevant provision in the EAL, i.e., Art. 13(1) thereof, was argued as unconstitutional before the Supreme Constitutional Court because it violates the citizens’ right of access to justice. However, the Supreme Constitutional Court confirmed the validity of Art. 13(1) of the EAL, holding that it merely gives effect to the parties’ agreement to resort to arbitration.92 Supreme Constitutional Court, Case No. 155 of Constitutional Year 20, Hearing session dated 13 January 2002. In this respect, it is worth noting that Art. 13(1) of the EAL, which partially reproduces Art. 8 of the UNCITRAL Model Law, has excluded the “referral exception” whereby the state court may accept to decide on jurisdiction if it finds that the arbitration agreement is null and void, inoperative or incapable of being performed. This implies that the arbitral tribunal enjoys priority to decide on its competence over state courts. However, the court is not under an obligation to reject the case ex officio for the mere existence of an arbitration agreement; the defendant must raise its objection at the commencement of the proceedings. This is principally due to the fact that an arbitration agreement does not form part of public policy. In the absence of a plea by the defendant in litigation, parallel proceedings will be conducted before the arbitral tribunal, and the court and decisions will be rendered irrespective of the parties’ prior agreement to arbitrate. In the event that the two decisions are contradictory, the successful party in the arbitration may elevate the conflict to the Supreme Constitutional Court in accordance with the law.93 Art. 25 of the Supreme Constitutional Court Law No. 48 of 1979. As to foreign arbitral awards where parties have agreed to arbitrate abroad, but one party makes an application to have the matter heard by an Egyptian national court, and assuming that Art. II(3) of the 1958 New York Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (the “New York Convention”) (which is similar to Art. 8 of the UNCITRAL Model law including the “referral exception”) does not apply, the Egyptian court shall refer the parties to arbitration abroad, if the other party (the defendant) invokes the arbitration agreement, if the parties agreed to apply the provisions of the EAL and in application of Art. 13(1) thereof in specific. In this regard, it is worth noting that the EAL provides for the supremacy of international conventions concluded by Egypt to be applicable to arbitral proceedings. However, if such international convention is not applicable and when an arbitration is conducted abroad, the provisions of the EAL apply thereto, provided that the parties to said arbitration have agreed to submit same to the provisions of the EAL.94 Art. 1 of the EAL. Hence, for Egyptian courts to declare the action inadmissible, due to the existence of an arbitration agreement, timing is crucial, such that the defendant shall raise such objection before submitting any defence in the action. b. Court examination of arbitration agreement The court examination of the arbitration agreement to stay proceedings is conditioned on the defendant’s objection, such that the court is not under an obligation to reject the case ex officio for the mere existence of an arbitration agreement. Generally, the courts adopt a prima facie review of the existence and scope of the arbitration agreement.95 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 224. Furthermore, parallel proceedings can take place, such that a judicial action does not prevent arbitral proceedings from being commenced or continued.96 Art. 13(2) of the EAL. The lack of competence of the national court shall be invoked by the defendant before submitting any defence in the action, i.e., prior to the commencement of the proceedings and submitting any defences on the substance of the dispute itself.97 Art. 13(1) of the EAL. In this respect, such objection is of a procedural nature, such that a party requesting the court to stay the proceedings and declaring same inadmissible due to the existence of an arbitration agreement, cannot be invoked at any time,98 Art. 115(1) of the CCCP permits invoking inadmissibility of an action at any time, however, this concerns substantive matters only, not procedural matters. as it does not tackle a substantive issue in the dispute.99 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 223. In a recent decision, the Court of Cassation held that the arbitration agreement is a contract with all the conditions and pillars of contracts at large, which must be respected by the parties to it, such that in its presence each party has an obligation to refrain from filing an action before the judiciary on any dispute subject to the contract including the arbitration agreement, and the court before which the action is filed shall declare the case inadmissible.100 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 3449 of JY 78, Hearing session dated 11 February 2020. c. Kompetenz-kompetenz The principle of kompetenz-kompetenz is generally recognized in Egypt.101 Cairo Court of appeal, 7th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 72 of JY 117, Hearing session dated 8 January 2002, whereby the Court upheld the principle of competence-competence, such that a plea was invoked during the course of this proceeding to declare Art. 22(1) of the EAL unconstitutional as it contradicts Arts. 165 and 166 of the Egyptian Constitution which relate to the independence of the judiciary, and Art. 68 of the Constitution which grants the citizens the right to resort to the national judge. Such plea was based on the fact that when the arbitral tribunal decides over its competence, the arbitrators lack their independence because they have a personal interest when deciding over their competence, which lies in ensuring they receive their fees in full. In this respect, the Court was of the view that the arbitral tribunal’s independence and impartiality is not attained when deciding over its competence. The Court added, that such power to decide over its competence is warranted by the annulment of the arbitral award if the arbitral tribunal decides over a matter that exceeds its competence. Furthermore, the Court stated that it is exceptional to have an arbitrator who is only interested and focused on receiving his/her fees, such that the principle of competence-competence prevents invoking the issue of competence before national courts, which in turn would prolong the time for settling the dispute and which would be contrary to the parties’ choice in resorting to arbitration for resolving their dispute in a timely manner without delay. The EAL provides that the arbitral tribunal shall decide over any jurisdiction-related claims, including the existence, validity and scope of the arbitration agreement.102 Art. 22(1) of the EAL. However, in practice, there are instances where Egyptian courts, in relation to administrative contracts and beyond, have decided on the existence and validity of an arbitration agreement prior to or while arbitral proceedings were still pending and irrespective of the arbitral tribunal’s jurisdiction.103 State Council, Challenge No. 8256 of JY 56, Hearing session dated 5 March 2016, whereby the State Council ruled that the arbitration agreement is void.

sections.title: CHAPTER III ARBITRATORS
sections.text: CHAPTER III ARBITRATORS 1. qualifications a. Requirements An arbitrator may be any person having legal capacity. A minor, any person under curatorship, guardianship, bankruptcy or who has been deprived from his/her civil rights by being convicted of a crime (felony or misdemeanour) of dishonesty, cannot act as an arbitrator, until he/she has been restored to the full exercise of his/her rights (Art. 16(1) of the Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I hereto)). There are no other specific legal or technical qualifications required. b. Restrictions According to the EAL, there are no restrictions on the choice of arbitrators, as to their gender, nationality or age (provided that he/she has attained the age of majority), unless otherwise agreed by the parties or provided by the law.104 Art. 16(2) of the EAL. The EAL warrants the parties the right to agree on specific requirements for the appointment of their arbitrators. Such specific requirements will be taken into consideration by the court who appoints an arbitrator on behalf of a reluctant party.105 Art. 17(3) of the EAL. In this respect, it is worth noting that diversity initiatives have been actively promoted in Egypt, and that the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”) signed the Pledge for Equal Representation in Arbitration in 2017, which resulted in an increase in the number of female arbitrators appointed by the Centre (eleven female arbitrators in 2018), as well as the number of arbitrators under the age of forty (thirteen arbitrators under the age of forty).106 Ismail Selim, “Interviews with our Editors: Cairo in the Spotlight with Dr Ismail Selim, Director at CRCICA”, Kluwer Arbitration Blog, 17 July 2019, available online at <http://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2019/07/17/interviews-with-our-editors-cairo-‌in-the-spotlight-with-dr-ismail-selim-director-at-crcica/>. Judges or members of the judiciary may sit as arbitrators, but they are required to obtain an administrative permission from the Supreme Judicial Council to sit as arbitrators in a specific case.107 Art. 63 of the Judicial Authority Law No. 46 of 1972. Furthermore, when a judge is appointed by the government or a public entity in a dispute to which they are party, his or her remuneration qua arbitrator is determined by the Supreme Judicial Council.108 Ibid. In this regard, in a recent judgment of the Egyptian Court of Cassation, the Court ruled that the absence of the Supreme Judicial Council authorization for a sitting judge to sit as an arbitrator in a specific case – despite being in breach of the Judicial Authority Law – does not affect the validity of the arbitral award.109 .Court of Cassation, Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 9968 of JY 81, Hearing session dated 9 January 2018. c. Disclosure The arbitrator shall accept his/her appointment in writing and shall disclose any circumstances that are likely to give rise to justifiable doubts as to his/her impartiality and independence (Art. 16(3) of the EAL). According to this provision, the arbitrator shall not only disclose the circumstances influencing his/her impartiality or independence, and which may lead to his/her disqualification as an arbitrator, but also the circumstances which may give an in abstracto rise to doubts as to the impartiality or independence of a reasonable person.110 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 286. Therefore, the arbitrator shall disclose any direct relationship with any of the parties to the dispute, their representatives, employees, relatives or friends, regardless of whether this relationship is physical, professional or social or whether it is a past or current relationship.111 Ibid. This does not signify that the arbitrator has the obligation to disclose everything that is related to this relationship. The arbitrator is only obliged to disclose the facts and circumstances which, according to a reasonable person, give rise to doubts as to the arbitrator’s impartiality or independence.112 Ibid. In this regard, it is worth noting that the Cairo Court of Appeal held that the arbitrator’s disclosure duty remains throughout the course of the arbitration proceedings. The Court expressly stated the following:113 Cairo Court of Appeal, 8th Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 75 of JY 125, Hearing session dated 18 May 2009. “This obligation [of disclosure] upon the arbitrator stands and continues during the course of the arbitration and till its adjudication, whenever circumstances appear to give rise to doubts as to [the arbitrator’s] independence and impartiality. [The arbitrator] shall disclose to the parties such circumstances, so that they are informed of it and hence, may challenge the arbitrator by the challenging procedure or waive such [right to challenge] and accept the arbitrator’s conduct of his mission without any objection from their part.” [Bracketed words added] Furthermore, in 2021, the Cairo Court of Appeal held that there was no breach of the duty of disclosure when an arbitrator failed to disclose (1) membership of the Board of Trustees of the CRCICA and of the Advisory Committee of the CRCICA (an independent international non-profit organization) when the respondent’s counsel is also a member of the same Advisory Committee; and that (2) additionally, two arbitrators (the Chairman and a co-arbitrator) were speakers on the same panel with the respondent’s counsel in an event held by the CRCICA, where the law firm of the respondent’s counsel was a golden sponsor to such event. The Court reasoned that the aforementioned circumstances do not constitute a “real danger of bias” or create “justifiable doubts” as to the independence or impartiality of the arbitrators, due to the absence of a connection of dependency, or a financial or psychological relation between the arbitrators and any of the parties.114 Cairo Court of Appeal, 3rd Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 42 of JY 136, Hearing session dated 8 March 2021. In 2022, for the first time ever, the Court of Cassation expressly referred to the IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration (2014), quoting Clause 3.3.5 of the Orange list of the IBA Guidelines, on the basis that these Guidelines serve as guidance in determining the duty of disclosure and its impact on the independence and impartiality of an arbitrator.115 .Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 13892 of JY 81, Hearing session dated 22 February 2022. The Court explained that the duty of disclosure of an arbitrator is a binding legal obligation that is necessary to warrant the integrity and impartiality in the conduct of the arbitration process. The Court added that the non-disclosure in itself does not lead to the setting aside of an award. However, to set aside an award, the Court shall assess on a case-by-case basis whether the undisclosed circumstance justifies in itself or leads in a reasonable manner to infer a real danger of bias. The Court concluded that the supervision exercised by the Egyptian judiciary with respect to the arbitrators’ independence and impartiality boosts the confidence of the parties to international arbitrations in choosing Egypt as a seat of arbitration. 2. Appointment of Arbitrators Arbitrators shall be appointed in the manner agreed upon by the parties. In this respect, the EAL allows the parties to choose the procedural rules governing their dispute, which may be a third party, i.e., an arbitral institution in Egypt or abroad.116 Art. 5 of the EAL. Therefore, a distinction between ad hoc and institutional arbitration is necessary in this context. In case of institutional arbitration, the applicable institutional rules shall apply to all procedural matters including the appointment of arbitrators. However, in case of ad hoc arbitration that is not subject to specific agreed arrangements, the EAL provisions shall apply. In this respect, absent an agreement between the parties on the method of appointment of the arbitrators, Art. 17 of the EAL provides therefor and differentiates between the appointment of a sole arbitrator and a panel of arbitrators (three or more). Firstly, a sole arbitrator is appointed by the competent court,117 Art. 9 of the EAL provides that the competent court is the Cairo Court of Appeal for international commercial arbitration, whether conducted in Egypt or abroad, otherwise it would be the court having original jurisdiction over the dispute. upon the request of either party.118 Art. 17(1)(a) of the EAL. Secondly, if the arbitral tribunal consists of three or more arbitrators, each party shall appoint its arbitrator within thirty days from the date of the request from the other party to appoint the arbitrator; then the two or more arbitrators appointed by the parties shall appoint the third, fifth or remaining arbitrator, within thirty days from the date of the latest appointment between the selected arbitrators (two or four or more) to become the chairperson of the arbitral tribunal.119 Art. 17(1)(b) of the EAL. If a party fails to appoint its arbitrator or if the appointed arbitrators fail to agree on the appointment of the chairperson, the competent court, specified in Art. 9 of the EAL, shall undertake the appointment of the arbitrator(s), upon the request of either party,120 Ibid. and its decision in this respect is final and not subject to any appeal or challenge. 3. Number of Arbitrators The EAL provides that the default number of arbitrators is three, and requires it to be an odd number.121 Art. 15 of the EAL. This requirement of having an odd number of arbitrators is a rule of public policy, such that the violation of such rule leads to the nullity of the arbitral award.122 Ibid. To continue with the proceedings the missing arbitrator(s) must be appointed. In this respect, an award rendered by an arbitral tribunal composed of an even number of arbitrators will be annulled, even if this requirement does not form part of the applicable law.123 Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 97 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 27 July 2003 whereby the Court has set aside an award rendered by an arbitral tribunal constituted of two arbitrators, on the grounds that it violates the court public policy. In a separate decision, the court held that an arbitral tribunal comprised of two arbitrators was not validly constituted: Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 71 of JY 115, Hearing session dated 29 January 2003. 4. Challenge to Arbitrators a. Grounds The appointment of arbitrators can be challenged. The EAL provides that an arbitrator may only be challenged if circumstances exist that give rise to serious and justifiable doubts as to his/her impartiality or independence.124 Art. 18(1) of the EAL. Moreover, the EAL specifies that a party may challenge the arbitrator it has appointed only for reasons that came to its knowledge after the appointment of the arbitrator.125 Art. 18(2) of the EAL. However, the EAL does not give a specific definition to what consists of serious and justifiable doubts as to the arbitrator’s impartiality and independence. That said, impartiality and independence remain hallmarks and fundamental obligations under the EAL, and there is a wealth of judgments rendered by Egyptian courts on challenges against arbitrators and arbitral awards on grounds pertaining to duties of disclosure, impartiality and independence. In this respect, the principle of independence is not related to emotion, but to an actual and legal position that can be objectively assessed.126 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 313. More specifically, the Cairo Court of Appeal defined the duty of independence of an arbitrator as follows:127 Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Appeal No.1 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 29 April 2003. “The independence of the arbitrator is the absence of his connection to or dependency on the parties to the dispute, the State or the third party, and the absence of any financial or psychological relation that is contradictory to his independence, whereas such [circumstances] constitute a definite danger resulting in the inclination to one of the parties of the arbitration. Hence, it is contradictory to the independence of the arbitrator to have financial benefits or a partnership or monetary connections with any of the parties to the dispute before it, or if the arbitrator is expecting a promotion or a raise from one of the parties, or if the arbitrator is subject to the party’s influence, instruction or subject to a promise or a threat from that party; as in the event where he provides a legal opinion and a technical help to one of the parties in return for a fee, or if he is working as a consultant for a company that is affiliated to the holding company related to one of the parties to the arbitration; or if he was appointed as an employee by one of the parties of the dispute on the day following the issuance of the arbitral award.” [Bracketed words added] Furthermore, impartiality has been defined as follows:128 Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Appeal No.78 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 30 March 2004. “The legislator did not provide a definition to the meaning of each, impartiality and independence, it can be said that impartiality, Impartialité, is any psychological or mental inclination of the arbitrator towards or against any of the parties to the dispute, a third party, or the State, which is likely to result in the arbitrator’s inability to rule without inclination towards or against any of the parties mentioned above. The presence of enmity or cordiality between the arbitrator and one of the parties is most likely to result in his inability to rule without inclination [towards or against any of the parties to the dispute]. However, this personal enmity or cordiality/friendship must be powerful enough as to infer the existence of the danger of lack of impartiality at the time of the issuance of the award.” These definitions have been confirmed and upheld by further court decisions.129 Cairo Court of Appeal, 7th Commercial Circuit, Appeal No.1 of JY 130, Hearing session dated 3 June 2013; Cairo Court of Appeal, 18th Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 92 of JY 135, Hearing session dated 12 January 2019; Cairo Court of Appeal, 3rd Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 42 of JY 136, Hearing session dated 8 March 2021; Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 13892 of JY 81, Hearing session dated 22 February 2022. For example, in a decision rendered by the Cairo Court of Appeal, after the issuance of the arbitral award, it came to the knowledge of the respondent that the chairman of the arbitral tribunal was a client of the co-arbitrator appointed by the claimant, and that neither had disclosed the existence of this relationship during the arbitral proceedings. However, the other co-arbitrator appointed by the respondent had disclosed at the time of his appointment that he was the lawyer of the respondent and confirmed to be impartial in this arbitral proceedings, and the claimant accepted his appointment after such disclosure. In this regard, the Cairo Court of Appeal held that the non-disclosure of the relationship existing between the chairman and the co-arbitrator appointed by the claimant created doubts as to their impartiality and independence, which consisted in fundamental requirements for the appointment of any arbitrator. Therefore, the Cairo Court of Appeal annulled the arbitral award on the ground of non-disclosure by the chairman and the co-arbitrator appointed by the claimant of their existing relationship prior to the commencement of the arbitral proceedings.130 Cairo Court of Appeal, 18th Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 92 of JY 135, Hearing session dated 12 January 2019. On another occasion, the Cairo Court of Appeal dismissed the nullity action brought on the ground of breach of the duty of disclosure by an arbitrator due to the fact that the undisclosed information does not constitute a “real danger of bias” or create “justifiable doubts” as to the independence or impartiality of the arbitrator.131 .Cairo Court of Appeal, 3rd Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 42 of JY 136, Hearing session dated 8 March 2021. Lastly, the Court of Cassation reaffirmed the well-established definitions of independence and impartiality of an arbitrator and held that these duties are fundamental under the EAL.132 .Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 13892 of JY 81, Hearing session dated 22 February 2022. In this judgment, the Court of Cassation expressly referred, for the first time ever, to the IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration (2014), quoting Clause 3.3.5 of the Orange list of the IBA Guidelines, on the basis that these Guidelines serve as guidance in determining the duty of disclosure and its impact on the independence and impartiality of an arbitrator.133 .Ibid. b. Procedure According to the EAL, the party challenging an arbitrator shall submit to the arbitral tribunal a challenge request in writing, incorporating the reasons for such challenge, within fifteen days from the date it becomes aware of the constitution of the arbitral tribunal or of the circumstances justifying such challenge.134 Art. 19(1) of the EAL. It is worth noting that Art. 19 originally provided that the arbitral tribunal would decide on the challenge. However, the Supreme Constitutional Court ruled that such provision was contrary to the 1971 Egyptian Constitution because it is contrary to the requirement of impartiality of the judges and arbitrators: Supreme Constitutional Court, Case No. 84 of Constitutional Year 19, Hearing session dated 6 November 1999. If the challenged arbitrator does not withdraw from his/her office within fifteen days from the date of submitting the challenge request, the request shall be forwarded to the competent court, specified in Art. 9 of the EAL, to decide on this matter and render a final decision that will be subject to no appeal.135 Ibid. Moreover, a party may not challenge the same arbitrator more than once in the same proceedings.136 Art. 19(2) of the EAL. Such challenge shall not entail the suspension of the arbitral proceedings, and if it is successful, the arbitral proceedings already conducted, including the arbitral award, shall be null and void.137 Art. 19(3) of the EAL. A challenge request shall only be formulated after the constitution of the arbitral tribunal and cannot be submitted directly by the requesting party to the state court.138 Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 120 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 26 June 2002. Accordingly, the request is submitted to the arbitral tribunal, and if the arbitrator fails to resign, the arbitral tribunal forwards such request to the competent state court which decides on the challenge, and there is no recourse against such decision. Once the arbitrator is challenged, a substitute arbitrator shall be appointed following the same procedure that governed the appointment of the challenged arbitrator.139 Art. 21 of the EAL. In case of institutional arbitration the applicable rules would include specific provisions on the regulation of challenges. For example, the current rule and practice of the CRCICA is that any challenges must be submitted by a party within fifteen days after it has been notified of the appointment of the challenged arbitrator, or within fifteen days from the date of knowledge of the circumstances giving rise to justifiable doubts as to an arbitrator’s impartiality and independence.140 Art. 14(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). After communicating the notice of challenge, the Centre shall request comments on the challenge from the parties, the challenged arbitrator and the other arbitrators.141 Art. 14(4) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). If the challenged arbitrator does not resign, the challenge shall be decided by an ad hoc legal committee of three members selected from among the members of the CRCICA’s Advisory Committee. According to the CRCICA’s Advisory Committee’s By-Laws, the decisions on challenges and replacement of arbitrators are considered final and unchallengeable. 5. Termination of the Arbitrator's Mandate An arbitrator may resign for inability to perform his or her mission or following a challenge by one of the parties.142 Ahmed Abdel Karim Salama, op. cit., p. 758. His/her mission may also be terminated by agreement of the parties. The EAL expressly provides that the competent court, specified in Art. 9 of the EAL, may order the termination of the arbitrator’s mandate upon request of either party, in any of the following circumstances: if the arbitrator is unable to perform his/her mission, fails to perform his/her task or interrupts the performance thereof in a manner which causes undue delay in the arbitral proceedings, and fails to withdraw or is not removed by agreement between the parties.143 Art. 20 of the EAL. In application of the above, the Cairo Court of Appeal ended the mandate of the chairman of an arbitral tribunal for his inability to manage the procedural hearings/meetings and for deciding to suspend the arbitral proceedings in the warranted circumstances.144 Cairo Court of Appeal, 50th Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 3 of JY 132, Hearing session dated 30 January 2019. It is worth noting that in institutional arbitration, the mission of an arbitrator appointed under the CRCICA Arbitration Rules may be terminated on similar grounds. Upon the request of a party, and after giving the arbitrator and the other party the opportunity to express their views, the arbitrator may be removed by a decision from an impartial and independent ad hoc committee composed of three members selected from among the members of the CRCICA’s Advisory Committee.145 Art. 13 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). Following the termination of an arbitrator’s mandate, a substitute arbitrator must be appointed following the same procedure that governed the appointment of the arbitrator whose mandate has been terminated.146 Art. 21 of the EAL. 6. Liability of Arbitrators Generally, the arbitrators enjoy immunity from suit and are not held liable towards the parties save in exceptional cases of fraud, corruption and/or gross negligence. Despite the absence of any legal text providing for the arbitrator’s immunity, such immunity is presumed and applied by analogy from the legislative immunity accorded to the judge/court. However, the immunity does not apply in cases of fraud, deceit or gross fault (gross negligence), in which cases the arbitrator’s civil liability can be exceptionally invoked before the courts.147 Fathi Waly, op. cit., pp. 369-371. Furthermore, in case of institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide for the exclusion of liability of the arbitrators, the Centre, its employees and any members of the Board or Advisory Committee thereof, save for intentional wrongdoing.148 Art. 55 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). In January and May 2019 the Egyptian courts passed and confirmed imprisonment sentences against certain arbitrators and members of a purported local arbitration institution who were engaged in the rendering of an arbitral award in sham arbitral proceedings. Charges of misappropriation by fraudulent means and forgery were made against the sentenced individuals.149 Al-Nozha Misdemeanor Court in Cairo, Case No. 12648 of JY 2018; and Cairo Court of Appeal, Appeal No. 695 of JY 2019 (East Cairo Appeals). This was an exceptional case that involved a flagrant criminal scheme that resulted in the issuance of a US$ 18 billion award against Chevron and enforcement petitions were also declined by US courts in California and Houston in relation to the award resulting from the sham proceedings in Cairo.

sections.title: CHAPTER IV: ARBITRAL PROCEDURE
sections.text: CHAPTER IV: ARBITRAL PROCEDURE 1. Place of Arbitration a. Determination The parties are free to agree on the place of arbitration (i.e., geographical venue, not the legal seat), whether in Egypt or abroad (Art. 28 of the Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I hereto)). However, if the parties have not decided where the arbitration shall take place, the arbitral tribunal shall determine the place of arbitration, taking into consideration the circumstances of the case and the convenience of the place to the parties.150 .Art. 28 of the EAL. b. Legal consequences The courts of the selected place of arbitration will be competent in relation to any action in support of the arbitral process, such as for example, the constitution of the arbitral tribunal or the ordering of provisional or conservatory measures.151 Ahmed Abdel Karim Salama, op. cit., pp. 771-772. The courts of the selected place of arbitration will also have jurisdiction over any nullity action of an arbitral award. The EAL distinguishes between the legal meaning of the place of arbitration (seat of arbitration) and its physical meaning (geographical venue). It expressly empowers the arbitral tribunal to convene in any place it deems appropriate, including places other than the seat of the arbitration, which entails the possible conduct of hearings and meetings abroad, such as hearing the parties, witnesses and experts or the perusal of documents, inspection of goods or monies or, finally, for deliberation purposes.152 Art. 28 of the EAL; and Mokhtar Berreri, International Commercial Arbitration (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2004) p. 103, para. 61. Egyptian courts have also acknowledged the distinction between the legal seat and the geographical venue already expressed in Art. 28 of the EAL.153 Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020; Cairo Court of Appeal, 3rd Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 42 of JY 136, Hearing session dated 8 March 2021; and Cairo Court of Appeal, 4th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 53 of JY 138, Hearing session dated 30 May 2022. 2. Arbitral Proceedings in General a. Mandatory provisions Most of the procedural rules governing the conduct of the proceedings are not mandatory and the parties may derogate therefrom by agreement. However, a few rules appear to be mandatory, such as lack of arbitrability of disputes that cannot be subject to a compromise and rights in rem; witnesses and experts may not be heard under oath; awards may not be rendered by truncated tribunals; tribunals may not be constituted from an even number of arbitrators; and parties may not agree to exclude the right to apply for setting aside of an award prior to the rendering of the said award. According to the EAL, the parties are free to agree on the procedure to be followed by the arbitral tribunal, including submitting the arbitral proceedings to institutional arbitration rules, in Egypt or abroad.154 Art. 25 of the EAL. However, if the parties fail to agree on the procedure to be followed, the arbitral tribunal may adopt the arbitral procedures it deems appropriate, taking into account the mandatory provisions of the EAL.155 Ibid. For instance, the arbitral tribunal must ensure that all parties are treated on equal footing and afforded an equal and full opportunity to present their case.156 Art. 26 of the EAL. It is worth noting that if the parties choose a foreign law or institutional rules to govern the procedure of the arbitration, such choice will exclude the application of the provisions of the EAL, except for those pertaining to public policy.157 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 7307 of JY 76, Hearing session dated 8 February 2007, whereby the Court held that the parties’ choice to refer their dispute to arbitration in Cairo under the ICC rules excluded the application of the EAL, with the exception of provisions relating to public policy. b. Determining procedure As explained under a above, the arbitration proceedings are conducted in the manner agreed upon by the parties, which includes the parties’ choice of institutional rules,158 The parties’ choice of an arbitral institution entails their choice to apply the arbitration rules of such institution: Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 385. and absent such agreement, the arbitral tribunal determines the procedure it deems appropriate, subject to the mandatory provisions of the EAL.159 Art. 25 of the EAL. In this respect, the EAL does not provide for specific guidelines to be adhered to by arbitral tribunals when determining the appropriate procedure and when the EAL is silent on a particular procedural matter. However, the EAL provides for some procedural matters, in absence of agreement between the parties thereon. For instance, the arbitral proceedings commence on the date the respondent receives the request for arbitration from the claimant, unless otherwise agreed by the parties.160 Art. 27 of the EAL. In that sense, the parties are encouraged to explicitly agree on the following: the scope of the arbitration agreement, as courts tend to construe arbitration agreements narrowly since arbitration is, hitherto, considered an exception to court jurisdiction; the explicit, accurate, and proper reference to the applicable institutional rules in case of institutional arbitration; the date of commencement of the arbitral proceedings if other than the default date of commencement, which is the date the respondent receives the request for arbitration from the claimant;161 Ibid. the realistic ratione temporis of arbitral proceedings in case of ad hoc arbitration, or otherwise the EAL dictates that the proceedings may be terminated by an application to the competent Egyptian court upon the lapse of a maximum period of eighteen months from the date of commencement of arbitral proceedings;162 Art. 45 of the EAL. the language of the proceedings, as the default language is Arabic in the absence of the parties’ choice;163 Art. 29 of the EAL. the place of arbitration (geographical venue), or otherwise the arbitral tribunal will determine same taking into account the circumstances of the case and the convenience of the place to the parties;164 Art. 28 of the EAL. the applicable lex causae, or otherwise the arbitral tribunal will determine the law that it deems to have the closest connection to the dispute;165 Art. 39 of the EAL. in case of adopting multi-tiered dispute resolution clauses, the parties must clearly define the exact procedures and time limits required prior to proceeding to the arbitration phase; and the arbitral tribunal’s power to grant or order interim measures and relief, if the parties so desire, as the EAL does not inherently confer such power on the arbitral tribunal in the absence of the parties’ consent.166 Art. 24 of the EAL. The tribunal may not overrule an agreement between the parties regarding procedure. The EAL clearly states that the arbitral tribunal determines the procedure to be followed absent an agreement between the parties.167 Art. 25 of the EAL. In this respect, there is an interesting judgment worthy of mention where the Cairo Court of Appeal considered that: (i) the COVID-19 illness of two arbitrators in a panel of three arbitrators constitutes a force majeure event that automatically interrupts the arbitration proceedings, by the force of law; and (ii) the use of new communication means (i.e., video-conference, email, WhatsApp or other similar programs and applications) is acceptable in the conduct of arbitral proceedings.168 .Cairo Court of Appeal, 4th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 43 of JY 138, Hearing session dated 26 April 2022. In this decision, the Cairo Court of Appeal held that WhatsApp is a valid means of communication in arbitral proceedings, provided that the fundamental principles of arbitration are observed, such as confidentiality, due process and fair and equitable treatment of the parties. In this regard, the Court explained that a party’s failure to object to the use of WhatsApp in the arbitral proceedings by continuing to participate therein amounts to its acceptance as a valid means of communication in application of the well-established principle of ‘non concedit venire contra factum proprium’ in Roman law, which is known as the principle of estoppel in Egyptian law.169 .Cairo Court of Appeal, 4th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 43 of JY 138, Hearing session dated 26 April 2022. c. Exchange of written pleadings The EAL expressly provides for the exchange of written pleadings between the parties before the case proceeds to a hearing, if there is one, otherwise the case will be decided on a documents only basis. In this respect, the EAL only refers to the exchange of a statement of claim and a statement of defence (and counterclaim if applicable).170 Art. 30 of the EAL. The EAL also reserves the right of the parties to amend or supplement their claims or defences, subject to the tribunal’s discretion, however it does not specify the manner in which it shall be done.171 Art. 32 of the EAL. d. Oral hearing It is not mandatory to hold an oral hearing on the merits. However, in practice, if one party requests a hearing, the arbitral tribunal is expected to hold one if warranted or justified. In this respect, Art. 33 of the EAL expressly provides that the arbitral tribunal may limit itself to decide the case on the basis of written submissions, provided that the parties have not agreed otherwise. Furthermore, the EAL does not prohibit the conduct of online (virtual) hearings and leaves it to the discretion of the arbitral tribunal, subject to the parties’ agreement or the applicable institutional rules. In practice, online hearings have been conducted even prior to COVID-19, but have increased owing to the COVID-19 restrictions. In this regard, in institutional arbitration, the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”) grants the arbitral tribunal the liberty to decide on the manner of conduct of proceedings as the tribunal considers appropriate, including the use of any technological means.172 Art. 17(1) and (3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). Moreover, the new CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024) expressly provide that hearings may be held in person, remotely by videoconference or other appropriate means, or in a hybrid form.173 Art. 28(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The Court of Cassation in 2020, and the Cairo Court of Appeal in 2022, expressly acknowledged the increased use of virtual hearings in arbitrations across the globe owing to the COVID-19 situation, and was keen on incorporating an express reference to the expression “virtual hearings” in English language in its very recent decision, which proves that the conduct of virtual hearings is consistent with the EAL.174 Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020; Cairo Court of Appeal, 4th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 43 of JY 138, Hearing session dated 26 April 2022; and Cairo Court of Appeal, 4th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 53 of JY 138, Hearing session dated 30 May 2022. 3. Evidence a. General The EAL states that the parties are free to agree on the procedural rules in an arbitration, and in the absence of such agreement the arbitral tribunal shall apply the rules it deems appropriate in accordance with the EAL.175 Art. 25 of the EAL. The EAL does not regulate the arbitrators’ powers with respect to evidence. It merely gives the arbitrators the right to request the originals of the documents submitted in support of the parties’ claims.176 Art. 30(3) of the EAL. Evidence is normally adduced by each party in support of its claims or defences. The EAL contains only few general provisions regarding evidence which relate to witnesses, party-appointed and tribunal-appointed experts, and documentary evidence. It is clear that the arbitral tribunal enjoys the power to admit, assess and weigh evidence.177 Cairo Court of Appeal, Appeal No. 26 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 22 November 2003 whereby the Court held that testimonies are to be weighed by the arbitral tribunal, such that it may decide not to take the testimony into consideration, if it has sufficient grounds to doubt its reliability; and Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 52 of JY 130, Hearing session dated 10 September 2014. The arbitral tribunal’s powers include: undertaking any evidentiary procedure it deems appropriate, reversing a procedure it had previously ordered and the discretion to decide on the evidence on record. Arbitrators also have the right to accept or deny a party’s request for an order on evidentiary procedures without prejudice to the party’s defence rights. If a party does not submit to, and comply with, the orders of the arbitral tribunal, the latter may draw negative inferences that could adversely affect the non-complying party’s position, especially if no adequate or reasonable justification is provided for a failure to comply. The evidence that may be admitted in arbitral proceedings in Egypt are documentary evidence, witness testimony, expert reports and/or site inspection by the arbitral tribunal.178 Art. 28 of the EAL which empowers the arbitral tribunal to decide to meet at any place it deems appropriate to undertake any of the arbitral proceedings which includes hearing parties, witnesses and experts, reviewing documentary evidence or inspecting goods or other property; and Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 52 of JY 130, Hearing session dated 10 September 2014. In institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide for several principles pertaining to evidence. In this respect, each party has the burden of establishing the facts relied upon in support of its claim or defence.179 Art. 27(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). Any individual may act as witness of fact or expert, to the extent permitted under the law governing the relevant issues, and unless otherwise directed by the arbitral tribunal, witness statements and expert reports are presented in writing and signed by the witness or expert.180 Art. 27(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). It is expressly admitted that the arbitral tribunal determines the admissibility, relevance, materiality and weight of any evidence offered.181 Art. 27(4) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). Other than where expressly stipulated by the EAL, arbitrators must consider the rules of evidence applicable in courts as part of the rules of procedure (to the extent it does not contradict the provisions of the EAL), which pursuant to Art. 25 of the EAL, the arbitral tribunal will determine absent an agreement between the parties. In this regard, for example, Art. 33(4) of the EAL stipulates that witnesses are not heard under oath, which is a departure from the rules of evidence applicable in the courts. This is explained by the fact that arbitral tribunals do not possess the coercive and executive powers enjoyed by courts. b. Evidence of fact witnesses Witnesses and experts may not be heard under oath.182 Art. 33(4) of the EAL, which constitutes a mandatory provision. A witness who refuses to appear before the arbitral tribunal to testify may, upon the request of the arbitral tribunal, be compelled to appear before the tribunal by the competent national court, specified in Art. 9 of the EAL, and also said court may penalize the witness and impose a fine thereon, as prescribed in Arts. 78 and 80 of the Egyptian Code of Evidence No. 25 of 1968.183 Art. 37 of the EAL. An order compelling witnesses to appear before an arbitral tribunal may be issued in “urgent circumstances”, such that the urgency of circumstances is decided by the judge on a case by case basis. The EAL does not prescribe any specific procedure for the hearing of witnesses. The procedure is determined by the parties’ agreement or, absent such agreement, by the arbitral tribunal itself as it deems appropriate.184 Art. 25 of the EAL. The EAL expressly allows the parties to cross-examine expert witnesses.185 Art. 36(4) of the EAL. Therefore, the same applies to fact witnesses, as cross-examination of fact witness is common practice in arbitrations conducted in Egypt. c. Evidence of party-appointed expert witnesses (for tribunal-appointed expert witnesses, see Chapter IV.4, below) The parties are entitled to rely upon expert evidence, by appointing their own expert witnesses, regardless of whether the arbitral tribunal has decided to appoint an expert.186 Ibid. The expert’s opinion is not binding on the arbitral tribunal, and his/her report and findings, are no more than an element of evidence subject to, as other evidence, examination and assessment by the arbitral tribunal.187 Cairo Court of Appeal, First Commercial Circuit, Case No. 48 of JY 137, Hearing session dated 9 December 2020. As to the procedure for hearing expert witnesses, the EAL provides that after the submission of the expert’s report (of tribunal-appointed experts), the arbitral tribunal may decide, on its own motion or upon a party’s request, to hold a hearing to hear the expert and to provide both parties the opportunity to cross-examine him/her on the content of his/her report.188 Art. 36(4) of the EAL. Furthermore, unless otherwise agreed by the parties, each party may present one or more expert witness to opine on the issues raised in the report of the tribunal-appointed expert.189 Ibid. d. Documentary evidence The EAL stipulates that the parties submit supporting evidence with their statement of claim and statement of defence, such that the copies of said documentary evidence relied upon is acceptable, subject to the arbitral tribunal’s right to demand the production of the originals at any stage of the proceedings.190 Art. 30(3) of the EAL. e. Document disclosure It is worth noting that the common law practice of discovery is neither recognized nor applicable in Egypt. While under the EAL the arbitral tribunal enjoys wide powers in conducting the proceedings and assessing the evidence submitted, the issue of production of documents is normally governed by the relevant provisions in the Egyptian Code of Evidence No. 25 of 1968, which provides for limited requests for production in specific exceptional cases. In this regard, Art. 21 of the Egyptian Code of Evidence No. 25 of 1968 confirms the requirements of document production, these are: (1) the specificity and characteristics of the document(s) requested, (2) the document’s content to the extent possible, (3) the document’s materiality and relevance to the disputed fact, (4) the circumstances and indications that prove that such document is in the possession of the opposing party, and (5) the basis for ordering the production thereof. As the parties are free to agree on the procedure to be followed in arbitration, as specified in Art. 25 of the EAL, the parties may agree to empower the arbitral tribunal to order the production of documents, or in absence of agreement between the parties and upon the request of one of the parties, the tribunal may order the production of documents as it deems appropriate. For example, the parties may agree on the use of the IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence in International Commercial Arbitration, which are increasingly used in international arbitrations seated in Egypt. The EAL addresses the consequences of a party’s failure to produce documents when so requested, which empowers the arbitral tribunal to continue the arbitral proceedings and render the award on the basis of the produced evidence.191 Art. 35 of the EAL. On the one hand, Art. 35 of the EAL implicitly recognizes the arbitral tribunal’s general power to order the production of documents, on its own initiative or upon a party’s request. On the other hand, a more restrictive reading of this Article would limit its interpretation to the express power granted to the tribunal to order originals of documents submitted by the parties, or to circumstances where the parties have expressly agreed to empower the arbitral tribunal to order the production of documents. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules empower the arbitral tribunal to order the parties to produce documents, exhibits or other evidence within a specified period of time, at its initiative or upon the request of any of the parties. Failure by a party to comply with the tribunal’s order to produce specific documents or evidence, without showing sufficient cause, entitles the arbitral tribunal to draw the necessary inferences.192 Art. 30(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). 4. Tribunal-Appointed Experts a. Power of the tribunal The EAL empowers the arbitral tribunal to appoint one or more experts to present a written or oral report on specific issues, as determined by the arbitral tribunal, and without the need to have the consent of both parties. However, the tribunal shall provide the parties with a copy of the expert’s terms of reference regarding his/her mission in this regard.193 Art. 36(1) of the EAL. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules slightly differ, as the arbitral tribunal may appoint one or more experts, after consultation with the parties and the expert report is only in writing.194 Art. 29(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). b. Role of the parties The parties are required to submit to the expert all relevant information and documents concerning the dispute, and to allow him/her to examine any documents, goods or other property for his/her inspection in relation thereto.195 Art. 36(2) of the EAL. The EAL does not provide for a duty upon the tribunal to consult the parties beforehand regarding the questions that will be submitted to the expert;196 Art. 36(1) of the EAL. however, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules impose on the tribunal to consult the parties before appointing an expert.197 Art. 29(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). c. Rules applicable to the tribunal-appointed expert Following the appointment of the tribunal-appointed expert and after each party submitted all relevant information and documents in relation to the dispute to the expert, the arbitral tribunal shall send each party a copy of the report immediately after its submission, enabling the parties to submit their comments thereon.198 Art. 36(3) of the EAL. The parties are also entitled to examine the documents relied upon by the expert in his/her report.199 Ibid. The arbitral tribunal may decide, on its own motion or upon a party’s request, to hold a hearing to hear the expert’s testimony, and to provide both parties the opportunity to cross-examine him/her on the content of his/her report.200 Art. 36(4) of the EAL. Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, each party may present one or more expert witness to opine on the issues raised in the report of the tribunal-appointed expert.201 Ibid. It is worth noting that there are no specific rules provided for under the EAL for fact witnesses regarding the submission of their witness statements. However, experts likewise fact witnesses cannot also be heard under oath.202 Art. 33(4) of the EAL. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide more or less the same as the EAL with slight variations and/or additions.203 Art. 29 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). Firstly, before accepting its appointment, the expert shall submit a description of his/her qualifications and a statement of independence and impartiality, and the parties have the right to object thereto before the arbitral tribunal.204 Art. 29(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). Secondly, a hearing will only be held if requested by one of the parties.205 Art. 29(5) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). d. Procedure Pursuant to the EAL, expert reports can either be in writing or submitted as an oral report to be included in the procès-verbal of the hearing.206 Art. 36(1) of the EAL. The parties are entitled to receive a copy of the written expert report immediately after its submission and prior to the hearing, to express their opinion thereon and to review and examine the documents relied upon by the expert in his/her report.207 Art. 36(3) of the EAL. The same applies in institutional arbitration, pursuant to the CRCICA Arbitration Rules, however, expert reports must only be in writing.208 Art. 29(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). 5. Interim Measures of Protection a. Categories of interim measures – tribunal The EAL grants the arbitral tribunal the right to award provisional or interim relief, upon the request of a party, and only if the parties have agreed to confer such power upon the arbitral tribunal.209 Art. 24(1) of the EAL. It is also acknowledged that such power could be conferred upon the arbitral tribunal by agreeing to the application of institutional rules that provide for such default power. The EAL does not provide a list of the types of relief available to arbitrators, but it is generally accepted that an arbitral tribunal, if the parties so agree, has the discretion to order any type of interim relief or provisional measures that are warranted provided that such relief is available under the applicable law to such relief. In this regard, scholars refer to the general rules of the Egyptian Code of Civil and Commercial Procedures (“CCCP”) regarding provisional measures210 Ahmed Sedki Mahmoud, “Conservatory Measures Necessary to Resolve Arbitration Proceedings”, 8 Journal of Arab Arbitration (August 2005) p. 64. as set out in Art. 45 of the CCCP. Pursuant to Art. 45 of the CCCP, interim measures will not be ordered unless the following conditions are satisfied: (1) there is an imminent danger or risk of substantial prejudice to the right of the requesting party; (2) delay to the final decision may prejudice the right or the legal status that the measure aims to protect; and (3) the required measure should not have an impact on the outcome or the merits of the dispute.211 Ahmed Abdel Karim Salama, op. cit., pp. 932-933. For instance in case of perishable goods, the arbitral tribunal may issue an order to preserve these goods, such as permitting the sale of these goods in the market before they perish.212 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 511. An arbitral tribunal may also order to stop the liquidation of a letter of guarantee.213 Ibid. However, arbitral tribunals cannot order an attachment of property or a conservatory seizure of movables/goods owned by the debtor or of the debtor’s property held by a third party, or even order forced execution, such that only courts have the authority to issue such orders.214 Ibid. The coercive powers of arbitrators differ than those of judges, as arbitrators lack the imperium to enforce their orders, which would need to be enforced by a national judge, if it is not complied with by the party. In this respect, the EAL expressly stipulates that in the event of non-compliance with the interim measure ordered by the arbitral tribunal, the other party may request the competent court, specified in Art. 9 of the EAL, to issue an execution order, or it may request the arbitral tribunal to allow it to undertake the necessary procedure for the execution of the order.215 Art. 24(2) of the EAL. The EAL also states that an arbitral tribunal is entitled to request adequate security for the costs incurred in securing the interim relief.216 Art. 24(1) of the EAL. Similarly, national courts are free to decide on whatever security is required to ensure the implementation of an order for interim or provisional measures. Alternatively, a party may directly seek to obtain interim relief directly from the competent court, such that the EAL allows national courts to issue interim and conservatory measures, upon the request of one of the parties, before commencing the arbitration proceedings or during said proceedings.217 Art. 14 of the EAL. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules contain detailed provisions regarding interim measures. They provide a definition for the expression “interim measures”218 Art. 26(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). and set forth the criteria that must be satisfied for such measures to be granted.219 Art. 26(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The arbitral tribunal is empowered to order such measures at the request of any of the parties,220 Art. 26(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). subject to security for costs if appropriate.221 Art. 26(6) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The tribunal may order the party requesting an interim measure to pay damages to the other party, at any time during the proceedings, if it later determines that the interim measure should have not been granted, at the circumstances prevailing at the time of granting such measure.222 Art. 26(8) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). It may also, upon the request of a party or, in exceptional cases, upon its own initiative, modify, suspend or terminate an interim measure previously granted.223 Art. 26(5) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). To this end, the arbitral tribunal may request any of the parties to disclose material changes to the circumstances on the basis of which the interim measure was granted.224 Art. 26(7) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). Finally, the Rules expressly stipulate that an application to the courts for interim measures does not constitute a waiver of the arbitration agreement.225 Art. 26(9) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). b. Comparison with 2006 Model Law The EAL has not adopted Chapter IV.A of the UNCITRAL Model Law (2006) regarding interim measures, as it was enacted prior to the UNCITRAL Model Law amendments in 2006. c. Form Under the EAL, arbitral tribunals may award interim relief by issuing an interim award,226 Art. 42 of the EAL. which makes it subject to the ordinary procedures for the enforcement and recognition of arbitral awards. Nonetheless, interim awards do not have res judicata effect, thus, enforcement of interim awards is not common. However, in 2017, the president of the Cairo Court of Appeal, in an ex parte proceedings, enforced, for the first time, an interim decision rendered by an ICC arbitral tribunal seated in Paris.227 Cairo Court of Appeal, Arbitration Orders, Ordinance No. 39 of JY 134, dated 8 November 2017. This enforcement order has been affirmed by the Cairo Court of Appeal in an adversarial proceeding.228 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 44 of JY 134, Hearing session dated 9 May 2018. d. Categories of interim measures – court The EAL expressly allows national courts to issue interim and conservatory measures, upon the request of one of the parties, before commencing the arbitration proceedings or during said proceedings, such that this cannot be considered as a waiver of the arbitration agreement.229 Art. 14 of the EAL. In this respect, national court enjoy a wider authority to order any type of interim measures unlike arbitral tribunals. A national court has the authority to order attachment of property or forced execution. Furthermore, in the event of non-compliance with the interim measure ordered by the arbitral tribunal and upon the request of the other party, the competent national court, specified in Art. 9 of the EAL, may issue an execution order of the interim measure.230 Art. 24(2) of the EAL. 6. Representation and Legal Assistance The EAL is silent on this matter. However, the Advocacy Law No. 17 of 1983 expressly stipulates that advocacy work is exclusively reserved to lawyers, such that non-lawyers cannot exercise any advocacy related work as listed in Art. 3 of the Advocacy Law, and which includes representing parties before arbitral tribunals.231 Art. 3 of the Advocacy Law No. 17 of 1983. In this regard, for a lawyer to represent a party before an arbitral tribunal, a special power of attorney is required and shall expressly mention that said lawyer shall represent the party in arbitration or be more specific and refer whether it is institutional (name of the institution) or ad hoc. The power of attorney may also specify the acts to be undertaken by the lawyer such as signing arbitration agreements, drafting memoranda, attending hearings, filing nullity actions, etc. Doctrinal views confirm the above-mentioned and adds that in arbitration seated in Egypt, only (1) lawyers admitted at the Egyptian Bar, or (2) Arab lawyers who may participate with Egyptian lawyers to appear in hearings before Egyptian courts, subject to judicial cooperation treaties concluded with Arab countries, may represent parties before arbitral tribunals. In this respect, foreign lawyers cannot represent parties in arbitrations seated in Egypt even if the concerned party is a foreign party.232 Fathi Waly, op. cit., pp. 432-433. However, there exist exceptions, such that in international commercial arbitration, a lawyer that is not admitted to the Egyptian Bar, may represent a party in the arbitral proceedings, given that such representation does not constitute a violation to Egyptian public policy. This is based on the principle of party autonomy in international arbitration. Another exception consists in the parties’ choice of the institutional arbitration rules which does not require representation of parties in arbitration by a lawyer, such as the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules,233 Art. 5 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024) provides that the parties may be represented or assisted by any person of their choice. and the ICC Arbitration Rules.234 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 433. A recent decision issued by the Egyptian Court of Cassation ended the debate on party representation and determined that there are no limitations or restrictions on party representation in arbitrations seated in Egypt. The Court of Cassation recognized that the parties to an arbitration, be it domestic or international proceedings, may be represented by any person of their choice, whether being a lawyer, Egyptian or foreigner, or a non-lawyer.235 Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020. 7. Default If the claimant fails to submit its written statement of claim, without a valid cause, the arbitral tribunal shall terminate the arbitral proceedings, unless otherwise agreed by the parties (Art. 34(1) of the EAL). However, if the respondent fails to submit its statement of defence, the EAL enables the arbitral tribunal to continue with the proceedings, without treating such failure as an admission by the respondent of the claimant’s allegations, unless otherwise agreed by the parties.236 Art. 34(2) of the EAL. Furthermore, if a party fails to appear at a hearing or submit a required document, the arbitral tribunal may continue with the proceedings and to render the arbitral award based on the submitted elements of evidence.237 Art. 35 of the EAL. If the submitted evidence is not sufficient for the arbitral tribunal to make an award, the proceedings may be terminated by a decision of the arbitral tribunal.238 Art. 48(1) of the EAL. Anyhow, it should be noted that a non-participating or absent party should be duly notified of all the documents submitted and orders issued, and must be given a proper and adequate opportunity to present its case and defences at every stage of the proceedings. National courts cannot compel participation of a party who failed to participate in the arbitration. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules contain similar provisions. In the event the claimant fails to submit its statement of claim, without showing sufficient cause, the arbitral tribunal shall issue an order for termination of the proceedings, unless there are remaining matters that need to be decided.239 Art. 30(1)(a) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). In the event the respondent fails to submit its response to the notice of arbitration or its statement of defence, without showing sufficient cause, the arbitral tribunal shall continue the arbitral proceedings, without treating such failure to submit as an admission of claimant’s allegations (the same applies to the claimant who fails to submit a defence to a counterclaim or a claim for the purpose of a set-off).240 Art. 30(1)(b) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The arbitral tribunal may also proceed with holding the hearing,241 Art. 30(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). if a party fails to appear at the hearing, and render the arbitral award based on the available evidence,242 Art. 30(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). if a party failed to submit documents, exhibits or other evidence, such that the tribunal may draw the necessary inferences from a party’s failure to comply with an order to produce evidence.243 Ibid. 8. Confidentiality of the Award and Proceedings a. Confidentiality of the arbitral award and proceedings Arbitral proceedings are generally confidential and the EAL provides that an arbitral award may not be published, in whole or in part, unless agreed by the parties.244 Art. 44(2) of the EAL. There is an implied duty of confidentiality not only with respect to the arbitral proceedings and documents submitted, but with respect to the award itself which may not be made public without the parties’ consent, unless the award has been subject to nullity or enforcement proceedings, where its content will then fall in the public domain. The principle of confidentiality is confirmed by the Explanatory Note of EAL, which explains that the confidentiality of the arbitration is of significant importance to the parties in order to preserve inter-commercial relations. It is also unchallenged that the evidence produced and the pleadings filed remain confidential. Nevertheless, a party may seek to rely on such evidence and pleadings in other proceedings provided that no privileged or confidential information is disclosed. Any such disclosure requires a court order to that effect or the consent of the concerned parties. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules place upon parties, arbitrators, tribunal-appointed experts, arbitral tribunal secretary and the Centre the obligation to keep confidential all awards, decisions, materials submitted by the parties in the arbitral proceedings, save when a disclosure may be required to pursue a legal right or to enforce or challenge an award before national courts.245 Art. 54(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The deliberations of the arbitral tribunal are also confidential.246 Art. 54(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The CRCICA undertakes not to publish any decision or arbitral award or any part thereof or reveal the identity of any of the parties, without the prior written consent of all parties.247 Art. 54(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). b. Confidentiality of arbitration-related court proceedings Like other court proceedings, arbitration-related court proceedings form part of the public record. The court may, however, hold the hearing in chambers if so requested by a party, for reasons related to public policy or to preserve morals or family secrets.248 Art. 101 of the CCCP. A copy of any court decision may be provided to any person upon payment of a fee.249 Art. 180 of the CCCP.

sections.title: CHAPTER V: ARBITRAL AWARD
sections.text: CHAPTER V: ARBITRAL AWARD 1. Types of Award The Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I hereto) stipulates that arbitral tribunals may issue interim (interlocutory), partial and final awards.250 Art. 42 of the EAL. Interim awards deal with incidental matters, such as the existence or non-existence of a valid arbitration agreement, or lack of arbitrability of the dispute. Partial awards dispose some, but not all, of the issues in dispute. However, they do not terminate the dispute,251 Ibid. as opposed to final awards which do.
