Finally, it is worth noting that an international commercial arbitration is not necessarily an arbitration seated in a foreign state resulting in the issuance of a foreign award. Article 3 of the EAL gives a definition of when an arbitration is international. In this respect, the Court of Cassation held that it is not a prerequisite for an arbitration to be international to have its subject matter related to international trade. The Court added that the EAL adopts specific legislative criteria for a “deemed internationalization”, amongst which is: resorting to arbitral institutions in Egypt or abroad.402 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 11348 of JY 88, Hearing session dated 11 April 2019, which reversed Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 8777 of JY 87, Hearing session dated 7 March 2018. Furthermore, the Court of Cassation has provided insight on what is meant by an arbitral institution under Art. 3 of the EAL and the made reference to the ICC International Court of Arbitration serving as an example of an arbitral institution fulfilling the criteria of Art. 3.403 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 14126 of JY 88, Hearing session dated 22 October 2019. Therefore, when arbitration qualifies as an international commercial arbitration, it may still be seated in Egypt and have Egyptian law as the applicable law to the substance, and consequently be subject to Egyptian public policy including mandatory rules.

sections.title: CHAPTER VII: MEANS OF RECOURSE
sections.text: CHAPTER VII: MEANS OF RECOURSE 1. Appeal on the Merits from an Arbitral Award a. Appeal to a second arbitral instance Neither the Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I) nor the Arbitration Rules of the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”) provide for an appeal before a court of law or a challenge before a second arbitral tribunal. b. Appeal to a court Arbitral awards are not subject to appeal before Egyptian courts and cannot be challenged on the merits. The challenge of an arbitral award by any of the means of recourse provided under the Egyptian Code of Civil and Commercial Procedures (“CCCP”) (namely appeal before the court of appeal; challenge before the Court of Cassation; and petition for review) is expressly prohibited by the EAL.404 Art. 52(1) of the EAL. 2. Setting Aside of the Arbitral Award (Action for Annulment, Vacation of the Award) a. Grounds for setting aside The grounds upon which an award can be set aside by the court are exhaustively listed in Art. 53(1) of the EAL, which reads in pertinent part: “1. An arbitral award may be annulled only: (a) If there is no arbitration agreement, if it was void, voidable or its duration had elapsed; (b) If either party to the arbitration agreement was at the time of the conclusion of the arbitration agreement fully or partially incapacitated according to the law governing its legal capacity; (c) If either party to the arbitration was unable to present its case as a result of not being given proper notice of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the arbitral proceedings, or for any other reason beyond its control; (d) If the arbitral award failed to apply the law agreed upon by the parties to govern the subject matter in dispute; (e) If the composition of the arbitral tribunal or the appointment of the arbitrators was in conflict with this Law or the parties’ agreement; (f) If the arbitral award dealt with matters not falling within the scope of the arbitration agreement or exceeding the limits of this agreement. However, in the case when matters falling within the scope of the arbitration can be separated from the part of the award which contains matters not included within the scope of the arbitration, the nullity affects exclusively the latter parts only; (g) If the arbitral award itself or the arbitration procedures affecting the award contain a legal violation that causes nullity. […]” Furthermore, the court adjudicating the nullity action shall ipso jure annul the arbitral award if it is contrary to public policy in Egypt.405 Art. 53(2) gof the EAL. Egyptian courts strictly adhere to the grounds for annulment provided under the EAL. The court cannot reconsider the merits of the dispute. On many occasions this principle has been affirmed by both, the Court of Cassation,406 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 7303 of JY 76, Hearing session dated 8 February 2007; and Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020; Court of Cassation, Civil and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 12262 of JY 90, Hearing session dated 24 June 2021; Court of Cassation, Civil and Commercial Circuit, Challenges Nos. 1964 and 1968 of JY 91, Hearing session dated 8 July 2021; and Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 13892 of JY 81, Hearing session dated 22 February 2022. and the Cairo Court of Appeal407 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 6 of JY 124, Hearing session dated 16 April 2007; Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 24 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 30 March 2004; Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 46 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 22 November 2003; Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 17 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 26 June 2002; and Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 61 of JY 115, Hearing session dated 21 April 1999. which have held that arbitral awards may not be reviewed on the merits and that the nullity action is not an appeal. In a recent decision, the Court of Cassation held that judicial review of awards should not transcend the normative limitations and courts may not (1) review the arbitral tribunal’s understanding and assessment of the facts; and (2) ascertain whether the arbitral tribunal erred on the application of the law governing the merits. The Court also held that there is a presumption that the proceedings have been properly conducted, which implies that the party, which claims otherwise, would naturally bear the burden of proof. The Court emphasized that the grounds for setting aside arbitral awards are exhaustively defined in Art. 53 of the EAL and that the Court may not review the arbitral award to examine its adequacy/appropriateness and/or to ascertain the soundness of the determinations of the arbitrators.408 Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020. An award cannot be set aside on the basis of an error in law. The Cairo Court of Appeal rejected an application to set aside in a case where the applicant alleged that the arbitral tribunal did not comply with the provisions of the contract, breached the provisions of the tender law and failed to properly apply the provisions of the Civil Code.409 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 4 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 30 December 2002. In the same vein, the Court rejected an application to set aside and held that the annulment of an arbitral award is only due to procedural defaults or errors which relate to fundamental procedural standards prescribed by law and which cannot be overridden. The defect in application of the law does not constitute any contravention to public policy rules and does not lead, in itself, to the annulment of the arbitral award.410 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 32 of JY 133, Hearing session dated 8 August 2016. Violation of public policy is one of the grounds for setting aside an award and is the only ground which must be invoked ex officio by the court.411 Art. 53(2) of the EAL. The court should not, under cover of setting aside the award for violation of public policy, review the merits of the case. This is confirmed by the Court of Appeal, which explained that a violation of Egyptian public policy consists in the contravention of the societal, political, economic, and ethical foundations in Egypt, which relates to the society’s higher interests, and is considered as elements of its structure. Therefore, if the arbitral tribunal violates a legal rule in the applicable law or defaults in choosing or interpreting the legal rule applicable to the merits of the dispute, this cannot be characterized – in light of standards of reason – as a deficiency constituting a violation of society’s higher interests or its fundamental values. The defect in application of the law does not constitute any contravention of public policy rules and does not lead, in itself, to the annulment of the arbitral award.412 Cairo Court of Appeal, 7th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 32 of JY 133, Hearing session dated 8 August 2016. With respect to the saga of setting aside the Al Kharafi v. Libya arbitral award rendered on 22 March 2013 under the auspices of the Unified Agreement for the Investment of Arab Capital in the Arab States (“UAIACAS”), the Court of Appeal annulled the arbitral award rendered in favour of Al Kharafi against the Libyan State on the ground of breach of the public policy principle of proportionality in the assessment of damages. The Court of Appeal considered, inter alia, that the arbitral tribunal awarded exorbitant compensation that is grossly exaggerated beyond the actual damage sustained by Al Kharafi.413 Cairo Court of Appeal, First Commercial Circuit, Case No. 39 of JY 130, Hearing session dated 3 June 2020. The annulled arbitral award was issued in 2013 awarding US$ 900+ Million to Al Kharafi Group for the damages sustained thereby as a result of being unable to take possession of the land and start its investment touristic project. This is the third time the Libyan State brings the application for setting aside before the Court of Appeal (constituted in different circuits after referral by the Court of Cassation). The Court of Appeal dismissed the first two nullity actions on different grounds: (i) inadmissibility; and (ii) lack of international jurisdiction, however, the Court of Cassation on the two occasions reversed the Court of Appeal’s decisions and held that on the first time that the Court of Appeal has jurisdiction to decide on the nullity action. On the second time, the Court of Cassation held that the Court of Appeal violated the former’s decision confirming that the latter has jurisdiction, and should have examined the case, on the basis of the principle of res judicata effect of the Court of Cassation decisions: (Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 18615 of JY 88, Hearing session dated 10 December 2019). However, after almost a decade, the Court of Cassation finally upheld the validity of the well-known Al Kharafi v. Libya arbitral award and dismissed the Libyan State’s motion to set aside the arbitral award.414 Court of Cassation, Civil and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 12262 of JY 90, Hearing session dated 24 June 2021. The Court affirmed the longstanding principle that the role of review courts is limited to examining the nullity of the arbitral award strictly based on the grounds set forth under Art. 53 of the EAL and that the review court (in this case the Cairo Court of Appeal) shall not conduct a de novo review of the merits. This means that an error in the arbitral tribunal’s assessment does not qualify as a ground for setting aside the arbitral award.415 .Ibid. On another occasion, the Court of Cassation, in a highly contentious decision, for the first time in its history annulled an ICC arbitral award without remanding it to the Court of Appeal, on the basis that the award violated a fundamental principle of Egyptian public policy by ruling on the legality of an administrative decision.416 Court of Cassation, Civil and Commercial Circuit, Challenges Nos. 1964 and 1968 of JY 91, Hearing session dated 8 July 2021. The Court of Cassation reasoned that judicial control on the legality of an administrative decision – its validity and fulfilment of formal requirements – falls within the exclusive jurisdictional competence of the State Council courts, that this is a fundamental principle of public policy, and therefore, in application of Art. 53.2 of the EAL, the Court should set aside the arbitral award on its own initiative.417 Ibid. Despite the unexpected outcome, the Court of Cassation reiterated its well-established principle that review courts shall not conduct a de novo review of the merits or even delve into the arbitral tribunal’s findings, unless a matter of public policy is in play. The Court of Cassation made it clear that review courts must strictly abide by the nullity grounds stipulated in Art. 53 of the EAL.418 Ibid. Under the EAL, the parties may exclude the right to set aside the award only after the issuance of the arbitral award. Any renouncement to the right for setting aside made prior to the issuance of the award will not preclude its admissibility.419 Art. 54(1) of the EAL. The only available action to be brought is an action for setting aside, the EAL does not provide for separate actions. The question as to whether the parties may restrict the grounds for setting aside is not addressed by the EAL and has yet to be considered by the courts. This should be possible as a matter of principle, because such an agreement would merely constitute a partial waiver by the parties of their right to set aside.420 Presumably, such a partial waiver would, like a complete waiver, only be valid if made after the issuance of the award. b. Procedure The action for setting aside must be brought within the ninety days from the date of notification of the award to the losing party.421 Art. 54(1) of the EAL. This time limit is the same for all actions of setting aside, regardless of the grounds upon which it is based. There is no special form prescribed for the action to set aside. It is an ordinary action like all court actions, beginning with the filing of an originating summons, exchanging memorials, and a hearing.422 Art. 297 of the CCCP. Under the EAL, a party can be barred from invoking one or more grounds by that party’s appearance in the arbitration without raising an objection within a reasonable time, as the latter shall be deemed to have waived its right to object.423 Art. 8 of the EAL. In this regard, the Court of Cassation held in a very recent decision that if a party continued with the arbitration proceedings without an objection, despite its knowledge of a breach of a condition of the arbitration agreement or one of the non-mandatory provisions of the EAL, this would be deemed a waiver of his/her right to object. The Court went further to hold that even if the party had invoked its objection earlier, the challenge would still be rejected because a person may not benefit from his/her own wrongdoing. The Court also added that the principle of estoppel would militate against the success of the appellant’s plea, and remarked that estoppel is in application of the universal maxim “non concedit venire contra factum proprium” which is derived from Roman law. The Court innovatively unveiled two conditions for application of this principle, these are: (1) a statement, an act, or an omission is made by a party and contradicts with its previous conduct; and (2) that contradiction would prejudice the other party who acted in reliance on the validity of the first party’s previous conduct.424 Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020. Proceedings to enforce the award may be initiated or continue pending the rendering of the court’s decision on the application to set aside. The EAL does not stipulate or contemplate the possibility for the court to remit the arbitral award to the tribunal to give it the opportunity to eliminate the ground(s) on which the award could otherwise be set aside.425 Without prejudice to the arbitral tribunal’s powers to correct typographical, computation and clerical errors, to interpret the award and to issue additional awards, as discussed under the appropriate headings herein above. If the award is set aside on grounds that are unrelated to the arbitration agreement, the agreement remains valid and in force between the parties.426 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 806. c. Waivers Waiver of the right to bring an action for setting aside is permitted only after the issuance of the arbitral award. The waiver of this right before the issuance of the award is ineffective and will not render a subsequent application to set aside inadmissible.427 Art. 54(1) of the EAL. In principle, the exclusion concerns all grounds for setting aside, as it consists in a waiver of the right itself. The EAL is silent on whether the exclusion may concern only some of the grounds for setting aside, however, this has yet to be tested by courts, as it would consist in a partial waiver of the setting aside grounds which follows the same logic of the full waiver. d. Effect of an award that has been set aside An award that has been set aside has no effect and cannot be enforced. Any decision regarding the correction or the interpretation of the award loses its effect accordingly. However, if the award has been partially set aside, only the part of the award which has been set aside has no effect, but the part of the award that was not set aside remains valid and is enforceable.428 Art. 53(1)(f) of the EAL; and Fathi Waly, op. cit., pp. 804-805. 3. Other Means of Recourse Whilst the nullity action is the only means of recourse against the enforcement of the arbitral award, the judgment rendered by the competent court annulling or refusing annulment of the award, may be subject to an appeal, and the appeal may be subject to a challenge before the Court of Cassation, which supervises and ensures the correct application of the law by the Appeal Courts.

sections.title: CHAPTER VIII: CONCILIATION / MEDIATION
sections.text: CHAPTER VIII: CONCILIATION / MEDIATION 1. General a. ADR in Egypt The UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Conciliation has not been adopted in Egypt. It is worth mentioning that it is common for parties to agree to include an amicable settlement phase in the dispute settlement clauses in their contracts, and this of itself shows an interest for the parties to attempt resolving their potential dispute amicably and keeping the arbitration as a last resort. However, the amicable settlement mechanisms usually provided for are not based on a set of conciliation rules, rather they are expressed in general terms without proposing a specific mechanism to follow. Egypt is among the most pro-ADR countries in the region and promotes ADR. There is a noticeable trend in Egypt to use conciliation or mediation, which is evidenced by the establishment of new institutions that comprise, besides arbitration, conciliation and mediation dedicated rules and services. b. Institutions The leading arbitration institution, the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”), offers ADR services which include mediation, conciliation and mini-trials,429 Mini-trials are procedures where the parties agree to refer their dispute to a panel composed of two members selected from among the senior corporate officers of each party, and a neutral umpire. The two-member panel should attempt to reach a settlement, failing which the umpire will propose one that the parties are not bound to accept. and promotes their use through its Mediation and ADR Centre.430 By way of example, on 26 December 2021, the CRCICA and the CIArb Egypt Branch jointly organized a mock mediation construction case, with the objective to raise awareness for the use of mediation in the construction industry. In early 2022, the CRCICA offered a two-level mediation training aiming at promoting the use of mediation as an important tool to settle disputes. The first level training was titled “Fundamentals of Mediation” and was held on 25-26 January 2022, and the second level training was titled “Qualifying the Mediator” and was held on 29-30 January and 1-2 February 2022. See CRCICA Annual Report 2021-2022 available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/wp-content/uploads/‌2022/11/Annual-Report-2021-2022.pdf>. The CRCICA also strongly recommends the parties to use Arb-Med-Arb and puts at their disposition a guidance note.431 Arb-Med-Arb Guidance Note available at: <https://crcica.org/FilesEnglish/NewPages_‌2019-06-10_09-43-0-809697.pdf>. The CRCICA has its own set of mediation rules, the most recent version thereof entered into force on 1 January 2013, and it is available in Arabic and English. There is also the Egyptian Center for Arbitration and Settlement of Non-Banking Financial Disputes (“ECAS”)432 The official website for the Egyptian Center for Arbitration and Settlement of Non-Banking Financial Disputes is available at: <https://ecas.org.eg/en>. established within the Financial Regulatory Authority for the settlement of financial non-banking disputes by virtue of the Presidential Decree No. 335 of 2019. The ECAS offers mediation services. In this regard, the ECAS statutes as well as its arbitration and mediation rules have been issued by virtue of the Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2597 of 2020 on 10 December 2020. 2. Legal Provisions There are several specific laws and regulations that introduce mediation or conciliation as an initial means to resolve disputes before referral to courts or arbitral tribunals, as the case may be. Amongst such laws, are the following: – Bankruptcy Law No. 11 of 2018:433 The Law regulating Restructuring, Preventive Reconciliation, and Bankruptcy No. 11 of 2018 (the “Bankruptcy Law”) is a special law that repealed and replaced the provisions incorporated under Title 5 of the Egyptian Commercial Code, which previously governed insolvency proceedings in Egypt. the Bankruptcy Law provides for mediation under the supervision of the bankruptcy judge, acting as the mediator aiming to reach an amicable settlement of the dispute at any stage of the bankruptcy proceedings at the request of any interested party.434 Art. 178 of the Bankruptcy Law. Mediation was not expressly provided for under the old bankruptcy regime, i.e., Title 5 of the Egyptian Commercial Code. The Bankruptcy Law added this express reference to mediation to encourage the creditors to settle their debts with the debtor in a faster and more efficient proceeding under the supervision of the judge. Thus, the bankruptcy judge’s mission is to reach a settlement agreement between parties through any process he/she deems necessary (meetings with all parties or with each party separately, by doing what is necessary to reach a compromise acceptable to the parties, etc.). If the dispute is amicably settled between the parties, the agreed settlement agreement shall be signed by all parties. The bankruptcy judge shall issue a decision ratifying the mediation. The settlement agreement shall have the force of an executive writ.435 Arts. 7 to 10 of the Bankruptcy Law. – The Settlement Committee under the Electricity Law No. 230 of 2016: This committee is specialized in examining disputes that arise between the electricity utility parties. The committee’s members include technical, financial, commercial and legal experts. The said committee issues its justified decisions in relation to the disputes within a time limit not exceeding sixty days from the date of submitting the request along with all its related documents. The committee’s decisions are presented to the board of directors of the electricity authority for approval. – Economic Courts Establishment Law No. 120 of 2008: in every economic court in Egypt, a preparatory committee of disputes is established to undertake the conciliation process of most of the cases in which such courts have jurisdiction prior to initiating the legal process.436 Art. 8 of Law No. 120 of 2008 establishing Economic Courts (the “Economic Courts Establishment Law”). If the dispute is amicably settled between the parties, the agreed and signed settlement agreement shall have the force of an executive writ. – Family Courts Establishment Law No. 10 of 2004: prior to initiating a legal action before family courts in relation to personal status matters that cannot be subject to compromise, the plaintiff shall submit a request for settlement of the dispute addressed to the dispute settlement division within the family court.437 Art. 6 of Law No. 10 of 2004 establishing Family Courts (the “Family Courts Establishment Law”). In the same context of mediation, the parties may contractually agree that all disagreements be settled amicably by mediation within a determined timeframe otherwise the parties may recourse to the competent courts or arbitral tribunals. The Egyptian Code of Civil and Commercial Procedures (the “CCCP”) provides for conciliation in relation to certain types of disputes, excluding disputes that are not capable of settlement, summary proceedings, execution disputes, and applications for writ of debt. A “Conciliation Panel” shall be constituted in every district court composed of the two parties and presided by a Prosecutor. The panel shall terminate its mission within a period of thirty days that may be extended for an additional thirty days, subject to the agreement of the parties to reach a settlement of the dispute. A record would be made of any settlement reached within the foregoing period, including its terms. In the absence of an agreement, the Conciliation Panel would refer the dispute to the court and a hearing would be scheduled.438 Art. 64 of the CCCP. In addition to the provisions set out under the CCCP, Law No. 7 of 2000 establishing conciliation panels in relation to some disputes to which ministries and public juridical persons are party, provides for further requirements as to conciliation of disputes involving public entities. A conciliation panel shall be established in each ministry, or governorate or public entity to seek an amicable settlement of civil, commercial and administrative disputes.439 Art. 1 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The Minister of Justice shall issue a decree to constitute the conciliation panel, which shall be presided by a former judge or member of a judicial entity having a minimum grade of counsel and who ceased exercising his/her profession together with the representative of the administrative authority (with minimum grade of general manager) from one side and the other party or his/her representative from the other side.440 Art. 2 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The disputes excluded from the scope of this Law relate to disputes involving the Ministry of Defence or any of its bodies, disputes relating to in rem rights, or other disputes specifically excluded by a law or a dispute that requires involvement of a judicial entity shall be referred to arbitration.441 Art. 4 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The time limit provided for under the CCCP is not applicable to conciliation governed by this Law.442 Art. 7 of Law No. 7 of 2000. Meetings are valid only when all members are present, and the panel may resort to an expert’s assistance, and renders its recommendations by majority. Deliberations are confidential.443 Art. 8 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The recommendations shall be issued along with a brief reference to the reasons based upon recorded in the minutes and within a time limit that does not exceed sixty days from the date of submission of the application for conciliation. The recommendation shall be presented to the concerned authority and the other party to the dispute within seven days from the date of its issuance. If the two parties agree in writing to its findings within fifteen days from the date of its receipt, the panel records what has been agreed in the minutes which shall be signed by the parties, and has the same force of a writ of execution, then shall inform the competent authority executing it.444 Art. 9 of Law No. 7 of 2000. However, if one of the parties does not agree to the panel’s recommendation or if no agreement is reached at the elapse of the period provided for in this Law, each of the parties has the right to resort to the competent court to settle the dispute, such that the limitation periods, time bar periods, and procedural deadlines are suspended during conciliation.445 Art. 10 of Law No. 7 of 2000. An action initiated before a court, in a matter subject to this Law, before conciliation is inadmissible, except for matters related to summary proceedings, execution disputes, applications for writ of debt or writ of execution, and administrative decisions related to stay of execution.446 Art. 11 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The CCCP is silent as to the confidential nature or otherwise of the contemplated conciliation proceedings. However, Law No. 7 of 2000 provides for confidentiality of conciliation proceedings.447 Art. 8 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The EAL (see Annex I hereto) does not specify whether an arbitrator may also act as a conciliator during the arbitral proceedings. However, the parties are free to agree on the procedure to be followed by the arbitral tribunal and may confer such power to the arbitrator,448 Art. 25 of the EAL. so if the parties agree, nothing would prevent an arbitrator to act as conciliator. There is no express restriction for appointment of a person as a conciliator then as arbitrator, subject to the disclosure requirement when the person is to be appointed as arbitrator, otherwise the arbitrator may be challenged.449 Art. 18 of the EAL. If the arbitration agreement does not impose to attempt to settle amicably the dispute before resorting to arbitration, the parties are free to decide whether they want to attempt amicable settlement or if they can immediately proceed with arbitration. Anyhow, at any stage of the arbitral proceedings, the parties may agree to settle and request the tribunal to issue an award by consent.450 Art. 41 of the EAL. However, when conciliation or mediation is conducted under the auspices of an institution, the rules for conciliation or mediation apply. This would be the case for the CRCICA and the ECAS.

sections.title: CHAPTER IX: INVESTMENT TREATY ARBITRATION
sections.text: CHAPTER IX: INVESTMENT TREATY ARBITRATION 1. conventions and treaties Egypt is a party to the Washington International Convention on the Settlement of Investment Disputes between States and Nationals of Other States of 1965 (the “ICSID Convention”), which was ratified on 3 May 1972. Egypt is also a party to multilateral arbitration-related treaties and conventions which include the following: the Convention of the Arab League on the Enforcement of Judgments and Arbitral Awards of 1952 (the “Arab League Convention”) ratified on 28 August 1954; the Unified Agreement for Investment of Arab Capital in the Arab States (the “Arab Investment Agreement”) signed on 26 November 1980 in Amman and which entered into force on 7 September 1981; the Organisation of the Islamic Conference Investment Agreement of 1981 (the “OIC Investment Agreement”) ratified in February 1988; the Convention establishing the Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency (the “MIGA Convention”) of 1985; the COMESA Investment Agreement signed on 23 May 2007; the Riyadh Arab Agreement for Judicial Cooperation of 1983 signed in 2014; and the Egypt-MERCOSUR Preferential Free Trade Agreement which has entered into force in September 2017. As to bilateral treaties, Egypt has signed more than 100 Bilateral Investment Treaties (“BITs”) of which around seventy-four BITs have entered into force.451 The list of BITs concluded by Egypt and their date of entry into force is as follows: Albania (6/4/1994); Algeria (3/5/2000); Argentina (3/12/1993); Armenia (2/3/2006); Australia (5/9/2002); Austria (29/4/2002); Bahrain (11/1/1999); Belarus (18/1/1999); Belgium–Luxembourg Economic Union (24/5/2002); Bosnia & Herzegovina (29/10/2001); Bulgaria (3/6/2000); Canada (3/11/1997); China (1/4/1996); Comoros (27/2/2000); Croatia (2/5/1999); Cyprus (9/6/1999); Czech Republic (5/6/1994); Denmark (29/1/2000); Ethiopia (27/5/2010); Finland (5/2/2005); France (1/10/1975); Germany (22/11/2009); Greece (6/4/1995); Hungary (21/8/1997); Iceland (15/6/2009); Italy (1/5/1994); Japan (14/1/1978); Jordan (11/4//1998); Kazakhstan (8/8/1996); Korean Democratic People’s Republic (20/12/1999); Korean Republic (25/5/1997); Kuwait (26/4/2002); Latvia (3/6/1998); Lebanon (6/2/1997); Libya (4/7/1991); Malawi (7/9/1999); Malaysia (8/7/2000); Mali (7/7/2000); Malta (18/7/2000); Mauritius (17/10/2014); Mongolia (25/1/2005); Morocco (27/6/1998); Netherlands (1/3/1998); Oman (3/5/2000); Palestine (19/6/1999); Poland (17/1/1998); Portugal (23/12/2000); Qatar (14/7/2006); Romania (3/4/1997); Russia (12/6/2000); Serbia (20/3/2006); Singapore (20/3/2002); Slovakia (1/1/2000); Slovenia (7/2/2000); Somalia (16/4/1983); Spain (26/4/1994); Sri Lanka (10/3/1998); State of Palestine (19/6/1999); Sudan (1/4/2003); Sweden (29/1/1979); Switzerland (15/5/2012); Syria (5/10/1998); Thailand (4/3/2002); Tunisia (2/1/1991); Turkey (31/7/2002); Turkmenistan (29/3/1996); United Arab Emirates (11/1/1999); Ukraine (13/10/1993); United Kingdom (24/2/1976); United States of America (27/6/1992); Uzbekistan (8/2/1994); Vietnam (4/3/2002); and Yemen (10/4/1998). (UNCTAD Investment Policy Hub, available at <https://investmentpolicy.unctad.org/international-investment-agreements/countries/62/‌egypt)>. The majority of these multilateral treaties and BITs are published on the UNCTAD website. Egypt employs a Model BIT that was adopted in 2022, adjusting some provisions of the preceding versions of the Model BIT of 2013 and 2019, to pose a model for renewal of BITs. In this regard, it is worth mentioning some of the new amendments/additions introduced to the 2022 Model BIT, which include the following: (1) The definition of an “investor” under the new Model BIT excludes commercial agents, companies’ branches and representative offices. Such exclusion was not provided under the preceding versions; (2) The 2022 Model BIT maintained the inclusion of provisions related to third-party funding which were initially added by the 2019 Model BIT, where it is recognized that a party may have recourse to third-party funding but has a strict duty of disclosure of the funding agreement, either before the commencement of the arbitral proceedings or immediately after the conclusion of the funding agreement, and such duty of disclosure is continuous throughout the proceedings. The third-party funder would not qualify as a party to the arbitral proceedings and would not be entitled to any rights under the BIT. The failure of disclosure of the funding agreement shall be deemed a manifest contravention of a fundamental rule of procedure agreed upon by the disputing parties. Also, the existence of a third-party funding agreement shall be considered in the probable existence of a conflict of interests with an arbitrator, an expert, or a legal representative; and (3) Article 17 of the 2022 Model BIT (“Settlement of Disputes between an Investor and a Contracting Party”) has been significantly amended, reintroducing the reference to ICSID arbitration but under strict conditions. The amended version of the Model BIT provides for a shorter period of six months (instead of twelve months) for amicable settlement through conducting negotiations, conciliation or mediation without providing for specific rules, unlike the preceding 2019 Model BIT, which listed examples of mediation and conciliation rules. If the disputing parties fail to amicably resolve their dispute, and unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the investor shall resort to the dispute resolution mechanisms provided under the laws (of the host state) regulating the investment. In the preceding version, the 2019 Model BIT only referred to dispute resolution mechanisms under the Egyptian Investment Law. However, the major change in the 2022 Model BIT is the introduction of a third dispute settlement mechanism, which the disputing parties may only resort to in case of failure to settle their dispute (1) amicably or (2) through the dispute resolution mechanisms provided under the laws regulating the investment (i.e., the Egyptian Investment Law) within a period of twelve months, provided that the parties have not agreed to a longer period. In this respect, and subject to not having resorted to national courts of the host state to settle the same dispute, either party may settle their dispute through arbitration before any of the following institutions: ICSID, PCA, ad hoc arbitration (UNCITRAL), or any arbitration rules that the parties agree upon, noting that the parties’ choice of any of the aforementioned options is final and excludes resorting to the other available options. 2. investment arbitration Egypt has been party to many investment arbitrations. At the time of drafting this report, there have been thirty-eight ICSID cases brought against Egypt, among which seven are still pending and twenty-eight have been concluded.452 ICSID official website: <https://icsid-archive.worldbank.org/en/Pages/cases/Advanced‌Search.aspx>. The recognition and enforcement of ICSID awards in Egypt remain, primarily, a theoretical matter. Practice has shown that: awards were either rendered in favour of the Egyptian state; disputes were settled before a final award was rendered; or where awards were rendered in favour of the investor, post-award settlements were reached between Egypt and the investor. 3. national investment legislation The Investment Law in Egypt witnessed considerable change and constant developments since the enactment of the first Investment Law No. 65 of 1971 until enactment of the current Investment Law No. 72 of 2017.453 In between the two Investment Laws, there were three other Investment Laws enacted, respectively: Law No. 43 of 1974 on Investment of Arab and Foreign Capital and Free Zones, repealed and replaced by Law No. 230 of 1989 and the Investment Guarantees and Incentives Law No. 8 of 1997 which repealed and replaced Law No. 230 of 1989. The provisions relating to dispute settlement have evolved throughout the different laws, aiming to set forth a rigid mechanism of dispute avoidance and amicable settlement to minimize the financial burden of investment arbitrations. The current Investment Law No. 72 of 2017, has a dedicated title named “Title (V) – Settlement of Investment Disputes” divided into four Chapters, respectively: Chapter 1 – The Grievance Committee; Chapter 2 – The Ministerial Committee on Investment Dispute Resolution; Chapter 3 – The Ministerial Committee for Settlement of Investment Contracts Disputes; and Chapter 4 – Amicable Dispute Settlement Means and the Arbitration and Mediation Centre. The Investment Law encourages amicable settlement of any dispute through negotiations between the parties and at any time, without prejudice to the right to litigation.454 Art. 82 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Grievance Committee has the authority to examine complaints filed by investors against decisions/orders relating to issuance of approvals, permits and licenses.455 Art. 83 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Ministerial Committee on Investment Dispute Resolution examines applications, complaints or disputes between investors and the State or any of its entities. The Committee’s decisions, after being approved by the Cabinet of Ministers shall be enforceable and binding upon the concerned administrative authority.456 Art. 87 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Ministerial Committee for Settlement of Investment Contracts Disputes shall examine the differences arising between the parties to the investment contract. The Committee is entitled, subject to the parties’ consent, to make the necessary settlement and restore the equilibrium of the contract, extend time limits and periods or grace periods provided under the contract. The Committee may also re-schedule financial dues or rectify procedures which precede the conclusion of contracts to restore the contractual equilibrium to achieve the best economic situation for both, public funds and investor’s rights.457 Art. 89 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. Investment disputes shall be settled in the manner agreed with the investor or pursuant to the provisions of the Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I). The parties may at any stage of the dispute agree to pursue any type of settlement pursuant to the rules applicable to the dispute which include ad hoc arbitration and institutional arbitration.458 Art. 90 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Egyptian Arbitration and Mediation Centre shall be established in Cairo to administer the settlement of investment disputes which may arise among investors or between an investor and the State or one of its entities.459 Art. 91 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Investment Law does not include express reference to ICSID arbitration or a specific arbitral institution other than the Egyptian Arbitration and Mediation Centre. Furthermore, in 2019, a new committee was established – the “High Committee for Studying and Opining on International Arbitration Disputes”.460 Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019. This committee’s role is to examine and opine on all arbitration disputes, whether commercial or investment arbitrations, to which the state or any ministry, or public state entities, or governmental bodies and their affiliated entities, or an affiliated company to the state or whereby the state is a shareholder to said company, is party thereto.461 Art. 2 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 (amending Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019). The decisions of this committee are binding upon the concerned state entities, once approved by the Council of Ministers.462 Art. 1 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019. Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019 issued on 24 April 2019 was amended twice by virtue of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 issued on 9 December 2020 and Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022 issued on 13 September 2022. These are the latest amendments in a series of decrees that date back to 2013 and were amended in 2014 and 2019.463 Decrees Nos. 108/2013 (revoked), 283/2013 (revoked), 847/2013 (revoked), 1255/2014 (revoked), 1966/2014 (revoked), 1062/2019 (amended by Decrees Nos. 2592/2020 and 3218/2022). Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 renamed an existing State Committee entrusted with overseeing arbitration proceedings and international disputes from the “High Committee for Studying and Opining on International Arbitration Cases” to the “High Committee for Arbitration and International Disputes”. The Committee, which is reconstituted every now and then, is entrusted with reviewing and opining on pending international arbitration cases in both commercial and investment cases involving the State, its organs and state-controlled entities. The rationale underlying the establishment of such Committee is to keep track of pending cases and streamline the approach taken by the State, its organs and the state-controlled entities regarding the arbitrations involving same. It is intended to serve as a central focal point for review, follow up and deciding on appointments, the approaches to be adopted and providing any necessary support. Art. 2 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 sets the expanded scope of the Committee, but clearly states that this is without prejudice to Arts. 89, 90 and 91 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. Art. 90 of the 2017 Investment Law clearly states that it is permissible to settle the investment disputes pertaining to the provisions of that Law in the manner agreed with the investor or in accordance with the Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994. The same provisions also refer to ad hoc and institutional arbitration as available options, in addition to other ADR processes. In light of the above, Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 does not present an obstacle to investor-state arbitration involving Egypt, especially as it makes express reference to the prevalence of Arts. 89, 90 and 91 of the Investment Law, and does not impact any of the IIAs (BITs/MITs) concluded by Egypt. In addition to what the previous decrees had traditionally provided regarding overseeing the proceedings, offering support, reviewing the cases and engaging in settlements, the Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 expands the scope of the Committee to cover the following: (1) arbitrations involving companies in which the State participates in any form (i.e., no minimum shareholding threshold, which could be practically problematic for JV companies in which the State or its organs hold very minimal stake and do not control); (2) endorsing the nomination of arbitrators, counsel, experts appointed/instructed by the State; (3) opining on state contracts including an arbitration clause, or other contracts concluded with foreign investors, such that the Committee will draft the clauses of such contracts (arbitration, force majeure, change in law, etc.) to achieve a balanced equilibrium, as expressly stated in Art. 2(2) of the Decree; (4) endorsing the budgets for the cases involving the State (fees, costs and expenses); and (5) any other matters entrusted to the Committee by the President or the Prime Minister. Art. 6 of the Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 instructs state organs, public entities, state-owned companies and companies in which the State participates in any form not to conclude any contracts with foreign investors or enter into contracts including an arbitration clause, and/or amending these contracts without having this first reviewed by the Committee is an attempt to streamline practices and approaches to these contracts and to the arbitration clauses contained therein. Lastly, in September 2022, Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022 amended for the second time Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019 by introducing a single amendment,464 Art. 1 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022. which consists in expanding the scope of review of the High Committee for Arbitration and International Disputes. Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022 instructs state organs, public entities, state-owned companies and companies in which the State participates in any form (i.e. no minimum shareholding threshold is required) not to take any measure that would lead to the rescission or the termination of any contract including an arbitration agreement, without having this first reviewed by the Committee. Another important legislative update is worth mentioning, namely the Supreme Constitutional Court’s Law No. 48 of 1979, which was amended on 15 August 2021 by Law No. 137 of 2021. The amendments introduced by Law No. 137 of 2021 extend the Supreme Constitutional Court rationae materiae jurisdiction when reviewing the constitutionality of decisions/judgments rendered against the Egyptian State, when enforcement is sought in Egypt.465 Art. 27 bis of Law No. 137 of 2021 amending the Supreme Constitutional Court Law No. 48 of 1979. Furthermore, the Prime Minister may request the Supreme Constitutional Court to disregard any decision or judgment rendered by an international organization or by a foreign court, and the obligations deriving therefrom, when enforcement is sought against the Egyptian State in Egypt, and the Supreme Constitutional Court shall promptly rule on said request.466 Art. 33 bis of Law No. 137 of 2021 amending the Supreme Constitutional Court Law No. 48 of 1979. The request shall indicate (1) the alleged violated constitutional provision or constitutional court judgment; and (2) the nature of said violation.467 Ibid. More precisely, the Supreme Constitutional Court is now vested with the authority to review the constitutionality of (1) decisions rendered by international organizations; and (2) foreign court judgments where enforcement is sought against the Egyptian State in Egypt.468 Art. 27 bis of Law No. 137 of 2021 amending the Supreme Constitutional Court Law No. 48 of 1979. Prior to its enactment, an earlier draft of Law No. 137 of 2021 had included the review of arbitral awards within the extended scope of review vested in the Supreme Constitutional Court. However, this reference to arbitral awards was not included in the final version of the Law as adopted by the Egyptian Parliament and published in the Official Gazette, such that the Supreme Constitutional Court will not have jurisdiction to review arbitral awards, which remain subject to the traditional review regimes under the EAL and any pertinent applicable international treaties.

plain_text:
CHAPTER I: INTRODUCTION 1. Law on Arbitration a. Legislation The Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I) was enacted on 18 April 1994 and is based on the 1985 UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration (the “Model Law”), with some variations. The differences between the EAL and the UNCITRAL Model Law, as amended in 2006, consist in the following: the applicability of the EAL to both domestic and international arbitrations;1 Art. 1 of the EAL. the possible extraterritorial application of the EAL to proceedings seated abroad only if the parties have agreed to such extraterritorial application;2 Ibid. the requirement that an arbitration agreement in an administrative contract is approved by the competent minister or whoever assumes his or her authority with respect to public entities, and delegation in this regard is prohibited;3 Ibid. the EAL introduces several criteria for the establishment of the international nature of an arbitration including, amongst others, whether the arbitration is institutional, whether it involves parties whose principal places of business are in different states or alternatively if the place of the arbitration determined by the arbitration agreement, the place of performance of the obligations or the place with the closest connection to the dispute is abroad;4 Art. 3 of the EAL. the EAL does not expressly include the possibility to enter into an arbitration agreement by way of electronic means. However, it does not exclude it and therefore nothing prohibits the conclusion of arbitration agreements by electronic means and insofar as the electronic communication fulfils the requirement of writing, the arbitration agreement shall be valid. In brief, the writing requirement under the EAL is a condition for the validity of the arbitration agreement and is not simply a mere evidentiary requirement. According to the EAL, an agreement is in writing if it is contained in a document signed by the parties or contained in an exchange of letters, telegrams or other means of communication. Absence of an arbitration agreement in writing results in the nullity of the arbitration agreement and the writing requirement under the arbitration law is stricter than the one under the UNCITRAL Model Law;5 Art. 12 of the EAL. in the case of incorporation by reference, the reference to the arbitration agreement must be explicit in order for the arbitration agreement to form an integral part of the main contract;6 Art. 10 of the EAL. the EAL does not provide for the “referral exception” whereby a state court may accept to decide over jurisdiction if it finds that the arbitration agreement is null and void, inoperative or incapable of being performed.7 Art. 13 of the EAL. However, in practice, some Egyptian courts have considered the validity and operability of the arbitration agreement before an arbitral tribunal rendered its award; the EAL requires an odd number of arbitrators for purposes of constitution of the arbitral tribunal, the violation of which leads to the nullity of the award;8 Art. 15 of the EAL. the threshold used by the EAL for the challenge of arbitrators is relatively higher than the UNCITRAL Model Law counterpart; the doubts as to the arbitrator’s impartiality and independence must be serious;9 Art. 18 of the EAL. a preliminary arbitral award on jurisdiction cannot be the subject of a court review prior to the tribunal’s rendering of the final award deciding on the entire dispute which must be rendered for purposes of the competent court’s review or annulment;10 Art. 22 of the EAL. the arbitral tribunal may only issue orders for interim relief if the parties bestow this power upon it,11 Art. 24 of the EAL. however the EAL does not provide for a list of the types of relief available to arbitrators or any further details in relation thereto; if the parties do not agree on the language of the arbitration, the latter shall be conducted in Arabic;12 Art. 29 of the EAL. if the parties do not agree on the applicable law, the arbitral tribunal may apply the law having the closest connection to the dispute;13 Art. 39 of the EAL. the EAL adds a ground for annulment based on the non-application by the arbitral tribunal of the lex causae chosen by the parties;14 Art. 53 of the EAL. and the EAL introduces a further condition for purposes of exequatur (leave for enforcement) that is not listed in the UNCITRAL Model Law, namely: the award does not contradict a prior judgment rendered by the Egyptian courts on the merits of the dispute.15 Art. 58 of the EAL. As to the proposals for a reform of the EAL, there are ongoing discussions for reform and possible foreseeable amendments to the EAL. A committee in charge of discussing such possible amendments to the EAL was established by virtue of Decree No. 8 of 2022 of the Deputy Minister of Justice for Arbitration and International Disputes issued on 22 March 2022. The said committee comprises members of the Ministry of Justice Arbitration and International Disputes’ department in addition to named Egyptian arbitration practitioners. The scope of the amendments is limited, and the committee’s work is still ongoing. b. Mandatory provisions Many of the procedural rules governing the conduct of the proceedings are not mandatory and the parties may derogate from by agreement. However, certain rules qualify as mandatory and this includes the following: the arbitration agreement in an administrative contract must be approved by the competent minister or whoever assumes his/her authority with respect to public entities, and delegation in this regard is prohibited;16 Art. 1 of the EAL; see also State Council Challenge No. 8256 of JY 56, Hearing session dated 5 March 2016, where the State Council ruled that the arbitration agreement is void when the competent minister, or whoever assumes his/her authority with respect to public entities, has not approved it and that such requirement is a matter of public policy. the form of the arbitration agreement, which includes the writing requirement of the arbitration agreement,17 Art. 12 of the EAL. as well as when the arbitration agreement is concluded after the dispute has arisen, must indicate the issues subject to arbitration, otherwise the arbitration agreement is null and void;18 Art. 10(2) of the EAL; see also State Council Challenge No. 8256 of JY 56, Hearing session dated 5 March 2016, where the State Council ruled that in the case of a submission agreement, the parties must identify the dispute subjected to the arbitral proceedings or the agreement would be null and void. non-arbitrability of disputes that cannot be subject to a compromise;19 Art. 11 of the EAL. arbitral tribunals must be constituted from an odd number of arbitrators;20 Art. 15(2) of the EAL. the same arbitrator cannot be challenged by the same party more than once in the same arbitration proceedings;21 Art. 19(2) of the EAL. equality between the parties and rights of defence;22 Art. 26 of the EAL. witnesses and experts may not be heard under oath;23 Art. 33(4) of the EAL. arbitral awards may not be challenged by any means of recourse;24 Art. 52(1) of the EAL. and parties may not agree to exclude the right to apply for setting aside of an award prior to the rendering of the said award.25 Art. 54(1) of the EAL. 2. practice of arbitration a. General Arbitration is the prominent mechanism for the settlement of investment and commercial disputes in Egypt. With the growing number of investors in the country and the parties to commercial transactions ultimately resorting to arbitration, Egypt adopts, by the year, measures and reforms that aim at aligning Egypt with best practices in international arbitration. By enacting the EAL, Egypt took a colossal step towards supporting arbitration and becoming an arbitration-friendly jurisdiction. b. Arbitral institutions CRCICA The leading arbitral institution in Egypt is the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”), which is an independent non-profit international organization established in 1979 under the auspices of the Asian African Legal Consultative Organisation. CRCICA administers domestic and international arbitral proceedings as well as other forms of Alternative Dispute Resolution (“ADR”) such as mediation, conciliation, mini-trials and dispute boards. CRCICA promotes arbitration and other ADR techniques in the Afro-Asian region through organizing international conferences and seminars as well as training programs and workshops in cooperation with other international institutions, and provides the necessary technical and administrative assistance at the request of the parties to ad hoc arbitration. CRCICA also acts as an appointing authority and generally adopts the “list procedure” in making its appointments. CRCICA can be contacted at: 1 Al-Saleh Ayoub Street 11211 Zamalek, Cairo Egypt Telephone: +20 2 2735 1333/5/7 Facsimile: +20 2 2735 1336 E-mail: info@crcica.org Website: <https://crcica.org> CRCICA has its own set of arbitration, mediation and dispute board rules. Firstly, with regard to arbitration, since its establishment CRCICA has based its rules on the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules (1976) with minor modifications. The Arbitration Rules have been amended several times, namely in 1998, 2000, 2002, 2007, 2011, and most recently in 2024. The latest version of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules came into force on 15 January 2024 and is available in Arabic, English and French.26 The CRCICA Arbitration Rules are available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/arbitration/crcica-arbitration-rules/>. In this regard, it is worth noting that the CRCICA Arbitration Rules were only available in Arabic and English until 2017, when CRCICA published the French version of its 2011 Arbitration Rules, aiming to reach francophone users and encourage them to choose CRCICA as the arbitral institution administering their dispute in the French language. The new 2024 Arbitration Rules introduce new provisions addressing, inter alia, consolidation, multiparty and multiple contracts arbitrations, third-party funding, emergency arbitrator and expedited procedures. Secondly, as to mediation, CRCICA’s Mediation Rules were issued in March 1990 and were amended in 2001 and 2013. The latest version of the CRCICA Mediation Rules came into force on 1 January 2013, and are available in Arabic and English. Furthermore, it is worth noting that in August 2001, the Mediation and ADR Centre was inaugurated as a branch of CRCICA to administer mediation and other ADR such as conciliation, technical expertise, mini-trials and claim review boards.27 The CRCICA Mediation Rules are available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/mediation/crcica-mediation-rules/>. Thirdly, CRCICA adopted its first Dispute Board Rules on 1 August 2021, offering the international business and construction community a cost-effective and impartial dispute resolution method. These rules were drafted by experts in the field guided by existing institutional rules, and are available in Arabic, English and French.28 .The CRCICA Dispute Board Rules are available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/crcica-dispute-board-rules/>. According to the statistics published by CRCICA,29 See CRCICA Newsletter 2022 available at: <https://crcica.org/news/caseload-2022-number-of-cases-registered-remains-steady/>. the total number of cases filed with CRCICA as at 31 December 2022 was 1618 (domestic and international). In 2022, the total number of cases filed was eighty-three cases, among which were twelve ad hoc proceedings; five mediations referred to CRCICA; and three dispute board cases were registered, equal to the number of dispute board cases registered in 2021, following the issuance of the Dispute Board Rules on 1 August 2021. CRCICA’s caseload in 2022 involved disputes relating to various sectors including construction (17% of the total number of cases); tourism and hospitality (14.5% of the total number of cases); banking, finance and real estate development disputes (8.5% of the total number of cases); oil and gas (7% of the total number of cases); corporate restructuring (6% of the total number of cases); media and entertainment (6% of the total number of cases); sports (5% of the total number of cases); medical and hospital (5% of the total number of cases); and retail (5% of the total number of cases). Also, 2022 witnessed the appointment of 163 arbitrators (149 Egyptians and fourteen non-Egyptians, from France, UK, Lebanon, Tunisia, Belgium, Italy and South Africa), among which four were female arbitrators, representing 2.5% of appointments, compared to twelve female arbitrators appointed in 2021, representing 7% of appointments; and fifteen arbitrators were under the age of forty, representing 9% of appointments in 2022, which equals the same number and percentage of arbitrators under forty appointed in 2021. Among the eighty-three cases filed in 2022, twenty cases (representing 24.5%) were conducted in English whilst sixty-two cases were conducted in Arabic (representing 74.5%) and one case was conducted in French (representing 1%), which was registered in January 2022, constituting the first arbitration case registered in French since the issuance of the French version of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules in 2017. Throughout 2022, 129 hearings were held at CRCICA (eighty-nine related to CRCICA cases; thirty related to ad hoc proceedings; eight related to non-CRCICA proceedings, including ICC proceedings; and two related to mediation), among which fifteen hearings (representing 12%) were held remotely, three hearings (representing 2%) were held in a hybrid format (physical and remote attendance), and the remaining 111 hearings (representing 86%) were held with physical attendance of a limited number of persons in compliance with CRCICA’s social distancing guidelines adopted since the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020.30 Ibid. In 2021, following the COVID-19 pandemic and during the gradual shift to a normal pre-pandemic work environment, more hearings were held remotely and in a hybrid format than in 2022, where most hearings were held with physical attendance. Throughout 2021, 131 hearings were held at CRCICA, among which thirty-eight hearings were held entirely remotely, seventeen hearings were held in a hybrid format and the remaining thirty-eight hearings were held with physical attendance of a limited number of persons in compliance with CRCICA’s social distancing guidelines adopted in 2020.31 See CRCICA Annual Report 2021-2022 available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/Annual-Report-2021-2022.pdf>. In this context it is worth mentioning that in 2020, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, CRCICA had adopted some measures and adapted some of its services by offering guidance notes to arbitration users to ensure smooth administration of ongoing proceedings and the filing of new cases. CRCICA strongly recommended and encouraged arbitration users to file notices of arbitrations, written submissions and exhibits online via email, and also, to the extent possible, hold hearings online. In this respect, physical hearings at CRCICA’s premises were suspended during the period of closure of CRCICA’s premises (from 20 March to 30 May 2020). Furthermore, it is also worthy of note that CRCICA entered into an agreement with the International Court of Arbitration for Sport (“ICAS”) in 2012, nominating it as a host of an Alternative Hearing Centre (“AHC”) for the Court of Arbitration for Sport (“CAS”) based in Switzerland. In May 2017, CRCICA signed the Belt and Road Arbitration Initiative Cooperation Agreement with the Beijing Arbitration Commission/Beijing International Arbitration Centre (“BAC/BIAC”) and the Kuala Lumpur Regional Centre for Arbitration (“KLRCA”). In December 2017, CRCICA also signed a cooperation agreement with the Permanent Court of Arbitration (“PCA”) during the conference celebrating the 50th anniversary of UNCITRAL: Global Inclusion and Dispute Resolution: Harmonizing Trends in International Arbitration, which enables PCA hearings to take place at CRCICA’s premises and vice versa. In 2019, CRCICA signed three cooperation agreements, respectively with (1) the Nairobi Centre for International Arbitration (“NCIA”) in August 2019; (2) the Lagos Court of Arbitration (“LCA”) in September 2019; and (3) the Abu Dhabi Global Market (“ADGM”) on 30 September 2019. The cooperation agreements with NCIA and LCA involve, inter alia, promoting and developing arbitration techniques through joint capacity-building events, cooperation in the achievement of the goals of the African Arbitration Association (“AfAA”), and that NCIA and LCA shall be alternative hearing centres of CRCICA in Kenya and Nigeria and vice versa. Similarly, the ADGM Arbitration Centre (“ADGMAC”) shall be CRCICA’s alternative hearing centre for its arbitrations and mediations to be conducted in the United Arab Emirates. Furthermore, the ADGM can be chosen as the seat of arbitration for cases administered under the CRCICA Rules. More recently, on 29 May 2020, CRCICA concluded a Memorandum of Understanding (“MoU”) with the China Guangzhou Arbitration Commission (“GZAC”), an online arbitration institution founded in response to the rapid growth of electronic business in China, with focus on mutual cooperation to promote online arbitration. The scope of this MoU includes sharing information, organizing joint arbitration and mediation activities, and mutual recommendation of arbitrators. Other arbitral institutions In addition to CRCICA, there is a specialized arbitration centre within the Financial Regulatory Authority named the Egyptian Center for Arbitration and Settlement of Non-Banking Financial Disputes (“ECAS”),32 The official website for the Egyptian Center for Arbitration and Settlement of Non-Banking Financial Disputes is available at: <https://ecas.org.eg/en>. established by virtue of the Presidential Decree No. 335 of 2019. ECAS offers conciliation, mediation and arbitration services. The members of the Board of Trustees have been appointed by virtue of the Financial Regulatory Authority Decision No. 133 of 2019. The statutes of ECAS as well as its arbitration and mediation rules were issued by virtue of the Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2597 of 2020 on 10 December 2020. 3. Bibliography a. Books Aboul-Enien, Mohamed Peaceful Settlement of Commercial Disputes, Commercial Arbitration and other ADR Techniques (CRCICA 2005) (in English) Abou el Wafa, Ahmed Arbitration in Arab Laws (Maktabet Al Wafaa Al Qanouneya 2015) (in Arabic) Allam El Din, Mohi El Din CRCICA Awards, CRCICA (2002) (in Arabic) Al Qalyoubi, Samiha Legal Foundations for Commercial Arbitration in accordance with Law No. 27 of 1994 (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya, 2012) (in Arabic) Barakat, Ali Challenge of Arbitral Awards (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2003) (in Arabic) Barakat, Ali The Control of the Constitutionality of Arbitration Law Provisions (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2003) (in Arabic) Berreri, Mokhtar Commercial International Arbitration (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2004) (in Arabic) El Rifaie, Ashraf Abdel Alim The Law Applicable to the Subject Matter of the Dispute (Dar Al Fikr Al Game’ee 2003) (in Arabic) El Rifaie, Ashraf Abdel Alim The Arbitration Agreement (Dar Al Fikr Al Game’ee 2003) (in Arabic) El Sharkawy, Samir International Commercial Arbitration (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 2011) (in Arabic) Hadad, Hamza Arbitration in Arab Laws (Dar Al Sakafa Lel Nashr wa Al Tawzī’ 2007) (in Arabic) Ibrahim, Ibrahim Ahmed Private International Arbitration, 4th ed. (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya) (in Arabic) Mabrouk, Ashour Legal Regime for the Enforcement of Arbitral Awards (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya, 2002) (in Arabic) Rachid, Samia Arbitration in Private International Relationships (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya, 1984) (in Arabic) Salama, Ahmed Abdel Karim Commercial International and Domestic Arbitration Law (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya, 2004) (in Arabic) Saleh, Samir Commercial Arbitration in the Arab Middle East: Shari'a, Lebanon, Syria and Egypt (Hart Publishing 2006) (in English) Shafik, Mohsen International Commercial Arbitration (Dar Al Nahda Al Arabiya 1997) (in Arabic) Waly, Fathi Arbitration in Domestic and International Commercial Disputes in Theory and Practice (Munsha’at Al Ma’aref 2014) (in Arabic) b. Articles Abdel Wahab, Mohamed “Investment Arbitration: An Update on Egypt” in J. Fouret (ed.) Enforcement of Investment Treaty Arbitration Awards: A Global Guide (Globe Business Publishing 2021) “The Egyptian Court of Cassation Sets Standards and Affirms Arbitration-Friendly Principles and Trends in a Ground-Breaking Judgment”, Kluwer Arbitration Blog (22 December 2020) <http://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2020/12/22/the-egyptian-court-of-cassation-sets-standards-and-affirms-arbitration-friendly-principles-and-trends-in-a-ground-breaking-judgment/> (in English) “Supreme Court Strikes a Chord: Establishing Support to Credible Institutional Arbitration”, ICC Bulletin (2020, no. 1) pp. 12-16 (in English) “What if parties don’t agree on a virtual hearing? A pandemic pathway by Mohamed S. Abdel Wahab”, Arbitration News, Global Arbitration Review (6 May 2020) (in English) “‘The “Deemed’ Internationalisation of Arbitration under Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 – Considerations Beyond Hope and Fear”, 3 BCDR International Arbitration Review (2016, no. 1) pp. 47-64 (in English) “Extension of Arbitration Agreements to Third Parties 2.0: Deconstruction, Evolution and Reconsideration”, Chapter 2, Conflict of Laws in International Commercial Arbitration, 2019 (in English) “The Nuts and Bolts of Construction Arbitration in the MENA: an Update”, in Part IV – Regional Construction Arbitration, The Guide to Construction Arbitration, second edition (2018) pp. 292-313 (in English) Aboul-Enein, Mohamed “Reflections on the New Egyptian Law on Arbitration”, 11 Arbitration International (1995, no. 1) pp. 75-84 (in English) El Ahdab, Abdel Hamid “Enforcement of Arbitral Awards in the Arab countries”, 11 Arbitration International (1995, no. 2) pp. 169-182 (in English) “The New Egyptian Arbitration Act in Civil and Commercial Matters”, 12 Journal of International Arbitration (1995, no. 2) pp. 65-102 (in English) El Kosheri, Ahmed Sadek “Main Characteristics of the International Arbitration Case Law Rendered by Egyptian Courts”, 3 BCDR International Arbitration Review (2016, no. 1) pp. 5-22 (in English) El Sharkawy, Samir “Different Forms of Arbitration in Investment Disputes in the Arab World”, 8 Journal of Arab Arbitration (August 2005) pp.19-33 (in Arabic). Mahmoud, Ahmed Sedki “Conservatory Measures Necessary to Resolve Arbitration Proceedings”, 8 Journal of Arab Arbitration (August 2005) p. 64 (in Arabic). c. Journals and periodicals The Journal of Arab Arbitration. (in Arabic) The World Journal of Arbitration, El Ahdab ed. (in Arabic) International Journal of Arab Arbitration, El Ahdab ed. (in English) BCDR International Arbitration Review, N. Ziadé ed. (in English) d. Publication of awards Excerpts of arbitral awards may be published in the above-cited journals, and the CRCICA has published eight volumes of excerpts of its arbitral awards in Arabic and four volumes of its arbitral awards in English, while maintaining confidentiality of these awards.33 CRCICA official website, available at <https://crcica.org/crcica-arbitral-awards/>.

CHAPTER II: ARBITRATION AGREEMENT 1. Form and Contents of the Agreement a. Arbitration clause and submission agreement The Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I hereto) defines the arbitration agreement as “an agreement by which the two parties agree to submit to arbitration in order to resolve all or certain disputes which have arisen or which may arise between them in connection with a defined legal relationship, whether contractual or not”.34 Art. 10(1) of the EAL. According to the EAL, an arbitration agreement may be concluded prior to the existence of the dispute or after it has arisen. In this respect, Art. 10(2) of the EAL specifically provides that arbitration clauses which are concluded prior to the existence of the dispute can be “either in the form of a separate agreement or as a clause in a given contract”, and its scope may extend to all or certain disputes which may arise between the parties, provided that the subject matter of the dispute must be determined in the statement of claim when submitted.35 Art. 10(2) of the EAL. Whereas submission agreements, i.e., arbitration agreements that are concluded after the dispute has arisen, may be concluded even after an action has already been brought before the judiciary, the arbitration agreement must indicate the issues subject to arbitration, otherwise such agreement would be null and void.36 Ibid. The mandatory requirement to identify the issues subject to arbitration in submission agreements has been confirmed through Egyptian court decisions. In this regard, the Cairo Court of Appeal has set aside an arbitral award on the ground that the arbitration agreement failed to specify the subject matter of the dispute to be settled by arbitration and was accordingly invalid.37 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 28 of JY 124, Hearing session dated 20 November 2007. In the same vein, in administrative contracts, the State Council confirmed that in the case of a submission agreement, the parties must identify the dispute subjected to the arbitral proceedings or the agreement would be null and void.38 State Council, Challenge No. 8256 of JY 56, Hearing session dated 5 March 2016. b. Form requirements Whether being an arbitration clause (in the form of a separate arbitration agreement or included in a given contract) or a submission agreement, the validity requirements of an arbitration agreement under the EAL are the following: the parties must have capacity to enter into the arbitration agreement39 Art. 11 of the EAL. and their consent to arbitrate must not tainted by any defects in consent, i.e., error, duress or misrepresentation; the subject matter of the arbitration must be arbitrable;40 Ibid. the subject of the dispute to be resolved by arbitration must be specified in the submission agreement, or in the statement of claim in case of a prior agreement to arbitrate;41 Art. 10(2) of the EAL. and the arbitration agreement must be in writing or else it is null. The writing requirement includes a document signed by the parties, an agreement by exchange of correspondences (letters and telegrams) or other means of communication,42 Art. 12 of the EAL. and/or when the parties subject their legal relationship to the provisions of a model contract, an international convention or another document containing provisions related to arbitration,43 Art. 6 of the EAL. and/or an incorporation into the contract by reference to a document containing an arbitration agreement insofar as the reference is explicit in considering the arbitration agreement part of the parties’ contract.44 Art. 10(3) of the EAL. However, it is worth noting that Art. 750(4) of the Egyptian Civil Code expressly provides that the arbitration agreement included in the general conditions of an insurance policy is null and void. Furthermore, as to the writing requirement, it is worth noting that the EAL does not expressly include the possibility to enter into an arbitration agreement by way of electronic means. However, it does not exclude it and therefore nothing prohibits the conclusion of arbitration agreements by electronic means and insofar as the electronic communication fulfils the requirement of writing, the arbitration agreement shall be valid. c. Model arbitration clause The Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”) proposes the following three model arbitration clauses:45 The CRCICA model arbitration clauses are available at: <https://crcica.org/arbitration/model-clauses/>. CRCICA Model Arbitration Clause for Future Disputes “For contracting parties who wish to have future disputes referred to arbitration under the CRCICA Arbitration Rules, the following clause is recommended: Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this contract, including but not limited to its interpretation, execution, the termination or invalidity thereof, shall be settled by arbitration in accordance with the Arbitration Rules of the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration. Note: Parties should consider adding: a. The number of arbitrators shall be …(1) b. The place of arbitration shall be … (city and country); and c. The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be… Note: Parties may consider adding: – The time limit within which the arbitral tribunal shall make its final award shall be… ; – The governing law of the arbitration agreement; and – The governing law of the contract [is/shall be] the substantive law of (country or state).” (1) State an odd number, such as one or three. CRCICA Model Arbitration Agreement for Existing Disputes “If a dispute has arisen, but there is no agreement between the parties to arbitrate, they may find an agreement to resolve their dispute by arbitration as follows: “The undersigned parties hereby agree to submit their dispute arising out of [name/date of the Contract, if any] and relating to [the description of the disputed issues], to final determination by arbitration in accordance with the Arbitration Rules of the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration. Note: Parties should consider adding: a. The number of arbitrators shall be …(2) b. The place of arbitration shall be [city and country]; and c. The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be … Note: Parties may consider adding: – The time limit within which the arbitral tribunal shall make its final award shall be… ; – The governing law of the arbitration agreement; and – The governing law of the contract [is/shall be] the substantive law of … ” (2) State an odd number, such as one or three. CRCICA Model Arbitration Clause under CRCICA Expedited Arbitration Rules “Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this contract, including but not limited to interpretation, execution, termination or invalidity thereof, shall be settled by arbitration in accordance with the CRCICA Expedited Arbitration Rules. Note: Parties should consider adding: a. The place of arbitration shall be [city and country]; and b.
