Categories of interim measures – tribunal The EAL grants the arbitral tribunal the right to award provisional or interim relief, upon the request of a party, and only if the parties have agreed to confer such power upon the arbitral tribunal.209 Art. 24(1) of the EAL. It is also acknowledged that such power could be conferred upon the arbitral tribunal by agreeing to the application of institutional rules that provide for such default power. The EAL does not provide a list of the types of relief available to arbitrators, but it is generally accepted that an arbitral tribunal, if the parties so agree, has the discretion to order any type of interim relief or provisional measures that are warranted provided that such relief is available under the applicable law to such relief. In this regard, scholars refer to the general rules of the Egyptian Code of Civil and Commercial Procedures (“CCCP”) regarding provisional measures210 Ahmed Sedki Mahmoud, “Conservatory Measures Necessary to Resolve Arbitration Proceedings”, 8 Journal of Arab Arbitration (August 2005) p. 64. as set out in Art. 45 of the CCCP. Pursuant to Art. 45 of the CCCP, interim measures will not be ordered unless the following conditions are satisfied: (1) there is an imminent danger or risk of substantial prejudice to the right of the requesting party; (2) delay to the final decision may prejudice the right or the legal status that the measure aims to protect; and (3) the required measure should not have an impact on the outcome or the merits of the dispute.211 Ahmed Abdel Karim Salama, op. cit., pp. 932-933. For instance in case of perishable goods, the arbitral tribunal may issue an order to preserve these goods, such as permitting the sale of these goods in the market before they perish.212 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 511. An arbitral tribunal may also order to stop the liquidation of a letter of guarantee.213 Ibid. However, arbitral tribunals cannot order an attachment of property or a conservatory seizure of movables/goods owned by the debtor or of the debtor’s property held by a third party, or even order forced execution, such that only courts have the authority to issue such orders.214 Ibid. The coercive powers of arbitrators differ than those of judges, as arbitrators lack the imperium to enforce their orders, which would need to be enforced by a national judge, if it is not complied with by the party. In this respect, the EAL expressly stipulates that in the event of non-compliance with the interim measure ordered by the arbitral tribunal, the other party may request the competent court, specified in Art. 9 of the EAL, to issue an execution order, or it may request the arbitral tribunal to allow it to undertake the necessary procedure for the execution of the order.215 Art. 24(2) of the EAL. The EAL also states that an arbitral tribunal is entitled to request adequate security for the costs incurred in securing the interim relief.216 Art. 24(1) of the EAL. Similarly, national courts are free to decide on whatever security is required to ensure the implementation of an order for interim or provisional measures. Alternatively, a party may directly seek to obtain interim relief directly from the competent court, such that the EAL allows national courts to issue interim and conservatory measures, upon the request of one of the parties, before commencing the arbitration proceedings or during said proceedings.217 Art. 14 of the EAL. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules contain detailed provisions regarding interim measures. They provide a definition for the expression “interim measures”218 Art. 26(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). and set forth the criteria that must be satisfied for such measures to be granted.219 Art. 26(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The arbitral tribunal is empowered to order such measures at the request of any of the parties,220 Art. 26(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). subject to security for costs if appropriate.221 Art. 26(6) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The tribunal may order the party requesting an interim measure to pay damages to the other party, at any time during the proceedings, if it later determines that the interim measure should have not been granted, at the circumstances prevailing at the time of granting such measure.222 Art. 26(8) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). It may also, upon the request of a party or, in exceptional cases, upon its own initiative, modify, suspend or terminate an interim measure previously granted.223 Art. 26(5) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). To this end, the arbitral tribunal may request any of the parties to disclose material changes to the circumstances on the basis of which the interim measure was granted.224 Art. 26(7) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). Finally, the Rules expressly stipulate that an application to the courts for interim measures does not constitute a waiver of the arbitration agreement.225 Art. 26(9) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). b. Comparison with 2006 Model Law The EAL has not adopted Chapter IV.A of the UNCITRAL Model Law (2006) regarding interim measures, as it was enacted prior to the UNCITRAL Model Law amendments in 2006. c. Form Under the EAL, arbitral tribunals may award interim relief by issuing an interim award,226 Art. 42 of the EAL. which makes it subject to the ordinary procedures for the enforcement and recognition of arbitral awards. Nonetheless, interim awards do not have res judicata effect, thus, enforcement of interim awards is not common. However, in 2017, the president of the Cairo Court of Appeal, in an ex parte proceedings, enforced, for the first time, an interim decision rendered by an ICC arbitral tribunal seated in Paris.227 Cairo Court of Appeal, Arbitration Orders, Ordinance No. 39 of JY 134, dated 8 November 2017. This enforcement order has been affirmed by the Cairo Court of Appeal in an adversarial proceeding.228 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 44 of JY 134, Hearing session dated 9 May 2018. d. Categories of interim measures – court The EAL expressly allows national courts to issue interim and conservatory measures, upon the request of one of the parties, before commencing the arbitration proceedings or during said proceedings, such that this cannot be considered as a waiver of the arbitration agreement.229 Art. 14 of the EAL. In this respect, national court enjoy a wider authority to order any type of interim measures unlike arbitral tribunals. A national court has the authority to order attachment of property or forced execution. Furthermore, in the event of non-compliance with the interim measure ordered by the arbitral tribunal and upon the request of the other party, the competent national court, specified in Art. 9 of the EAL, may issue an execution order of the interim measure.230 Art. 24(2) of the EAL. 6. Representation and Legal Assistance The EAL is silent on this matter. However, the Advocacy Law No. 17 of 1983 expressly stipulates that advocacy work is exclusively reserved to lawyers, such that non-lawyers cannot exercise any advocacy related work as listed in Art. 3 of the Advocacy Law, and which includes representing parties before arbitral tribunals.231 Art. 3 of the Advocacy Law No. 17 of 1983. In this regard, for a lawyer to represent a party before an arbitral tribunal, a special power of attorney is required and shall expressly mention that said lawyer shall represent the party in arbitration or be more specific and refer whether it is institutional (name of the institution) or ad hoc. The power of attorney may also specify the acts to be undertaken by the lawyer such as signing arbitration agreements, drafting memoranda, attending hearings, filing nullity actions, etc. Doctrinal views confirm the above-mentioned and adds that in arbitration seated in Egypt, only (1) lawyers admitted at the Egyptian Bar, or (2) Arab lawyers who may participate with Egyptian lawyers to appear in hearings before Egyptian courts, subject to judicial cooperation treaties concluded with Arab countries, may represent parties before arbitral tribunals. In this respect, foreign lawyers cannot represent parties in arbitrations seated in Egypt even if the concerned party is a foreign party.232 Fathi Waly, op. cit., pp. 432-433. However, there exist exceptions, such that in international commercial arbitration, a lawyer that is not admitted to the Egyptian Bar, may represent a party in the arbitral proceedings, given that such representation does not constitute a violation to Egyptian public policy. This is based on the principle of party autonomy in international arbitration. Another exception consists in the parties’ choice of the institutional arbitration rules which does not require representation of parties in arbitration by a lawyer, such as the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules,233 Art. 5 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024) provides that the parties may be represented or assisted by any person of their choice. and the ICC Arbitration Rules.234 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 433. A recent decision issued by the Egyptian Court of Cassation ended the debate on party representation and determined that there are no limitations or restrictions on party representation in arbitrations seated in Egypt. The Court of Cassation recognized that the parties to an arbitration, be it domestic or international proceedings, may be represented by any person of their choice, whether being a lawyer, Egyptian or foreigner, or a non-lawyer.235 Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020. 7. Default If the claimant fails to submit its written statement of claim, without a valid cause, the arbitral tribunal shall terminate the arbitral proceedings, unless otherwise agreed by the parties (Art. 34(1) of the EAL). However, if the respondent fails to submit its statement of defence, the EAL enables the arbitral tribunal to continue with the proceedings, without treating such failure as an admission by the respondent of the claimant’s allegations, unless otherwise agreed by the parties.236 Art. 34(2) of the EAL. Furthermore, if a party fails to appear at a hearing or submit a required document, the arbitral tribunal may continue with the proceedings and to render the arbitral award based on the submitted elements of evidence.237 Art. 35 of the EAL. If the submitted evidence is not sufficient for the arbitral tribunal to make an award, the proceedings may be terminated by a decision of the arbitral tribunal.238 Art. 48(1) of the EAL. Anyhow, it should be noted that a non-participating or absent party should be duly notified of all the documents submitted and orders issued, and must be given a proper and adequate opportunity to present its case and defences at every stage of the proceedings. National courts cannot compel participation of a party who failed to participate in the arbitration. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules contain similar provisions. In the event the claimant fails to submit its statement of claim, without showing sufficient cause, the arbitral tribunal shall issue an order for termination of the proceedings, unless there are remaining matters that need to be decided.239 Art. 30(1)(a) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). In the event the respondent fails to submit its response to the notice of arbitration or its statement of defence, without showing sufficient cause, the arbitral tribunal shall continue the arbitral proceedings, without treating such failure to submit as an admission of claimant’s allegations (the same applies to the claimant who fails to submit a defence to a counterclaim or a claim for the purpose of a set-off).240 Art. 30(1)(b) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The arbitral tribunal may also proceed with holding the hearing,241 Art. 30(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). if a party fails to appear at the hearing, and render the arbitral award based on the available evidence,242 Art. 30(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). if a party failed to submit documents, exhibits or other evidence, such that the tribunal may draw the necessary inferences from a party’s failure to comply with an order to produce evidence.243 Ibid. 8. Confidentiality of the Award and Proceedings a. Confidentiality of the arbitral award and proceedings Arbitral proceedings are generally confidential and the EAL provides that an arbitral award may not be published, in whole or in part, unless agreed by the parties.244 Art. 44(2) of the EAL. There is an implied duty of confidentiality not only with respect to the arbitral proceedings and documents submitted, but with respect to the award itself which may not be made public without the parties’ consent, unless the award has been subject to nullity or enforcement proceedings, where its content will then fall in the public domain. The principle of confidentiality is confirmed by the Explanatory Note of EAL, which explains that the confidentiality of the arbitration is of significant importance to the parties in order to preserve inter-commercial relations. It is also unchallenged that the evidence produced and the pleadings filed remain confidential. Nevertheless, a party may seek to rely on such evidence and pleadings in other proceedings provided that no privileged or confidential information is disclosed. Any such disclosure requires a court order to that effect or the consent of the concerned parties. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules place upon parties, arbitrators, tribunal-appointed experts, arbitral tribunal secretary and the Centre the obligation to keep confidential all awards, decisions, materials submitted by the parties in the arbitral proceedings, save when a disclosure may be required to pursue a legal right or to enforce or challenge an award before national courts.245 Art. 54(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The deliberations of the arbitral tribunal are also confidential.246 Art. 54(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The CRCICA undertakes not to publish any decision or arbitral award or any part thereof or reveal the identity of any of the parties, without the prior written consent of all parties.247 Art. 54(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). b. Confidentiality of arbitration-related court proceedings Like other court proceedings, arbitration-related court proceedings form part of the public record. The court may, however, hold the hearing in chambers if so requested by a party, for reasons related to public policy or to preserve morals or family secrets.248 Art. 101 of the CCCP. A copy of any court decision may be provided to any person upon payment of a fee.249 Art. 180 of the CCCP.

CHAPTER V: ARBITRAL AWARD 1. Types of Award The Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I hereto) stipulates that arbitral tribunals may issue interim (interlocutory), partial and final awards.250 Art. 42 of the EAL. Interim awards deal with incidental matters, such as the existence or non-existence of a valid arbitration agreement, or lack of arbitrability of the dispute. Partial awards dispose some, but not all, of the issues in dispute. However, they do not terminate the dispute,251 Ibid. as opposed to final awards which do. Situations where partial awards can be issued are not exhaustively defined, but can include: (1) issues of jurisdiction and/or admissibility; (2) claims or counterclaims; (3) the determination of the applicable substantive law; and/or (4) the determination of liability, quantum, interest, costs. Normally, decisions on liability are dealt with in partial or final awards. However, it is possible that certain issues of liability may be dealt with in interim awards such as liability to deliver a site, draw a bond, etc. Nevertheless, it should be noted that interim awards do not have res judicata effect. The enforcement of interim awards is not common given the lack of a res judicata effect. However, in 2017 the president of the Cairo Court of Appeal, in ex parte proceedings for the first time enforced an interim decision rendered by an ICC arbitral tribunal seated in Paris.252 Cairo Court of Appeal, Arbitration Orders, Ordinance No. 39 of JY 134, dated 8 November 2017. This enforcement order has been affirmed by the Cairo Court of Appeal in an adversarial proceeding.253 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 44 of JY 134, Hearing session dated 9 May 2018. A final award must be issued at the end of the arbitral proceedings to terminate the dispute.254 Art. 45(1) of the EAL. Otherwise, if the final award is not rendered within the period agreed upon by the parties or, in absence of agreement between the parties, within the period provided for under the EAL, i.e., eighteen months, the president of the competent court may, upon the request of a party, issue an order either extending the period of time or terminating the arbitral proceedings.255 Art. 45(2) of the EAL. Furthermore, the arbitral tribunal may render a decision terminating the arbitral proceedings in specific cases provided for under the EAL.256 Art. 48(1) of the EAL which reads in pertinent part: “[…] The arbitral proceedings can also be terminated by a decision of the arbitral tribunal in the following cases: a) If the two parties agree on the termination of the proceedings; b) If the claimant withdraws its claim, unless the arbitral tribunal decides, upon request of the respondent, that the latter has a legitimate interest in continuing the arbitral proceedings until the dispute is settled by a final award; c) If for any other reason the arbitral tribunal finds that the continuation of the proceedings has become unnecessary or impossible.” Interim and partial awards cannot be set aside prior to the issue of the final award.257 Court of Cassation, Commercial Circuit, Challenges No. 648 of JY 73 and Nos. 5745, 6467 and 6787 of JY 75, Hearing session dated 13 December 2005; and Cairo Court of Appeal, 7th Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 66 of JY 128, Hearing session dated 6 August 2012. This is confirmed by analogy to the same situation before national courts, whereby Art. 212 of the Egyptian Code of Civil and Commercial Procedures (“CCCP”) provides that decisions rendered during the course of the judicial proceedings and which do not terminate the dispute cannot be subject to appeal until the issuance of the judgment terminating the dispute. 2. Making of the Award a. Decision-making Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, an arbitral tribunal which consists of more than one arbitrator shall render its award by majority following deliberation (Art. 40 of the EAL). All arbitrators shall participate in the deliberation, otherwise the award will be rendered in breach of the fundamental judicial safeguards.258 Cairo Court of Appeal, Appeals Nos. 34 and 35 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 29 January 2003; and Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 47 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 29 June 2003 and Appeals Nos. 34 and 35 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 29 January 2003. The award must be signed by the arbitrators, and signatures of the majority of the arbitrators are sufficient provided that the award states the reason(s) for non-signature by the minority,259 Art. 43(1) of the EAL. otherwise the award runs the risk of being set aside.260 Court of Cassation, Challenges Nos. 88 and 515 of JY 73, Hearing session dated 9 February 2010; and Cairo Court of Appeal, 50 Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 46 of JY 135, Hearing session dated 27 January 2019, whereby the Court of Appeal held that the arbitral award was only signed by the Chairman of the arbitral tribunal without stating the reasons of non-signature of the minority of the arbitrators, which violates Art. 43 of the EAL. Hence, the court annulled the arbitral award because it lacks one of its essential requirements. The majority under Egyptian law is quantitative, so the presiding arbitrator may not solely render the award if no majority is achieved. In a recent judgment, the Court of Cassation rejected the setting aside of an award where the minority arbitrator abstained from signing the award, provided that the following three conditions are met: (i) deliberations have taken place between all the arbitrators, including the arbitrator(s) abstaining from signing the award; (ii) the award is signed by the majority of the arbitrators; and (iii) the award states the reasons for abstaining to sign the award by the abstaining arbitrator(s), only if the latter had not stated the said reasons.261 .Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 8199 of JY 80, Hearing session dated 22 March 2022. The Court explained that deliberations consist in the exchange of views between the arbitrators regarding the facts of the dispute, the claims put forward by the parties and their respective relief sought. The Court further added that issuing dissenting opinions is an undisputed practice in arbitration, which is founded upon the arbitrator’s judicial duty to issue a reasoned award.262 .Ibid. As to institutional arbitration, the Arbitration Rules of the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”) prescribe a majority decision.263 Art. 33(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). b. Time limits The time limit for issuing arbitral awards provided under the EAL normally apply to ad hoc arbitration. In this respect, the EAL stipulates that the arbitral tribunal shall issue the final arbitral award within the time agreed upon by the parties (which includes the parties’ choice of the applicable institutional rules).264 Art. 45(1) of the EAL. However, in absence of an agreement between the parties, the award must be made within twelve months from the date of commencement of the arbitral proceedings,265 Art. 27 of the EAL which reads: “The arbitral proceedings commence on the date on which the respondent receives the request for arbitration from the claimant, unless the two parties agree on another date.” the tribunal may decide to extend the time limit for a maximum of six months, unless the parties agree on a longer period.266 Art. 45(1) of the EAL. In total, the EAL provides for a time limit of eighteen months from the date of commencement of the arbitral proceedings, unless otherwise agreed by the parties. Upon failure by the arbitral tribunal to issue the award within the forgoing time limits, either party may request from the president of the competent court, specified in Art. 9 of the EAL, to issue an order either extending the period of time or terminating the arbitral proceedings. Thereafter, if the court orders the termination of the proceedings, either party may bring the dispute to the court having initial jurisdiction to adjudicate the dispute.267 Art. 45(2) of the EAL. The time limit prescribed by the EAL for the issuance of arbitral awards does not strictly constitute a mandatory rule if the parties agree otherwise. In this respect, the President of the Cairo Court of Appeal refused to issue an order to terminate the arbitral proceedings, when seized by a party upon the expiry of the time limit provided under Art. 45(1) of the EAL requesting the termination order. The President of the Court found that the parties agreed to the application of institutional rules which do not provide for a specific time limit to render the award. Furthermore, the failure of the requesting party to object to the expiry of the time limit prescribed under Art. 45(1) of the EAL before the arbitral tribunal, amounts of its right in this regard as stated under Art. 8 of the EAL.268 President of Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Refusal Ordinance for Cases Nos. 18 & 19 of JY 120, dated 27 October 2003. In the same vein, the Cairo Court of Appeal refused to set aside an arbitral award and added that even when the provisions of the EAL apply, the expiry of the time limit prescribed under Art. 45(1) of the EAL does not constitute a ground for setting aside an arbitral award, as the grounds for annulment of arbitral awards are exhaustively prescribed under Art. 53 of the EAL.269 Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Case No. 55 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 27 February 2005. On another occasion, where a party alleged that the arbitral tribunal failed to render its arbitral award within the time limit agreed upon by the parties, the Cairo Court of Appeal refused to set aside the said arbitral award.270 .Cairo Court of Appeal, 4th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 43 of JY 138, Hearing session dated 26 April 2022. The Court of Appeal held that the arbitral tribunal enjoys the authority to rule over the dispute within the time limit agreed upon with the parties or as determined by the applicable procedural rules, in order to warrant that the length of the arbitral proceedings are not unreasonably extended, which would result in losing one of the main pillars of resorting to arbitration. The Court added that the final award shall be rendered within the agreed time limit unless a force majeure event arises which makes the issuance of the arbitral award in the agreed timing impossible. Therefore, in the event of any force majeure, the time limit for rendering the arbitral award is interrupted, i.e., it stops running, and the arbitration agreement does not become extinct so long as the arbitral tribunal is unable to pursue its mission on deciding and ruling over the dispute. In the case at hand, two arbitrators had caught COVID-19; in this respect, the Cairo Court of Appeal held that the COVID-19 illness of two arbitrators in a panel of three arbitrators constitutes a force majeure event that automatically interrupts the arbitration proceedings, by the force of law.271 .Ibid. c. Dissenting opinions Dissenting opinions are permitted under Egyptian law. Whilst such opinions are not expressly addressed under the EAL, they are not uncommon. Practice in Egypt shows that the submission of such opinions is actually dependent on the arbitral tribunal dynamics, the manner of deliberations and the arbitrator’s perception of his/her role and views on issues in dispute. In a recent judgment, the Court of Cassation held that the issuance of dissenting opinions is an undisputed practice in arbitration, which is founded upon the arbitrator’s judicial duty to issue a reasoned award.272 .Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 8199 of JY 80, Hearing session dated 22 March 2022. Dissenting opinions are generally attached, as a separate document, to the award or included in the award itself. However, they can also be delivered as a separate document that is not necessarily attached to the award. If delivered separately, whether attached to the award or not, dissenting opinions will not form part of the award. 3. Form of the Award Pursuant to the EAL, the arbitral award must: (1) be in writing and signed by the arbitrators, if the minority refused to sign the award, the majority must include the reasons for the minority’s refusal to sign;273 Art. 43(1) of the EAL. (2) be reasoned unless the parties have agreed otherwise, or the applicable procedural law mandates such reasoning;274 Art. 43(2) of the EAL. (3) include the names and addresses of the parties, the names, addresses, nationalities and title of arbitrators, a copy of the arbitration agreement (an explicit citation of the arbitration agreement would suffice), a summary of the parties’ claims, statements, and relevant documents, have an operative part (dispositive) ordering specific remedies, and include the date and place of issuing the award.275 Art. 43(3) of the EAL. The Cairo Court of Appeal has set aside arbitral awards that failed to set out reasons, in the event where the parties did not agree that no reasons should be stated. In this respect, the Court of Appeal held that summary reasons, or “reasons that are not serious”, which include unclear or unjustifiable reasons, or reasons that are not based on true facts, amount to an absence of reasons.276 Cairo Court of Appeal, 63 Commercial Circuit, Case No. 26 of JY 114, Hearing session dated 18 February 1998; and Cairo Court of Appeal, 63 Commercial Circuit, Case No. 2240 of JY 111, Hearing session dated 22 February 1995. The Cairo Court of Appeal has further considered that contradictory reasons, which consist in “vice de fond” (defect in substance), do not amount to an absence of reasons, which consist in “vice de forme” (defect in the form), and therefore contradictory reasons in an arbitral award cannot justify its setting aside.277 Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Case No. 69 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 29 June 2003; Cairo Court of Appeal 91 Commercial Circuit, Case No. 89 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 28 February 2004; and Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Case No. 66 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 26 May 2004. More specifically, arbitral awards can only be set aside if one of the grounds for setting aside prescribed by Art. 53 of the EAL is identified, such that the nullity court shall limit its review to formal requirements. In a recent judgment, the Cairo Court of Appeal refused to set aside an arbitral award on the ground of contradiction of the reasoning of two arbitral awards. The Court held that the requirement for the award to be reasoned is not a public policy requirement, given that Art. 43 of the EAL enables the parties to agree on releasing the arbitral tribunal from its duty to render a reasoned award.278 .Cairo Court of Appeal, 4th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 53 of JY 138, Hearing session dated 30 May 2022. In this specific case, the Cairo Court of Appeal held that the validity of an arbitral award is examined with respect to its issuance in compliance with the procedures provided by the law. The Court further explained that the defect or lack in the reasoning of the award does not lead to its setting aside, given that this ground is not provided under Art. 53 of the EAL, which strictly stipulates all grounds for setting aside. Accordingly, from the Court’s formal and apparent scrutiny of the arbitral awards subject to the motion for setting aside, the Court found that both awards were sufficiently reasoned, by including a clear and unambiguous description of the facts in dispute, expressly referring to submitted evidence and complying with the dispositive part of the award. In this context, the Court clarified that a contradicting reasoning is not synonymous with a lack of reasoning, because the former is a defect in substance, whilst the latter is a defect in the form. The Court explained that by permitting the court adjudicating the motion for setting aside to examine whether there exists any contradiction in the reasoning of an award, this would be a backdoor for the court to re-examine the merits of the case which falls outside the scope thereof.279 .Ibid. The Court of Cassation has set aside an award for failure to set out the arbitration agreement. The Court held that this requirement is essential for the tribunal to assess whether the arbitrators have exceeded the scope of the arbitration agreement.280 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 4623 of JY 66, Hearing session 1997. The Court reached this decision in circumstances where the arbitration agreement had been deposited with the award and was available to the court. As to institutional arbitration, Art. 34 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024) prescribes for similar provisions as to the form and effect of arbitral awards. 4. Jurisdiction a. Power of arbitrators Arbitrators may rule on their own jurisdiction, as the principle of competence-competence is recognized under the EAL. The arbitral tribunal has the power to decide over any jurisdiction-related claims including the existence, validity and scope of the arbitration agreement.281 Art. 22(1) of the EAL. b. Form of jurisdictional decision The arbitral tribunal may rule on its jurisdiction in a separate award, being regarded as a preliminary question before ruling on the merits, or it can decide on jurisdiction in its final award on the merits.282 Art. 22(3) of the EAL. Awards on jurisdiction and other partial awards rendered during the arbitral proceedings cannot, in light of the current practice before the courts, be challenged separately; they can be challenged with the final award. c. Appeal A preliminary decision on jurisdiction cannot be challenged separately, it can only be challenged through an action for setting aside the final award terminating the dispute.283 Ibid. The EAL empowers arbitrators to decide over their jurisdiction.284 Art. 22(1) of the EAL. The EAL expressly refers to the circumstances where national courts may interfere and assist the parties. d. Timing Objections to jurisdiction shall be raised in limine litis (at the beginning of the arbitral procedure), at a date not later than the date of submitting the respondent’s statement of defence.285 Art. 22(2) of the EAL. The appointment or participation in the appointment of an arbitrator by a party shall not preclude same from raising an objection to jurisdiction.286 Ibid. A plea that the arbitration agreement does not cover the disputed issues, must be raised immediately, otherwise the failure to raise the plea timely would constitute a waiver of a party’s right to raise such a plea. The arbitral tribunal may exceptionally accept a late plea (objection) if it considers the delay justified.287 Ibid. If the jurisdictional objection pertains to the subject matter of the dispute being not capable of settlement by arbitration, there is no specific time limit set to raise it, since Art. 11 of the EAL, which is a mandatory provision, expressly stipulates that matters that cannot be settled are not arbitrable. Thus, if the arbitral proceedings continue, the arbitral award would be set aside and/or not enforceable. e. Role of court Egyptian courts are under a legal obligation to dismiss litigation with respect to disputes subject to an arbitration agreement if the defendant, at the commencement of the proceedings, advances a plea pertaining to the existence of an arbitration agreement.288 Art. 13(1) of the EAL. The EAL does not stipulate for a “referral exception” whereby the state court may accept to decide over jurisdiction if it finds that the arbitration agreement is null and void, inoperative or incapable of being performed. This entails that the arbitral tribunal enjoys the priority to decide its competence over state courts. However, the court is not under an obligation to reject the case ex officio for the mere existence of an arbitration agreement, the defendant must raise its objection at the commencement of the proceedings. This is principally due to the fact that an arbitration agreement is not constitutive of public policy. In the absence of a plea by the defendant in litigation, parallel proceedings will be conducted before the arbitral tribunals and the courts, such that there is no suspension to arbitral proceedings, and decisions will be rendered irrespective of the parties’ prior agreement to arbitrate. In the event that the two decisions are contradictory, the successful party in the arbitration may elevate the conflict to the Supreme Constitutional Court in accordance with the law. 5. Applicable Law a. Domestic arbitration The EAL recognizes the principle of party autonomy where the parties are free to determine the law applicable to the substance of the dispute, subject to exceptional legislative constraints (as in technology transfer contracts for example, where application of Egyptian law is mandatory).289 Art. 39(1) of the EAL, if the parties agree to apply a national law, its conflict of law rules will not apply unless the parties have agreed otherwise. The arbitrators may decide as amiables compositeurs or ex aequo et bono only if the parties have agreed to confer such power upon them.290 Art. 39(4) of the EAL. In this respect, the Cairo Court of Appeal held that an “amiable compositeur” enjoys a wide discretionary authority to rule over the subject matter of the dispute without being bound or restricted by the rules of law which do not relate to public policy. When deciding a dispute, the arbitrator acting as “amiable compositeur” does not conclude a settlement, but he/she renders a binding award that is enforceable and has a res judicata effect. The arbitrator is not a representative to both parties in dispute to conclude a settlement on their behalf, he/she is a “judge” who enjoys, on his/her own, the authority to decide over the dispute by rendering an award that may be ordering to award one party all of its claims.291 Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Case No. 89 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 28 February 2004. b. International commercial arbitration The arbitrators may decide as amiables compositeurs or ex aequo et bono only if the parties have agreed to confer such power upon them.292 Art. 39(4) of the EAL. However, if the parties have not agreed on specific rules or law applicable to the substance of their dispute, the EAL provides that the arbitral tribunal shall apply the substantive rules of the law it considers having the closest connection to the dispute.293 Art. 39(2) of the EAL. Also, when adjudicating the merits of the dispute, the tribunal must in all cases take into consideration the provisions of the contract, the custom and trade usages prevailing in the field of the transaction.294 Art. 39(3) of the EAL. It is worth mentioning that the EAL has not provided for a specific set of connecting factors that the arbitrators shall follow in determining the substantive rules having the closest connection with the dispute. The choice of the applicable substantive rules will be dependent on the nature of the dispute and shall be determined on a case-by-case basis. For example, if the validity of a contract is disputed, the law having the closest connection with the dispute will be the law of the state where the contract has been concluded. Also, if the dispute is related to the performance of an obligation, then the law having the closest connection with the dispute is the law of the state where the obligation has been performed or that of the agreed place of performance of this obligation. It is also submitted that Egyptian law is considered having the closest connection with a dispute when the significant elements of the legal relationship forming the dispute is linked to Egypt.295 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 537. Furthermore, in an arbitration case administered by the CRCICA, an arbitral tribunal listed how it determined the law applicable to the merits of the dispute, and these were the law of the place of arbitration, the law of the place of signing of the original contract, the law of residency of the parties to the contract, the law of the state where the contract is performed, etc.296 CRCICA Case No. 95/1997, award dated 12 March 1998, in Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 537. 6. Settlement The parties may, during the arbitral proceedings, agree to settle their dispute and request the arbitral tribunal to record the terms of their settlement in the form of an order that terminates the proceedings. Said order is normally rendered in the form of an award by consent, as it has the same effect of arbitral awards with regard to their enforcement only (Art. 41 of the EAL). However, an award by consent cannot be set aside.297 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 501. The EAL does not provide that the arbitral tribunal may refuse a request by the parties to terminate the proceedings and record their settlement terms. The EAL is also silent on the possibility for the parties to request the arbitrators to assist them in settlement negotiations during the arbitration. In this respect, the award by consent is subject to certain regulations by virtue of its nature, which include: (1) it is sufficient for such award by consent to be in writing and signed by the arbitrators (majority shall sign with the reasons for the non-signature of the minority); (2) there being no requirement for the award by consent to state the reasons upon which it is based; (3) there being no requirement to provide a summary of the parties’ claims, requests, submissions and documents.298 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 500. The EAL also gives the award by consent the same legal effect of a standard award. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide that, before the issuance of the arbitral award, the arbitral tribunal shall record the settlement agreed by the parties regarding their dispute in the form of an award on agreed terms (i.e., award by consent), if requested by the parties and accepted by the tribunal.299 Art. 37(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). In this regard, there is no requirement that the award on agreed terms states the reasons upon which it is based,300 Ibid. however, it shall be made in writing, it becomes final and binding upon the parties, it shall be signed by the arbitrators (in case of non-signature by all, the majority must sign and state the reasons for non-signature by the minority) and it shall contain the date on which the award is issued and the place of arbitration.301 Art. 34(2), (4) and (6) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). 7A. Correction and Interpretation of the Award a. Correction The arbitral tribunal may correct any exclusively material errors whether typographical errors, errors in computation or clerical errors, either upon its own initiative or upon a party’s request (Art. 50(1) of the EAL). The arbitral tribunal shall issue the correction decision in writing (without holding any hearing)302 Art. 50(1) of the EAL. and notify same to the parties within thirty days from the date of its issuance. If the arbitral tribunal exceeds its powers of correction, its decision may be challenged by means of a nullity action in conformity with Arts. 53 and 54 of the EAL.303 Art. 50(2) of the EAL. According to the wording of Art. 50(1) of the EAL, if the date or place of arbitration or place of signature of the award are not contained in the award, these may not be subject to correction, as they do not constitute typographical errors, or errors in computation. However, these form part of the required information to be included in the arbitral award at the time of its issuance.304 Art. 43(3) of the EAL. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide that the arbitral tribunal may correct any error in computation or any clerical or typographical errors, upon the request of a party, if the tribunal considers such request justified, or the tribunal may correct any of these errors on its own initiative within thirty days from the communication of the award to the parties. Prior to submitting its request for correction, the party requesting a correction shall first notify the other party and the Centre.305 Art. 39(1) and (2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). b. Interpretation The arbitral tribunal may, upon the request of a party, give an interpretation clarifying an ambiguity that appears in the dispositive part of the award. That said, interpretation is limited to the dispositive only. The party requesting clarification must notify the other party of the request before presenting it to the arbitral tribunal.306 Art. 49(1) of the EAL. The interpretation decision shall be issued in writing within thirty days from the date of receipt of the clarification request and the tribunal may extend this period by another thirty days if it deems such extension necessary.307 Art. 49(2) of the EAL. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide that the arbitral tribunal may give an interpretation of the award (without limiting the scope of interpretation to dispositive, as is the case under the EAL), upon the request of a party, who has notified the other party and the Centre of its request for interpretation prior to submitting it. The arbitral tribunal may invite the other party to comment on the request for interpretation within fifteen days. If the tribunal considers the request to be justified, it shall give the interpretation in writing within forty-five days after the expiry of the date of commenting on the request.308 Art. 38 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). c. Form The EAL stipulates that correction/interpretation shall be made in writing,309 Arts. 49(2) and 50(2) of the EAL. however it uses different terminology when referring to the form of correction and interpretation decisions. It provides for issuing correction orders310 Art. 50(2) of the EAL. and provides that the interpretation award forms an integral part complementing the arbitral award and is governed by the same rules.311 Art. 49(3) of the EAL. As to the formal requirement of correction/interpretation in institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide that it shall be made in writing; shall be final and binding upon the parties; shall be signed by the arbitrators (in case of non-signature by all, the majority must sign and state the reasons for non-signature by the minority); shall contain the date on which the award was rendered, the arbitration agreement and indicate the place of arbitration; that correction/interpretation shall form part of the award; and the originals shall be communicated to each party.312 Arts. 34, 38(2) and 39(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). Only interpretation decisions shall state the reasons upon which they are based.313 Art. 34(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). d. Timing The time limit for requesting a correction/interpretation of an award is thirty days from the date of receipt of the arbitral award (or from the date of issuance of the arbitral award in case of correction undertaken by the arbitral award on its own initiative).314 Arts. 49(1) and 50(1) of the EAL. The time limit for issuing a correction/interpretation of an award by the arbitral tribunal is initially thirty days from the date of receipt of the request for correction/clarification (or from the date of issuance of the arbitral award in case of correction undertaken by the arbitral award on its own initiative). The tribunal may extend the period for issuing the correction/interpretation for another thirty days if it deems necessary. Therefore, the maximum time limit for the tribunal to issue the correction/interpretation is sixty days from (1) the date of rendering the award, or the submission of a correction request by one of the parties; and (2) the date of submitting a request by a party in case of interpretation awards.315 Arts. 49(2) and 50(2) of the EAL. As to time limits in institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide that the request for correction/interpretation shall be made by a party within thirty days from the receipt of the award, or the correction may be made by the tribunal on its own initiative within thirty days from the date of communication of the award to the parties.316 Arts. 38(1) and 39(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The time limit to issue the decision for correction/interpretation is forty-five days from receipt of a party’s request for correction; or from the date of expiry of the date of commenting on the request for interpretation by the other party.317 Arts. 38(2) and 39(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). 7B. Additional Award Each party may, within thirty days from the date of receipt of the arbitral award, and after notifying the other party, submit a request to the arbitral tribunal to issue an additional award to decide on claims presented in the arbitral proceedings but omitted from the award (Art. 51(1) of the EAL). The arbitral tribunal shall issue its decision within sixty days from the date of submittal of the request and may extend such period for a further thirty days, if it considers it necessary, amounting to a maximum of ninety days.318 Art. 51(2) of the EAL. When the arbitral award has omitted deciding on a party’s request, this does not constitute a ground for setting aside the award.319 Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Case No. 26 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 22 November 2003. Additional awards may be requested in respect of claims related to the merits but not procedural matters.320 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 606. The EAL does not prescribe a form of the additional award or address who is liable to pay the costs of rendering an additional award. However, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide that the arbitral tribunal may charge its costs but not additional fees.321 Art. 47(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The doctrinal view consists in considering the additional award subject to the same rules governing arbitral awards with respect to its issuance, notification, setting aside and enforcement.322 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 609. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide that after notifying the other party and the Centre, a party may request the arbitral tribunal to issue an additional award with respect to claims presented in the arbitral proceedings but not decided upon by the tribunal. The other party may be invited by the tribunal to comment on said request within fifteen days. The time limit to render an additional award, if the tribunal considers the request for an additional award to be justified, is sixty days after the expiry of the date of commenting on the request, such that the tribunal may extend said period if necessary. The formal requirements of arbitral awards provided under Art. 34(2), (4) and (6) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules shall apply to additional awards.323 Art. 40 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). In practice, CRCICA tribunals have rejected requests to issue additional awards in cases where the purpose and effect of the request is to re-litigate issues that have already been decided by the tribunal, to amend the award, or to raise a claim that was not presented before the tribunal in the course of the proceedings.324 CRCICA Case No. 324/2001, award dated 9 January 2002, 5 Journal of Arab Arbitration (September 2002) p. 194, para. 22 (in Arabic). 8. Fees and Costs a. General / Types of fees and costs The EAL does not include any provision relating to the allocation of costs which accords the tribunal a broad discretion in assessing the reasonableness of the fees and allocating the fees and costs between the parties, unless otherwise agreed between the parties. In practice, arbitral tribunals will apply the standard(s) agreed by the parties. Failing such agreement, it is common for arbitral tribunals to follow the principle of the costs follow the event, subject to any adaptations the arbitral tribunal deems appropriate in light of how the proceedings were conducted. In this respect, the costs of arbitration would cover all costs in relation to the proceedings, which include the arbitrator’s fees and expenses, the tribunal’s expert fees as well as party-appointed experts, the administrative fees (in case of institutional arbitration) as well as the legal counsel costs. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules define the costs of arbitration, such that costs exclusively include: (1) the registration fee; (2) the administrative fees; (3) the fees of the arbitral tribunal; (4) the reasonable travel and other expenses incurred by the arbitrators; (5) the fees and reasonable expenses of tribunal-appointed experts as per Art. 29 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules and costs of other assistance (e.g., translation and court reporting services) required by the arbitral tribunal; (6) the reasonable travel and other expenses of witnesses to the extent these expenses are approved by the tribunal; (7) the legal and other costs incurred by the parties (including party-appointed experts’ fees and expenses) in relation to the arbitration to the extent the tribunal determines as reasonable; and (8) any fees and expenses of the appointing authority in the event the Centre is not designated as the appointing authority.325 Art. 41(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). b. Deposit The EAL does not address the issue of fees, but institutional rules include specific provisions on fees and their payment. Arbitrators may secure payment of their fees in Egypt by withholding the award until the agreed and/or applicable fees are paid. In institutional arbitration, the fees are held in an account or in escrow by the institution until the final award is rendered. More specifically, Art. 46 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules sets out the requirements for deposit of costs. The administrative fees and the arbitrators’ fees are determined based on the sum in dispute, which consists of the aggregate value of all claims, counterclaims and set-offs, save for the cases where the Centre fixes separate costs for the claims and counterclaims.326 Arts. 44 and 45 of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The parties shall deposit the administrative and arbitrators’ fees at the Centre before the constitution of the arbitral tribunal. Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, these deposits are split between the parties in equal shares (excluding the registration fee which is borne by the party initiating a case).327 Art. 46(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). If a party does not comply with the request for deposit and fails to make the required payment in full within fifteen days from the date of receipt of the request for payment, the Centre informs the other party or parties so that one or more of them may make the required payment within a specified period of time set by the Centre. If the required deposit remains unpaid by the other party or parties after being informed, the Centre may suspend or terminate the arbitral proceedings if the arbitral tribunal has not yet been constituted in full, or if it has not commenced the proceedings. If the tribunal is fully constituted, the Centre may request the arbitral tribunal to make such suspension or termination of the arbitral proceedings.328 Art. 46(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). c. Fees of arbitrators The EAL does not address the issue of fees, but institutional rules include specific provisions on fees and their payment. In this regard, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide that the fees of the arbitrators are determined based on the sum in dispute, which consists of the aggregate value of all claims, counterclaims and set-offs save for the cases where the Centre fixes separate costs for the claims and counterclaims.329 Art. 45(1) and 45(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The system determining the arbitrators’ fees differs based on the sum in dispute, as follows: – If the amount in dispute does not exceed US$ 500,000 (five hundred thousand US Dollars), the fees of the sole arbitrator are determined as a flat amount in accordance with Table (2) of Annex 1 to the Rules and the fees of the arbitral tribunal constituted of three or more arbitrators are determined as a flat amount in accordance with Table (3) of Annex 1 to the Rules;330 Art. 45(4) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). This flat rate for the sole arbitrator varies from a minimum of US$ 1,050 (one thousand and fifty US Dollars) if the amount in dispute does not exceed US$ 25,000 (twenty five thousand US Dollars) to a maximum of US$ 3,675 (three thousand six hundred and seventy five US Dollars) + a percentage of 1.3125% of the amount above US$ 200,000 if the amount in dispute is between the range of US$ 200,001 (two hundred thousand and one US Dollars) and US$ 500,000 (five hundred thousand US Dollars); see Table (2) of Annex 1 to the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). This flat rate for the arbitral tribunal constituted of three or more arbitrators varies from a minimum of US$ 3000 (three thousand US Dollars) if the amount in dispute does not exceed US$ 25,000 (twenty five thousand US Dollars) to a maximum of US$ 10,500 (ten thousand and five hundred US Dollars) + a percentage of 3.75% of the amount above US$ 200,000 if the amount in dispute is between the range of US$ 200,001 (two hundred thousand and one US Dollars) and US$ 500,000 (five hundred thousand US Dollars); see Table (3) of Annex 1 to the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024) and – If the amount in dispute exceeds US$ 500,000 (five hundred thousand US Dollars), the fees of the sole arbitrator are determined in accordance with the scales set out in Table (2) of Annex 1 to the Rules and the fees of the arbitral tribunal constituted of three or more arbitrators are determined in accordance with the scales set out in Table (3) of Annex 1 to the Rules.331 Art. 45(5) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The scale provides for a minimum fee and a maximum fee corresponding to varying ranges of the sum in dispute.332 With respect to the sole arbitrator fees, if the sum in dispute is between the range of US$ 500,001 (five hundred thousand and one US Dollars) and US$ 1,000,000 (one million US Dollars), the minimum fee of the sole arbitrator is US$ 7,612.5 (seven thousand six hundred and twelve US Dollars and fifty cents) + a percentage of 0.7% of the amount over US$ 500,000 (five hundred thousand US Dollars), and the maximum fee of the sole arbitrator is US$ 34,256 (thirty four thousand two hundred and fifty six US Dollars) + a percentage of 3.15% of the amount over US$ 500,000 (five hundred thousand US Dollars). However, if the sum in dispute is over US$ 100,000,000 (one hundred millions US Dollars), the minimum fees of the sole arbitrator would be US$ 69,387.5 (sixty nine thousand three hundred and eighty seven US Dollars and fifty cents) + a percentage of 0.00875% of the amount over US$ 100,000,000 (one hundred millions US Dollars), and the maximum fees of the sole arbitrator would be US$ 312,243.75 (three hundred and twelve thousand and two hundred forty three US Dollars and seventy five cents) + a percentage of 0.039375% of the amount over US$ 100,000,000 (one hundred millions US Dollars); see Table (2) of Annex 1 to the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). With respect to the fees of the arbitral tribunal constituted of three or more arbitrators, if the sum in dispute is between the range of US$ 500,001 (five hundred thousand and one US Dollars) and US$ 1,000,000 (one million US Dollars), the minimum fee of the arbitral tribunal is US$ 21,750 (twenty one thousand seven hundred and fifty US Dollars) + a percentage of 2% of the amount over US$ 500,000 (five hundred thousand US Dollars), and the maximum fee of the arbitral tribunal is US$ 97,875 (ninety seven thousand eight hundred and seventy five US Dollars) + a percentage of 9% of the amount over US$ 500,000 (five hundred thousand US Dollars). However, if the sum in dispute is over US$ 100,000,000 (one hundred millions US Dollars), the minimum fees of the arbitral tribunal would be US$ 198,250 (one hundred ninety eight thousand and two hundred and fifty US Dollars) + a percentage of 0.025% of the amount over US$ 100,000,000 (one hundred millions US Dollars), and the maximum fees of the arbitral tribunal would be US$ 892,125 (eight hundred ninety two thousand and one hundred and twenty five US Dollars) + a percentage of 0.1125% of the amount over US$ 100,000,000 (one hundred millions US Dollars); see Table (3) of Annex 1 to the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The total arbitrators’ fees are distributed in the following shares: 40% for the Chairman of the arbitral tribunal and 30% for each co-arbitrator, unless otherwise agreed by the members of the arbitral tribunal.333 Art. 45(6) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). When accepting his/her mission, the arbitrator approves that he/she is only entitled to the fees determined in accordance with Table (2) and Table (3) of Annex 1 to the Rules.334 Art. 45(7) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The Centre determines the arbitral tribunal fees within the scale of fees set out in Table (2) and Table (3) of Annex 1 to the Rules and following the determination of the fees, any changes to such fees within the scales of fees shall be upon a reasoned request from the arbitral tribunal which shall be decided by the Centre upon its discretion taking into consideration the complexity, the high amounts in dispute, the arbitrators’ experience and any other relevant circumstances.335 Ibid. The arbitrators’ fees are paid upon rendering its final award signed by them. It is possible that arbitrators be paid an advance not exceeding half of the deposited arbitrators’ fees, before the rendering of the final award at the request of the arbitral tribunal, taking into consideration the work performed by the arbitral tribunal and other relevant circumstances.336 Art. 45(8) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). d. Costs of legal assistance to parties The EAL does not address the legal counsel fees paid by the parties, but the CCCP addresses these legal fees regarding cases before national courts. Accordingly, under the CCCP, the general principle is that the losing party bears all costs including legal representation costs.337 Art. 184 of the CCCP. However, there exists exceptions under the CCCP where the court may allocate costs (i.e., the judicial proceedings costs plus legal counsel fees) between the parties or even order the winning party to bear all costs. Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, it is common for arbitrators to award legal costs to the winning party, subject to the arbitral tribunal’s discretion in considering the reasonableness and substantiation of such fees as well as any other relevant circumstances. e. Allocation of costs Parties are generally able to recover fees paid and costs incurred. While the general practice is that costs follow the event, the absence of any provision on allocation of costs in the EAL has enabled arbitral tribunals to enjoy broad discretion in splitting or allocating the fees and costs between the parties, unless otherwise agreed amongst the parties. In practice, the culture of “splitting the baby” is gradually declining in respect of costs. The gradual shift towards the application of international norms of costs allocation can be traced in practice, and cases are increasingly decided on the basis of the “loser pays” rule or the “costs follow the event” rule in so far as the winning party is able to justify and substantiate its fees and costs. In this respect, common practice has also shown that when deciding on the allocation of costs, tribunals may consider the conduct of the parties during the arbitral proceedings as well as the nature and complexity of the dispute. When parties have won on some claims and lost on others, arbitral tribunals may apportion the costs, such that each party may be ordered to bear its own costs, or have the costs apportioned.338 Art. 186 of the CCCP. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA Arbitration Rules provide that before awarding the costs, the arbitral tribunal shall request the Centre to provide a statement relating to the deposits made by the parties and, to the extent applicable, request from the parties their legal fees and other costs related to the arbitration.339 Art. 49(1) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The Rules further provide for the general principle of “costs follow the event” such that the losing party bears the costs. However, the Rules warrant the arbitral tribunal’s discretion in apportioning the costs between the parties if it determines that apportionment is reasonable in due consideration of the circumstances of the case, which includes the parties’ contributions to the efficient conduct of the proceedings and the avoidance of unnecessary costs and delays.340 Art. 49(2) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). The tribunal shall decide on the allocation of costs in the final award or in any other award by determining the amount a party may have to pay to the other party or parties.341 Art. 49(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). 9. notification of the award and registration There are two types of notifications which must be distinguished. Firstly, the notification of the award to both parties once rendered and signed by the arbitrators within thirty days from the date of its issuance. To this extent, there are no legal requirements other than delivering the award to the parties (Art. 44(1) of the EAL). Failure to comply with this provision does not affect the validity of the arbitral award. Secondly, the notification of the award for setting aside or enforcing same, where the winning party must notify the award through court bailiff procedure,342 Art. 6 of the CCCP. so that the time limit (ninety days) for initiating a nullity action starts running from the date of this notification,343 Art. 54 of the EAL. and after the elapse of said time limit, the application for enforcement of the arbitral award would be admissible.344 Art. 58(1) of the EAL. Therefore, notification of the award through court bailiff is a prerequisite for enforcement. The successful party shall deposit the original award or a copy thereof in the language in which it was rendered, or a certified translation thereof, to the secretariat of the competent court referred to under Art. 9 of the EAL. The secretariat of the court shall record such deposit in a procès-verbal and each party may request a copy of the procès-verbal.345 Art. 47 of the EAL. In this respect, the procès-verbal recording the deposit of the award is necessary for its application for enforcement, as it figures among the required documents to be accompanied therewith.346 Art. 56 of the EAL. It is worth noting that in domestic arbitration the secretariat of the competent court is that of the court having original jurisdiction over the dispute, whilst in international commercial arbitration, it is the secretariat of the Cairo Court of Appeal, unless otherwise agreed by the parties.347 Art. 9 of the EAL. The failure to deposit the arbitral award to the secretariat of the competent court does not have any effect on its validity and does not constitute a ground for setting aside.348 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 46 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 22 November 2003. There is no time limit for the deposit of the arbitral award by the winning party to enforce same, the deposit thereof only activates the running of the time limit for initiating a nullity action. There are no taxes payable on the deposit of the arbitral award. Since 2008, the deposited award is subject to scrutiny of the Technical Bureau at the Ministry of Justice,349 Decree No. 8310/2008 issued on 21 September 2008. It is worth noting the said Decree was amended twice by virtue of Decree No. 9739/2011 issued on 5 October 2011 and Decree No. 1096/2017 issued on 2 February 2017. which receives a copy of the deposited award to verify that there is no violation of public policy, that the arbitration does not involve a non-arbitrable matter and that the court to which the award is deposited is the competent court in accordance with the provisions of Arts. 9 and 47 of the EAL. The Technical Bureau issues an opinion in this regard, which is advisory and non-binding upon the secretariat of the competent court.350 Art. 4 of Decree No. 9739/2011 issued on 5 October 2011, amending Decree No. 8310/2008. It is the competent court that ultimately decides whether to accept or reject the application for exequatur (leave for enforcement). Whether the arbitration is institutional or ad hoc, the award is to be deposited at the secretariat of the competent court as provided under Art. 47 of the EAL. 10. Enforcement of Domestic and International Awards Rendered In [Country of Report] a. General To enforce an arbitral award, the winning party must obtain an exequatur (leave for enforcement) to then proceed with the actual execution of the award. Once the award is issued, the winning party shall deposit the original award or a signed copy thereof in the language it was rendered, or a certified Arabic translation if the award was rendered in another language, at the secretariat of the competent court as prescribed by Art. 9 of the EAL, which is the court having initial jurisdiction for domestic awards and the Cairo Court of Appeal for international awards, unless the parties have agreed to a different court of appeal. The Secretariat of the competent court records the deposit of the award in a procès-verbal , and each party may request a copy thereof.351 Art. 47 of the EAL. There is no time limit to deposit the award. After depositing the award, the winning party shall wait until the elapse of the period for filing a nullity action,352 Art. 58(1) of the EAL. which corresponds to ninety days from the date of the notification of the award to the other party,353 Art. 54(1) of the EAL. then request a leave for enforcement from the president of the competent court. Otherwise, the request for leave of enforcement submitted before the elapse of the ninety days’ time limit will be declared inadmissible.354 Art. 58(1) of the EAL. A leave for enforcement is submitted in an ex parte application to the president of the competent court and shall be accompanied by the following: (1) the original award or a signed copy thereof; (2) a copy of the arbitration agreement; (3) a certified Arabic translation of the award if it was rendered in a foreign language; and (4) a copy of the procès-verbal attesting the deposit of the arbitral award pursuant to Art. 47 of the EAL.355 Art. 56 of the EAL. Nevertheless, the leave of enforcement is granted and notified to the losing party, the latter is entitled to challenge the order in adversarial proceedings within thirty days from the date of issuance of the order. b. Leave for enforcement The party requesting to enforce an arbitral award must obtain a leave for enforcement from the president of the competent court as explained in a above in order to execute the award. The leave for enforcement is a separate application from the procedure of execution of the award. The leave for enforcement is issued by the president of the competent court in ex parte proceedings.356 Ibid. It is inadmissible if it is submitted before the elapse of the ninety days’ time limit for filing a nullity action.357 Arts. 54(1) and 58(1) of the EAL. In practice, courts observe the ninety days’ time limit whether the proceedings are governed by Egyptian law or not, which is indeed questionable because if the lex loci arbitri is not Egyptian law, there is no legal basis to impose the ninety days’ time limit because Egyptian courts will not be the courts of primary jurisdiction and will have no competence to decide over any nullity action. Insofar as the EAL is the lex loci arbitri, the losing party may initiate a nullity action within the ninety days’ time limit provided under Art. 54(1) of the EAL, which starts running from the date of notification of the award. The filing of a nullity action does not suspend the enforcement of the arbitral award, however, the applicant may request the court to suspend enforcement upon serious grounds.358 Art. 57 of the EAL. If the leave for enforcement is granted, the losing party may challenge the order granting enforcement before the competent court, as provided under Art. 9 of the EAL, within thirty days from the date of the decision.359 Art. 58(3) of the EAL; and Supreme Constitutional Court, Challenge No. 92 of Constitutional Year 21, Hearing session dated 6 January 2001. c. Enforcement i. Grounds for refusal Subject to the application of the 1958 New York Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (the “New York Convention”), enforcement under the EAL may be refused based on any of the following grounds: (1) if the award contradicts a judgment previously rendered by the Egyptian courts on the subject matter of the dispute; (2) if the award violates the rules of public policy in Egypt;360 It is worth noting that Art. 4 of Decree No. 9739/2011 issued on 5 October 2011, amending Decree No. 8310/2008 further elaborates that when rendering its advisory opinion in relation to an award deposited at the secretariat of the competent court, the Technical Bureau of the Ministry of Justice shall conduct a test for arbitrability of the dispute subject to arbitration, by verifying that the deposited award does not violate public policy in Egypt, and that the arbitration did not pertain to a matter that cannot be settled under Egyptian Law, i.e., it is not inarbitrable. and (3) if the award has not been properly notified to the party against whom it was rendered.361 Art. 58(2) of the EAL. ii. Procedure The EAL stipulates in Art. 58(3) that the order granting enforcement is not subject to appeal, but that the order refusing to grant enforcement may be subject to a petition. This prohibition was held unconstitutional by the Supreme Constitutional Court on the grounds that there should be equality between the rights of the parties and that both decisions shall be reviewable.362 Supreme Constitutional Court, Challenge No. 92 of Constitutional Year 21, Hearing session dated 6 January 2001. Accordingly, since the publication of this decision in the Official Gazette on 18 January 2001, the party against whom the exequatur (leave of enforcement) is issued may challenge it before the competent court within thirty days from the date of the decision, in order to maintain the equality of rights between the two parties. 11. Publication of the Award Arbitral award may not be published, in whole or in part, unless agreed by the parties.363 Art. 44(2) of the EAL. Therefore an award can only be published with the prior approval of both parties, or all if there are more than two. Consent cannot be presumed. Further, the award may not be made public without the parties’ consent, unless the award has been subject to nullity or enforcement proceedings, where its content may fall in the public domain. As to institutional arbitration, the CRCICA undertakes not to publish any decision or arbitral award or any part thereof that reveals the identity of any of the parties without the prior consent of all parties.364 Art. 54(3) of the CRCICA Arbitration Rules (2024). CRCICA arbitral awards are regularly published, while maintaining the confidentiality of these awards. To date, four volumes of CRCICA English awards and eight volumes of CRCICA Arabic awards have been published.365 See CRCICA official website at: <https://crcica.org/arbitration/crcica-arbitral-awards/>.

CHAPTER VI: ENFORCEMENT OF FOREIGN ARBITRAL AWARDS 1. Enforcement under Conventions and Treaties a. Conventions in force In support of the effectiveness of arbitration and to facilitate enforcement of foreign arbitral awards, Egypt has signed and ratified the 1958 New York Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (the “New York Convention”), without any reservations or declarations, and it entered into force on 7 June 1959. Egypt also joined the Riyadh Arab Agreement for Judicial Cooperation (1983) on 19 August 2014. The Riyadh Convention sets out the grounds for refusal of enforcement as follows: (1) lack of arbitrability; (2) invalidity of the arbitration agreement; (3) lack of jurisdiction of arbitrators under the contract, the arbitration agreement, or the law according to which the award has been issued; (4) the parties have been served subpoenas improperly; (5) the award violates a provision of Islamic Shari’a, or public policy, or morals of the party against whom the enforcement is brought.366 Art. 37 of the Riyadh Arab Agreement for Judicial Cooperation. Egypt has also concluded several bilateral treaties on judicial cooperation that refer to mutual cooperation in the recognition and enforcement of arbitral awards.367 Egypt has concluded bilateral judicial cooperation treaties with several states including the following: Tunisia (1976); Italy (1978); France (1982); Jordan (1987); Morocco (1989); Bahrain (1989); Libya (1993); China (1994); Hungary (1996); Syria (1998); United Arab Emirates (2000); Oman (2002); and Kuwait (2017). These bilateral treaties provide for different enforcement regimes. For instance, some refer to the New York Convention,368 Egypt-France (1982); Egypt-Hungary (1996); and Egypt-China (1994). and may add additional grounds for denying enforcement.369 Egypt-Syria (1998). Also, in other treaties enforcement is subject to the provisions of the national law of the state where enforcement is sought (without prejudice to the provisions of the treaty).370 Egypt-UAE (2000). In this regard, the reference to apply the national law may ultimately lead to the application of the New York Convention. It is worth noting that at the time of signing this judicial cooperation treaty, only Egypt was a party to the New York Convention, the UAE later signed and ratified the New York Convention in 2006. Furthermore, some treaties prescribe the requirements upon which enforcement is granted, such as the existence of a written arbitration agreement, the arbitrability of the dispute, compliance with public policy and the constitution of either contracting state, proper notification, absence of a prior court decision rendered by a court of the state where enforcement is sought in relation to the same dispute, or exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the state where enforcement is sought over the subject matter of the dispute.371 Egypt-Libya (1993); and Egypt-Jordan (1987). b. Procedure The Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I) does not provide for different standards of review for recognition and enforcement of foreign and domestic awards. Egypt has signed and ratified the New York Convention, therefore the recognition and enforcement of foreign awards are subject to said Convention, which in turn provides for the application of the procedural rules of the territory of the state where the award is sought to be recognized and enforced. Initially, the Egyptian Code of Civil and Commercial Procedures (“CCCP”) provides for the enforcement procedure of foreign awards and expressly states that its provisions apply to foreign arbitral awards, provided that the subject matter of the dispute is arbitrable.372 Art. 299 of the CCCP. In this respect, Arts. 296 to 299 of the CCCP govern the procedures for requesting enforcement of foreign arbitral awards in Egypt. Pursuant to the CCCP provisions, enforcement of foreign awards is sought through the filing of an ordinary action before the Court of First Instance in whose district enforcement is sought. The enforcement action is by means of an originating summons, followed by a hearing. The order for enforcement is only enforceable upon exhausting the appeal stage.373 Art. 297 of the CCCP reads: “A request for enforcement shall be submitted to the competent court of first instance located in the area where enforcement is sought, and in the manner to file a cause of action.” However, since 2005, the Court of Cassation validly held that foreign awards are enforceable under the EAL,374 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 966 of JY 73, Hearing session dated 10 January 2005; and Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 15912 of JY 76, Hearing session dated 6 April 2015. while continuing allowing the applicant (the winning party) to elect to enforce foreign awards under the provisions of local law, which would be the EAL.375 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 913 of JY 73, Hearing session dated 23 February 2010; and Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 5000 of JY 78, Hearing session dated 28 April 2015. Under the EAL, the enforcement order is obtained through ex parte proceedings.
