Nevertheless, once an order is granted and notified to the losing party, the latter is entitled to challenge the order in adversarial proceedings within thirty days from the date of issuance of the order. The party in whose favour the award is rendered shall deposit a certified translation of the foreign award at the secretariat of the Cairo Court of Appeal, being the competent court in this regard, which records such deposit in a procès-verbal and each party is entitled to obtain a copy thereof.376 Art. 47 of the EAL. The secretariat of the Cairo Court of Appeal will subsequently forward the deposited award to the Technical Bureau at the Ministry of Justice which reviews that the award and verifies that it does not contravene public policy in Egypt, the subject matter of the dispute is arbitrable and that the award is deposited at the competent court as provided under Arts. 9 and 47 of the EAL. The Technical Bureau renders an advisory opinion that is non-binding on the court that ultimately decides whether to accept or reject the application for enforcement.377 Decree No. 8310/2008 issued on 21 September 2008, as amended by virtue of Decree No. 9739/2011 issued on 5 October 2011 and Decree No. 1096/2017 issued on 2 February 2017. Following the deposit of the award, the President of the Cairo Court of Appeal is entrusted with the power of issuing the exequatur (leave for enforcement). The application for enforcement must be accompanied by the following documents: (1) the original award or a signed copy thereof; (2) a copy of the arbitration agreement, with an attested Arabic translation; (3) a certified Arabic translation of the award; and (4) a copy of the procès-verbal evidencing deposit of the award pursuant to Art. 47 of the EAL.378 Art. 56 of the EAL. It is worth noting that for domestic awards the application for enforcement shall only be admissible upon the lapse of the period prescribed for filing a nullity action, i.e., ninety days pursuant to Art. 58(1) of the EAL. However, for foreign arbitral awards seated outside Egypt and which are not governed by the EAL, the applicant must submit evidence concerning the status of any nullity action in the country where the award was rendered, as Egyptian courts do not generally have jurisdiction to entertain a setting aside action. The extent of the court review in enforcement procedure differs, subject to the applicable rules to enforcement, be it the EAL or the CCCP. Under the EAL, the grounds for refusal of enforcement are as follows: (1) contradiction of the award with a judgment previously rendered by an Egyptian court on the subject matter of the dispute; (2) violation of public policy in Egypt; and (3) improper notification of the party against whom the award was rendered.379 Art. 58(2) of the EAL. Under the EAL, an enforcement order may be challenged in adversarial proceedings within thirty days of its issuance. A further challenge is possible before the Court of Cassation. c. Court decisions The Egyptian courts’ conception of enforcement and recognition of foreign arbitral award is generally consistent with the international pro-arbitration practice. There are many Egyptian court decisions implementing and interpreting the New York Convention, some of the most relevant and recent ones are mentioned herein below. In 2005, the Court of Cassation rendered an important decision whereby the court held that foreign awards are enforceable under the EAL, given that the enforcement mechanism thereunder is less onerous than the provisions of the CCCP which govern the enforcement of foreign awards. The Court of Cassation highlighted the principle of supremacy of international conventions over national laws by referring to Art. 1 of the EAL, Art. 23 of the Egyptian Civil Code (“ECC”) and Art. 301 of the CCCP, which all provide for the same principle. Therefore, the provisions of the New York Convention apply, and more specifically the Court referred to Art. III thereof380 Art. III of the New York Convention reads in pertinent part: “[…] There shall not be imposed substantially more onerous conditions or higher fees or charges on the recognition or enforcement of arbitral awards to which this convention applies than are imposed on the recognition or enforcement of domestic arbitral awards.” concluding that the more onerous provisions of the CCCP should not apply to foreign awards.381 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 966 of JY 73, Hearing session dated 10 January 2005; and Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 15912 of JY 76, Hearing session dated 6 April 2015; and Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 44 of JY 134, Hearing session dated 9 May 2018. Nevertheless, the Court of Cassation has not excluded the application of the provisions of the CCCP, it continues to allow the party requesting enforcement to elect to enforce the foreign award under the provisions of the CCCP and adds that foreign awards.382 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 913 of JY 73, Hearing session dated 23 February 2010; and Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 5000 of JY 78, Hearing session dated 28 April 2015. It is well established in several court decisions that Egyptian courts do not have jurisdiction to rule over nullity actions of foreign awards, as the competent court would be that of the seat of arbitration where the award was rendered in compliance with the terms of Art. V.1(e) of the New York Convention, and that Egyptian courts may only refuse to grant the enforcement order if any of the grounds for refusal provided under Art. V of the New York Convention is fulfilled.383 Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 1458 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 24 December 2019; Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenged No. 12476 of JY 81, Hearing session dated 23 April 2019; and Cairo Court of Appeal, 50 Commercial Circuit, Appeal No. 57 of JY 127, Hearing session dated 29 May 2012. In a 2020 decision in relation to an international Egyptian-seated arbitration (and not a foreign award), the Court of Cassation held that party representation in arbitral proceedings seated in Egypt (domestic and international) need not be restricted by the requirement under the Egyptian Advocacy Law to be represented by Egyptian lawyers, such that the parties can elect to be represented by any person of their choice, being a lawyer or non-lawyer, an Egyptian or a foreigner, as the rules relating to party representation in arbitration are not part of public policy. In this respect, the Court of Cassation referred to the New York Convention and emphasized that arbitration has gradually distanced itself from strict territorial limitations following the release of the New York Convention, as parties would prefer to appoint non-lawyers to represent them in disputes dealing with complex technicalities, especially if the core of the dispute involves the technical aspect more than the legal issues.384 Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020. In another recent judgment, the Cairo Court of Appeal has expressly held that internationally, arbitration is a conventional and efficient means for resolving commercial disputes and that the success of international commercial arbitration is attributed to the New York Convention and the UNCITRAL Model Law of 1985.385 .Cairo Court of Appeal, (First) Commercial Circuit, Petition No. 2 of JY 139, Hearing session dated 9 March 2022. In this respect, the Court shed light on the importance of the New York Convention, being a distinguished international legal instrument governing the settlement of international trade disputes through arbitration in an equitable, swift and efficient manner. The Court upheld that the New York Convention forms part of the Egyptian legal system and extends the applicability of the EAL provisions to the enforcement of foreign arbitral awards, given that the EAL provisions are less onerous than the default provisions for the enforcement of foreign judgments in the CCCP.386 .Ibid. Accordingly, the Court concluded that national courts are bound to respect the unified and well-known principles in international commercial arbitration, as these “most probably” relate to international procedural public policy.387 .Ibid. In the same vein, the Court of Cassation has expressly held that the Egyptian judiciary adopts a “pro-arbitration policy” with respect to the recognition and enforcement of arbitral awards, by including the English wording of “pro-arbitration policy” in its judgment.388 Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 8199 of JY 80, Hearing session dated 22 March 2022. The Court held that limiting the grounds for the setting aside of an arbitral award is a manifestation of the pro-arbitration policy adopted by Egyptian courts in application of the principle of party autonomy by enforcing their agreement to arbitrate, such that in practice arbitration is the most used mechanism for resolution of international commercial disputes.389 .Ibid. 2. Enforcement Where No Convention or Treaty Applies Foreign arbitral awards can be enforced in Egypt according to the EAL, noting that Egypt has signed the New York Convention without any reservation, hence it has pledged to apply the Convention vis-à-vis all countries irrespective of whether they are signatories to the New York Convention or not, since the reciprocity reservation is not made by Egypt. There are however some scholarly views which refer to the possible application of the provisions of the CCCP applicable to enforcement of foreign judgments,390 Arts. 296-299 of the CCCP. but this view is not consistent with Egypt’s position regarding the New York Convention and is not consistent with the EAL, which enactment was subsequent to the CCCP (i.e., the EAL was enacted twenty-six years after the CCCP). The time limit for seeking enforcement is ten years for awards rendered in commercial matters,391 Art. 68 of the Egyptian Commercial Code. and fifteen years for awards rendered in civil matters.392 Art. 385(2) of the ECC. Both periods run from the date of issuance of the award. There is no requirement to obtain a leave for enforcement from the country where the award was rendered as a prerequisite to enforcement in Egypt. 3. Rules of Public Policy Violation of the rules of public policy is a ground for refusal of enforcement of foreign arbitral awards393 Art. 58(2) of the EAL and Art. 298(4) of the CCCP. and a ground for annulment of domestic awards.394 Art. 53(2) of the EAL. The EAL generally refers to public policy without differentiating between domestic and international public policy, however, Egyptian courts have adopted the distinction between domestic and international public policy in their decisions regarding the enforcement of foreign awards, foreign judgments and the application of foreign laws. The existence of this distinction is clearly stated in the Explanatory Memorandum to Art. 28 of the ECC, which provides that foreign laws that would otherwise apply according to the Egyptian rules of conflict of laws will be excluded if they violate public policy in Egypt.395 Art. 28 of the ECC reads: “The provisions of a foreign law applicable by virtue of the preceding articles shall not be applied if these provisions are contrary to public policy or to morality in Egypt.” The Explanatory Memorandum states that public policy, as envisaged by Art. 28 of the ECC, differs from public policy in a domestic context. The Egyptian Court of Cassation has defined public policy as “the social, political, economic or moral bases of the nation, which pertain to the nation’s consciousness or fundamental public interests of the society”. The Court emphasized that the provisions of a foreign law are not contrary to public policy, as defined above merely because they differ from, or are less favourable than, those of the lex fori.396 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 371 of JY 32, Hearing session dated 5 April 1967. In a case concerning arbitration, the Court of Cassation restated, in clear terms, that the mere contradiction between the foreign law and a mandatory provision of Egyptian law does not suffice to exclude the application of the foreign law. The foreign law must violate the social, political and moral bases of the State to be excluded on grounds of public policy. In that case, the alleged violation related to “the old” Art. 502(3)397 Art. 502(3) of the CCCP has been repealed as it forms part of the old provisions governing arbitration in Egypt prior to the enactment of the EAL in 1994. of the CCCP, which sets a condition for the validity of the arbitration agreement consisting in specifying the names of the arbitrators. The Court held that this requirement was not a matter of international public policy and that the validity of the arbitration agreement is properly governed by the applicable law, which does not mandate that the names of the arbitrators be specified in the arbitration agreement (in this instance English law, being the law of the seat of the arbitration).398 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 714 of JY 47, Hearing session dated 26 April 1982; and Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 1259 of JY 49, Hearing session dated 13 June 1983. In 2013, the Cairo Court of Appeal expressly referred to international public policy while addressing and setting a definition for the fundamental principle of due process in arbitral proceedings, being a ground for initiating a nullity action. The Court held that the principle which nature embodies the notion of justice, requiring the arbitrator to preserve the rights of defence of the parties as well as providing equal treatment to the parties and ensuring that each party is awarded a fair opportunity to submit its claims, defences and documents, and is aware of the other party’s claims, defences and documents which he/she shall be allowed to refute it throughout the proceedings.399 Cairo Court of Appeal, 7th Commercial Circuit, Cases Nos. 20 and 64 of JY 128, Nos. 16, 20 and 47 of 129, Hearing session dated 7 April 2013. Not all mandatory provisions of Egyptian law are principles of public policy within the meaning of Art. 28 of the ECC, such that the courts determine on a case by case basis whether a violation of a particular provision results in excluding the application of the foreign law for contravening public policy in Egypt. In the same vein, the Cairo Court of Appeal considered that a mandatory rule is not necessarily part of public policy and stated that “if public policy encompasses, in general, the fundamental interests of the society affecting its economic, social and political values on which it is based, it is difficult to provide an exhaustive definition for public policy since its content is relative, flexible and evolving.”400 Cairo Court of Appeal, 91 Commercial Circuit, Cases Nos. 108 and 111 of JY 121, Hearing session dated 30 May 2005. The Court of Cassation confirmed the same approach and ruled that the breach of a mandatory rule does not ex officio or ipso facto qualify as a ground for annulment of an award for violation of public policy.401 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 12790 of JY 75, Hearing session dated 22 March 2011. Finally, it is worth noting that an international commercial arbitration is not necessarily an arbitration seated in a foreign state resulting in the issuance of a foreign award. Article 3 of the EAL gives a definition of when an arbitration is international. In this respect, the Court of Cassation held that it is not a prerequisite for an arbitration to be international to have its subject matter related to international trade. The Court added that the EAL adopts specific legislative criteria for a “deemed internationalization”, amongst which is: resorting to arbitral institutions in Egypt or abroad.402 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 11348 of JY 88, Hearing session dated 11 April 2019, which reversed Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 8777 of JY 87, Hearing session dated 7 March 2018. Furthermore, the Court of Cassation has provided insight on what is meant by an arbitral institution under Art. 3 of the EAL and the made reference to the ICC International Court of Arbitration serving as an example of an arbitral institution fulfilling the criteria of Art. 3.403 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 14126 of JY 88, Hearing session dated 22 October 2019. Therefore, when arbitration qualifies as an international commercial arbitration, it may still be seated in Egypt and have Egyptian law as the applicable law to the substance, and consequently be subject to Egyptian public policy including mandatory rules.

CHAPTER VII: MEANS OF RECOURSE 1. Appeal on the Merits from an Arbitral Award a. Appeal to a second arbitral instance Neither the Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I) nor the Arbitration Rules of the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”) provide for an appeal before a court of law or a challenge before a second arbitral tribunal. b. Appeal to a court Arbitral awards are not subject to appeal before Egyptian courts and cannot be challenged on the merits. The challenge of an arbitral award by any of the means of recourse provided under the Egyptian Code of Civil and Commercial Procedures (“CCCP”) (namely appeal before the court of appeal; challenge before the Court of Cassation; and petition for review) is expressly prohibited by the EAL.404 Art. 52(1) of the EAL. 2. Setting Aside of the Arbitral Award (Action for Annulment, Vacation of the Award) a. Grounds for setting aside The grounds upon which an award can be set aside by the court are exhaustively listed in Art. 53(1) of the EAL, which reads in pertinent part: “1. An arbitral award may be annulled only: (a) If there is no arbitration agreement, if it was void, voidable or its duration had elapsed; (b) If either party to the arbitration agreement was at the time of the conclusion of the arbitration agreement fully or partially incapacitated according to the law governing its legal capacity; (c) If either party to the arbitration was unable to present its case as a result of not being given proper notice of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the arbitral proceedings, or for any other reason beyond its control; (d) If the arbitral award failed to apply the law agreed upon by the parties to govern the subject matter in dispute; (e) If the composition of the arbitral tribunal or the appointment of the arbitrators was in conflict with this Law or the parties’ agreement; (f) If the arbitral award dealt with matters not falling within the scope of the arbitration agreement or exceeding the limits of this agreement. However, in the case when matters falling within the scope of the arbitration can be separated from the part of the award which contains matters not included within the scope of the arbitration, the nullity affects exclusively the latter parts only; (g) If the arbitral award itself or the arbitration procedures affecting the award contain a legal violation that causes nullity. […]” Furthermore, the court adjudicating the nullity action shall ipso jure annul the arbitral award if it is contrary to public policy in Egypt.405 Art. 53(2) gof the EAL. Egyptian courts strictly adhere to the grounds for annulment provided under the EAL. The court cannot reconsider the merits of the dispute. On many occasions this principle has been affirmed by both, the Court of Cassation,406 Court of Cassation, Challenge No. 7303 of JY 76, Hearing session dated 8 February 2007; and Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020; Court of Cassation, Civil and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 12262 of JY 90, Hearing session dated 24 June 2021; Court of Cassation, Civil and Commercial Circuit, Challenges Nos. 1964 and 1968 of JY 91, Hearing session dated 8 July 2021; and Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 13892 of JY 81, Hearing session dated 22 February 2022. and the Cairo Court of Appeal407 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 6 of JY 124, Hearing session dated 16 April 2007; Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 24 of JY 120, Hearing session dated 30 March 2004; Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 46 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 22 November 2003; Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 17 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 26 June 2002; and Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 61 of JY 115, Hearing session dated 21 April 1999. which have held that arbitral awards may not be reviewed on the merits and that the nullity action is not an appeal. In a recent decision, the Court of Cassation held that judicial review of awards should not transcend the normative limitations and courts may not (1) review the arbitral tribunal’s understanding and assessment of the facts; and (2) ascertain whether the arbitral tribunal erred on the application of the law governing the merits. The Court also held that there is a presumption that the proceedings have been properly conducted, which implies that the party, which claims otherwise, would naturally bear the burden of proof. The Court emphasized that the grounds for setting aside arbitral awards are exhaustively defined in Art. 53 of the EAL and that the Court may not review the arbitral award to examine its adequacy/appropriateness and/or to ascertain the soundness of the determinations of the arbitrators.408 Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020. An award cannot be set aside on the basis of an error in law. The Cairo Court of Appeal rejected an application to set aside in a case where the applicant alleged that the arbitral tribunal did not comply with the provisions of the contract, breached the provisions of the tender law and failed to properly apply the provisions of the Civil Code.409 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 4 of JY 119, Hearing session dated 30 December 2002. In the same vein, the Court rejected an application to set aside and held that the annulment of an arbitral award is only due to procedural defaults or errors which relate to fundamental procedural standards prescribed by law and which cannot be overridden. The defect in application of the law does not constitute any contravention to public policy rules and does not lead, in itself, to the annulment of the arbitral award.410 Cairo Court of Appeal, Case No. 32 of JY 133, Hearing session dated 8 August 2016. Violation of public policy is one of the grounds for setting aside an award and is the only ground which must be invoked ex officio by the court.411 Art. 53(2) of the EAL. The court should not, under cover of setting aside the award for violation of public policy, review the merits of the case. This is confirmed by the Court of Appeal, which explained that a violation of Egyptian public policy consists in the contravention of the societal, political, economic, and ethical foundations in Egypt, which relates to the society’s higher interests, and is considered as elements of its structure. Therefore, if the arbitral tribunal violates a legal rule in the applicable law or defaults in choosing or interpreting the legal rule applicable to the merits of the dispute, this cannot be characterized – in light of standards of reason – as a deficiency constituting a violation of society’s higher interests or its fundamental values. The defect in application of the law does not constitute any contravention of public policy rules and does not lead, in itself, to the annulment of the arbitral award.412 Cairo Court of Appeal, 7th Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 32 of JY 133, Hearing session dated 8 August 2016. With respect to the saga of setting aside the Al Kharafi v. Libya arbitral award rendered on 22 March 2013 under the auspices of the Unified Agreement for the Investment of Arab Capital in the Arab States (“UAIACAS”), the Court of Appeal annulled the arbitral award rendered in favour of Al Kharafi against the Libyan State on the ground of breach of the public policy principle of proportionality in the assessment of damages. The Court of Appeal considered, inter alia, that the arbitral tribunal awarded exorbitant compensation that is grossly exaggerated beyond the actual damage sustained by Al Kharafi.413 Cairo Court of Appeal, First Commercial Circuit, Case No. 39 of JY 130, Hearing session dated 3 June 2020. The annulled arbitral award was issued in 2013 awarding US$ 900+ Million to Al Kharafi Group for the damages sustained thereby as a result of being unable to take possession of the land and start its investment touristic project. This is the third time the Libyan State brings the application for setting aside before the Court of Appeal (constituted in different circuits after referral by the Court of Cassation). The Court of Appeal dismissed the first two nullity actions on different grounds: (i) inadmissibility; and (ii) lack of international jurisdiction, however, the Court of Cassation on the two occasions reversed the Court of Appeal’s decisions and held that on the first time that the Court of Appeal has jurisdiction to decide on the nullity action. On the second time, the Court of Cassation held that the Court of Appeal violated the former’s decision confirming that the latter has jurisdiction, and should have examined the case, on the basis of the principle of res judicata effect of the Court of Cassation decisions: (Court of Cassation, Commercial and Economic Circuit, Challenge No. 18615 of JY 88, Hearing session dated 10 December 2019). However, after almost a decade, the Court of Cassation finally upheld the validity of the well-known Al Kharafi v. Libya arbitral award and dismissed the Libyan State’s motion to set aside the arbitral award.414 Court of Cassation, Civil and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 12262 of JY 90, Hearing session dated 24 June 2021. The Court affirmed the longstanding principle that the role of review courts is limited to examining the nullity of the arbitral award strictly based on the grounds set forth under Art. 53 of the EAL and that the review court (in this case the Cairo Court of Appeal) shall not conduct a de novo review of the merits. This means that an error in the arbitral tribunal’s assessment does not qualify as a ground for setting aside the arbitral award.415 .Ibid. On another occasion, the Court of Cassation, in a highly contentious decision, for the first time in its history annulled an ICC arbitral award without remanding it to the Court of Appeal, on the basis that the award violated a fundamental principle of Egyptian public policy by ruling on the legality of an administrative decision.416 Court of Cassation, Civil and Commercial Circuit, Challenges Nos. 1964 and 1968 of JY 91, Hearing session dated 8 July 2021. The Court of Cassation reasoned that judicial control on the legality of an administrative decision – its validity and fulfilment of formal requirements – falls within the exclusive jurisdictional competence of the State Council courts, that this is a fundamental principle of public policy, and therefore, in application of Art. 53.2 of the EAL, the Court should set aside the arbitral award on its own initiative.417 Ibid. Despite the unexpected outcome, the Court of Cassation reiterated its well-established principle that review courts shall not conduct a de novo review of the merits or even delve into the arbitral tribunal’s findings, unless a matter of public policy is in play. The Court of Cassation made it clear that review courts must strictly abide by the nullity grounds stipulated in Art. 53 of the EAL.418 Ibid. Under the EAL, the parties may exclude the right to set aside the award only after the issuance of the arbitral award. Any renouncement to the right for setting aside made prior to the issuance of the award will not preclude its admissibility.419 Art. 54(1) of the EAL. The only available action to be brought is an action for setting aside, the EAL does not provide for separate actions. The question as to whether the parties may restrict the grounds for setting aside is not addressed by the EAL and has yet to be considered by the courts. This should be possible as a matter of principle, because such an agreement would merely constitute a partial waiver by the parties of their right to set aside.420 Presumably, such a partial waiver would, like a complete waiver, only be valid if made after the issuance of the award. b. Procedure The action for setting aside must be brought within the ninety days from the date of notification of the award to the losing party.421 Art. 54(1) of the EAL. This time limit is the same for all actions of setting aside, regardless of the grounds upon which it is based. There is no special form prescribed for the action to set aside. It is an ordinary action like all court actions, beginning with the filing of an originating summons, exchanging memorials, and a hearing.422 Art. 297 of the CCCP. Under the EAL, a party can be barred from invoking one or more grounds by that party’s appearance in the arbitration without raising an objection within a reasonable time, as the latter shall be deemed to have waived its right to object.423 Art. 8 of the EAL. In this regard, the Court of Cassation held in a very recent decision that if a party continued with the arbitration proceedings without an objection, despite its knowledge of a breach of a condition of the arbitration agreement or one of the non-mandatory provisions of the EAL, this would be deemed a waiver of his/her right to object. The Court went further to hold that even if the party had invoked its objection earlier, the challenge would still be rejected because a person may not benefit from his/her own wrongdoing. The Court also added that the principle of estoppel would militate against the success of the appellant’s plea, and remarked that estoppel is in application of the universal maxim “non concedit venire contra factum proprium” which is derived from Roman law. The Court innovatively unveiled two conditions for application of this principle, these are: (1) a statement, an act, or an omission is made by a party and contradicts with its previous conduct; and (2) that contradiction would prejudice the other party who acted in reliance on the validity of the first party’s previous conduct.424 Court of Cassation, Economic and Commercial Circuit, Challenge No. 18309 of JY 89, Hearing session dated 27 October 2020. Proceedings to enforce the award may be initiated or continue pending the rendering of the court’s decision on the application to set aside. The EAL does not stipulate or contemplate the possibility for the court to remit the arbitral award to the tribunal to give it the opportunity to eliminate the ground(s) on which the award could otherwise be set aside.425 Without prejudice to the arbitral tribunal’s powers to correct typographical, computation and clerical errors, to interpret the award and to issue additional awards, as discussed under the appropriate headings herein above. If the award is set aside on grounds that are unrelated to the arbitration agreement, the agreement remains valid and in force between the parties.426 Fathi Waly, op. cit., p. 806. c. Waivers Waiver of the right to bring an action for setting aside is permitted only after the issuance of the arbitral award. The waiver of this right before the issuance of the award is ineffective and will not render a subsequent application to set aside inadmissible.427 Art. 54(1) of the EAL. In principle, the exclusion concerns all grounds for setting aside, as it consists in a waiver of the right itself. The EAL is silent on whether the exclusion may concern only some of the grounds for setting aside, however, this has yet to be tested by courts, as it would consist in a partial waiver of the setting aside grounds which follows the same logic of the full waiver. d. Effect of an award that has been set aside An award that has been set aside has no effect and cannot be enforced. Any decision regarding the correction or the interpretation of the award loses its effect accordingly. However, if the award has been partially set aside, only the part of the award which has been set aside has no effect, but the part of the award that was not set aside remains valid and is enforceable.428 Art. 53(1)(f) of the EAL; and Fathi Waly, op. cit., pp. 804-805. 3. Other Means of Recourse Whilst the nullity action is the only means of recourse against the enforcement of the arbitral award, the judgment rendered by the competent court annulling or refusing annulment of the award, may be subject to an appeal, and the appeal may be subject to a challenge before the Court of Cassation, which supervises and ensures the correct application of the law by the Appeal Courts.

CHAPTER VIII: CONCILIATION / MEDIATION 1. General a. ADR in Egypt The UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Conciliation has not been adopted in Egypt. It is worth mentioning that it is common for parties to agree to include an amicable settlement phase in the dispute settlement clauses in their contracts, and this of itself shows an interest for the parties to attempt resolving their potential dispute amicably and keeping the arbitration as a last resort. However, the amicable settlement mechanisms usually provided for are not based on a set of conciliation rules, rather they are expressed in general terms without proposing a specific mechanism to follow. Egypt is among the most pro-ADR countries in the region and promotes ADR. There is a noticeable trend in Egypt to use conciliation or mediation, which is evidenced by the establishment of new institutions that comprise, besides arbitration, conciliation and mediation dedicated rules and services. b. Institutions The leading arbitration institution, the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (“CRCICA”), offers ADR services which include mediation, conciliation and mini-trials,429 Mini-trials are procedures where the parties agree to refer their dispute to a panel composed of two members selected from among the senior corporate officers of each party, and a neutral umpire. The two-member panel should attempt to reach a settlement, failing which the umpire will propose one that the parties are not bound to accept. and promotes their use through its Mediation and ADR Centre.430 By way of example, on 26 December 2021, the CRCICA and the CIArb Egypt Branch jointly organized a mock mediation construction case, with the objective to raise awareness for the use of mediation in the construction industry. In early 2022, the CRCICA offered a two-level mediation training aiming at promoting the use of mediation as an important tool to settle disputes. The first level training was titled “Fundamentals of Mediation” and was held on 25-26 January 2022, and the second level training was titled “Qualifying the Mediator” and was held on 29-30 January and 1-2 February 2022. See CRCICA Annual Report 2021-2022 available on CRCICA’s official website at: <https://crcica.org/wp-content/uploads/‌2022/11/Annual-Report-2021-2022.pdf>. The CRCICA also strongly recommends the parties to use Arb-Med-Arb and puts at their disposition a guidance note.431 Arb-Med-Arb Guidance Note available at: <https://crcica.org/FilesEnglish/NewPages_‌2019-06-10_09-43-0-809697.pdf>. The CRCICA has its own set of mediation rules, the most recent version thereof entered into force on 1 January 2013, and it is available in Arabic and English. There is also the Egyptian Center for Arbitration and Settlement of Non-Banking Financial Disputes (“ECAS”)432 The official website for the Egyptian Center for Arbitration and Settlement of Non-Banking Financial Disputes is available at: <https://ecas.org.eg/en>. established within the Financial Regulatory Authority for the settlement of financial non-banking disputes by virtue of the Presidential Decree No. 335 of 2019. The ECAS offers mediation services. In this regard, the ECAS statutes as well as its arbitration and mediation rules have been issued by virtue of the Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2597 of 2020 on 10 December 2020. 2. Legal Provisions There are several specific laws and regulations that introduce mediation or conciliation as an initial means to resolve disputes before referral to courts or arbitral tribunals, as the case may be. Amongst such laws, are the following: – Bankruptcy Law No. 11 of 2018:433 The Law regulating Restructuring, Preventive Reconciliation, and Bankruptcy No. 11 of 2018 (the “Bankruptcy Law”) is a special law that repealed and replaced the provisions incorporated under Title 5 of the Egyptian Commercial Code, which previously governed insolvency proceedings in Egypt. the Bankruptcy Law provides for mediation under the supervision of the bankruptcy judge, acting as the mediator aiming to reach an amicable settlement of the dispute at any stage of the bankruptcy proceedings at the request of any interested party.434 Art. 178 of the Bankruptcy Law. Mediation was not expressly provided for under the old bankruptcy regime, i.e., Title 5 of the Egyptian Commercial Code. The Bankruptcy Law added this express reference to mediation to encourage the creditors to settle their debts with the debtor in a faster and more efficient proceeding under the supervision of the judge. Thus, the bankruptcy judge’s mission is to reach a settlement agreement between parties through any process he/she deems necessary (meetings with all parties or with each party separately, by doing what is necessary to reach a compromise acceptable to the parties, etc.). If the dispute is amicably settled between the parties, the agreed settlement agreement shall be signed by all parties. The bankruptcy judge shall issue a decision ratifying the mediation. The settlement agreement shall have the force of an executive writ.435 Arts. 7 to 10 of the Bankruptcy Law. – The Settlement Committee under the Electricity Law No. 230 of 2016: This committee is specialized in examining disputes that arise between the electricity utility parties. The committee’s members include technical, financial, commercial and legal experts. The said committee issues its justified decisions in relation to the disputes within a time limit not exceeding sixty days from the date of submitting the request along with all its related documents. The committee’s decisions are presented to the board of directors of the electricity authority for approval. – Economic Courts Establishment Law No. 120 of 2008: in every economic court in Egypt, a preparatory committee of disputes is established to undertake the conciliation process of most of the cases in which such courts have jurisdiction prior to initiating the legal process.436 Art. 8 of Law No. 120 of 2008 establishing Economic Courts (the “Economic Courts Establishment Law”). If the dispute is amicably settled between the parties, the agreed and signed settlement agreement shall have the force of an executive writ. – Family Courts Establishment Law No. 10 of 2004: prior to initiating a legal action before family courts in relation to personal status matters that cannot be subject to compromise, the plaintiff shall submit a request for settlement of the dispute addressed to the dispute settlement division within the family court.437 Art. 6 of Law No. 10 of 2004 establishing Family Courts (the “Family Courts Establishment Law”). In the same context of mediation, the parties may contractually agree that all disagreements be settled amicably by mediation within a determined timeframe otherwise the parties may recourse to the competent courts or arbitral tribunals. The Egyptian Code of Civil and Commercial Procedures (the “CCCP”) provides for conciliation in relation to certain types of disputes, excluding disputes that are not capable of settlement, summary proceedings, execution disputes, and applications for writ of debt. A “Conciliation Panel” shall be constituted in every district court composed of the two parties and presided by a Prosecutor. The panel shall terminate its mission within a period of thirty days that may be extended for an additional thirty days, subject to the agreement of the parties to reach a settlement of the dispute. A record would be made of any settlement reached within the foregoing period, including its terms. In the absence of an agreement, the Conciliation Panel would refer the dispute to the court and a hearing would be scheduled.438 Art. 64 of the CCCP. In addition to the provisions set out under the CCCP, Law No. 7 of 2000 establishing conciliation panels in relation to some disputes to which ministries and public juridical persons are party, provides for further requirements as to conciliation of disputes involving public entities. A conciliation panel shall be established in each ministry, or governorate or public entity to seek an amicable settlement of civil, commercial and administrative disputes.439 Art. 1 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The Minister of Justice shall issue a decree to constitute the conciliation panel, which shall be presided by a former judge or member of a judicial entity having a minimum grade of counsel and who ceased exercising his/her profession together with the representative of the administrative authority (with minimum grade of general manager) from one side and the other party or his/her representative from the other side.440 Art. 2 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The disputes excluded from the scope of this Law relate to disputes involving the Ministry of Defence or any of its bodies, disputes relating to in rem rights, or other disputes specifically excluded by a law or a dispute that requires involvement of a judicial entity shall be referred to arbitration.441 Art. 4 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The time limit provided for under the CCCP is not applicable to conciliation governed by this Law.442 Art. 7 of Law No. 7 of 2000. Meetings are valid only when all members are present, and the panel may resort to an expert’s assistance, and renders its recommendations by majority. Deliberations are confidential.443 Art. 8 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The recommendations shall be issued along with a brief reference to the reasons based upon recorded in the minutes and within a time limit that does not exceed sixty days from the date of submission of the application for conciliation. The recommendation shall be presented to the concerned authority and the other party to the dispute within seven days from the date of its issuance. If the two parties agree in writing to its findings within fifteen days from the date of its receipt, the panel records what has been agreed in the minutes which shall be signed by the parties, and has the same force of a writ of execution, then shall inform the competent authority executing it.444 Art. 9 of Law No. 7 of 2000. However, if one of the parties does not agree to the panel’s recommendation or if no agreement is reached at the elapse of the period provided for in this Law, each of the parties has the right to resort to the competent court to settle the dispute, such that the limitation periods, time bar periods, and procedural deadlines are suspended during conciliation.445 Art. 10 of Law No. 7 of 2000. An action initiated before a court, in a matter subject to this Law, before conciliation is inadmissible, except for matters related to summary proceedings, execution disputes, applications for writ of debt or writ of execution, and administrative decisions related to stay of execution.446 Art. 11 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The CCCP is silent as to the confidential nature or otherwise of the contemplated conciliation proceedings. However, Law No. 7 of 2000 provides for confidentiality of conciliation proceedings.447 Art. 8 of Law No. 7 of 2000. The EAL (see Annex I hereto) does not specify whether an arbitrator may also act as a conciliator during the arbitral proceedings. However, the parties are free to agree on the procedure to be followed by the arbitral tribunal and may confer such power to the arbitrator,448 Art. 25 of the EAL. so if the parties agree, nothing would prevent an arbitrator to act as conciliator. There is no express restriction for appointment of a person as a conciliator then as arbitrator, subject to the disclosure requirement when the person is to be appointed as arbitrator, otherwise the arbitrator may be challenged.449 Art. 18 of the EAL. If the arbitration agreement does not impose to attempt to settle amicably the dispute before resorting to arbitration, the parties are free to decide whether they want to attempt amicable settlement or if they can immediately proceed with arbitration. Anyhow, at any stage of the arbitral proceedings, the parties may agree to settle and request the tribunal to issue an award by consent.450 Art. 41 of the EAL. However, when conciliation or mediation is conducted under the auspices of an institution, the rules for conciliation or mediation apply. This would be the case for the CRCICA and the ECAS.

CHAPTER IX: INVESTMENT TREATY ARBITRATION 1. conventions and treaties Egypt is a party to the Washington International Convention on the Settlement of Investment Disputes between States and Nationals of Other States of 1965 (the “ICSID Convention”), which was ratified on 3 May 1972. Egypt is also a party to multilateral arbitration-related treaties and conventions which include the following: the Convention of the Arab League on the Enforcement of Judgments and Arbitral Awards of 1952 (the “Arab League Convention”) ratified on 28 August 1954; the Unified Agreement for Investment of Arab Capital in the Arab States (the “Arab Investment Agreement”) signed on 26 November 1980 in Amman and which entered into force on 7 September 1981; the Organisation of the Islamic Conference Investment Agreement of 1981 (the “OIC Investment Agreement”) ratified in February 1988; the Convention establishing the Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency (the “MIGA Convention”) of 1985; the COMESA Investment Agreement signed on 23 May 2007; the Riyadh Arab Agreement for Judicial Cooperation of 1983 signed in 2014; and the Egypt-MERCOSUR Preferential Free Trade Agreement which has entered into force in September 2017. As to bilateral treaties, Egypt has signed more than 100 Bilateral Investment Treaties (“BITs”) of which around seventy-four BITs have entered into force.451 The list of BITs concluded by Egypt and their date of entry into force is as follows: Albania (6/4/1994); Algeria (3/5/2000); Argentina (3/12/1993); Armenia (2/3/2006); Australia (5/9/2002); Austria (29/4/2002); Bahrain (11/1/1999); Belarus (18/1/1999); Belgium–Luxembourg Economic Union (24/5/2002); Bosnia & Herzegovina (29/10/2001); Bulgaria (3/6/2000); Canada (3/11/1997); China (1/4/1996); Comoros (27/2/2000); Croatia (2/5/1999); Cyprus (9/6/1999); Czech Republic (5/6/1994); Denmark (29/1/2000); Ethiopia (27/5/2010); Finland (5/2/2005); France (1/10/1975); Germany (22/11/2009); Greece (6/4/1995); Hungary (21/8/1997); Iceland (15/6/2009); Italy (1/5/1994); Japan (14/1/1978); Jordan (11/4//1998); Kazakhstan (8/8/1996); Korean Democratic People’s Republic (20/12/1999); Korean Republic (25/5/1997); Kuwait (26/4/2002); Latvia (3/6/1998); Lebanon (6/2/1997); Libya (4/7/1991); Malawi (7/9/1999); Malaysia (8/7/2000); Mali (7/7/2000); Malta (18/7/2000); Mauritius (17/10/2014); Mongolia (25/1/2005); Morocco (27/6/1998); Netherlands (1/3/1998); Oman (3/5/2000); Palestine (19/6/1999); Poland (17/1/1998); Portugal (23/12/2000); Qatar (14/7/2006); Romania (3/4/1997); Russia (12/6/2000); Serbia (20/3/2006); Singapore (20/3/2002); Slovakia (1/1/2000); Slovenia (7/2/2000); Somalia (16/4/1983); Spain (26/4/1994); Sri Lanka (10/3/1998); State of Palestine (19/6/1999); Sudan (1/4/2003); Sweden (29/1/1979); Switzerland (15/5/2012); Syria (5/10/1998); Thailand (4/3/2002); Tunisia (2/1/1991); Turkey (31/7/2002); Turkmenistan (29/3/1996); United Arab Emirates (11/1/1999); Ukraine (13/10/1993); United Kingdom (24/2/1976); United States of America (27/6/1992); Uzbekistan (8/2/1994); Vietnam (4/3/2002); and Yemen (10/4/1998). (UNCTAD Investment Policy Hub, available at <https://investmentpolicy.unctad.org/international-investment-agreements/countries/62/‌egypt)>. The majority of these multilateral treaties and BITs are published on the UNCTAD website. Egypt employs a Model BIT that was adopted in 2022, adjusting some provisions of the preceding versions of the Model BIT of 2013 and 2019, to pose a model for renewal of BITs. In this regard, it is worth mentioning some of the new amendments/additions introduced to the 2022 Model BIT, which include the following: (1) The definition of an “investor” under the new Model BIT excludes commercial agents, companies’ branches and representative offices. Such exclusion was not provided under the preceding versions; (2) The 2022 Model BIT maintained the inclusion of provisions related to third-party funding which were initially added by the 2019 Model BIT, where it is recognized that a party may have recourse to third-party funding but has a strict duty of disclosure of the funding agreement, either before the commencement of the arbitral proceedings or immediately after the conclusion of the funding agreement, and such duty of disclosure is continuous throughout the proceedings. The third-party funder would not qualify as a party to the arbitral proceedings and would not be entitled to any rights under the BIT. The failure of disclosure of the funding agreement shall be deemed a manifest contravention of a fundamental rule of procedure agreed upon by the disputing parties. Also, the existence of a third-party funding agreement shall be considered in the probable existence of a conflict of interests with an arbitrator, an expert, or a legal representative; and (3) Article 17 of the 2022 Model BIT (“Settlement of Disputes between an Investor and a Contracting Party”) has been significantly amended, reintroducing the reference to ICSID arbitration but under strict conditions. The amended version of the Model BIT provides for a shorter period of six months (instead of twelve months) for amicable settlement through conducting negotiations, conciliation or mediation without providing for specific rules, unlike the preceding 2019 Model BIT, which listed examples of mediation and conciliation rules. If the disputing parties fail to amicably resolve their dispute, and unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the investor shall resort to the dispute resolution mechanisms provided under the laws (of the host state) regulating the investment. In the preceding version, the 2019 Model BIT only referred to dispute resolution mechanisms under the Egyptian Investment Law. However, the major change in the 2022 Model BIT is the introduction of a third dispute settlement mechanism, which the disputing parties may only resort to in case of failure to settle their dispute (1) amicably or (2) through the dispute resolution mechanisms provided under the laws regulating the investment (i.e., the Egyptian Investment Law) within a period of twelve months, provided that the parties have not agreed to a longer period. In this respect, and subject to not having resorted to national courts of the host state to settle the same dispute, either party may settle their dispute through arbitration before any of the following institutions: ICSID, PCA, ad hoc arbitration (UNCITRAL), or any arbitration rules that the parties agree upon, noting that the parties’ choice of any of the aforementioned options is final and excludes resorting to the other available options. 2. investment arbitration Egypt has been party to many investment arbitrations. At the time of drafting this report, there have been thirty-eight ICSID cases brought against Egypt, among which seven are still pending and twenty-eight have been concluded.452 ICSID official website: <https://icsid-archive.worldbank.org/en/Pages/cases/Advanced‌Search.aspx>. The recognition and enforcement of ICSID awards in Egypt remain, primarily, a theoretical matter. Practice has shown that: awards were either rendered in favour of the Egyptian state; disputes were settled before a final award was rendered; or where awards were rendered in favour of the investor, post-award settlements were reached between Egypt and the investor. 3. national investment legislation The Investment Law in Egypt witnessed considerable change and constant developments since the enactment of the first Investment Law No. 65 of 1971 until enactment of the current Investment Law No. 72 of 2017.453 In between the two Investment Laws, there were three other Investment Laws enacted, respectively: Law No. 43 of 1974 on Investment of Arab and Foreign Capital and Free Zones, repealed and replaced by Law No. 230 of 1989 and the Investment Guarantees and Incentives Law No. 8 of 1997 which repealed and replaced Law No. 230 of 1989. The provisions relating to dispute settlement have evolved throughout the different laws, aiming to set forth a rigid mechanism of dispute avoidance and amicable settlement to minimize the financial burden of investment arbitrations. The current Investment Law No. 72 of 2017, has a dedicated title named “Title (V) – Settlement of Investment Disputes” divided into four Chapters, respectively: Chapter 1 – The Grievance Committee; Chapter 2 – The Ministerial Committee on Investment Dispute Resolution; Chapter 3 – The Ministerial Committee for Settlement of Investment Contracts Disputes; and Chapter 4 – Amicable Dispute Settlement Means and the Arbitration and Mediation Centre. The Investment Law encourages amicable settlement of any dispute through negotiations between the parties and at any time, without prejudice to the right to litigation.454 Art. 82 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Grievance Committee has the authority to examine complaints filed by investors against decisions/orders relating to issuance of approvals, permits and licenses.455 Art. 83 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Ministerial Committee on Investment Dispute Resolution examines applications, complaints or disputes between investors and the State or any of its entities. The Committee’s decisions, after being approved by the Cabinet of Ministers shall be enforceable and binding upon the concerned administrative authority.456 Art. 87 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Ministerial Committee for Settlement of Investment Contracts Disputes shall examine the differences arising between the parties to the investment contract. The Committee is entitled, subject to the parties’ consent, to make the necessary settlement and restore the equilibrium of the contract, extend time limits and periods or grace periods provided under the contract. The Committee may also re-schedule financial dues or rectify procedures which precede the conclusion of contracts to restore the contractual equilibrium to achieve the best economic situation for both, public funds and investor’s rights.457 Art. 89 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. Investment disputes shall be settled in the manner agreed with the investor or pursuant to the provisions of the Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994 (the “EAL”, see Annex I). The parties may at any stage of the dispute agree to pursue any type of settlement pursuant to the rules applicable to the dispute which include ad hoc arbitration and institutional arbitration.458 Art. 90 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Egyptian Arbitration and Mediation Centre shall be established in Cairo to administer the settlement of investment disputes which may arise among investors or between an investor and the State or one of its entities.459 Art. 91 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. The Investment Law does not include express reference to ICSID arbitration or a specific arbitral institution other than the Egyptian Arbitration and Mediation Centre. Furthermore, in 2019, a new committee was established – the “High Committee for Studying and Opining on International Arbitration Disputes”.460 Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019. This committee’s role is to examine and opine on all arbitration disputes, whether commercial or investment arbitrations, to which the state or any ministry, or public state entities, or governmental bodies and their affiliated entities, or an affiliated company to the state or whereby the state is a shareholder to said company, is party thereto.461 Art. 2 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 (amending Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019). The decisions of this committee are binding upon the concerned state entities, once approved by the Council of Ministers.462 Art. 1 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019. Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019 issued on 24 April 2019 was amended twice by virtue of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 issued on 9 December 2020 and Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022 issued on 13 September 2022. These are the latest amendments in a series of decrees that date back to 2013 and were amended in 2014 and 2019.463 Decrees Nos. 108/2013 (revoked), 283/2013 (revoked), 847/2013 (revoked), 1255/2014 (revoked), 1966/2014 (revoked), 1062/2019 (amended by Decrees Nos. 2592/2020 and 3218/2022). Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 renamed an existing State Committee entrusted with overseeing arbitration proceedings and international disputes from the “High Committee for Studying and Opining on International Arbitration Cases” to the “High Committee for Arbitration and International Disputes”. The Committee, which is reconstituted every now and then, is entrusted with reviewing and opining on pending international arbitration cases in both commercial and investment cases involving the State, its organs and state-controlled entities. The rationale underlying the establishment of such Committee is to keep track of pending cases and streamline the approach taken by the State, its organs and the state-controlled entities regarding the arbitrations involving same. It is intended to serve as a central focal point for review, follow up and deciding on appointments, the approaches to be adopted and providing any necessary support. Art. 2 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 sets the expanded scope of the Committee, but clearly states that this is without prejudice to Arts. 89, 90 and 91 of the Investment Law No. 72 of 2017. Art. 90 of the 2017 Investment Law clearly states that it is permissible to settle the investment disputes pertaining to the provisions of that Law in the manner agreed with the investor or in accordance with the Egyptian Arbitration Law No. 27 of 1994. The same provisions also refer to ad hoc and institutional arbitration as available options, in addition to other ADR processes. In light of the above, Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 does not present an obstacle to investor-state arbitration involving Egypt, especially as it makes express reference to the prevalence of Arts. 89, 90 and 91 of the Investment Law, and does not impact any of the IIAs (BITs/MITs) concluded by Egypt. In addition to what the previous decrees had traditionally provided regarding overseeing the proceedings, offering support, reviewing the cases and engaging in settlements, the Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 expands the scope of the Committee to cover the following: (1) arbitrations involving companies in which the State participates in any form (i.e., no minimum shareholding threshold, which could be practically problematic for JV companies in which the State or its organs hold very minimal stake and do not control); (2) endorsing the nomination of arbitrators, counsel, experts appointed/instructed by the State; (3) opining on state contracts including an arbitration clause, or other contracts concluded with foreign investors, such that the Committee will draft the clauses of such contracts (arbitration, force majeure, change in law, etc.) to achieve a balanced equilibrium, as expressly stated in Art. 2(2) of the Decree; (4) endorsing the budgets for the cases involving the State (fees, costs and expenses); and (5) any other matters entrusted to the Committee by the President or the Prime Minister. Art. 6 of the Prime Ministerial Decree No. 2592 of 2020 instructs state organs, public entities, state-owned companies and companies in which the State participates in any form not to conclude any contracts with foreign investors or enter into contracts including an arbitration clause, and/or amending these contracts without having this first reviewed by the Committee is an attempt to streamline practices and approaches to these contracts and to the arbitration clauses contained therein. Lastly, in September 2022, Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022 amended for the second time Prime Ministerial Decree No. 1062 of 2019 by introducing a single amendment,464 Art. 1 of Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022. which consists in expanding the scope of review of the High Committee for Arbitration and International Disputes. Prime Ministerial Decree No. 3218 of 2022 instructs state organs, public entities, state-owned companies and companies in which the State participates in any form (i.e. no minimum shareholding threshold is required) not to take any measure that would lead to the rescission or the termination of any contract including an arbitration agreement, without having this first reviewed by the Committee. Another important legislative update is worth mentioning, namely the Supreme Constitutional Court’s Law No. 48 of 1979, which was amended on 15 August 2021 by Law No. 137 of 2021. The amendments introduced by Law No. 137 of 2021 extend the Supreme Constitutional Court rationae materiae jurisdiction when reviewing the constitutionality of decisions/judgments rendered against the Egyptian State, when enforcement is sought in Egypt.465 Art. 27 bis of Law No. 137 of 2021 amending the Supreme Constitutional Court Law No. 48 of 1979. Furthermore, the Prime Minister may request the Supreme Constitutional Court to disregard any decision or judgment rendered by an international organization or by a foreign court, and the obligations deriving therefrom, when enforcement is sought against the Egyptian State in Egypt, and the Supreme Constitutional Court shall promptly rule on said request.466 Art. 33 bis of Law No. 137 of 2021 amending the Supreme Constitutional Court Law No. 48 of 1979. The request shall indicate (1) the alleged violated constitutional provision or constitutional court judgment; and (2) the nature of said violation.467 Ibid. More precisely, the Supreme Constitutional Court is now vested with the authority to review the constitutionality of (1) decisions rendered by international organizations; and (2) foreign court judgments where enforcement is sought against the Egyptian State in Egypt.468 Art. 27 bis of Law No. 137 of 2021 amending the Supreme Constitutional Court Law No. 48 of 1979. Prior to its enactment, an earlier draft of Law No. 137 of 2021 had included the review of arbitral awards within the extended scope of review vested in the Supreme Constitutional Court. However, this reference to arbitral awards was not included in the final version of the Law as adopted by the Egyptian Parliament and published in the Official Gazette, such that the Supreme Constitutional Court will not have jurisdiction to review arbitral awards, which remain subject to the traditional review regimes under the EAL and any pertinent applicable international treaties.

title: Law on Mediation, in effect 20 December 2004, as amended
