sections.text: Previous version# For a copy of a previous version of this document, should this be available, please contact customer support.

LAW ON MEDIATION* In effect from 20 December 2004, as amended. Unofficial translation by Assen Alexiev; the translator acknowledges the assistance of an earlier translation of this Law by the Bulgarian Chamber of Commerce and Industry Mediation Center. Promulgated SG No. 110 of 17 December 2004, amended and supplemented, SG No. 86 of 24 October 2006, amended SG No. 9 of 28 January 2011, amended SG No. 27 of 1 April 2011, amended and supplemented SG No. 77 of 18 September 2018, amended SG No. 17 of 26 February 2019, amended and supplemented SG No. 11 of 2 February 2023, amended SG 57 of 5 July 2024.

sections.title: CHAPTER ONE. GENERAL PROVISIONS
sections.text: CHAPTER ONE. GENERAL PROVISIONS Scope of Application Art. 1. (1) (Former text of Art. 1 – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) This Law shall regulate the relationships associated with mediation as an alternative method of resolution of legal and non-legal1 Translator’s note: In the legal doctrine, a “non-legal” dispute is a dispute for gap filling in a contract or its adaptation/termination in case of hardship. disputes. (2) (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) The Law shall also regulate the mediation procedure in pending court cases. Concept of Mediation Art. 2. Mediation is a voluntary and confidential procedure for out of court dispute settlement whereby a third-party mediator assists the disputing parties in reaching a settlement. Subject Matter of the Mediation Art. 3. (1) (Supplemented – SG No. 27 of 1 April 2011) Civil, commercial, labour, family and administrative disputes related to consumer rights, and other disputes between natural and/or legal persons, including where such disputes are cross border, may be the subject matter of mediation. (2) Mediation may also be conducted in the cases envisaged in the Criminal Procedure Code. (3) Mediation may not be conducted if a law or any other statutory instrument provides for another procedure for the conclusion of a settlement. Organization of the Mediation Art. 4. Mediation shall be carried out by natural persons. Such persons may become partners for the purpose of carrying out the activity. Persons performing judicial functions in the judiciary branch of government may not carry out mediation activities.

sections.title: CHAPTER TWO. PRINCIPLES OF MEDIATION
sections.text: CHAPTER TWO. PRINCIPLES OF MEDIATION Voluntary Recourse and Equal Treatment Art. 5. The parties shall have an equal opportunity to participate in the mediation procedure. They participate in the procedure of their own free will and may withdraw at any time. Neutrality and Impartiality Art. 6. (1) A mediator shall not be partial and shall not impose a decision on the dispute. (2) In the mediation procedure, all issues shall be settled by mutual consent of the parties. Confidentiality Art. 7. (1) (Previous text of Art. 7 – SG No. 27 of 2011) Discussions related to the dispute shall be confidential. The participants in the mediation procedure shall be bound to keep as confidential any and all circumstances, facts and documents they become aware of in the course of the procedure. (2) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) The mediator cannot be interrogated as a witness in respect of circumstances that have been disclosed to him by the participants and are relevant to the resolution of the dispute that is the subject of the mediation, except with the explicit consent of the participant who has disclosed them to the mediator. (3) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) Exception to the confidential character of the mediation may be made in cases when: 1. this is necessary for considerations for the purposes of criminal proceedings or related to the protection of the public order; 2. the protection of the interests of children has to be ensured, or harm to the physical or psychological integrity of a specific person has to be prevented; or 3. the disclosure of the content of the agreement reached as a result of mediation is necessary for the implementation and performance of that agreement.

sections.title: CHAPTER THREE. LEGAL STATUS OF THE MEDIATOR
sections.text: CHAPTER THREE. LEGAL STATUS OF THE MEDIATOR General Requirements Art. 8. (Amended – SG No. 86 of 2006) (1) A mediator may only be a legally capable person who meets the following requirements: 1. he has not been convicted of criminal offences of a general nature; 2. he has successfully completed a course for mediators; 3. he has not been deprived of the right to exercise a profession or an activity; 4. (Supplemented – SG No. 9 of 2011) he has a permanent or long-term residence permit for the Republic of Bulgaria, if the person is a foreign national; 5. he is registered in the Unified Register of Mediators with the Minister of Justice. (2) (In force as of 1 January 2007) The requirement under sub-para. 1(4) does not apply to nationals of Member States of the European Union, of the other States of the European Economic Area and of Switzerland. (3) (Supplemented – SG No.77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) The Minister of Justice or an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry shall issue a certificate to the mediator for his registration in the Unified Register of Mediators. (4) The Minister of Justice shall approve the organizations that provide training of mediators by order. The terms and the procedure for their approval, as well as the requirements for mediation training shall be set out in an ordinance of the Minister of Justice. (5) (Amended – SG No. 77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) When the person applying to be a mediator or the organization applying for authorization to provide training of mediators does not meet the statutory requirements, the Minister of Justice or an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry shall by order respectively refuse registration in the Unified Register of Mediators or approval. The order is subject to appeal before the respective administrative court following the procedure set out in the Administrative Procedure Code. Unified Register of Mediators Art. 8a. (New – SG No. 86 of 2006) (1) (Supplemented – SG No. 77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) The Minister of Justice or an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry shall create and maintain the Unified Register of Mediators. (2) The Unified Register of Mediators shall contain: 1. name, personal identification number (or personal number of a foreigner), nationality, education, profession, additional specialization in the field of mediation, the organization that has trained the mediator, foreign language skills, address and telephone number for contacting the mediator and number of the mediator; 2. deletion and striking off of the mediator; 3. the organization where the mediator had his training course; 4. changes in the circumstances under Art. 8(1)(1), (3) and (4). (3) The Unified Register of Mediators shall be public. (4) (Supplemented – SG No. 77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) A person registered in the Unified Register of Mediators shall declare in writing to the Minister of Justice or to an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry any changes in the circumstances subject to registration, within 14 days after their occurrence. (5) (Amended – SG No. 77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) When a requirement under Article 8(1), sub-paras (1), (3) and (4) is no longer met, the Minister of Justice or an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry shall issue an order for deletion of the mediator from the Unified Register of Mediators. The order may be appealed before the respective administrative court following the procedure set out in the Administrative Procedure Code. (6) The procedure for registration, striking off and deletion from the Unified Register of Mediators is determined by the ordinance under Article 8(4). (7) (Amended – SG No. 17 of 2019) The information under sub-para. 2(1), concerning the personal identification number (or personal number of a foreigner) shall be provided in compliance with the requirements for personal data protection. Fees Art. 8b. (New – SG No. 86 of 2006) The Ministry of Justice shall collect a fee for registration in the Unified Register of Mediators and for approval of organizations that provide training to mediators, in an amount as defined in a tariff adopted by the Council of Ministers. Rules of Conduct of the Mediator Art. 9. (1) (Supplemented – SG No. 86 of 2006) The mediator shall carry out its activity in good faith in compliance with the law, good morals, and the procedural and ethical rules of mediator conduct. These rules shall be determined in the order under Article 8(4). (2) A mediator shall agree to conduct the procedure only if he is able to guarantee his independence, impartiality and neutrality. Obligations and Liability of the Mediator Art. 10. (1) A mediator may not provide legal advice. (2) During the procedure, a mediator shall be obligated to take into account the opinion of each of the disputing parties. (3) A mediator shall withdraw from the procedure upon the occurrence of any circumstances that may cast doubt as to his independence, impartiality and neutrality. (4) A mediator may not communicate to the other participants in the procedure any circumstances solely concerning one of the disputants without its consent. (5) A mediator shall not be liable if the parties fail to reach a settlement. (6) A mediator shall not be liable for non-performance of the settlement agreement.

sections.title: CHAPTER FOUR. MEDIATION PROCEDURE
sections.text: CHAPTER FOUR. MEDIATION PROCEDURE Initiation of Procedure Art. 11 (1) A mediation procedure may commence on the initiative of the parties to the dispute, and each of the parties may propose the dispute to be resolved through mediation. (2) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) The day on which the parties have reached an explicit agreement for the commencement of the mediation procedure shall be deemed to be its beginning, and the day of the first meeting of all participants with the mediator when such an explicit agreement is absent. (3) (Previous subsection 2 – SG No. 27 of 2011) A proposal for resolution of the dispute through mediation may also be made by the court or another competent authority to which the dispute has been referred for resolution. (4) (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) In the cases provided for by law the court shall oblige the parties to participate in a first meeting in a mediation procedure. (5) (Previous subsection 3 – SG 27/2011, previous subsection 4 – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) The agreement of the parties to resolve a possible future dispute between them through mediation may also be agreed as a clause in a contract. Effect of the Commencement of the Mediation Procedure on the Limitation Period Art. 11a (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) The limitation period shall not run during the pendency of the mediation procedure. Participants Art. 12. (1) A mediation procedure shall be carried out by one or more mediators selected by the parties. (2) (Supplemented – SG No. 86 of 2006) The disputants shall participate in the procedure personally or through a representative. Authorization shall be made in writing. (3) Lawyers and other experts may also participate in the mediation procedure. Actions of the Mediator Art. 13. (1) Prior to the carrying out of the procedure, the mediator shall inform the parties of the essence of mediation and of its consequences and shall require the written or oral consent of the said parties for their participation. (2) (Amended – SG No. 27 of 2011) The mediator shall be obliged to indicate all circumstances that may give rise to reasonable doubt in the parties as to his impartiality and neutrality, including when the mediator is a person: 1. who is a spouse or a relative in a direct line of kinship without limitation, in an indirect line – up to the fourth degree, and by marriage – up to the third degree, of either party or of its representative; 2. who lives in de facto marriage cohabitation with a party to the dispute subject of the mediation; 3. who had been a representative or attorney of a party to the dispute subject of the mediation; 4. for whom there exist other circumstances that give rise to a reasonable doubt as to his impartiality. (3) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) The mediator shall sign declarations of impartiality in which he shall specify all circumstances under subsection 2 for each procedure for which he has been chosen and shall provide the declarations to the parties to the dispute. (4) (Previous subsection 3 – SG No. 27 of 2011) In the course of the procedure, the essence of the dispute shall be clarified, the mutually acceptable solutions shall be detailed and the possible framework of an agreement shall be outlined. (5) (Previous subsection 4 – SG No. 27 of 2011) In the course of the performance of the said actions, the mediator may schedule separate meetings with each of the parties while respecting their equal rights for participation in the procedure. Grounds for Suspension of the Procedure Art. 14. (1) The mediation shall be stayed: 1. by mutual consent of the parties, or at the request of one of the parties; 2. upon the death of the mediator; 3. in the cases provided for in Article 10(3). (2) (Supplemented – SG No. 86 of 2006, Supplemented – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) If a mediation is carried out while a proceeding is pending, the parties shall immediately inform the competent authority of the suspension of the mediation procedure. Where the mediation procedure is in a pending court case, the coordinator of the court centre shall notify the court of the suspension. Grounds for Termination of the Procedure Art. 15. (1) A mediation procedure shall be terminated: 1. upon reaching a settlement; 2. by mutual consent of the parties; 3. upon withdrawal of one of the parties; 4. upon the death of a disputing party; 5. upon the dissolution of a disputing party that is a legal person. 6. (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) with the lapse of six months from the beginning of the procedure. (2) The consent of the parties for termination of the dispute must be expressed clearly and unambiguously. (3) Upon the termination of the mediation procedure, a pending procedure that has been stayed shall be resumed in accordance with the provisions of the law.

sections.title: CHAPTER FIVE. SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT
sections.text: CHAPTER FIVE. SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT Form and Content Art. 16. (1) (Previous text of Article 16 – Supplemented – SG No. 27 of 2011) The form and content of the settlement agreement shall be defined by the parties. The form may be oral, written or written with notarization of the signatures of the parties to the dispute. A written agreement shall state the place and date where the said agreement was reached, the names of the parties and their addresses, the issues agreed upon, the name of the mediator, the date under Article 11(2) and the signatures of the parties. (2) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) In the agreement, the parties may provide for a liability for non-performance of the obligations prescribed in it. Effect of the Agreement Art. 17. (1) The agreement shall be binding solely on the disputing parties and may not be used against third persons that have not participated in the procedure. (2) (Amended – SG No. 86 of 2006) The agreement shall be binding on the parties solely regarding the issues they have agreed on. (3) (New – SG No. 86 of 2006) An agreement that contradicts or circumvents the law, and an agreement that violates good morals, shall be null and void. Making the Agreement Enforceable Art. 18. (New – SG 27/2011) (1) (Amended – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) An agreement on a legal dispute within the meaning of Art. 1 of this law, reached in a mediation procedure, shall have the force of a court settlement and shall be subject to approval by the district courts in the country. (2) (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) Where the dispute is the subject of a pending court case, the agreement shall be subject to the approval of the court hearing the case, including where the parties have agreed on matters outside the subject matter of the case. (3) (Previous subsection 2 – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) The court shall approve the agreement after its confirmation by the parties if it does not contradict the law and good morals. The court shall hear the opinion of the public prosecutor if the same is a party to the proceedings.

sections.title: CHAPTER SIX. MEDIATION IN PENDING COURT CASES (NEW – SG 11/2023, IN FORCE FROM 1 JULY 2024)
sections.text: CHAPTER SIX. MEDIATION IN PENDING COURT CASES (NEW – SG 11/2023, IN FORCE FROM 1 JULY 2024) Mediation Procedures Conducted in a Judicial Centre Art. 19. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) (1) In the cases and under the conditions provided for by law, the court shall oblige the parties to a pending court case to participate in a first meeting in a mediation procedure, which shall take place in a judicial mediation centre of the respective court. (2) Except in the cases referred to in subsection 1, a mediation procedure in a court mediation centre may also be held at the initiative of the parties to a pending court case. Mediator at a Court Mediation Centre Art. 20. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) (1) A mediator at a judicial mediation centre may be a person with legal education who meets the requirements of Art. 8, subsection 1 and has undergone additional selection and specialised training in accordance with the procedure laid down in the Regulation under Art. 25. (2) Lists of mediators shall be kept at the judicial mediation centres at the district courts. No fee shall be charged for the inclusion of mediators in the lists. Appointment of a mediator Art. 21. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) A mediation procedure in a pending court case shall be conducted by one or more mediators registered in the list of mediators of a court mediation centre, who are appointed by the court mediation centre, unless they are appointed by mutual agreement of the parties. Carrying Out of the Mediation Procedure Art. 22. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) (1) In the cases referred to in Art. 19, subsection 1, the parties shall be obliged to participate in good faith in a first meeting in a mediation procedure with a total duration of one to three hours. The mediator may also schedule separate meetings with each of the parties, respecting their equal rights to participate in the procedure. (2) The day and time of the first meeting with the mediator, as notified to the parties by the court centre coordinator, shall be considered the beginning of the mediation procedure. The meeting shall be postponed in the event of special unforeseen circumstances of which the party shall notify the coordinator. A party may request a postponement only once. (3) The mediation procedure may continue by agreement of the parties after the completion of the meetings referred to in subsection 1. On the proposal of the mediator and with the consent of the parties, the procedure may be continued outside the court mediation centre under the conditions and in the manner set out in the Regulation under Article 25. (4) The mediation procedure in a pending court case shall be terminated: 1. in the cases referred to in Article 15, subsection 1, points 1–5; 2. after the expiration of two months from the commencement of the procedure, unless by mutual agreement of the parties it is continued beyond that period, but not later than the expiration of six months from the commencement of the procedure; 3. where the parties fail to reach agreement; 4. where the parties do not agree to its continuation. (5) In the event of a death of a party to the proceedings or dissolution of a legal entity party to the proceedings occurring before the meetings in the proceedings under subsection 1, the judicial mediation procedure shall start anew with the participation of the successor of the party to the proceedings. Information about the Mediation Procedure Art. 23. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) The mediator shall provide the court with information about the outcome of the mediation procedure and the participation of the parties in it, in compliance with the principle of confidentiality and under the conditions and in accordance with the procedure provided for in the Regulation under Art. 25. Mediated Settlement in a Pending Court Case Art. 24. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, amended SG 57/2024 by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024) (1) (Declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) The agreement reached in a procedure under this Chapter shall be concluded in writing. (2) Where the parties wish the agreement to be approved as a court settlement pursuant to Article 234 of the Civil Procedure Code, it shall be approved by the court before which the mediation procedure is pending. (3) Where the parties have reached a settlement in relation to the subject matter of several pending cases, it shall be approved by the court hearing the case that was commenced first. The parties shall submit the approved settlement to the other courts, which shall terminate the proceedings pending before them in whole or in part according to the content of the settlement. Secondary Legislation Art. 25. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) The selection of mediators to the judicial mediation centres, the procedure for their entry and deletion from the lists of the district courts, their training, mandate and control over their activity, as well as the activity of the coordinators of the centres shall be regulated by a Regulation adopted by the Supreme Judicial Council. The Regulation shall regulate the amount of fees payable by the parties to the judicial centre and the costs of the mediation procedure in pending court cases.

sections.title: ADDITIONAL PROVISIONS
sections.text: ADDITIONAL PROVISIONS §1. (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) Within the meaning of the law, a “cross-border dispute” shall mean also: 1. A dispute in which at least one of the parties is domiciled or habitually resident in a Member State other than that of any other party on the date on which: (a) the parties have agreed to use mediation after the dispute has arisen; or (b) an invitation has been made to the parties by the court seized with the case to use mediation for the settlement of the dispute. 2. A dispute in which after the mediation between the parties a court or arbitration proceeding commences in a Member State other than that in which the parties were domiciled or habitually resident as of the date, specified in point 1. For the purposes of subsections (1) and (2), the domicile is defined in accordance with Articles 59 and 60 of Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 of 22 December 2000 on jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters.

sections.title: TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS
sections.text: TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS §1a. (Previous §1 – SG No. 27 of 2011) Within six months as from the entry into force of this Law, the Minister of Justice shall adopt mediator training standards, Procedural and Ethical Rules of Mediator Conduct, and shall establish and maintain the Unified Register of Mediators. §2. The implementation of this Law shall be entrusted to the Minister of Justice. This Law was passed by the 39th National Assembly on 2 December 2004, and the Official Seal of the National Assembly has been affixed thereto.

sections.title: TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS TO THE AMENDMENT AND SUPPLEMENTARY LAW TO THE LAW ON MEDIATION (PROMULGATED – SG No. 86 of 2006)
sections.text: TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS TO THE AMENDMENT AND SUPPLEMENTARY LAW TO THE LAW ON MEDIATION (PROMULGATED – SG No. 86 of 2006) §7. The persons who have been registered in the Unified Register of Mediators have to submit documentation to the Ministry of Justice, within 6 months of the coming into effect of this Law, that they meet the requirements of Article 8, para. 1, items 1, 3 and 4. In the same term, persons who meet the requirements of Article 8, para. 1, items 1 4 and who have been trained as mediators in the country or abroad prior to the entering into force of this Law, may file applications for registration in the Unified Register of Mediators. §8. The Minister of Justice shall issue the ordinance mentioned at Article 8, para. 4 within three months after the entry into force of this Law. §9. The Council of Ministers shall adopt the tariff under Article 8b within three months after the entry into force of this Law. §10. Para. 1 concerning Article 8, para. 2 shall enter into force as of the date of accession of the Republic of Bulgaria to the European Union. ADDITIONAL PROVISIONS TO THE AMENDMENT AND SUPPLEMENTARY LAW TO THE LAW OF MEDIATION (Published – SG No. 27 of 2011) §11. With this law, the requirements of Directive 2008/52/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 21 May 2008 on certain aspects of mediation in civil and commercial matters (OB, L 136/3 of 24 May 2008) are implemented.

sections.title: TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS TO THE ACT AMENDING AND SUPPLEMENTING THE ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE CODE TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS (PROMULGATED, SG NO. 77/2018, in force from 1 January 2019)
sections.text: TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS TO THE ACT AMENDING AND SUPPLEMENTING THE ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE CODE TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS (PROMULGATED, SG NO. 77/2018, in force from 1 January 2019) § 156. The Law shall enter into force on 1 January 2019, except for: 1. paragraphs 4, 11, 14, 16, 20, 30, 31, 74 and § 105, item 1 concerning the first sentence and item 2, which shall enter into force on 10 October 2019; 2. paragraphs 38 and 77, which shall enter into force two months after the promulgation of this Law in the State Gazette; 3. paragraph 79(1), (2), (3), (5), (6) and (7), sections 150 and 153, which shall come into force on the day of the promulgation of this Law in the State Gazette.

sections.title: TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS TO THE LAW AMENDING AND SUPPLEMENTING THE LAW ON MEDIATION (PROMULGATED IN SG NO. 11 OF 2023, IN FORCE FROM 1 JULY.2023)
sections.text: TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS TO THE LAW AMENDING AND SUPPLEMENTING THE LAW ON MEDIATION (PROMULGATED IN SG NO. 11 OF 2023, IN FORCE FROM 1 JULY.2023) § 10. The Supreme Judicial Council shall adopt the regulations under Article 25 of the Mediation Act and Article 84a, paragraph 4 of the Law on the Judiciary within three months from the promulgation of the Law in the State Gazette. § 12. The Law shall enter into force on 1 July 2024.

plain_text:
Previous version# For a copy of a previous version of this document, should this be available, please contact customer support.

LAW ON MEDIATION* In effect from 20 December 2004, as amended. Unofficial translation by Assen Alexiev; the translator acknowledges the assistance of an earlier translation of this Law by the Bulgarian Chamber of Commerce and Industry Mediation Center. Promulgated SG No. 110 of 17 December 2004, amended and supplemented, SG No. 86 of 24 October 2006, amended SG No. 9 of 28 January 2011, amended SG No. 27 of 1 April 2011, amended and supplemented SG No. 77 of 18 September 2018, amended SG No. 17 of 26 February 2019, amended and supplemented SG No. 11 of 2 February 2023, amended SG 57 of 5 July 2024.

CHAPTER ONE. GENERAL PROVISIONS Scope of Application Art. 1. (1) (Former text of Art. 1 – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) This Law shall regulate the relationships associated with mediation as an alternative method of resolution of legal and non-legal1 Translator’s note: In the legal doctrine, a “non-legal” dispute is a dispute for gap filling in a contract or its adaptation/termination in case of hardship. disputes. (2) (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) The Law shall also regulate the mediation procedure in pending court cases. Concept of Mediation Art. 2. Mediation is a voluntary and confidential procedure for out of court dispute settlement whereby a third-party mediator assists the disputing parties in reaching a settlement. Subject Matter of the Mediation Art. 3. (1) (Supplemented – SG No. 27 of 1 April 2011) Civil, commercial, labour, family and administrative disputes related to consumer rights, and other disputes between natural and/or legal persons, including where such disputes are cross border, may be the subject matter of mediation. (2) Mediation may also be conducted in the cases envisaged in the Criminal Procedure Code. (3) Mediation may not be conducted if a law or any other statutory instrument provides for another procedure for the conclusion of a settlement. Organization of the Mediation Art. 4. Mediation shall be carried out by natural persons. Such persons may become partners for the purpose of carrying out the activity. Persons performing judicial functions in the judiciary branch of government may not carry out mediation activities.

CHAPTER TWO. PRINCIPLES OF MEDIATION Voluntary Recourse and Equal Treatment Art. 5. The parties shall have an equal opportunity to participate in the mediation procedure. They participate in the procedure of their own free will and may withdraw at any time. Neutrality and Impartiality Art. 6. (1) A mediator shall not be partial and shall not impose a decision on the dispute. (2) In the mediation procedure, all issues shall be settled by mutual consent of the parties. Confidentiality Art. 7. (1) (Previous text of Art. 7 – SG No. 27 of 2011) Discussions related to the dispute shall be confidential. The participants in the mediation procedure shall be bound to keep as confidential any and all circumstances, facts and documents they become aware of in the course of the procedure. (2) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) The mediator cannot be interrogated as a witness in respect of circumstances that have been disclosed to him by the participants and are relevant to the resolution of the dispute that is the subject of the mediation, except with the explicit consent of the participant who has disclosed them to the mediator. (3) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) Exception to the confidential character of the mediation may be made in cases when: 1. this is necessary for considerations for the purposes of criminal proceedings or related to the protection of the public order; 2. the protection of the interests of children has to be ensured, or harm to the physical or psychological integrity of a specific person has to be prevented; or 3. the disclosure of the content of the agreement reached as a result of mediation is necessary for the implementation and performance of that agreement.

CHAPTER THREE. LEGAL STATUS OF THE MEDIATOR General Requirements Art. 8. (Amended – SG No. 86 of 2006) (1) A mediator may only be a legally capable person who meets the following requirements: 1. he has not been convicted of criminal offences of a general nature; 2. he has successfully completed a course for mediators; 3. he has not been deprived of the right to exercise a profession or an activity; 4. (Supplemented – SG No. 9 of 2011) he has a permanent or long-term residence permit for the Republic of Bulgaria, if the person is a foreign national; 5. he is registered in the Unified Register of Mediators with the Minister of Justice. (2) (In force as of 1 January 2007) The requirement under sub-para. 1(4) does not apply to nationals of Member States of the European Union, of the other States of the European Economic Area and of Switzerland. (3) (Supplemented – SG No.77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) The Minister of Justice or an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry shall issue a certificate to the mediator for his registration in the Unified Register of Mediators. (4) The Minister of Justice shall approve the organizations that provide training of mediators by order. The terms and the procedure for their approval, as well as the requirements for mediation training shall be set out in an ordinance of the Minister of Justice. (5) (Amended – SG No. 77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) When the person applying to be a mediator or the organization applying for authorization to provide training of mediators does not meet the statutory requirements, the Minister of Justice or an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry shall by order respectively refuse registration in the Unified Register of Mediators or approval. The order is subject to appeal before the respective administrative court following the procedure set out in the Administrative Procedure Code. Unified Register of Mediators Art. 8a. (New – SG No. 86 of 2006) (1) (Supplemented – SG No. 77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) The Minister of Justice or an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry shall create and maintain the Unified Register of Mediators. (2) The Unified Register of Mediators shall contain: 1. name, personal identification number (or personal number of a foreigner), nationality, education, profession, additional specialization in the field of mediation, the organization that has trained the mediator, foreign language skills, address and telephone number for contacting the mediator and number of the mediator; 2. deletion and striking off of the mediator; 3. the organization where the mediator had his training course; 4. changes in the circumstances under Art. 8(1)(1), (3) and (4). (3) The Unified Register of Mediators shall be public. (4) (Supplemented – SG No. 77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) A person registered in the Unified Register of Mediators shall declare in writing to the Minister of Justice or to an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry any changes in the circumstances subject to registration, within 14 days after their occurrence. (5) (Amended – SG No. 77 of 2018, in force as of 1 January 2019) When a requirement under Article 8(1), sub-paras (1), (3) and (4) is no longer met, the Minister of Justice or an official designated by him from the staff of the Ministry shall issue an order for deletion of the mediator from the Unified Register of Mediators. The order may be appealed before the respective administrative court following the procedure set out in the Administrative Procedure Code. (6) The procedure for registration, striking off and deletion from the Unified Register of Mediators is determined by the ordinance under Article 8(4). (7) (Amended – SG No. 17 of 2019) The information under sub-para. 2(1), concerning the personal identification number (or personal number of a foreigner) shall be provided in compliance with the requirements for personal data protection. Fees Art. 8b. (New – SG No. 86 of 2006) The Ministry of Justice shall collect a fee for registration in the Unified Register of Mediators and for approval of organizations that provide training to mediators, in an amount as defined in a tariff adopted by the Council of Ministers. Rules of Conduct of the Mediator Art. 9. (1) (Supplemented – SG No. 86 of 2006) The mediator shall carry out its activity in good faith in compliance with the law, good morals, and the procedural and ethical rules of mediator conduct. These rules shall be determined in the order under Article 8(4). (2) A mediator shall agree to conduct the procedure only if he is able to guarantee his independence, impartiality and neutrality. Obligations and Liability of the Mediator Art. 10. (1) A mediator may not provide legal advice. (2) During the procedure, a mediator shall be obligated to take into account the opinion of each of the disputing parties. (3) A mediator shall withdraw from the procedure upon the occurrence of any circumstances that may cast doubt as to his independence, impartiality and neutrality. (4) A mediator may not communicate to the other participants in the procedure any circumstances solely concerning one of the disputants without its consent. (5) A mediator shall not be liable if the parties fail to reach a settlement. (6) A mediator shall not be liable for non-performance of the settlement agreement.

CHAPTER FOUR. MEDIATION PROCEDURE Initiation of Procedure Art. 11 (1) A mediation procedure may commence on the initiative of the parties to the dispute, and each of the parties may propose the dispute to be resolved through mediation. (2) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) The day on which the parties have reached an explicit agreement for the commencement of the mediation procedure shall be deemed to be its beginning, and the day of the first meeting of all participants with the mediator when such an explicit agreement is absent. (3) (Previous subsection 2 – SG No. 27 of 2011) A proposal for resolution of the dispute through mediation may also be made by the court or another competent authority to which the dispute has been referred for resolution. (4) (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) In the cases provided for by law the court shall oblige the parties to participate in a first meeting in a mediation procedure. (5) (Previous subsection 3 – SG 27/2011, previous subsection 4 – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) The agreement of the parties to resolve a possible future dispute between them through mediation may also be agreed as a clause in a contract. Effect of the Commencement of the Mediation Procedure on the Limitation Period Art. 11a (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) The limitation period shall not run during the pendency of the mediation procedure. Participants Art. 12. (1) A mediation procedure shall be carried out by one or more mediators selected by the parties. (2) (Supplemented – SG No. 86 of 2006) The disputants shall participate in the procedure personally or through a representative. Authorization shall be made in writing. (3) Lawyers and other experts may also participate in the mediation procedure. Actions of the Mediator Art. 13. (1) Prior to the carrying out of the procedure, the mediator shall inform the parties of the essence of mediation and of its consequences and shall require the written or oral consent of the said parties for their participation. (2) (Amended – SG No. 27 of 2011) The mediator shall be obliged to indicate all circumstances that may give rise to reasonable doubt in the parties as to his impartiality and neutrality, including when the mediator is a person: 1. who is a spouse or a relative in a direct line of kinship without limitation, in an indirect line – up to the fourth degree, and by marriage – up to the third degree, of either party or of its representative; 2. who lives in de facto marriage cohabitation with a party to the dispute subject of the mediation; 3. who had been a representative or attorney of a party to the dispute subject of the mediation; 4. for whom there exist other circumstances that give rise to a reasonable doubt as to his impartiality. (3) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) The mediator shall sign declarations of impartiality in which he shall specify all circumstances under subsection 2 for each procedure for which he has been chosen and shall provide the declarations to the parties to the dispute. (4) (Previous subsection 3 – SG No. 27 of 2011) In the course of the procedure, the essence of the dispute shall be clarified, the mutually acceptable solutions shall be detailed and the possible framework of an agreement shall be outlined. (5) (Previous subsection 4 – SG No. 27 of 2011) In the course of the performance of the said actions, the mediator may schedule separate meetings with each of the parties while respecting their equal rights for participation in the procedure. Grounds for Suspension of the Procedure Art. 14. (1) The mediation shall be stayed: 1. by mutual consent of the parties, or at the request of one of the parties; 2. upon the death of the mediator; 3. in the cases provided for in Article 10(3). (2) (Supplemented – SG No. 86 of 2006, Supplemented – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) If a mediation is carried out while a proceeding is pending, the parties shall immediately inform the competent authority of the suspension of the mediation procedure. Where the mediation procedure is in a pending court case, the coordinator of the court centre shall notify the court of the suspension. Grounds for Termination of the Procedure Art. 15. (1) A mediation procedure shall be terminated: 1. upon reaching a settlement; 2. by mutual consent of the parties; 3. upon withdrawal of one of the parties; 4. upon the death of a disputing party; 5. upon the dissolution of a disputing party that is a legal person. 6. (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) with the lapse of six months from the beginning of the procedure. (2) The consent of the parties for termination of the dispute must be expressed clearly and unambiguously. (3) Upon the termination of the mediation procedure, a pending procedure that has been stayed shall be resumed in accordance with the provisions of the law.

CHAPTER FIVE. SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT Form and Content Art. 16. (1) (Previous text of Article 16 – Supplemented – SG No. 27 of 2011) The form and content of the settlement agreement shall be defined by the parties. The form may be oral, written or written with notarization of the signatures of the parties to the dispute. A written agreement shall state the place and date where the said agreement was reached, the names of the parties and their addresses, the issues agreed upon, the name of the mediator, the date under Article 11(2) and the signatures of the parties. (2) (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) In the agreement, the parties may provide for a liability for non-performance of the obligations prescribed in it. Effect of the Agreement Art. 17. (1) The agreement shall be binding solely on the disputing parties and may not be used against third persons that have not participated in the procedure. (2) (Amended – SG No. 86 of 2006) The agreement shall be binding on the parties solely regarding the issues they have agreed on. (3) (New – SG No. 86 of 2006) An agreement that contradicts or circumvents the law, and an agreement that violates good morals, shall be null and void. Making the Agreement Enforceable Art. 18. (New – SG 27/2011) (1) (Amended – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) An agreement on a legal dispute within the meaning of Art. 1 of this law, reached in a mediation procedure, shall have the force of a court settlement and shall be subject to approval by the district courts in the country. (2) (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) Where the dispute is the subject of a pending court case, the agreement shall be subject to the approval of the court hearing the case, including where the parties have agreed on matters outside the subject matter of the case. (3) (Previous subsection 2 – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024) The court shall approve the agreement after its confirmation by the parties if it does not contradict the law and good morals. The court shall hear the opinion of the public prosecutor if the same is a party to the proceedings.

CHAPTER SIX. MEDIATION IN PENDING COURT CASES (NEW – SG 11/2023, IN FORCE FROM 1 JULY 2024) Mediation Procedures Conducted in a Judicial Centre Art. 19. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) (1) In the cases and under the conditions provided for by law, the court shall oblige the parties to a pending court case to participate in a first meeting in a mediation procedure, which shall take place in a judicial mediation centre of the respective court. (2) Except in the cases referred to in subsection 1, a mediation procedure in a court mediation centre may also be held at the initiative of the parties to a pending court case. Mediator at a Court Mediation Centre Art. 20. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) (1) A mediator at a judicial mediation centre may be a person with legal education who meets the requirements of Art. 8, subsection 1 and has undergone additional selection and specialised training in accordance with the procedure laid down in the Regulation under Art. 25. (2) Lists of mediators shall be kept at the judicial mediation centres at the district courts. No fee shall be charged for the inclusion of mediators in the lists. Appointment of a mediator Art. 21. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) A mediation procedure in a pending court case shall be conducted by one or more mediators registered in the list of mediators of a court mediation centre, who are appointed by the court mediation centre, unless they are appointed by mutual agreement of the parties. Carrying Out of the Mediation Procedure Art. 22. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) (1) In the cases referred to in Art. 19, subsection 1, the parties shall be obliged to participate in good faith in a first meeting in a mediation procedure with a total duration of one to three hours. The mediator may also schedule separate meetings with each of the parties, respecting their equal rights to participate in the procedure. (2) The day and time of the first meeting with the mediator, as notified to the parties by the court centre coordinator, shall be considered the beginning of the mediation procedure. The meeting shall be postponed in the event of special unforeseen circumstances of which the party shall notify the coordinator. A party may request a postponement only once. (3) The mediation procedure may continue by agreement of the parties after the completion of the meetings referred to in subsection 1. On the proposal of the mediator and with the consent of the parties, the procedure may be continued outside the court mediation centre under the conditions and in the manner set out in the Regulation under Article 25. (4) The mediation procedure in a pending court case shall be terminated: 1. in the cases referred to in Article 15, subsection 1, points 1–5; 2. after the expiration of two months from the commencement of the procedure, unless by mutual agreement of the parties it is continued beyond that period, but not later than the expiration of six months from the commencement of the procedure; 3. where the parties fail to reach agreement; 4. where the parties do not agree to its continuation. (5) In the event of a death of a party to the proceedings or dissolution of a legal entity party to the proceedings occurring before the meetings in the proceedings under subsection 1, the judicial mediation procedure shall start anew with the participation of the successor of the party to the proceedings. Information about the Mediation Procedure Art. 23. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) The mediator shall provide the court with information about the outcome of the mediation procedure and the participation of the parties in it, in compliance with the principle of confidentiality and under the conditions and in accordance with the procedure provided for in the Regulation under Art. 25. Mediated Settlement in a Pending Court Case Art. 24. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, amended SG 57/2024 by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024) (1) (Declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) The agreement reached in a procedure under this Chapter shall be concluded in writing. (2) Where the parties wish the agreement to be approved as a court settlement pursuant to Article 234 of the Civil Procedure Code, it shall be approved by the court before which the mediation procedure is pending. (3) Where the parties have reached a settlement in relation to the subject matter of several pending cases, it shall be approved by the court hearing the case that was commenced first. The parties shall submit the approved settlement to the other courts, which shall terminate the proceedings pending before them in whole or in part according to the content of the settlement. Secondary Legislation Art. 25. (New – SG 11/2023, in force from 1 July 2024, declared unconstitutional by Decision No. 11 of 01.07.2024 of the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Bulgaria in constitutional case No. 11/2024, published in SG 57/2024) The selection of mediators to the judicial mediation centres, the procedure for their entry and deletion from the lists of the district courts, their training, mandate and control over their activity, as well as the activity of the coordinators of the centres shall be regulated by a Regulation adopted by the Supreme Judicial Council. The Regulation shall regulate the amount of fees payable by the parties to the judicial centre and the costs of the mediation procedure in pending court cases.

ADDITIONAL PROVISIONS §1. (New – SG No. 27 of 2011) Within the meaning of the law, a “cross-border dispute” shall mean also: 1. A dispute in which at least one of the parties is domiciled or habitually resident in a Member State other than that of any other party on the date on which: (a) the parties have agreed to use mediation after the dispute has arisen; or (b) an invitation has been made to the parties by the court seized with the case to use mediation for the settlement of the dispute. 2. A dispute in which after the mediation between the parties a court or arbitration proceeding commences in a Member State other than that in which the parties were domiciled or habitually resident as of the date, specified in point 1. For the purposes of subsections (1) and (2), the domicile is defined in accordance with Articles 59 and 60 of Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 of 22 December 2000 on jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters.

TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS §1a. (Previous §1 – SG No. 27 of 2011) Within six months as from the entry into force of this Law, the Minister of Justice shall adopt mediator training standards, Procedural and Ethical Rules of Mediator Conduct, and shall establish and maintain the Unified Register of Mediators. §2. The implementation of this Law shall be entrusted to the Minister of Justice. This Law was passed by the 39th National Assembly on 2 December 2004, and the Official Seal of the National Assembly has been affixed thereto.

TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS TO THE AMENDMENT AND SUPPLEMENTARY LAW TO THE LAW ON MEDIATION (PROMULGATED – SG No. 86 of 2006) §7. The persons who have been registered in the Unified Register of Mediators have to submit documentation to the Ministry of Justice, within 6 months of the coming into effect of this Law, that they meet the requirements of Article 8, para. 1, items 1, 3 and 4. In the same term, persons who meet the requirements of Article 8, para. 1, items 1 4 and who have been trained as mediators in the country or abroad prior to the entering into force of this Law, may file applications for registration in the Unified Register of Mediators. §8. The Minister of Justice shall issue the ordinance mentioned at Article 8, para. 4 within three months after the entry into force of this Law. §9. The Council of Ministers shall adopt the tariff under Article 8b within three months after the entry into force of this Law. §10. Para. 1 concerning Article 8, para. 2 shall enter into force as of the date of accession of the Republic of Bulgaria to the European Union. ADDITIONAL PROVISIONS TO THE AMENDMENT AND SUPPLEMENTARY LAW TO THE LAW OF MEDIATION (Published – SG No. 27 of 2011) §11. With this law, the requirements of Directive 2008/52/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 21 May 2008 on certain aspects of mediation in civil and commercial matters (OB, L 136/3 of 24 May 2008) are implemented.

TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS TO THE ACT AMENDING AND SUPPLEMENTING THE ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE CODE TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS (PROMULGATED, SG NO. 77/2018, in force from 1 January 2019) § 156. The Law shall enter into force on 1 January 2019, except for: 1. paragraphs 4, 11, 14, 16, 20, 30, 31, 74 and § 105, item 1 concerning the first sentence and item 2, which shall enter into force on 10 October 2019; 2. paragraphs 38 and 77, which shall enter into force two months after the promulgation of this Law in the State Gazette; 3. paragraph 79(1), (2), (3), (5), (6) and (7), sections 150 and 153, which shall come into force on the day of the promulgation of this Law in the State Gazette.

TRANSITIONAL AND FINAL PROVISIONS TO THE LAW AMENDING AND SUPPLEMENTING THE LAW ON MEDIATION (PROMULGATED IN SG NO. 11 OF 2023, IN FORCE FROM 1 JULY.2023) § 10. The Supreme Judicial Council shall adopt the regulations under Article 25 of the Mediation Act and Article 84a, paragraph 4 of the Law on the Judiciary within three months from the promulgation of the Law in the State Gazette. § 12. The Law shall enter into force on 1 July 2024.

title: National Report for Latvia (2018 through 2025)

sections.title: CHAPTER I: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: CHAPTER I: INTRODUCTION 1. Law on Arbitration a. Legislation The regulation of arbitration procedures in Latvia is encompassed in the Latvian Arbitration Law, which was adopted on 11 September 2014 and came into force on 1 January 2015 and was last amended by the Law of 6 June 2024 (“the Arbitration Law”, see Annex I hereto). The Arbitration Law is devoted exclusively to the regulation of arbitration proceedings. Except for the regulation of the enforcement of arbitral awards, the Latvian regulatory framework does not distinguish between domestic and international arbitration, as long as the seat of arbitration is in Latvia. Consequently, the Arbitration Law applies to both domestic and international arbitration. The Latvian Constitutional Court has been an important driving force for the improvement of the arbitration regulation, as it on several occasions has assessed different shortcomings of the existing regulation: first, in 2014, it held that the absence of any control mechanism over the validity of the arbitration agreement by the courts was unconstitutional.1. Judgment of the Satversme (Constitutional) Court of 28 November 2014 in case no. 2014-09-01 “On Compliance of Section 495(1) of the Civil Procedure Law with the first sentence in Article 92 of the Satversme of the Republic of Latvia”; more information is available at: <https://www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/en/press-release/the-norm-regarding-the-competence-of-‌arbitration-court-to-determine-jurisdiction-regarding-a-dispute-is-incompatible-with-the-satversme/>; judgment available at: <https://www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/web/viewer.html?file=/wp-content/‌uploads/2014/03/2014-09-01_Spriedums_ENG.pdf#search=>. In 2023, the Constitutional Court found that overall state control over the arbitration process was insufficient, which led to the introduction of a mechanism for setting aside arbitral awards and a significant expansion of judicial support for arbitration. Until the adoption of the Arbitration Law in 2014, the regulations of arbitration procedures formed part of the Latvian Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) Part D. Now, Part D of the CPL contains only provisions relating to the court support to arbitral processes and annulment and enforcement of domestic arbitral awards (see Annex II hereto). The CPL also contains a separate section on the “Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards” (Part F, Chap. 78, Annex III hereto). The Arbitration Law and the CPL encompass all essential aspects of the UNCITRAL Model Law on Commercial Arbitration (referred to as “the Model Law”); however, they do not repeat the text of the Model Law and exhibit numerous deviations. For example, they introduce excessive regulations concerning the establishment and operation of permanent arbitration courts and cover many procedural matters thus making the regulation close to the regulation of the court civil procedure. Latvia is a signatory to a number of international conventions and treaties in the field of arbitration, such as the 1958 Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (the “New York Convention”) and the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration (see Chapter VI.1.a). Therefore, the statutory regulation of arbitration in Latvia is largely in line with international standards. b. Mandatory provisions While most provisions of the Arbitration Law may be modified by party agreement, both the Arbitration Law and the CPL nevertheless contain a number of mandatory norms designed to safeguard due process, preserve the authority of arbitral tribunals, and ensure effective judicial assistance and oversight where necessary. One of these mandatory rules is Art. 5 of the Arbitration Law, which defines the scope of arbitrable matters and establishes the boundary between disputes that may be resolved by arbitration and those reserved for state courts. Following the 2024 amendments, the role of the courts has been significantly strengthened to guarantee minimum procedural safeguards at various stages of the arbitration. These provisions protect fundamental rights of the parties – for example, judicial review of jurisdictional rulings under Art. 532.5 of the CPL; court assistance in the appointment, challenge, or removal of arbitrators under Arts. 532.2-532.4 CPL; and the possibility to request interim measures under Art. 137(7) CPL or to obtain evidence through the courts under Art. 532.6 CPL. Equally fundamental are the mandatory procedural fairness guarantees. Article 19 of the Arbitration Law, which requires equal treatment of the parties and ensures each party’s right to be heard, reflects a universal standard of due process. Article 40(4) of the Arbitration Law reinforces this principle by requiring the arbitral tribunal to hold an oral hearing whenever a party so requests, even if the parties have previously agreed to proceed solely on the basis of written submissions. Finally, the mechanism for challenging and enforcing arbitral awards – set out in Arts. 533-537 CPL – contains several essential mandatory elements. The grounds for setting aside or refusing enforcement of an award are exhaustive and cannot be expanded, limited, or waived by party agreement. 2. Practice on Arbitration a. General Arbitration is a popular means of dispute resolution in Latvia. According to the data assembled by the Court Administration of Latvia,2. The Court Administration of the Republic of Latvia organizes and provides the organizational management of district (city) courts, regional courts and Land Registry Offices. For statistics see <https://dati.ta.gov.lv/MicroStrategy/asp/Main.aspx?src=Main.aspx.2048001&evt=2048001‌&share=1&documentID=BF8E206E48039A9F66E436BB511763C6&currentViewMedia=1&‌visMode=0&Server=TA-MSTR2-NEW&Port=0&Project=TA+Dati+New>. on average there are approximately 500 requests to issue a writ of execution of arbitral awards per year. Although this number has considerably decreased over the years (from 7,648 requests in 2005 to 1,092 requests in 2014), it is still noteworthy. The decrease compared to 2005 to a large extent was related to finding arbitration agreements encompassed in the consumer contracts invalid as unfair consumer clause, if it has not been individually negotiated with the consumer.3. For the pioneering judgment by the Supreme Court, see case no. SKA-59/2006, available (in Latvian) at <http://at.gov.lv/files/uploads/files/archive/department3/2006/ska59-2006.pdf>. Decisions by the Consumer Rights Protection Center are available at <www.ptac.gov.lv/?lang=LV&menu=113>. Further reduction has been facilitated due to rather fast and accessible court proceedings. It should also be underlined that the above data does not distinguish between purely domestic and international awards. However, the survey conducted by the Ministry of the Justice revealed that in 2021 at least thirty-two cases were with international elements. Most disputes giving rise to international arbitration are between Latvian companies and their trading partners from neighbouring countries – Baltic and Scandinavian States, as well as Ukraine, and EU Member States (mostly Germany and the Netherlands) and the United Kingdom. It should also be noted that the majority of arbitral proceedings involve relatively small sums – 56% of all cases have a claim value of up to EUR 7,000, 26% of cases range from EUR 7,000 to EUR 28,000, 13% from EUR 28,001 to EUR 145,000, 5% from EUR 145,000 to EUR 700,000, and only 1% of cases have a value above EUR 700,000. The primary characteristic of Latvian arbitration is the quantity of permanent (registered) arbitration courts. Article 2 of the Arbitration Law stipulates that “a permanent arbitration court may be established by an association registered in the Register of Enterprises (the founder of the permanent arbitration court), with the objective being the operation of the permanent arbitration court”. There have been numerous efforts to reduce the number of these arbitration courts, for instance, by introducing additional requirements regarding premises and visiting hours and by demanding the submission of extra documents to the Register of Enterprises. According to the data from the Latvian Register of Enterprises,4. Every arbitration institution must register with the Latvian Register of Enterprises before it is authorized to offer its services. Since 1 April 2005, the Latvian Register of Enterprises has overseen the registration of arbitration institutions (see Amendments to the Law “On Register of Enterprises of the Republic of Latvia”, 40 Latvijas Vestnesis(9 March 2005); Regulations of the Cabinet of Ministers, no. 204 and “Regulations on the Register of Arbitration Institutions”, published in 52 Latvijas Vestnesis(1 April 2005); and Regulations of the Cabinet of Ministers, no. 205 “On state fees for records in the Register of Arbitration Institutions”, published in 52 Latvijas Vestnesis(1 April 2005). in 2009 there were 214 arbitration courts registered; however, by 2016 this number had decreased to sixty-nine and has since remained roughly at this level. At the time of writing (in mid-2025) there are fifty-seven arbitration courts. In recent years, there have been widespread allegations that at least some of these arbitration courts act as “pocket courts” and cater to specific interests, particularly given that until 2024, they were legally obliged to maintain closed lists of arbitrators, and judicial oversight of the arbitration process was extremely limited. b. Arbitral institutions Taking into account the numerous permanent arbitration courts, we shall refer to two of the largest arbitration courts in Latvia – the Court of Arbitration of the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry (“LCCI Court of Arbitration”) and the Baltic International Arbitration Court. LCCI Court of Arbitration The LCCI Court of Arbitration is the oldest, with its origins dating back to 1934, during Latvia’s first period of independence. It was re-established in 1991, subsequent to Latvia proclaiming its independence from the Soviet Union. The LCCI Court of Arbitration administers arbitration proceedings in accordance with its own Arbitration Rules (updated in 2021). According to Art. 17 of the Arbitration Rules of the LCCI Court of Arbitration, if the parties have consented to arbitrate any existing or future dispute at the LCCI Court of Arbitration, without specifying the applicable rules, it is presumed that they have agreed on the application of the Court’s current Arbitration Rules. It should also be noted that the President of the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry assumes the role of appointing authority in ad hoc arbitrations, under Art. 3 of the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration. This Article stipulates that when the parties have agreed to submit any dispute to ad hoc arbitration, and where, within thirty days of the notification of the request for arbitration to the respondent, one of the parties fails to appoint its arbitrator, the appointment shall be, at the request of the other party, be made by the President of the competent Chamber of Commerce of the country where the defaulting party has its habitual residence or seat at the time the request for arbitration was introduced. Furthermore, the LCCI Court of Arbitration is also the most active arbitration court in Latvia in terms of organizing arbitration seminars and conferences, although these activities are not carried out on a regular basis. Further information on the LCCI Court of Arbitration, its Arbitration Rules and other information can be obtained at: Court of Arbitration of the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry Kr. Valdemara Street 35 Riga, LV-1010 Latvia Telephone: + 371 67225595; +371 26627788 Facsimile: + 371 67820092 Email: info@chamber.lv Website: <www.chamber.lv> Baltic International Arbitration Court The Baltic International Arbitration Court is another widely recognized arbitration court, featuring both Latvian and international arbitrators on its list of arbitrators. Arbitral proceedings are conducted in accordance with the parties’ arbitration agreement and the arbitration court’s Arbitration Rules.5. The Arbitration Rules of the Baltic International Arbitration Court are available in Latvian at: <https://arbitration.lv/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/2025-REGLAMENTS_Baltijas-Starptautiska-‌skirejtiesa.pdf>. Further information about the Baltic International Arbitration Court and its Arbitration Rules is available at: Baltic International Arbitration Court Strelnieku iela 9-7 Riga, LV-1010 Latvia Telephone: +371 67240347 Facsimile: +371 67292607 Email: mail@arbitration.lv Website: <www.arbitration.lv> 3. Bibliography a. Books Kacevska, I. and Fillers, A. “There is Ordinary Situation and There Is Latvia’s Situation”, Chapter 18 in: Danielsson, Christer and Schöldström, Patrik (eds.), Stockholm Arbitration Yearbook 2023 (Wolters Kluwer 2024), ISBN: 9789403503585 Krumins, T. Arbitration and Human Rights: Approaches to Excluding the Annulment of Arbitral Awards and Their Compatibility with the ECHR (Springer 2020), 352 pages, ISBN: 978-3-030-54236-8 Torgans, K. Civilprocesa likuma komentāri. III daļa (61.-86.nodaļa) [Commentary on the Law on Civil Procedure, Part III (Chapter 61-86)], (Tiesu Nama Aģentūra, Riga, 2014) 1026 pages (in Latvian), ISBN: 978-9934-508-05-9 b. Articles Baumane, L. and Bērtaitis, S. “Prasības nodrošinājums un pagaidu aizsardzība šķīrējtiesas procesā” [“Securing the claim and interim measures in the arbitral process”], Jurista Vārds, (24 September 2024, no. 39 (1357)), pp. 26-29 (in Latvian) Hartmane, V. and Fillers, A. “Izmaiņas ad hoc šķīrējtiesa regulējumā: liels solis pareizajā virzienā” [“Changes to the ad hoc arbitration framework: a big step in the right direction”], Jurista Vārds (24 September 2024, no. 39 (1357)), pp. 37-42 (in Latvian) Ilgaza, I. “Situācija šķīrējtiesa jomā un šā brīža attīstības izrāviens” [“Existing state of arbitration and current developments”], Jurista Vārds, 24 September 2024, no. 39 (1357), pp. 8-11 (in Latvian) Kacevska, I. “Šķīrējtiesnesis – spiežam taustiņu F5” [“An Arbitrator – let’s press key F5!”], Jurista Vārds, 24 September 2024, no. 39 (1357), pp. 30-32 (in Latvian) “Ņujorkas konvencija par ārvalsts šķīrējtiesas spriedumu atzīšanu un izpildi V panta otrās daļas piemērošanas problemātika” [“Applicability problems of Article V(2) of the New York Convention on Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards”] (Collection of research papers of the 79th International Scientific Conference of the University of Latvia (Riga: University of Latvia Press, 2021), pp. 104-111 (in Latvian, with summary in English) available at: <https://doi.org/10.22364/juzk.79.10> “Pušu autonomijas princips un šķīrējtiesas līgums” [“The Principle of Party Authonomy and the Arbitration Agreement”], 51/52 Jurista Vārds (18 December 2007, no. 504) (in Latvian) “Šķīrējtiesneša iecelšana no saraksta - juridiskās kultūras krustceles?” [“Appointment of Arbitrator from the List – the Crossroads of the Legal Culture? Jurisprudence and Culture: Past Lessons and Future Challenges”], The 5th International Scientific Conference of the University of Latvia (University of Latvia Press 2014) pp. 102-110 (in Latvian) Kacevska, I. and Fillers, A. “Privātautonomijas ierobežojumi starptautiskajās privāttiesībās: Šķīrējtiesu likuma kontekstā” / “Limitations of Private Autonomy in Private International Law in the Context of Arbitration Law”, pp. 214-220, in: Tiesību ierobežojumu pieļaujamība un attaisnojamība demokrātiskā tiesiskā valstī. Latvijas Universitātes 81. starptautiskās zinātniskās konferences tiesību zinātnes rakstu krājums / Admissibility and Justifiability of Restrictions of Rights in a Democratic State Governed by the Rule of Law. Article collection in legal science, the 81st international scientific conference of the University of Latvia (LU Akadēmiskais apgāds, Riga, 2023) 432 pages (in Latvian, summary in English), ISBN: 978-9934-36-021-3 Krumins, T. “Vai Satversmes tiesai jābūt panacejai visām Latvijas šķīrējtiesa regulējuma kaitēm?” [“Should the Constitutional Court be the panacea for all the ills of Latvia’s arbitration regulation?”], Jurista Vārds domnica, 27 February 2023, at: <https://juristavards.lv/‌eseja/‌282858-vai-satversmes-tiesai-jabut-panacejai-visam-latvijas-skirejtiesu-regulejuma-‌kaitem/> Liepa, L.; Tipaine, M. and Krumins, T. “Nepieciešamie uzlabojumi Latvijas šķīrējtiesu regulējumā” [“Improvements needed in the Latvian arbitration regulations”]. Jurista Vārds (16 May 2023, no. 20 (1286)), pp. 7-12 (in Latvian) Ramane, I. “Tiesu iesaiste šķīrējtiesas procesā” (“Court involvement in arbitration process”), Jurista Vārds (24 September 2024, no. 39 (1357)), pp. 19-21 (in Latvian) Rasnacs, L. “UNCITRAL parauglikumam atbilstoša regulējuma gaidās” [“Waiting for regulation in compliance with the UNCITRAL Model Law: Commentary to the Constitutional Court Judgment in Case No 2022-03-01”, Jurista Vārds (16 May 2023, no. 20 (1286)), pp. 30-31 (in Latvian) Tiesnieks, J. “Šķīrējtiesu regulējuma izmaiņas no šķīrējtiesa institūcijas skatpunkta” [“Changes to the Arbitration Rules from the Perspective of the Arbitral Institution”], Jurista Vārds (24 September 2024, no. 39 (1357)), pp. 33-36 (in Latvian) Tipaine, M. “Šķīrējtiesnešu sertifikācijas ietekme uz šķīrējtiesas procesa kvalitāti” [“Effect of Arbitrator Certification on the Quality of Arbitration Process. Jurisprudence and Culture: Past Lessons and Future Challenges”], The 5th International Scientific Conference of the University of Latvia (University of Latvia Press 2014) (in Latvian) “Aktuālā tiesu prakse atzīstot šķīrējtiesas līgumus par spēkā neesošiem” [“Recent Case Law Developments in Declaring Arbitration Agreement Invalid”], Bilances Juridiskie padomi (2014, no. 4) (in Latvian) Tipaine, M. and Krumins, T. “Latvia Introduces a Set-Aside Mechanism and Other Needed Changes to Its Arbitration Law”, Kluwer Arbitration Blog, 11 June 2024, at: <https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2024/06/11/latvia-introduces-a-set-aside-mechanism-and-other-needed-changes-to-its-arbitration-law/> “Latvian Constitutional Court: Absence of a Mechanism for Setting Aside of Arbitral Awards Violates the Rights to a Fair Trial”, Kluwer Arbitration Blog, 22 March 2023, at: <https://arbitrationblog.kluwerarbitration.com/2023/03/22/latvian-constitutional-court-absence-of-a-mechanism-for-setting-aside-of-arbitral-awards-violates-the-rights-to-a-fair-trial/> Tipaine, M.; Krumins, T. and Madsena, E. “Šķīrējtiesas sprieduma apstrīdēšana: jauns šķīrējtiesas kontroles mehānisms Latvijā” [“Setting aside of arbitral awards: new arbitration control mechanism in Latvia”], Jurista Vārds (24 September, No. 39 (1357)), pp. 12-18 (in Latvian) Zivtiņa, D. “Pierādījumu iegūšana šķīrējtiesas procesā un jaunās iespējas Latvijas regulējumā”, [“Obtaining of evidence in arbitration proceedings and new possibilities available in the Latvian regulation”], Jurista Vārds (24 September 2024, no. 39 (1357)), pp. 22-25 (in Latvian) c. Journals and periodicals The official State newspaper, Latvijas Vestnesis, has a weekly addendum Jurista Vārds [Lawyer’s Word] which occasionally also publishes articles on arbitration. Besides Jurista Vārds, there are no specialized journals on arbitration in Latvia. d. Publication of awards Similarly, there are no periodicals in which arbitral awards are published. As none of the arbitration courts publish their awards, information about awards can only be obtained through court judgments in proceedings for setting aside or enforcement of a particular award.

sections.title: CHAPTER II: ARBITRATION AGREEMENT
sections.text: CHAPTER II: ARBITRATION AGREEMENT 1. Form and Contents of the Agreement a. Arbitration clause and submission agreement In accordance with Art. 12 of the Arbitration Law (see Annex I hereto), the arbitration agreement can either be concluded in the form of a separate agreement (submission agreement) or can be included as a clause in any agreement (arbitration clause). According to Art. 10(2) of the Arbitration Law the parties may agree to submit to arbitration both already existing disputes as well as disputes that may arise in the future. b. Form requirements Under Art. 12(1) of the Arbitration Law, an arbitration agreement must be made in writing. The signing of the arbitration agreement may occur through either a traditional wet ink signature on a physical document or by employing an electronic signature. According to Latvian court practice,6. Riga District Court, matter no. 3-12/12946-24/14, referring to the judgment of the Senate of 6 December 2006 in matter no. SKC-584/2006, and decisions of 27 April 2011 in matter no. SKC-86/2011 and of 30 March 2017 in matter no. SKC-43/2017 (in Latvian). should an individual sign a contract in multiple capacities representing different parties, a single electronic signature from this individual suffices to bind all the parties they represent, including making the arbitration agreement contained within such a contract binding on all the represented parties. The Arbitration Law further specifies, in Art. 12(2), when the requirement “in writing” is met, i.e., if the arbitration agreement has been entered into between the parties by: “(1) exchange of communication via post; (2) exchange of communication via electronic means; (3) a statement of claim and explanations about the claim, where the arbitration agreement is alleged by one party and not denied by the other.” The reference to the exchange of communication via post (point (1)) and via electronic methods (point (2)) has been introduced to reflect the prevalent business practice in which contracts are frequently concluded through various communication forms without the formal drafting of a contract, for instance, through the exchange of offers and acceptances between parties. The arbitration clause will also be considered validly entered into if the exchange of communication includes a reference to general terms and conditions containing an arbitration clause. Regarding emails, they are generally accepted as written evidence. In practice, if a party can prove the authenticity of the relevant email correspondence (which, if challenged by the opposing party, may require a statement from internet providers on the authenticity of the specific email correspondence, or other means available to the party), an arbitral tribunal or the court will recognize such an arbitration agreement and consider the respective requirement to be met. Since the 2024 amendments, an arbitration agreement is also considered to be entered into in writing if it is contained in an exchange of statements of claim and defence in which the existence of an agreement is alleged by one party and not denied by the other (Art. 12(2) point (3)). The Arbitration Law provides a list of elements that parties may include in the arbitration agreement; however, these elements are presented merely as illustrative examples of what might be incorporated into the arbitration clause. For instance, it is stipulated that parties have the option to choose between institutional or ad hoc arbitration, designate the place of arbitration, the language, the number of arbitrators, and the procedure for covering the costs of arbitration (Art. 12(3)). Furthermore, Art. 29 clarifies that parties are permitted to decide on the number of arbitrators, provided that it must be an odd number. These are not the exhaustive elements upon which parties may reach an agreement, as, unless expressly prohibited by the Arbitration Law, they possess broad discretion to also agree on additional elements. For instance, parties may agree on the law governing the arbitration agreement, select the appointing authority, establish deadlines for the issuance of the award7. This conclusion derives from arbitral practice. For a commentary, see K. Torgans, Commentary on the Law on Civil Procedure(Tiesu Nama Aģentūra, Riga 1999 (1st ed.), 2001 (2nd ed.), 2006 (3rd ed.)., and stipulate other dispute resolution mechanisms that must be pursued prior to resorting to arbitration, such as mediation. Pursuant to Art. 12 of the Arbitration Law, “in writing” requirement applies also to modification or termination of the arbitration agreement. c. Model arbitration clause The LCCI Court of Arbitration has two model clauses: “1.
