Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this contract, or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof, shall be settled in the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry Court of Arbitration in Riga in accordance with its Rules of Arbitration. 2. The number of arbitrators shall be______ (one or three). 3. The language of the arbitration shall be______.” Or: “Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this contract, or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof shall be settled in the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry Court of Arbitration in Riga in accordance with its Rules of Arbitration.” In Latvian: “1. Jebkurš strīds, nesaskaņa vai prasība, kas izriet no šī līguma vai kas skar to vai tā pārkāpšanu, izbeigšanu vai spēkā neesamību, tiks izšķirts Latvijas Tirdzniecības un rūpniecības kameras Šķīrējtiesā Rīgā, saskaņā ar tās reglamentu. 2. Šķīrējtiesnešu skaits būs ____ (viens vai trīs). 3. Šķīrējtiesas izskatīšanas valoda būs ______.” Or: “Jebkurš strīds, nesaskaņa vai prasība, kas izriet no šī līguma vai kas skar to vai tā pārkāpšanu, izbeigšanu vai spēkā neesamību, tiks izšķirts Latvijas Tirdzniecības un rūpniecības kameras Šķīrējtiesā Rīgā, saskaņā ar tās reglamentu.” The Riga International Court of Arbitration recommends the following model arbitration clause: “All disputes, differences or claims arising out of or in connection with the present agreement, which concerns it or an infringement of it, termination or invalidity, shall be finally settled at the Riga International Arbitration Court in accordance with its Arbitration Rules. The parties may supplement the Arbitration Clause, adding to it: 1) the number of arbitrators is ________ (one or three); 2) the language of arbitration is ________; 3) place of arbitration is ________________.” In Latvian: “Jebkurš strīds, domstarpība vai prasība, kas izriet no šī līguma, kas skar to vai tā pārkāpšanu, izbeigšanu vai spēkā neesamību tiks galīgi izšķirts Rīgas Starptautiskajā šķīrējtiesā saskaņā ar šīs šķīrējtiesas reglamentu.Puses var papildināt šķīrējtiesas klauzulu, pievienojot tai: 1) šķīrējtiesnešu skaits _________ (viens vai trīs); 2) šķīrējtiesas valoda ___________; 3) šķīrējtiesas procesa vieta _____________.” The recommended arbitration clause of the Baltic International Court of Arbitration is the following: In English: All disputes, differences or claims arising out of or in connection with the present agreement, which concerns it or an infringement of it, termination or invalidity, shall be finally settled at the Baltic International Arbitration Court (Strelnieku Str. 9-7, Riga, LV-1010) in accordance with its Arbitration Rules.” The parties may supplement arbitration clause by adding following: 1. The number of arbitrators is …………………………………….; 2. The language of arbitration is ………………………………………..; 3. Place of arbitration is ……………………………………………… 4. ……………………………………………………………….. laws. In Latvian: “Jebkurš strīds, domstarpība vai prasība, kas izriet no šī līguma, kas skar to vai tā pārkāpšanu, izbeigšanu vai spēkā neesamību tiks galīgi izšķirts Baltijas Starptautiskā Šķīrējtiesā (Strēlnieku iela 9-7, Rīga, LV-1010) saskaņā ar minētās šķīrējtiesas reglamentu.” Puses var papildināt šķīrējtiesas klauzulu, pievienojot tai: 1. šķīrējtiesnešu skaits …………………………………………….; 2. šķīrējtiesas valoda ……………………………………………… 3. šķīrējtiesas procesa vieta …………………………………….; 4. …………………………………………………………….. likumiem.” 2. Parties to the Agreement a. Generally/capacity Article 11 of the Arbitration Law states that an arbitration agreement can be entered into by any natural person who has the legal capacity to do so, a legal entity governed by private law, or a legal entity governed by public law in the realm of private law. b. Bankruptcy Article 5(1)(8) of the Arbitration Law provides that “disputes concerning rights and obligations of persons who have entered into insolvency procedures” are not arbitrable. This restriction refers to objective arbitrability rather than subjective arbitrability, thus the arbitration is possible also in case of bankruptcy of one of the parties as long as the foreign law is applicable on the substance of the dispute. c. State or state agencies Article 5(1)(2) of the Arbitration Law provides that disputes “in which at least one of the parties is the State or a local government authority, or the arbitral award may infringe the rights of the State or a local government authority” are not arbitrable. This restriction, however, applies only to domestic arbitration proceedings. With respect to international arbitration, Latvia maintained – until 23 December 2013 – a reservation under Art. 2(2) of the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration of 21 April 1961, declaring that the rights to enter into arbitration agreements did not extend to state or municipal institutions.8. Declaration Latvia made under Art. II (2) made upon accession to the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration: In 2013, Latvia withdrew this reservation, thereby allowing state and municipal institutions to enter into arbitration agreements in international arbitration proceedings. d. Multiparty arbitration The Arbitration Law does not include specific provisions regarding multiparty arbitration, the addition of a third party, or consolidation. However, if the parties have entered into an arbitration agreement that allows for such possibilities, or if they have selected arbitration rules that include provisions for these scenarios, then multiparty arbitration or the addition of a third party would be feasible. Similarly, consolidation could also be possible in principle, provided there is a written agreement among all the involved parties or if the arbitration rules allow for it. Latvian courts have been rather reluctant to bind a non-signatory to an arbitration agreement. Such solutions have been allowed in case law only in situations where the party that did not sign the arbitration agreement has replaced the signatory party by inheritance, reorganization or other similar mechanism of succeeding rights and obligations (not by way of assignment). 3. Domain of Arbitration a. Arbitrability In accordance with Art. 5(1) of the Arbitration Law, the following matters are not arbitrable. This is in the case of a dispute: “1) the award of which may infringe the rights or interests protected by law of a person who is not a party to the arbitration agreement; 2) in which at least one party is the State or a local government institution or if the arbitral award may affect the rights of the State or a local government institution; 3) relating to entries in the Civil Records Registry; 4) relating to the rights and duties of persons under guardianship or trusteeship or to their interests protected by law; 5) regarding the establishment, alteration or termination of property rights with regard to immovable property, if among the parties to the dispute there is a person whose rights to acquire ownership, possession or use of immovable property are restricted by law; 6) regarding the eviction of a person from dwelling premises; 7) between an employee and an employer if the dispute has arisen by entering into, amending, terminating or implementing an employment contract, as well as in applying or translating provisions of laws and regulations, collective labour contract or working procedures (individual labour rights dispute); 8) regarding the rights and duties of such persons in respect of whom insolvency proceedings have been initiated.” Besides above-mentioned matters, disputes that are adjudicated in special adjudication proceedings provided for in the Latvian Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) are also not arbitrable.9. According to Art. 251 of the CPL, special adjudication proceedings are provided for matters: In decision SPC-46/2013 of the Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia of 29 October 2013,10. Case no. SPC-46/2013, available (in Latvian) at <http://at.gov.lv/files/uploads/files/archive/‌department1/2013/46-spc-2013.doc>. the Supreme Court has ruled that restrictions posed on the “State [...] institution” within meaning of Art. 5 (1)(2) of the Arbitration Law are not applicable to private companies established and owned by the State, since they do not meet the criteria of state institutions set forth in Art. 1(2) of the State Administration Structure Law. It has been confirmed also in more recent court practice.11. Case no. SKA-24/2022, available (in Latvian) at <https://www.at.gov.lv/download‌lawfile/8454>. It should also be noted that while the Consumer Rights Protection Law of 199912. In force as of 15 April 1999, published in Latvijas Vestnesis, 1 April 1999, no. 104/105, available at <www.ptac.gov.lv>. does not limit the parties’ rights to enter into an arbitration agreement, such an agreement would be invalid as unfair contractual provision, unless it has been individually negotiated with the consumer. Ever since judgment SKA-59/2026 of the Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia of 6 March 2006,13. See case no. SKA-59, available (in Latvian) at <http://at.gov.lv/files/uploads/files/archive/‌department3/2006/ska59-2006.pdf>. Decisions by the Consumer Rights Protection Center are available at <www.ptac.gov.lv/? lang=LV&men=113>. national courts have consistently found all arbitration clauses in consumer contracts to be invalid as they had not been negotiated individually with the consumer. So far, only once has the service provider been successful in proving that the arbitration agreement had been individually negotiated with the consumer.14. A decision by the Consumer Rights Protection Center, not publicly available, is on file with the authors. In that case, the arbitration clause was proposed by the consumer himself. b. Filling gaps Latvian law does not expressly address the power of arbitrators to fill contractual gaps. At the same time, the general principles of contract interpretation do not extend so far as to permit arbitrators to create new contractual provisions that do not derive from the parties’ existing rights and obligations. Introducing such new terms through gap-filling would only be permissible where the parties have explicitly authorized the arbitrators to do so. c. Adapting contracts Latvian law does not have a clear provision explicitly empowering arbitrators to adapt a contract on their own initiative in response to fundamentally changed circumstances (such as a major price shift in a long-term contract). Adopting a contract to fundamentally changed circumstances would be permissible where the parties have explicitly authorized the arbitrators to do so. 4. Separability of Arbitration Clause An arbitration clause is considered to be an independent and separate agreement from the main contract in which the arbitration clause is included. Therefore, the validity of the main contract does not have any bearing on the validity of the arbitration agreement between the parties. This principle is included in Art. 13(3) of the Arbitration Law, which further specifies that an arbitration agreement shall be in effect as long as the legal relations in connection to which it has been entered into have not been terminated. The principle of separability, however, does not apply in cases of assignment, which under Latvian Civil Law is defined narrowly as assignment (cession) of the right to claim, and Art. 13(4) of the Arbitration Law provides that in case of an assignment, the assignee shall be assigned of the rights to claim and not of the arbitration clause. 5. Effect of the Agreement a. Duty of court Pursuant to Art. 132(1)(3) of the Latvian CPL, the judge refuses to accept the statement of claim “if the parties have agreed, in accordance with procedures laid down in law, to submit the dispute for examination in an arbitration court, except for the case when a statement of claim regarding recognition of the agreement of the arbitration court as invalid is submitted to the court”. Similar duty of the court is provided in Art. 223(6) of the CPL providing that a court shall terminate court proceedings, “if the parties have agreed, in accordance with procedures laid down in law, to submit the dispute for examination in an arbitration court”. Both of these provisions are applied by Latvian courts on their own motion, even if neither party has pointed to the arbitration agreement and requested to terminate court proceedings.15. CPL Arts. 132(1)(3) and 223(6). The courts have reasoned that such approach is justified by Art. 12 of the Arbitration Law providing that an arbitration agreement can be amended or rescinded by the parties only in writing and thus it cannot be waived. Although there is at least one recent court decision16. Riga Regional Court decision of 21 May 2025 in case no. 3-10/0455 (Archive no. CA-1142-25/27) (not published) (in Latvian). suggesting that such approach should be taken also if the arbitration is international, it has not been overruled by the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia17. See decision no. SKC-579/2012 of 17 October 2012, available (in Latvian) at <at.gov.lv/files/uploads/files/579-skc-2012.doc>. has confirmed that in cases where the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration is applicable, parties may waive the arbitration agreement by referring to the court of general jurisdiction, if the respondent fails to raise objections as to the jurisdiction of the court not later than by filing its response to the claim. In one particular case the respondent had filed a counterclaim in the court and only in a cassation action did it bring the court’s attention to the arbitration clause. The Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia explained that parties by their actions (conclusive actions) had expressed their joint intent to submit their dispute to the court of general jurisdiction. Finally, it should be noted that as a general rule, in a case where the arbitration agreement provides for arbitration abroad and one of the parties invokes the agreement, while the claimant has reverted to a national court, a Latvian court, following provisions of the New York Convention, will refer the parties to arbitration, as long as it is prima facie satisfied that the arbitration agreement provides to this effect. b. Court examination of arbitration agreement Generally speaking, Latvian courts will only conduct a prima facie review where one of the parties invokes the arbitration agreement or the court discovers the existence of the arbitration agreement by itself. Unless the court finds it obvious that an arbitration agreement is invalid, it will refer parties to arbitration. As far as the court’s powers regarding the stay of arbitral proceedings is concerned, it is only where a party directly attacks the validity of an arbitration agreement in its statement of claim submitted to the court that Latvian courts would conduct a full review of the arbitration agreement and, based on its findings, decide whether to stay proceedings or continue with the merits of the case. Such powers of the court were reconfirmed by the judgment of the Satversme Court (Latvian Constitutional Court) of 28 November 2014,18. Satversmes case no 2014-09-01On Compliance of Section 495(1) of Civil Procedure Law with the First Sentence in Article 92 of the Satversme of the Republic of Latvia, available at: <https://www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/web/viewer.html?file=/wp-content/uploads/2014/03/2014-09-01_Spriedums_ENG.pdf#search=>. interpreting the competence-competence principle and reminding the courts of their rights to decide on the validity of an arbitration agreement upon a party’s request. The validity of the arbitration agreement must be challenged expressly and unambiguously, and it can be done by bringing a separate claim in the court of general jurisdiction. Such claim is subject to a state fee of EUR 300. The CPL provides no time limits with respect to the party’s right to challenge the court’s jurisdiction on the grounds of an existing arbitration agreement. In practice, however, a plea that the court does not have jurisdiction must be raised not later than the submission of the statement of defence. c. Kompetenz-kompetenz The principle of kompetenz-kompetenz is enshrined in Art. 24 of the Arbitration Law, which provides that an arbitral tribunal has the authority to rule on its own jurisdiction, including on the existence, validity, and scope of the arbitration agreement. The tribunal may determine its jurisdiction at any stage of the proceedings – either as a preliminary issue or together with the merits of the dispute. Where the tribunal issues a preliminary decision on jurisdiction, the party contesting that decision may apply to the district (city) court for judicial review in accordance with Art. 24(3) of the Arbitration Law. Importantly, the submission of such a request to the district (city) court does not suspend or impede the continuation of the arbitration proceedings, allowing the tribunal to proceed with the case unless the court decides otherwise.

sections.title: CHAPTER III ARBITRATORS
sections.text: CHAPTER III ARBITRATORS 1. Qualifications a. Requirements In accordance with Art. 14(1) of the Arbitration Law (see Annex I hereto), an arbitrator is “a person who complies with the provisions of this Law and is appointed to resolve a civil dispute pursuant to an arbitration agreement and provisions of this Law.” Article 14(2) goes on to provide that any person above the age of eighteen who has accepted in writing may be appointed as an arbitrator, provided that the person “(1) is not under legal guardianship; (2) has a flawless reputation; (3) has higher professional or academic education (with the exception of first level professional education)”. The parties to the arbitration agreement may agree on additional qualifications for the arbitrators. Similarly, the applicable arbitration rules may also foresee certain qualifications that the arbitrators must meet, although in practice, arbitration rules do not provide for such additional requirements. Until 2024, the Arbitration Law set more restrictive requirements for arbitrators, e.g., that only persons with higher legal education and at least three years of legal experience could be appointed as arbitrators. However, in reviewing the legal framework, the legislator noted that the requirements were unduly restrictive of the parties’ right to freely choose an arbitrator who is not a lawyer, but has another profession. It should also be noted that until 2024, the Arbitration Law required all arbitration courts to maintain a list of arbitrators. The parties were only permitted to appoint arbitrators from this list. The arbitration courts had a duty to compose a list of at least ten arbitrators and publish such list not only on the website of[ the arbitration court, but also to report it to the Register of Arbitration Courts. Since June 2024, arbitration courts are still required to have a list of arbitrators; however, these lists are not closed and the arbitration courts may allow the parties to appoint also arbitrators who are not on the list. For example, Art. 9(3) and (4) of the Arbitration Rules of the Baltic International Arbitration Court provides that any party may appoint as an arbitrator a person who is not on the list of arbitrators provided that the tribunal consists of at least three arbitrators and that the Council of the Baltic International Arbitration Court has approved such candidate. The presiding arbitrator still has to be from the list and the non-listed candidate must present the documents to the Council showing that candidate satisfies the requirements of arbitrators set in the Arbitration Law. b. Restrictions Importantly, Art. 14(4) provides that an arbitrator of an arbitration court “shall not be included in more than three lists of permanent arbitration courts”, while Art. 14(5) requires arbitrators to be “impartial, objective and fair” in deciding the dispute before them. Article 15 sets out a list of persons that may not be appointed as arbitrators. This includes persons who have been recognized as a suspect or have been accused or have been punished in criminal proceedings for committing an intentional criminal offence, or against whom criminal proceedings regarding the commission of an intentional criminal offence have been terminated for reasons other than exoneration, or who have been declared insolvent during the last five years. The Law on Prevention of Conflicts of Interests in Activities of Public Officials forbids judges, notaries, prosecutors and civil servants to serve as arbitrators. c. Disclosure Before accepting an appointment, a prospective arbitrator must disclose in writing any facts or circumstances that may give rise to reasonable doubts regarding their impartiality or independence. If such circumstances arise or become known after the arbitration has commenced but before its conclusion, the arbitrator is required to disclose them immediately to the parties (see Art. 14(5) of the Arbitration Law) The disclosure must be provided to all other parties to the proceedings and/or to the appointing authority, which may be the parties themselves, a permanent arbitration institution, the other arbitrators, if any, or—if the appointment is being made by the district (city) court acting as the appointing authority—the court. 2. Appointment of Arbitrators As a default rule, the parties are free to agree on the procedure for appointment of arbitrators (Art. 30(1) Arbitration Law). The Arbitration Law expressly differentiates the appointment procedure for institutional and ad hoc arbitration. If a dispute is referred to a permanent arbitral court, arbitrators shall be appointed pursuant to the arbitration rules of the respective institution, taking into account the principle of equality of parties (Art. 30(3)). The Arbitration Rules of the LCCI Court of Arbitration provide that each party appoints one arbitrator and that the Secretariat of the LCCI Court of Arbitration, after consultations with the Council of the LCCI Court of Arbitration, nominates the third arbitrator to act as the chairman of the tribunal within fifteen days from the date when the Answer to the Request for Arbitration has been received (Art. 43 of LCCI Arbitration Rules). In addition to standard provisions mirroring regulations of the Arbitration Law, there are also more recent amendments to the arbitration regulations, for example, in the Arbitration Rules of the Baltic International Arbitration Court. According to Art. 9(3) of these Rules, with prior consent of the presidium of the permanent arbitration court, the parties may also appoint as an arbitrator a person who is not on the list of arbitrators of the Baltic International Arbitration Court. If a dispute is referred to an ad hoc arbitration, each party shall appoint one arbitrator and these two, mutually agreeing between themselves, shall then appoint the presiding arbitrator (Art. 30(4)). In ad hoc arbitration the parties are also entitled to agree on a sole arbitrator. Article 30(5) of the Arbitration Law provides that once a party has appointed an arbitrator and has notified the other party of this, the appointing party can no longer revoke this appointment without the consent of the other party. A party may apply to the court and request the court to act as an appointing authority if the other party, the appointed arbitrators, the permanent arbitration court, or any other person who has been selected as an appointing authority, fails to act in accordance with the procedures laid down in the Arbitration Law or in the parties’ agreement, (Art. 30(6)). The party applying to the court must suggest the arbitrator to be appointed and provide the contact details of the candidate. The application must also be accompanied by a signed consent from the candidate arbitrator to be appointed for the specific dispute, along with a declaration that he or she is not subject to any restrictions provided in the Arbitration Law. Upon satisfying the application, the court shall appoint an arbitrator from the candidates suggested by the parties or, if the permanent arbitration court has failed to appoint the arbitrator, from the list of arbitrators of the arbitration court. When making an appointment, the court shall consider, as far as possible, the requirements for the arbitrator set out in the agreement of the parties. If it is not possible to appoint an arbitrator from the list of arbitrators of the arbitration court, the court shall appoint an arbitrator from the list established by the Latvian Council on Sworn Advocates.19. Art. 532.[2](4) and (5) of the CPL. The Latvian Council of Attorneys at Law has established a list of candidates for arbitrators by inviting all arbitrators who wish to be included in the list to express their interest in being included in the list.20. The list of arbitrators is published and available at: <https://www.advokatura.lv/lv/‌skirejtiesnesu-saraksts>. The Latvian Bar Council has not set any qualification or experience requirements. To the author’s knowledge, no arbitrator has ever been appointed from the list established by the Latvian Bar Council. In international ad hoc arbitration taking place in Latvia, the arbitral tribunal can be established in accordance with Art. IV of the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration of 1961, whereby the LCCI Court of Arbitration acts as an appointing authority. 3. Number of Arbitrators The parties are free to agree on the number of arbitrators. The Arbitration Law provides in Art. 29(1) that the number of arbitrators must be an odd number. If the parties have not determined the number of arbitrators, and unless specified otherwise in the arbitration rules of the chosen permanent arbitration court, the Arbitration Law provides that the arbitral tribunal must consist of three arbitrators. Article 29(2) provides, however, that “[a]n arbitral tribunal may be comprised of one arbitrator, if the parties so agree”]. The arbitration rules of permanent arbitration courts typically establish as a default provision that the number of arbitrators shall be three (for instance, see Art. 40 of LCCI Arbitration Rules, Art. 8(2) of the Arbitration Rules of the Baltic International Arbitration Court). Neither the secretariat, nor the council of arbitration courts usually possess the discretion to decide whether the arbitral tribunal should be made up of a single arbitrator or three arbitrators, taking into account the complexity of the case, the amount in dispute and any other relevant circumstance. Likewise, neither the Arbitration Law, nor the arbitration rules of permanent arbitration courts outline the procedure for appointing arbitrators in the event of multiparty arbitration. However, in such instances, the principle of party equality would be applied. 4. Challenge to Arbitrators a. Grounds Article 14(5) of the Arbitration Law provides that every arbitrator must be “impartial, objective and fair” at the time of accepting an appointment to serve and throughout the arbitration proceedings until the final award has been rendered. The Article further stipulates that an arbitrator must disclose in writing any facts and circumstances that may cast reasonable doubt on his or her objectivity, impartiality or independence, before accepting the appointment. Such facts and circumstances must be disclosed to the parties, to the permanent arbitration court or other arbitrators, if any, or, if the district court is serving as an appointing authority, also to the district (city) court. If such facts or circumstances arise or become known after the arbitration has commenced, but before the arbitration proceedings are concluded, the arbitrator must immediately disclose these facts to the parties. Article 17 of the Arbitration Law contains various grounds on which a party may challenge an arbitrator. These grounds range from the requirements imposed on the arbitrators by the Arbitration Law, the requirements agreed by the parties, conflict-of-interest situations and “other circumstances” raising concerns about the impartiality and independence of the arbitrator. A non-exhaustive and illustrative list of conflict-of-interest situations is provided in Art. 16(1) of the Arbitration Law, naming both previous involvement of the arbitrator in the dispute or relation to the parties or party representatives. When revising the Arbitration Law in 2024, the legislative summary (annotation) of the draft21. Annotation to the Amendments of the Arbitration Law, the Cabinet of Ministers, 9 January 2024, <https://titania.saeima.lv/LIVS14/SaeimaLIVS14.nsf/0/EE8C273FA8BFA8A9C2258‌AA20046BC9F?OpenDocument>. contained a reference to the IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration, setting them as an international standard to the issues related to the conflict of interests in arbitration. This has encouraged the courts to apply as a guidance these IBA Guidelines, which has been done on several occasions already.22. No. 3-12/13528-24/23, Riga City Court decision (in Latvian), not published. Finally, for the sake of completeness, it should be noted that the Latvian Constitutional Court has remarked that in accordance with the practice of the ECHR, the lack of independence and impartiality can be found not only in cases where it has been established, but also in cases where there are well-grounded doubts as to the existence of independence and impartiality.23. See Delcourt v. Belgium[1970] ECHR 1, para. 31; Piersack v. Belgium [1982] ECHR 6, para. 30. The structure of the arbitration court, previous relations of the arbitrators with the parties as well as other factors may serve as the reason for such doubt.24. See <www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/2004-10-01_Spriedums.pdf>, at para. 10. b. Procedure The parties may agree on the procedure for challenge of the arbitrator, but in the absence of such agreement, the challenge procedure is determined in accordance with the Arbitration Law. The challenge can be made within five days from the date on which the party became aware of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the circumstances giving rise to the challenge. If the challenge is not made within five days, it is considered that the party has waived the right to object. The challenge has to be made in writing stating the grounds for challenge. The party may challenge an arbitrator that it has appointed or in whose appointment it has participated only if the party became aware of ground for challenge after the appointment. The party requesting the challenge shall bear the burden of proving at when it became aware of the circumstances giving rise to the challenge. In the absence of proof, the objection may be deemed to be declaratory and unsubstantiated25. Riga City Court decision of 20 December 2024 in matter no. 3-12/21946-24/15 (not published), Riga City Court decision of 16 September 2024 in matter no. 3-12/13528-24/23 (not published).. According to Art. 17(6) of the Arbitration Law, the challenge first of all shall be decided by the arbitrator himself/herself and, only if the arbitrator does not remove himself/herself, the challenge shall be decided by the rest of the arbitral tribunal. If the arbitration proceedings are governed by the arbitration rules of the permanent arbitration court, the arbitration rules may provide a different procedure. For example, the Arbitration Rules of LCCI Court of Arbitration which provide that where the request for challenge has been received before the appointment of the arbitrator or the challenged arbitrator refuses to withdraw voluntarily, the challenge shall be decided by the Council of the LCCI Court of Arbitration within ten days of receipt of the request (Art. 19(3)). If the challenged arbitrator has not accepted the challenge and also the arbitral tribunal or the council of the permanent arbitration court has not accepted the challenge, the party may request the district (city) court to decide the challenge. Pursuant to Art. 532.[3] of the Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) (see Annex II hereto), the party may submit the challenge to the district (city) court within thirty days from the date of receipt of the decision of the arbitrator or the arbitral tribunal to refuse the challenge. The challenge must state the grounds for challenge and response to the decision on not accepting the challenge. The application shall be accompanied with the decision of the arbitral tribunal or arbitrator on not to accepting the challenge. Filing of the challenge to the state court is subject to a EUR 300 state fee and is heard by the court in accordance with the general rules of court procedure by written proceedings, unless the court deems it necessary to hold a hearing. The failure of these persons to appear does not prevent the application from being considered. The court hears the case in a closed session and the decision of the court is not subject to appeal. The decision of the court is communicated to the parties involved and also to the permanent arbitration court. 5. Termination of the Arbitrator's Mandate In accordance with Art. 18, the arbitrator’s authority expires and his/her mandate is terminated in the following circumstances: “1) when a decision on the dismissal of the arbitrator is taken; 2) if the arbitrator has resigned from deciding a civil dispute; 3) if the parties have agreed on removal of the arbitrator; 4) if limitations stipulated in Article 15 [Person who may not be appointed as an arbitrator] of this Law apply to the arbitrator; 5) upon death of the arbitrator; 6) in other cases as stipulated in arbitration rules of a permanent arbitration court; 7) with the decision of district (city) court on removal of an arbitrator.” Latvian law does not provide a possibility to request judicial review of a decision terminating an arbitrator’s mandate. When an arbitrator is removed, a substitute arbitrator must be appointed in the same manner used to appoint the removed arbitrator. 6. Liability of Arbitrators Latvian law does not contain any special provisions relating to the liability of arbitrators. Therefore, the general rules of liability would apply, as generally contained in Latvian civil law. In one important decision, the Latvian Constitutional Court remarked in passing that arbitrators may in principle be held liable for rendering an unenforceable award.26. Judgment 2004-10-01 as of 17 January 2005, available (in Latvian) at: <www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/upload/2004-10-01.rtf>. However, the Constitutional Court did not elaborate on the topic any further. Besides this obiter dictum statement by the Latvian Constitutional Court, to the authors’ knowledge, no case relating to the liability of arbitrators has been decided in Latvia.

sections.title: CHAPTER IV: ARBITRAL PROCEDURE
sections.text: CHAPTER IV: ARBITRAL PROCEDURE 1. Place of Arbitration a. Determination The parties are free to determine the place of arbitration. If the parties have not agreed on the place of arbitration, it is determined by the arbitral tribunal, taking into account the efficiency considerations (Art. 25 of the Arbitration Law, see Annex I hereto). For example, the Rules of Arbitration of the LCCI Court of Arbitration provide that the place of arbitration will be the seat of the LCCI unless otherwise agreed by the parties. If the place of arbitration is in Latvia, Art. 1(2) of the Arbitration Law provides that the provisions of the Arbitration Law shall apply. b. Legal consequences The place of arbitration is a legal concept, not necessarily the physical location where hearings take place. It identifies which country’s arbitration law applies to the proceedings, including the rules on court intervention and support, challenges to arbitrators, procedural guarantees and setting aside (annulment) of an award (see Art. 533.1 of the Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”, see Annex II hereto) providing that an arbitral award can be set aside only if it has been “adopted in Latvia”). 2. Arbitral Proceedings in General a. Mandatory provisions All arbitral proceedings seated in Latvia are governed by the Arbitration Law. The Arbitration Law contains relatively detailed rules and thus does not leave much discretion to either the arbitral tribunal or the parties. b. Determining procedure Article 19 of the Arbitration Law provides that both parties shall have equal procedural rights in the arbitration proceedings. The arbitral tribunal must ensure that all parties have equal possibilities to exercise their rights to protect their interests. Article 26 of the Arbitration Law provides that arbitration proceedings must be conducted in accordance with the arbitration agreement, the arbitration rules of the permanent arbitration court, applicable laws and general legal principles. If the parties have agreed to conduct arbitration under the arbitration rules of a permanent arbitration court, the proceedings are conducted in accordance with the arbitration rules and provisions of the Arbitration Law. Whereas in ad hoc proceedings, the proceedings are organized by arbitral tribunal as they consider it appropriate in accordance with requirements set in the Arbitration Law. The chairman of the arbitral tribunal is entitled to decide procedural issues single-handedly, if either the parties or other arbitrators have entrusted him to do so. The arbitration procedure is commenced by presenting a statement of claim (Art. 34(1)), outlining the facts, and presenting the available evidence (Art. 35). The respondent is required to submit its response presenting its position and evidence. The term for providing the response may not be less than fifteen days from the date when the statement of claim has been dispatched to the respondent (Art. 37(1)). c. Exchange of written pleadings In arbitration proceedings – similarly to litigation before courts of general jurisdiction in Latvia – the claimant begins by submitting a statement of claim setting out its position, all claims, and providing the supporting evidence. In turn, the respondent submits a statement of defence in response to the claim. Neither the Arbitration Law nor the arbitration rules of local arbitration courts require the parties to exchange any additional written pleadings. However, the arbitral tribunal may order further written exchanges if it considers them necessary for the proper conduct of the proceedings or if such an exchange is requested by a party. d. Oral hearing Pursuant to Art. 40 of the Arbitration Law, an arbitral tribunal, paying due regard to the parties’ arbitration agreement, may either hold hearings in order to examine the explanations and objections of the parties as well as the evidence (i.e., an oral procedure) or resolve the dispute on the basis of the written evidence and submitted materials only (i.e., a written procedure). An arbitral tribunal can settle the case based merely on written evidence only where the parties have provided in the arbitration agreement that the arbitral proceedings shall take place in writing. If none of the parties requests the arbitral tribunal to hold oral hearings, the arbitral proceedings shall be conducted only by written procedure. However, a request for a hearing can be submitted at any time before the final award is rendered according to Art. 40(4) of the Arbitration Law, even if the parties in the arbitration agreement have agreed on the written procedure. The Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia in decision no. SPC-38/2012 of 18 June 201227. Decision no. SPC38/2012 of 18 June 2012, available (in Latvian) at: <http://at.gov.lv/‌files/uploads/files/archive/department1/2012/38-spc-2012.doc>. has confirmed that “law grants a higher priority to oral proceedings than written. Therefore, even in cases where parties have agreed on written proceedings in arbitration, any party, notwithstanding it, unilaterally may request oral proceedings”. The Supreme Court ruled that arbitration rules that as a precondition for holding an oral hearing require the requesting party to deposit an amount even higher than the overall expenses of the arbitral proceedings, were in violation of the CPL provisions (now Arbitration Law provisions) and that refusal to hold an oral hearing without a deposit placed the requesting party in a situation where it is unable to present its case. 3. Evidence a. General Article 41(1) of the Arbitration Law defines evidence as “information on the basis of which the arbitral tribunal determines the existence or non-existence of such facts that have relevance in deciding the civil dispute”. Under Latvian law, the power to determine the admissibility and eligibility of evidence fully rests with the arbitral tribunal (Art. 41(5) Arbitration Law). Each party must prove the circumstances on which it is substantiating its claims or objections (Art. 41(2) Arbitration Law) and it is the duty of the parties to present the evidence. The Arbitration Law provides that admissible forms of evidence consist of: (1) statements of the parties; (2) written evidence (written documents, audio recordings, videotapes, electronic data carriers, digital videodiscs and others); (3) physical evidence; (4) witnesses and (5) expert opinions. Generally, the arbitral tribunal is not bound by the rules of evidence applied by Latvian courts; however, in practice arbitral tribunals often opt to follow these rules. The Arbitration Law explicitly states that no evidence shall possess predetermined force that may bind the arbitral tribunal (Art. 42(1) Arbitration Law). Furthermore, when drafting an arbitral award, the arbitral tribunal has to give reasons why it has relied on particular evidence instead of other and why it has considered some facts as proved and others unproved. b. Evidence of fact witnesses Until 2024, witness statements were not permissible as evidence in arbitral proceedings. This was due to concerns over the lack of control over this type of evidence, particularly as it could not be subsequently reviewed by a court when determining whether to enforce the arbitral award and the witness could not be held responsible for knowingly false testimony. In practice, to circumvent this restriction, parties frequently introduced witnesses and party-appointed experts as party representatives. These individuals were permitted to attend the hearing and provide “testimony” in the form of a statement on behalf of the party. During their testimony, both the arbitral tribunal and the opposing party were allowed to pose questions to these “party representatives”. However, this workaround had numerous flaws. For instance, “party representatives” had the right to attend the entire hearing and familiarize themselves with the case materials, and they could not be cross-examined. From 2024 onwards, witness statements have been accepted as evidence in arbitral proceedings. Should a witness fail to comply with the requests of the parties or the arbitral tribunal, the tribunal may seek the court’s assistance to hear the witness. According to Art. 532.[6] of the CPL, upon the arbitral tribunal’s request, the district (city) court located at the seat of arbitration is required to undertake the necessary procedural steps to gather evidence that may be pertinent to the case, including hearing witnesses. The district (city) court has the authority to evaluate the validity of such a request, which must therefore include a rationale explaining the relevance of the specific evidence. Per Art. 41[1] of the Arbitration Law, the arbitral tribunal’s request must contain details of the parties involved in the arbitral proceedings, the nature of the dispute, and the facts to be established, as well as the specific evidence to be collected by the court enforcing the request. If the request involves hearing a witness, the questions to be asked of the witness must be outlined. Article 532.[6] of the CPL stipulates that the court shall consider the request in a closed hearing; however, it does not provide further procedural guidance. Given that the request to hear a witness must be submitted by the arbitral tribunal to the court, there was debate within the working group regarding the attendance of court hearings and the potential for parties to cross -examine the witness. It was generally agreed that such an option should be available; however, neither the Arbitration Law nor the CPL explicitly addresses these issues. The minutes of the hearing and other evidence collected during the execution of the request must be forwarded by the court to the arbitral tribunal. The Arbitration Law addresses the issue on who should cover the costs related to obtaining the evidence, and Art. 41.[1](3) of the Arbitration Law provides that expenses related to obtaining of evidence shall be paid by the party requesting the arbitral tribunal to obtain such evidence through the court. Where both parties have made such a request, they shall pay the costs of taking evidence in equal parts. c. Evidence of party-appointed expert witnesses (for tribunal-appointed expert witnesses, see Chapter IV.4) Until 2024, party-appointed experts were treated the same way as fact witnesses and they were not cross-examined by the arbitral tribunal. As of 2024, the situation has changed and now party-appointed (as well as arbitral tribunal appointed) experts can be heard and cross-examined during the hearing, if necessary. d. Documentary evidence Article 41(3) of the Arbitration Law specifies that written evidence inter alia shall include any written documents, audio recordings, videotapes, electronic data carriers, digital videodiscs and others. Written evidence must be submitted in original or in the form of certified copy or excerpt. If a party submits a copy of a document, the arbitral tribunal may of its own accord, or pursuant to the request of the other party, require that the original document is presented. The arbitral tribunal shall return the original document, pursuant to the request of the person who has submitted it, and add its certified copy to the file of the procedure. e. Document disclosure Pursuant to Art. 43(1) of the Arbitration Law, the arbitral tribunal may require the parties to submit documents or other evidence that it has in its disposal, upon the reasoned request from the opposing party. The party filing such request must describe the evidence and provide its reasons why it believes that particular evidence is at the disposal of another party or any third party. The arbitral tribunal has no coercive power to enforce orders for disclosure of the documents, and any such order would be dependent on voluntary compliance by the parties. If a party refuses to provide particular written evidence and does not deny that it is at this party’s disposal, the arbitral tribunal can consider as proven those alleged facts in support of which the requesting party has referred to particular evidence. As regards documents possessed by third parties, as of 2024, the arbitral tribunal may also seek the court’s assistance to gather evidence from any third party, pursuant to the same procedure as set for obtaining witness statements (above); however, in any case the request must specify the document that party is requesting to be collected and must state the reasons why the party believes that particular document is in possession of the third party. 4. Tribunal-Appointed Experts a. Power of the tribunal Unless it has been provided otherwise in the arbitration agreement, upon request of either party the arbitral tribunal may appoint one or several experts (Art. 44(1) Arbitration Law). An arbitral tribunal may not appoint an expert on its own initiative. Article 45(3) of the Arbitration Law provides that the expert’s fees are to be paid by the party who has requested an expert; however, if both parties have requested an expert, the costs are split between the parties equally. b. Role of the parties In practice, the party requesting the expert’s appointment submits to the arbitral tribunal questions it wishes to be answered by the expert. The other party (through the arbitral tribunal) and the arbitral tribunal are allowed to submit their questions to the expert. In accordance with Art. 44(2) of the Arbitration Law, the parties must submit necessary information or documents and present the relevant goods or other articles to the expert. c. Rules applicable to the tribunal-appointed expert Article 44 of the Arbitration Law regulates the use of tribunal-appointed expert evidence in arbitration proceedings. Unless the arbitration agreement provides otherwise, the arbitral tribunal may, at the request of a party, appoint one or more experts and instruct them to prepare an expert opinion. The law does not limit the number of experts that may be appointed: the tribunal may designate multiple independent experts or direct several experts to work together and produce a joint expert opinion. Although the Arbitration Law does not expressly regulate this, arbitral tribunals in practice invite the parties to comment on proposed expert candidates and on the questions to be submitted to the experts. Once appointed, the parties are obliged to provide the expert with all information, documents, goods, or other materials necessary for the preparation of the expert report, as required by the tribunal. Cross-examination of the tribunal-appointed expert is not mandatory. The expert is invited to attend a hearing only if requested by a party, and at such a hearing the parties have the right to put questions to the expert and examine the conclusions set out in the expert opinion. This framework promotes transparency in the preparation and presentation of expert evidence and ensures that both parties have an equal opportunity to engage with, challenge, and test the expert’s findings. Unless the parties agree otherwise, the costs associated with the expert are borne by the party requesting the expert examination. These expenses are treated as costs related to the taking of evidence and may be recovered by the successful party from the unsuccessful party. d. Procedure While the Arbitration Law does not expressly state that the report of the expert must be in writing, this requirement is implied by Art. 44(3), which provides that an expert can be invited to participate in the arbitral hearing and can be cross-examined by the parties and the arbitral tribunal. The Arbitration Law is silent with respect to the parties’ right to receive a copy of the written report of the expert before the hearing. However, as a general rule, the arbitral tribunal must provide the parties with all materials it has received from the other party or any other person. 5. Interim Measures of Protection a. Categories of interim measures – tribunal The Arbitration Law does not specify what sort of interim measures are allowed in arbitral proceedings. The Arbitration Law merely provides that the arbitral tribunal is entitled to adopt a decision for solving procedural questions without deciding the dispute on merits (Art. 52 Arbitration Law). On this basis, the arbitral tribunal is, for instance, allowed to issue orders to preserve perishable goods, or to order a bank guarantee on behalf of one of the parties and to grant any other interim measures of protection. Such measures, adopted by the arbitral tribunal, cannot be enforced compulsory, but the party can comply with them voluntarily. b. Comparison with 2006 Model Law The Arbitration Law does not follow the UNCITRAL Model Law provisions on interim measures of protection. Considering that interim measures ordered by the tribunal are not enforceable through coercive means, the Arbitration Law neither establishes conditions for granting interim measures, nor provides the procedure how preliminary orders are adopted, nor regulate their modification, suspension or termination. c. Form Given that provisional measures ordered by arbitral tribunals are not enforceable through coercive means, the law does not prescribe any specific form in which they must be adopted. Accordingly, the arbitral tribunal may decide at its discretion whether to issue such measures as a separate procedural order, record them in the minutes of the hearing, or render them in the form of an interim award. d. Categories of interim measures – court According to Art. 33 of the Arbitration Law, the district (city) court may decide on securing a claim and granting interim measures in disputes subject to arbitration in accordance with the procedures laid down in the CPL. The district (city) court shall, upon the request by a party, decide as to termination or modification the security measures or interim measures. Until 2024 the court assistance was rather limited allowing the court to decide only on security measures before the commencement of arbitration. Now, the court’s powers are considerably broader allowing to grant all types of interim measures, including security measures, throughout all arbitral proceedings. When deciding on granting security measures and/or interim measures the court shall follow the procedure provided in the CPL. According to Art. 138.1 of the CPL, the court may grant following interim measures: (1) seizing the moveable property owned by the defendant; (2) entering of a pledge notation or another notation in the Land Register, the register of the respective movable property or any other public register; (3) prohibition on a defendant to perform certain activities or an obligation for the defendant to perform specific activities within a specified period; (4) postponement of enforcement activities (also enjoining bailiffs from transferring money or property to a creditor or debtor, or staying of sale of property); (5) interim regulation of contested relations. The court shall apply interim measures upon a reasoned application by the claimant if: (1) there are sufficient grounds to believe that the claimant has the right whose protection he or she is seeking; (2) there are sufficient grounds to believe that the right belonging to the claimant is being infringed or will be infringed; (3) there are sufficient grounds to believe that the aforementioned infringement causes or will cause significant damage to the claimant if provisional protection is not applied; (4) the balance of interests is in favour of applying interim protection. The first two conditions together constitute the prima facie validity of the claim. As regards security measures, Art. 138 provides that the means by which a claim may be secured are: (1) seizure of movable property and monetary funds of the defendant; (2) entering of a pledge notation in the register of the respective movable property or any other public register; (3) entering of a notation regarding the securing of a claim in the Land Register or Ship Register; (4) arrest of a ship; (5) prohibition on a defendant to perform certain activities or an obligation for the defendant to perform specific activities within a specified period; (6) seizure of those payments which are due from third persons, including monetary funds in credit institutions and other financial authorities; (7) postponement of enforcement activities (also enjoining bailiffs from transferring money or property to a creditor or debtor, or staying of sale of property). The court will only grant security for the claim if there is a prima facie claim and: (1) the claim is of a pecuniary nature; (2) enforcement of the arbitral award without security for the claim may be difficult or impossible; (3) the means of security for the claim chosen by the claimant are appropriate to secure the claim; (4) the choice of a specific means of securing the claim is a necessary prerequisite in this dispute in order to ensure the enforcement of the judgment in the future; (5) the infringement of the defendant’s interests by the application of the specified means of securing the claim does not have significantly greater negative consequences than the infringement of the claimant’s interests. Both interim measures and security measures can be requested at any stage of the arbitral proceedings and also before commencement of arbitral proceedings. If either the security measures or the interim measures are granted before commencement of the arbitral proceedings, the court will set a deadline for commencement of the arbitral proceedings that shall not be more than thirty days. The competent court to decide on granting interim measures and security measures would be the district (city) court where the debtor (defendant) is residing or where the assets of the debtor (defendant) are located (Art. 137(7) of the CPL). Although the CPL refers only to jurisdiction of these two courts, the purpose of the amendments to the CPL introduced in 2024 introducing court’s assistance to the arbitral proceedings was never intended to limit the number of competent courts, therefore also the court at the place of arbitration or at the registered address of the permanent arbitration court should recognize their competence to assist. The same court who granted security measures and/or interim measures may decide on their modification, suspension or termination. The request to the court for granting security measures and/or interim measures is subject to fixed state fee of EUR 80. 6. Representation and Legal Assistance Natural persons may participate in arbitral proceedings themselves or through their authorized representatives. Legal persons are represented by their officials who act within the scope of the authorization conferred by them in accordance with law, charter or by-laws, or by other authorized representatives of legal persons (Arbitration Law Art. 32(1)). Unless a party is represented by itself or by its legal representatives, any authorized representative must possess a power of attorney. Art. 32(2) of the Arbitration Law sets limitations for persons who may be authorized representative in the arbitration proceedings, i.e., the representative can be any natural person except a person: “1) who has not attained the age of majority; 2) who is under legal guardianship; 3) who has been deprived of conducting matters of other persons pursuant to a court decision; 4) who is kindred to the third degree or in affinity to the second degree with an arbitrator that is deciding the case; 5) who has given legal advice in a civil dispute to the other party in this case or in a case related to this dispute; 6) who has participated in mediation in this case or in a case related to this dispute.” If any of the above circumstances is established, the arbitral tribunal has a duty not to admit such representative in the arbitration process (Art. 32(3) Arbitration Law). It is prohibited (Art. 32(4) Arbitration Law) for a person who is or within the last two years was in the list of arbitrators of the respective permanent arbitration court, to be a representative of a party. Such person also may not be asked to provide legal assistance during proceedings of this permanent arbitration court. This limitation was imposed in order to reduce the interconnection between arbitrators, party representatives and representatives of the arbitral institution. Until 2024, the prohibition period during which the arbitrators included in the list of permanent arbitration court are not allowed to provide parties legal assistance, was reduced from five to two years. There are no rules requiring that the representative of the party must be a lawyer. A party may be represented by any authorized person, including a non-lawyer. 7. Default Failure of a party (either for claimant or for respondent) to appear before the arbitral tribunal without a valid cause after due notice has been issued and delivered or a failure to submit written evidence is not an obstacle to the continuation of the arbitral proceedings by the arbitral tribunal and deciding the dispute on the basis of the evidence at its disposal (Art. 46 Arbitration Law). 8. Confidentiality of the Award and Proceedings a. Confidentiality of the arbitral award and proceedings Unless parties have agreed otherwise, the arbitration process is confidential (Art. 23 Arbitration Law). The Arbitration Law further provides that the arbitral hearings are held in camera, and the arbitral tribunal shall not provide information to other persons or public regarding arbitration proceedings, unless the parties have agreed otherwise. Persons, who are not parties to arbitration process, may participate in arbitration hearing only with permission of the parties. In practice it is also recognized in Latvia that there is an implied duty of confidentiality on the parties to maintain confidentiality of documents and other information exchanged in the arbitration proceedings. However, there have been no court rulings on this issue. Most of the arbitration rules of permanent arbitration courts provide for the confidentiality of arbitral proceedings and arbitral hearings. The LCCI Rules of Arbitration provide that a duty of confidentiality is imposed also on the tribunal and the administrative staff of the institution (Art. 12 of the LCCI Rules of Arbitration). b. Confidentiality of arbitration-related court proceedings In accordance with Arts. 532.[1](6) and 532.[6](2) of the CPL, any requests for the appointment, challenge, or removal of an arbitrator, or challenges to the jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal, shall be conducted in private hearings. This includes not disclosing the identities of the parties in the court’s hearing schedule and prohibiting any third party from participating in the hearings or accessing the case materials. However, legal proceedings for annulment and recognition and enforcement of arbitral awards are governed by the general rules for national court proceedings as set out in the CPL; as a result, documents filed in legal proceedings become part of the public record and are therefore not confidential. The Arbitration Law stipulates that information about the arbitration process is provided to the individuals who are entitled to such information for the fulfilment of their duties as prescribed by the law (Art. 23 Arbitration Law). According to the provisions of the CPL, such individuals include, for example, a judge reviewing an application for setting aside or the enforcement of an arbitral award. Although in practice the judge returns the arbitration case file to the permanent arbitration court after examination, he or she may make necessary copies and refer to the contents of the file in court’s decision. All information upon which the judge bases his or her decision must remain in the court file of the case and thus becomes part of the public record. According to the general provisions of the CPL, a party may request the court to treat certain documents as confidential and may ask the court to conduct a closed hearing (Art. 11(3) CPL).28. Art. 11(3) of the CPL provides: The court has the discretion to decide whether the hearing shall be heard in camera. In such instances, only the conclusive part of the judgment will be announced publicly. However, only the enforcement of international arbitral awards is determined in a court hearing; domestic arbitral awards are decided by a judge through written proceedings without the need to summon the parties. All court decisions, including those on setting aside and recognition and enforcement, should be recorded in the public database of the Court Administration, although in practice this is not always the case. Only the judgments of Latvian courts that have become final are published in the public database, but procedural decisions and decisions, including those regarding the setting aside and enforcement of arbitral awards that are issued as decisions and not judgments, remain unpublished. Before publishing of the judgments, the judgments are anonymized, i.e., the names, addresses and other identifying information of the parties is removed. There is no Latvian privacy legislation specifically relevant to arbitration.

sections.title: CHAPTER V: ARBITRAL AWARD
sections.text: CHAPTER V: ARBITRAL AWARD 1. Types of Award The Arbitration Law (see Annex I hereto) provides that an arbitral tribunal may adopt procedural decisions or arbitral awards. The decisions resolve procedural issues without solving the merits of the case, but an arbitral award covers the merits of the case. After the final award has been rendered and if the parties have not agreed otherwise, the arbitral tribunal may make a supplementary award upon a request from either of the parties filed within thirty days from the day the award is sent to the parties, if any of the claims submitted prior to the making of the award have not been decided in the final award (Art. 55(3) Arbitration Law). A decision ordering interim relief may not be issued in the form of an award and, even if it were so issued, it would not be enforceable as an award. 2. Making of the Award a. Decision-making Pursuant to Art. 51 of the Arbitration Law, where the arbitral tribunal is composed of three or more arbitrators, the arbitral award must be adopted by a majority vote. An exception exists only with respect to procedural matters, where the law expressly provides that the presiding arbitrator may decide independently if such authority has been delegated by the parties or by the other arbitrators. Permanent arbitration institutions typically include in their procedural rules a provision authorizing the presiding arbitrator to decide on procedural issues independently. b. Time limits As to the timing of issuing an award, Art. 54 of the Arbitration Law provides that the arbitral tribunal shall render the arbitral award within fourteen days after it has finished reviewing the merits of the dispute. The arbitral award must be dispatched to the parties within three days after it has been rendered. Since the role of the courts in arbitral proceedings is very limited, it is impossible to approach the court for an extension of time. However, in practice, in most cases, the tribunal and the parties come to an agreement on the extension of the time limit. c. Dissenting opinions Even though the Arbitration Law contains no specific provisions on dissenting opinions, it is accepted practice in Latvia that an arbitrator may produce a dissenting opinion. Whether the dissenting opinion must be annexed to the award is not regulated. 3. Form of the Award Article 54 of the Arbitration Law provides that the award shall be made in writing and shall be signed by the arbitrators. If the arbitral tribunal consists of several arbitrators, the award shall be signed by all the arbitrators. If an arbitrator, for any reason, does not sign the award, the award shall stipulate the reasons explaining why the arbitrator’s signature is missing. The award must also contain the reasons on which it is based, unless otherwise expressly agreed by the parties. More specifically, in accordance with Art. 54(4) of the Arbitration Law, the award must contain the following information: “1) the composition of the arbitral tribunal; 2) the date of rendering of the arbitral award and the place of arbitration; 3) information concerning parties – for natural persons – name, surname, personal ID code, other identification data and declared place of residence or other address for communication; for legal persons – name; registration number; other identification data and legal address; 4) the subject matter of the dispute; 5) reasons for the arbitral award, if parties have not agreed otherwise; 6) the conclusion as to full or partial satisfaction of a claim or full or partial dismissal of a claim as well as the nature of the arbitral award; 7) the amount to be recovered, if the arbitral award is issued regarding the recovery of a monetary sum, by indicating separately the underlying debt and interest, time period as to which interest has been granted and the rights of a claimant to claim interest for the time period until the enforcement of the arbitral award and its amount; 8) the specific asset and its value that shall be recovered in case the asset has perished, if the arbitral award is issued regarding the restitution of an asset in integrum; 9) what actions shall be carried out by whom and in what time period if the arbitral award stipulates the carrying out of specific actions; 10) which part of an arbitral award relates to which claimant if an arbitral award is issued for the benefit of multiple claimants, or which part of an arbitral award shall be carried out by which defendant if an arbitral award is issued against multiple defendants; 11) the costs of arbitration process and division of the costs between the parties; 12) the costs of parties relating to legal assistance, if any, and division of legal costs between the parties; 13) other information that the arbitral tribunal deems relevant.” Pursuant to Art. 55 of the Arbitration Law, until the execution of the award, each party, notifying other party thereof, may request the arbitral tribunal to correct the award, clarify (interpret) the award or make a supplementary award. In addition, Art. 536 of the Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) (with reference to Art. 533.[4] CPL) (see Annex II hereto) sets grounds on which enforcement of an award may be refused (see Chapter VI). If the enforcement of the award has been refused, a party is not entitled to apply to the arbitral tribunal for correction of the award, but depending on the ground for refusal to enforce arbitral award, may submit the dispute anew to an arbitration. 4. Jurisdiction a. Power of arbitrators The arbitral tribunal may rule on its jurisdiction, including when a party disputes the validity or existence of the arbitral agreement (Art. 24(1) Arbitration Law). The party may challenge the jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal until the term set for filing of the response to the statement of claim. If the party fails to submit the challenge, such failure will be considered as a waiver of the right to object to jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal. In case there is no arbitration agreement, failure to submit the response challenging the jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal will result in entering into an arbitration agreement (see Art. 12(2)(3) Arbitration Law). b. Form of jurisdictional decision Arbitrators have the discretion to rule on jurisdiction as a preliminary ruling (procedural decision) or as part of the final award. c. Appeal If the arbitral tribunal has issued a preliminary ruling on jurisdiction, this ruling may be challenged in the district court within thirty days from the date the ruling was received (Art. 532.[5](1) CPL). The challenge must articulate the grounds for the appeal and include a copy of the arbitral tribunal’s decision. The submission of the appeal incurs a state fee of EUR 300 and is processed by the court following the standard rules of court procedure. The proceedings are conducted in writing, unless the court finds it necessary to convene a hearing. The absence of the opposing party does not hinder the consideration of the application. The court reviews the case in a closed session. The court’s decision is final and cannot be appealed. The court’s decision is communicated to both parties and to the permanent arbitration court or, in the case of ad hoc arbitration, to the arbitral tribunal. d. Timing The plea that arbitrators have no jurisdiction can be submitted to the arbitral tribunal not later than on the day the statement of defence is due. e. Role of court Where the tribunal issues a preliminary decision on jurisdiction, the party contesting that decision may apply to the district (city) court for judicial review in accordance with Art. 24(3) of the Arbitration Law. The submission of a request to the district (city) court to decide on jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal does not suspend or impede the continuation of the arbitration proceedings, allowing the tribunal to proceed with the case unless the court decides otherwise. 5. Applicable Law a. Domestic arbitration The arbitral tribunal may decide the dispute as amiables compositeurs or ex aequo et bono only if explicitly empowered to do so by the parties. It is uncommon that parties opt to settle the dispute in this manner. b. International commercial arbitration Article 19 of the Civil Law provides that with respect to any obligations, rights and duties arising from the contract, one must first ascertain whether the contracting parties have agreed to determine their relationship in accordance with any specific law. Any such agreement on the applicable law between the parties shall be in effect insofar as it is not in conflict with mandatory or prohibitory norms of Latvian law. Article 19 of the Civil Law further provides that if no such agreement exists, it is presumed that the contracting parties have subjected their obligations to the laws of the state where the obligation is to be performed. If it is not possible to determine the place where the obligation is to be performed, the law of the place where the contract was entered into shall apply. Finally, contracts entered into by institutions of the State of Latvia and local governments of Latvia shall be adjudged in respect of their substance and consequences in accordance with Latvian law, provided that the contract itself does not stipulate otherwise. 6. Settlement If parties enter into a settlement during any stage of the arbitral proceedings, the arbitration procedure shall be terminated and, pursuant to a request of the parties, the arbitral tribunal shall confirm the settlement in the form of an award, encompassing terms and conditions of the settlement that parties have agreed upon. Such an award shall have the same status and legal force as any other arbitral award would have whereby the dispute would be decided on its merits. The arbitral tribunal shall refuse to confirm the settlement if it violates the rights or legal interests of a third party (Art. 53(2) Arbitration Law). Settlement confirmed by the arbitral tribunal in the form of an award must correspond and comply with the same requirements that have been made for any regular arbitral award and it may be enforced in the same way as a regular arbitral award. If the arbitration procedure has been terminated because the parties have reached a settlement, repeated recourse to the arbitration or court between the same parties concerning the same dispute and on the same grounds is not permissible. (Art. 57(2) Arbitration Law). 7A. Correction and Interpretation of the Award a. Correction An arbitral tribunal on its own motion or upon the request of one of the parties, may correct any grammatical or printing error or any calculations in the award. Such corrections can be made without summoning the parties, but the arbitral tribunal is free to hold a hearing to decide on the corrections necessary. If the arbitral tribunal decides to hold a hearing, it shall notify parties thereof at least fifteen days in advance. If as a result of the corrections the dispositive part of the award will change, the arbitral tribunal is required to invite the parties to express their opinion. It is established practice that if the arbitral tribunal has failed to provide the date or the place of arbitration in the award or the award has not been signed, it cannot be considered as a calculation, grammatical or printing error and consequently cannot be corrected. Latvian law does not provide for an option to revert to the arbitral tribunal during the setting aside or enforcement proceedings, if at the district (city) court it is concluded that the arbitration award is found to have defects that could be remedied by correction or interpretation. b. Interpretation Unless the parties have agreed otherwise, each party, notifying the other party thereof within thirty days after the award has been dispatched to the parties or receipt of the award, if it has been handed out in person, may ask the arbitral tribunal to interpret the award, without altering its content (Art. 55(2) Arbitration Law). Such an interpretation of the award becomes an integral part of the arbitral award. c. Form The correction or interpretation of the arbitral award shall be made in writing and signed by all members of the arbitral tribunal in order to become an integral (and thus enforceable) part of the final award. It is confirmed in the Latvian court practice, that correction or interpretation must be made in a separate document, i.e., not by incorporating the changes in the initial documents and signing it as the original document. d. Timing Although the law does not expressly define time limits for corrections, in practice all and any corrections shall be made before the execution of the award. 7B. Additional Award The Arbitration Law provides for the possibility of issuing an additional award “if some of the claims raised have not been dealt with, provided that evidence had been submitted and parties had given explanations regarding it” (Art. 55(3) Arbitration Law). The Arbitration Law provides that unless otherwise agreed by parties, a party may, within thirty days after the arbitral institution has sent out the arbitral award or the date of receipt thereof if an award has been served in person, request an arbitral tribunal to make an additional award if any of the claims have not been dealt with, provided that evidence had submitted and parties had given explanations regarding it. If an arbitral tribunal deems such request justified, it shall make an additional award. If the arbitral tribunal deems such request to be justified, it must notify parties regarding an arbitration hearing in which the decision may be taken on the making of such an additional award no later than fifteen days in advance. Failure to appear by parties shall not hinder the making of an additional award. The supplementary award must be rendered following the same content and form requirements that have been set for regular awards. 8. Fees and Costs a. General / Types of fees and costs The Arbitration Law stipulates that costs of the arbitration proceedings include costs relating to the adjudication of the dispute and arbitrators’ fees (Art. 45(1) Arbitration Law). Arbitration rules of permanent arbitral institutions contain more detailed provisions regarding fees and costs related to arbitration proceedings. The Arbitration Law specifies that, unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the costs of expertise, the tribunal’s secretary, translator, and expert witness involvement in the arbitration proceedings, as well as other expenses in the arbitration process, are to be borne by the party who requested the services of the secretary, translator, or expert, or who has sought expertise. If such a request is made by both parties, the costs are shared equally between them (Art. 45(3)). This allocation refers to covering of the costs during the arbitration process, and can be allocated by the arbitral tribunal when deciding who will bear the costs. b. Deposit Although the payment of a deposit is not covered by the Arbitration Law, this issue is governed by the arbitration rules of permanent arbitration institutions. For instance, the Rules of Arbitration of the LCCI Court of Arbitration stipulate that the arbitrators’ fees must be fully paid – by one or both parties – before the case is handed over to the arbitral tribunal. Each party is invited to pay a half of the costs; however, if the respondent refuses, the claimant is invited to pay the deposit in full.
