A failure to pay the deposit may result in the Secretariat of the LCCI Court of Arbitration deciding to terminate the arbitration proceedings (Art. 66 and Art. 2 of the Second Appendix of the LCCI Rules of Arbitration). c. Fees of arbitrators In institutional arbitration, the amount of the arbitrators’ fees is set out in the arbitration rules. Nonetheless, the parties are entitled to agree on different fees, provided they have the arbitrator’s consent. As a general rule, in institutional arbitration, there is a fixed fee rate, calculated on the basis of the sum in dispute. At the time of writing, the LCCI Court of Arbitration rates are as follows (excluding VAT): Sum in dispute Administrative expenses Each arbitrator’s fees up to EUR 1,000 EUR 250 EUR 125 from EUR 1,001 to EUR 5,000 EUR 250 EUR 175 From EUR 5,001 to EUR 10,000 EUR 250 EUR 250 from EUR 10,001 to EUR 25,000 EUR 500 EUR 250 from EUR 25,001 to EUR 100,000 EUR 750 + 1 % of sum which exceeds EUR 25,001 EUR 500 from EUR 100,001 to EUR 500,000 EUR 1,500 + 0.7 % of sum which exceeds EUR 100,001 EUR 1,250 from EUR 500,001 to EUR 1,000,000 EUR 2,000 + 0.5 % of sum which exceeds EUR 500,001 EUR 2,000 from EUR 1,000,001 to EUR 5,000,000 EUR 3,000 + 0.2 % of sum which exceeds EUR 1,000,001 EUR 3,000 over EUR 5,000,001 EUR 5,000 + 0.05 % of sum which exceeds EUR 5,000,001 EUR 5,000 In the Baltic International Court of Arbitration, the rates are as follows (excluding VAT): Sum in dispute Administrative expenses Each arbitrator’s feesIf the dispute is settled by 1arbitrator 3arbitrators Up to EUR 1 500 10% of the sum,but not less than EUR 70 EUR 100 EUR 70 From EUR 1,500 to EUR 6,999.99 EUR 150 + 2% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 1 500 EUR 150 EUR 100 From EUR 7,000 to EUR 29,999.99 EUR 260 + 1.5% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 7,000 EUR 300 EUR 200 From EUR 30,000 to EUR 149,999.99 EUR 605 + 1% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 30,000 EUR 700 EUR 400 From EUR 150,000 to EUR 699,999.99 EUR 1,805 + 0.6% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 150,000 EUR 1,200 EUR 800 From EUR 700,000 to EUR 1 499,999.99 EUR 5,105 + 0.3% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 700,000 EUR 2,000 EUR 1,400 Over EUR 1,500,000 EUR 7,505 + 0.1% of the sum,which exceeds 1,500,000 EUR EUR 5,000 EUR 3,500 Non-property claim EUR 200 EUR 200 EUR 150 d. Costs of legal assistance to parties The parties are entitled to be compensated for costs of legal assistance. There is no requirement setting a cap for legal costs that could be awarded or that party costs be reasonable, nor are there any special rules that would apply to the award of party costs. e. Allocation of costs The amount of costs shall be determined by the arbitral tribunal, “taking into account the amount of a claim, complexity of a dispute and provisions referred to in an arbitration agreement and other relevant circumstances” (Art. 45(2) Arbitration Law). As a general rule, arbitration costs follow the event, i.e., the loser pays. In practice, arbitrators can also apportion the costs between the parties, where the parties have won on some claims and lost on others or there have been other particularities of the case (for example, the party has tried to delay proceedings or due to the party’s fault there have been unnecessary costs incurred). 9. Notification of the Award and Registration An arbitral award does not need to be registered or deposited with a court or any other institution. There is no official record of issued arbitral awards. 10. Enforcement of Domestic and International Awards Rendered In [Country of Report] a. General Where a party voluntarily complies with the award or compulsory execution of the award is not necessary, no leave for enforcement is required. An award has to provide for a period for voluntary compliance with the award, which cannot be less than ten days (Art. 58(1) Arbitration Law). Where a party does not comply voluntarily, the interested party is entitled to apply to the district (city) court at the opposing party’s place of registration or declared address, or at the place of enforcement of the award for issue of a writ of execution for compulsory enforcement of the arbitral award (Art. 534 CPL). The state fee depends on amount of awarded sum (see table below), but cannot exceed EUR 300.00 (Annex III of the CPL). Amount of awarded sum(euro) State fee(euro) 1–700 5 701–1800 15 1801–3300 25 3301–5200 45 5201–7500 65 7501–10 200 90 10 201–13 300 115 13 301–16 800 150 16 801–20 700 185 20 701–25 000 225 above 25 000 300 Article 535(2) of the CPL provides that the court may request from the parties and also from the permanent arbitration court or the arbitral tribunal the arbitration case file or any other information necessary for deciding the request. The CPL states that if a dispute is resolved under by institutional arbitration, the arbitration case file shall remain in the archives of the permanent arbitration court for ten years after the end of the arbitral proceedings (Art. 50 Arbitration Law). The permanent arbitration court shall store the arbitration case file in accordance with the archival storage procedures provided for by law. If a dispute is resolved by ad hoc arbitration, the number of copies of the procedural documents (arbitration case file) shall correspond to the number of the parties to the dispute, and they shall remain with the parties. b. Leave for enforcement There is no requirement to apply for leave to enforce an arbitral award. c. Enforcement i. Grounds for refusal Article 534(2) of the CPL provides that a party requesting enforcement of the award must present to the court (1) the arbitral award; (2) a document containing the arbitration agreement, and (3) a document evidencing payment of the state fee. The Arbitration Law provides in Art. 535(2) CPL) that upon request of the court, the arbitral institution (if institutional arbitration) or the parties (if ad hoc arbitration) must provide information necessary for deciding on whether to grant a request for a writ of execution. A decision on whether to issue or refuse a writ of execution is taken by a judge on the basis of the submitted documents without summoning the parties. According to Art. 536(1) of the CPL, the judge shall refuse to issue a writ of execution, on the same grounds that are provided for setting aside of the award (Art. 533[4] CPL), with the addition that writ of execution also can be refused if the award has not become binding on the parties or has been set aside in the country where it was adopted. ii. Procedure The decision of the district (city) court to refuse enforcement of an arbitral award can be appealed by filing ancillary complaint about the court’s decision. Only the applicant is entitled to appeal, i.e., a party against whom the award shall be enforced may not appeal the decision to enforce arbitral award, as he could have exercised its rights to object by filing a request for annulment of arbitral award. An ancillary complaint may be submitted within ten days from the adoption of the decision of the court and it must be addressed to the regional court, i.e., a court of appeal. An ancillary complaint is subject to security deposit payment in amount of EUR 80 (Art. 34(5) and Annex III of the CPL).29. Available at <www.likumi.lv/doc.php?id=50500>. The court’s ruling on an ancillary complaint is not subject to appeal to the Supreme Court. 11. Publication of the Award Arbitral awards are not generally published in Latvia. An award might be published if all parties so agree, but as a general rule such an option is not even discussed between the parties. The consent of the parties may not be presumed. There are no special rules for cases where publication would be needed in the public interest.

sections.title: CHAPTER VI: ENFORCEMENT OF FOREIGN ARBITRAL AWARDS
sections.text: CHAPTER VI: ENFORCEMENT OF FOREIGN ARBITRAL AWARDS 1. Enforcement under Conventions and Treaties a. Conventions in force Latvia has been a party to the 1958 Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (the “New York Convention”)30. The New York Convention has been in force in Latvia from 13 July 1992. since 1992, and to the Convention on Settlement of Investment Disputes Between States and Nationals of Other States (“ICSID Convention”)31. The ICSID Convention has been in force in Latvia from 7 July 1997. See <https://icsid.worldbank.org/sites/default/files/ICSID_Convention_EN.pdf>. since 1997. As of 2003, Latvia has been a party to the 1961 European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration.32. The European Convention has been in force in Latvia from 12 February 2003, with a reservation as to Art. 2.2, which was waived only in 13 November 2013. It was ratified by the “Law on European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration”, adopted on 23 January 2003, published in 23 Latvijas Vēstnesis(12 February 2003, no. 2788). b. Procedure The Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) contains a separate chapter (Chap. 78) on Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (see Annex III hereto). The CPL stipulates that enforcement of foreign arbitral awards takes place “in accordance with this Law [the CPL] and international agreements in force for the Republic of Latvia” (Art. 646 CPL). The procedure for enforcing a foreign arbitral award is set out in Arts. 647-651 of the CPL, which provide for the making of an application for recognition and execution of a foreign award to the appropriate district court. Documents to be submitted with the application include “the original of the foreign arbitral award or a properly certified true copy of it” (Art. 647(3)(1)), an officially translated document containing the arbitration agreement and confirmation of payment of the state fee. The application shall also include the information provided in Art. 638 CPL.33. Art. 638(2) provides: c. Court decisions According to Art. 649(4) of the CPL, “[a]n application shall only be rejected in the cases provided for by international treaties binding upon the Republic of Latvia”. In practice, therefore, the court review in enforcement proceedings is carried out by directly applying the provisions of the New York Convention, including the limited grounds for refusal of enforcement included in the text of the Convention. The CPL also provides that an “ancillary complaint” may be submitted regarding a court decision to recognize and enforce – or refuse the recognition and enforcement of – a foreign arbitral award (Art. 649(5)). A party cannot attack the award on the merits. Such an ancillary complaint may be submitted within ten days from the adoption of the decision of the court and it must be addressed to the appropriate regional court, i.e., a court of appeal. An ancillary complaint is subject to security deposit payment in amount of EUR 80. (Art. 34(5) and Annex III of the CPL).34. Available at <www.likumi.lv/doc.php?id=50500>. The court’s ruling on an ancillary complaint is not subject to appeal. The authors are not aware of any court decision that gave a notable interpretation of the New York Convention. 2. Enforcement Where No Convention or Treaty Applies The CPL does not require that for a foreign arbitral award to be enforceable, it must fall under any convention or treaty. The authors are not aware of any court decisions in Latvia dealing with recognition and enforcement of an award issued in a non-New York Convention country; however, in such a case, we consider it likely that the award would still be recognized and enforced under the Convention’s provisions. The CPL does not set any time limits for making the request for enforcement, nor does it require leave for enforcement in the country where the award was made. 3. Rules of Public Policy In the case of enforcement of foreign awards, the public policy ground mentioned in the New York Convention would be applicable (see Art. 649(4) CPL). The authors are not aware of any Latvian court decision where the foreign award would be not recognized and enforced on the basis of public policy.

sections.title: CHAPTER VII: MEANS OF RECOURSE
sections.text: CHAPTER VII: MEANS OF RECOURSE 1. Appeal on the Merits from an Arbitral Award a. Appeal to a second arbitral instance The arbitral award comes into effect on the day it is made and it may not be appealed (Art. 51(2) of the Arbitration Law, see Annex I hereto). Thus, the Arbitration Law establishes a single-tier arbitral process, and once the arbitral tribunal issues the award, the proceedings are deemed final within the arbitral system. The parties cannot create an appellate arbitral body by agreement, nor can an arbitral institution establish such a mechanism in its rules, as the legislation does not recognize or permit a hierarchy of arbitral tribunals. b. Appeal to a court Under Latvian law, no appeal on the merits of an arbitral award is permitted, and the Arbitration Law provides no mechanism by which a court may re-examine the substance of the case decided by the arbitral tribunal. The only recourse against an arbitral award is an application for its setting aside on the limited grounds expressly provided in the Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”). 2. Setting Aside of the Arbitral Award (Action for Annulment, Vacation of the Award) a. Grounds for setting aside Following the Constitutional Court judgment of 25 January 2022 “On Compliance of Section 534, 534[1], 535, 536 and 537 with the first sentence of Art. 92 of the Satversme of the Republic of Latvia”, in 2024 the legislator introduced a mechanism for setting aside of arbitral awards. This mechanism was introduced by adopting amendments to the CPL and Arbitration Law. Only those awards that have been rendered in Latvia can be set aside by Latvian courts. The award can be set aside only on limited grounds that correspond to those listed in the Model Law. These grounds are listed in Art. 533[4] CPL and differentiate between grounds which existence must be proved by the applicant, and those assessed by the court ex officio: “(1) The court may set aside an arbitral award if: 1) the arbitration agreement was concluded by a party who was not entitled to enter into the arbitration agreement or the arbitration agreement is not valid; 2) a party to the arbitration agreement was not given proper notice of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the arbitral proceedings or was otherwise unable to present his case; 3) the arbitral award deals with a dispute which is not provided for in or is inconsistent with the arbitration agreement, or exceeds the limits of the claim or the competence of the arbitration court; 4) the composition of an arbitral tribunal or the arbitration proceedings were not conducted in accordance with the arbitration agreement of the parties, unless such agreement is contrary to the provisions of laws and regulations which the parties could not have waived. (2) The court sets aside the arbitral award if it finds that: 1) the particular civil legal dispute may be resolved only by a court; 2) the arbitral award is contrary to public policy. (3) If the arbitral award deals with several matters and the court finds the ground referred to in Clause 3 of Paragraph One of this Article in respect of any such matter, the court shall set aside the award partially in relation to that matter.” In assessing whether grounds for setting aside an arbitral award exist, the court will also consider whether the applicant has raised objections in a timely and appropriate manner and whether it has established the existence of the infringement or non-compliance in question. For instance, objections that the arbitrator was not impartial and neutral will also be assessed in conjunction with whether the party was already aware at the time of the arbitration of the circumstances giving rise to doubts about the person’s impartiality and neutrality and if it had adequately informed the arbitral tribunal and the other party thereof. Any objections must be supported by evidence, as otherwise objections will be considered as ungrounded. b. Procedure The application for setting aside of the arbitral award or supplementary award may be submitted within thirty days from the date of rendering the arbitral award or supplementary award. Arbitral award can be set aside only on basis of limited grounds set forth in Art. 533[4] CPL and only if the arbitral award has not already been enforced. Such limitation was introduced to emphasize the importance for the party against whom the award has been rendered to be active and request setting aside of arbitral award in due manner. An application for setting aside of the arbitral award may be submitted to the district (city) court at the declared place of residence or registered office of the interested party; if there is no such place, it should be submitted at the place of residence of the interested party or at the place of enforcement of the arbitral award. If the interested party does not have a place of residence or registered office in Latvia and the place of enforcement of the arbitral award is not determinable, the application for setting aside the arbitral award may be filed with the district (city) court at the seat of the arbitration or the declared place of residence or registered office of the applicant. The application for the setting aside of the arbitral award must be accompanied by: (1) the arbitral award; (2) the arbitration agreement, if it is in the possession of the applicant; and (3) documents confirming the payment of the state fee. The applicant may appeal only a negative decision of the court, i.e., a refusal to a request to either set aside or to issue an enforcement order. An ancillary complaint may be submitted within ten days from the adoption of the decision of the district (city) court and it must be addressed to the regional court, i.e., a court of appeal. An ancillary complaint is subject to security deposit payment (Art. 34(5) and Annex III of the CPL). The ruling of the regional court on an ancillary complaint is not subject to appeal to the Supreme Court. The state fee depends on the amount of the awarded sum (see table below), but in any case, it cannot exceed EUR 300.00 (Annex III CPL). The amount of state fee depends on the amount of awarded sum: Amount of awarded sum (euro) State fee (euro) 1–700 5 701–1800 15 1801–3300 25 3301–5200 45 5201–7500 65 7501–10 200 90 10 201–13 300 115 13 301–16 800 150 16 801–20 700 185 20 701–25 000 225 above 25 000 300 This schedule applies to both setting aside proceedings and also to the enforcement process. c. Waivers The parties cannot waive the possibility of bringing an action for setting aside an arbitral award. The setting-aside mechanism is a state-mandated form of judicial control over the arbitral process and constitutes a mandatory safeguard that operates irrespective of party autonomy. As such, the right to apply for setting aside an arbitral award cannot be excluded, limited, or waived by agreement of the parties, whether in the arbitration clause or during the proceedings. d. Effect of an award that has been set aside The dispute that was decided by the award that was set aside, may be resolved by a court of a general jurisdiction, if the award was set aside for the following reasons: (1) there is no valid arbitration agreement; or (2) the arbitral award has been made on a dispute that is not provided for in the arbitration agreement or does not comply with it, or exceeds the limits of the claim or the jurisdiction of the arbitration court; or (3) the dispute is non-arbitrable. However, the dispute may be submitted to arbitration de novo in the following circumstances: (1) a party to the arbitration agreement was not duly informed of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the arbitral proceedings, or was otherwise deprived of the opportunity to present its case; (2) the arbitral tribunal was not constituted, or the arbitral proceedings were not conducted, in accordance with the parties’ agreement, unless such agreement contradicts mandatory provisions of law from which the parties are not permitted to derogate; (3) the arbitral award is contrary to public order; (4) the enforcement of the arbitral award has been refused on the ground that it has not yet become binding on the parties, or because the award has been set aside in the state in which it was made. 3. Other Means of Recourse The Public Prosecutor has the right to protest against the recognition and enforcement of arbitral awards rendered by the arbitral tribunal. More specifically, in accordance with Arts. 483 and 484 of the CPL, if serious material or procedural errors are found in a case that has been reviewed only by a court of first instance and has not been appealed under the procedure provided under the CPL for reasons independent from the parties, or if the rights of state or municipality institutions that have not been parties to the case are breached, the Public Prosecutor may file a protest to the Supreme Court.35. Art. 484 provides: This means of recourse is possible only if not more than ten years have passed since the court judgment was issued (Art. 483).36. Art. 483 of the CPL, entitled “Submitting a Protest”, provides:

sections.title: CHAPTER VIII: CONCILIATION / MEDIATION
sections.text: CHAPTER VIII: CONCILIATION / MEDIATION 1. General a. ADR in Latvia There was a broad publicity campaign in operation in Latvia encouraging the use of mediation; however, at the time of writing, there is no major recourse to mediation as one of the means of dispute settlement in commercial matters. Mediation is mostly used in family and criminal disputes. In addition, the Law on Labour Disputes (in force as of 1 January 2003) provides for the possibility of settling collective labour disputes by means of conciliation or mediation. The decision of a “reconciliation committee” established for the resolution of the collective labour dispute, is binding on all the parties involved. A settlement achieved by means of mediation, which takes a form of a written agreement, has the same force as a collective agreement. b. Institutions The Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry adopted the Rules of Mediation in 2006.37. For more information, contact <www.chamber.lv>. Generally, however, parties are very reluctant to base their attempt at mediation on a set of mediation or conciliation rules. More information about mediation in Latvia can be found on <www.mediacija.lv> and <www.sertificetimediatori.lv>. 2. Legal Provisions The Latvian Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) provides that the judge shall refuse a statement of claim if the claimant has not complied with agreed procedures in regard to preliminary extra-judicial attempts at settlement of the dispute or has not taken the measures prescribed by law to resolve the dispute with the respondent prior to action being brought before the court (Art. 132(7) CPL). If there is no agreement between the parties to settle the dispute through alternative dispute resolution (“ADR”), the claimant must indicate in the statement of claim what its opinion towards use of ADR is. If the claimant has failed to do so, the court is under an obligation to refuse to accept a statement of claim.

sections.title: CHAPTER IX: INVESTMENT TREATY ARBITRATION
sections.text: CHAPTER IX: INVESTMENT TREATY ARBITRATION 1. conventions and treaties Latvia became party to the ICSID Convention in 1997, which has been in force since 7 July 1997. Latvia has also been a signatory to the Energy Charter Treaty since 1996,38. See <http://www.energycharter.org/fileadmin/DocumentsMedia/Legal/ECT_Guide_en.pdf>. including the Charter’s provision on investment. In addition, Latvia is party to a number of bilateral investment treaties (“BITs”). Latvia does not employ a model BIT. Generally, BITs concluded between Latvian and other States contain dispute resolution provisions providing for submission of disputes to ad hoc arbitration (often under the UNCITRAL Rules or to ICSID, the Stockholm Chamber of Commerce (SCC Institute) or the International Chamber of Commerce Court of Arbitration (ICC). 2. investment arbitration Latvia ensures the protection of its interests in international investment disputes through centralized state representation. Under Cabinet Regulation No. 358 of 4 July 2023, the State Chancellery39. <https://www.mk.gov.lv/en/representation-of-state>. is entrusted with representing Latvia in investment arbitration proceedings, coordinating the government’s legal position and acting as the primary institution responsible for communication with arbitral bodies and the parties involved. The State Chancellery publishes information on any international arbitration proceedings initiated against Latvia once the arbitral tribunal has been formally constituted, unless confidentiality obligations arising from treaties, arbitration rules, or party agreements require otherwise.40. <https://www.mk.gov.lv/en/media/1218/download?attachment>. This framework strengthens both accountability and procedural clarity in Latvia’s participation in international investment dispute resolution. At the time of writing, Latvia has three active investment arbitration proceedings, one of them being in the award enforcement stage,41. Eugene Kazmin v. Latvia(ICSID Case No. ARB/17/5) (The arbitral proceeding was concluded in favour of Latvia. Enforcement of the arbitral tribunal’s award is currently pending.). another terminated without review on merits,42. R.S.E. Holdings v. Republic of Latvia(PCA Case No. 202241) (The arbitral proceedings were concluded in favour of Latvia by an Award of 28 May 2025, meaning that the arbitration has been terminated and the case will not be examined on its merits. Currently, proceedings are ongoing to recover the expenses incurred by the State in the amount of EUR 678,545.20, as well as the interest.). and the third one still pending.43. AS PNB Banka and others v. Republic of Latvia(ICSID Case No. ARB/17/47) (On 19 May 2025, the claimants submitted a proposal to disqualify arbitrator Peter Tomka, and the proceedings were suspended. On 10 June 2025, the motion to disqualify arbitrator Peter Tomka was rejected and the decision is being prepared.). 3. national investment legislation There is no national investment legislation in Latvia.

plain_text:
CHAPTER I: INTRODUCTION 1. Law on Arbitration a. Legislation The regulation of arbitration procedures in Latvia is encompassed in the Latvian Arbitration Law, which was adopted on 11 September 2014 and came into force on 1 January 2015 and was last amended by the Law of 6 June 2024 (“the Arbitration Law”, see Annex I hereto). The Arbitration Law is devoted exclusively to the regulation of arbitration proceedings. Except for the regulation of the enforcement of arbitral awards, the Latvian regulatory framework does not distinguish between domestic and international arbitration, as long as the seat of arbitration is in Latvia. Consequently, the Arbitration Law applies to both domestic and international arbitration. The Latvian Constitutional Court has been an important driving force for the improvement of the arbitration regulation, as it on several occasions has assessed different shortcomings of the existing regulation: first, in 2014, it held that the absence of any control mechanism over the validity of the arbitration agreement by the courts was unconstitutional.1. Judgment of the Satversme (Constitutional) Court of 28 November 2014 in case no. 2014-09-01 “On Compliance of Section 495(1) of the Civil Procedure Law with the first sentence in Article 92 of the Satversme of the Republic of Latvia”; more information is available at: <https://www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/en/press-release/the-norm-regarding-the-competence-of-‌arbitration-court-to-determine-jurisdiction-regarding-a-dispute-is-incompatible-with-the-satversme/>; judgment available at: <https://www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/web/viewer.html?file=/wp-content/‌uploads/2014/03/2014-09-01_Spriedums_ENG.pdf#search=>. In 2023, the Constitutional Court found that overall state control over the arbitration process was insufficient, which led to the introduction of a mechanism for setting aside arbitral awards and a significant expansion of judicial support for arbitration. Until the adoption of the Arbitration Law in 2014, the regulations of arbitration procedures formed part of the Latvian Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) Part D. Now, Part D of the CPL contains only provisions relating to the court support to arbitral processes and annulment and enforcement of domestic arbitral awards (see Annex II hereto). The CPL also contains a separate section on the “Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards” (Part F, Chap. 78, Annex III hereto). The Arbitration Law and the CPL encompass all essential aspects of the UNCITRAL Model Law on Commercial Arbitration (referred to as “the Model Law”); however, they do not repeat the text of the Model Law and exhibit numerous deviations. For example, they introduce excessive regulations concerning the establishment and operation of permanent arbitration courts and cover many procedural matters thus making the regulation close to the regulation of the court civil procedure. Latvia is a signatory to a number of international conventions and treaties in the field of arbitration, such as the 1958 Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (the “New York Convention”) and the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration (see Chapter VI.1.a). Therefore, the statutory regulation of arbitration in Latvia is largely in line with international standards. b. Mandatory provisions While most provisions of the Arbitration Law may be modified by party agreement, both the Arbitration Law and the CPL nevertheless contain a number of mandatory norms designed to safeguard due process, preserve the authority of arbitral tribunals, and ensure effective judicial assistance and oversight where necessary. One of these mandatory rules is Art. 5 of the Arbitration Law, which defines the scope of arbitrable matters and establishes the boundary between disputes that may be resolved by arbitration and those reserved for state courts. Following the 2024 amendments, the role of the courts has been significantly strengthened to guarantee minimum procedural safeguards at various stages of the arbitration. These provisions protect fundamental rights of the parties – for example, judicial review of jurisdictional rulings under Art. 532.5 of the CPL; court assistance in the appointment, challenge, or removal of arbitrators under Arts. 532.2-532.4 CPL; and the possibility to request interim measures under Art. 137(7) CPL or to obtain evidence through the courts under Art. 532.6 CPL. Equally fundamental are the mandatory procedural fairness guarantees. Article 19 of the Arbitration Law, which requires equal treatment of the parties and ensures each party’s right to be heard, reflects a universal standard of due process. Article 40(4) of the Arbitration Law reinforces this principle by requiring the arbitral tribunal to hold an oral hearing whenever a party so requests, even if the parties have previously agreed to proceed solely on the basis of written submissions. Finally, the mechanism for challenging and enforcing arbitral awards – set out in Arts. 533-537 CPL – contains several essential mandatory elements. The grounds for setting aside or refusing enforcement of an award are exhaustive and cannot be expanded, limited, or waived by party agreement. 2. Practice on Arbitration a. General Arbitration is a popular means of dispute resolution in Latvia. According to the data assembled by the Court Administration of Latvia,2. The Court Administration of the Republic of Latvia organizes and provides the organizational management of district (city) courts, regional courts and Land Registry Offices. For statistics see <https://dati.ta.gov.lv/MicroStrategy/asp/Main.aspx?src=Main.aspx.2048001&evt=2048001‌&share=1&documentID=BF8E206E48039A9F66E436BB511763C6&currentViewMedia=1&‌visMode=0&Server=TA-MSTR2-NEW&Port=0&Project=TA+Dati+New>. on average there are approximately 500 requests to issue a writ of execution of arbitral awards per year. Although this number has considerably decreased over the years (from 7,648 requests in 2005 to 1,092 requests in 2014), it is still noteworthy. The decrease compared to 2005 to a large extent was related to finding arbitration agreements encompassed in the consumer contracts invalid as unfair consumer clause, if it has not been individually negotiated with the consumer.3. For the pioneering judgment by the Supreme Court, see case no. SKA-59/2006, available (in Latvian) at <http://at.gov.lv/files/uploads/files/archive/department3/2006/ska59-2006.pdf>. Decisions by the Consumer Rights Protection Center are available at <www.ptac.gov.lv/?lang=LV&menu=113>. Further reduction has been facilitated due to rather fast and accessible court proceedings. It should also be underlined that the above data does not distinguish between purely domestic and international awards. However, the survey conducted by the Ministry of the Justice revealed that in 2021 at least thirty-two cases were with international elements. Most disputes giving rise to international arbitration are between Latvian companies and their trading partners from neighbouring countries – Baltic and Scandinavian States, as well as Ukraine, and EU Member States (mostly Germany and the Netherlands) and the United Kingdom. It should also be noted that the majority of arbitral proceedings involve relatively small sums – 56% of all cases have a claim value of up to EUR 7,000, 26% of cases range from EUR 7,000 to EUR 28,000, 13% from EUR 28,001 to EUR 145,000, 5% from EUR 145,000 to EUR 700,000, and only 1% of cases have a value above EUR 700,000. The primary characteristic of Latvian arbitration is the quantity of permanent (registered) arbitration courts. Article 2 of the Arbitration Law stipulates that “a permanent arbitration court may be established by an association registered in the Register of Enterprises (the founder of the permanent arbitration court), with the objective being the operation of the permanent arbitration court”. There have been numerous efforts to reduce the number of these arbitration courts, for instance, by introducing additional requirements regarding premises and visiting hours and by demanding the submission of extra documents to the Register of Enterprises. According to the data from the Latvian Register of Enterprises,4. Every arbitration institution must register with the Latvian Register of Enterprises before it is authorized to offer its services. Since 1 April 2005, the Latvian Register of Enterprises has overseen the registration of arbitration institutions (see Amendments to the Law “On Register of Enterprises of the Republic of Latvia”, 40 Latvijas Vestnesis(9 March 2005); Regulations of the Cabinet of Ministers, no. 204 and “Regulations on the Register of Arbitration Institutions”, published in 52 Latvijas Vestnesis(1 April 2005); and Regulations of the Cabinet of Ministers, no. 205 “On state fees for records in the Register of Arbitration Institutions”, published in 52 Latvijas Vestnesis(1 April 2005). in 2009 there were 214 arbitration courts registered; however, by 2016 this number had decreased to sixty-nine and has since remained roughly at this level. At the time of writing (in mid-2025) there are fifty-seven arbitration courts. In recent years, there have been widespread allegations that at least some of these arbitration courts act as “pocket courts” and cater to specific interests, particularly given that until 2024, they were legally obliged to maintain closed lists of arbitrators, and judicial oversight of the arbitration process was extremely limited. b. Arbitral institutions Taking into account the numerous permanent arbitration courts, we shall refer to two of the largest arbitration courts in Latvia – the Court of Arbitration of the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry (“LCCI Court of Arbitration”) and the Baltic International Arbitration Court. LCCI Court of Arbitration The LCCI Court of Arbitration is the oldest, with its origins dating back to 1934, during Latvia’s first period of independence. It was re-established in 1991, subsequent to Latvia proclaiming its independence from the Soviet Union. The LCCI Court of Arbitration administers arbitration proceedings in accordance with its own Arbitration Rules (updated in 2021). According to Art. 17 of the Arbitration Rules of the LCCI Court of Arbitration, if the parties have consented to arbitrate any existing or future dispute at the LCCI Court of Arbitration, without specifying the applicable rules, it is presumed that they have agreed on the application of the Court’s current Arbitration Rules. It should also be noted that the President of the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry assumes the role of appointing authority in ad hoc arbitrations, under Art. 3 of the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration. This Article stipulates that when the parties have agreed to submit any dispute to ad hoc arbitration, and where, within thirty days of the notification of the request for arbitration to the respondent, one of the parties fails to appoint its arbitrator, the appointment shall be, at the request of the other party, be made by the President of the competent Chamber of Commerce of the country where the defaulting party has its habitual residence or seat at the time the request for arbitration was introduced. Furthermore, the LCCI Court of Arbitration is also the most active arbitration court in Latvia in terms of organizing arbitration seminars and conferences, although these activities are not carried out on a regular basis. Further information on the LCCI Court of Arbitration, its Arbitration Rules and other information can be obtained at: Court of Arbitration of the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry Kr. Valdemara Street 35 Riga, LV-1010 Latvia Telephone: + 371 67225595; +371 26627788 Facsimile: + 371 67820092 Email: info@chamber.lv Website: <www.chamber.lv> Baltic International Arbitration Court The Baltic International Arbitration Court is another widely recognized arbitration court, featuring both Latvian and international arbitrators on its list of arbitrators. Arbitral proceedings are conducted in accordance with the parties’ arbitration agreement and the arbitration court’s Arbitration Rules.5. The Arbitration Rules of the Baltic International Arbitration Court are available in Latvian at: <https://arbitration.lv/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/2025-REGLAMENTS_Baltijas-Starptautiska-‌skirejtiesa.pdf>. Further information about the Baltic International Arbitration Court and its Arbitration Rules is available at: Baltic International Arbitration Court Strelnieku iela 9-7 Riga, LV-1010 Latvia Telephone: +371 67240347 Facsimile: +371 67292607 Email: mail@arbitration.lv Website: <www.arbitration.lv> 3. Bibliography a. Books Kacevska, I. and Fillers, A. “There is Ordinary Situation and There Is Latvia’s Situation”, Chapter 18 in: Danielsson, Christer and Schöldström, Patrik (eds.), Stockholm Arbitration Yearbook 2023 (Wolters Kluwer 2024), ISBN: 9789403503585 Krumins, T. Arbitration and Human Rights: Approaches to Excluding the Annulment of Arbitral Awards and Their Compatibility with the ECHR (Springer 2020), 352 pages, ISBN: 978-3-030-54236-8 Torgans, K. Civilprocesa likuma komentāri. III daļa (61.-86.nodaļa) [Commentary on the Law on Civil Procedure, Part III (Chapter 61-86)], (Tiesu Nama Aģentūra, Riga, 2014) 1026 pages (in Latvian), ISBN: 978-9934-508-05-9 b. Journals and periodicals The official State newspaper, Latvijas Vestnesis, has a weekly addendum Jurista Vārds [Lawyer’s Word] which occasionally also publishes articles on arbitration. Besides Jurista Vārds, there are no specialized journals on arbitration in Latvia. d. Publication of awards Similarly, there are no periodicals in which arbitral awards are published. As none of the arbitration courts publish their awards, information about awards can only be obtained through court judgments in proceedings for setting aside or enforcement of a particular award.

CHAPTER II: ARBITRATION AGREEMENT 1. Form and Contents of the Agreement a. Arbitration clause and submission agreement In accordance with Art. 12 of the Arbitration Law (see Annex I hereto), the arbitration agreement can either be concluded in the form of a separate agreement (submission agreement) or can be included as a clause in any agreement (arbitration clause). According to Art. 10(2) of the Arbitration Law the parties may agree to submit to arbitration both already existing disputes as well as disputes that may arise in the future. b. Form requirements Under Art. 12(1) of the Arbitration Law, an arbitration agreement must be made in writing. The signing of the arbitration agreement may occur through either a traditional wet ink signature on a physical document or by employing an electronic signature. According to Latvian court practice,6. Riga District Court, matter no. 3-12/12946-24/14, referring to the judgment of the Senate of 6 December 2006 in matter no. SKC-584/2006, and decisions of 27 April 2011 in matter no. SKC-86/2011 and of 30 March 2017 in matter no. SKC-43/2017 (in Latvian). should an individual sign a contract in multiple capacities representing different parties, a single electronic signature from this individual suffices to bind all the parties they represent, including making the arbitration agreement contained within such a contract binding on all the represented parties. The Arbitration Law further specifies, in Art. 12(2), when the requirement “in writing” is met, i.e., if the arbitration agreement has been entered into between the parties by: “(1) exchange of communication via post; (2) exchange of communication via electronic means; (3) a statement of claim and explanations about the claim, where the arbitration agreement is alleged by one party and not denied by the other.” The reference to the exchange of communication via post (point (1)) and via electronic methods (point (2)) has been introduced to reflect the prevalent business practice in which contracts are frequently concluded through various communication forms without the formal drafting of a contract, for instance, through the exchange of offers and acceptances between parties. The arbitration clause will also be considered validly entered into if the exchange of communication includes a reference to general terms and conditions containing an arbitration clause. Regarding emails, they are generally accepted as written evidence. In practice, if a party can prove the authenticity of the relevant email correspondence (which, if challenged by the opposing party, may require a statement from internet providers on the authenticity of the specific email correspondence, or other means available to the party), an arbitral tribunal or the court will recognize such an arbitration agreement and consider the respective requirement to be met. Since the 2024 amendments, an arbitration agreement is also considered to be entered into in writing if it is contained in an exchange of statements of claim and defence in which the existence of an agreement is alleged by one party and not denied by the other (Art. 12(2) point (3)). The Arbitration Law provides a list of elements that parties may include in the arbitration agreement; however, these elements are presented merely as illustrative examples of what might be incorporated into the arbitration clause. For instance, it is stipulated that parties have the option to choose between institutional or ad hoc arbitration, designate the place of arbitration, the language, the number of arbitrators, and the procedure for covering the costs of arbitration (Art. 12(3)). Furthermore, Art. 29 clarifies that parties are permitted to decide on the number of arbitrators, provided that it must be an odd number. These are not the exhaustive elements upon which parties may reach an agreement, as, unless expressly prohibited by the Arbitration Law, they possess broad discretion to also agree on additional elements. For instance, parties may agree on the law governing the arbitration agreement, select the appointing authority, establish deadlines for the issuance of the award7. This conclusion derives from arbitral practice. For a commentary, see K. Torgans, Commentary on the Law on Civil Procedure(Tiesu Nama Aģentūra, Riga 1999 (1st ed.), 2001 (2nd ed.), 2006 (3rd ed.)., and stipulate other dispute resolution mechanisms that must be pursued prior to resorting to arbitration, such as mediation. Pursuant to Art. 12 of the Arbitration Law, “in writing” requirement applies also to modification or termination of the arbitration agreement. c. Model arbitration clause The LCCI Court of Arbitration has two model clauses: “1. Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this contract, or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof, shall be settled in the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry Court of Arbitration in Riga in accordance with its Rules of Arbitration. 2. The number of arbitrators shall be______ (one or three). 3. The language of the arbitration shall be______.” Or: “Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this contract, or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof shall be settled in the Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry Court of Arbitration in Riga in accordance with its Rules of Arbitration.” In Latvian: “1. Jebkurš strīds, nesaskaņa vai prasība, kas izriet no šī līguma vai kas skar to vai tā pārkāpšanu, izbeigšanu vai spēkā neesamību, tiks izšķirts Latvijas Tirdzniecības un rūpniecības kameras Šķīrējtiesā Rīgā, saskaņā ar tās reglamentu. 2. Šķīrējtiesnešu skaits būs ____ (viens vai trīs). 3. Šķīrējtiesas izskatīšanas valoda būs ______.” Or: “Jebkurš strīds, nesaskaņa vai prasība, kas izriet no šī līguma vai kas skar to vai tā pārkāpšanu, izbeigšanu vai spēkā neesamību, tiks izšķirts Latvijas Tirdzniecības un rūpniecības kameras Šķīrējtiesā Rīgā, saskaņā ar tās reglamentu.” The Riga International Court of Arbitration recommends the following model arbitration clause: “All disputes, differences or claims arising out of or in connection with the present agreement, which concerns it or an infringement of it, termination or invalidity, shall be finally settled at the Riga International Arbitration Court in accordance with its Arbitration Rules. The parties may supplement the Arbitration Clause, adding to it: 1) the number of arbitrators is ________ (one or three); 2) the language of arbitration is ________; 3) place of arbitration is ________________.” In Latvian: “Jebkurš strīds, domstarpība vai prasība, kas izriet no šī līguma, kas skar to vai tā pārkāpšanu, izbeigšanu vai spēkā neesamību tiks galīgi izšķirts Rīgas Starptautiskajā šķīrējtiesā saskaņā ar šīs šķīrējtiesas reglamentu.Puses var papildināt šķīrējtiesas klauzulu, pievienojot tai: 1) šķīrējtiesnešu skaits _________ (viens vai trīs); 2) šķīrējtiesas valoda ___________; 3) šķīrējtiesas procesa vieta _____________.” The recommended arbitration clause of the Baltic International Court of Arbitration is the following: In English: All disputes, differences or claims arising out of or in connection with the present agreement, which concerns it or an infringement of it, termination or invalidity, shall be finally settled at the Baltic International Arbitration Court (Strelnieku Str. 9-7, Riga, LV-1010) in accordance with its Arbitration Rules.” The parties may supplement arbitration clause by adding following: 1. The number of arbitrators is …………………………………….; 2. The language of arbitration is ………………………………………..; 3. Place of arbitration is ……………………………………………… 4. ……………………………………………………………….. laws. In Latvian: “Jebkurš strīds, domstarpība vai prasība, kas izriet no šī līguma, kas skar to vai tā pārkāpšanu, izbeigšanu vai spēkā neesamību tiks galīgi izšķirts Baltijas Starptautiskā Šķīrējtiesā (Strēlnieku iela 9-7, Rīga, LV-1010) saskaņā ar minētās šķīrējtiesas reglamentu.” Puses var papildināt šķīrējtiesas klauzulu, pievienojot tai: 1. šķīrējtiesnešu skaits …………………………………………….; 2. šķīrējtiesas valoda ……………………………………………… 3. šķīrējtiesas procesa vieta …………………………………….; 4. …………………………………………………………….. likumiem.” 2. Parties to the Agreement a. Generally/capacity Article 11 of the Arbitration Law states that an arbitration agreement can be entered into by any natural person who has the legal capacity to do so, a legal entity governed by private law, or a legal entity governed by public law in the realm of private law. b. Bankruptcy Article 5(1)(8) of the Arbitration Law provides that “disputes concerning rights and obligations of persons who have entered into insolvency procedures” are not arbitrable. This restriction refers to objective arbitrability rather than subjective arbitrability, thus the arbitration is possible also in case of bankruptcy of one of the parties as long as the foreign law is applicable on the substance of the dispute. c. State or state agencies Article 5(1)(2) of the Arbitration Law provides that disputes “in which at least one of the parties is the State or a local government authority, or the arbitral award may infringe the rights of the State or a local government authority” are not arbitrable. This restriction, however, applies only to domestic arbitration proceedings. With respect to international arbitration, Latvia maintained – until 23 December 2013 – a reservation under Art. 2(2) of the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration of 21 April 1961, declaring that the rights to enter into arbitration agreements did not extend to state or municipal institutions.8. Declaration Latvia made under Art. II (2) made upon accession to the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration: In 2013, Latvia withdrew this reservation, thereby allowing state and municipal institutions to enter into arbitration agreements in international arbitration proceedings. d. Multiparty arbitration The Arbitration Law does not include specific provisions regarding multiparty arbitration, the addition of a third party, or consolidation. However, if the parties have entered into an arbitration agreement that allows for such possibilities, or if they have selected arbitration rules that include provisions for these scenarios, then multiparty arbitration or the addition of a third party would be feasible. Similarly, consolidation could also be possible in principle, provided there is a written agreement among all the involved parties or if the arbitration rules allow for it. Latvian courts have been rather reluctant to bind a non-signatory to an arbitration agreement. Such solutions have been allowed in case law only in situations where the party that did not sign the arbitration agreement has replaced the signatory party by inheritance, reorganization or other similar mechanism of succeeding rights and obligations (not by way of assignment). 3. Domain of Arbitration a. Arbitrability In accordance with Art. 5(1) of the Arbitration Law, the following matters are not arbitrable. This is in the case of a dispute: “1) the award of which may infringe the rights or interests protected by law of a person who is not a party to the arbitration agreement; 2) in which at least one party is the State or a local government institution or if the arbitral award may affect the rights of the State or a local government institution; 3) relating to entries in the Civil Records Registry; 4) relating to the rights and duties of persons under guardianship or trusteeship or to their interests protected by law; 5) regarding the establishment, alteration or termination of property rights with regard to immovable property, if among the parties to the dispute there is a person whose rights to acquire ownership, possession or use of immovable property are restricted by law; 6) regarding the eviction of a person from dwelling premises; 7) between an employee and an employer if the dispute has arisen by entering into, amending, terminating or implementing an employment contract, as well as in applying or translating provisions of laws and regulations, collective labour contract or working procedures (individual labour rights dispute); 8) regarding the rights and duties of such persons in respect of whom insolvency proceedings have been initiated.” Besides above-mentioned matters, disputes that are adjudicated in special adjudication proceedings provided for in the Latvian Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) are also not arbitrable.9. According to Art. 251 of the CPL, special adjudication proceedings are provided for matters: In decision SPC-46/2013 of the Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia of 29 October 2013,10. Case no. SPC-46/2013, available (in Latvian) at <http://at.gov.lv/files/uploads/files/archive/‌department1/2013/46-spc-2013.doc>. the Supreme Court has ruled that restrictions posed on the “State [...] institution” within meaning of Art. 5 (1)(2) of the Arbitration Law are not applicable to private companies established and owned by the State, since they do not meet the criteria of state institutions set forth in Art. 1(2) of the State Administration Structure Law. It has been confirmed also in more recent court practice.11. Case no. SKA-24/2022, available (in Latvian) at <https://www.at.gov.lv/download‌lawfile/8454>. It should also be noted that while the Consumer Rights Protection Law of 199912. In force as of 15 April 1999, published in Latvijas Vestnesis, 1 April 1999, no. 104/105, available at <www.ptac.gov.lv>. does not limit the parties’ rights to enter into an arbitration agreement, such an agreement would be invalid as unfair contractual provision, unless it has been individually negotiated with the consumer. Ever since judgment SKA-59/2026 of the Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia of 6 March 2006,13. See case no. SKA-59, available (in Latvian) at <http://at.gov.lv/files/uploads/files/archive/‌department3/2006/ska59-2006.pdf>. Decisions by the Consumer Rights Protection Center are available at <www.ptac.gov.lv/? lang=LV&men=113>. national courts have consistently found all arbitration clauses in consumer contracts to be invalid as they had not been negotiated individually with the consumer. So far, only once has the service provider been successful in proving that the arbitration agreement had been individually negotiated with the consumer.14. A decision by the Consumer Rights Protection Center, not publicly available, is on file with the authors. In that case, the arbitration clause was proposed by the consumer himself. b. Filling gaps Latvian law does not expressly address the power of arbitrators to fill contractual gaps. At the same time, the general principles of contract interpretation do not extend so far as to permit arbitrators to create new contractual provisions that do not derive from the parties’ existing rights and obligations. Introducing such new terms through gap-filling would only be permissible where the parties have explicitly authorized the arbitrators to do so. c. Adapting contracts Latvian law does not have a clear provision explicitly empowering arbitrators to adapt a contract on their own initiative in response to fundamentally changed circumstances (such as a major price shift in a long-term contract). Adopting a contract to fundamentally changed circumstances would be permissible where the parties have explicitly authorized the arbitrators to do so. 4. Separability of Arbitration Clause An arbitration clause is considered to be an independent and separate agreement from the main contract in which the arbitration clause is included. Therefore, the validity of the main contract does not have any bearing on the validity of the arbitration agreement between the parties. This principle is included in Art. 13(3) of the Arbitration Law, which further specifies that an arbitration agreement shall be in effect as long as the legal relations in connection to which it has been entered into have not been terminated. The principle of separability, however, does not apply in cases of assignment, which under Latvian Civil Law is defined narrowly as assignment (cession) of the right to claim, and Art. 13(4) of the Arbitration Law provides that in case of an assignment, the assignee shall be assigned of the rights to claim and not of the arbitration clause. 5. Effect of the Agreement a. Duty of court Pursuant to Art. 132(1)(3) of the Latvian CPL, the judge refuses to accept the statement of claim “if the parties have agreed, in accordance with procedures laid down in law, to submit the dispute for examination in an arbitration court, except for the case when a statement of claim regarding recognition of the agreement of the arbitration court as invalid is submitted to the court”. Similar duty of the court is provided in Art. 223(6) of the CPL providing that a court shall terminate court proceedings, “if the parties have agreed, in accordance with procedures laid down in law, to submit the dispute for examination in an arbitration court”. Both of these provisions are applied by Latvian courts on their own motion, even if neither party has pointed to the arbitration agreement and requested to terminate court proceedings.15. CPL Arts. 132(1)(3) and 223(6). The courts have reasoned that such approach is justified by Art. 12 of the Arbitration Law providing that an arbitration agreement can be amended or rescinded by the parties only in writing and thus it cannot be waived. Although there is at least one recent court decision16. Riga Regional Court decision of 21 May 2025 in case no. 3-10/0455 (Archive no. CA-1142-25/27) (not published) (in Latvian). suggesting that such approach should be taken also if the arbitration is international, it has not been overruled by the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia17. See decision no. SKC-579/2012 of 17 October 2012, available (in Latvian) at <at.gov.lv/files/uploads/files/579-skc-2012.doc>. has confirmed that in cases where the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration is applicable, parties may waive the arbitration agreement by referring to the court of general jurisdiction, if the respondent fails to raise objections as to the jurisdiction of the court not later than by filing its response to the claim. In one particular case the respondent had filed a counterclaim in the court and only in a cassation action did it bring the court’s attention to the arbitration clause. The Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia explained that parties by their actions (conclusive actions) had expressed their joint intent to submit their dispute to the court of general jurisdiction. Finally, it should be noted that as a general rule, in a case where the arbitration agreement provides for arbitration abroad and one of the parties invokes the agreement, while the claimant has reverted to a national court, a Latvian court, following provisions of the New York Convention, will refer the parties to arbitration, as long as it is prima facie satisfied that the arbitration agreement provides to this effect. b. Court examination of arbitration agreement Generally speaking, Latvian courts will only conduct a prima facie review where one of the parties invokes the arbitration agreement or the court discovers the existence of the arbitration agreement by itself. Unless the court finds it obvious that an arbitration agreement is invalid, it will refer parties to arbitration. As far as the court’s powers regarding the stay of arbitral proceedings is concerned, it is only where a party directly attacks the validity of an arbitration agreement in its statement of claim submitted to the court that Latvian courts would conduct a full review of the arbitration agreement and, based on its findings, decide whether to stay proceedings or continue with the merits of the case. Such powers of the court were reconfirmed by the judgment of the Satversme Court (Latvian Constitutional Court) of 28 November 2014,18. Satversmes case no 2014-09-01On Compliance of Section 495(1) of Civil Procedure Law with the First Sentence in Article 92 of the Satversme of the Republic of Latvia, available at: <https://www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/web/viewer.html?file=/wp-content/uploads/2014/03/2014-09-01_Spriedums_ENG.pdf#search=>. interpreting the competence-competence principle and reminding the courts of their rights to decide on the validity of an arbitration agreement upon a party’s request. The validity of the arbitration agreement must be challenged expressly and unambiguously, and it can be done by bringing a separate claim in the court of general jurisdiction. Such claim is subject to a state fee of EUR 300. The CPL provides no time limits with respect to the party’s right to challenge the court’s jurisdiction on the grounds of an existing arbitration agreement. In practice, however, a plea that the court does not have jurisdiction must be raised not later than the submission of the statement of defence. c. Kompetenz-kompetenz The principle of kompetenz-kompetenz is enshrined in Art. 24 of the Arbitration Law, which provides that an arbitral tribunal has the authority to rule on its own jurisdiction, including on the existence, validity, and scope of the arbitration agreement. The tribunal may determine its jurisdiction at any stage of the proceedings – either as a preliminary issue or together with the merits of the dispute. Where the tribunal issues a preliminary decision on jurisdiction, the party contesting that decision may apply to the district (city) court for judicial review in accordance with Art. 24(3) of the Arbitration Law. Importantly, the submission of such a request to the district (city) court does not suspend or impede the continuation of the arbitration proceedings, allowing the tribunal to proceed with the case unless the court decides otherwise.

CHAPTER III ARBITRATORS 1. Qualifications a. Requirements In accordance with Art. 14(1) of the Arbitration Law (see Annex I hereto), an arbitrator is “a person who complies with the provisions of this Law and is appointed to resolve a civil dispute pursuant to an arbitration agreement and provisions of this Law.” Article 14(2) goes on to provide that any person above the age of eighteen who has accepted in writing may be appointed as an arbitrator, provided that the person “(1) is not under legal guardianship; (2) has a flawless reputation; (3) has higher professional or academic education (with the exception of first level professional education)”. The parties to the arbitration agreement may agree on additional qualifications for the arbitrators. Similarly, the applicable arbitration rules may also foresee certain qualifications that the arbitrators must meet, although in practice, arbitration rules do not provide for such additional requirements. Until 2024, the Arbitration Law set more restrictive requirements for arbitrators, e.g., that only persons with higher legal education and at least three years of legal experience could be appointed as arbitrators. However, in reviewing the legal framework, the legislator noted that the requirements were unduly restrictive of the parties’ right to freely choose an arbitrator who is not a lawyer, but has another profession. It should also be noted that until 2024, the Arbitration Law required all arbitration courts to maintain a list of arbitrators. The parties were only permitted to appoint arbitrators from this list. The arbitration courts had a duty to compose a list of at least ten arbitrators and publish such list not only on the website of[ the arbitration court, but also to report it to the Register of Arbitration Courts. Since June 2024, arbitration courts are still required to have a list of arbitrators; however, these lists are not closed and the arbitration courts may allow the parties to appoint also arbitrators who are not on the list. For example, Art. 9(3) and (4) of the Arbitration Rules of the Baltic International Arbitration Court provides that any party may appoint as an arbitrator a person who is not on the list of arbitrators provided that the tribunal consists of at least three arbitrators and that the Council of the Baltic International Arbitration Court has approved such candidate. The presiding arbitrator still has to be from the list and the non-listed candidate must present the documents to the Council showing that candidate satisfies the requirements of arbitrators set in the Arbitration Law. b. Restrictions Importantly, Art. 14(4) provides that an arbitrator of an arbitration court “shall not be included in more than three lists of permanent arbitration courts”, while Art. 14(5) requires arbitrators to be “impartial, objective and fair” in deciding the dispute before them. Article 15 sets out a list of persons that may not be appointed as arbitrators. This includes persons who have been recognized as a suspect or have been accused or have been punished in criminal proceedings for committing an intentional criminal offence, or against whom criminal proceedings regarding the commission of an intentional criminal offence have been terminated for reasons other than exoneration, or who have been declared insolvent during the last five years. The Law on Prevention of Conflicts of Interests in Activities of Public Officials forbids judges, notaries, prosecutors and civil servants to serve as arbitrators. c. Disclosure Before accepting an appointment, a prospective arbitrator must disclose in writing any facts or circumstances that may give rise to reasonable doubts regarding their impartiality or independence. If such circumstances arise or become known after the arbitration has commenced but before its conclusion, the arbitrator is required to disclose them immediately to the parties (see Art. 14(5) of the Arbitration Law) The disclosure must be provided to all other parties to the proceedings and/or to the appointing authority, which may be the parties themselves, a permanent arbitration institution, the other arbitrators, if any, or—if the appointment is being made by the district (city) court acting as the appointing authority—the court. 2. Appointment of Arbitrators As a default rule, the parties are free to agree on the procedure for appointment of arbitrators (Art. 30(1) Arbitration Law). The Arbitration Law expressly differentiates the appointment procedure for institutional and ad hoc arbitration. If a dispute is referred to a permanent arbitral court, arbitrators shall be appointed pursuant to the arbitration rules of the respective institution, taking into account the principle of equality of parties (Art. 30(3)). The Arbitration Rules of the LCCI Court of Arbitration provide that each party appoints one arbitrator and that the Secretariat of the LCCI Court of Arbitration, after consultations with the Council of the LCCI Court of Arbitration, nominates the third arbitrator to act as the chairman of the tribunal within fifteen days from the date when the Answer to the Request for Arbitration has been received (Art. 43 of LCCI Arbitration Rules). In addition to standard provisions mirroring regulations of the Arbitration Law, there are also more recent amendments to the arbitration regulations, for example, in the Arbitration Rules of the Baltic International Arbitration Court. According to Art. 9(3) of these Rules, with prior consent of the presidium of the permanent arbitration court, the parties may also appoint as an arbitrator a person who is not on the list of arbitrators of the Baltic International Arbitration Court. If a dispute is referred to an ad hoc arbitration, each party shall appoint one arbitrator and these two, mutually agreeing between themselves, shall then appoint the presiding arbitrator (Art. 30(4)). In ad hoc arbitration the parties are also entitled to agree on a sole arbitrator. Article 30(5) of the Arbitration Law provides that once a party has appointed an arbitrator and has notified the other party of this, the appointing party can no longer revoke this appointment without the consent of the other party. A party may apply to the court and request the court to act as an appointing authority if the other party, the appointed arbitrators, the permanent arbitration court, or any other person who has been selected as an appointing authority, fails to act in accordance with the procedures laid down in the Arbitration Law or in the parties’ agreement, (Art. 30(6)). The party applying to the court must suggest the arbitrator to be appointed and provide the contact details of the candidate. The application must also be accompanied by a signed consent from the candidate arbitrator to be appointed for the specific dispute, along with a declaration that he or she is not subject to any restrictions provided in the Arbitration Law. Upon satisfying the application, the court shall appoint an arbitrator from the candidates suggested by the parties or, if the permanent arbitration court has failed to appoint the arbitrator, from the list of arbitrators of the arbitration court. When making an appointment, the court shall consider, as far as possible, the requirements for the arbitrator set out in the agreement of the parties. If it is not possible to appoint an arbitrator from the list of arbitrators of the arbitration court, the court shall appoint an arbitrator from the list established by the Latvian Council on Sworn Advocates.19. Art. 532.[2](4) and (5) of the CPL. The Latvian Council of Attorneys at Law has established a list of candidates for arbitrators by inviting all arbitrators who wish to be included in the list to express their interest in being included in the list.20. The list of arbitrators is published and available at: <https://www.advokatura.lv/lv/‌skirejtiesnesu-saraksts>. The Latvian Bar Council has not set any qualification or experience requirements. To the author’s knowledge, no arbitrator has ever been appointed from the list established by the Latvian Bar Council. In international ad hoc arbitration taking place in Latvia, the arbitral tribunal can be established in accordance with Art. IV of the European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration of 1961, whereby the LCCI Court of Arbitration acts as an appointing authority. 3. Number of Arbitrators The parties are free to agree on the number of arbitrators. The Arbitration Law provides in Art. 29(1) that the number of arbitrators must be an odd number. If the parties have not determined the number of arbitrators, and unless specified otherwise in the arbitration rules of the chosen permanent arbitration court, the Arbitration Law provides that the arbitral tribunal must consist of three arbitrators. Article 29(2) provides, however, that “[a]n arbitral tribunal may be comprised of one arbitrator, if the parties so agree”]. The arbitration rules of permanent arbitration courts typically establish as a default provision that the number of arbitrators shall be three (for instance, see Art. 40 of LCCI Arbitration Rules, Art. 8(2) of the Arbitration Rules of the Baltic International Arbitration Court). Neither the secretariat, nor the council of arbitration courts usually possess the discretion to decide whether the arbitral tribunal should be made up of a single arbitrator or three arbitrators, taking into account the complexity of the case, the amount in dispute and any other relevant circumstance. Likewise, neither the Arbitration Law, nor the arbitration rules of permanent arbitration courts outline the procedure for appointing arbitrators in the event of multiparty arbitration. However, in such instances, the principle of party equality would be applied. 4. Challenge to Arbitrators a. Grounds Article 14(5) of the Arbitration Law provides that every arbitrator must be “impartial, objective and fair” at the time of accepting an appointment to serve and throughout the arbitration proceedings until the final award has been rendered. The Article further stipulates that an arbitrator must disclose in writing any facts and circumstances that may cast reasonable doubt on his or her objectivity, impartiality or independence, before accepting the appointment. Such facts and circumstances must be disclosed to the parties, to the permanent arbitration court or other arbitrators, if any, or, if the district court is serving as an appointing authority, also to the district (city) court. If such facts or circumstances arise or become known after the arbitration has commenced, but before the arbitration proceedings are concluded, the arbitrator must immediately disclose these facts to the parties. Article 17 of the Arbitration Law contains various grounds on which a party may challenge an arbitrator. These grounds range from the requirements imposed on the arbitrators by the Arbitration Law, the requirements agreed by the parties, conflict-of-interest situations and “other circumstances” raising concerns about the impartiality and independence of the arbitrator. A non-exhaustive and illustrative list of conflict-of-interest situations is provided in Art. 16(1) of the Arbitration Law, naming both previous involvement of the arbitrator in the dispute or relation to the parties or party representatives. When revising the Arbitration Law in 2024, the legislative summary (annotation) of the draft21. Annotation to the Amendments of the Arbitration Law, the Cabinet of Ministers, 9 January 2024, <https://titania.saeima.lv/LIVS14/SaeimaLIVS14.nsf/0/EE8C273FA8BFA8A9C2258‌AA20046BC9F?OpenDocument>. contained a reference to the IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration, setting them as an international standard to the issues related to the conflict of interests in arbitration. This has encouraged the courts to apply as a guidance these IBA Guidelines, which has been done on several occasions already.22. No. 3-12/13528-24/23, Riga City Court decision (in Latvian), not published. Finally, for the sake of completeness, it should be noted that the Latvian Constitutional Court has remarked that in accordance with the practice of the ECHR, the lack of independence and impartiality can be found not only in cases where it has been established, but also in cases where there are well-grounded doubts as to the existence of independence and impartiality.23. See Delcourt v. Belgium[1970] ECHR 1, para. 31; Piersack v. Belgium [1982] ECHR 6, para. 30. The structure of the arbitration court, previous relations of the arbitrators with the parties as well as other factors may serve as the reason for such doubt.24. See <www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/2004-10-01_Spriedums.pdf>, at para. 10. b. Procedure The parties may agree on the procedure for challenge of the arbitrator, but in the absence of such agreement, the challenge procedure is determined in accordance with the Arbitration Law. The challenge can be made within five days from the date on which the party became aware of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the circumstances giving rise to the challenge. If the challenge is not made within five days, it is considered that the party has waived the right to object. The challenge has to be made in writing stating the grounds for challenge. The party may challenge an arbitrator that it has appointed or in whose appointment it has participated only if the party became aware of ground for challenge after the appointment. The party requesting the challenge shall bear the burden of proving at when it became aware of the circumstances giving rise to the challenge. In the absence of proof, the objection may be deemed to be declaratory and unsubstantiated25. Riga City Court decision of 20 December 2024 in matter no. 3-12/21946-24/15 (not published), Riga City Court decision of 16 September 2024 in matter no. 3-12/13528-24/23 (not published).. According to Art. 17(6) of the Arbitration Law, the challenge first of all shall be decided by the arbitrator himself/herself and, only if the arbitrator does not remove himself/herself, the challenge shall be decided by the rest of the arbitral tribunal. If the arbitration proceedings are governed by the arbitration rules of the permanent arbitration court, the arbitration rules may provide a different procedure. For example, the Arbitration Rules of LCCI Court of Arbitration which provide that where the request for challenge has been received before the appointment of the arbitrator or the challenged arbitrator refuses to withdraw voluntarily, the challenge shall be decided by the Council of the LCCI Court of Arbitration within ten days of receipt of the request (Art. 19(3)). If the challenged arbitrator has not accepted the challenge and also the arbitral tribunal or the council of the permanent arbitration court has not accepted the challenge, the party may request the district (city) court to decide the challenge. Pursuant to Art. 532.[3] of the Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) (see Annex II hereto), the party may submit the challenge to the district (city) court within thirty days from the date of receipt of the decision of the arbitrator or the arbitral tribunal to refuse the challenge. The challenge must state the grounds for challenge and response to the decision on not accepting the challenge. The application shall be accompanied with the decision of the arbitral tribunal or arbitrator on not to accepting the challenge. Filing of the challenge to the state court is subject to a EUR 300 state fee and is heard by the court in accordance with the general rules of court procedure by written proceedings, unless the court deems it necessary to hold a hearing. The failure of these persons to appear does not prevent the application from being considered. The court hears the case in a closed session and the decision of the court is not subject to appeal. The decision of the court is communicated to the parties involved and also to the permanent arbitration court. 5. Termination of the Arbitrator's Mandate In accordance with Art. 18, the arbitrator’s authority expires and his/her mandate is terminated in the following circumstances: “1) when a decision on the dismissal of the arbitrator is taken; 2) if the arbitrator has resigned from deciding a civil dispute; 3) if the parties have agreed on removal of the arbitrator; 4) if limitations stipulated in Article 15 [Person who may not be appointed as an arbitrator] of this Law apply to the arbitrator; 5) upon death of the arbitrator; 6) in other cases as stipulated in arbitration rules of a permanent arbitration court; 7) with the decision of district (city) court on removal of an arbitrator.” Latvian law does not provide a possibility to request judicial review of a decision terminating an arbitrator’s mandate. When an arbitrator is removed, a substitute arbitrator must be appointed in the same manner used to appoint the removed arbitrator. 6. Liability of Arbitrators Latvian law does not contain any special provisions relating to the liability of arbitrators. Therefore, the general rules of liability would apply, as generally contained in Latvian civil law. In one important decision, the Latvian Constitutional Court remarked in passing that arbitrators may in principle be held liable for rendering an unenforceable award.26. Judgment 2004-10-01 as of 17 January 2005, available (in Latvian) at: <www.satv.tiesa.gov.lv/upload/2004-10-01.rtf>. However, the Constitutional Court did not elaborate on the topic any further. Besides this obiter dictum statement by the Latvian Constitutional Court, to the authors’ knowledge, no case relating to the liability of arbitrators has been decided in Latvia.
