CHAPTER IV: ARBITRAL PROCEDURE 1. Place of Arbitration a. Determination The parties are free to determine the place of arbitration. If the parties have not agreed on the place of arbitration, it is determined by the arbitral tribunal, taking into account the efficiency considerations (Art. 25 of the Arbitration Law, see Annex I hereto). For example, the Rules of Arbitration of the LCCI Court of Arbitration provide that the place of arbitration will be the seat of the LCCI unless otherwise agreed by the parties. If the place of arbitration is in Latvia, Art. 1(2) of the Arbitration Law provides that the provisions of the Arbitration Law shall apply. b. Legal consequences The place of arbitration is a legal concept, not necessarily the physical location where hearings take place. It identifies which country’s arbitration law applies to the proceedings, including the rules on court intervention and support, challenges to arbitrators, procedural guarantees and setting aside (annulment) of an award (see Art. 533.1 of the Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”, see Annex II hereto) providing that an arbitral award can be set aside only if it has been “adopted in Latvia”). 2. Arbitral Proceedings in General a. Mandatory provisions All arbitral proceedings seated in Latvia are governed by the Arbitration Law. The Arbitration Law contains relatively detailed rules and thus does not leave much discretion to either the arbitral tribunal or the parties. b. Determining procedure Article 19 of the Arbitration Law provides that both parties shall have equal procedural rights in the arbitration proceedings. The arbitral tribunal must ensure that all parties have equal possibilities to exercise their rights to protect their interests. Article 26 of the Arbitration Law provides that arbitration proceedings must be conducted in accordance with the arbitration agreement, the arbitration rules of the permanent arbitration court, applicable laws and general legal principles. If the parties have agreed to conduct arbitration under the arbitration rules of a permanent arbitration court, the proceedings are conducted in accordance with the arbitration rules and provisions of the Arbitration Law. Whereas in ad hoc proceedings, the proceedings are organized by arbitral tribunal as they consider it appropriate in accordance with requirements set in the Arbitration Law. The chairman of the arbitral tribunal is entitled to decide procedural issues single-handedly, if either the parties or other arbitrators have entrusted him to do so. The arbitration procedure is commenced by presenting a statement of claim (Art. 34(1)), outlining the facts, and presenting the available evidence (Art. 35). The respondent is required to submit its response presenting its position and evidence. The term for providing the response may not be less than fifteen days from the date when the statement of claim has been dispatched to the respondent (Art. 37(1)). c. Exchange of written pleadings In arbitration proceedings – similarly to litigation before courts of general jurisdiction in Latvia – the claimant begins by submitting a statement of claim setting out its position, all claims, and providing the supporting evidence. In turn, the respondent submits a statement of defence in response to the claim. Neither the Arbitration Law nor the arbitration rules of local arbitration courts require the parties to exchange any additional written pleadings. However, the arbitral tribunal may order further written exchanges if it considers them necessary for the proper conduct of the proceedings or if such an exchange is requested by a party. d. Oral hearing Pursuant to Art. 40 of the Arbitration Law, an arbitral tribunal, paying due regard to the parties’ arbitration agreement, may either hold hearings in order to examine the explanations and objections of the parties as well as the evidence (i.e., an oral procedure) or resolve the dispute on the basis of the written evidence and submitted materials only (i.e., a written procedure). An arbitral tribunal can settle the case based merely on written evidence only where the parties have provided in the arbitration agreement that the arbitral proceedings shall take place in writing. If none of the parties requests the arbitral tribunal to hold oral hearings, the arbitral proceedings shall be conducted only by written procedure. However, a request for a hearing can be submitted at any time before the final award is rendered according to Art. 40(4) of the Arbitration Law, even if the parties in the arbitration agreement have agreed on the written procedure. The Supreme Court of the Republic of Latvia in decision no. SPC-38/2012 of 18 June 201227. Decision no. SPC38/2012 of 18 June 2012, available (in Latvian) at: <http://at.gov.lv/‌files/uploads/files/archive/department1/2012/38-spc-2012.doc>. has confirmed that “law grants a higher priority to oral proceedings than written. Therefore, even in cases where parties have agreed on written proceedings in arbitration, any party, notwithstanding it, unilaterally may request oral proceedings”. The Supreme Court ruled that arbitration rules that as a precondition for holding an oral hearing require the requesting party to deposit an amount even higher than the overall expenses of the arbitral proceedings, were in violation of the CPL provisions (now Arbitration Law provisions) and that refusal to hold an oral hearing without a deposit placed the requesting party in a situation where it is unable to present its case. 3. Evidence a. General Article 41(1) of the Arbitration Law defines evidence as “information on the basis of which the arbitral tribunal determines the existence or non-existence of such facts that have relevance in deciding the civil dispute”. Under Latvian law, the power to determine the admissibility and eligibility of evidence fully rests with the arbitral tribunal (Art. 41(5) Arbitration Law). Each party must prove the circumstances on which it is substantiating its claims or objections (Art. 41(2) Arbitration Law) and it is the duty of the parties to present the evidence. The Arbitration Law provides that admissible forms of evidence consist of: (1) statements of the parties; (2) written evidence (written documents, audio recordings, videotapes, electronic data carriers, digital videodiscs and others); (3) physical evidence; (4) witnesses and (5) expert opinions. Generally, the arbitral tribunal is not bound by the rules of evidence applied by Latvian courts; however, in practice arbitral tribunals often opt to follow these rules. The Arbitration Law explicitly states that no evidence shall possess predetermined force that may bind the arbitral tribunal (Art. 42(1) Arbitration Law). Furthermore, when drafting an arbitral award, the arbitral tribunal has to give reasons why it has relied on particular evidence instead of other and why it has considered some facts as proved and others unproved. b. Evidence of fact witnesses Until 2024, witness statements were not permissible as evidence in arbitral proceedings. This was due to concerns over the lack of control over this type of evidence, particularly as it could not be subsequently reviewed by a court when determining whether to enforce the arbitral award and the witness could not be held responsible for knowingly false testimony. In practice, to circumvent this restriction, parties frequently introduced witnesses and party-appointed experts as party representatives. These individuals were permitted to attend the hearing and provide “testimony” in the form of a statement on behalf of the party. During their testimony, both the arbitral tribunal and the opposing party were allowed to pose questions to these “party representatives”. However, this workaround had numerous flaws. For instance, “party representatives” had the right to attend the entire hearing and familiarize themselves with the case materials, and they could not be cross-examined. From 2024 onwards, witness statements have been accepted as evidence in arbitral proceedings. Should a witness fail to comply with the requests of the parties or the arbitral tribunal, the tribunal may seek the court’s assistance to hear the witness. According to Art. 532.[6] of the CPL, upon the arbitral tribunal’s request, the district (city) court located at the seat of arbitration is required to undertake the necessary procedural steps to gather evidence that may be pertinent to the case, including hearing witnesses. The district (city) court has the authority to evaluate the validity of such a request, which must therefore include a rationale explaining the relevance of the specific evidence. Per Art. 41[1] of the Arbitration Law, the arbitral tribunal’s request must contain details of the parties involved in the arbitral proceedings, the nature of the dispute, and the facts to be established, as well as the specific evidence to be collected by the court enforcing the request. If the request involves hearing a witness, the questions to be asked of the witness must be outlined. Article 532.[6] of the CPL stipulates that the court shall consider the request in a closed hearing; however, it does not provide further procedural guidance. Given that the request to hear a witness must be submitted by the arbitral tribunal to the court, there was debate within the working group regarding the attendance of court hearings and the potential for parties to cross -examine the witness. It was generally agreed that such an option should be available; however, neither the Arbitration Law nor the CPL explicitly addresses these issues. The minutes of the hearing and other evidence collected during the execution of the request must be forwarded by the court to the arbitral tribunal. The Arbitration Law addresses the issue on who should cover the costs related to obtaining the evidence, and Art. 41.[1](3) of the Arbitration Law provides that expenses related to obtaining of evidence shall be paid by the party requesting the arbitral tribunal to obtain such evidence through the court. Where both parties have made such a request, they shall pay the costs of taking evidence in equal parts. c. Evidence of party-appointed expert witnesses (for tribunal-appointed expert witnesses, see Chapter IV.4) Until 2024, party-appointed experts were treated the same way as fact witnesses and they were not cross-examined by the arbitral tribunal. As of 2024, the situation has changed and now party-appointed (as well as arbitral tribunal appointed) experts can be heard and cross-examined during the hearing, if necessary. d. Documentary evidence Article 41(3) of the Arbitration Law specifies that written evidence inter alia shall include any written documents, audio recordings, videotapes, electronic data carriers, digital videodiscs and others. Written evidence must be submitted in original or in the form of certified copy or excerpt. If a party submits a copy of a document, the arbitral tribunal may of its own accord, or pursuant to the request of the other party, require that the original document is presented. The arbitral tribunal shall return the original document, pursuant to the request of the person who has submitted it, and add its certified copy to the file of the procedure. e. Document disclosure Pursuant to Art. 43(1) of the Arbitration Law, the arbitral tribunal may require the parties to submit documents or other evidence that it has in its disposal, upon the reasoned request from the opposing party. The party filing such request must describe the evidence and provide its reasons why it believes that particular evidence is at the disposal of another party or any third party. The arbitral tribunal has no coercive power to enforce orders for disclosure of the documents, and any such order would be dependent on voluntary compliance by the parties. If a party refuses to provide particular written evidence and does not deny that it is at this party’s disposal, the arbitral tribunal can consider as proven those alleged facts in support of which the requesting party has referred to particular evidence. As regards documents possessed by third parties, as of 2024, the arbitral tribunal may also seek the court’s assistance to gather evidence from any third party, pursuant to the same procedure as set for obtaining witness statements (above); however, in any case the request must specify the document that party is requesting to be collected and must state the reasons why the party believes that particular document is in possession of the third party. 4. Tribunal-Appointed Experts a. Power of the tribunal Unless it has been provided otherwise in the arbitration agreement, upon request of either party the arbitral tribunal may appoint one or several experts (Art. 44(1) Arbitration Law). An arbitral tribunal may not appoint an expert on its own initiative. Article 45(3) of the Arbitration Law provides that the expert’s fees are to be paid by the party who has requested an expert; however, if both parties have requested an expert, the costs are split between the parties equally. b. Role of the parties In practice, the party requesting the expert’s appointment submits to the arbitral tribunal questions it wishes to be answered by the expert. The other party (through the arbitral tribunal) and the arbitral tribunal are allowed to submit their questions to the expert. In accordance with Art. 44(2) of the Arbitration Law, the parties must submit necessary information or documents and present the relevant goods or other articles to the expert. c. Rules applicable to the tribunal-appointed expert Article 44 of the Arbitration Law regulates the use of tribunal-appointed expert evidence in arbitration proceedings. Unless the arbitration agreement provides otherwise, the arbitral tribunal may, at the request of a party, appoint one or more experts and instruct them to prepare an expert opinion. The law does not limit the number of experts that may be appointed: the tribunal may designate multiple independent experts or direct several experts to work together and produce a joint expert opinion. Although the Arbitration Law does not expressly regulate this, arbitral tribunals in practice invite the parties to comment on proposed expert candidates and on the questions to be submitted to the experts. Once appointed, the parties are obliged to provide the expert with all information, documents, goods, or other materials necessary for the preparation of the expert report, as required by the tribunal. Cross-examination of the tribunal-appointed expert is not mandatory. The expert is invited to attend a hearing only if requested by a party, and at such a hearing the parties have the right to put questions to the expert and examine the conclusions set out in the expert opinion. This framework promotes transparency in the preparation and presentation of expert evidence and ensures that both parties have an equal opportunity to engage with, challenge, and test the expert’s findings. Unless the parties agree otherwise, the costs associated with the expert are borne by the party requesting the expert examination. These expenses are treated as costs related to the taking of evidence and may be recovered by the successful party from the unsuccessful party. d. Procedure While the Arbitration Law does not expressly state that the report of the expert must be in writing, this requirement is implied by Art. 44(3), which provides that an expert can be invited to participate in the arbitral hearing and can be cross-examined by the parties and the arbitral tribunal. The Arbitration Law is silent with respect to the parties’ right to receive a copy of the written report of the expert before the hearing. However, as a general rule, the arbitral tribunal must provide the parties with all materials it has received from the other party or any other person. 5. Interim Measures of Protection a. Categories of interim measures – tribunal The Arbitration Law does not specify what sort of interim measures are allowed in arbitral proceedings. The Arbitration Law merely provides that the arbitral tribunal is entitled to adopt a decision for solving procedural questions without deciding the dispute on merits (Art. 52 Arbitration Law). On this basis, the arbitral tribunal is, for instance, allowed to issue orders to preserve perishable goods, or to order a bank guarantee on behalf of one of the parties and to grant any other interim measures of protection. Such measures, adopted by the arbitral tribunal, cannot be enforced compulsory, but the party can comply with them voluntarily. b. Comparison with 2006 Model Law The Arbitration Law does not follow the UNCITRAL Model Law provisions on interim measures of protection. Considering that interim measures ordered by the tribunal are not enforceable through coercive means, the Arbitration Law neither establishes conditions for granting interim measures, nor provides the procedure how preliminary orders are adopted, nor regulate their modification, suspension or termination. c. Form Given that provisional measures ordered by arbitral tribunals are not enforceable through coercive means, the law does not prescribe any specific form in which they must be adopted. Accordingly, the arbitral tribunal may decide at its discretion whether to issue such measures as a separate procedural order, record them in the minutes of the hearing, or render them in the form of an interim award. d. Categories of interim measures – court According to Art. 33 of the Arbitration Law, the district (city) court may decide on securing a claim and granting interim measures in disputes subject to arbitration in accordance with the procedures laid down in the CPL. The district (city) court shall, upon the request by a party, decide as to termination or modification the security measures or interim measures. Until 2024 the court assistance was rather limited allowing the court to decide only on security measures before the commencement of arbitration. Now, the court’s powers are considerably broader allowing to grant all types of interim measures, including security measures, throughout all arbitral proceedings. When deciding on granting security measures and/or interim measures the court shall follow the procedure provided in the CPL. According to Art. 138.1 of the CPL, the court may grant following interim measures: (1) seizing the moveable property owned by the defendant; (2) entering of a pledge notation or another notation in the Land Register, the register of the respective movable property or any other public register; (3) prohibition on a defendant to perform certain activities or an obligation for the defendant to perform specific activities within a specified period; (4) postponement of enforcement activities (also enjoining bailiffs from transferring money or property to a creditor or debtor, or staying of sale of property); (5) interim regulation of contested relations. The court shall apply interim measures upon a reasoned application by the claimant if: (1) there are sufficient grounds to believe that the claimant has the right whose protection he or she is seeking; (2) there are sufficient grounds to believe that the right belonging to the claimant is being infringed or will be infringed; (3) there are sufficient grounds to believe that the aforementioned infringement causes or will cause significant damage to the claimant if provisional protection is not applied; (4) the balance of interests is in favour of applying interim protection. The first two conditions together constitute the prima facie validity of the claim. As regards security measures, Art. 138 provides that the means by which a claim may be secured are: (1) seizure of movable property and monetary funds of the defendant; (2) entering of a pledge notation in the register of the respective movable property or any other public register; (3) entering of a notation regarding the securing of a claim in the Land Register or Ship Register; (4) arrest of a ship; (5) prohibition on a defendant to perform certain activities or an obligation for the defendant to perform specific activities within a specified period; (6) seizure of those payments which are due from third persons, including monetary funds in credit institutions and other financial authorities; (7) postponement of enforcement activities (also enjoining bailiffs from transferring money or property to a creditor or debtor, or staying of sale of property). The court will only grant security for the claim if there is a prima facie claim and: (1) the claim is of a pecuniary nature; (2) enforcement of the arbitral award without security for the claim may be difficult or impossible; (3) the means of security for the claim chosen by the claimant are appropriate to secure the claim; (4) the choice of a specific means of securing the claim is a necessary prerequisite in this dispute in order to ensure the enforcement of the judgment in the future; (5) the infringement of the defendant’s interests by the application of the specified means of securing the claim does not have significantly greater negative consequences than the infringement of the claimant’s interests. Both interim measures and security measures can be requested at any stage of the arbitral proceedings and also before commencement of arbitral proceedings. If either the security measures or the interim measures are granted before commencement of the arbitral proceedings, the court will set a deadline for commencement of the arbitral proceedings that shall not be more than thirty days. The competent court to decide on granting interim measures and security measures would be the district (city) court where the debtor (defendant) is residing or where the assets of the debtor (defendant) are located (Art. 137(7) of the CPL). Although the CPL refers only to jurisdiction of these two courts, the purpose of the amendments to the CPL introduced in 2024 introducing court’s assistance to the arbitral proceedings was never intended to limit the number of competent courts, therefore also the court at the place of arbitration or at the registered address of the permanent arbitration court should recognize their competence to assist. The same court who granted security measures and/or interim measures may decide on their modification, suspension or termination. The request to the court for granting security measures and/or interim measures is subject to fixed state fee of EUR 80. 6. Representation and Legal Assistance Natural persons may participate in arbitral proceedings themselves or through their authorized representatives. Legal persons are represented by their officials who act within the scope of the authorization conferred by them in accordance with law, charter or by-laws, or by other authorized representatives of legal persons (Arbitration Law Art. 32(1)). Unless a party is represented by itself or by its legal representatives, any authorized representative must possess a power of attorney. Art. 32(2) of the Arbitration Law sets limitations for persons who may be authorized representative in the arbitration proceedings, i.e., the representative can be any natural person except a person: “1) who has not attained the age of majority; 2) who is under legal guardianship; 3) who has been deprived of conducting matters of other persons pursuant to a court decision; 4) who is kindred to the third degree or in affinity to the second degree with an arbitrator that is deciding the case; 5) who has given legal advice in a civil dispute to the other party in this case or in a case related to this dispute; 6) who has participated in mediation in this case or in a case related to this dispute.” If any of the above circumstances is established, the arbitral tribunal has a duty not to admit such representative in the arbitration process (Art. 32(3) Arbitration Law). It is prohibited (Art. 32(4) Arbitration Law) for a person who is or within the last two years was in the list of arbitrators of the respective permanent arbitration court, to be a representative of a party. Such person also may not be asked to provide legal assistance during proceedings of this permanent arbitration court. This limitation was imposed in order to reduce the interconnection between arbitrators, party representatives and representatives of the arbitral institution. Until 2024, the prohibition period during which the arbitrators included in the list of permanent arbitration court are not allowed to provide parties legal assistance, was reduced from five to two years. There are no rules requiring that the representative of the party must be a lawyer. A party may be represented by any authorized person, including a non-lawyer. 7. Default Failure of a party (either for claimant or for respondent) to appear before the arbitral tribunal without a valid cause after due notice has been issued and delivered or a failure to submit written evidence is not an obstacle to the continuation of the arbitral proceedings by the arbitral tribunal and deciding the dispute on the basis of the evidence at its disposal (Art. 46 Arbitration Law). 8. Confidentiality of the Award and Proceedings a. Confidentiality of the arbitral award and proceedings Unless parties have agreed otherwise, the arbitration process is confidential (Art. 23 Arbitration Law). The Arbitration Law further provides that the arbitral hearings are held in camera, and the arbitral tribunal shall not provide information to other persons or public regarding arbitration proceedings, unless the parties have agreed otherwise. Persons, who are not parties to arbitration process, may participate in arbitration hearing only with permission of the parties. In practice it is also recognized in Latvia that there is an implied duty of confidentiality on the parties to maintain confidentiality of documents and other information exchanged in the arbitration proceedings. However, there have been no court rulings on this issue. Most of the arbitration rules of permanent arbitration courts provide for the confidentiality of arbitral proceedings and arbitral hearings. The LCCI Rules of Arbitration provide that a duty of confidentiality is imposed also on the tribunal and the administrative staff of the institution (Art. 12 of the LCCI Rules of Arbitration). b. Confidentiality of arbitration-related court proceedings In accordance with Arts. 532.[1](6) and 532.[6](2) of the CPL, any requests for the appointment, challenge, or removal of an arbitrator, or challenges to the jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal, shall be conducted in private hearings. This includes not disclosing the identities of the parties in the court’s hearing schedule and prohibiting any third party from participating in the hearings or accessing the case materials. However, legal proceedings for annulment and recognition and enforcement of arbitral awards are governed by the general rules for national court proceedings as set out in the CPL; as a result, documents filed in legal proceedings become part of the public record and are therefore not confidential. The Arbitration Law stipulates that information about the arbitration process is provided to the individuals who are entitled to such information for the fulfilment of their duties as prescribed by the law (Art. 23 Arbitration Law). According to the provisions of the CPL, such individuals include, for example, a judge reviewing an application for setting aside or the enforcement of an arbitral award. Although in practice the judge returns the arbitration case file to the permanent arbitration court after examination, he or she may make necessary copies and refer to the contents of the file in court’s decision. All information upon which the judge bases his or her decision must remain in the court file of the case and thus becomes part of the public record. According to the general provisions of the CPL, a party may request the court to treat certain documents as confidential and may ask the court to conduct a closed hearing (Art. 11(3) CPL).28. Art. 11(3) of the CPL provides: The court has the discretion to decide whether the hearing shall be heard in camera. In such instances, only the conclusive part of the judgment will be announced publicly. However, only the enforcement of international arbitral awards is determined in a court hearing; domestic arbitral awards are decided by a judge through written proceedings without the need to summon the parties. All court decisions, including those on setting aside and recognition and enforcement, should be recorded in the public database of the Court Administration, although in practice this is not always the case. Only the judgments of Latvian courts that have become final are published in the public database, but procedural decisions and decisions, including those regarding the setting aside and enforcement of arbitral awards that are issued as decisions and not judgments, remain unpublished. Before publishing of the judgments, the judgments are anonymized, i.e., the names, addresses and other identifying information of the parties is removed. There is no Latvian privacy legislation specifically relevant to arbitration.

CHAPTER V: ARBITRAL AWARD 1. Types of Award The Arbitration Law (see Annex I hereto) provides that an arbitral tribunal may adopt procedural decisions or arbitral awards. The decisions resolve procedural issues without solving the merits of the case, but an arbitral award covers the merits of the case. After the final award has been rendered and if the parties have not agreed otherwise, the arbitral tribunal may make a supplementary award upon a request from either of the parties filed within thirty days from the day the award is sent to the parties, if any of the claims submitted prior to the making of the award have not been decided in the final award (Art. 55(3) Arbitration Law). A decision ordering interim relief may not be issued in the form of an award and, even if it were so issued, it would not be enforceable as an award. 2. Making of the Award a. Decision-making Pursuant to Art. 51 of the Arbitration Law, where the arbitral tribunal is composed of three or more arbitrators, the arbitral award must be adopted by a majority vote. An exception exists only with respect to procedural matters, where the law expressly provides that the presiding arbitrator may decide independently if such authority has been delegated by the parties or by the other arbitrators. Permanent arbitration institutions typically include in their procedural rules a provision authorizing the presiding arbitrator to decide on procedural issues independently. b. Time limits As to the timing of issuing an award, Art. 54 of the Arbitration Law provides that the arbitral tribunal shall render the arbitral award within fourteen days after it has finished reviewing the merits of the dispute. The arbitral award must be dispatched to the parties within three days after it has been rendered. Since the role of the courts in arbitral proceedings is very limited, it is impossible to approach the court for an extension of time. However, in practice, in most cases, the tribunal and the parties come to an agreement on the extension of the time limit. c. Dissenting opinions Even though the Arbitration Law contains no specific provisions on dissenting opinions, it is accepted practice in Latvia that an arbitrator may produce a dissenting opinion. Whether the dissenting opinion must be annexed to the award is not regulated. 3. Form of the Award Article 54 of the Arbitration Law provides that the award shall be made in writing and shall be signed by the arbitrators. If the arbitral tribunal consists of several arbitrators, the award shall be signed by all the arbitrators. If an arbitrator, for any reason, does not sign the award, the award shall stipulate the reasons explaining why the arbitrator’s signature is missing. The award must also contain the reasons on which it is based, unless otherwise expressly agreed by the parties. More specifically, in accordance with Art. 54(4) of the Arbitration Law, the award must contain the following information: “1) the composition of the arbitral tribunal; 2) the date of rendering of the arbitral award and the place of arbitration; 3) information concerning parties – for natural persons – name, surname, personal ID code, other identification data and declared place of residence or other address for communication; for legal persons – name; registration number; other identification data and legal address; 4) the subject matter of the dispute; 5) reasons for the arbitral award, if parties have not agreed otherwise; 6) the conclusion as to full or partial satisfaction of a claim or full or partial dismissal of a claim as well as the nature of the arbitral award; 7) the amount to be recovered, if the arbitral award is issued regarding the recovery of a monetary sum, by indicating separately the underlying debt and interest, time period as to which interest has been granted and the rights of a claimant to claim interest for the time period until the enforcement of the arbitral award and its amount; 8) the specific asset and its value that shall be recovered in case the asset has perished, if the arbitral award is issued regarding the restitution of an asset in integrum; 9) what actions shall be carried out by whom and in what time period if the arbitral award stipulates the carrying out of specific actions; 10) which part of an arbitral award relates to which claimant if an arbitral award is issued for the benefit of multiple claimants, or which part of an arbitral award shall be carried out by which defendant if an arbitral award is issued against multiple defendants; 11) the costs of arbitration process and division of the costs between the parties; 12) the costs of parties relating to legal assistance, if any, and division of legal costs between the parties; 13) other information that the arbitral tribunal deems relevant.” Pursuant to Art. 55 of the Arbitration Law, until the execution of the award, each party, notifying other party thereof, may request the arbitral tribunal to correct the award, clarify (interpret) the award or make a supplementary award. In addition, Art. 536 of the Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) (with reference to Art. 533.[4] CPL) (see Annex II hereto) sets grounds on which enforcement of an award may be refused (see Chapter VI). If the enforcement of the award has been refused, a party is not entitled to apply to the arbitral tribunal for correction of the award, but depending on the ground for refusal to enforce arbitral award, may submit the dispute anew to an arbitration. 4. Jurisdiction a. Power of arbitrators The arbitral tribunal may rule on its jurisdiction, including when a party disputes the validity or existence of the arbitral agreement (Art. 24(1) Arbitration Law). The party may challenge the jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal until the term set for filing of the response to the statement of claim. If the party fails to submit the challenge, such failure will be considered as a waiver of the right to object to jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal. In case there is no arbitration agreement, failure to submit the response challenging the jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal will result in entering into an arbitration agreement (see Art. 12(2)(3) Arbitration Law). b. Form of jurisdictional decision Arbitrators have the discretion to rule on jurisdiction as a preliminary ruling (procedural decision) or as part of the final award. c. Appeal If the arbitral tribunal has issued a preliminary ruling on jurisdiction, this ruling may be challenged in the district court within thirty days from the date the ruling was received (Art. 532.[5](1) CPL). The challenge must articulate the grounds for the appeal and include a copy of the arbitral tribunal’s decision. The submission of the appeal incurs a state fee of EUR 300 and is processed by the court following the standard rules of court procedure. The proceedings are conducted in writing, unless the court finds it necessary to convene a hearing. The absence of the opposing party does not hinder the consideration of the application. The court reviews the case in a closed session. The court’s decision is final and cannot be appealed. The court’s decision is communicated to both parties and to the permanent arbitration court or, in the case of ad hoc arbitration, to the arbitral tribunal. d. Timing The plea that arbitrators have no jurisdiction can be submitted to the arbitral tribunal not later than on the day the statement of defence is due. e. Role of court Where the tribunal issues a preliminary decision on jurisdiction, the party contesting that decision may apply to the district (city) court for judicial review in accordance with Art. 24(3) of the Arbitration Law. The submission of a request to the district (city) court to decide on jurisdiction of the arbitral tribunal does not suspend or impede the continuation of the arbitration proceedings, allowing the tribunal to proceed with the case unless the court decides otherwise. 5. Applicable Law a. Domestic arbitration The arbitral tribunal may decide the dispute as amiables compositeurs or ex aequo et bono only if explicitly empowered to do so by the parties. It is uncommon that parties opt to settle the dispute in this manner. b. International commercial arbitration Article 19 of the Civil Law provides that with respect to any obligations, rights and duties arising from the contract, one must first ascertain whether the contracting parties have agreed to determine their relationship in accordance with any specific law. Any such agreement on the applicable law between the parties shall be in effect insofar as it is not in conflict with mandatory or prohibitory norms of Latvian law. Article 19 of the Civil Law further provides that if no such agreement exists, it is presumed that the contracting parties have subjected their obligations to the laws of the state where the obligation is to be performed. If it is not possible to determine the place where the obligation is to be performed, the law of the place where the contract was entered into shall apply. Finally, contracts entered into by institutions of the State of Latvia and local governments of Latvia shall be adjudged in respect of their substance and consequences in accordance with Latvian law, provided that the contract itself does not stipulate otherwise. 6. Settlement If parties enter into a settlement during any stage of the arbitral proceedings, the arbitration procedure shall be terminated and, pursuant to a request of the parties, the arbitral tribunal shall confirm the settlement in the form of an award, encompassing terms and conditions of the settlement that parties have agreed upon. Such an award shall have the same status and legal force as any other arbitral award would have whereby the dispute would be decided on its merits. The arbitral tribunal shall refuse to confirm the settlement if it violates the rights or legal interests of a third party (Art. 53(2) Arbitration Law). Settlement confirmed by the arbitral tribunal in the form of an award must correspond and comply with the same requirements that have been made for any regular arbitral award and it may be enforced in the same way as a regular arbitral award. If the arbitration procedure has been terminated because the parties have reached a settlement, repeated recourse to the arbitration or court between the same parties concerning the same dispute and on the same grounds is not permissible. (Art. 57(2) Arbitration Law). 7A. Correction and Interpretation of the Award a. Correction An arbitral tribunal on its own motion or upon the request of one of the parties, may correct any grammatical or printing error or any calculations in the award. Such corrections can be made without summoning the parties, but the arbitral tribunal is free to hold a hearing to decide on the corrections necessary. If the arbitral tribunal decides to hold a hearing, it shall notify parties thereof at least fifteen days in advance. If as a result of the corrections the dispositive part of the award will change, the arbitral tribunal is required to invite the parties to express their opinion. It is established practice that if the arbitral tribunal has failed to provide the date or the place of arbitration in the award or the award has not been signed, it cannot be considered as a calculation, grammatical or printing error and consequently cannot be corrected. Latvian law does not provide for an option to revert to the arbitral tribunal during the setting aside or enforcement proceedings, if at the district (city) court it is concluded that the arbitration award is found to have defects that could be remedied by correction or interpretation. b. Interpretation Unless the parties have agreed otherwise, each party, notifying the other party thereof within thirty days after the award has been dispatched to the parties or receipt of the award, if it has been handed out in person, may ask the arbitral tribunal to interpret the award, without altering its content (Art. 55(2) Arbitration Law). Such an interpretation of the award becomes an integral part of the arbitral award. c. Form The correction or interpretation of the arbitral award shall be made in writing and signed by all members of the arbitral tribunal in order to become an integral (and thus enforceable) part of the final award. It is confirmed in the Latvian court practice, that correction or interpretation must be made in a separate document, i.e., not by incorporating the changes in the initial documents and signing it as the original document. d. Timing Although the law does not expressly define time limits for corrections, in practice all and any corrections shall be made before the execution of the award. 7B. Additional Award The Arbitration Law provides for the possibility of issuing an additional award “if some of the claims raised have not been dealt with, provided that evidence had been submitted and parties had given explanations regarding it” (Art. 55(3) Arbitration Law). The Arbitration Law provides that unless otherwise agreed by parties, a party may, within thirty days after the arbitral institution has sent out the arbitral award or the date of receipt thereof if an award has been served in person, request an arbitral tribunal to make an additional award if any of the claims have not been dealt with, provided that evidence had submitted and parties had given explanations regarding it. If an arbitral tribunal deems such request justified, it shall make an additional award. If the arbitral tribunal deems such request to be justified, it must notify parties regarding an arbitration hearing in which the decision may be taken on the making of such an additional award no later than fifteen days in advance. Failure to appear by parties shall not hinder the making of an additional award. The supplementary award must be rendered following the same content and form requirements that have been set for regular awards. 8. Fees and Costs a. General / Types of fees and costs The Arbitration Law stipulates that costs of the arbitration proceedings include costs relating to the adjudication of the dispute and arbitrators’ fees (Art. 45(1) Arbitration Law). Arbitration rules of permanent arbitral institutions contain more detailed provisions regarding fees and costs related to arbitration proceedings. The Arbitration Law specifies that, unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the costs of expertise, the tribunal’s secretary, translator, and expert witness involvement in the arbitration proceedings, as well as other expenses in the arbitration process, are to be borne by the party who requested the services of the secretary, translator, or expert, or who has sought expertise. If such a request is made by both parties, the costs are shared equally between them (Art. 45(3)). This allocation refers to covering of the costs during the arbitration process, and can be allocated by the arbitral tribunal when deciding who will bear the costs. b. Deposit Although the payment of a deposit is not covered by the Arbitration Law, this issue is governed by the arbitration rules of permanent arbitration institutions. For instance, the Rules of Arbitration of the LCCI Court of Arbitration stipulate that the arbitrators’ fees must be fully paid – by one or both parties – before the case is handed over to the arbitral tribunal. Each party is invited to pay a half of the costs; however, if the respondent refuses, the claimant is invited to pay the deposit in full. A failure to pay the deposit may result in the Secretariat of the LCCI Court of Arbitration deciding to terminate the arbitration proceedings (Art. 66 and Art. 2 of the Second Appendix of the LCCI Rules of Arbitration). c. Fees of arbitrators In institutional arbitration, the amount of the arbitrators’ fees is set out in the arbitration rules. Nonetheless, the parties are entitled to agree on different fees, provided they have the arbitrator’s consent. As a general rule, in institutional arbitration, there is a fixed fee rate, calculated on the basis of the sum in dispute. At the time of writing, the LCCI Court of Arbitration rates are as follows (excluding VAT): Sum in dispute Administrative expenses Each arbitrator’s fees up to EUR 1,000 EUR 250 EUR 125 from EUR 1,001 to EUR 5,000 EUR 250 EUR 175 From EUR 5,001 to EUR 10,000 EUR 250 EUR 250 from EUR 10,001 to EUR 25,000 EUR 500 EUR 250 from EUR 25,001 to EUR 100,000 EUR 750 + 1 % of sum which exceeds EUR 25,001 EUR 500 from EUR 100,001 to EUR 500,000 EUR 1,500 + 0.7 % of sum which exceeds EUR 100,001 EUR 1,250 from EUR 500,001 to EUR 1,000,000 EUR 2,000 + 0.5 % of sum which exceeds EUR 500,001 EUR 2,000 from EUR 1,000,001 to EUR 5,000,000 EUR 3,000 + 0.2 % of sum which exceeds EUR 1,000,001 EUR 3,000 over EUR 5,000,001 EUR 5,000 + 0.05 % of sum which exceeds EUR 5,000,001 EUR 5,000 In the Baltic International Court of Arbitration, the rates are as follows (excluding VAT): Sum in dispute Administrative expenses Each arbitrator’s feesIf the dispute is settled by 1arbitrator 3arbitrators Up to EUR 1 500 10% of the sum,but not less than EUR 70 EUR 100 EUR 70 From EUR 1,500 to EUR 6,999.99 EUR 150 + 2% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 1 500 EUR 150 EUR 100 From EUR 7,000 to EUR 29,999.99 EUR 260 + 1.5% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 7,000 EUR 300 EUR 200 From EUR 30,000 to EUR 149,999.99 EUR 605 + 1% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 30,000 EUR 700 EUR 400 From EUR 150,000 to EUR 699,999.99 EUR 1,805 + 0.6% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 150,000 EUR 1,200 EUR 800 From EUR 700,000 to EUR 1 499,999.99 EUR 5,105 + 0.3% of the sum,which exceeds EUR 700,000 EUR 2,000 EUR 1,400 Over EUR 1,500,000 EUR 7,505 + 0.1% of the sum,which exceeds 1,500,000 EUR EUR 5,000 EUR 3,500 Non-property claim EUR 200 EUR 200 EUR 150 d. Costs of legal assistance to parties The parties are entitled to be compensated for costs of legal assistance. There is no requirement setting a cap for legal costs that could be awarded or that party costs be reasonable, nor are there any special rules that would apply to the award of party costs. e. Allocation of costs The amount of costs shall be determined by the arbitral tribunal, “taking into account the amount of a claim, complexity of a dispute and provisions referred to in an arbitration agreement and other relevant circumstances” (Art. 45(2) Arbitration Law). As a general rule, arbitration costs follow the event, i.e., the loser pays. In practice, arbitrators can also apportion the costs between the parties, where the parties have won on some claims and lost on others or there have been other particularities of the case (for example, the party has tried to delay proceedings or due to the party’s fault there have been unnecessary costs incurred). 9. Notification of the Award and Registration An arbitral award does not need to be registered or deposited with a court or any other institution. There is no official record of issued arbitral awards. 10. Enforcement of Domestic and International Awards Rendered In [Country of Report] a. General Where a party voluntarily complies with the award or compulsory execution of the award is not necessary, no leave for enforcement is required. An award has to provide for a period for voluntary compliance with the award, which cannot be less than ten days (Art. 58(1) Arbitration Law). Where a party does not comply voluntarily, the interested party is entitled to apply to the district (city) court at the opposing party’s place of registration or declared address, or at the place of enforcement of the award for issue of a writ of execution for compulsory enforcement of the arbitral award (Art. 534 CPL). The state fee depends on amount of awarded sum (see table below), but cannot exceed EUR 300.00 (Annex III of the CPL). Amount of awarded sum(euro) State fee(euro) 1–700 5 701–1800 15 1801–3300 25 3301–5200 45 5201–7500 65 7501–10 200 90 10 201–13 300 115 13 301–16 800 150 16 801–20 700 185 20 701–25 000 225 above 25 000 300 Article 535(2) of the CPL provides that the court may request from the parties and also from the permanent arbitration court or the arbitral tribunal the arbitration case file or any other information necessary for deciding the request. The CPL states that if a dispute is resolved under by institutional arbitration, the arbitration case file shall remain in the archives of the permanent arbitration court for ten years after the end of the arbitral proceedings (Art. 50 Arbitration Law). The permanent arbitration court shall store the arbitration case file in accordance with the archival storage procedures provided for by law. If a dispute is resolved by ad hoc arbitration, the number of copies of the procedural documents (arbitration case file) shall correspond to the number of the parties to the dispute, and they shall remain with the parties. b. Leave for enforcement There is no requirement to apply for leave to enforce an arbitral award. c. Enforcement i. Grounds for refusal Article 534(2) of the CPL provides that a party requesting enforcement of the award must present to the court (1) the arbitral award; (2) a document containing the arbitration agreement, and (3) a document evidencing payment of the state fee. The Arbitration Law provides in Art. 535(2) CPL) that upon request of the court, the arbitral institution (if institutional arbitration) or the parties (if ad hoc arbitration) must provide information necessary for deciding on whether to grant a request for a writ of execution. A decision on whether to issue or refuse a writ of execution is taken by a judge on the basis of the submitted documents without summoning the parties. According to Art. 536(1) of the CPL, the judge shall refuse to issue a writ of execution, on the same grounds that are provided for setting aside of the award (Art. 533[4] CPL), with the addition that writ of execution also can be refused if the award has not become binding on the parties or has been set aside in the country where it was adopted. ii. Procedure The decision of the district (city) court to refuse enforcement of an arbitral award can be appealed by filing ancillary complaint about the court’s decision. Only the applicant is entitled to appeal, i.e., a party against whom the award shall be enforced may not appeal the decision to enforce arbitral award, as he could have exercised its rights to object by filing a request for annulment of arbitral award. An ancillary complaint may be submitted within ten days from the adoption of the decision of the court and it must be addressed to the regional court, i.e., a court of appeal. An ancillary complaint is subject to security deposit payment in amount of EUR 80 (Art. 34(5) and Annex III of the CPL).29. Available at <www.likumi.lv/doc.php?id=50500>. The court’s ruling on an ancillary complaint is not subject to appeal to the Supreme Court. 11. Publication of the Award Arbitral awards are not generally published in Latvia. An award might be published if all parties so agree, but as a general rule such an option is not even discussed between the parties. The consent of the parties may not be presumed. There are no special rules for cases where publication would be needed in the public interest.

CHAPTER VI: ENFORCEMENT OF FOREIGN ARBITRAL AWARDS 1. Enforcement under Conventions and Treaties a. Conventions in force Latvia has been a party to the 1958 Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (the “New York Convention”)30. The New York Convention has been in force in Latvia from 13 July 1992. since 1992, and to the Convention on Settlement of Investment Disputes Between States and Nationals of Other States (“ICSID Convention”)31. The ICSID Convention has been in force in Latvia from 7 July 1997. See <https://icsid.worldbank.org/sites/default/files/ICSID_Convention_EN.pdf>. since 1997. As of 2003, Latvia has been a party to the 1961 European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration.32. The European Convention has been in force in Latvia from 12 February 2003, with a reservation as to Art. 2.2, which was waived only in 13 November 2013. It was ratified by the “Law on European Convention on International Commercial Arbitration”, adopted on 23 January 2003, published in 23 Latvijas Vēstnesis(12 February 2003, no. 2788). b. Procedure The Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) contains a separate chapter (Chap. 78) on Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards (see Annex III hereto). The CPL stipulates that enforcement of foreign arbitral awards takes place “in accordance with this Law [the CPL] and international agreements in force for the Republic of Latvia” (Art. 646 CPL). The procedure for enforcing a foreign arbitral award is set out in Arts. 647-651 of the CPL, which provide for the making of an application for recognition and execution of a foreign award to the appropriate district court. Documents to be submitted with the application include “the original of the foreign arbitral award or a properly certified true copy of it” (Art. 647(3)(1)), an officially translated document containing the arbitration agreement and confirmation of payment of the state fee. The application shall also include the information provided in Art. 638 CPL.33. Art. 638(2) provides: c. Court decisions According to Art. 649(4) of the CPL, “[a]n application shall only be rejected in the cases provided for by international treaties binding upon the Republic of Latvia”. In practice, therefore, the court review in enforcement proceedings is carried out by directly applying the provisions of the New York Convention, including the limited grounds for refusal of enforcement included in the text of the Convention. The CPL also provides that an “ancillary complaint” may be submitted regarding a court decision to recognize and enforce – or refuse the recognition and enforcement of – a foreign arbitral award (Art. 649(5)). A party cannot attack the award on the merits. Such an ancillary complaint may be submitted within ten days from the adoption of the decision of the court and it must be addressed to the appropriate regional court, i.e., a court of appeal. An ancillary complaint is subject to security deposit payment in amount of EUR 80. (Art. 34(5) and Annex III of the CPL).34. Available at <www.likumi.lv/doc.php?id=50500>. The court’s ruling on an ancillary complaint is not subject to appeal. The authors are not aware of any court decision that gave a notable interpretation of the New York Convention. 2. Enforcement Where No Convention or Treaty Applies The CPL does not require that for a foreign arbitral award to be enforceable, it must fall under any convention or treaty. The authors are not aware of any court decisions in Latvia dealing with recognition and enforcement of an award issued in a non-New York Convention country; however, in such a case, we consider it likely that the award would still be recognized and enforced under the Convention’s provisions. The CPL does not set any time limits for making the request for enforcement, nor does it require leave for enforcement in the country where the award was made. 3. Rules of Public Policy In the case of enforcement of foreign awards, the public policy ground mentioned in the New York Convention would be applicable (see Art. 649(4) CPL). The authors are not aware of any Latvian court decision where the foreign award would be not recognized and enforced on the basis of public policy.

CHAPTER VII: MEANS OF RECOURSE 1. Appeal on the Merits from an Arbitral Award a. Appeal to a second arbitral instance The arbitral award comes into effect on the day it is made and it may not be appealed (Art. 51(2) of the Arbitration Law, see Annex I hereto). Thus, the Arbitration Law establishes a single-tier arbitral process, and once the arbitral tribunal issues the award, the proceedings are deemed final within the arbitral system. The parties cannot create an appellate arbitral body by agreement, nor can an arbitral institution establish such a mechanism in its rules, as the legislation does not recognize or permit a hierarchy of arbitral tribunals. b. Appeal to a court Under Latvian law, no appeal on the merits of an arbitral award is permitted, and the Arbitration Law provides no mechanism by which a court may re-examine the substance of the case decided by the arbitral tribunal. The only recourse against an arbitral award is an application for its setting aside on the limited grounds expressly provided in the Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”). 2. Setting Aside of the Arbitral Award (Action for Annulment, Vacation of the Award) a. Grounds for setting aside Following the Constitutional Court judgment of 25 January 2022 “On Compliance of Section 534, 534[1], 535, 536 and 537 with the first sentence of Art. 92 of the Satversme of the Republic of Latvia”, in 2024 the legislator introduced a mechanism for setting aside of arbitral awards. This mechanism was introduced by adopting amendments to the CPL and Arbitration Law. Only those awards that have been rendered in Latvia can be set aside by Latvian courts. The award can be set aside only on limited grounds that correspond to those listed in the Model Law. These grounds are listed in Art. 533[4] CPL and differentiate between grounds which existence must be proved by the applicant, and those assessed by the court ex officio: “(1) The court may set aside an arbitral award if: 1) the arbitration agreement was concluded by a party who was not entitled to enter into the arbitration agreement or the arbitration agreement is not valid; 2) a party to the arbitration agreement was not given proper notice of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the arbitral proceedings or was otherwise unable to present his case; 3) the arbitral award deals with a dispute which is not provided for in or is inconsistent with the arbitration agreement, or exceeds the limits of the claim or the competence of the arbitration court; 4) the composition of an arbitral tribunal or the arbitration proceedings were not conducted in accordance with the arbitration agreement of the parties, unless such agreement is contrary to the provisions of laws and regulations which the parties could not have waived. (2) The court sets aside the arbitral award if it finds that: 1) the particular civil legal dispute may be resolved only by a court; 2) the arbitral award is contrary to public policy. (3) If the arbitral award deals with several matters and the court finds the ground referred to in Clause 3 of Paragraph One of this Article in respect of any such matter, the court shall set aside the award partially in relation to that matter.” In assessing whether grounds for setting aside an arbitral award exist, the court will also consider whether the applicant has raised objections in a timely and appropriate manner and whether it has established the existence of the infringement or non-compliance in question. For instance, objections that the arbitrator was not impartial and neutral will also be assessed in conjunction with whether the party was already aware at the time of the arbitration of the circumstances giving rise to doubts about the person’s impartiality and neutrality and if it had adequately informed the arbitral tribunal and the other party thereof. Any objections must be supported by evidence, as otherwise objections will be considered as ungrounded. b. Procedure The application for setting aside of the arbitral award or supplementary award may be submitted within thirty days from the date of rendering the arbitral award or supplementary award. Arbitral award can be set aside only on basis of limited grounds set forth in Art. 533[4] CPL and only if the arbitral award has not already been enforced. Such limitation was introduced to emphasize the importance for the party against whom the award has been rendered to be active and request setting aside of arbitral award in due manner. An application for setting aside of the arbitral award may be submitted to the district (city) court at the declared place of residence or registered office of the interested party; if there is no such place, it should be submitted at the place of residence of the interested party or at the place of enforcement of the arbitral award. If the interested party does not have a place of residence or registered office in Latvia and the place of enforcement of the arbitral award is not determinable, the application for setting aside the arbitral award may be filed with the district (city) court at the seat of the arbitration or the declared place of residence or registered office of the applicant. The application for the setting aside of the arbitral award must be accompanied by: (1) the arbitral award; (2) the arbitration agreement, if it is in the possession of the applicant; and (3) documents confirming the payment of the state fee. The applicant may appeal only a negative decision of the court, i.e., a refusal to a request to either set aside or to issue an enforcement order. An ancillary complaint may be submitted within ten days from the adoption of the decision of the district (city) court and it must be addressed to the regional court, i.e., a court of appeal. An ancillary complaint is subject to security deposit payment (Art. 34(5) and Annex III of the CPL). The ruling of the regional court on an ancillary complaint is not subject to appeal to the Supreme Court. The state fee depends on the amount of the awarded sum (see table below), but in any case, it cannot exceed EUR 300.00 (Annex III CPL). The amount of state fee depends on the amount of awarded sum: Amount of awarded sum (euro) State fee (euro) 1–700 5 701–1800 15 1801–3300 25 3301–5200 45 5201–7500 65 7501–10 200 90 10 201–13 300 115 13 301–16 800 150 16 801–20 700 185 20 701–25 000 225 above 25 000 300 This schedule applies to both setting aside proceedings and also to the enforcement process. c. Waivers The parties cannot waive the possibility of bringing an action for setting aside an arbitral award. The setting-aside mechanism is a state-mandated form of judicial control over the arbitral process and constitutes a mandatory safeguard that operates irrespective of party autonomy. As such, the right to apply for setting aside an arbitral award cannot be excluded, limited, or waived by agreement of the parties, whether in the arbitration clause or during the proceedings. d. Effect of an award that has been set aside The dispute that was decided by the award that was set aside, may be resolved by a court of a general jurisdiction, if the award was set aside for the following reasons: (1) there is no valid arbitration agreement; or (2) the arbitral award has been made on a dispute that is not provided for in the arbitration agreement or does not comply with it, or exceeds the limits of the claim or the jurisdiction of the arbitration court; or (3) the dispute is non-arbitrable. However, the dispute may be submitted to arbitration de novo in the following circumstances: (1) a party to the arbitration agreement was not duly informed of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the arbitral proceedings, or was otherwise deprived of the opportunity to present its case; (2) the arbitral tribunal was not constituted, or the arbitral proceedings were not conducted, in accordance with the parties’ agreement, unless such agreement contradicts mandatory provisions of law from which the parties are not permitted to derogate; (3) the arbitral award is contrary to public order; (4) the enforcement of the arbitral award has been refused on the ground that it has not yet become binding on the parties, or because the award has been set aside in the state in which it was made. 3. Other Means of Recourse The Public Prosecutor has the right to protest against the recognition and enforcement of arbitral awards rendered by the arbitral tribunal. More specifically, in accordance with Arts. 483 and 484 of the CPL, if serious material or procedural errors are found in a case that has been reviewed only by a court of first instance and has not been appealed under the procedure provided under the CPL for reasons independent from the parties, or if the rights of state or municipality institutions that have not been parties to the case are breached, the Public Prosecutor may file a protest to the Supreme Court.35. Art. 484 provides: This means of recourse is possible only if not more than ten years have passed since the court judgment was issued (Art. 483).36. Art. 483 of the CPL, entitled “Submitting a Protest”, provides:

CHAPTER VIII: CONCILIATION / MEDIATION 1. General a. ADR in Latvia There was a broad publicity campaign in operation in Latvia encouraging the use of mediation; however, at the time of writing, there is no major recourse to mediation as one of the means of dispute settlement in commercial matters. Mediation is mostly used in family and criminal disputes. In addition, the Law on Labour Disputes (in force as of 1 January 2003) provides for the possibility of settling collective labour disputes by means of conciliation or mediation. The decision of a “reconciliation committee” established for the resolution of the collective labour dispute, is binding on all the parties involved. A settlement achieved by means of mediation, which takes a form of a written agreement, has the same force as a collective agreement. b. Institutions The Latvian Chamber of Commerce and Industry adopted the Rules of Mediation in 2006.37. For more information, contact <www.chamber.lv>. Generally, however, parties are very reluctant to base their attempt at mediation on a set of mediation or conciliation rules. More information about mediation in Latvia can be found on <www.mediacija.lv> and <www.sertificetimediatori.lv>. 2. Legal Provisions The Latvian Civil Procedure Law (“CPL”) provides that the judge shall refuse a statement of claim if the claimant has not complied with agreed procedures in regard to preliminary extra-judicial attempts at settlement of the dispute or has not taken the measures prescribed by law to resolve the dispute with the respondent prior to action being brought before the court (Art. 132(7) CPL). If there is no agreement between the parties to settle the dispute through alternative dispute resolution (“ADR”), the claimant must indicate in the statement of claim what its opinion towards use of ADR is. If the claimant has failed to do so, the court is under an obligation to refuse to accept a statement of claim.

CHAPTER IX: INVESTMENT TREATY ARBITRATION 1. conventions and treaties Latvia became party to the ICSID Convention in 1997, which has been in force since 7 July 1997. Latvia has also been a signatory to the Energy Charter Treaty since 1996,38. See <http://www.energycharter.org/fileadmin/DocumentsMedia/Legal/ECT_Guide_en.pdf>. including the Charter’s provision on investment. In addition, Latvia is party to a number of bilateral investment treaties (“BITs”). Latvia does not employ a model BIT. Generally, BITs concluded between Latvian and other States contain dispute resolution provisions providing for submission of disputes to ad hoc arbitration (often under the UNCITRAL Rules or to ICSID, the Stockholm Chamber of Commerce (SCC Institute) or the International Chamber of Commerce Court of Arbitration (ICC). 2. investment arbitration Latvia ensures the protection of its interests in international investment disputes through centralized state representation. Under Cabinet Regulation No. 358 of 4 July 2023, the State Chancellery39. <https://www.mk.gov.lv/en/representation-of-state>. is entrusted with representing Latvia in investment arbitration proceedings, coordinating the government’s legal position and acting as the primary institution responsible for communication with arbitral bodies and the parties involved. The State Chancellery publishes information on any international arbitration proceedings initiated against Latvia once the arbitral tribunal has been formally constituted, unless confidentiality obligations arising from treaties, arbitration rules, or party agreements require otherwise.40. <https://www.mk.gov.lv/en/media/1218/download?attachment>. This framework strengthens both accountability and procedural clarity in Latvia’s participation in international investment dispute resolution. At the time of writing, Latvia has three active investment arbitration proceedings, one of them being in the award enforcement stage,41. Eugene Kazmin v. Latvia(ICSID Case No. ARB/17/5) (The arbitral proceeding was concluded in favour of Latvia. Enforcement of the arbitral tribunal’s award is currently pending.). another terminated without review on merits,42. R.S.E. Holdings v. Republic of Latvia(PCA Case No. 202241) (The arbitral proceedings were concluded in favour of Latvia by an Award of 28 May 2025, meaning that the arbitration has been terminated and the case will not be examined on its merits. Currently, proceedings are ongoing to recover the expenses incurred by the State in the amount of EUR 678,545.20, as well as the interest.). and the third one still pending.43. AS PNB Banka and others v. Republic of Latvia(ICSID Case No. ARB/17/47) (On 19 May 2025, the claimants submitted a proposal to disqualify arbitrator Peter Tomka, and the proceedings were suspended. On 10 June 2025, the motion to disqualify arbitrator Peter Tomka was rejected and the decision is being prepared.). 3. national investment legislation There is no national investment legislation in Latvia.
