ANNEX III: The Law of the Republic of Latvia, Civil Procedure Law, Part F “International Civil Procedure”, Chapter 78 “Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards”

title: The New Moroccan Law on Arbitration: A Commentary
copyright: International Journal of Arab Arbitration

sections.title: 1. MAIN FEATURES OF THE NEW ARBITRATION CODE:
sections.text: 1. MAIN FEATURES OF THE NEW ARBITRATION CODE: Below is a summary of the main features of the new Arbitration Code in terms of form and substance. 1.1 General presentation The new Arbitration Code comprises 105 articles divided into three Titles: • Title I on Arbitration, which is the main focus of this commentary, contains three chapters: the first provides definitions and general provisions (Arts. 1-19), the second deals with domestic arbitration (Arts. 20-70), and the third addresses international arbitration (Arts. 71-85); • Title II on Conventional Mediation (Arts. 86-100), which is beyond the scope of this commentary; and • Title III on Final and Transitory Provisions (Arts. 101-105), which is discussed in the immediately following paragraphs. 1.2 Entry into force The new law entered into force on 14 June 2022, the day following its publication in the Official Bulletin (Art. 103), thereby repealing the provisions of Chapter VIII, Title V of the CCP, as amended and completed by Law No. 08-05 (Art. 105). Pursuant to Article 6 of the 2011 Moroccan Constitution,7 Dahir (Royal Edict) No. 1-11-91 of 29 July 2011 promulgating the text of the Constitution (Official Bulletin No. 5964bis of 30 July 2011). laws cannot have a retroactive effect and, in that regard, Article 103 of the new Arbitration Code expressly provides that the provisions of Chapter VIII, Title V of the CCP, as amended and completed by Law No. 08-05, continue to apply to (i) arbitration agreements concluded prior to 14 June 2022 and (ii) arbitration claims pending before arbitral tribunals or disputes submitted to court proceedings in relation thereto as of 14 June 2022, until final settlement and the exhaustion of all available recourses and remedies. While this transitory provision may appear straightforward, the most recent interpretation of Article 2 of Law No. 08-05 given by the Cour de Cassation, which also applies to arbitration agreements and cases existing as of its date of entry into force (thus governed by the 1974 CCP), would lead one to conclude that such agreements and cases are subject to substantive rules of the previous regime, but that the procedural rules applicable are those set out by the law in force at the time the arbitration proceedings are initiated by the parties.8 Moroccan Court of Cassation, Decision No. 300 of 22 March 2018 – Administrative matter No. 2015/1/4/1542, which reversed two previous decisions where the Court considered that the former law applies to both the procedure and the merits of such arbitration cases: Moroccan Court of Cassation, Decision No. 427 of 14 November 2013 – Commercial Matter No. 2012/1/3/1738 and Moroccan Court of Cassation, Decision No. 133 of 19 February 2015 – Matter No. 2013/2/3/1349. In concreto, this means that the validity of an arbitration clause contained in a contract signed in 2020 is to be determined pursuant to the rules of the 1974 CCP, as amended and completed by Law No. 08-05, but the procedure of recognition and enforcement (exequatur) of an award rendered pursuant to the said clause in arbitration proceedings initiated after 13 June 2022 is governed by the provisions of the new Arbitration Code. Considering that the Arbitration Code was drafted before the decision of the Cour de Cassation, it is questionable whether this is the effect intended by the Moroccan legislator and if it will be implemented by the lower courts in upcoming decisions.

sections.title: 2. MAIN CHANGES COMPARED WITH THE PRIOR REGIME:
sections.text: 2. MAIN CHANGES COMPARED WITH THE PRIOR REGIME: This section presents and comments on the main changes brought on by the new Arbitration Code. 2.1 Fewer conditions for the validity of arbitration clauses Law No. 95-17 finally addresses the uncertainty arising from Article 317 CCP, which subjected the validity of any arbitration clause to the inclusion of the arbitrators’ names or the conditions for their appointment, often leading, in practice, to the cancellation of these clauses or, if the arbitral tribunal proceeded on the basis of a defective arbitration clause, the cancellation of the award or the refusal of the court to enforce it. For the arbitration clause to be valid, it now suffices that it be stipulated unequivocally, in writing, in the main agreement or in a document to which it refers. As to what constitutes an agreement in writing, the new law expressly allows the use of “other means of written communication”, including e-mail, in addition to the traditional “exchange of letters and telegrams” (Art. 3).9 Cf. New York Convention, Art. II-2. However, the use of e-mails as evidence of an agreement remains subject to the provisions of Law No. 53-05 of the Electronic Exchange of Legal Data, which poses a set of conditions that, in effect, limit the recognition of e-signatures to those made through a national authentication system, thereby significantly reducing the opening made by the new Arbitration Code toward e-arbitration (see 2.2, infra). Such restriction on the use of electronic signatures within the framework of international arbitration is unfortunate, especially when considering, on the flip side of the issue of consent to arbitration, the possibility for the extension of arbitration clauses to non-signatories signalled by the Cour de Cassation in Ynna Holding,10 .Ynna Holding v. Fives FCB: Moroccan Court of Cassation, Decision of 3 October 2022. As part of the enforcement proceedings regarding a 2011 ICC arbitral award that held Ynna Holding jointly liable with its subsidiary (Ynna Asment) to pay damages to Fives FCB for abusive termination of a contract (containing an arbitration clause) to which it is not a party, the Casablanca Commercial Court refused to enforce the award against Ynna Holding, a decision that was overturned by the Commercial Court of Appeal in 2015. The Cour de Cassation, while clearly insisting on the exceptional nature of such an extension, decided to invalidate the appellate court’s decision for lack of reasoning and to remit the matter to it for a new decision on this issue. albeit on an exceptional and sufficiently reasoned basis only. A more arbitration-friendly approach would have been to allow for any nationally-certified authentication system to be used by the parties. 2.2 A step towards e-arbitration Still, and although the conduct of arbitration exclusively by digital means11 For more developments on e-arbitration as part of Online Dispute Resolution (ODR), see: Amro, Ihab. 2019. Online Arbitration in Theory and in Practice: A Comparative Study of Cross-Border Commercial Transactions in Common Law and Civil Law Countries. Cambridge Scholars Publishing. is not yet provided for, the new law has introduced the use of information and communication technology at various steps of the arbitration procedure, from the conclusion of the arbitration agreement (see above) to the delivery of the arbitral award. Indeed, the Arbitration Code allows for the electronic exchange of motions and briefs between the parties and the arbitral tribunal, the holding of meetings and hearings by means of teleconference and the signature and delivery of the arbitral award by electronic means (cf. Arts. 35 and 51) but, for the time being, only within the framework of arbitration proceedings conducted in Morocco, where the use of information technology in arbitration should further decrease the time required to resolve commercial disputes brought before the arbitrator(s), as one of the main incentives for economic actors to submit their disputes to arbitration rather than to the jurisdiction of national courts.12 The other main incentives are as follows: the confidentiality of the proceedings and the freedom of the parties and/or arbitrator(s) to choose the language in which the proceedings will be conducted. In Morocco, the total cost of arbitration, whether institutional or ad hoc, is often much higher than that of court proceedings and, as such, is not an argument often used in favour of arbitration. However, the dematerialization of arbitration proceedings and the use of e-mails raise issues of trust, security and the protection of confidential information and personal data, as documents and evidence become subject to cybercrime and other Internet abuses (such as the delivery to unintended recipients). It would thus be judicious to complete the new law with regulations providing for the creation of e-arbitration centres subject to specific obligations regarding the security of information systems and, more generally, the confidentiality of the procedure, which are currently matters governed by different laws and regulations that may not be suitable for arbitration proceedings conducted fully online, making the resulting awards unenforceable in Morocco. 2.3 Ability to bind Moroccan public entities Under the provisions of Article 311 CCP, Moroccan public companies could only be bound by an arbitration clause concluded in the form and conditions decided by their board of directors or management bodies, with the contracts containing such clauses also being subject to a special deliberation. As a result, many arbitration clauses were avoided by public companies that had themselves failed to meet legal conditions of validity (knowingly or not). Given that it can be considered “contrary to good faith for a public company, which has first dissimulated the existence of such rules of internal law, to invoke them later on, if it is in its interest in relation to a given dispute, to deny the validity of an undertaking made with full knowledge thereof”,13 ICC case No. 5103, [1988] J.D.I. p. 1209: “ Il serait contraire à la bonne foi qu’une entreprise publique, qui a dissimulé dans un premier temps l’existence de telles règles de droit interne, les invoque ultérieurement, si tel est son intérêt dans un litige déterminé, pour dénier la validité d’un engagement qu’elle a souscrit pourtant en parfaite connaissance de cause”. In that case, the arbitral tribunal upheld its jurisdiction over four Tunisian public companies that were party to a contract containing an arbitration clause that was challenged on the basis that Tunisian public companies cannot submit their disputes to arbitration. the decision of the Moroccan legislator to completely open up the possibility for public companies and all other public entities to enter into an arbitration agreement without any further conditions (Art. 17), thereby greatly reducing the risk of avoidance based on their failure to comply with their own “internal” obligations, is a very positive development for the choice and use of arbitration as a means to settle disputes with the Moroccan administration. 2.4 Redefining international arbitration One of the major changes brought about by the new Arbitration Code relates to the definition of international arbitration, which has been greatly simplified so as to provide that arbitration involving the “interests of international trade” where “at least one party is domiciled or based abroad” qualifies as “international arbitration” (Art. 72). By eliminating all other geographical requirements previously set out by Article 327-40 CCP,14 Article 327-40 CCP provided that “international arbitration” includes cases where (1) the parties have their places of business in different countries at the time the arbitration agreement is entered into and where (2) one of the following locations is outside the country where the parties have their places of business: (a) the seat of arbitration or (b) any location where a substantial part of the obligations arising from the commercial relationship is to be performed or the location with which the object of the dispute has the closest ties, or where (3) the parties have expressly agreed that the object of the arbitration agreement has ties with more than one country. the new Arbitration Code allows (i) for proceedings conducted in Morocco to qualify as international arbitration, but also (ii) for proceedings conducted abroad between two Moroccan residents to qualify as domestic arbitration and for the award to be treated as a domestic award. Overall, this should increase the appeal of Morocco as a seat for both types of proceedings. 2.5 Filing and enforcement requirements Under the former legal regime, the party seeking the recognition and enforcement (exequatur) of the arbitral award had to file the award within seven days from the date of its issue, which often proved difficult (if not outright impossible) whenever the award was not rendered in Arabic, as a full translation had to be secured from a sworn translator and produced with the original award within that same short timeframe. The Moroccan legislator did address the issue, by extending, through Article 55 of the new Arbitration Code, the time limit for the arbitral tribunal or a party to file the award with the arbitration agreement (or authenticated copies thereof) with the court from seven to 15 days from the date the decision is rendered, which, if these documents are in a foreign language, allows a bit more time for securing the sworn translation in Arabic (which must still be produced at the time of filing: cf. Art. 78), although, in some cases, that may remain difficult. It may have been more appropriate to clearly differentiate between the filing of the arbitral award with the court clerk’s office, an administrative procedure where it should be sufficient to present the award in its original language, and the exequatur procedure, which is subject to a court ruling that requires a sworn translation in Arabic. Although the former law did allow for the exequatur procedure to be conducted ex parte by the president of the competent court as a matter of urgency, the Moroccan jurisdictions had developed the practice of systematically calling the other party to participate in adversarial proceedings before deciding on the recognition and enforcement of the arbitral award, which considerably reduced the efficiency of the post-arbitral process. Whereas many practitioners had hoped for a return to the ex parte approach, the Arbitration Code has now confirmed the court practice by requiring that the order of the president of the competent court be rendered as a matter of urgency but only after the parties are duly called to appear (Art. 78), which has the merit of being a clear legal requirement.

sections.title: 3. OTHER RECENT REFORMS:
sections.text: 3. OTHER RECENT REFORMS: The enactment of the Arbitration Code is one aspect of a complete overhaul of Morocco’s legislation designed to attract more foreign direct investment. It supports other reforms that have affected almost all aspects of doing business in Morocco and that are too numerous to enumerate here. Yet, this commentary would not be complete if it failed to present, even summarily, the recent fundamental changes to the Moroccan investment and business environment, as part of the country’s admitted ambition to become a leading financial centre and new regional hub for international arbitration in the near future, which includes the adoption of a new Model BIT (section 3.1) and a new Investment Charter (section 3.2), the amendment of the CFC status (section 3.3) and the creation of a new international arbitration centre (section 3.4). 3.1 A new Model BIT In 2019, Morocco adopted a new model bilateral investment treaty (BIT),15 Kingdom of Morocco. (2019, June). Accord entre le Royaume du Maroc et … pour la promotion et la protection réciproques des investissements. in line with the latest policy recommendation and international trends,16 . El-Kady, M. and Rwananga, Y. (2020, June). Morocco’s New Model BIT: Innovative features and policy considerations. International Institute for Sustainable Development (IISD). [online]. including new definitions of “investment” (which must contribute to the sustainable development of the host country), “fair and equitable treatment” (the violation of which must result from a breach of specific obligations, such as a denial of justice, a fundamental breach of due process, discrimination on wrongful grounds or abusive treatment), “national treatment” and “MFN treatment” (which now subjects investors to the condition of being “in like circumstances”, with clear benchmark elements) and “expropriation” (so as to include, under certain conditions, indirect expropriation). It also includes an obligation, in case of a dispute, to attempt amicable settlement (with provisions that mirror those of Articles 87, 88 and 89 of the Arbitration Code), before initiating court or arbitration proceedings. In that last regard, the new Model BIT limits investor-state dispute settlement to disputes concerning a violation of the state’s treaty obligations only and provides for a three-year time bar. It also denies treaty-based dispute settlement mechanisms to investors who engage in corruption, money laundering or the financing of terrorism. 3.2 A new Investment Charter A new Investment Charter, published on 12 December 2022,17 Framework-Law No. 03-22 on the Investment Charter, promulgated by Dahir No. 1-22-76 of 9 December 2022 (Official Bulletin No. 7151 of 12 December 2022) (in Arabic). provides the conditions for investors to benefit from state support and guarantees, as part of the development of strategic investment projects, through the conclusion of an Investment Agreement with the Moroccan government, the threshold for which is set at MAD 50 million (about EUR 5 million), significantly lower than the original threshold of MAD 200 million (about EUR 20 million) set under the previous regime that dated back to 199518 Framework-Law No. 18-95 forming the Investment Charter, promulgated by Dahir No. 1-95-213 of 8 November 1995 (Official Bulletin No. 4336 of 6 December 1995). (which was eventually reduced). The new regime is also more comprehensive, as it provides support for the development of “Very Small, Small and Medium-size Businesses”, as well as for the development of Moroccan businesses abroad. 3.3 A revised CFC regime Casablanca Finance City (CFC) was created in 201019 Law 44-10 on the Status of Casablanca Finance City, promulgated by Dahir No. 1-10-196 of 11 February 2010 (Official Bulletin No. 6922 of 1 October 2010). to become a regional financial hub that offers a series of tax incentives to its members within the local and international community. Managed by the former Morocco Financial Board (MFB), rebranded as the Casablanca Finance City Authority (CFCA), it is currently ranked 54th in the Global Financial Centres Index (GFCI) and first on the African continent, just ahead of Cape Town (55th) and Johannesburg (56th).20 Z/Yen Partners. (2022, March). GFCI32. The CFC Status was reorganized in 202021 Decree-Law No. 2-20-665 of 30 September 2020 Reorganising “Casablanca Finance City”, ratified by Dahir No. 1-20-102 of 30 December 2020(Official Bulletin No. 7106 of 7 July 2022) and application Decree No. 2-20-841of 23 December 2020 (Official Bulletin No. 6950 of 7 January 2021). to extend incentives to six new activities (Investment Companies, Collective Placement Undertakings, Investment Advisors, Crowdfunding Companies/Platforms, Trading Companies and Regional Offices) while also reinforcing the authority of the CFCA. 3.4 A new international arbitration centre Besides the Moroccan Arbitration Centre (CMA) of ICC Morocco, which recently celebrated its 25th anniversary and essentially handles domestic arbitration cases, the creation, in 2016, of the Casablanca International Mediation and Arbitration Centre (CIMAC), which aims to become “the first arbitration centre on the African continent with a truly international and independent governing body”, was intended “to meet the demands of the international business community for a neutral, impartial, efficient and reliable dispute resolution institution in Africa, and beyond”.22 CIMAC. History. (www.cimac.org). Closely tied to Casablanca Finance City, CIMAC is poised to become the go-to arbitration institution for CFC members and their clients before extending its reach to the region and the African continent.

sections.title: CONCLUSION:
sections.text: CONCLUSION: Although the new Arbitration Code does not constitute a reform of the century-old practice of arbitration in Morocco as profound as the one that had resulted from the adoption of Law No. 08-05, the new Arbitration Code, despite leaving room for more improvement, still provides for a more modern approach by putting fewer conditions on the validity of the arbitration agreement, allowing for a wider use of new communication technologies, binding all public entities that submit their disputes to arbitration without other conditions, and removing geographical conditions for international arbitration. As part of a wider set of reforms and new structures, it should contribute to the development of an investment and business environment that meets international standards and expectations and that is anchored in sustainable development so as to help Morocco achieve its ambition to become a leading financial centre and new regional hub for international arbitration. These are all small steps in the right direction, promoting the giant leap forward that Morocco is making to be at the forefront of the economic development of the region and the rest of Africa, a position that is today, maybe more than ever, within its grasp.

As part of the enforcement proceedings regarding a 2011 ICC arbitral award that held Ynna Holding jointly liable with its subsidiary (Ynna Asment) to pay damages to Fives FCB for abusive termination of a contract (containing an arbitration clause) to which it is not a party, the Casablanca Commercial Court refused to enforce the award against Ynna Holding, a decision that was overturned by the Commercial Court of Appeal in 2015. The Cour de Cassation, while clearly insisting on the exceptional nature of such an extension, decided to invalidate the appellate court’s decision for lack of reasoning and to remit the matter to it for a new decision on this issue. albeit on an exceptional and sufficiently reasoned basis only. A more arbitration-friendly approach would have been to allow for any nationally-certified authentication system to be used by the parties. 2.2 A step towards e-arbitration Still, and although the conduct of arbitration exclusively by digital means11 For more developments on e-arbitration as part of Online Dispute Resolution (ODR), see: Amro, Ihab. 2019. Online Arbitration in Theory and in Practice: A Comparative Study of Cross-Border Commercial Transactions in Common Law and Civil Law Countries. Cambridge Scholars Publishing. is not yet provided for, the new law has introduced the use of information and communication technology at various steps of the arbitration procedure, from the conclusion of the arbitration agreement (see above) to the delivery of the arbitral award. Indeed, the Arbitration Code allows for the electronic exchange of motions and briefs between the parties and the arbitral tribunal, the holding of meetings and hearings by means of teleconference and the signature and delivery of the arbitral award by electronic means (cf. Arts. 35 and 51) but, for the time being, only within the framework of arbitration proceedings conducted in Morocco, where the use of information technology in arbitration should further decrease the time required to resolve commercial disputes brought before the arbitrator(s), as one of the main incentives for economic actors to submit their disputes to arbitration rather than to the jurisdiction of national courts.12 The other main incentives are as follows: the confidentiality of the proceedings and the freedom of the parties and/or arbitrator(s) to choose the language in which the proceedings will be conducted. In Morocco, the total cost of arbitration, whether institutional or ad hoc, is often much higher than that of court proceedings and, as such, is not an argument often used in favour of arbitration. However, the dematerialization of arbitration proceedings and the use of e-mails raise issues of trust, security and the protection of confidential information and personal data, as documents and evidence become subject to cybercrime and other Internet abuses (such as the delivery to unintended recipients). It would thus be judicious to complete the new law with regulations providing for the creation of e-arbitration centres subject to specific obligations regarding the security of information systems and, more generally, the confidentiality of the procedure, which are currently matters governed by different laws and regulations that may not be suitable for arbitration proceedings conducted fully online, making the resulting awards unenforceable in Morocco. 2.3 Ability to bind Moroccan public entities Under the provisions of Article 311 CCP, Moroccan public companies could only be bound by an arbitration clause concluded in the form and conditions decided by their board of directors or management bodies, with the contracts containing such clauses also being subject to a special deliberation. As a result, many arbitration clauses were avoided by public companies that had themselves failed to meet legal conditions of validity (knowingly or not). Given that it can be considered “contrary to good faith for a public company, which has first dissimulated the existence of such rules of internal law, to invoke them later on, if it is in its interest in relation to a given dispute, to deny the validity of an undertaking made with full knowledge thereof”,13 ICC case No. 5103, [1988] J.D.I. p. 1209: “ Il serait contraire à la bonne foi qu’une entreprise publique, qui a dissimulé dans un premier temps l’existence de telles règles de droit interne, les invoque ultérieurement, si tel est son intérêt dans un litige déterminé, pour dénier la validité d’un engagement qu’elle a souscrit pourtant en parfaite connaissance de cause”. In that case, the arbitral tribunal upheld its jurisdiction over four Tunisian public companies that were party to a contract containing an arbitration clause that was challenged on the basis that Tunisian public companies cannot submit their disputes to arbitration. the decision of the Moroccan legislator to completely open up the possibility for public companies and all other public entities to enter into an arbitration agreement without any further conditions (Art. 17), thereby greatly reducing the risk of avoidance based on their failure to comply with their own “internal” obligations, is a very positive development for the choice and use of arbitration as a means to settle disputes with the Moroccan administration. 2.4 Redefining international arbitration One of the major changes brought about by the new Arbitration Code relates to the definition of international arbitration, which has been greatly simplified so as to provide that arbitration involving the “interests of international trade” where “at least one party is domiciled or based abroad” qualifies as “international arbitration” (Art. 72). By eliminating all other geographical requirements previously set out by Article 327-40 CCP,14 Article 327-40 CCP provided that “international arbitration” includes cases where (1) the parties have their places of business in different countries at the time the arbitration agreement is entered into and where (2) one of the following locations is outside the country where the parties have their places of business: (a) the seat of arbitration or (b) any location where a substantial part of the obligations arising from the commercial relationship is to be performed or the location with which the object of the dispute has the closest ties, or where (3) the parties have expressly agreed that the object of the arbitration agreement has ties with more than one country. the new Arbitration Code allows (i) for proceedings conducted in Morocco to qualify as international arbitration, but also (ii) for proceedings conducted abroad between two Moroccan residents to qualify as domestic arbitration and for the award to be treated as a domestic award. Overall, this should increase the appeal of Morocco as a seat for both types of proceedings. 2.5 Filing and enforcement requirements Under the former legal regime, the party seeking the recognition and enforcement (exequatur) of the arbitral award had to file the award within seven days from the date of its issue, which often proved difficult (if not outright impossible) whenever the award was not rendered in Arabic, as a full translation had to be secured from a sworn translator and produced with the original award within that same short timeframe. The Moroccan legislator did address the issue, by extending, through Article 55 of the new Arbitration Code, the time limit for the arbitral tribunal or a party to file the award with the arbitration agreement (or authenticated copies thereof) with the court from seven to 15 days from the date the decision is rendered, which, if these documents are in a foreign language, allows a bit more time for securing the sworn translation in Arabic (which must still be produced at the time of filing: cf. Art. 78), although, in some cases, that may remain difficult. It may have been more appropriate to clearly differentiate between the filing of the arbitral award with the court clerk’s office, an administrative procedure where it should be sufficient to present the award in its original language, and the exequatur procedure, which is subject to a court ruling that requires a sworn translation in Arabic. Although the former law did allow for the exequatur procedure to be conducted ex parte by the president of the competent court as a matter of urgency, the Moroccan jurisdictions had developed the practice of systematically calling the other party to participate in adversarial proceedings before deciding on the recognition and enforcement of the arbitral award, which considerably reduced the efficiency of the post-arbitral process. Whereas many practitioners had hoped for a return to the ex parte approach, the Arbitration Code has now confirmed the court practice by requiring that the order of the president of the competent court be rendered as a matter of urgency but only after the parties are duly called to appear (Art. 78), which has the merit of being a clear legal requirement.

3. OTHER RECENT REFORMS: The enactment of the Arbitration Code is one aspect of a complete overhaul of Morocco’s legislation designed to attract more foreign direct investment. It supports other reforms that have affected almost all aspects of doing business in Morocco and that are too numerous to enumerate here. Yet, this commentary would not be complete if it failed to present, even summarily, the recent fundamental changes to the Moroccan investment and business environment, as part of the country’s admitted ambition to become a leading financial centre and new regional hub for international arbitration in the near future, which includes the adoption of a new Model BIT (section 3.1) and a new Investment Charter (section 3.2), the amendment of the CFC status (section 3.3) and the creation of a new international arbitration centre (section 3.4). 3.1 A new Model BIT In 2019, Morocco adopted a new model bilateral investment treaty (BIT),15 Kingdom of Morocco. (2019, June). Accord entre le Royaume du Maroc et … pour la promotion et la protection réciproques des investissements. in line with the latest policy recommendation and international trends,16 . El-Kady, M. and Rwananga, Y. (2020, June). Morocco’s New Model BIT: Innovative features and policy considerations. International Institute for Sustainable Development (IISD). [online]. including new definitions of “investment” (which must contribute to the sustainable development of the host country), “fair and equitable treatment” (the violation of which must result from a breach of specific obligations, such as a denial of justice, a fundamental breach of due process, discrimination on wrongful grounds or abusive treatment), “national treatment” and “MFN treatment” (which now subjects investors to the condition of being “in like circumstances”, with clear benchmark elements) and “expropriation” (so as to include, under certain conditions, indirect expropriation). It also includes an obligation, in case of a dispute, to attempt amicable settlement (with provisions that mirror those of Articles 87, 88 and 89 of the Arbitration Code), before initiating court or arbitration proceedings. In that last regard, the new Model BIT limits investor-state dispute settlement to disputes concerning a violation of the state’s treaty obligations only and provides for a three-year time bar. It also denies treaty-based dispute settlement mechanisms to investors who engage in corruption, money laundering or the financing of terrorism. 3.2 A new Investment Charter A new Investment Charter, published on 12 December 2022,17 Framework-Law No. 03-22 on the Investment Charter, promulgated by Dahir No. 1-22-76 of 9 December 2022 (Official Bulletin No. 7151 of 12 December 2022) (in Arabic). provides the conditions for investors to benefit from state support and guarantees, as part of the development of strategic investment projects, through the conclusion of an Investment Agreement with the Moroccan government, the threshold for which is set at MAD 50 million (about EUR 5 million), significantly lower than the original threshold of MAD 200 million (about EUR 20 million) set under the previous regime that dated back to 199518 Framework-Law No. 18-95 forming the Investment Charter, promulgated by Dahir No. 1-95-213 of 8 November 1995 (Official Bulletin No. 4336 of 6 December 1995). (which was eventually reduced). The new regime is also more comprehensive, as it provides support for the development of “Very Small, Small and Medium-size Businesses”, as well as for the development of Moroccan businesses abroad. 3.3 A revised CFC regime Casablanca Finance City (CFC) was created in 201019 Law 44-10 on the Status of Casablanca Finance City, promulgated by Dahir No. 1-10-196 of 11 February 2010 (Official Bulletin No. 6922 of 1 October 2010). to become a regional financial hub that offers a series of tax incentives to its members within the local and international community. Managed by the former Morocco Financial Board (MFB), rebranded as the Casablanca Finance City Authority (CFCA), it is currently ranked 54th in the Global Financial Centres Index (GFCI) and first on the African continent, just ahead of Cape Town (55th) and Johannesburg (56th).20 Z/Yen Partners. (2022, March). GFCI32. The CFC Status was reorganized in 202021 Decree-Law No. 2-20-665 of 30 September 2020 Reorganising “Casablanca Finance City”, ratified by Dahir No. 1-20-102 of 30 December 2020(Official Bulletin No. 7106 of 7 July 2022) and application Decree No. 2-20-841of 23 December 2020 (Official Bulletin No. 6950 of 7 January 2021). to extend incentives to six new activities (Investment Companies, Collective Placement Undertakings, Investment Advisors, Crowdfunding Companies/Platforms, Trading Companies and Regional Offices) while also reinforcing the authority of the CFCA. 3.4 A new international arbitration centre Besides the Moroccan Arbitration Centre (CMA) of ICC Morocco, which recently celebrated its 25th anniversary and essentially handles domestic arbitration cases, the creation, in 2016, of the Casablanca International Mediation and Arbitration Centre (CIMAC), which aims to become “the first arbitration centre on the African continent with a truly international and independent governing body”, was intended “to meet the demands of the international business community for a neutral, impartial, efficient and reliable dispute resolution institution in Africa, and beyond”.22 CIMAC. History. (www.cimac.org). Closely tied to Casablanca Finance City, CIMAC is poised to become the go-to arbitration institution for CFC members and their clients before extending its reach to the region and the African continent.

CONCLUSION: Although the new Arbitration Code does not constitute a reform of the century-old practice of arbitration in Morocco as profound as the one that had resulted from the adoption of Law No. 08-05, the new Arbitration Code, despite leaving room for more improvement, still provides for a more modern approach by putting fewer conditions on the validity of the arbitration agreement, allowing for a wider use of new communication technologies, binding all public entities that submit their disputes to arbitration without other conditions, and removing geographical conditions for international arbitration. As part of a wider set of reforms and new structures, it should contribute to the development of an investment and business environment that meets international standards and expectations and that is anchored in sustainable development so as to help Morocco achieve its ambition to become a leading financial centre and new regional hub for international arbitration. These are all small steps in the right direction, promoting the giant leap forward that Morocco is making to be at the forefront of the economic development of the region and the rest of Africa, a position that is today, maybe more than ever, within its grasp.

title: Chapter 5: Signs of a Subjective Approach to Treaty Interpretation in Investment Arbitration: A Justified Divergence from the VCLT?
container_title: The Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties in Investor-State Disputes: History, Evolution and Future
copyright: Kluwer Law International

sections.title: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: §5.01 INTRODUCTION As its now most popular name itself suggests, investment treaty arbitration presents an amalgam of arbitration and treaty law in the context of investments. It merges two quite different legal traditions, its arbitration aspect being dominated by the practices of the much larger field of international commercial arbitration and its treaty aspect drawing on principles at the core of the sui generis system of law between States known as public international law. This dual nature creates difficulty for lawyers of both types. International commercial arbitration practitioners and scholars tackling problems of investment treaty arbitration often need to shift their private-law dominated mindset to address the substantive legal issues of an overwhelmingly public-law nature that are presented by investment disputes. For example, rules relating to the attribution of acts and omissions to States as a matter of State responsibility have been extensively developed by public international lawyers primarily in the factual context of armed conflicts,1 See, e.g., ILC ARSIWA 2001 with commentaries in Yearbook 2001, vol. II, part 2 (United Nations, 2001) 47-49 and the references cited therein. and present a legal logic that is almost completely foreign to the many commercial lawyers operating in the international arbitration space. Public international lawyers are, for their part, forced to reckon with not only unfamiliar procedures drawn from contract-based arbitration and domestic commercial law traditions, but also the impacts of the international commercial arbitration mindset on the practice – and therefore development – of the investment law sub-field and even on the field of public international law itself. The present book, by offering those interested in international arbitration a range of insights into the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties (VCLT) can itself be considered an example of the modern-day literature that helps international commercial arbitration practitioners come to terms with the practices, concepts and principles of public international law. Relatively less attention has been paid to how the practices, concepts and principles of international commercial arbitration have affected the field of public international law. This is itself somewhat surprising. Approximately twenty years ago, the hottest topic in international law was ‘fragmentation’, the fear that the well-documented proliferation of differently structured international courts and tribunals across functionally distinct sub-fields of international law would fracture the core of the discipline.2 See, e.g., Georges Abi-Saab, ‘Fragmentation or unification: Some Concluding Remarks’ (1998) 31 NYU Journal of International Law and Politics 919; PS Rao, ‘Multiple International Judicial Forums: A Reflection of the Growing Strength of International Law or Its Fragmentation’ (2003) 25 Mich. J. Int’l L. 929; Tullio Treves, ‘Judicial Lawmaking in an Era of “Proliferation” of International Courts and Tribunals: Development or Fragmentation of International Law?’ in Rüdiger Wolfrum (ed.), Developments of International Law in Treaty Making (Berlin, Springer, 2005). One might have expected international lawyers to look into every corner of international law – and especially at the particular practices of every type of international court or tribunal. There has been enough of this kind of work for Shaffer and Ginsburg to cite it as part of what they call an ‘empirical turn’ in international legal scholarship.3 Gregory Shaffer and Tom Ginsburg, ‘The Empirical Turn in International Legal Scholarship’ (2012) 106 The American Journal of International Law 1, 11-19, in particular. However, the level of attention that the public international law literature afforded the fragmentation issue itself has, after reaching its zenith around the time of the International Law Commission’s (ILC’s) fragmentation report of 2006,4 International Law Commission and Martii Koskenniemi, ‘Fragmentation of International Law: Difficulties Arising from Diversification and Expansion of International Law’ (United Nations, 2006) A/CN.4/L.682. substantially declined in recent years. This is perhaps due to a greater acceptance that public international law could not be – and did not need to be – a unified and homogenous legal system and should instead celebrate the diversity that comes with its continued growth. Alternatively, it may result from a recognition that a comprehensive and non-superficial assessment of the issue of fragmentation was – in light of the quantity of international law being generated by States and international courts and tribunals – simply too large an undertaking to be carried out in any meaningful way. None of this implies, however, that the risks that initially prompted concern about the fragmentation of international law have ceased to exist. What are often called ‘cross-cutting’ areas of international law, including State responsibility law and treaty law, remain vulnerable to being interpreted and applied differently by differently structured and staffed international courts and tribunals operating in disparate factual, legal and procedural contexts.5 See, e.g., Philippe Sands, ‘Treaty, Custom and the Cross-Fertilization of International Law’ (1998) 1 Yale Hum. Rts. & Dev. LJ 85, 92. Indeed, while the use of the ‘fragmentation’ buzzword may have declined, comparative approaches to the study of the different sub-systems of international law have become increasingly prevalent. Residual fears surrounding whether there is a problematic fragmentation of public international law – or any fragmentation in the first place – are being partially addressed by comparative analyses focused on specific cross-cutting areas, including treaty law, that seek to tease out the similarities and differences between the different branches of contemporary public international law. Since international commercial arbitration is a form of dispute settlement operating outside the bounds of public international law, hardly any attention has been paid to how its practices, concepts and principles may affect international law. This seems to disavow the demonstrable fact that practitioners who are primarily international commercial arbitration lawyers play an important role in a sub-field of public international law, investment law, including as regards issues including treaty interpretation, which is not only a feature of investment arbitration but of all fields of international law. The present contribution seeks to fill part of that gap by examining one respect in which an international commercial arbitration mindset may have caused the practice of investment tribunals to diverge from the practice of other international courts and tribunals interpreting and applying the VCLT. It argues that the objective approach to treaty interpretation mandated by VCLT Articles 31 through 33 is, at least in the context of consent to jurisdiction, not being as strictly respected by investment tribunals as it is by other international courts and tribunals and, further, that this divergence is a product of the particular features of the forum in which issues of investment law are now examined: the world of international arbitration. This chapter begins by explaining the difference between the subjective and objective approaches to the interpretation of legal texts in general (section 5.02) and then describes how, in relation to treaty interpretation specifically, there has been a shift from a historically subjective approach to the VCLT’s objective approach (section 5.03). It then notes what appears to be a re-emergence of the subjective approach to treaty interpretation in a specific part of the case law of investment arbitration tribunals (section 5.04), before concluding with a consideration of some possible explanations of this apparent divergence from general international law (section 5.05).

sections.title: SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE APPROACHES TO THE INTERPRETATION OF LEGAL TEXTS
sections.text: §5.02 SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE APPROACHES TO THE INTERPRETATION OF LEGAL TEXTS In broad terms, the subjective approach to the interpretation of legal texts considers the meaning of a term to be that which it possessed in the mind of the person uttering it and focuses on the intentions of the drafter(s) of the text. The subjective approach can be contrasted with what is usually called the objective – but could also be appropriately termed the intersubjective6 At least in so far as these terms are understood in the senses used by philosophical disciplines including epistemology and phenomenology, the type of meaning that legal interpretation seeks to identify is ‘intersubjective’ rather than ‘objective’ in the sense that it constitutes an understanding that is shared among many people, rather than a truth that is independent of the views of any one or more individuals. – approach to interpretation, pursuant to which the meaning of a term is that which is disclosed by the text of the instrument alone without any separate reference to what its drafters intended it to say. Whereas the subjective approach effectively emphasises the intentions of the writer, the objective approach effectively emphasises the understanding of the reader. The distinction between subjective and objective approaches to interpretation is particularly important in the context of comparative contract law. In a jurisdiction favouring a subjective approach to contract interpretation, such as Switzerland, the aim of contract interpretation is to give terms the meaning that concurs with the ‘tatsächlicher Parteiwille’, the actual intention of the parties.7 See, e.g., 140 III 134 (Swiss Federal Supreme Court, 27 February 2014) [3.2] (present author’s translation). The Swiss Code of Obligations even decrees that ‘the true and common intention of the parties must be ascertained without dwelling on any inexact expressions or designations they may have used’ in their agreement.8 Swiss Federal Act on the Amendment of the Civil Code (Part Five: The Code of Obligations) 1911, Art. 18(1). The text of a contract is examined in order to infer a subjective fact, the otherwise obscure intention of the parties.9 See, e.g., 130 III 66 (Swiss Federal Supreme Court, 21 November 2003) [3.2]. By contrast, in a jurisdiction favouring objective (or intersubjective) contract interpretation, such as Australia, ‘it is not the subjective beliefs or understandings of the parties about their rights and liabilities that govern their contractual relations’,10 Toll (FGCT) Pty Ltd v. Alphapharm Pty Ltd (2004) [2004] HCA 52 (High Court of Australia). but rather ‘what a reasonable person having all the background knowledge of the “surrounding circumstances” available to the parties would have understood’ from ‘the language in the contract’.11 Byrnes v. Kendle (2011) [2011] HCA [98]. In such systems, evidence of the parties’ statements reflecting their actual intentions is inadmissible and other evidence extrinsic to the text of the contract is only admitted where it is necessary to help determine an intersubjective or objective fact, namely ‘what a reasonable person would understand by the language in which the parties have expressed their agreement’.12 Mount Bruce Mining Pty Limited v. Wright Prospecting Pty Limited (2015) [2015] HCA 37 (High Court of Australia); Toll (FGCT) Pty Ltd v. Alphapharm Pty Ltd (n 10) para. 40. As these examples suggest, civil law jurisdictions are generally considered to favour a subjective approach and common law jurisdictions an objective approach. The reality is much more nuanced. Some prominent civil law jurisdictions introduce objective elements such as the Empfängerhorizont that German law uses to ensure that the intention feeding into the interpretative process is that which a reasonable addressee of the relevant declaration would understand the intention to be in the circumstances,13 See: Otto Palandt and Gerd Brudermüller, Palandt Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch: mit Nebengesetzen insbesondere mit Einführungsgesetz (München, C H Beck, 2017) 124-125 (§133, paras 9, 14); see also: Basil Markesinis and Others, The German Law of Contract: A Comparative Treatise (Oxford, Hart Publishing, 2006) 288. or the principe de la confiance which allows a Court, in certain circumstances, to impute to a party the objective meaning of his or her declaration even if it does not correspond with his or her internal will.14 136 III 186 (Swiss Federal Supreme Court, 6 April 2010) s. 3.2.1. Equally, modern-day contract law in the United States (US), by pursuing interpretations that maximally accord with the contracting parties’ purposes, will frequently allow subjectively held common intentions to prevail over the more reasonable objective meaning of a contractual term.15 See, e.g., Melvin A Eisenberg, Foundational Principles of Contract Law (Oxford University Press, 2018) 398-406; Robert Braucher E Allan Farnsworth and American Law Institute, Restatement of the Law, Second: Contracts 2d, Revised and enlarged (St. Paul, Minn., American Law Institute Publishers, 1981) s. 201. What is critical for present purposes, however, is that a wide variety of interpretative approaches, ranging from the highly subjective to the highly objective, is present in the world’s different systems of contract law and thereby inform the mindsets of the many commercial disputes lawyers participating as advocates and adjudicators in international commercial arbitration and investment treaty arbitration proceedings.

sections.title: SHIFT FROM THE SUBJECTIVE TO THE OBJECTIVE APPROACH IN THE INTERPRETATION OF TREATIES
sections.text: §5.03 SHIFT FROM THE SUBJECTIVE TO THE OBJECTIVE APPROACH IN THE INTERPRETATION OF TREATIES The modern-day international law of treaty interpretation has its origins in the work of glossators in the Middle Ages who reinstated Roman principles for judicial interpretation, including those in Justinian’s corpus juris civilis of 530 AD. In the present context, it is important to observe that the glossators’ body of work was ‘intent on developing a general theory of interpretation for legal instruments’ of all kinds.16 Roger Simonds, Rational Individualism: The Perennial Philosophy of Legal Interpretation (Amsterdam, Rodopi, 1995) 70. The glossators accordingly privileged the subjective approach to legal interpretation that was overwhelmingly favoured for the interpretation of common Roman instruments including commercial contracts and wills. The glossa magna of Francis Accursius, for example, drew on the Digest’s section on the particular issue of legacies to state a general interpretative maxim which appears to allow for a purely subjective interpretation of all legal instruments, even where there is no textual ambiguity:Non aliter a significatione verborum recedi oportet, quam cum manifestum est aliud sensisse testatorem. // (Marcellus, Opinions) It is not proper to depart from the literal meaning of the words except when it is manifest that the testator had something else in mind.17 Justinian, Omini nostri sacratissimi principis iustiniani iuris enucleati ex omni vetere iure collecti: digestorum seu pandectarum (530) Book XXXII, No. 69pr (Marcellus l.S. resp.); English translation from: Simonds (n 16) 70. The founders of international law who followed in the Western European footsteps of the glossators, while on many occasions specifically declaring principles for the interpretation of treaties, also invoked interpretative rules that had been formulated in relation to a wider range of instruments and were therefore overwhelmingly subjective. Vattel, for example, considered it to be an ‘unerring rule’ that ‘[w]hen we evidently see what is the sense that agrees with the intention of the contracting parties, it is not allowable to wrest their words to a contrary meaning’ and declared that ‘[t]he intention, sufficiently known, furnishes the true matter of the convention, – what is promised and accepted, demanded and granted’.18 Emer de Vattel, The Law of Nations 896, Book II, §274. In its formative years, international law explicitly followed an intention-based, subjective approach to the interpretation of treaties. In the nineteenth century, Francis Lieber’s influential Legal and Political Hermeneutics specifically stated that the ordinary meaning of a term is intrinsically related to how the drafter – not the reader – understood that term when he or she committed it to writing, saying ‘we are to take the words in that meaning which we may faithfully believe their utterer attached to them’.19 Francis Lieber, Legal and Political Hermeneutics or Principles of Interpretation and Construction in Law and Politics with Remarks on Precedents and Authorities (Boston, Charles C Little and James Brown, 1839) 102 (Chapter IV, s. IX). Right up until the Second World War, the most frequently cited authors on treaty interpretation overwhelmingly favoured an approach which, in the words used by Ehrlich’s Hague Course of 1928, followed the principle of seeking ‘la véritable volonté des parties’ (the true will of the parties) by all useful means.20 Ludwik Ehrlich, ‘L’interprétation des traités’ (1928) 24 Collected Courses of the Hague Academy of International Law 1, 126. Prominent decisions of international courts and tribunals dating from the first decades of the twentieth century confirmed the subjective approach to treaty interpretation. For example, in the Permanent Court of Arbitration’s seminal 1910 award in the North Atlantic Coast Fisheries Case (Great Britain, United States), the Tribunal had no qualms in rejecting one of the US’ interpretative arguments on the basis that a practical distinction between the terms ‘coasts’ and ‘bays’ had ‘been shown from the correspondence and the documents in evidence […] to have been in all probability present to the minds of the negotiators of the [relevant t]reaty’.21 The North Atlantic Coast Fisheries Case (Great Britain, United States), Award (1910) XI RIAA/RSA 167, 198. When efforts to codify international law became much more prominent in the aftermath of the Second World War, an early set of draft articles on the interpretation of treaties produced by Hersch Lauterpacht as a Special Rapporteur for the Institut de droit international (IDI) continued to favour a strongly subjective approach. Article 5 of those draft articles stated explicitly that ‘the intention of the parties is a decisive element in treaty interpretation’ and draft Article 2 specifically identified resort to the preparatory work of a treaty in order to establish the intention of the parties.22 See Institut de droit international, ‘De l’interprétation des traités’, Annuaire, vol. 46 (1956) 318-319 (present author’s translation). Yet Lauterpacht’s draft articles on treaty interpretation met vehement resistance as soon as they were first proposed, particularly at a 1952 meeting of the IDI in Sien(n)a, where various luminaries of international law took issue with his overtly subjective approach and particularly the emphasis on the importance of a treaty’s travaux préparatoires for the purposes of its interpretation.23 Ibid., 320-321. Upon taking over from Lauterpacht as the IDI’s Special Rapporteur on treaty interpretation, Gerald Fitzmaurice chartered a new and much less subjective path that emphasised the role of the text and downgraded the role of the travaux préparatoires from being the primary source of reference in all interpretative disputes to being one of three ‘other means of interpretation’ to be used where the interpreter considers the use of such means necessary.24 Ibid., 318-325, 330-332. Treaty law had, of course, been listed among the fourteen topics for codification at the first session of the United Nations’ (UN) ILC in 1949,25 International Law Commission, ‘Yearbook 1949, vol I’ (New York, United Nations, 1956) 53 (6th meeting – 20 April 1949). and the interpretation of treaties fell within the scope of that codification project. When, in 1962, the Commission instructed the fourth special rapporteur on the law of treaties, Humphrey Waldock, to ‘prepare draft articles on the law of treaties intended to serve as the basis for a convention’, not a code, and to ‘cover the whole subject in two years’,26 UN Document A/4843 quoted in Humphrey Waldock’s first report on the law of treaties: UN. International Law Commission, Yearbook of the International Law Commission (ILC YB 1962v1) (United Nations, 1962) vol. 2, 29. none of his predecessors had yet managed to formulate draft articles on treaty interpretation. Waldock had clearly been heavily influenced by the views on treaty interpretation belonging to his compatriot and predecessor in the role, Fitzmaurice, and put together draft articles that not only presented a much more objective than subjective approach but even relegated any express mention of party intention to a secondary status.27 See: International Law Commission, ‘Yearbook 1964, vol II’ (New York, United Nations, 1965) 52-54. Indeed, the term ‘intention’ did not even itself appear in the ‘General Rule of Interpretation’ in his draft Article 70 that largely became VCLT Article 31 paragraphs 1, 2 and 3. This shift from a subjective to an objective approach initially encountered foreseeable resistance from partisans of the subjective school in the ILC’s Law of Treaties Study Group, with many seeking a reintegration of elements of the so-called école subjectiviste, including primary reference to a treaty’s preparatory work. Abdul Hakim Tabibi specifically stated that he would give greater weight to the intention of the parties – as had been the opinion of Hersch Lauterpacht – and, with the support of Shabtai Rosenne,28 International Law Commission, ‘Yearbook 1964, vol I’ (New York; Geneva, United Nations, 1965) 278 (per Rosenne, 765th meeting – 14 July 1964, para. 40). sought to elevate the importance of intention by combining the merely subsidiary means of interpretation placed in draft Article 71 (what became VCLT Article 32) into the set of primary means placed in draft Article 70 (what became VCLT Article 31).29 Ibid., 276 (per Tabibi, 765th meeting – 14 July 1964, para. 25). Milan Bartos went so far as to associate the draft articles’ preference for an objective and textual approach with a specifically ‘English school of legal thought, that interpretation meant interpretation of the text rather than of the spirit of a treaty’ and argued forcefully for a more subjective approach saying that ‘[w]here interpretation was concerned, the autonomy of the will of the parties was paramount’ and ‘[w]hat the parties had intended was more important than what they had actually said in the treaty’.30 Ibid., 279 (per Bartos, 765th meeting – 14 July 1964, para. 64). At the first session of the UN Conference on the Law of Treaties in 1968, the apparent rejection of the subjective approach to treaty interpretation came under some quite vociferous attack. Introducing an amendment specifically targeted at combining what became VCLT Article 32 into what became VCLT Article 31, Myres McDougal of the US railed against a ‘textual approach to interpretation [that] was impossible to apply’ and had, at least according to his reading of its accompanying commentary, ‘flatly rejected […] common intent as the goal of interpretation’.31 UN Conference on the Law of Treaties, ‘Official Records, First Session, Vienna’ (Vienna, United Nations, 1968) 167 (Thirty-first meeting – 19 April 1968, per McDougal [United States of America], paras 43 and 39 respectively). McDougal’s proposition attracted some support from other delegates. The Greek delegation offered its support to the amendment explicitly,32 Ibid., 172 (per Mr Krispis [Greece], Thirty-second meeting – 20 April 1968, para. 9). as did the Ghanaian delegation, whose representative followed McDougal’s criticisms of the textual approach with the statement that ‘[t]he crucial point in the function of interpretation was to ascertain the intention of the parties with regard to a particular problem’.33 Ibid., 170-171 (per Mr Dadzié [Ghana], Thirty-first meeting – 19 April 1968, para. 70). The Ukrainian delegate, Nikolai Petrovich Makarevich, considered that while the ILC ‘had rightly rejected proposals whereby a treaty might be interpreted exclusively in connexion with the intention of the parties’, ‘[i]t had, however, gone to the other extreme in deciding that the interpretation should be based exclusively on the terms of the treaty in their context and in the light of its object and purpose’.34 Ibid., 168 (per Mr Makarevich [Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic], Thirty-first meeting – 19 April 1968, para. 54). However, McDougal’s proposal was also roundly criticised and, on 22 April 1968, was ultimately rejected by sixty-six votes to eight with ten abstentions.35 Ibid., 185 (Thirty-third meeting – 22 April 1968, para. 75). As I have explained elsewhere,36 Julian Wyatt, Intertemporal Linguistics in International Law (Oxford, Hart, 2019) 203-204. the Vienna Conference – and therefore the VCLT – did not reject the importance of party intention tout court, but of a subjective understanding of that intention which would effectively permit the actionable meaning of a treaty term to be determined by the internal thoughts of certain drafters that were not disclosed anywhere other than in the preparatory work buried in the vaults of a handful of powerful governments. It is undoubtedly significant that the rejection of a subjective approach to the interpretation of treaties ultimately occurred in April 1968, right in the middle of the most politically charged and fast-changing peacetime period in the life of the international community when international institutions – and public international law – were adjusting to (i) the impact of the wave of decolonisation then approaching its zenith; (ii) the social and civil rights movements sweeping through the Western world at that time; and (iii) the fall-out from the International Court of Justice’s (ICJ’s) Southwest Africa (Ethiopia v. South Africa; Liberia v. South Africa) (Second Phase) Judgment (1966).37 South West Africa Cases (Ethiopia v. South Africa; Liberia v. South Africa) (Second Phase), Judgment [1966] ICJ Rep. 6; See also, e.g., Rosalyn Higgins, ‘The International Court and South West Africa: The Implications of the Judgment’ (October 1966) 42 International Affairs 573; Georges Abi-Saab, Cours général de droit international public (The Hague, Martinus Nijhoff, 1996) 395-402. Newly independent States could not be expected to respect international treaties drafted by their prior colonial masters or to accede to key multilateral treaties drafted before they had a seat at the table if the meaning of the terms of those treaties could be determined by subjective intentions of which the newly independent States often had no knowledge. In this context, as Eduardo Jiménez de Aréchaga of Uruguay pointed out in a strident criticism of McDougal’s proposal at the Vienna Conference, the ‘supposed real will of the parties’ could not be permitted to ‘float like a cloud over the terra firma of a contractual text’.38 Vienna Conference Records, 1968 Session (n 31) 170 (per Jiménez de Aréchaga [Uruguay], Thirty-first meeting – 19 April 1968, para. 63). The result in the VCLT’s rules for treaty interpretation is, of course, limited reference to both intention and the preparatory work, with the former only decisive if it exceptionally proves the existence of a special meaning and recourse to the latter only permitted when interpretation according to the objective elements outlined in Article 31 leaves the meaning ambiguous or obscure or leads to a result which is manifestly absurd or unreasonable.39 VCLT, Arts 31 and 32. The VCLT rules for the interpretation of treaties, which are focused on an objective approach to interpretation, have no clear competitor, are now widely regarded as reflecting customary international law and are widely invoked before international courts and tribunals of all types. For the interpretation of treaties, the objective approach evidently prevailed over the subjective approach.

sections.title: SIGNS OF A RE-EMERGENCE OF THE SUBJECTIVE APPROACH IN INVESTMENT ARBITRATION’S ASSESSMENT OF CONSENT TO JURISDICTION
sections.text: §5.04 SIGNS OF A RE-EMERGENCE OF THE SUBJECTIVE APPROACH IN INVESTMENT ARBITRATION’S ASSESSMENT OF CONSENT TO JURISDICTION By the time investment treaty arbitration became commonplace in the mid-1990s, the VCLT (including its Articles 31 through 33 on interpretation) had become well established. The participants in the leading investment arbitration cases, including the many that hailed from commercial civil law backgrounds favouring a subjective approach to interpretation, therefore began by applying the VCLT’s emphasis on the ordinary meaning of treaty terms in their context. This prevented the emergence of the kind of divergence from the VCLT’s objective approach that had been a feature of interpretations conducted by General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) panels prior to the 1994 inauguration of the World Trade Organization (WTO).40 See, e.g., Isabelle Van Damme, Treaty Interpretation by the WTO Appellate Body (Oxford, Oxford University Press, 2009) 311. Indeed, in that field, trade diplomats who had long been accustomed to relying primarily on the preparatory work of the treaties for questions of their interpretation simply ignored the VCLT’s relegation of the importance of a treaty’s travaux préparatoires and, until the WTO’s Appellate Body was set up, often turned to that preparatory work as a first – and not subsidiary – insight into the true meaning of the treaty text. Perhaps aided by the contribution of noted professors of public international law as arbitrators and expert witnesses in many investment arbitrations in the late 1990s and early 2000s, contemporary investment tribunals have generally been very careful not to fall into the habit of interpreting a treaty in a manner similar to how an important subset of commercial lawyers would interpret a contract. Several awards have reminded parties of the hierarchy under VCLT Articles 31 and 32 pursuant to which they need to look at the ordinary meaning of the text of the treaty in its context before referring to extrinsic evidence of a drafter’s own apparent intention as disclosed, inter alia, by the treaty’s preparatory work. For example, in the Eiser Infrastructure v. Spain Award (2017), the Tribunal stated that the Respondent’s ‘[r]eliance upon the negotiators’ claimed intention br[ought] to the fore the limited role of supplementary means of interpretation under Article 32(a) of the VCLT’ and, on the basis of a finding that there was nothing ambiguous or obscure in Article 26 of the Energy Charter Treaty (ECT), declared that recourse to evidence of such intention was ‘not required, or even permitted’.41 Eiser Infrastructure Limited and Energía Solar Luxembourg Sà r.l v. Spain, Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/13/36, 4 May 2017) [205]. Later, in Eskosol v. Italy, Italy argued that the interpretation of the relevant section of the bilateral investment treaty (BIT) was an issue of Italy’s own intent and invoked the preparatory work of the ECT to support its intra-EU-only reading of the treaty.42 Eskosol SpA in liq v. Italy, Decision on Italy’s Request for Immediate Termination and Italy’s Jurisdictional Objection (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/15/50, 7 May 2019) [23-26]. In its 2019 Award, the Tribunal bluntly responded to this by declaring that the historical materials cited by Italy could not be relevant to the interpretation of the provision in circumstances where there is ‘nothing “ambiguous or obscure” in the ECT that permits resort to supplementary materials under Article 32(a) of the VCLT’.43 Ibid., 103. Investment tribunals have also not allowed parties to follow their natural or strategic bias to subjective interpretation by invoking –and unduly expanding the scope of – VCLT Article 31(4). As I have noted elsewhere,44 Wyatt (n 36) 205. even if the VCLT’s interpretative rules clearly establish ordinary meaning as the default and special meaning as only the exception, the explicit mention of that which it can be ‘established that the parties […] intended’ in VCLT Article 31(4) has encouraged some to consider that it allows the spirit of the école subjectiviste to live on.45 See, e.g., Myres S McDougal, ‘The International Law Commission’s Draft Articles upon Interpretation: Textuality Redivivus’ (1 October 1967) 61 The American Journal of International Law 992, 994; Ole Spiermann, International Legal Argument in the Permanent Court of International Justice: The Rise of the International Judiciary (Cambridge, Cambridge University Press, 2005) 99; Jean-Marc Sorel, ‘Article 31 – Convention de Vienne de 1969’ in Olivier Corten and Pierre Klein (eds), Les conventions de Vienne sur le droit des traités: commentaire article par article, vol. 2 (Bruxelles, Bruylant, 2006) 1324. Investment arbitration has not permitted this feature of VCLT Article 31(4) to be used as, to borrow Kolb’s words, a ‘Trojan horse’ for the subjectivist approach to treaty interpretation that the law of treaties codification project was meant to have left behind.46 Robert Kolb, Interprétation et création du droit international: Esquisses d’une herméneutique juridique moderne pour le droit international public (Bruxelles, Bruylant, 2006) 437. This is at its clearest in the Award of three arbitrators (all professors) in the El Paso v. Argentina arbitration chaired by Lucius Caflisch, a world-renowned expert of international law. To guide interpretation of the US-Argentina BIT, Argentina sought to rely on exchanges between the US Department of State and the US Senate in 1988 and 1992 relating to the ‘essential security interests’ clause in other BITs and in the 1992 Model Treaty as ‘evidence of the […] US position on Article XI of the 1991 BIT’ applicable to that arbitration and invoked VCLT Article 31(4) in this connexion.47 El Paso Energy International Company v. Argentina, Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/03/15, 31 October 2011) [581-582]; El Paso Energy International Company v. Argentina, Witness Statement of Anne-Marie Slaughter and William Burke-White (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/03/15, 4 March 2007) [36 (especially footnote 52)]. In response to that claim, the El Paso Tribunal notably assigned a very demanding burden for the establishment of an intention justifying the use of a special meaning under VCLT Article 31(4),48 El Paso Energy International Company v. Argentina, Award (n 47) para. 582. and turned its attention to the much more objective interpretative element of context as set out in VCLT Article 31(2) stating: [… W]hat the US may have intended with regard to Article XI, according to the Respondent, would not be relevant context. The status of relevant context would be limited to intentions agreed upon by the Parties, and there is no evidence of agreement on this point. Treaty interpretation does not consist in looking for the unilateral and silent wishes of a Party.49 Ibid., 583. The above practice notwithstanding, in one specific and hotly debated area of investment law, and by means of the citation of one specific and regularly misunderstood principle of international law, investment law appears to have reintroduced aspects of the subjective approach to treaty interpretation. International treaty law’s principle of contemporaneity – or principle of contemporaneous (treaty) interpretation – requires that ‘[t]he terms of a treaty [are] interpreted according to the meaning which they possessed […] and in the light of current linguistic usage, at the time when the treaty was originally concluded’.50 Gerald Fitzmaurice, ‘Law and Procedure of the International Court of Justice 1951-4: Treaty Interpretation and Other Treaty Points’ (1957) 33 British Year Book of International Law 203, 212. It is, as I have detailed elsewhere, a temporal interpretative principle that favours the original semantic content of a treaty term over the different modern-day semantic content that the term may have taken on as a result of linguistic, social, political, moral or legal changes.51 Wyatt (n 36) 22-23, 104-124. Properly defined, the principle of contemporaneity accordingly sits outside of the subjective/objective interpretation debate or, more precisely, wholly within the objective interpretation camp. It simply provides a nuance to the objective approach centred on the ordinary meaning of a treaty term, determining which of the multiple ordinary meanings for that term occurring across time should be considered the true ordinary meaning of the term appearing in the treaty. Indeed, when Fitzmaurice promulgated the principle of contemporaneity in 1957, he characterised it as ‘constituting in a sense no more than a qualification to the principle […] of the natural and ordinary meaning’ and ‘a special application of the principle of natural and ordinary meaning’.52 Fitzmaurice (n 50) 212 and 226. The principle of contemporaneity has been widely misunderstood, including as requiring the opposite of what Fitzmaurice initially said it required.53 See, e.g., Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros Project (Hungary/Slovakia), Separate Opinion of Vice-President Weeramantry [1997] ICJ Rep. 88, 113-115; Jorge E Viñuales, Foreign Investment and the Environment in International Law (Cambridge, Cambridge University Press, 2012) 156-157. However, as originally promulgated and most often understood and applied, it is clear that the principle does not favour the subjective approach over the objective approach. Indeed, many of the cases most clearly advocating and applying the principle patently determine the original meaning of the treaty term they favour by referring to objective (and not subjective) indicia of that meaning including dictionaries, published treaties or legal decisions involving parties other than the litigants. For example, in the ICJ’s Rights of US Nationals in Morocco Judgment (1952) that prompted Fitzmaurice to declare the existence of the principle of contemporaneity, the Court placed considerable reliance on other treaties contemporaneous with the drafting and conclusion of the treaty at issue in both (i) its much-cited interpretation of the word ‘différend’ (dispute);54 Case concerning Rights of Nationals of the United States of America in Morocco (France v. United States of America), Judgment [1952] ICJ Rep. 176, 189. and (ii) its oft ignored contemporaneous interpretation of the phrase ‘economic liberty without any inequality’.55 Ibid., 183-184; See further HW Baade, ‘Intertemporales Völkerrecht’ (1956) 7 Jahrbuch für internationales Recht 229, 250-251. However, in a ‘fiercely contested no-man’s land’56 Garanti Koza LLP v. Turkmenistan, Decision on the Objection to Jurisdiction for Lack of Consent (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/11/20, 3 July 2013) [40]. of investment arbitration characterised by starkly opposed views on whether most-favoured-nation clauses extend to dispute resolution provisions (the MFN-DRP issue), an oddly subjective version of the principle of contemporaneity has recently emerged. The re-emergence of the principle of contemporaneity in modern-day investment arbitration is itself remarkable. Since being proclaimed by Gerald Fitzmaurice, the principle of contemporaneity has consistently fallen ever further from grace to the point that the countervailing doctrine of evolutionary treaty interpretation has become recognised and accepted as international law’s default position on how to interpret treaties through time.57 See, e.g., Abi-Saab and Others, Evolutionary Interpretation and International Law (Oxford, Hart, 2019) 48-49 in particular. Even if it is still occasionally applied in the specific context of boundary disputes,58 See, e.g., Case concerning the delimitation of maritime boundary between Guinea-Bissau and Senegal, Decision of 31 July 1989 (1989) XX RIAA/RSA 119 [84-85] (interpretation of ‘la zone économique exclusive’ [EEZ]); Boundary dispute between Argentina and Chile concerning the frontier line between boundary post 62 and Mount Fitzroy (‘Laguna del Desierto’), Award (1994) XXII RIAA/RSA 3 [127-128]; Kasikili/Sedudu Island (Botswana/ Namibia), Judgment [1999] ICJ Reports 1045 [25, 46] (interpretation of ‘Thalweg’). the principle of contemporaneity has, in other parts of international law, essentially fallen into disrepute. Important statements expressly preferring the counterpoint doctrine of ‘evolutionary interpretation’, such as those in the Iron Rhine Award (2005)59 Iron Rhine (‘Ijzeren Rijn’) Railway between the Kingdom of Belgium and the Kingdom of the Netherlands, Award (2005) XXVII RIAA/RSA 35, 37. and the Related Rights Judgment (2009),60 Dispute regarding Navigational and Related Rights (Costa Rica v. Nicaragua), Judgment [2009] ICJ Rep. 213 [63-67]. appeared to have consigned the principle of contemporaneity to the dustbin of history. For present purposes, however, it is the form in which the principle of contemporaneity was introduced into the fabric of investment arbitration that is most noteworthy. Ever since it was first mentioned by investment arbitration tribunals, the principle of contemporaneity has been defined in a manner that is broadly consistent with how the general international law authorities define it. The Wintershall Award (2008) may have misspelt it three of the four times it mentioned the principle,61 Wintershall Aktiengesellschaft v.
