Argentina, Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/04/14, 8 December 2008) [129, 125 (footnote 103) and 128]. but ultimately defined it correctly as requiring ‘that the terms of a treaty have to be interpreted according to the meaning they possessed (and in the circumstances prevailing), at the time the treaty was concluded’.62 Ibid., 129 and also 174 (footnote 147). The parallel ICS and Daimler Awards (both issued in 2012) also explicitly defined it as ‘requir[ing] that the meaning and scope of [the relevant treaty] term be ascertained as of the time when the [parties to the treaty] negotiated their BIT’.63 ICS Inspection and Control Services Limited v. Argentina (Jurisdiction), Award (Permanent Court of Arbitration Tribunal, PCA Case No. 2010-9, 10 February 2012) [289]; Daimler Financial Services AG v. Argentina (Jurisdiction), Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/05/1, 22 August 2012) 220. These definitions of the principle – like Fitzmaurice’s original formulation of it – are consistent with a completely objective approach to treaty interpretation. The parties fix the time at which the meaning of the term needs to be ascertained, not the meaning itself. However, investment arbitration advocates and adjudicators have not understood and applied the principle of contemporaneity (merely) in the manner in which they originally defined it. Instead, they have used it as a segue into considering the apparently subjective intentions of the State involved in the treaty as a drafter and the relevant investment arbitration as a party. One of the Wintershall Award’s clearest descriptions of the principle of contemporaneity was made in a footnote to the parenthetical statement that the dispute resolution clause under examination ‘obviously represents the expressed will of the Contracting Parties’.64 Wintershall Award (n 61) para. 174. This signalled the emphasis placed on the will of the parties to the treaty by previous investment cases analysing the scope and operation of dispute resolution clauses. Indeed, it is notable that the principle of contemporaneity was first employed by an investment tribunal in the context of a claimant’s attempt to avoid a local remedies pre-condition to arbitration in the manner of the seminal – and controversial65 See, e.g., Zachary Douglas, ‘The MFN Clause in Investment Arbitration: Treaty Interpretation Off the Rails’ (2 January 2011) 2 Journal of International Dispute Settlement 97, 5. – Maffezini Decision on Objections to Jurisdiction (2000).66 Emilio Agustín Maffezini v. The Kingdom of Spain, Decision on Objections to Jurisdiction (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/97/7, 25 January 2000). In the parts of their award most clearly referencing the principle of contemporaneity, the members of the Wintershall tribunal referred extensively to sections of the Maffezini Decision that themselves bear two often overlooked features: extensive reference to international case law that predates the VCLT and a patent emphasis on the Respondent State’s intentions when negotiating the relevant BIT. The latter is perhaps a function of the former as it surely cannot be a coincidence that the three international decisions considered the most analogous to the MFN-DRP issue addressed in Maffezini – the jurisdictional decisions in the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company case,67 Anglo-Iranian Oil Co (Jurisdiction), Judgment [1952] ICJ Rep. 83. the Rights of US Nationals in Morocco case68 US Nationals in Morocco, Judgment (n 54). and the Ambatielos case69 Ambatielos case (Merits – obligation to arbitrate), Judgment [1953] ICJ Rep. 10; The Ambatielos Claim (Greece/United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland), Award (1956) XII RIAA/RSA 83. – were all handed down in the early to mid-1950s when, as noted above, the subjective approach to treaty interpretation was still the default position among international law scholars. Whether the Wintershall Award’s apparent insistence on a subjective approach to interpretation was a product of the relevant issue being one of State consent or of the mere happenstance of the international decisions most relevant to it pre-dating the shift to objective treaty interpretation that occurred in the 1960s, it is clear that the principle of contemporaneity was introduced into the case law of investment tribunals in the context of a subjective – and not objective – approach to the interpretation of treaties. It is important to note that while the Wintershall Award was the first investment award to explicitly mention the principle of contemporaneity, it was not, to borrow an expression from the world of public health in these exceptional times, investment arbitration’s ‘index case’ for using the contemporaneous approach to treaty interpretation as a means to import a subjective approach. In the Berschader v. Russia Award (2006), the Tribunal did not expressly refer to the principle of contemporaneity but went to pains to focus on the time the relevant treaty was concluded echoing the best-known formulations of the principle including those of Gerald Fitzmaurice70 Fitzmaurice (n 50) 212. and Charles Rousseau.71 Ehrlich (n 20) 109. In doing so, it adopted a starkly subjective approach, even regretting that it was ‘not of course, possible today to read the minds of the Belgian and Soviet negotiators at the time of the conclusion of the Treaty’ and further noting that the Tribunal did not ‘have access to any preparatory works which could help us to clarify the views and intentions of the Contracting Parties’.72 Vladimir Berschader and Moise Berschader v. Russia, Award (Arbitration Institute of the Stockholm Chamber of Commerce, 080/2004, 21 October 2006) [199] (present author’s emphasis). It was in the parallel ICS v. Argentina and Daimler v. Argentina Awards (both of 2012) that investment arbitrators began both explicitly and extensively to rely on the principle of contemporaneity in a form of the principle that betrays a subjective approach to treaty interpretation. The ICS and Daimler tribunals were forced to wade into the controversy of the MFN-DRP issue and logically considered that its application could be settled through a careful interpretation of the term ‘treatment’ in the MFN clause before them. The interpretative approach that the tribunals adopted to this question was surprisingly subjective, the tribunals referencing ‘clues that suggest that the term “treatment” was not simply intended to hold the broad ordinary meaning suggested by the Claimant’ and explicitly noting that the parties had not ‘submitted direct evidence revealing the particular understanding held by the Contracting Parties of the term “treatment” as at the time of the conclusion of the Treaty’.73 See also: ICS, Award (n 63) para. 289 (present author’s emphases); Daimler, Award (n 63) para. 220. When more explicitly applying the principle of contemporaneity (as they understood it), the ICS and Daimler Awards do refer to more objective sources of the meaning of the term, most notably the World Bank Guidelines that they consider ‘a valuable indication of the prevailing view among the community of States during the period leading up to the adoption of the [relevant] BIT[s]’.74 ICS, Award (n 63) para. 295; see also: Daimler, Award (n 63) para. 222. However, even in those parts of the Awards, there is a strong emphasis on the subjective approach, with the ICS tribunal conditioning the relevance of the World Bank Guidelines on the absence of ‘countervailing’ – and presumably more subjective – evidence of the meaning of the treaty term and concluding in a starkly subjective matter. Indeed, instead of stating the meaning of the term ‘treatment’, the Tribunal declared its ‘view [… of what the term] was most likely meant by the two Contracting Parties to refer [to]’, before implicitly stating that this constituted an ‘ascertainment of the ordinary meaning of the terms of the MFN clause’.75 ICS, Award (n 63) paras 295-296 (present author’s emphasis). In reply memorials submitted a matter of months after the ICS and Daimler Awards respectively, the Respondent States in the Philip Morris v. Uruguay and Kiliç v. Turkmenistan cases seized the opportunity to plead the principle of contemporaneity in this context and again in a strikingly subjective form. In Philip Morris, Uruguay notably insisted on what ‘an examination of contemporaneous sources reveal[ed …] the parties … [to] have intended’, ‘the subject matter which the two States had in mind when they inserted the clause in their treaty’ and what ‘the Contracting Parties [… could and] could not have envisioned’.76 ‘Uruguay’s Reply on Jurisdiction’, Philip Morris Brands Sàrl, Philip Morris Products SA and ABAL Hermanos SA v. Uruguay (2012) paras 125-127 (present author’s emphasis). In Kilic, Turkmenistan asserted that the principle of contemporaneity required the tribunal ‘to appreciate whether Turkey and Turkmenistan intended the term “treatment” to cover the BITs DRPs’ and invoked the fact that the claimant had ‘not adduced any evidence to suggest that either Turkey or Turkmenistan maintained a different [view of what …] the word “treatment” was meant by both Contracting Parties at the time of the conclusion of the BIT to refer to’.77 Kiliç Ĭnşaat Ĭthalat Ĭhracat Sanayi ve Ticaret Anonim Şirketiv Turkmenistan, Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/10/01, 2 July 2013) [3.3.27 and 3.3.33] (present author’s emphases). When Turkmenistan again advanced the principle of contemporaneity as centred on what ‘the State parties could have contemplated’ in the subsequent Garanti Koza arbitration,78 Garanti Koza LLP v. Turkmenistan, Decision on the Objection to Jurisdiction for Lack of Consent (n 56) para. 55 (present author’s emphasis). the Claimant objected that this undermined the hierarchy of VCLT Articles 31 and 3279 Ibid., 56. and the Tribunal (notably constituted by two common law lawyers and a Geneva-based professor of public international law) found it necessary to remind the parties of the primacy of the text of the treaty in treaty interpretation (the objective approach), in apparent opposition to this subjective form of the principle of contemporaneity.80 Ibid., 57. In later cases, investment arbitration tribunals and parties have referred more heavily to the text of the relevant treaty, but still appear to understand the principle of contemporaneity as seeking to determine the particular parties’ concrete intentions. In Chèque Déjeuner v. Hungary (where a decision on preliminary issues of jurisdiction was rendered in 2016), the parties submitted – and the Tribunal referred to – ‘contemporary evidence of the understanding of the parties at the time of the conclusion’ of their treaty and whether ‘the parties to the treaty intended to exclude arbitration in respect of FET claims’,81 Le Chèque Déjeuner and CD Holding Internationale v. Hungary, Decision on Preliminary Issues of Jurisdiction (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/13/35, 3 March 2016) [157] (present author’s emphases). while the Venezuela US v. Venezuela tribunal’s Interim Award (2016) partially followed the Respondent State’s extensive reference to the principle of contemporaneity to draw conclusions about what ‘the Parties had in mind’.82 Venezuela US SRL v. Venezuela, Interim Award on Jurisdiction (Permanent Court of Arbitration, 2013-34, 26 July 2016) [83]. While these examples may be limited, investment tribunals’ references to the principle of contemporaneity are themselves almost as limited, so in the admittedly quite specific investment law context in which the principle is referenced, it can, without hesitation, be broadly understood as importing a surprisingly subjective approach to treaty interpretation into an area of international dispute settlement and a sub-field of public international law.

sections.title: EXPLANATIONS FOR THE APPARENT DIVERGENCE OF INVESTMENT ARBITRATION TRIBUNALS FROM THE VCLT’S CLEARLY STATED PREFERENCE FOR OBJECTIVE TREATY INTERPRETATION
sections.text: §5.05 EXPLANATIONS FOR THE APPARENT DIVERGENCE OF INVESTMENT ARBITRATION TRIBUNALS FROM THE VCLT’S CLEARLY STATED PREFERENCE FOR OBJECTIVE TREATY INTERPRETATION It is remarkable that investment tribunals appear to have adopted a subjective interpretative approach through the citation of only one particular interpretative principle – the principle of contemporaneity – and in relation to only one particular issue – the MFN-DRP issue. This itself obliges us to query whether there is something particular to the principle of contemporaneity and/or the MFN-DRP issue that justifies, or at least prompts, the use of an unusually subjective approach. By asking such questions, we inevitably enter the morass of mere speculation. Certain potential explanations of this phenomenon can nonetheless be posited and evaluated. First, there is substantial evidence that the somewhat obscure principle of contemporaneity is poorly understood. This may appear to have contributed to the understanding of the principle in a variant form. Yet, as noted above, investment tribunals citing the principle of contemporaneity have usually defined it in a manner consistent with its original meaning and the strongly objective approach that the original formulation of the principle entails. Investment tribunals accordingly do not appear to have misapplied the principle as a result of wrongly defining it. Second, one might contend that the particular context in which this subjective form of the principle of contemporaneity is being invoked in investment arbitration, by centring on ratione voluntatis, is more inherently subjective than the contexts in which the principle has been applied in other international courts and tribunals. It is, after all, clear that (a) the issue of whether an MFN clause extends to dispute resolution provisions arises in relation to challenges to the jurisdiction of the tribunal; (b) that such jurisdiction is given by consent; and (c) that consent is something given by a particular party. Over the history of public international law dispute settlement, there have been suggestions that matters of ratione voluntatis require a different interpretative approach that may be considerably more subjective. In the Chorzow Factory Judgment on Jurisdiction (1927), the Permanent Court of International Justice asserted that ‘[w]hen considering whether it has jurisdiction or not, the Court’s aim is always to ascertain whether an intention on the part of the Parties exists to confer jurisdiction upon it’.83 Factory at Chorzów, Jurisdiction, Judgment [1927] PCIJ Reports (Series A) No. 9, 32. That decision obviously predated the shift to the objective approach to treaty interpretation and the VCLT, but the same idea has still found occasional support in the post-VCLT era. Judge de Castro, for example, in his dissenting opinion to the ICJ’s Aegean Sea Judgment (1978), stated that the unilateral nature of consent required it to be viewed as a ‘declaration of intention’ and that the terms used in such a document must be regarded not as typical treaty terms, but instead as traces, clues and indicia of the intention of its author.84 Aegean Sea Continental Shelf (Greece v. Turkey), Dissenting Opinion of Judge de Castro [1978] ICJ Reports 62, 63. However, even if this position appears logical, it remains a minority view. There are, after all, strong policy reasons for not allowing evidence of subjective intentions to define the scope of a treaty term in the context of consent to jurisdiction, including – and perhaps especially – in an investment arbitration context. It was noted above that one of the reasons international law shifted to a strongly objective approach to treaty interpretation was the risk of informational asymmetry as to the subjective intentions underlying treaty texts and the need to avoid blindsiding parties who were not involved in the treaty drafting process, yet had their legal rights determined in part by the meaning of the treaty others drafted. While this ‘objectivisation’ development may, in the 1960s, have been prompted primarily by concern for the international legal rights of newly independent States acceding to earlier multilateral treaties, it equally applies to claimants in the investment arbitration context. Indeed, there is something unsavoury and unfair in allowing respondent States to escape liability to a claimant investor under an investment treaty by revealing heretofore unknown documents from their archives to establish a meaning of the jurisdictional provision different to that which appears on the face of the treaty. It is perhaps for this reason that ICJ practice – including even that which predates the adoption of the VCLT – generally takes a much more objective approach to the interpretation of jurisdictional clauses, including where it invokes contemporaneous interpretation in that context.85 See, e.g., US Nationals in Morocco, Judgment (n 54) 189; South West Africa (Second Phase), 1966 Judgment (n 37) para. 17. Third, one might follow the key paragraphs of the ICS and Daimler Awards and argue that, because the principle of contemporaneity is ‘particularly pertinent in the case of bilateral treaties’,86 ICS, Award (n 63) para. 289; Daimler, Award (n 63) para. 220. bilateral investment treaties (BITs) require – or at least endorse – a more subjective approach to treaty interpretation, then point to the fact that the majority of investment arbitration decisions are made under BITs. Bilateral treaties are often regarded by international lawyers as being more akin to contracts principally on account of the greater ease in amending them relative to large multilateral treaties that are, for their part, often considered more akin to laws.87 See, e.g., Paul Duez, ‘L’interprétation des traités internationaux’ (1925) 32 Revue générale de droit international public 429, 430-431; Tom Ginsburg, ‘Bounded Discretion in International Judicial Lawmaking’ (2004) 45 Virginia Journal of International Law 631, 661. However, as explained above, many systems of contract law themselves require an objective approach to interpretation, so there is nothing inherent in the nature of bilateral as opposed to multilateral treaties that necessarily implies the use of a subjective interpretative approach. Further, while there is less likely to be informational asymmetry as to drafters’ subjective intentions between the parties to a State versus State dispute under a bilateral treaty, the nature of investor versus State disputes means that, even if the relevant treaty is bilateral rather than multilateral, the claimant investor could never be in a better position – and would usually be in a much worse position – than the respondent State if a subjective approach to the meaning of BIT’s terms were generally favoured. One might retort that document production, a procedural device designed in part to alleviate information asymmetry in arbitration could, by requiring the respondent State to produce the preparatory work it holds, remove the asymmetry created by a subjective interpretative approach. However, document production by the respondent State would only remove some – not all – of the inevitable information asymmetry. Moreover, document production usually only intervenes well after the arbitration has been launched by the claimant investor, often at considerable expense. A subjective approach that allowed the sometimes quite late production of evidence of the subjective intentions of the drafters to determine the meaning of a key treaty term and, as a result, the claimant’s prospects of success, would still be unfair to claimants, frequently causing them to lose time, waste legal costs and potentially even damage an important relationship, losses that could have been avoided had they been better positioned to predict the likely interpretation of that treaty term at the outset. Only an objective approach to the interpretation of treaty terms, including those relevant to the tribunal’s jurisdiction, can ensure that claimant investors are not put at an informational and/or practical disadvantage vis-à-vis respondent States. It accordingly appears that there is nothing in the structure of investment arbitration or even in the particular features of jurisdictional decisions to justify a more subjective approach to interpretation than that which is provided for in the VCLT and favoured under general international law. We must accordingly look elsewhere for the source of this apparent divergence. It is submitted here that investment arbitration’s understanding of the principle of contemporaneity in a manner which authorises a subjective approach to treaty interpretation is likely to be a consequence not of the nature of investment arbitration or the treaties being interpreted, but instead of the kinds of lawyers frequently involved in investment arbitration as arbitrators and counsel. It is undeniable, at least at the present time, that investment arbitration predominantly involves those with a background and practice largely in international commercial arbitration, particularly at the level of counsel. While all forms of the game of public international law dispute resolution are now played by lawyers, it is only investment disputes that are predominantly argued by practitioners equally or more accustomed to applying domestic laws to the interpretation of contracts, many of which favour a subjective approach. Indeed, international commercial arbitration is very prevalent in the civil law world and has a long and influential history on the European Continent where many of the largest legal practices operating in this very international space are presently based. It is inevitable that many of those practising investment arbitration instinctively approach the interpretation of treaties as they would approach their much more frequent interpretation of contracts. It is equally inevitable that features of contract interpretation may thereby leech into the fabric of investment arbitration. For the possibility of this infiltration to arise, it only takes one group of arbitration lawyers more accustomed to subjective contract interpretation; one fact pattern that simultaneously involves both the interpretation of a contract under an applicable law that favours subjective interpretation and the interpretation of a treaty; or one shrewd counsel – or even arbitrator – who is, on account of her or his background, instinctively able to see the strategic benefit of subtly introducing a subjective element to the interpretation of a particular treaty term, including through the vessel of a poorly understood notion such as the principle of contemporaneity. Increased understanding of core principles of general international law may render parties’ memorials and tribunals’ decisions invoking a subjective approach to treaty interpretation ever rarer. Attempts to import this aspect of many systems of contract law to this field of public international law may even disappear altogether. However, even if this phenomenon fades away, there are likely to be other respects in which the presence of players on the field of investment arbitration who are primarily commercial lawyers and not public international lawyers will, through the vehicle of this relatively heavily adjudicated field of international law, threaten to influence the development of core areas of general public international law. Such influences will not necessarily be positive, but they will not necessarily be negative either. After worrying about the fragmentation of public international law through the increased prevalence of forms of dispute settlement such as investment arbitration, it appears that international law has now reached a point where it can not only accommodate, but also welcome such influences. We must simply be careful not to fail to notice their impacts and then to carefully interrogate the extent to which those developments that affect cross-cutting areas such as treaty interpretation should be generalised across the wide variety of sub-fields now belonging to the discipline of public international law.

plain_text:
§5.01 INTRODUCTION As its now most popular name itself suggests, investment treaty arbitration presents an amalgam of arbitration and treaty law in the context of investments. It merges two quite different legal traditions, its arbitration aspect being dominated by the practices of the much larger field of international commercial arbitration and its treaty aspect drawing on principles at the core of the sui generis system of law between States known as public international law. This dual nature creates difficulty for lawyers of both types. International commercial arbitration practitioners and scholars tackling problems of investment treaty arbitration often need to shift their private-law dominated mindset to address the substantive legal issues of an overwhelmingly public-law nature that are presented by investment disputes. For example, rules relating to the attribution of acts and omissions to States as a matter of State responsibility have been extensively developed by public international lawyers primarily in the factual context of armed conflicts,1 See, e.g., ILC ARSIWA 2001 with commentaries in Yearbook 2001, vol. II, part 2 (United Nations, 2001) 47-49 and the references cited therein. and present a legal logic that is almost completely foreign to the many commercial lawyers operating in the international arbitration space. Public international lawyers are, for their part, forced to reckon with not only unfamiliar procedures drawn from contract-based arbitration and domestic commercial law traditions, but also the impacts of the international commercial arbitration mindset on the practice – and therefore development – of the investment law sub-field and even on the field of public international law itself. The present book, by offering those interested in international arbitration a range of insights into the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties (VCLT) can itself be considered an example of the modern-day literature that helps international commercial arbitration practitioners come to terms with the practices, concepts and principles of public international law. Relatively less attention has been paid to how the practices, concepts and principles of international commercial arbitration have affected the field of public international law. This is itself somewhat surprising. Approximately twenty years ago, the hottest topic in international law was ‘fragmentation’, the fear that the well-documented proliferation of differently structured international courts and tribunals across functionally distinct sub-fields of international law would fracture the core of the discipline.2 See, e.g., Georges Abi-Saab, ‘Fragmentation or unification: Some Concluding Remarks’ (1998) 31 NYU Journal of International Law and Politics 919; PS Rao, ‘Multiple International Judicial Forums: A Reflection of the Growing Strength of International Law or Its Fragmentation’ (2003) 25 Mich. J. Int’l L. 929; Tullio Treves, ‘Judicial Lawmaking in an Era of “Proliferation” of International Courts and Tribunals: Development or Fragmentation of International Law?’ in Rüdiger Wolfrum (ed.), Developments of International Law in Treaty Making (Berlin, Springer, 2005). One might have expected international lawyers to look into every corner of international law – and especially at the particular practices of every type of international court or tribunal. There has been enough of this kind of work for Shaffer and Ginsburg to cite it as part of what they call an ‘empirical turn’ in international legal scholarship.3 Gregory Shaffer and Tom Ginsburg, ‘The Empirical Turn in International Legal Scholarship’ (2012) 106 The American Journal of International Law 1, 11-19, in particular. However, the level of attention that the public international law literature afforded the fragmentation issue itself has, after reaching its zenith around the time of the International Law Commission’s (ILC’s) fragmentation report of 2006,4 International Law Commission and Martii Koskenniemi, ‘Fragmentation of International Law: Difficulties Arising from Diversification and Expansion of International Law’ (United Nations, 2006) A/CN.4/L.682. substantially declined in recent years. This is perhaps due to a greater acceptance that public international law could not be – and did not need to be – a unified and homogenous legal system and should instead celebrate the diversity that comes with its continued growth. Alternatively, it may result from a recognition that a comprehensive and non-superficial assessment of the issue of fragmentation was – in light of the quantity of international law being generated by States and international courts and tribunals – simply too large an undertaking to be carried out in any meaningful way. None of this implies, however, that the risks that initially prompted concern about the fragmentation of international law have ceased to exist. What are often called ‘cross-cutting’ areas of international law, including State responsibility law and treaty law, remain vulnerable to being interpreted and applied differently by differently structured and staffed international courts and tribunals operating in disparate factual, legal and procedural contexts.5 See, e.g., Philippe Sands, ‘Treaty, Custom and the Cross-Fertilization of International Law’ (1998) 1 Yale Hum. Rts. & Dev. LJ 85, 92. Indeed, while the use of the ‘fragmentation’ buzzword may have declined, comparative approaches to the study of the different sub-systems of international law have become increasingly prevalent. Residual fears surrounding whether there is a problematic fragmentation of public international law – or any fragmentation in the first place – are being partially addressed by comparative analyses focused on specific cross-cutting areas, including treaty law, that seek to tease out the similarities and differences between the different branches of contemporary public international law. Since international commercial arbitration is a form of dispute settlement operating outside the bounds of public international law, hardly any attention has been paid to how its practices, concepts and principles may affect international law. This seems to disavow the demonstrable fact that practitioners who are primarily international commercial arbitration lawyers play an important role in a sub-field of public international law, investment law, including as regards issues including treaty interpretation, which is not only a feature of investment arbitration but of all fields of international law. The present contribution seeks to fill part of that gap by examining one respect in which an international commercial arbitration mindset may have caused the practice of investment tribunals to diverge from the practice of other international courts and tribunals interpreting and applying the VCLT. It argues that the objective approach to treaty interpretation mandated by VCLT Articles 31 through 33 is, at least in the context of consent to jurisdiction, not being as strictly respected by investment tribunals as it is by other international courts and tribunals and, further, that this divergence is a product of the particular features of the forum in which issues of investment law are now examined: the world of international arbitration. This chapter begins by explaining the difference between the subjective and objective approaches to the interpretation of legal texts in general (section 5.02) and then describes how, in relation to treaty interpretation specifically, there has been a shift from a historically subjective approach to the VCLT’s objective approach (section 5.03). It then notes what appears to be a re-emergence of the subjective approach to treaty interpretation in a specific part of the case law of investment arbitration tribunals (section 5.04), before concluding with a consideration of some possible explanations of this apparent divergence from general international law (section 5.05).

§5.02 SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE APPROACHES TO THE INTERPRETATION OF LEGAL TEXTS In broad terms, the subjective approach to the interpretation of legal texts considers the meaning of a term to be that which it possessed in the mind of the person uttering it and focuses on the intentions of the drafter(s) of the text. The subjective approach can be contrasted with what is usually called the objective – but could also be appropriately termed the intersubjective6 At least in so far as these terms are understood in the senses used by philosophical disciplines including epistemology and phenomenology, the type of meaning that legal interpretation seeks to identify is ‘intersubjective’ rather than ‘objective’ in the sense that it constitutes an understanding that is shared among many people, rather than a truth that is independent of the views of any one or more individuals. – approach to interpretation, pursuant to which the meaning of a term is that which is disclosed by the text of the instrument alone without any separate reference to what its drafters intended it to say. Whereas the subjective approach effectively emphasises the intentions of the writer, the objective approach effectively emphasises the understanding of the reader. The distinction between subjective and objective approaches to interpretation is particularly important in the context of comparative contract law. In a jurisdiction favouring a subjective approach to contract interpretation, such as Switzerland, the aim of contract interpretation is to give terms the meaning that concurs with the ‘tatsächlicher Parteiwille’, the actual intention of the parties.7 See, e.g., 140 III 134 (Swiss Federal Supreme Court, 27 February 2014) [3.2] (present author’s translation). The Swiss Code of Obligations even decrees that ‘the true and common intention of the parties must be ascertained without dwelling on any inexact expressions or designations they may have used’ in their agreement.8 Swiss Federal Act on the Amendment of the Civil Code (Part Five: The Code of Obligations) 1911, Art. 18(1). The text of a contract is examined in order to infer a subjective fact, the otherwise obscure intention of the parties.9 See, e.g., 130 III 66 (Swiss Federal Supreme Court, 21 November 2003) [3.2]. By contrast, in a jurisdiction favouring objective (or intersubjective) contract interpretation, such as Australia, ‘it is not the subjective beliefs or understandings of the parties about their rights and liabilities that govern their contractual relations’,10 Toll (FGCT) Pty Ltd v. Alphapharm Pty Ltd (2004) [2004] HCA 52 (High Court of Australia). but rather ‘what a reasonable person having all the background knowledge of the “surrounding circumstances” available to the parties would have understood’ from ‘the language in the contract’.11 Byrnes v. Kendle (2011) [2011] HCA [98]. In such systems, evidence of the parties’ statements reflecting their actual intentions is inadmissible and other evidence extrinsic to the text of the contract is only admitted where it is necessary to help determine an intersubjective or objective fact, namely ‘what a reasonable person would understand by the language in which the parties have expressed their agreement’.12 Mount Bruce Mining Pty Limited v. Wright Prospecting Pty Limited (2015) [2015] HCA 37 (High Court of Australia); Toll (FGCT) Pty Ltd v. Alphapharm Pty Ltd (n 10) para. 40. As these examples suggest, civil law jurisdictions are generally considered to favour a subjective approach and common law jurisdictions an objective approach. The reality is much more nuanced. Some prominent civil law jurisdictions introduce objective elements such as the Empfängerhorizont that German law uses to ensure that the intention feeding into the interpretative process is that which a reasonable addressee of the relevant declaration would understand the intention to be in the circumstances,13 See: Otto Palandt and Gerd Brudermüller, Palandt Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch: mit Nebengesetzen insbesondere mit Einführungsgesetz (München, C H Beck, 2017) 124-125 (§133, paras 9, 14); see also: Basil Markesinis and Others, The German Law of Contract: A Comparative Treatise (Oxford, Hart Publishing, 2006) 288. or the principe de la confiance which allows a Court, in certain circumstances, to impute to a party the objective meaning of his or her declaration even if it does not correspond with his or her internal will.14 136 III 186 (Swiss Federal Supreme Court, 6 April 2010) s. 3.2.1. Equally, modern-day contract law in the United States (US), by pursuing interpretations that maximally accord with the contracting parties’ purposes, will frequently allow subjectively held common intentions to prevail over the more reasonable objective meaning of a contractual term.15 See, e.g., Melvin A Eisenberg, Foundational Principles of Contract Law (Oxford University Press, 2018) 398-406; Robert Braucher E Allan Farnsworth and American Law Institute, Restatement of the Law, Second: Contracts 2d, Revised and enlarged (St. Paul, Minn., American Law Institute Publishers, 1981) s. 201. What is critical for present purposes, however, is that a wide variety of interpretative approaches, ranging from the highly subjective to the highly objective, is present in the world’s different systems of contract law and thereby inform the mindsets of the many commercial disputes lawyers participating as advocates and adjudicators in international commercial arbitration and investment treaty arbitration proceedings.

§5.03 SHIFT FROM THE SUBJECTIVE TO THE OBJECTIVE APPROACH IN THE INTERPRETATION OF TREATIES The modern-day international law of treaty interpretation has its origins in the work of glossators in the Middle Ages who reinstated Roman principles for judicial interpretation, including those in Justinian’s corpus juris civilis of 530 AD. In the present context, it is important to observe that the glossators’ body of work was ‘intent on developing a general theory of interpretation for legal instruments’ of all kinds.16 Roger Simonds, Rational Individualism: The Perennial Philosophy of Legal Interpretation (Amsterdam, Rodopi, 1995) 70. The glossators accordingly privileged the subjective approach to legal interpretation that was overwhelmingly favoured for the interpretation of common Roman instruments including commercial contracts and wills. The glossa magna of Francis Accursius, for example, drew on the Digest’s section on the particular issue of legacies to state a general interpretative maxim which appears to allow for a purely subjective interpretation of all legal instruments, even where there is no textual ambiguity:Non aliter a significatione verborum recedi oportet, quam cum manifestum est aliud sensisse testatorem. // (Marcellus, Opinions) It is not proper to depart from the literal meaning of the words except when it is manifest that the testator had something else in mind.17 Justinian, Omini nostri sacratissimi principis iustiniani iuris enucleati ex omni vetere iure collecti: digestorum seu pandectarum (530) Book XXXII, No. 69pr (Marcellus l.S. resp.); English translation from: Simonds (n 16) 70. The founders of international law who followed in the Western European footsteps of the glossators, while on many occasions specifically declaring principles for the interpretation of treaties, also invoked interpretative rules that had been formulated in relation to a wider range of instruments and were therefore overwhelmingly subjective. Vattel, for example, considered it to be an ‘unerring rule’ that ‘[w]hen we evidently see what is the sense that agrees with the intention of the contracting parties, it is not allowable to wrest their words to a contrary meaning’ and declared that ‘[t]he intention, sufficiently known, furnishes the true matter of the convention, – what is promised and accepted, demanded and granted’.18 Emer de Vattel, The Law of Nations 896, Book II, §274. In its formative years, international law explicitly followed an intention-based, subjective approach to the interpretation of treaties. In the nineteenth century, Francis Lieber’s influential Legal and Political Hermeneutics specifically stated that the ordinary meaning of a term is intrinsically related to how the drafter – not the reader – understood that term when he or she committed it to writing, saying ‘we are to take the words in that meaning which we may faithfully believe their utterer attached to them’.19 Francis Lieber, Legal and Political Hermeneutics or Principles of Interpretation and Construction in Law and Politics with Remarks on Precedents and Authorities (Boston, Charles C Little and James Brown, 1839) 102 (Chapter IV, s. IX). Right up until the Second World War, the most frequently cited authors on treaty interpretation overwhelmingly favoured an approach which, in the words used by Ehrlich’s Hague Course of 1928, followed the principle of seeking ‘la véritable volonté des parties’ (the true will of the parties) by all useful means.20 Ludwik Ehrlich, ‘L’interprétation des traités’ (1928) 24 Collected Courses of the Hague Academy of International Law 1, 126. Prominent decisions of international courts and tribunals dating from the first decades of the twentieth century confirmed the subjective approach to treaty interpretation. For example, in the Permanent Court of Arbitration’s seminal 1910 award in the North Atlantic Coast Fisheries Case (Great Britain, United States), the Tribunal had no qualms in rejecting one of the US’ interpretative arguments on the basis that a practical distinction between the terms ‘coasts’ and ‘bays’ had ‘been shown from the correspondence and the documents in evidence […] to have been in all probability present to the minds of the negotiators of the [relevant t]reaty’.21 The North Atlantic Coast Fisheries Case (Great Britain, United States), Award (1910) XI RIAA/RSA 167, 198. When efforts to codify international law became much more prominent in the aftermath of the Second World War, an early set of draft articles on the interpretation of treaties produced by Hersch Lauterpacht as a Special Rapporteur for the Institut de droit international (IDI) continued to favour a strongly subjective approach. Article 5 of those draft articles stated explicitly that ‘the intention of the parties is a decisive element in treaty interpretation’ and draft Article 2 specifically identified resort to the preparatory work of a treaty in order to establish the intention of the parties.22 See Institut de droit international, ‘De l’interprétation des traités’, Annuaire, vol. 46 (1956) 318-319 (present author’s translation). Yet Lauterpacht’s draft articles on treaty interpretation met vehement resistance as soon as they were first proposed, particularly at a 1952 meeting of the IDI in Sien(n)a, where various luminaries of international law took issue with his overtly subjective approach and particularly the emphasis on the importance of a treaty’s travaux préparatoires for the purposes of its interpretation.23 Ibid., 320-321. Upon taking over from Lauterpacht as the IDI’s Special Rapporteur on treaty interpretation, Gerald Fitzmaurice chartered a new and much less subjective path that emphasised the role of the text and downgraded the role of the travaux préparatoires from being the primary source of reference in all interpretative disputes to being one of three ‘other means of interpretation’ to be used where the interpreter considers the use of such means necessary.24 Ibid., 318-325, 330-332. Treaty law had, of course, been listed among the fourteen topics for codification at the first session of the United Nations’ (UN) ILC in 1949,25 International Law Commission, ‘Yearbook 1949, vol I’ (New York, United Nations, 1956) 53 (6th meeting – 20 April 1949). and the interpretation of treaties fell within the scope of that codification project. When, in 1962, the Commission instructed the fourth special rapporteur on the law of treaties, Humphrey Waldock, to ‘prepare draft articles on the law of treaties intended to serve as the basis for a convention’, not a code, and to ‘cover the whole subject in two years’,26 UN Document A/4843 quoted in Humphrey Waldock’s first report on the law of treaties: UN. International Law Commission, Yearbook of the International Law Commission (ILC YB 1962v1) (United Nations, 1962) vol. 2, 29. none of his predecessors had yet managed to formulate draft articles on treaty interpretation. Waldock had clearly been heavily influenced by the views on treaty interpretation belonging to his compatriot and predecessor in the role, Fitzmaurice, and put together draft articles that not only presented a much more objective than subjective approach but even relegated any express mention of party intention to a secondary status.27 See: International Law Commission, ‘Yearbook 1964, vol II’ (New York, United Nations, 1965) 52-54. Indeed, the term ‘intention’ did not even itself appear in the ‘General Rule of Interpretation’ in his draft Article 70 that largely became VCLT Article 31 paragraphs 1, 2 and 3. This shift from a subjective to an objective approach initially encountered foreseeable resistance from partisans of the subjective school in the ILC’s Law of Treaties Study Group, with many seeking a reintegration of elements of the so-called école subjectiviste, including primary reference to a treaty’s preparatory work. Abdul Hakim Tabibi specifically stated that he would give greater weight to the intention of the parties – as had been the opinion of Hersch Lauterpacht – and, with the support of Shabtai Rosenne,28 International Law Commission, ‘Yearbook 1964, vol I’ (New York; Geneva, United Nations, 1965) 278 (per Rosenne, 765th meeting – 14 July 1964, para. 40). sought to elevate the importance of intention by combining the merely subsidiary means of interpretation placed in draft Article 71 (what became VCLT Article 32) into the set of primary means placed in draft Article 70 (what became VCLT Article 31).29 Ibid., 276 (per Tabibi, 765th meeting – 14 July 1964, para. 25). Milan Bartos went so far as to associate the draft articles’ preference for an objective and textual approach with a specifically ‘English school of legal thought, that interpretation meant interpretation of the text rather than of the spirit of a treaty’ and argued forcefully for a more subjective approach saying that ‘[w]here interpretation was concerned, the autonomy of the will of the parties was paramount’ and ‘[w]hat the parties had intended was more important than what they had actually said in the treaty’.30 Ibid., 279 (per Bartos, 765th meeting – 14 July 1964, para. 64). At the first session of the UN Conference on the Law of Treaties in 1968, the apparent rejection of the subjective approach to treaty interpretation came under some quite vociferous attack. Introducing an amendment specifically targeted at combining what became VCLT Article 32 into what became VCLT Article 31, Myres McDougal of the US railed against a ‘textual approach to interpretation [that] was impossible to apply’ and had, at least according to his reading of its accompanying commentary, ‘flatly rejected […] common intent as the goal of interpretation’.31 UN Conference on the Law of Treaties, ‘Official Records, First Session, Vienna’ (Vienna, United Nations, 1968) 167 (Thirty-first meeting – 19 April 1968, per McDougal [United States of America], paras 43 and 39 respectively). McDougal’s proposition attracted some support from other delegates. The Greek delegation offered its support to the amendment explicitly,32 Ibid., 172 (per Mr Krispis [Greece], Thirty-second meeting – 20 April 1968, para. 9). as did the Ghanaian delegation, whose representative followed McDougal’s criticisms of the textual approach with the statement that ‘[t]he crucial point in the function of interpretation was to ascertain the intention of the parties with regard to a particular problem’.33 Ibid., 170-171 (per Mr Dadzié [Ghana], Thirty-first meeting – 19 April 1968, para. 70). The Ukrainian delegate, Nikolai Petrovich Makarevich, considered that while the ILC ‘had rightly rejected proposals whereby a treaty might be interpreted exclusively in connexion with the intention of the parties’, ‘[i]t had, however, gone to the other extreme in deciding that the interpretation should be based exclusively on the terms of the treaty in their context and in the light of its object and purpose’.34 Ibid., 168 (per Mr Makarevich [Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic], Thirty-first meeting – 19 April 1968, para. 54). However, McDougal’s proposal was also roundly criticised and, on 22 April 1968, was ultimately rejected by sixty-six votes to eight with ten abstentions.35 Ibid., 185 (Thirty-third meeting – 22 April 1968, para. 75). As I have explained elsewhere,36 Julian Wyatt, Intertemporal Linguistics in International Law (Oxford, Hart, 2019) 203-204. the Vienna Conference – and therefore the VCLT – did not reject the importance of party intention tout court, but of a subjective understanding of that intention which would effectively permit the actionable meaning of a treaty term to be determined by the internal thoughts of certain drafters that were not disclosed anywhere other than in the preparatory work buried in the vaults of a handful of powerful governments. It is undoubtedly significant that the rejection of a subjective approach to the interpretation of treaties ultimately occurred in April 1968, right in the middle of the most politically charged and fast-changing peacetime period in the life of the international community when international institutions – and public international law – were adjusting to (i) the impact of the wave of decolonisation then approaching its zenith; (ii) the social and civil rights movements sweeping through the Western world at that time; and (iii) the fall-out from the International Court of Justice’s (ICJ’s) Southwest Africa (Ethiopia v. South Africa; Liberia v. South Africa) (Second Phase) Judgment (1966).37 South West Africa Cases (Ethiopia v. South Africa; Liberia v. South Africa) (Second Phase), Judgment [1966] ICJ Rep. 6; See also, e.g., Rosalyn Higgins, ‘The International Court and South West Africa: The Implications of the Judgment’ (October 1966) 42 International Affairs 573; Georges Abi-Saab, Cours général de droit international public (The Hague, Martinus Nijhoff, 1996) 395-402. Newly independent States could not be expected to respect international treaties drafted by their prior colonial masters or to accede to key multilateral treaties drafted before they had a seat at the table if the meaning of the terms of those treaties could be determined by subjective intentions of which the newly independent States often had no knowledge. In this context, as Eduardo Jiménez de Aréchaga of Uruguay pointed out in a strident criticism of McDougal’s proposal at the Vienna Conference, the ‘supposed real will of the parties’ could not be permitted to ‘float like a cloud over the terra firma of a contractual text’.38 Vienna Conference Records, 1968 Session (n 31) 170 (per Jiménez de Aréchaga [Uruguay], Thirty-first meeting – 19 April 1968, para. 63). The result in the VCLT’s rules for treaty interpretation is, of course, limited reference to both intention and the preparatory work, with the former only decisive if it exceptionally proves the existence of a special meaning and recourse to the latter only permitted when interpretation according to the objective elements outlined in Article 31 leaves the meaning ambiguous or obscure or leads to a result which is manifestly absurd or unreasonable.39 VCLT, Arts 31 and 32. The VCLT rules for the interpretation of treaties, which are focused on an objective approach to interpretation, have no clear competitor, are now widely regarded as reflecting customary international law and are widely invoked before international courts and tribunals of all types. For the interpretation of treaties, the objective approach evidently prevailed over the subjective approach.

§5.04 SIGNS OF A RE-EMERGENCE OF THE SUBJECTIVE APPROACH IN INVESTMENT ARBITRATION’S ASSESSMENT OF CONSENT TO JURISDICTION By the time investment treaty arbitration became commonplace in the mid-1990s, the VCLT (including its Articles 31 through 33 on interpretation) had become well established. The participants in the leading investment arbitration cases, including the many that hailed from commercial civil law backgrounds favouring a subjective approach to interpretation, therefore began by applying the VCLT’s emphasis on the ordinary meaning of treaty terms in their context. This prevented the emergence of the kind of divergence from the VCLT’s objective approach that had been a feature of interpretations conducted by General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) panels prior to the 1994 inauguration of the World Trade Organization (WTO).40 See, e.g., Isabelle Van Damme, Treaty Interpretation by the WTO Appellate Body (Oxford, Oxford University Press, 2009) 311. Indeed, in that field, trade diplomats who had long been accustomed to relying primarily on the preparatory work of the treaties for questions of their interpretation simply ignored the VCLT’s relegation of the importance of a treaty’s travaux préparatoires and, until the WTO’s Appellate Body was set up, often turned to that preparatory work as a first – and not subsidiary – insight into the true meaning of the treaty text. Perhaps aided by the contribution of noted professors of public international law as arbitrators and expert witnesses in many investment arbitrations in the late 1990s and early 2000s, contemporary investment tribunals have generally been very careful not to fall into the habit of interpreting a treaty in a manner similar to how an important subset of commercial lawyers would interpret a contract. Several awards have reminded parties of the hierarchy under VCLT Articles 31 and 32 pursuant to which they need to look at the ordinary meaning of the text of the treaty in its context before referring to extrinsic evidence of a drafter’s own apparent intention as disclosed, inter alia, by the treaty’s preparatory work. For example, in the Eiser Infrastructure v. Spain Award (2017), the Tribunal stated that the Respondent’s ‘[r]eliance upon the negotiators’ claimed intention br[ought] to the fore the limited role of supplementary means of interpretation under Article 32(a) of the VCLT’ and, on the basis of a finding that there was nothing ambiguous or obscure in Article 26 of the Energy Charter Treaty (ECT), declared that recourse to evidence of such intention was ‘not required, or even permitted’.41 Eiser Infrastructure Limited and Energía Solar Luxembourg Sà r.l v. Spain, Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/13/36, 4 May 2017) [205]. Later, in Eskosol v. Italy, Italy argued that the interpretation of the relevant section of the bilateral investment treaty (BIT) was an issue of Italy’s own intent and invoked the preparatory work of the ECT to support its intra-EU-only reading of the treaty.42 Eskosol SpA in liq v. Italy, Decision on Italy’s Request for Immediate Termination and Italy’s Jurisdictional Objection (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/15/50, 7 May 2019) [23-26]. In its 2019 Award, the Tribunal bluntly responded to this by declaring that the historical materials cited by Italy could not be relevant to the interpretation of the provision in circumstances where there is ‘nothing “ambiguous or obscure” in the ECT that permits resort to supplementary materials under Article 32(a) of the VCLT’.43 Ibid., 103. Investment tribunals have also not allowed parties to follow their natural or strategic bias to subjective interpretation by invoking –and unduly expanding the scope of – VCLT Article 31(4). As I have noted elsewhere,44 Wyatt (n 36) 205. even if the VCLT’s interpretative rules clearly establish ordinary meaning as the default and special meaning as only the exception, the explicit mention of that which it can be ‘established that the parties […] intended’ in VCLT Article 31(4) has encouraged some to consider that it allows the spirit of the école subjectiviste to live on.45 See, e.g., Myres S McDougal, ‘The International Law Commission’s Draft Articles upon Interpretation: Textuality Redivivus’ (1 October 1967) 61 The American Journal of International Law 992, 994; Ole Spiermann, International Legal Argument in the Permanent Court of International Justice: The Rise of the International Judiciary (Cambridge, Cambridge University Press, 2005) 99; Jean-Marc Sorel, ‘Article 31 – Convention de Vienne de 1969’ in Olivier Corten and Pierre Klein (eds), Les conventions de Vienne sur le droit des traités: commentaire article par article, vol. 2 (Bruxelles, Bruylant, 2006) 1324. Investment arbitration has not permitted this feature of VCLT Article 31(4) to be used as, to borrow Kolb’s words, a ‘Trojan horse’ for the subjectivist approach to treaty interpretation that the law of treaties codification project was meant to have left behind.46 Robert Kolb, Interprétation et création du droit international: Esquisses d’une herméneutique juridique moderne pour le droit international public (Bruxelles, Bruylant, 2006) 437. This is at its clearest in the Award of three arbitrators (all professors) in the El Paso v. Argentina arbitration chaired by Lucius Caflisch, a world-renowned expert of international law. To guide interpretation of the US-Argentina BIT, Argentina sought to rely on exchanges between the US Department of State and the US Senate in 1988 and 1992 relating to the ‘essential security interests’ clause in other BITs and in the 1992 Model Treaty as ‘evidence of the […] US position on Article XI of the 1991 BIT’ applicable to that arbitration and invoked VCLT Article 31(4) in this connexion.47 El Paso Energy International Company v. Argentina, Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/03/15, 31 October 2011) [581-582]; El Paso Energy International Company v. Argentina, Witness Statement of Anne-Marie Slaughter and William Burke-White (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/03/15, 4 March 2007) [36 (especially footnote 52)]. In response to that claim, the El Paso Tribunal notably assigned a very demanding burden for the establishment of an intention justifying the use of a special meaning under VCLT Article 31(4),48 El Paso Energy International Company v. Argentina, Award (n 47) para. 582. and turned its attention to the much more objective interpretative element of context as set out in VCLT Article 31(2) stating: [… W]hat the US may have intended with regard to Article XI, according to the Respondent, would not be relevant context. The status of relevant context would be limited to intentions agreed upon by the Parties, and there is no evidence of agreement on this point. Treaty interpretation does not consist in looking for the unilateral and silent wishes of a Party.49 Ibid., 583. The above practice notwithstanding, in one specific and hotly debated area of investment law, and by means of the citation of one specific and regularly misunderstood principle of international law, investment law appears to have reintroduced aspects of the subjective approach to treaty interpretation. International treaty law’s principle of contemporaneity – or principle of contemporaneous (treaty) interpretation – requires that ‘[t]he terms of a treaty [are] interpreted according to the meaning which they possessed […] and in the light of current linguistic usage, at the time when the treaty was originally concluded’.50 Gerald Fitzmaurice, ‘Law and Procedure of the International Court of Justice 1951-4: Treaty Interpretation and Other Treaty Points’ (1957) 33 British Year Book of International Law 203, 212. It is, as I have detailed elsewhere, a temporal interpretative principle that favours the original semantic content of a treaty term over the different modern-day semantic content that the term may have taken on as a result of linguistic, social, political, moral or legal changes.51 Wyatt (n 36) 22-23, 104-124. Properly defined, the principle of contemporaneity accordingly sits outside of the subjective/objective interpretation debate or, more precisely, wholly within the objective interpretation camp. It simply provides a nuance to the objective approach centred on the ordinary meaning of a treaty term, determining which of the multiple ordinary meanings for that term occurring across time should be considered the true ordinary meaning of the term appearing in the treaty. Indeed, when Fitzmaurice promulgated the principle of contemporaneity in 1957, he characterised it as ‘constituting in a sense no more than a qualification to the principle […] of the natural and ordinary meaning’ and ‘a special application of the principle of natural and ordinary meaning’.52 Fitzmaurice (n 50) 212 and 226. The principle of contemporaneity has been widely misunderstood, including as requiring the opposite of what Fitzmaurice initially said it required.53 See, e.g., Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros Project (Hungary/Slovakia), Separate Opinion of Vice-President Weeramantry [1997] ICJ Rep. 88, 113-115; Jorge E Viñuales, Foreign Investment and the Environment in International Law (Cambridge, Cambridge University Press, 2012) 156-157. However, as originally promulgated and most often understood and applied, it is clear that the principle does not favour the subjective approach over the objective approach. Indeed, many of the cases most clearly advocating and applying the principle patently determine the original meaning of the treaty term they favour by referring to objective (and not subjective) indicia of that meaning including dictionaries, published treaties or legal decisions involving parties other than the litigants. For example, in the ICJ’s Rights of US Nationals in Morocco Judgment (1952) that prompted Fitzmaurice to declare the existence of the principle of contemporaneity, the Court placed considerable reliance on other treaties contemporaneous with the drafting and conclusion of the treaty at issue in both (i) its much-cited interpretation of the word ‘différend’ (dispute);54 Case concerning Rights of Nationals of the United States of America in Morocco (France v. United States of America), Judgment [1952] ICJ Rep. 176, 189. and (ii) its oft ignored contemporaneous interpretation of the phrase ‘economic liberty without any inequality’.55 Ibid., 183-184; See further HW Baade, ‘Intertemporales Völkerrecht’ (1956) 7 Jahrbuch für internationales Recht 229, 250-251. However, in a ‘fiercely contested no-man’s land’56 Garanti Koza LLP v. Turkmenistan, Decision on the Objection to Jurisdiction for Lack of Consent (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/11/20, 3 July 2013) [40]. of investment arbitration characterised by starkly opposed views on whether most-favoured-nation clauses extend to dispute resolution provisions (the MFN-DRP issue), an oddly subjective version of the principle of contemporaneity has recently emerged. The re-emergence of the principle of contemporaneity in modern-day investment arbitration is itself remarkable. Since being proclaimed by Gerald Fitzmaurice, the principle of contemporaneity has consistently fallen ever further from grace to the point that the countervailing doctrine of evolutionary treaty interpretation has become recognised and accepted as international law’s default position on how to interpret treaties through time.57 See, e.g., Abi-Saab and Others, Evolutionary Interpretation and International Law (Oxford, Hart, 2019) 48-49 in particular. Even if it is still occasionally applied in the specific context of boundary disputes,58 See, e.g., Case concerning the delimitation of maritime boundary between Guinea-Bissau and Senegal, Decision of 31 July 1989 (1989) XX RIAA/RSA 119 [84-85] (interpretation of ‘la zone économique exclusive’ [EEZ]); Boundary dispute between Argentina and Chile concerning the frontier line between boundary post 62 and Mount Fitzroy (‘Laguna del Desierto’), Award (1994) XXII RIAA/RSA 3 [127-128]; Kasikili/Sedudu Island (Botswana/ Namibia), Judgment [1999] ICJ Reports 1045 [25, 46] (interpretation of ‘Thalweg’). the principle of contemporaneity has, in other parts of international law, essentially fallen into disrepute. Important statements expressly preferring the counterpoint doctrine of ‘evolutionary interpretation’, such as those in the Iron Rhine Award (2005)59 Iron Rhine (‘Ijzeren Rijn’) Railway between the Kingdom of Belgium and the Kingdom of the Netherlands, Award (2005) XXVII RIAA/RSA 35, 37. and the Related Rights Judgment (2009),60 Dispute regarding Navigational and Related Rights (Costa Rica v. Nicaragua), Judgment [2009] ICJ Rep. 213 [63-67]. appeared to have consigned the principle of contemporaneity to the dustbin of history. For present purposes, however, it is the form in which the principle of contemporaneity was introduced into the fabric of investment arbitration that is most noteworthy. Ever since it was first mentioned by investment arbitration tribunals, the principle of contemporaneity has been defined in a manner that is broadly consistent with how the general international law authorities define it. The Wintershall Award (2008) may have misspelt it three of the four times it mentioned the principle,61 Wintershall Aktiengesellschaft v. Argentina, Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/04/14, 8 December 2008) [129, 125 (footnote 103) and 128]. but ultimately defined it correctly as requiring ‘that the terms of a treaty have to be interpreted according to the meaning they possessed (and in the circumstances prevailing), at the time the treaty was concluded’.62 Ibid., 129 and also 174 (footnote 147). The parallel ICS and Daimler Awards (both issued in 2012) also explicitly defined it as ‘requir[ing] that the meaning and scope of [the relevant treaty] term be ascertained as of the time when the [parties to the treaty] negotiated their BIT’.63 ICS Inspection and Control Services Limited v. Argentina (Jurisdiction), Award (Permanent Court of Arbitration Tribunal, PCA Case No. 2010-9, 10 February 2012) [289]; Daimler Financial Services AG v. Argentina (Jurisdiction), Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/05/1, 22 August 2012) 220. These definitions of the principle – like Fitzmaurice’s original formulation of it – are consistent with a completely objective approach to treaty interpretation. The parties fix the time at which the meaning of the term needs to be ascertained, not the meaning itself. However, investment arbitration advocates and adjudicators have not understood and applied the principle of contemporaneity (merely) in the manner in which they originally defined it. Instead, they have used it as a segue into considering the apparently subjective intentions of the State involved in the treaty as a drafter and the relevant investment arbitration as a party. One of the Wintershall Award’s clearest descriptions of the principle of contemporaneity was made in a footnote to the parenthetical statement that the dispute resolution clause under examination ‘obviously represents the expressed will of the Contracting Parties’.64 Wintershall Award (n 61) para. 174. This signalled the emphasis placed on the will of the parties to the treaty by previous investment cases analysing the scope and operation of dispute resolution clauses. Indeed, it is notable that the principle of contemporaneity was first employed by an investment tribunal in the context of a claimant’s attempt to avoid a local remedies pre-condition to arbitration in the manner of the seminal – and controversial65 See, e.g., Zachary Douglas, ‘The MFN Clause in Investment Arbitration: Treaty Interpretation Off the Rails’ (2 January 2011) 2 Journal of International Dispute Settlement 97, 5. – Maffezini Decision on Objections to Jurisdiction (2000).66 Emilio Agustín Maffezini v. The Kingdom of Spain, Decision on Objections to Jurisdiction (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/97/7, 25 January 2000). In the parts of their award most clearly referencing the principle of contemporaneity, the members of the Wintershall tribunal referred extensively to sections of the Maffezini Decision that themselves bear two often overlooked features: extensive reference to international case law that predates the VCLT and a patent emphasis on the Respondent State’s intentions when negotiating the relevant BIT. The latter is perhaps a function of the former as it surely cannot be a coincidence that the three international decisions considered the most analogous to the MFN-DRP issue addressed in Maffezini – the jurisdictional decisions in the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company case,67 Anglo-Iranian Oil Co (Jurisdiction), Judgment [1952] ICJ Rep. 83. the Rights of US Nationals in Morocco case68 US Nationals in Morocco, Judgment (n 54). and the Ambatielos case69 Ambatielos case (Merits – obligation to arbitrate), Judgment [1953] ICJ Rep. 10; The Ambatielos Claim (Greece/United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland), Award (1956) XII RIAA/RSA 83. – were all handed down in the early to mid-1950s when, as noted above, the subjective approach to treaty interpretation was still the default position among international law scholars. Whether the Wintershall Award’s apparent insistence on a subjective approach to interpretation was a product of the relevant issue being one of State consent or of the mere happenstance of the international decisions most relevant to it pre-dating the shift to objective treaty interpretation that occurred in the 1960s, it is clear that the principle of contemporaneity was introduced into the case law of investment tribunals in the context of a subjective – and not objective – approach to the interpretation of treaties. It is important to note that while the Wintershall Award was the first investment award to explicitly mention the principle of contemporaneity, it was not, to borrow an expression from the world of public health in these exceptional times, investment arbitration’s ‘index case’ for using the contemporaneous approach to treaty interpretation as a means to import a subjective approach. In the Berschader v. Russia Award (2006), the Tribunal did not expressly refer to the principle of contemporaneity but went to pains to focus on the time the relevant treaty was concluded echoing the best-known formulations of the principle including those of Gerald Fitzmaurice70 Fitzmaurice (n 50) 212. and Charles Rousseau.71 Ehrlich (n 20) 109. In doing so, it adopted a starkly subjective approach, even regretting that it was ‘not of course, possible today to read the minds of the Belgian and Soviet negotiators at the time of the conclusion of the Treaty’ and further noting that the Tribunal did not ‘have access to any preparatory works which could help us to clarify the views and intentions of the Contracting Parties’.72 Vladimir Berschader and Moise Berschader v. Russia, Award (Arbitration Institute of the Stockholm Chamber of Commerce, 080/2004, 21 October 2006) [199] (present author’s emphasis). It was in the parallel ICS v. Argentina and Daimler v. Argentina Awards (both of 2012) that investment arbitrators began both explicitly and extensively to rely on the principle of contemporaneity in a form of the principle that betrays a subjective approach to treaty interpretation. The ICS and Daimler tribunals were forced to wade into the controversy of the MFN-DRP issue and logically considered that its application could be settled through a careful interpretation of the term ‘treatment’ in the MFN clause before them. The interpretative approach that the tribunals adopted to this question was surprisingly subjective, the tribunals referencing ‘clues that suggest that the term “treatment” was not simply intended to hold the broad ordinary meaning suggested by the Claimant’ and explicitly noting that the parties had not ‘submitted direct evidence revealing the particular understanding held by the Contracting Parties of the term “treatment” as at the time of the conclusion of the Treaty’.73 See also: ICS, Award (n 63) para. 289 (present author’s emphases); Daimler, Award (n 63) para. 220. When more explicitly applying the principle of contemporaneity (as they understood it), the ICS and Daimler Awards do refer to more objective sources of the meaning of the term, most notably the World Bank Guidelines that they consider ‘a valuable indication of the prevailing view among the community of States during the period leading up to the adoption of the [relevant] BIT[s]’.74 ICS, Award (n 63) para. 295; see also: Daimler, Award (n 63) para. 222. However, even in those parts of the Awards, there is a strong emphasis on the subjective approach, with the ICS tribunal conditioning the relevance of the World Bank Guidelines on the absence of ‘countervailing’ – and presumably more subjective – evidence of the meaning of the treaty term and concluding in a starkly subjective matter. Indeed, instead of stating the meaning of the term ‘treatment’, the Tribunal declared its ‘view [… of what the term] was most likely meant by the two Contracting Parties to refer [to]’, before implicitly stating that this constituted an ‘ascertainment of the ordinary meaning of the terms of the MFN clause’.75 ICS, Award (n 63) paras 295-296 (present author’s emphasis). In reply memorials submitted a matter of months after the ICS and Daimler Awards respectively, the Respondent States in the Philip Morris v. Uruguay and Kiliç v. Turkmenistan cases seized the opportunity to plead the principle of contemporaneity in this context and again in a strikingly subjective form. In Philip Morris, Uruguay notably insisted on what ‘an examination of contemporaneous sources reveal[ed …] the parties … [to] have intended’, ‘the subject matter which the two States had in mind when they inserted the clause in their treaty’ and what ‘the Contracting Parties [… could and] could not have envisioned’.76 ‘Uruguay’s Reply on Jurisdiction’, Philip Morris Brands Sàrl, Philip Morris Products SA and ABAL Hermanos SA v. Uruguay (2012) paras 125-127 (present author’s emphasis). In Kilic, Turkmenistan asserted that the principle of contemporaneity required the tribunal ‘to appreciate whether Turkey and Turkmenistan intended the term “treatment” to cover the BITs DRPs’ and invoked the fact that the claimant had ‘not adduced any evidence to suggest that either Turkey or Turkmenistan maintained a different [view of what …] the word “treatment” was meant by both Contracting Parties at the time of the conclusion of the BIT to refer to’.77 Kiliç Ĭnşaat Ĭthalat Ĭhracat Sanayi ve Ticaret Anonim Şirketiv Turkmenistan, Award (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/10/01, 2 July 2013) [3.3.27 and 3.3.33] (present author’s emphases). When Turkmenistan again advanced the principle of contemporaneity as centred on what ‘the State parties could have contemplated’ in the subsequent Garanti Koza arbitration,78 Garanti Koza LLP v. Turkmenistan, Decision on the Objection to Jurisdiction for Lack of Consent (n 56) para. 55 (present author’s emphasis). the Claimant objected that this undermined the hierarchy of VCLT Articles 31 and 3279 Ibid., 56. and the Tribunal (notably constituted by two common law lawyers and a Geneva-based professor of public international law) found it necessary to remind the parties of the primacy of the text of the treaty in treaty interpretation (the objective approach), in apparent opposition to this subjective form of the principle of contemporaneity.80 Ibid., 57. In later cases, investment arbitration tribunals and parties have referred more heavily to the text of the relevant treaty, but still appear to understand the principle of contemporaneity as seeking to determine the particular parties’ concrete intentions. In Chèque Déjeuner v. Hungary (where a decision on preliminary issues of jurisdiction was rendered in 2016), the parties submitted – and the Tribunal referred to – ‘contemporary evidence of the understanding of the parties at the time of the conclusion’ of their treaty and whether ‘the parties to the treaty intended to exclude arbitration in respect of FET claims’,81 Le Chèque Déjeuner and CD Holding Internationale v. Hungary, Decision on Preliminary Issues of Jurisdiction (ICSID Tribunal, ARB/13/35, 3 March 2016) [157] (present author’s emphases). while the Venezuela US v. Venezuela tribunal’s Interim Award (2016) partially followed the Respondent State’s extensive reference to the principle of contemporaneity to draw conclusions about what ‘the Parties had in mind’.82 Venezuela US SRL v. Venezuela, Interim Award on Jurisdiction (Permanent Court of Arbitration, 2013-34, 26 July 2016) [83]. While these examples may be limited, investment tribunals’ references to the principle of contemporaneity are themselves almost as limited, so in the admittedly quite specific investment law context in which the principle is referenced, it can, without hesitation, be broadly understood as importing a surprisingly subjective approach to treaty interpretation into an area of international dispute settlement and a sub-field of public international law.

§5.05 EXPLANATIONS FOR THE APPARENT DIVERGENCE OF INVESTMENT ARBITRATION TRIBUNALS FROM THE VCLT’S CLEARLY STATED PREFERENCE FOR OBJECTIVE TREATY INTERPRETATION It is remarkable that investment tribunals appear to have adopted a subjective interpretative approach through the citation of only one particular interpretative principle – the principle of contemporaneity – and in relation to only one particular issue – the MFN-DRP issue. This itself obliges us to query whether there is something particular to the principle of contemporaneity and/or the MFN-DRP issue that justifies, or at least prompts, the use of an unusually subjective approach. By asking such questions, we inevitably enter the morass of mere speculation. Certain potential explanations of this phenomenon can nonetheless be posited and evaluated. First, there is substantial evidence that the somewhat obscure principle of contemporaneity is poorly understood. This may appear to have contributed to the understanding of the principle in a variant form. Yet, as noted above, investment tribunals citing the principle of contemporaneity have usually defined it in a manner consistent with its original meaning and the strongly objective approach that the original formulation of the principle entails. Investment tribunals accordingly do not appear to have misapplied the principle as a result of wrongly defining it. Second, one might contend that the particular context in which this subjective form of the principle of contemporaneity is being invoked in investment arbitration, by centring on ratione voluntatis, is more inherently subjective than the contexts in which the principle has been applied in other international courts and tribunals. It is, after all, clear that (a) the issue of whether an MFN clause extends to dispute resolution provisions arises in relation to challenges to the jurisdiction of the tribunal; (b) that such jurisdiction is given by consent; and (c) that consent is something given by a particular party. Over the history of public international law dispute settlement, there have been suggestions that matters of ratione voluntatis require a different interpretative approach that may be considerably more subjective. In the Chorzow Factory Judgment on Jurisdiction (1927), the Permanent Court of International Justice asserted that ‘[w]hen considering whether it has jurisdiction or not, the Court’s aim is always to ascertain whether an intention on the part of the Parties exists to confer jurisdiction upon it’.83 Factory at Chorzów, Jurisdiction, Judgment [1927] PCIJ Reports (Series A) No. 9, 32. That decision obviously predated the shift to the objective approach to treaty interpretation and the VCLT, but the same idea has still found occasional support in the post-VCLT era.
