Judge de Castro, for example, in his dissenting opinion to the ICJ’s Aegean Sea Judgment (1978), stated that the unilateral nature of consent required it to be viewed as a ‘declaration of intention’ and that the terms used in such a document must be regarded not as typical treaty terms, but instead as traces, clues and indicia of the intention of its author.84 Aegean Sea Continental Shelf (Greece v. Turkey), Dissenting Opinion of Judge de Castro [1978] ICJ Reports 62, 63. However, even if this position appears logical, it remains a minority view. There are, after all, strong policy reasons for not allowing evidence of subjective intentions to define the scope of a treaty term in the context of consent to jurisdiction, including – and perhaps especially – in an investment arbitration context. It was noted above that one of the reasons international law shifted to a strongly objective approach to treaty interpretation was the risk of informational asymmetry as to the subjective intentions underlying treaty texts and the need to avoid blindsiding parties who were not involved in the treaty drafting process, yet had their legal rights determined in part by the meaning of the treaty others drafted. While this ‘objectivisation’ development may, in the 1960s, have been prompted primarily by concern for the international legal rights of newly independent States acceding to earlier multilateral treaties, it equally applies to claimants in the investment arbitration context. Indeed, there is something unsavoury and unfair in allowing respondent States to escape liability to a claimant investor under an investment treaty by revealing heretofore unknown documents from their archives to establish a meaning of the jurisdictional provision different to that which appears on the face of the treaty. It is perhaps for this reason that ICJ practice – including even that which predates the adoption of the VCLT – generally takes a much more objective approach to the interpretation of jurisdictional clauses, including where it invokes contemporaneous interpretation in that context.85 See, e.g., US Nationals in Morocco, Judgment (n 54) 189; South West Africa (Second Phase), 1966 Judgment (n 37) para. 17. Third, one might follow the key paragraphs of the ICS and Daimler Awards and argue that, because the principle of contemporaneity is ‘particularly pertinent in the case of bilateral treaties’,86 ICS, Award (n 63) para. 289; Daimler, Award (n 63) para. 220. bilateral investment treaties (BITs) require – or at least endorse – a more subjective approach to treaty interpretation, then point to the fact that the majority of investment arbitration decisions are made under BITs. Bilateral treaties are often regarded by international lawyers as being more akin to contracts principally on account of the greater ease in amending them relative to large multilateral treaties that are, for their part, often considered more akin to laws.87 See, e.g., Paul Duez, ‘L’interprétation des traités internationaux’ (1925) 32 Revue générale de droit international public 429, 430-431; Tom Ginsburg, ‘Bounded Discretion in International Judicial Lawmaking’ (2004) 45 Virginia Journal of International Law 631, 661. However, as explained above, many systems of contract law themselves require an objective approach to interpretation, so there is nothing inherent in the nature of bilateral as opposed to multilateral treaties that necessarily implies the use of a subjective interpretative approach. Further, while there is less likely to be informational asymmetry as to drafters’ subjective intentions between the parties to a State versus State dispute under a bilateral treaty, the nature of investor versus State disputes means that, even if the relevant treaty is bilateral rather than multilateral, the claimant investor could never be in a better position – and would usually be in a much worse position – than the respondent State if a subjective approach to the meaning of BIT’s terms were generally favoured. One might retort that document production, a procedural device designed in part to alleviate information asymmetry in arbitration could, by requiring the respondent State to produce the preparatory work it holds, remove the asymmetry created by a subjective interpretative approach. However, document production by the respondent State would only remove some – not all – of the inevitable information asymmetry. Moreover, document production usually only intervenes well after the arbitration has been launched by the claimant investor, often at considerable expense. A subjective approach that allowed the sometimes quite late production of evidence of the subjective intentions of the drafters to determine the meaning of a key treaty term and, as a result, the claimant’s prospects of success, would still be unfair to claimants, frequently causing them to lose time, waste legal costs and potentially even damage an important relationship, losses that could have been avoided had they been better positioned to predict the likely interpretation of that treaty term at the outset. Only an objective approach to the interpretation of treaty terms, including those relevant to the tribunal’s jurisdiction, can ensure that claimant investors are not put at an informational and/or practical disadvantage vis-à-vis respondent States. It accordingly appears that there is nothing in the structure of investment arbitration or even in the particular features of jurisdictional decisions to justify a more subjective approach to interpretation than that which is provided for in the VCLT and favoured under general international law. We must accordingly look elsewhere for the source of this apparent divergence. It is submitted here that investment arbitration’s understanding of the principle of contemporaneity in a manner which authorises a subjective approach to treaty interpretation is likely to be a consequence not of the nature of investment arbitration or the treaties being interpreted, but instead of the kinds of lawyers frequently involved in investment arbitration as arbitrators and counsel. It is undeniable, at least at the present time, that investment arbitration predominantly involves those with a background and practice largely in international commercial arbitration, particularly at the level of counsel. While all forms of the game of public international law dispute resolution are now played by lawyers, it is only investment disputes that are predominantly argued by practitioners equally or more accustomed to applying domestic laws to the interpretation of contracts, many of which favour a subjective approach. Indeed, international commercial arbitration is very prevalent in the civil law world and has a long and influential history on the European Continent where many of the largest legal practices operating in this very international space are presently based. It is inevitable that many of those practising investment arbitration instinctively approach the interpretation of treaties as they would approach their much more frequent interpretation of contracts. It is equally inevitable that features of contract interpretation may thereby leech into the fabric of investment arbitration. For the possibility of this infiltration to arise, it only takes one group of arbitration lawyers more accustomed to subjective contract interpretation; one fact pattern that simultaneously involves both the interpretation of a contract under an applicable law that favours subjective interpretation and the interpretation of a treaty; or one shrewd counsel – or even arbitrator – who is, on account of her or his background, instinctively able to see the strategic benefit of subtly introducing a subjective element to the interpretation of a particular treaty term, including through the vessel of a poorly understood notion such as the principle of contemporaneity. Increased understanding of core principles of general international law may render parties’ memorials and tribunals’ decisions invoking a subjective approach to treaty interpretation ever rarer. Attempts to import this aspect of many systems of contract law to this field of public international law may even disappear altogether. However, even if this phenomenon fades away, there are likely to be other respects in which the presence of players on the field of investment arbitration who are primarily commercial lawyers and not public international lawyers will, through the vehicle of this relatively heavily adjudicated field of international law, threaten to influence the development of core areas of general public international law. Such influences will not necessarily be positive, but they will not necessarily be negative either. After worrying about the fragmentation of public international law through the increased prevalence of forms of dispute settlement such as investment arbitration, it appears that international law has now reached a point where it can not only accommodate, but also welcome such influences. We must simply be careful not to fail to notice their impacts and then to carefully interrogate the extent to which those developments that affect cross-cutting areas such as treaty interpretation should be generalised across the wide variety of sub-fields now belonging to the discipline of public international law.
